[]{#endorsement.xhtml}

::: {.section .front aria-label="Endorsement Page"}
> []{#endorsement.xhtml_pi .pagebreak aria-label=" page i. " role="doc-pagebreak"}This comprehensive and balanced collection of papers on learning from input processing will be welcomed by those beginning their study of input processing as well as those who are familiar with the field. The papers included tackle the major issues and do so with clarity and authority. It is especially pleasing to see that vocabulary is now getting the attention it deserves in this crucial area of second language acquisition.
>
> [**Paul Nation**, Victoria University of Wellington, New Zealand]{.credit role="doc-credit"}[]{#endorsement.xhtml_pii .pagebreak aria-label=" page ii is blank. " role="doc-pagebreak"}
:::

[]{#series.xhtml}

::: {.section .front .seriespage}
# []{#series.xhtml_piv .pagebreak aria-label=" page iv. " role="doc-pagebreak"}Routledge Handbooks in Second Language Acquisition {#series.xhtml_h1}

Susan M. Gass and Alison Mackey, Series Editors

Kimberly L. Geeslin, Associate Editor

The *Routledge Handbooks in Second Language Acquisition* are a comprehensive, must-have survey of this core sub-discipline of applied linguistics. With a truly global reach and featuring diverse contributing voices, each handbook provides an overview of both the fundamentals and new directions for each topic.

The Routledge Handbook of Second Language Acquisition and Individual Differences

*Edited by Shaofeng Li, Phil Hiver and Mostafa Papi*

The Routledge Handbook of Second Language Acquisition and Psycholinguistics

*Edited by Aline Godfroid and Holger Hopp*

The Routledge Handbook of Second Language Acquisition and Neurolinguistics

*Edited by Kara Morgan-Short and Janet G. van Hell*

The Routledge Handbook of Second Language Acquisition, Morphosyntax, and Semantics

*Edited by Tania Ionin, Silvina Montrul, and Roumyana Slabakova*

The Routledge Handbook of Second Language Acquisition and Discourse

*Edited by Brian Paltridge and Matthew T. Prior*

The Routledge Handbook of Second Language Acquisition and Input Processing

*Edited by Wynne Wong and Joe Barcroft*

For more information about this series, please visit: <https://www.routledge.com/The-Routledge-Handbooks-in-Second-Language-Acquisition/book-series/RHSLA>
:::

[]{#contributors.xhtml}

::: {.section .front .contributors}
# []{#contributors.xhtml_pxiv .pagebreak aria-label=" page xiv. " role="doc-pagebreak"}Contributors {#contributors.xhtml_h1}

[Joe Barcroft]{.bio-name} is Professor of Spanish and Second Language Acquisition and Affiliate Professor of Psychological & Brain Sciences at Washington University in St. Louis, USA. His books include *Lexical Input Processing and Vocabulary Learning* (2015); *Input-Based Incremental Vocabulary Acquisition* (2012); and the volume, co-edited with Javier Muñoz-Basols, *Spanish Vocabulary Learning in Meaning-Oriented Instruction* (2021).

[Philippa Bell]{.bio-name} is Associate Professor of Second Language Education at the *Université du Québec à Montréal* (Canada) and Director of Graduate Programmes in the *Département de didactique des langues*. Her research interests focus on form-focused instruction, task-based language teaching/learning, and the development of literacy in compulsory additional language education.

[Alessandro Giovanni Benati]{.bio-name} is Head of the School of Education at University College Dublin (Ireland). He has extensively researched/taught in the areas of SLA and language teaching. He is author of various books and journal articles on the effects of processing instruction on the acquisition of romance and non-romance languages.

[Susanne E. Carroll]{.bio-name} is an emerita/adjunct professor at the University of Calgary (Canada). Informed by explicit/formal descriptions of linguistic phenomena, her research has explored how input determines language learning outcomes. Her approach is broadly generative but reflects her early training in various linguistic theories/research methods + a healthy scientific scepticism.

[Laurent Dekydtspotter]{.bio-name} is Professor of French Linguistics and Second Language Studies at Indiana University, Bloomington, Indiana, USA. His research addresses second language learners' development of morphosyntax and semantics, nonnative language processing with a focus on its neurocognitive bases, and the role of the parser in nonnative language acquisition and development.

[Friederike Fichtner]{.bio-name} is Associate Professor of German and Second Language Acquisition at California State University, Chico. She received her Ph.D. from the University of Wisconsin-Madison. Her current research interests include learner perceptions of language use, L2 pragmatics, vocabulary acquisition, and sociolinguistic and variationist issues in language learning and teaching.

[[]{#contributors.xhtml_pxv .pagebreak aria-label=" page xv. " role="doc-pagebreak"}Sarah Grey]{.bio-name} is an associate professor at Fordham University (USA) and Associate Editor for *Language Learning*. She studies how adults learn and process multiple languages, and her work in this area has been published in leading journals including *Bilingualism: Language and Cognition* and *Studies in Second Language Acquisition*.

[Nick Henry]{.bio-name} is an assistant professor at The University of Texas at Austin (USA). His research focuses on the interaction between sentence processing and second language pedagogy. This research centers on understanding the effects of psycholinguistically motivated instructional techniques and describing how learners process input.

[Lindsay Hracs]{.bio-name} is a data scientist at the University of Calgary (Canada) with a background in computational modelling of input and exposure in language acquisition. Lindsay's focus has shifted to the field of medicine where she uses machine learning/natural language processing to support epidemiologic research on inflammatory bowel disease.

[Jill Jegerski]{.bio-name} is Associate Professor of Spanish and SLATE (Second Language Acquisition and Teacher Education) in the Department of Spanish and Portuguese at the University of Illinois at Urbana-Champaign (USA). Her primary research interests include second language and bilingual sentence processing, psycholinguistic research methods, and Spanish as a heritage language.

[Shusaku Kida]{.bio-name} is an associate professor at Doshisha University, Japan. His research interests include second language vocabulary acquisition and visual word recognition. He has contributed research papers to journals including *Memory and Cognition, Reading in a Foreign Language, Second Language Research, Studies in Second Language Acquisition*, among others.

[Michael J. Leeser]{.bio-name} is Professor of Spanish and Linguistics at Florida State University (USA). His research has centered on input processing in second language acquisition, the impact of methodological choices on language study outcomes and theory development, and how insights on second language processing can impact instructed language learning.

[Ronald P. Leow]{.bio-name} is Professor of Applied Linguistics and Director of Spanish Language Instruction at Georgetown University, Washington, DC, USA. His areas of expertise include language curriculum development, teacher education, instructed language learning, psycholinguistics, cognitive processes, research methodology, CALL, and feedback. Professor Leow has published extensively in many prestigious journals.

[Fei Li]{.bio-name} is Associate Professor of Applied Linguistics at China University of Geosciences in PR China. Her research interests are instructed language learning, cognitive processes, and written corrective feedback. She has published papers in *Applied Linguistics, Journal of English for Academic Purposes, System*, Routledge books, and key journals in China.

[Silvia Marijuan]{.bio-name} is Associate Professor of Spanish Applied Linguistics at California Polytechnic State University, San Luis Obispo, CA (USA). Her main research is on second and heritage language acquisition and study abroad. She has published in *Second Language Research, Foreign Language Annals, System* and for Routledge and Georgetown University Press.

[[]{#contributors.xhtml_pxvi .pagebreak aria-label=" page xvi. " role="doc-pagebreak"}Alfonso Morales-Front]{.bio-name} is Associate Professor of Spanish Linguistics at Georgetown University (Washington DC, USA). His research interests focus on prosodic aspects of Spanish phonology (syllable, stress, and intonation), usage-based approaches to the acquisition of L1, L2, L3/Ln phonology; and context of acquisition (i.e., study abroad).

[Caroline Payant]{.bio-name} is a professor in the Department of Language Education at the Université du Québec à Montréal (USA). She specializes in additional language teaching and learning from a plurilingual lens. Her research focuses on how plurilingual learners/users mobilize their resources to create meaning in various communicative contexts.

[Patrick Rebuschat]{.bio-name} is Professor of Linguistics and Cognitive Science at Lancaster University (UK), and Distinguished International Professor at the LEAD Graduate School, University of Tübingen (Germany). His research interests include implicit learning, statistical learning, heritage language bilingualism, and second language acquisition.

[Susanne Rott]{.bio-name} is Associate Professor of Germanic Studies and Second Language Acquisition at the University of Illinois Chicago, USA. Her research focuses on second language vocabulary learning, with a focus on polysemy and multi-word expressions. She is also interested in computer-assisted language learning and instructional design.

[Simón Ruiz]{.bio-name} is Lecturer in TESOL in the Department of Philology, Faculty of Education at the University of Cantabria, Spain. His research focuses on the interface of instructed SLA and psycholinguistics, both in fundamental research and applied to second language teaching.

[Cristina Sanz]{.bio-name} is a professor at Georgetown University, Washington DC, USA. She has published several volumes and over 100 articles/chapters on the interaction between individual differences and learning contexts in multilingual development. Recent volumes include *Introducción a la Lingüística Hispánica* (Cambridge) and *Manual para la Formación de Profesores de Español*.

[John W. Schwieter]{.bio-name} is Professor of Spanish, Linguistics, and Psychology, Director of the Language Acquisition, Multilingualism, and Cognition Laboratory and Bilingualism Matters at Wilfrid Laurier University (Canada), and Adjunct Professor of Linguistics at McMaster University (Canada). Research interests include cognitive/neuroscientific approaches to multilingualism; SLA and instruction; and translation and interpreting processes.

[Michael Sharwood Smith]{.bio-name} is Professor Emeritus at Heriot-Watt University in Scotland, co-founder and former editor of *Second Language Research*. His publications cover second language acquisition theory, multilingualism and language cognition. His particular interests include metalinguistic knowledge, crosslinguistic influence, language attrition, processing and the embedding of language within cognition in general.

[Russell Simonsen]{.bio-name} is Assistant Professor of Spanish at Miami University of Ohio (USA). He specializes in Spanish linguistics and second language acquisition. His research examines how native and non-native speakers use and process language, with a particular focus on factors related to syntax and semantics.

[[]{#contributors.xhtml_pxvii .pagebreak aria-label=" page xvii. " role="doc-pagebreak"}Paul D. Toth]{.bio-name} is Associate Professor of Spanish Applied Linguistics at Temple University (USA), where he teaches Spanish language and linguistics and mentors language teachers and doctoral students. His research on language education was twice awarded the *Paul Pimsleur Award* from the American Council on the Teaching of Foreign Languages.

[Annie Tremblay]{.bio-name} is Professor of Linguistics at the University of Texas at El Paso (USA). She obtained her Ph.D. in SLA at the University of Hawaii (2007). Her research focuses on second-language speech perception and spoken-word recognition, with focus on the processing of prosody, lexical stress, and other suprasegmental phenomena.

[John Truscott]{.bio-name} is Professor Emeritus in the Institute of Learning Sciences and Technologies at Taiwan's National Tsing Hua University (Taiwan). His research is focused on the Modular Cognition Framework but also extends to cognitive science more generally and to various areas of second language learning and teaching, notably error correction.

[Bill VanPatten]{.bio-name} is an independent scholar widely known for his work in second language acquisition and second language teaching, with a focus on processing and its relationship to the development of linguistic competence. He currently resides in his home in California where he writes fiction.

[Justin P. White]{.bio-name} is Professor of Spanish, Second Language Acquisition, and Linguistics, Director of the Spanish Basic Language Program at Florida Atlantic University (USA), and Editor of the *Southern Journal of Linguistics*. His research interests include input processing, interactive tasks, and Heritage Learner/L2 learner interface.

[Brent Wolter]{.bio-name} is a professor at Idaho State University (USA) and Ocean University of China. Main research area is L2 vocabulary acquisition with a focus on understanding how vocabulary acquisition and processing occur on a psycholinguistic level. His work has been published in a many leading journals in the field.

[Wynne Wong]{.bio-name} is Professor of French and Second Language Acquisition at The Ohio State University, USA. She is the author of *Input Enhancement: From Theory and Research to the Classroom* (2005) and lead author of two French textbooks: *Liaisons* (2013, 2019) and *Encore* (2016, 2020). She is/has been on the editorial board of the journal *Instructed Second Language Acquisition* and on the advisory committee of *The Canadian Modern Language Review*.

[Akifumi Yanagisawa]{.bio-name} is an assistant professor at the Faculty of Humanities and Social Sciences at the University of Tsukuba, Japan. His research focuses on second language vocabulary acquisition. His work has appeared in journals such as *Language Learning, Studies in Second Language Acquisition*, and *Modern Language Journal*.

[Janire Zalbidea]{.bio-name} is Assistant Professor of Spanish Linguistics and Director of the Multilingualism and Second Language Acquisition Lab at Temple University. Her research focuses on second and heritage language acquisition, including task-based language teaching and development in immersion contexts abroad.
:::

[]{#ack1.xhtml}

::: {#ack1.xhtml_ack .section .acknowledgments role="doc-acknowledgments"}
# []{#ack1.xhtml_pxviii .pagebreak aria-label=" page xviii. " role="doc-pagebreak"}Acknowledgements {#ack1.xhtml_h1}

We would like to thank the series editors, Susan Gass and Alison Mackey, for inviting us to create and edit this volume, which is the first handbook devoted to the topic of input processing in language acquisition. It has been an honor to work with the different authors of this volume and to engage the field in critical reflection and discussion of this essential area of SLA. We thank them for their insights and the many hours of work they dedicated to their chapters. We also thank all of the reviewers for generously sharing their expertise and their time to help improve the quality and thoroughness of the handbook. Finally, we extend a special thank you to Bill VanPatten for agreeing to answer questions in our interview with him as a means of sharing with the readership a series of timely reflections and assessments from someone who has been so directly involved in the development and advancement of IP as an area of inquiry in SLA.
:::

[]{#ch1.xhtml}

:::::::::::::::::::::::::::::: {.section .introduction role="doc-introduction"}
::: {}
# []{#ch1.xhtml_p1 .pagebreak aria-label=" page 1. " role="doc-pagebreak"}[1 ]{.chnum .ordinal}[Introduction ]{.chtitle}[Input Processing, Where Language Acquisition Begins]{.chsubtitle .subtitle role="doc-subtitle"} {#ch1.xhtml_h1}

Wynne Wong

The Ohio State University

Joe Barcroft

Washington University in Saint Louis

DOI: [10.4324/9781003041412-1](https://dx.doi.org/10.4324/9781003041412-1){aria-label="D.O.I. link to this document."}
:::

::: {.section aria-labelledby="sec1_1"}
## Introduction {#ch1.xhtml_sec1_1}

In language acquisition, *input* refers to samples of a target language to which learners are exposed (spoken, written version of that spoken language, sign language, and tactile languages) during some type of communicative act. *Input processing* (IP), in turn, refers to deriving *intake*, a subset of the input that provides pertinent data to a learner's developing linguistic system that can be accommodated in one way or another. In this light, input processing, which is the focus of this volume in the series *Routledge Handbooks in Second Language Acquisition*, is where language acquisition begins.

Historically, Pit [Corder (1967)](#ch1.xhtml_ref1_6){#ch1.xhtml_R_ref1_6 .biblioref role="doc-biblioref"} was the first to make the distinction between input and intake when it comes to language acquisition, arguing that "input is what goes in, not 'what is *available* for going in,' and we may reasonably suppose that it is the learner who controls this input, or more properly \[their\] intake" (p. 165). As the fields of second language acquisition (SLA) and second language (L2) pedagogy advanced, researchers increasingly underscored the importance of the circumstances in which input does or does not become intake (e.g., [Chaudron, 1985](#ch1.xhtml_ref1_5){#ch1.xhtml_R_ref1_5 .biblioref role="doc-biblioref"}; [Faerch & Kasper, 1980](#ch1.xhtml_ref1_7){#ch1.xhtml_R_ref1_7 .biblioref role="doc-biblioref"}). Within this larger context of growing interest in input, intake, and language acquisition from an information processing perspective, [VanPatten (1996)](#ch1.xhtml_ref1_15){#ch1.xhtml_R_ref1_15 .biblioref role="doc-biblioref"} proposed a model[^1] of IP that provided an account of how L2 learners derive intake from sentence-level input as related to the acquisition of morphosyntax. It did so by addressing the following critical questions: *What data do L2 learners attend to and not attend to? Why do learners process certain data and not others? How do answers to these questions affect acquisition?*

Over the past three decades or so, the field of SLA has witnessed a surge of research on how learning mechanisms interact with input to impact acquisition. Much of this research has focused on the acquisition of morphosyntax, but research on IP has also branched out to include the acquisition of other linguistic subsystems, especially lexis. It also has expanded its use of new research methodologies and technologies, such as eye-tracking and priming. Innovations such as these have allowed us to better observe how L2 learners interact with input in real time, leading to a more robust understanding of key questions about IP. Bearing in mind this []{#ch1.xhtml_p2 .pagebreak aria-label=" page 2. " role="doc-pagebreak"}historical context and the centrality of IP in language acquisition, this volume was designed to present and critically assess past and current research on IP while engaging critical reflection about what lies ahead when it comes to theory-building, novel empirical research, and pedagogical implications of future work in this area. *The Routledge Handbook of Second Language Acquisition and Input Processing* is a comprehensive reference that brings together different theoretical perspectives, empirical developments, methodological considerations, and pedagogical implications related to IP in SLA. The volume also highlights lacunae that remain in theory and research on IP and discusses specific directions new research might take to advance the field.
:::

::: {.section aria-labelledby="sec1_2"}
## Defining Key Constructs {#ch1.xhtml_sec1_2}

Below we define seven key constructs pertinent to understanding critical issues related to IP.

- ***Input***. Samples of the target language to which learners are exposed during some type of communicative act. Input commonly takes the form of sentences and discourse but can also include isolated lexical items, which [Brent and Siskind (2001)](#ch1.xhtml_ref1_4){#ch1.xhtml_R_ref1_4 .biblioref role="doc-biblioref"} found to comprise around 10 percent of caregiver speech to children during their first language (L1) acquisition. For example, if someone asks a language learner *Would you like some coffee?* or says *Hurricane Nicole is about to make landfall* in any given target language, these sentences constitute input.
- ***Intake***. The subset of input that contains pertinent data for a learner's developing linguistic system, which in turn may be accommodated by the developing linguistic system. For example, if a language learner hears *I walked to school* and makes use of *-ed* to understand pastness (albeit implicitly), the form--meaning mapping between *-ed* and pastness can be considered intake that becomes available to the implicit developing linguistic system for potential accommodation related to the morphosyntax of the past tense system of the target language. Another example would be if a learner hears the sentence *Look, there's a spider in the corner!*, sees the spider, and begins the process of encoding the word form *spider* and mapping it to its referent. Because lexical acquisition is an incremental process, whatever amount of the target word form (perhaps only *sp....*) its meaning, and the mapping between the two can be considered lexical intake at that given point in time. The same holds true when learners hear or see isolated lexical items, such as *exit* or *bathroom* and begin the incremental process of acquiring them.
- ***Developing linguistic system***. The implicit linguistic system in the learner's mind/brain. [VanPatten (1996)](#ch1.xhtml_ref1_15){.biblioref role="doc-biblioref"} refers to it as "the mental representation of the second language the learner is constructing over time" (p. 5). The parts of this system that subserve morphosyntax are, at least arguably, part of procedural memory whereas the parts of this system that subserve lexis (as well as other more "idiosyncratic" aspects of linguistic knowledge) are subserved by declarative memory (see, e.g., [Ullman, 2016](#ch1.xhtml_ref1_13){#ch1.xhtml_R_ref1_13 .biblioref role="doc-biblioref"}). This system is implicit and unconscious in nature.
- ***Input processing***. The real-time (on-line) process of extracting intake from input, which involves parsing and making appropriate form--meaning and form--function connections over time. The nature of IP changes as one focuses on different levels (or types) of linguistic analysis with respect to different linguistic subsystems. For example, sentence-level IP for the acquisition of morphosyntax pertains to deriving intake about morphosyntactic (grammatical) forms such as verbal morphology and case agreement, whereas lexical IP for the acquisition of lexis pertains to deriving intake about form, meaning, and mapping within developing lexico-semantic networks.
- ***Multilevel (multitype) input processing***. IP across all linguistic subsystems. [Barcroft (2015)](#ch1.xhtml_ref1_3){#ch1.xhtml_R_ref1_3 .biblioref role="doc-biblioref"} referred to it as "how processing resources are allocated at each of many different levels of input processing" []{#ch1.xhtml_p3 .pagebreak aria-label=" page 3. " role="doc-pagebreak"}(p. 15) and posited the need for some type of control mechanism(s) to allocate limited processing resources among each of the many different levels (or types) of IP, including phonological IP, lexical IP, morphosyntactic IP, syntactic IP, pragmatic IP, and sociolinguistic IP. For example, suppose that a learner hears the sentence *She is working at this very moment*. If the learner has already acquired *-ing* verbal forms but not the lexical phrase *at this very moment*, the learner has an opportunity to process *at this very moment*, which is lexical IP (lex-IP). However, if the learner already knows the lexical phrase but has not acquired *-ing* verbal forms for their meaning, the learner has an opportunity to process the *-ing* suffix for its meaning, which is (sentence-level) morphosyntactic IP.
- ***Processing resource allocation***. The manner in which a learner allocates limited processing resources in different ways during IP. This type of allocation may take place within one level or type of IP as well as across different levels or types of IP. For example, suppose that a learner hears the sentence *That house was designed by Frank Lloyd Wright*. If the learner comprehends all of the lexical items in the sentence with relative ease, the learner may have sufficient processing resources available to process the passive verb form for its meaning. However, if the learner has difficulty comprehending all of the lexical items, processing resources would be directed towards the lexical items, and, consequently, the learner may be left with insufficient processing resources to process the passive for its meaning. This example concerns within-level allocation of resources for morphosyntax. On the other hand, if the lexical item *design* is completely novel to the learner, the learner's ability to process the word as input may depend on the extent to which processing the rest of the sentence, including its morphosyntax, exhausts processing resources. This example concerns across-level allocation of resources.
- ***Form--meaning connections/mapping***. The implicit process by which mental/neural connections or mappings are made in real-time between linguistic forms and the referential (real-world) meanings or functions they represent. An example is the form--meaning connection between plural *-s/es* form and plurality in English. For example, a learner hears *dogs* and understands that there is more than one dog. *Intake* is the product of form--meaning connections of this nature.
:::

:::::::::: {.section aria-labelledby="sec1_3"}
## Critical Issues in IP {#ch1.xhtml_sec1_3}

Now let us consider some key issues that are integral to the theory and research on IP.

::: {.section aria-labelledby="sec1_3_1"}
### Why Are Models of IP Considered Internal Theories? {#ch1.xhtml_sec1_3_1}

Theories of different aspects of SLA can be either external or internal. *External theories* deal with contextual factors, such as the environment in which a learner acquires the target language, the nature of the relationship between interlocutors, and sociopolitical issues such as the relative status of L1 and L2. *Internal theories*, on the other hand, deal with how we acquire the target language at the cognitive and neural levels, such as with regard to how we encode different types of formal and semantic features of language and retain them over time, how the developing (mental/neural) linguistic system changes over time, and how central features of different linguistic subsystems (e.g., tense and aspect in verbal morphology, word form and collocational properties of words in lexico-semantic space, mental mechanisms that control what is appropriate versus inappropriate to say in different communicative contexts) are acquired and maintained over time in the mind/brain.

[]{#ch1.xhtml_p4 .pagebreak aria-label=" page 4. " role="doc-pagebreak"}This distinction being made, all models of IP, at least to date, are internal theories because they focus on the internal mental mechanisms that learners use to allocate attention and to parse different aspects of the input to which they are exposed. For example, learners must attend to input to derive intake related to patterns of grammatical gender and number agreement that may appear in a target language. IP for morphosyntactic features of this nature takes place in the mind/brain of the learner as opposed to being a contextual factor related to other potential external theories. From the perspective of [VanPatten's (1996)](#ch1.xhtml_ref1_15){.biblioref role="doc-biblioref"} model of IP, which is an internal theory, a learner may attend to lexical items that are redundant with morphological features such as number agreement as their first means of deriving meaning, as in the case of attending to *three* instead of *-s/es* in the sentence *I saw three cars*. As an additional example, a novel word form such as *wombat* can be processed as input and become, as intake, represented in a learner's developing lexico-semantic system. If that learner already knows what a wombat is, based on previous experience, appropriate meaning can become connected to the novel word form. However, if the learner does not know what a wombat is, the learner also needs to develop and refine an appropriate semantic representation for the word. According to the type of processing -- resource allocation (TOPRA) model ([Barcroft, 2002](#ch1.xhtml_ref1_1){#ch1.xhtml_R_ref1_1 .biblioref role="doc-biblioref"}), which is an internal theory focused on the dissociability of formal, semantic, and mapping components of vocabulary learning, one should not expect increased processing of the word form to increase processing and learning of the semantic component, and vice versa.
:::

::: {.section aria-labelledby="sec1_3_2"}
### How Is IP Conceptualized Across Different Linguistic Subsystems? {#ch1.xhtml_sec1_3_2}

IP does not just lead to the acquisition of syntax and morphosyntax but also to the acquisition of other linguistic subsystems, such as lexis, phonology, and pragmatics. Historically, the primary foci of research on IP have been (a) sentence-level IP and the acquisition of morphosyntax and, at least within the past 25 years or so, (b) lexical IP and the acquisition of vocabulary. IP can be analyzed, however, across other linguistic subsystems, including phonetics and phonology, pragmatics, and sociolinguistic competence. As one changes one's focus from any one of these linguistic subsystems to another, the nature of what intake and developing system means also changes, oftentimes posing difficult but intriguing new challenges for IP researchers. For example: What constitutes units of knowledge about pragmatics? How is input that contains information about pragmatics processed? What is the nature of mental representations with regard to pragmatic competence? The multilevel (multitype) perspective raises these and many other intriguing questions to be addressed.
:::

::: {.section aria-labelledby="sec1_3_3"}
### How Does IP Fit in Different Theories of Language Acquisition? {#ch1.xhtml_sec1_3_3}

Even though IP is where the acquisition of any human language begins, the nature of what we "start with" before exposure to input is another issue. Two major theories of language acquisition that differ in this regard are *generative* (Chomskyan, minimalist, etc.) and *usage-based* (connectionist, emergentist, etc.) theories. The former theory posits Universal Grammar (UG) as an inborn knowledge source for language acquisition that constrains options entertained by our developing linguistic systems over time. The latter theory, on the other hand, posits no such domain-specific constraint and views language acquisition as the result of low-level learning mechanisms that process statistical properties of input over long periods of time. In generative theory, linguistic data interact with UG as a driving force in syntactic development. In usage-based approaches, learners extract regularities from the data to shape the development of syntax. These differences being noted, both generative and usage-based approaches have a role for input.
:::

::: {.section aria-labelledby="sec1_3_4"}
### []{#ch1.xhtml_p5 .pagebreak aria-label=" page 5. " role="doc-pagebreak"}What Is the Relationship Between Input Modality and IP? {#ch1.xhtml_sec1_3_4}

Modalities of input include spoken input, written input, and in the case of non-spoken languages, visual input (as in sign languages) and tactile input (as in the case of Braille). Does modality have an impact on how input is processed? While at least some studies have demonstrated that attention to forms can decrease attention to meaning in more than one modality (see [Greenslade et al., 1999](#ch1.xhtml_ref1_8){#ch1.xhtml_R_ref1_8 .biblioref role="doc-biblioref"}), more research is needed to understand the full range of impacts that modality can have on IP. Some studies (see the combined findings of [Ito & Wong, 2019](#ch1.xhtml_ref1_9){#ch1.xhtml_R_ref1_9 .biblioref role="doc-biblioref"} and [Wong & Ito, 2018](#ch1.xhtml_ref1_18){#ch1.xhtml_R_ref1_18 .biblioref role="doc-biblioref"}) have found, for example, that spoken input is more challenging to process than written input due to the immediate online and fleeting nature of the former. The ways in which this increased difficulty quantitatively or qualitatively affects observable patterns in sentence-level IP for morphosyntax remain to be explored. Similar issues hold true for lex-IP and the acquisition of vocabulary, including, for example, how *multimodal input* (e.g., both spoken and written input at the same time) affects lex-IP and resultant quality and quantity of vocabulary learning.
:::

::: {.section aria-labelledby="sec1_3_5"}
### What Is the Difference Between IP and Language Processing? {#ch1.xhtml_sec1_3_5}

While IP takes place during language use for meaningful purposes, it is useful to clarify the extent to which language processing in general may or may not involve IP. All of us are language learners, even when we process input in our L1 as adults. For example, there is always the possibility that we can learn a new L1 word or lexical phrase. In this sense, language processing always includes the possibility of IP. However, it is important to distinguish between the goals and implications of research focused on IP versus language processing. The former typically focuses on how a language is acquired and how mental representations for a given language develop over time whereas the latter focuses on how language is used by engaging existing mental representations. For example, a native speaker and a novice language learner may be watching the same movie trailer to decide if they want to go see that movie. They are both processing input for meaning to comprehend the same informational content. However, the L2 learner is also processing input at a qualitatively different level as a means of deriving data (intake) that can be provided to their developing linguistic system (mental representation). While this handbook may touch upon issues related to both of these types of language processing, its central focus is *input* processing as it relates to acquisition.
:::

::: {.section aria-labelledby="sec1_3_6"}
### How Can IP Theory and Research Inform L2 Instruction? {#ch1.xhtml_sec1_3_6}

Theory and research on IP have led to evidence-based pedagogical options that previously did not exist. One example is the use of structured input to promote the acquisition of implicit knowledge of target grammatical forms. *Structured input* (SI) activities are informed by different types of processing challenges associated with a given target grammatical structure. The input in SI activities is structured in a way that requires learners to rely on targeted grammatical forms to extract the meaning or functions that they encode. Another example is providing language instruction consistent with the findings of research on lexical IP regarding how vocabulary learning is affected by (a) different ways of presenting vocabulary as input, and (b) different tasks that learners may perform when they are exposed to novel words and lexical phrases as input. These and other examples of pedagogical tools informed by IP theory and research are discussed in detail in this volume.
:::

::: {.section aria-labelledby="sec1_3_7"}
### []{#ch1.xhtml_p6 .pagebreak aria-label=" page 6. " role="doc-pagebreak"}What Innovations in Research Methodology Can Take IP Research to the Next Level? {#ch1.xhtml_sec1_3_7}

If IP is the "moment-by-moment computation of sentence structure" and "the linking of form and meaning during real time comprehension" ([VanPatten, 2015](#ch1.xhtml_ref1_16){#ch1.xhtml_R_ref1_16 .biblioref role="doc-biblioref"}, pp. 92--93), then existing and future research methodologies that can tap into and track learners' online parsing and creation of form--meaning connections should help to advance our understanding in this area greatly, especially when one also triangulates behavioral data with online data. To date, the bulk of the research on processing instruction (PI) has focused on the extent to which this intervention can alter strategies that learners use to process input (e.g., [VanPatten & Cadierno, 1993](#ch1.xhtml_ref1_17){#ch1.xhtml_R_ref1_17 .biblioref role="doc-biblioref"}) as well as the type of cues that they rely on to process sentence-level input. Research of this nature has traditionally involved paper-and-pencil tasks and pretest-posttest designs to assess the effectiveness of PI on various target structures. However, the use of online measures, such as self-paced reading and eye-tracking, are becoming increasingly common, providing a clearer picture of how learners interact with different aspects of input and target structures in real time (e.g., [Wong & Ito, 2018](#ch1.xhtml_ref1_18){.biblioref role="doc-biblioref"}).

While increases in the use of the online measures and triangulation-oriented studies in more recent years to investigate PI and IP in general are welcome and needed, there are additional online tools available that may further advance IP research. These include (a) *pupillometry* as a potential means of measuring levels of effort or cognitive load based on pupil dilation patterns during IP; (b) *event-related potentials* (ERPs) in electroencephalography (EEG) as a means of assessing learners' immediate reactions to syntactic and semantic anomalies when processing sentences; and (c) *functional magnetic resonance imaging* (fMRI) as a means of discerning different types of sentence-level IP processes and processes during lexical IP (e.g., encoding of acoustically varied versus acoustically consistent input). In order to make use of these measures, and in particular the last two, IP researchers should become increasingly familiar with neurophysiological processes and brain regions that subserve different aspects of IP, including both sentence-level IP, lexical IP, and other levels of IP.
:::
::::::::::

::::: {.section aria-labelledby="sec1_4"}
## Structure and Features of This Handbook {#ch1.xhtml_sec1_4}

This handbook is unique in that it is the first volume to explore the evolution of theory and research on IP as a critical process in SLA since its inception over nearly five decades ago. Taking as its point of departure that only input that has been processed can impact SLA, the volume consists of seven sections, 26 chapters, and an epilogue, all of which examine IP through the lens of constructs and contexts related to IP, the role of IP in various theories and linguistic subsystems, mechanisms involved in IP, instructional innovation derived from research on IP, and present and future options for research methodology as they relate to the study of IP.

::: {.section aria-labelledby="sec1_4_1"}
### Sections of the Handbook {#ch1.xhtml_sec1_4_1}

The topics in the volume are divided into the following seven main parts: I. Input Processing: Levels, Contexts, and Modalities; II. Issues in Formal Linguistic Theories; III. Issues in Other Theories and Models; IV. Mechanisms of Input Processing and the Acquisition of Morphosyntax; V. Mechanisms of Input Processing and the Acquisition of Phonology, Lexis, and Pragmatics; VI. Input Processing and L2 Instruction; and VII. Methodological Issues in Research on Input Processing.
:::

::: {.section aria-labelledby="sec1_4_2"}
### []{#ch1.xhtml_p7 .pagebreak aria-label=" page 7. " role="doc-pagebreak"}Components of Each Chapter {#ch1.xhtml_sec1_4_2}

Each chapter in the handbook (with some exceptions) consists of the following sections: (1) Introduction and Definitions, (2) Historical Perspectives, (3) Critical Issues and Topics, (4) Current Contributions and Research, (5) Main Research Methods (as appropriate), (6) Recommendations for Practice, (7) Future Directions, (8) References, and (9) Further Reading.
:::
:::::

::::::::::: {.section aria-labelledby="sec1_5"}
## Content of This Handbook {#ch1.xhtml_sec1_5}

The overarching goal of this handbook is to assess past and current research on IP and to engage the reader in a critical reflection about what lies ahead for future research, theory-building, and implications for practice. As such, the handbook brings together different theoretical perspectives, empirical developments, methodological considerations, and instructional implications that highlight the centrality of IP in SLA. We recognize that most research to date on IP has focused on IP at the sentence level and the acquisition of morphosyntax. Therefore, many of the chapters in the handbook focus on IP from this perspective. Other chapters, however, attend to more recent research and theoretical advances on IP at other levels of linguistic analysis, including IP for the acquisition of lexis and IP at the levels of phonology and pragmatics. As such, it is our hope that the volume will inspire new research and theoretical work that can help to advance our understanding of IP in its totality.

::: {.section aria-labelledby="sec1_5_1"}
### Part I. Types and Contexts of Input Processing {#ch1.xhtml_sec1_5_1}

[Part 1](#pt1.xhtml) of the handbook provides the reader with a view of IP from a broad perspective by considering different *types of IP*, including (a) IP across different linguistic subsystems and (b) IP in both spoken and written modes, and (c) IP in different contexts, including when one acquires their L1 (L1 acquisition) and when one is acquiring two languages as L1s (simultaneous bilingualism). In [Chapter 2](#ch2.xhtml), "Multilevel Input Processing: The Framework and Its Future", Joe Barcroft provides an updated review and discussion of multilevel IP, clarifying how IP can be studied by (a) focusing on one linguistic subsystem (such as lexis or morphosyntax), (b) focusing on multiple linguistic subsystems at the same time, or (c) a combination of both. In [Chapter 3](#ch3.xhtml), "Input Processing in Spoken Versus Written Language", Ronald P. Leow and Fei Li consider how modality of input, written or spoken, may or may not impact how input is processed by L2 learners, considering as well the potential effects of simplification and saliency of input in each of the two modes. In [Chapter 4](#ch4.xhtml), "Input Processing in L1 Acquisition and Simultaneous Bilingualism", John W. Schwieter and Alessandro Benati focus on IP during L1 acquisition and during simultaneous bilingualism, two contexts that are in many ways distinct from SLA but that should be, nevertheless, of interest to L2 researchers wishing to maintain an understanding of what IP is about more broadly.
:::

::: {.section aria-labelledby="sec1_5_2"}
### Part II. Issues in Linguistic Theories {#ch1.xhtml_sec1_5_2}

[Part II](#pt2.xhtml) focuses on the extent to which and the ways in which IP plays a role in three formal linguistic theories: generativism, usage-based approaches, and conceptual semantics. In [Chapter 5](#ch5.xhtml), "Input Processing in Generative Second Language Acquisition", Laurent Dekydtspotter discusses the role of IP in generative theory, including issues such as how the language parser is updated in light of specifications based on Universal Grammar (UG) and how specific []{#ch1.xhtml_p8 .pagebreak aria-label=" page 8. " role="doc-pagebreak"}grammatical elements are selected into a workspace from the minimalist perspective on implicit language learning. In [Chapter 6](#ch6.xhtml), "Input Processing and Usage-Based Approaches", Alfonso Morales-Front and Joe Barcroft consider how usage-based perspectives contribute to our understanding of IP by highlighting areas in which research on IP and usage-based approaches are compatible and mutually informative. In [Chapter 7](#ch7.xhtml), "Input Processing in Conceptual Semantics", Susanne E. Carroll and Lindsay Hracs explore the potential theoretical contributions of conceptual semantics regarding how L2 learners infer ontological conceptual categories from visual stimuli and then map these to nouns and sub-classes of nouns expressing the grammatical category of Number and its semantic correlates.
:::

::: {.section aria-labelledby="sec1_5_3"}
### Part III. Issues in Other Theories and Models {#ch1.xhtml_sec1_5_3}

[Part III](#pt3.xhtml) examines theories and models that directly concern IP or in which IP plays a role in one manner or another. Michael J. Leeser, in [Chapter 8](#ch8.xhtml), "VanPatten's Theory of Input Processing", provides an updated review of [VanPatten's (1996)](#ch1.xhtml_ref1_15){.biblioref role="doc-biblioref"} model/theory of IP, including a current version of its principles and research findings that are pertinent to them. Michael Sharwood Smith and John Truscott, in [Chapter 9](#ch9.xhtml), "Input Processing as an Interaction Between Internal and External Context: The Modular Cognition Framework", view IP as an interaction between internal context and external context within the Modular Cognition Framework ([Truscott & Sharwood Smith, 2019](#ch1.xhtml_ref1_12){#ch1.xhtml_R_ref1_12 .biblioref role="doc-biblioref"}) and offer implications for language instruction, including potential benefits and limitations of input enhancement. Sarah Grey, in [Chapter 10](#ch10.xhtml), "The Declarative/Procedural Model and Input Processing", reviews key tenets of the declarative/procedural (DM) model (e.g., [Ullman, 2016](#ch1.xhtml_ref1_13){.biblioref role="doc-biblioref"}) and considers potential implications of this model for IP with a particular focus on adult SLA, a context of language acquisition in which learners have the cognitive ability to learn grammatical structures implicitly in procedural memory, to learn explicitly "about them" in declarative memory, or both. Finally, in [Chapter 11](#ch11.xhtml), "The Type of Processing -- Resource Allocation (TOPRA) Model", Shusaku Kida explains and reviews numerous studies on the type of processing -- resource allocation (TOPRA) model ([Barcroft, 2002](#ch1.xhtml_ref1_1){.biblioref role="doc-biblioref"}), which posits strong (if not complete) dissociability between different types of processing (for form, meaning, and form--meaning mapping) during IP and predicts trade-offs among these types of processing that are observable when it comes to learning the formal, semantic, and mapping-related components of vocabulary learning.
:::

::: {.section aria-labelledby="sec1_5_4"}
### Part IV. Mechanisms of Input Processing and the Acquisition of Morphosyntax {#ch1.xhtml_sec1_5_4}

[Part IV](#pt4.xhtml) highlights a series of mechanisms involved in IP for the acquisition of morphosyntax. The four chapters in this section address issues related to attention to form and meaning during IP, assignment of agency (e.g., inferring agent from first-noun status as opposed to from grammatical roles) during sentence-level input processing, and the roles of interaction and output in IP. In [Chapter 12](#ch12.xhtml), "Attention to Form and Meaning: [VanPatten (1990)](#ch1.xhtml_ref1_14){#ch1.xhtml_R_ref1_14 .biblioref role="doc-biblioref"} and Beyond", Cristina Sanz traces the historical development of (a) the notion of form--meaning connections within a functional view of language and (b) the learner as a limited capacity processor with a focus on [VanPatten's (1990)](#ch1.xhtml_ref1_14){.biblioref role="doc-biblioref"} study on the role of conscious attention to form and meaning during IP and subsequent replications of this study. Justin P. White and Wynne Wong, in [Chapter 13](#ch13.xhtml), "Lexical Preference in Input Processing", attempt to understand why L2 learners tend to rely on lexical rather than grammatical cues during IP when both encode the same meaning and whether instructional interventions can successfully override this processing strategy for different target structures. Focusing on another IP strategy, Russell Simonsen and Bill VanPatten, in [Chapter 14](#ch14.xhtml), []{#ch1.xhtml_p9 .pagebreak aria-label=" page 9. " role="doc-pagebreak"}"The First-Noun Principle", address L2 learners' tendency to interpret the first noun in an utterance as the subject, positioning this strategy as universal in light of challenges to its universality and highlighting instructional implications of research findings in this area. Lastly, in [Chapter 15](#ch15.xhtml), "Roles of Interaction and Output in Input Processing", Janire Zalbidea and Paul Toth provide an overview of how L2 interaction and output can impact L2 IP in ways that promote linguistic development, focusing primarily on cognitive and psycholinguistic approaches to SLA but also on sociocultural dimensions of L2 learning.
:::

::: {.section aria-labelledby="sec1_5_5"}
### Part V. Mechanisms of Input Processing and the Acquisition of Phonology, Lexis, and Pragmatics {#ch1.xhtml_sec1_5_5}

[Part V](#pt5.xhtml) focuses on mechanisms involved in IP as they relate to the acquisition of phonology, vocabulary, and pragmatics. Two chapters are devoted to vocabulary learning, one on intentional vocabulary learning and the other on incidental vocabulary learning, whereas each of the other two chapters in the section focus on IP for the acquisition of phonology and IP for the development of pragmatic competence. In [Chapter 16](#ch16.xhtml), "Input Processing and the Acquisition of Phonology", Annie Tremblay reviews research on L2 listeners' perceptual learning of L2 sounds and implications of this research for IP, focusing on how acoustic information in the speech signal is mapped onto L2 phonetic categories. Susanne Rott, in [Chapter 17](#ch17.xhtml), "Input Processing and Incidental Vocabulary Acquisition", discusses cognitive processes involved in incidental vocabulary acquisition, highlighting how attention to different word aspects (e.g., form or meaning) affects the learning of novel word form or its meaning as delineated in the type of processing -- resource allocation (TOPRA) model ([Barcroft, 2015](#ch1.xhtml_ref1_3){.biblioref role="doc-biblioref"}). In [Chapter 18](#ch18.xhtml), "Input Processing and Intentional Vocabulary Learning", Akifumi Yanagisawa considers theoretical and pedagogical implications that stem from lexical IP research with regard to intentional vocabulary learning, reviewing tenets of [Barcroft's (2012)](#ch1.xhtml_ref1_2){#ch1.xhtml_R_ref1_2 .biblioref role="doc-biblioref"} input-based incremental (IBI) vocabulary instruction and analyzing [Nation and Webb's (2011)](#ch1.xhtml_ref1_11){#ch1.xhtml_R_ref1_11 .biblioref role="doc-biblioref"} technique feature analysis (TFA) from an IP perspective. Lastly, in [Chapter 19](#ch19.xhtml), "Input Processing and the Acquisition of Pragmatics", Friederike Fichtner proposes an IP perspective on the acquisition of L2 pragmatics as a promising new direction in both L2 pragmatics research and IP research, stimulating crosstalk between the two areas.
:::

::: {.section aria-labelledby="sec1_5_6"}
### Part VI. Input Processing and L2 Instruction {#ch1.xhtml_sec1_5_6}

[Part VI](#pt6.xhtml) focuses on pedagogical interventions informed by theory and research related to input and IP. In [Chapter 20](#ch20.xhtml), "Thirty Years of Processing Instruction and Structured Input", Wynne Wong reviews three decades of research on processing instruction (PI) and structured input (SI) and discusses key issues in this area, such as whether SI activities can be created for linguistic subsystems other than morphosyntax, for example, pragmatics and phonology. In [Chapter 21](#ch21.xhtml), "Explicit Information, Input Processing and Second Language Acquisition", Nick Henry traces the evolution of research on the role of explicit information (EI) in L2 instruction with a focus on how EI influences IP and pushes learners to link form and meaning. In [Chapter 22](#ch22.xhtml), "Comprehensible Input in Language Instruction: Approaches and Techniques", Philippa Bell and Caroline Payant make a distinction between input and comprehensible input (CI) and discuss how the latter is critical for SLA while reviewing various meaning-oriented approaches and techniques that provide CI to L2 learners. Lastly, in [Chapter 23](#ch23.xhtml), "Approaches to Vocabulary Instruction from an Input Processing Perspective", Brent Wolter considers implications for vocabulary teaching and learning from an IP perspective, considering issues such as the role of input and different ways of assessing the effectiveness of instructional approaches and techniques.
:::

::: {.section aria-labelledby="sec1_5_7"}
### []{#ch1.xhtml_p10 .pagebreak aria-label=" page 10. " role="doc-pagebreak"}Part VII. Methodological Issues in Research on Input Processing {#ch1.xhtml_sec1_5_7}

[Part VII](#pt7.xhtml) of the handbook addresses methodological issues in research on IP. In [Chapter 24](#ch24.xhtml), "A Systematic Methodological Review of Offline Input Processing Research", Simón Ruiz and Patrick Rebuschat evaluate behavioral IP research designs by addressing issues such as the choice of participant populations and types of statistical analyses. In [Chapter 25](#ch25.xhtml), "Research on Online Input Processing", Jill Jegerski focuses on online data collection techniques such as eye-tracking, EEG/ERP, and neuroimaging in consideration of issues such as the empirical indicators of learning during IP, strategies and pitfalls that characterize the processing of language learners, and effects of instruction on online processing. Lastly, in [Chapter 26](#ch26.xhtml), "Think-aloud Protocol, Trials-to-criterion, and Triangulation between Online and Offline Measures in IP Research", Silvia Marijuan highlights the benefits of triangulating online and offline measures with particular attention to think-aloud protocols and trials-to-criterion in IP/PI research and where these two procedures fall with respect to the distinction between online and offline.
:::

::: {.section aria-labelledby="sec1_5_8"}
### Epilogue: Interview with Bill VanPatten {#ch1.xhtml_sec1_5_8}

Finally, the handbook concludes with an epilogue, which is an interview conducted with Bill VanPatten about the future of IP theory and research. This interview was conducted on September 17, 2021, and included the following five key questions: (1) Where would you like to see research on IP go in the coming years and decades? (2) What do you think about efforts to broaden the focus of IP research beyond a primary focus on morphosyntax? (3) To what extent do you think it is useful to review research not conducted within an IP perspective from an IP perspective? (4) Do you have any suggestions when it comes to the future of research methodology in studies on IP or processing instruction? (5) What do you view to be the most critical issues for future research on processing instruction and structured input and its instructional implications?
:::
:::::::::::

::: {.section aria-labelledby="sec1_6"}
## Intended Audience of the Handbook {#ch1.xhtml_sec1_6}

The target readership for this handbook includes researchers, graduate students, and practitioners whose interests lie in cognitive and psycholinguistic aspects of SLA and language instruction. The volume may be used as a textbook in graduate seminars that deal with L2 processing issues, such as the acquisition of grammar, vocabulary, or both. Linguistics courses that deal with acquisition can also benefit from this volume. We also expect this volume to be an important reference book for any SLA course and a valuable tool for graduate students writing masters theses or doctoral dissertations. We anticipate that this volume will be an important reference book for university libraries.

The handbook should also appeal to readers interested in applied linguistics in a broader sense. Consider, as one example, specialists in language education. Researchers in this area clearly want to have a solid understanding of the role of IP given its central role in language acquisition, and the pedagogical outgrowths of research on IP, such as processing instruction for L2 grammar or input-focused approaches to vocabulary learning, should be within the main wheelhouse of what specialists in language education need to know. As a second example, consider specialists in the neurolinguistics of SLA, such as those who do functional magnetic resonance imaging (fMRI) or EEG/ERP research on the acquisition of L2 syntax. Researchers in this area should be equipped with an understanding of how L2 learners process input as they pursue different more neurally focused on-line research on SLA.
:::

::: {.section aria-labelledby="sec1_7"}
## []{#ch1.xhtml_p11 .pagebreak aria-label=" page 11. " role="doc-pagebreak"}New Directions in the Future of IP Theory and Research {#ch1.xhtml_sec1_7}

In bringing together different theoretical perspectives, empirical advances, considerations in research methodology, and instructional implications, this handbook highlights the central role of IP, the place where language acquisition (first or additional) begins. Importantly, the handbook includes chapters on topics that we anticipate to be novel and, hopefully, intriguing to the entire readership, including those who already specialize in and conduct research on IP. Readers are provided with novel content to consider in a number of chapters, such as those that focus on multilevel IP; the relationship between IP and major theories such as generativism, usage-based perspectives, and conceptual semantics; the relationship between IP and the declarative/procedural model; and the role of IP in the acquisition of linguistic subsystems beyond morphosyntax and lexis, in the present case, phonology and pragmatics. In light of the new perspectives and data presented in these and other chapters, we hope that this volume will serve as a springboard to spark critical reflection about what the future of IP research can look like for both theory-building and practice.

We also hope that this handbook will engage scholars who do not normally work on IP to consider their existing research and potential future studies from an IP perspective. As one example, L2 vocabulary researchers who previously have not been motivated by IP as a theoretical perspective may be encouraged to do so by examples provided in this handbook, such as when Kida ([Chapter 11](#ch11.xhtml)) discusses past and future applications of the TOPRA model and when Yanagisawa ([Chapter 18](#ch18.xhtml)) chooses to reanalyze (after considering TOPRA) [Nation and Webb's (2011)](#ch1.xhtml_ref1_11){.biblioref role="doc-biblioref"} technique feature analysis (TFA) from an IP perspective. Researchers in L2 pragmatics also may benefit from Fichtner's consideration of existing and potential future research from an IP perspective in a groundbreaking effort that requires us to reconsider what we mean by constructs such as *intake* and *developing system* for linguistic subsystems other than morphosyntax and lexis. In addition to clarifying this opportunity for researchers who previously have not focused on IP, we also note that future research can address issues related to IP that are not areas of primary focus in this volume, including (a) the role of IP in non-spoken (e.g., signed) languages; (b) the role of individual differences, such as working memory, in IP; and (c) the role of IP in previously uninvestigated populations such as learners at high proficiency levels, heritage language learners, learners in study abroad contexts, and older adults who are 60 years of age and above.

Finally, we conclude by highlighting the potential of present and future technological advances in research methodology. All levels, types, and stages of IP ultimately take place in the brain. Chapters in this volume on online methodologies (Jegerski, [Chapter 25](#ch25.xhtml)) and triangulation between behavioral and online techniques (Marijuan, [Chapter 26](#ch26.xhtml)) review and analyze the potential of existing techniques that can provide us with more direct views of IP at the neural level, techniques that clearly have not been exploited to their full potential to date. Techniques reviewed in these chapters include think-aloud protocols, trials-to-criterion, self-paced reading, eye-tracking, event-related potentials (ERPs), and neuroimaging (e.g., functional neuroimaging \[fMRI\]). Researchers can make use of the discussion and analyses of advantages and disadvantages of these techniques with a view toward a more fine-grained, real-time, and neurally focused account of different aspects of IP. Staying abreast of and utilizing these methodologies should continue to advance our understanding of IP and make us more prepared when it comes to adopting other innovations as they emerge.
:::

::: {.endnotes role="doc-endnotes"}
## Note {#ch1.xhtml_h2}
:::
