::: {.section .part role="doc-part"}
# []{#pt1.xhtml_p13 .pagebreak aria-label=" page 13. " role="doc-pagebreak"}[Part I ]{.chnum .ordinal}[Input Processing ]{.chtitle}[Types and Contexts[]{#pt1.xhtml_p14 .pagebreak aria-label=" page 14 is blank. " role="doc-pagebreak"}]{.chsubtitle .subtitle role="doc-subtitle"} {#pt1.xhtml_h1}
:::

[]{#ch2.xhtml}

::: {}
# []{#ch2.xhtml_p15 .pagebreak aria-label=" page 15. " role="doc-pagebreak"}[2 ]{.chnum .ordinal}[Multilevel Input Processing ]{.chtitle}[The Framework and Its Future]{.chsubtitle .subtitle role="doc-subtitle"} {#ch2.xhtml_h1}

Joe Barcroft

Washington University in St. Louis

DOI: [10.4324/9781003041412-3](https://dx.doi.org/10.4324/9781003041412-3){aria-label="D.O.I. link to this document."}
:::

::: {.section .introduction role="doc-introduction"}
## Introduction and Definitions {#ch2.xhtml_sec2_1}

This chapter focuses on *multilevel (multitype) input processing*, which refers to how learners process input for language acquisition across all linguistic subsystems, including phonology, vocabulary, grammar, pragmatics, and sociolinguistic competence. We begin by defining some key terms related to input processing (IP) that, when considered across different levels or types of input, are useful in understanding why we need to study multilevel/multitype IP. Below we define the following terms with regard to their general meaning and, as appropriate, how they pertain to multilevel IP as a framework for research and theory: *input, input processing, intake, developing linguistic system*, and *level (type) of input processing*.

- ***Input*** refers to samples of a target language to which a learner is exposed. Although we may tend to think of input as individual sentences (sentence-level input) or series of sentences (discourse-level input), in many instances input also consists of isolated lexical items. [Brent and Siskind (2001)](#ch2.xhtml_ref2_11){#ch2.xhtml_R_ref2_11 .biblioref role="doc-biblioref"} found, for example, that an average of 9% of input provided to children acquiring their first language (L1) consisted of isolated lexical items. One can infer that something similar holds true by extension for learners acquiring a second language (L2) in both formal and naturalistic contexts of L2 acquisition. Input needed for the acquisition of other levels/types of linguistic knowledge includes elements that go beyond those of word-level, sentence-level, or discourse-level input alone. The issue of what constitutes input for the acquisition of pragmatics, for example, may go beyond definitions of input that have been offered to date (see Fichtner, this volume).
- ***Input processing*** refers to how learners attend to and parse different aspects of input as they derive intake (defined below) from input. [VanPatten (2015)](#ch2.xhtml_ref2_23){#ch2.xhtml_R_ref2_23 .biblioref role="doc-biblioref"} drew an analogy between IP and the process of nectar-gathering in bees. With reference to multilevel IP, the nature of what IP means (along with to what the analogy of gathering nectar refers to, from lexical and their meanings to morphosyntactic structures and their meanings and so forth) changes as one focuses on different levels and aspects of language acquisition. For example, IP that leads to the acquisition of a phonological contrast such as /r/ versus /l/ in English is different in nature than IP that leads to what counts as a pragmatically appropriate response in English greeting such as *What's up?*
- ***[]{#ch2.xhtml_p16 .pagebreak aria-label=" page 16. " role="doc-pagebreak"}Intake*** refers to the subset of the input that a learner processes and that is made available to the developing linguistic system. As is the case for the meanings of input and IP, the meaning of intake also changes when one considers the acquisition of different levels, types, or aspects of language. At the lexical level, for example, intake has been defined as "the multiple bits and pieces of word form and the multiple bits and pieces of semantic information associated with word forms that are attended to in the input and made available to the developing lexicosemantic system" ([Barcroft, 2015](#ch2.xhtml_ref2_6){#ch2.xhtml_R_ref2_6 .biblioref role="doc-biblioref"}, p. 23). The definition of intake with respect to other linguistic subsystems, such as phonology or pragmatics, is likely to change substantially as well.
- ***Developing linguistic system*** refers to the knowledge source upon which language learners/users rely to communicate (comprehend language, produce language, etc.) at any point in time during their language development. The nature of the knowledge source differs according to which types/levels of language processing need to be engaged at any given point in time. The neurological underpinnings of the knowledge source also can be different. According to Ullman ([2004](#ch2.xhtml_ref2_19){#ch2.xhtml_R_ref2_19 .biblioref role="doc-biblioref"}, [2005](#ch2.xhtml_ref2_20){#ch2.xhtml_R_ref2_20 .biblioref role="doc-biblioref"}), for example, memory for vocabulary/lexis, which is of an idiosyncratic nature, is stored in brain regions that subserve declarative memory whereas morphosyntax, which is of a procedural nature, is stored in brain regions that subserve procedural memory. *Developing lexical system* has been defined, for example, as "a lexicosemantic system in the target language in which degree of knowledge of individual word forms and the semantic networks associated with each of those word forms gradually develops and is refined over time" ([Barcroft, 2015](#ch2.xhtml_ref2_6){.biblioref role="doc-biblioref"}, p. 23).
- ***Level/type of input of input processing*** refers to one of the various levels at which learners may process different levels/types of input, that is, attend to and parse pertinent aspects of different levels/types of input. These include information at the phonetic-phonological, lexical, derivational-morphological, inflectional-morphological, syntactic, and pragmatic levels as well as others, such as at the level of sociolinguistic competence.

Subsequent sections of the chapter focus on (a) the history of work on multilevel IP to date; (b) critical issues and topics related to multilevel/multitype IP; (c) current contributions of research in this area to date, which is limited; (d) research methods that can be used to investigate multilevel IP; (e) some practical recommendations that can be gleaned from viewing input processing from a multilevel/multitype perspective; and (f) consideration of potential new research that can advance our understanding in the future.
:::

::: {.section aria-labelledby="sec2_2"}
## Historical Perspectives {#ch2.xhtml_sec2_2}

The development of theory and research on IP in the 1990s focused largely, if not completely, on sentence-level IP as it relates to the acquisition of grammar, or (morpho)syntax. VanPatten and Cadierno's [(1993)](#ch2.xhtml_ref2_25){#ch2.xhtml_R_ref2_25 .biblioref role="doc-biblioref"} seminal study assessed how manipulations in sentence-level input---or sentence-level *structured input*---affected the extent to which L2 Spanish learners could make use of grammatical markers such as the personal *a* to determine subjects versus direct objects in L2 Spanish sentences by requiring them to interpret Spanish sentences that followed object--verb word order. Their results favored the use of structured input and *processing instruction*, the latter of which also included explicit instruction and strategies instruction (see Henry, this volume, and Wong, this volume, for more on these) as related to how subjects and direct objects work in Spanish. Since 1993, numerous other studies have investigated how different types of sentence-level input affect the extent to which learners attend to different morphosyntactic features within those sentences. One commonly examined manipulation of this nature has been to remove []{#ch2.xhtml_p17 .pagebreak aria-label=" page 17. " role="doc-pagebreak"}lexical items (key words such as *yesterday* or *right now*) that convey meaning that morphosyntactic features might otherwise convey (key grammatical markers such as the morphemes underlined in *walk[ed]{.underline}, is walk[ing]{.underline}*) (see White & Wong, this volume on *the lexical preference principle*). Another has been to require learners to attend to grammatical markers such as the direct-object marker *a* in Spanish (following [VanPatten & Cadierno, 1993](#ch2.xhtml_ref2_25){.biblioref role="doc-biblioref"}) in order not to interpret the first noun in a sentence as the subject, that is, to move beyond what VanPatten denominated *the first-noun principle*.

This historical trend regarding work on IP in the 1990s is accompanied by what can be considered some exceptions when it comes to types of input other than individual sentences. One example is [VanPatten's (1990)](#ch2.xhtml_ref2_21){#ch2.xhtml_R_ref2_21 .biblioref role="doc-biblioref"} study, which revealed negative effects of attention to a grammatical form as compared to attention to a content word on learners' comprehension of discourse-level input. Even if not conceived of or discussed from the perspective of multilevel IP in particular, this study and numerous partial replications of it since 1990 (see Sanz, this volume), considered manipulations of discourse-level input as opposed to individual sentences only. Research of this nature, in addition to numerous studies on the effects of structuring input at the level of individual sentences, have informed VanPatten's theoretical proposals and instructional recommendations related to IP---IP principles, a role for structured input in language instruction, and so forth (see, e.g., VanPatten, [1996](#ch2.xhtml_ref2_22){#ch2.xhtml_R_ref2_22 .biblioref role="doc-biblioref"}, [2015](#ch2.xhtml_ref2_23){.biblioref role="doc-biblioref"})---which have continued to inspire new research on IP to date.

In addition to research on sentence-level IP and the acquisition of grammar, since approximately 2000, research on lexical IP and the acquisition of vocabulary (lexis) has been developing and expanding. Much of the research in this area has focused on how learners allocated limited processing resources when presented with isolated lexical items as input (e.g., [Barcroft, 2002](#ch2.xhtml_ref2_3){#ch2.xhtml_R_ref2_3 .biblioref role="doc-biblioref"}), but other research has examined lexical IP and vocabulary learning in contexts of discourse-level input (e.g., [Barcroft, 2009](#ch2.xhtml_ref2_4){#ch2.xhtml_R_ref2_4 .biblioref role="doc-biblioref"}; [Pulido, 2009](#ch2.xhtml_ref2_15){#ch2.xhtml_R_ref2_15 .biblioref role="doc-biblioref"}; Rott [1999](#ch2.xhtml_ref2_16){#ch2.xhtml_R_ref2_16 .biblioref role="doc-biblioref"}, [2007](#ch2.xhtml_ref2_17){#ch2.xhtml_R_ref2_17 .biblioref role="doc-biblioref"}) or sentence-level input ([Barcroft, 2019](#ch2.xhtml_ref2_7){#ch2.xhtml_R_ref2_7 .biblioref role="doc-biblioref"}). The growing number of studies on lexical IP (lex-IP) since 2000 have led to key findings about how learners attend to different aspects of vocabulary in the input based on how the input is presented (*input-based effects*) and the types of tasks that learners are asked to perform while attending to the input (*task-based effects*). [Barcroft (2015)](#ch2.xhtml_ref2_6){.biblioref role="doc-biblioref"} provides a summary and analysis of these effects, including 30 observations that can be made about lex-IP and vocabulary learning (see Appendix) based on research on a variety of issues related to the processing and learning of novel lexical forms, their meanings, and mappings between lexical forms and their meanings. To provide one example, several observations concern how different types of processing (semantic, structural, and mapping) are at least largely dissociable from one another, as is consistent with the *type of processing -- resource allocation* (TOPRA) model and its predictions regarding how increases in semantic processing can necessarily reduce processing of novel word form and, consequently, novel word form learning (see Kida, this volume, for more on theory, research, and instructional implications of the TOPRA model).

One natural outgrowth of increased research and theoretical developments on lex-IP, in addition to all of the previous and continuing work on sentence-level IP, was the need to view both types of IP from a larger theoretical perspective. In this light, [Barcroft (2015)](#ch2.xhtml_ref2_6){.biblioref role="doc-biblioref"} proposed multilevel IP as a new framework within which one can view IP and within which one might conduct new research on IP. The proposal included a role for multiple levels of IP for different types of linguistic knowledge, including IP for phonology, lexis, morphology, syntax, pragmatics, and sociolinguistic competence. It also broached key issues related to multilevel IP, such as how the definitions of constructs like *input*, *intake*, and *developing system* can change for different levels of linguistic analysis. Issues of this nature are critical and continue to pose challenges to the future of multilevel IP as a framework.

[]{#ch2.xhtml_p18 .pagebreak aria-label=" page 18. " role="doc-pagebreak"}Where are we with theory and research on multilevel IP today? Much of the most recent work in this area concerns (a) further defining what is meant by multilevel IP; (b) interpreting existing research on sentence-level IP and lex-IP with regard to how they may be informative to one another; (c) expanding theory and research on IP regarding linguistic subsystems other than morphosyntax and lexis; and (d) reinterpreting pertinent research beyond that of sentence-level or lexical IP, such as IP for phonology and IP for pragmatic competence. [Barcroft (2021)](#ch2.xhtml_ref2_8){#ch2.xhtml_R_ref2_8 .biblioref role="doc-biblioref"} recently addressed the first two of these four issues. The last two, concerning increased attention to IP for phonology and pragmatics (beyond morphosyntax and lexis), are addressed in two chapters in this volume: Tremblay's chapter about IP and the acquisition of phonology and Fichtner's chapter about IP and the acquisition of pragmatics. Defining notions such as *input*, *intake*, and *developing system* may be challenging and may change drastically as one moves between linguistic subsystems as different as are phonology and pragmatics, but efforts to do so may be more than worthwhile, expanding our understanding of IP across the board and expanding beyond other areas of research on the acquisition of phonology and pragmatics.
:::

:::::::: {.section aria-labelledby="sec2_3"}
## Critical Issues and Topics {#ch2.xhtml_sec2_3}

Viewing IP from a multilevel perspective makes the study of IP a more complex endeavor, but the complexity reflects the complex nature of IP and language acquisition. Numerous variables and interactions between them come into play. Moreover, there is very little research to date on the topic of multilevel IP (exceptions are discussed later in this chapter), which is a major limitation but also an opportunity when it comes to what new information we might learn about IP and language acquisition as the amount of research increases over time. These points being made, this section of the chapter presents and discusses five questions that point to critical issues and topics to be addressed with regard to multilevel IP. They are the following:

1.  How are processing resources allocated across different levels of IP?
2.  How are processing resources allocated within different levels of IP?
3.  How does the meaning of *intake* change across different levels of IP?
4.  How do different levels of IP interact with one another?
5.  How do different levels of intake inform linguistic systems?

Each of these five questions is addressed in turn below, but one note related to the use of the term "level" is in order before proceeding. The use of this term is meant to reflect how language acquisition and use involve multiple linguistic subsystems---phonetics and phonology, lexis, derivational morphology, inflectional morphology, syntax, pragmatics, and so forth. These indeed can be viewed as different levels of linguistic knowledge, but the relationship between them need not always be viewed as being hierarchical in nature, both in terms of the relationship between them and how they may be stored at the neurological level. Different types of lexicalized language need not be viewed as something "lower" (in importance or with regard to top-down versus bottom-up processing) than different types of morphosyntactic competence, for example, and maintenance of more idiosyncratic aspects of language such as vocabulary in brain regions that subserve declarative memory need not be viewed as "lower" or "higher" than maintenance of morphosyntactic competence in brain regions that subserve procedural memory. It is with this note or caveat that the term "level" is used and discussed here; it is generally interchangeable with the term "type" such that the discussion could be about multitype IP, different types of IP, different types of intake, and so forth.

::: {.section aria-labelledby="sec2_3_1"}
### []{#ch2.xhtml_p19 .pagebreak aria-label=" page 19. " role="doc-pagebreak"}How Are Processing Resources Allocated Across Different Levels of IP? {#ch2.xhtml_sec2_3_1}

A first critical issue related to multilevel IP concerns how learners' limited processing resources are allocated among different levels of IP once the different levels have been identified. As learners push to get meaning from any level of input, their limited processing resources may be directed in different ways due to a variety of different factors. As one example, a learner of Spanish may be presented with a single sentence that encodes pastness morphologically and lexically, as in the case of *Roma cayó hace siglos* "Rome fell centuries ago". According to VanPatten's lexical preference principle, in the push to get meaning, the learner is likely to get pastness from the lexical phrase *hace siglos* "centuries ago" instead of getting pastness by processing the third-person past marker *-ó* in *cayó* "fell". But what if the learner has not yet acquired the lexical phrase *hace siglos*? In such a case, the learner may be more likely to rely on *-ó* to get pastness. Therefore, allocation of processing resources toward processing input at level of inflectional morphology increases due to lack of knowledge on the lexical front.

Barcroft ([2015](#ch2.xhtml_ref2_1){#ch2.xhtml_R_ref2_1 .biblioref role="doc-biblioref"}, [2021](#ch2.xhtml_ref2_2){#ch2.xhtml_R_ref2_2 .biblioref role="doc-biblioref"}) presents a model indicating how limited processing resources must be allocated across different levels of IP as part of the overall process of multilevel IP. Although it does not include detailed mechanisms for how specific variables affect the allocation of different amounts of processing resources across different levels, as in the case of the example above between the levels of lexis and inflectional morphology, it does indicate a wide range of different levels of IP that are going to require processing resources for IP to ensue at each level.

As can be seen in the present version of the model ([Figure 2.1](#ch2.xhtml_fig2_1){#ch2.xhtml_R_fig2_1}), eight different levels of IP are depicted. Each of these eight levels needs sufficient processing resources for IP to ensue at the level in question. The different levels (types) of IP include phonological IP (phon-IP), lex-IP, derivational-morphological IP (d.morph-IP), inflectional-morphological IP (i.morph-IP), syntactic IP (syn-IP), pragmatic IP (prag-IP), discourse-level IP (disc-IP), and sociolinguistic IP (soc-IP). New research on multilevel IP may help to clarify more specifically, how limited processing resources are allocated or distributed among the different levels. In doing so, they should []{#ch2.xhtml_p20 .pagebreak aria-label=" page 20. " role="doc-pagebreak"}help to explain how variables such as whether a key lexical item in the input is known or not known affect processing allocation across different levels of IP at a given point in time.

![[[Figure]{.label} [2.1 ]{.ordinal}Depiction of how processing resources need to be allocated in multilevel IP](#ch2.xhtml_R_fig2_1){.backlink aria-label=" This link returns to its first in-text citation. " role="doc-backlink"} [(adapted from Barcroft, [2015](#ch2.xhtml_ref2_1){.biblioref role="doc-biblioref"}, [2021](#ch2.xhtml_ref2_2){.biblioref role="doc-biblioref"}).]{.source .credit role="doc-credit"}](images/fig2_1_B.jpg){#fig2_1 alt="A circle in the middle that is labeled Resource Allocation System and eight circles surrounding the middle circle to illustrate that resources are allocated to different levels of input processing. Circle one is labeled phon-I P, circle two is labeled lex-I P, circle three is labeled d.morph-I P, circle four is labeled i-morph-I P, circle five is labeled syn-I P, circle six is labeled prag-I P, circle seven is labeled disc-I P, and circle eight is labeled soc-I P. An arrow connects each outer circle to the inner circle."}
:::

::: {.section aria-labelledby="sec2_3_2"}
### How Are Processing Resources Allocated Within Different Levels of IP? {#ch2.xhtml_sec2_3_2}

Another critical issue raised by the multilevel perspective concerns how processing resources are allocated within any single level of IP, including single levels of IP for which there is little if any research to date. Because sentence-level IP has been investigated, one finds proposals regarding how processing resources tend to be allocated at that level. VanPatten's (e.g., [2015](#ch2.xhtml_ref2_23){.biblioref role="doc-biblioref"}, [2020](#ch2.xhtml_ref2_24){#ch2.xhtml_R_ref2_24 .biblioref role="doc-biblioref"}) principles indicate, for example, that learners apply a default strategy of assigning the role of subject to the first noun in a sentence (the first-noun principle) and tend to process items in sentence-initial position to the greatest extent, those in sentence-final position to a lesser extent, and those in sentence-medial position to the least extent (the sentence location principle). Furthermore, because lexical IP has been investigated increasingly over the past 25 years, we also have observations and proposals for that level, such as the proposal that processing word form, word meaning, and the mapping between word form and word meaning are (at least) largely dissociable processes (see appendix for 30 observations about lexical IP from [Barcroft, 2015](#ch2.xhtml_ref2_1){.biblioref role="doc-biblioref"}).

While research and theoretical work on sentence-level IP and lexical IP have helped us to understand more about how processing resources are allocated within each of those two levels, what might future research from the perspective of multilevel IP tell us about allocation of limited processing resources at other levels? The general idea that input is processed across different levels implies that each level might also be investigated independently, leading to questions such as *How are limited processing resources allocated during IP and acquisition of phonemic contrasts?* and *How are limited processing resources allocated during IP and acquisition of different components of pragmatic competence?* Given that little (if any) research has been conducted to date on the acquisition of phonology and pragmatics from an IP perspective, the potential for new research from an IP perspective in these areas to break new ground is strong.
:::

::: {.section aria-labelledby="sec2_3_3"}
### How Does the Meaning of *Intake* Change Across Different Levels of IP? {#ch2.xhtml_sec2_3_3}

A third critical issue related to multilevel IP is how the meaning of intake changes across different levels of IP for different linguistic subsystems. As mentioned previously, intake at the lexical level consists of bits and pieces of lexical form and meaning and mappings between them. With reference to IP for morphosyntax, intake is data about how the grammatical system works. These data are provided to the developing grammatical system. Whether or not they are acted upon by a separate in-born knowledge source such as Universal Grammar, the data concern how the language works at the level of morphosyntax. But what about other levels of IP? What does intake mean when it comes to the acquisition of the phonological system of a language? And what does it mean when it comes to the acquisition of pragmatics? To what extent is the construct of *intake* even pertinent when it comes certain linguistic subsystems? Questions such as these are critical to address as theory and research continue to advance in this area.
:::

::: {.section aria-labelledby="sec2_3_4"}
### How Do Different Levels of IP Interact with One Another? {#ch2.xhtml_sec2_3_4}

A fourth issue concerns how different levels of IP may interact and impinge upon one another. The example above regarding how lack of knowledge of a lexical phrase such as *centuries ago* (knowledge at the lexical level) may lead to more processing of a grammatical form such as a third-person past tense marker is one case in point, and the possibilities of different types of interactions []{#ch2.xhtml_p21 .pagebreak aria-label=" page 21. " role="doc-pagebreak"}across other levels are numerous. As another example, consider how the extent to which one can distinguish between phonemes at the phonological may impact vocabulary learning. If a learner of L2 English has not processed enough input of the appropriate type to allow the learner to perceive the distinction between /r/ and /l/ in English, how might that affect IP at the lexical level for words such as *rake* and *lake* or *right* and *light*? Research (e.g., [Lively et al., 1993](#ch2.xhtml_ref2_14){#ch2.xhtml_R_ref2_14 .biblioref role="doc-biblioref"}) suggests that talker-varied input can be useful when it comes to acquiring the /r/-/l/ distinction in L2 English, so how might input of that nature at the phonological level impact lexical IP and, by extension, IP at the sentence level regarding different morphosyntactic features? Clearly, research on potential interactions of this nature across levels and other types of interactions across different levels of IP may lead to intriguing new findings that improve our understanding of multilevel IP in general.
:::

::: {.section aria-labelledby="sec2_3_5"}
### How Do Different Levels of Intake Inform Linguistic Systems? {#ch2.xhtml_sec2_3_5}

Finally, a fifth critical issue related to multilevel IP concerns the relationship between intake and the development of different levels or types of linguistic knowledge. Intake at the lexical level informs the learner's developing lexicosemantic system in a manner that is appropriate for that level. Bits and pieces of word form, word meaning, mappings between form and meaning, and all other information that lead to fluent use of different types of lexical items (collocational properties, idiomatic use, associations, and so forth) are encoded and stored in the manner that vocabulary is maintained in declarative memory in the brain. The form--meaning mappings are of a particular nature such that a single word form, such as *tablet* is mapped onto its appropriate referential meanings (pill, computer, etc.) along with connections to appropriate collocations and associations (aspirin, iPad, etc.). Intake for the inflectional-morphological system, however, is of a different nature. It commonly involves mapping a single morpheme and its allomorphs onto multiple verbs, a type of one-to-many mapping relationship, as in the case of third-person *-s/es* in English in conjugated verbs such as *walks*, *talks*, *thinks*, *wishes*, and *reaches*. Parallel issues come into play as one reflects upon the nature of the relationship between intake at other levels of IP, such as at the phonological and pragmatic levels, and how these types of intake inform developing systems at those levels as linguistic competence develops over time.
:::
::::::::

:::::: {.section aria-labelledby="sec2_4"}
## Current Contributions and Research {#ch2.xhtml_sec2_4}

Although research conducted from the perspective of multilevel IP to date is limited, in this section, we first review two levels of IP for which a substantial amount of research has been conducted---IP for grammar or (morpho)syntax and IP for vocabulary. We then highlight studies that have implications regarding interactions between different levels of IP.

::: {.section aria-labelledby="sec2_4_1"}
### Research on IP for Grammar {#ch2.xhtml_sec2_4_1}

Most key theoretical advances and research findings regarding IP for grammar are tied to VanPatten's principles of IP and the structured input component of processing instruction (see Wong, this volume). We have already mentioned three key overarching principles that have emerged in this area:

1.  Learners tend to rely on lexical items over grammatical items to get meaning.
2.  Learners tend to process the first noun in a sentence as the subject.
3.  Learners tend to process items in sentence-initial position most and in sentence-medial position least.

[]{#ch2.xhtml_p22 .pagebreak aria-label=" page 22. " role="doc-pagebreak"}While it is beyond the goals of this chapter to review all of the research supporting each of these and other principles, it is worthwhile to point them out. They have improved our understanding of how learners attend to and parse different components of sentence-level input and have been pivotal in the development of structured input as an option available to language instructors when it comes to facilitating the acquisition of target grammatical forms while maintaining a focus on meaning.

The reader is also referred to other chapters in this volume for more detailed reviews of research on IP for grammar, such as the chapters by White and Wong (this volume) and Leeser (this volume), among others.
:::

::: {.section aria-labelledby="sec2_4_2"}
### Research on IP for Vocabulary {#ch2.xhtml_sec2_4_2}

Theoretical advances and research findings related to IP for vocabulary are reviewed and discussed by [Barcroft (2015)](#ch2.xhtml_ref2_1){.biblioref role="doc-biblioref"}, including thirty observations about lex-IP and vocabulary learning. For example, the following observations about lex-IP concern specificity in type of processing, processing resource allocation for different subcomponents of vocabulary learning, and the attention-drawing and resource-depleting potential of tasks in contexts of incidental vocabulary learning.

Specificity in type of processing

a.  Different tasks result in increases and decreases in different types of processing.
b.  Semantic and structural processing are (at least largely) dissociable.
c.  Semantic and mapping-oriented processing are (at least largely) dissociable.
d.  Structural and mapping-oriented processing are (at least largely) dissociable.

Processing resource allocation for subcomponents of vocabulary learning

a.  Processing for one subcomponent of vocabulary learning exhausts processing resources.
b.  Increased processing for one subcomponent can increase learning for that subcomponent.
c.  Increased processing for one subcomponent can decrease processing/learning for others.

Attention-drawing and resource-depleting potential of tasks in incidental contexts

a.  Tasks increase different types of processing in incidental contexts.
b.  Tasks have both attention-drawing and resource-depleting potential in these contexts.
c.  Resource depletion from semantic tasks can decrease word form processing and learning.

As is the case for IP for grammar, a full review of research tied to these observations about lexical IP is beyond the goals of this chapter. The reader is referred, however, to Kida's chapter (this volume) on the TOPRA model for more discussion of research on the above selection of observations, to a research article by [Wong and Pyun (2012)](#ch2.xhtml_ref2_28){#ch2.xhtml_R_ref2_28 .biblioref role="doc-biblioref"} for a telling study on the negative effects of increased semantic processing (via sentence writing) on novel word form learning, and to [Barcroft's (2015)](#ch2.xhtml_ref2_6){.biblioref role="doc-biblioref"} book for a full review or research and theory related to these and other observations related to lex-IP and vocabulary learning.
:::

::: {.section aria-labelledby="sec2_4_3"}
### Research on Interaction Between Different Levels of IP {#ch2.xhtml_sec2_4_3}

We also find in the research literature some studies that, by their nature, have touched upon issues related to interactions between different levels of IP. These include (a) a study by [VanPatten (1990)](#ch2.xhtml_ref2_21){.biblioref role="doc-biblioref"} on the effects of attending to grammatical forms in discourse-level input, (b) studies by []{#ch2.xhtml_p23 .pagebreak aria-label=" page 23. " role="doc-pagebreak"}[Barcroft (2009)](#ch2.xhtml_ref2_4){.biblioref role="doc-biblioref"} and [Kida (2020)](#ch2.xhtml_ref2_12){#ch2.xhtml_R_ref2_12 .biblioref role="doc-biblioref"} on how tasks involving different types of processing affect incidental new word learning during reading, and (c) a study by [Barcroft (2019)](#ch2.xhtml_ref2_7){.biblioref role="doc-biblioref"} on how the amount of informational content in a series of sentences affects new word learning.

To begin, [VanPatten's (1990)](#ch2.xhtml_ref2_21){.biblioref role="doc-biblioref"} study concerned the relationship between lexis, morphosyntax, and discourse-level processing among L2 learners of Spanish. By assessing the effects of having learners attend to a grammatical functor, a verb form, or a content word, the study explored how attention to single items within a discourse-level passage would affect overall comprehension at the discourse level. The study's findings indicated that attention to grammatical form can decrease comprehension of discourse-level input due to the difficulty that can sometimes arise between attention to both form and meaning. Even if this multilevel aspect may not have been of primary interest, the study does demonstrate interaction across levels.

In another study, [Barcroft (2009)](#ch2.xhtml_ref2_4){.biblioref role="doc-biblioref"} assessed how requiring some learners to perform a semantically oriented synonym-generation task might affect word learning during reading in both intentional and incidental learning conditions. As such, the study assessed lexical IP and vocabulary learning in the context of discourse-level written input. Results indicated more vocabulary learning for learners who were not required to perform the semantically elaborative synonym-generation task. Interestingly, this pattern held for both intentional and incidental learning conditions, suggesting that the *resource-depletion potential* of the semantically oriented task outweighed its *attention-drawing potential* (see, e.g., [Barcroft, 2015](#ch2.xhtml_ref2_6){.biblioref role="doc-biblioref"}). [Kida (2020)](#ch2.xhtml_ref2_12){.biblioref role="doc-biblioref"} also assessed how incidental L2 vocabulary learning during reading might be affected by requiring learners to perform a semantically oriented task---in this case, pleasantness ratings---as compared to when learners were required to perform a structurally oriented (form-focused task)---on this occasion, having learners rewrite L2 English words in Japanese script---and when they were not asked to perform any task. The results again indicated decreased novel word learning for the semantic task when compared to no task and when compared to the structural task, and, interestingly, learners who completed the structural task learned significantly more words than learners in the no-task condition. The findings of both these studies, which concern the relationship between (a) discourse-level input and lex-IP and (b) vocabulary learning suggest that predictions of the TOPRA model regarding the resource-depletion potential of increased semantic processing are applicable to incidental word learning during reading.

Finally, in another study, [Barcroft (2019)](#ch2.xhtml_ref2_7){.biblioref role="doc-biblioref"} also assessed the effects of increased semantic processing on L2 word learning but did so, within the framework of multilevel IP, by manipulating the amount of informational content in individual sentences. In neither of two experiments in the study did increased informational content increase word learning, and in one of the two experiments, increased informational content decreased new word learning. These findings favor predictions of the TOPRA model over unqualified positions that "deeper processing" associated with increased semantic processing should somehow increase new word form learning.
:::
::::::

::: {.section aria-labelledby="sec2_5"}
## Main Research Methods {#ch2.xhtml_sec2_5}

Applying a multilevel perspective may increase the range of different types of research methods we use to investigate IP. To begin, the same research methods already used in studies on sentence-level IP for the acquisition of grammar and studies on lexical IP for the acquisition of vocabulary would fall under the umbrella of methods used to study multilevel IP. These include pre/posttest designs in between-participants sentence-level IP studies, Latin-square designs to counterbalance target word groups and learning conditions in within-participants word-level lexical IP studies, and the use of online psycholinguistic measures such as reaction time and priming in studies on both sentence-level IP and lex-IP. The reader is referred to the three chapters of the []{#ch2.xhtml_p24 .pagebreak aria-label=" page 24. " role="doc-pagebreak"}section on research methodology (Ruiz & Patrick Rebuschat, this volume; Marijuan, this volume; Jegerski, this volume) for more on options for different types of behavioral studies; studies with online measures such as priming, eye-tracking, and electroencephalogram (EEG); and studies that combine both behavioral and online measures.

The nature of research methods used to investigate different levels of IP may also change, even when the primary questions of interest parallel one another. Consider studies on privileging of location of items in sentences and within words in different types of input. [Barcroft and VanPatten (1997)](#ch2.xhtml_ref2_10){#ch2.xhtml_R_ref2_10 .biblioref role="doc-biblioref"} examined this issue as it pertains to grammatical structures in sentence-level input by asking participants with no prior study of Spanish to try to repeat a series of sentences out loud to the best of their ability. The results indicated more repetition of items located in sentence-initial and sentence-final locations than in sentence-medial positions. [Barcroft and Rott (2010)](#ch2.xhtml_ref2_9){#ch2.xhtml_R_ref2_9 .biblioref role="doc-biblioref"}, on the other hand, investigated the role of location in word learning by asking learners to produce both complete and partial word forms for words they had recently learned. Their results indicated that word-initial and word-final segments were produced more often than word-medial segments and that word-initial privileging was greater in shorter words as compared to more word-final privileging in longer words. While the findings of both studies provide useful information about different aspects of IP, the use of the repetition procedure was more appropriate for the sentence-level study whereas the use of the partial word production procedure after a study phase was more appropriate for the word-level study.

Research methods that may be of particular interest in research on multilevel IP include those that help to discern between different levels of IP when assessing the effects of different tasks (task-based effects) and different ways of presenting input (input-based effects). Neuroimaging studies also may be of particular benefit when it comes to (a) discerning between different levels of IP and (b) the effects of a given variable within one level of IP, at least to the extent that these involve observable differences in localization of function. Take the set of research findings indicating that increases in the amount of talker, speaking-style, and speaking-rate variability used when presenting new L2 words in the input results in better vocabulary learning (more accurate recall and faster production) (see, e.g., [Sommers & Barcroft, 2007](#ch2.xhtml_ref2_18){#ch2.xhtml_R_ref2_18 .biblioref role="doc-biblioref"}). It would be particularly informative to assess the extent to which these different types of acoustically varied input lead to different patterns in localization in the brain.
:::

::: {.section aria-labelledby="sec2_6"}
## Recommendations for Practice {#ch2.xhtml_sec2_6}

Numerous recommendations for language instruction have been made based upon theory and research on (a) sentence-level IP and the acquisition of grammar and (b) lexical IP and the acquisition of vocabulary. The former has yielded important pedagogical innovations, such as the use of structured input to facilitate the acquisition of target grammatical forms during instruction focused on meaning. The latter provided grounding for the development of the input-based incremental (IBI) approach to vocabulary ([Barcroft, 2012](#ch2.xhtml_ref2_5){#ch2.xhtml_R_ref2_5 .biblioref role="doc-biblioref"}; see also Wolter, this volume), which was designed to maximize vocabulary learning in programs of meaning-oriented instruction, including communicative language teaching, task-based language teaching, and content-based instruction. Are there any recommendations that stem directly or solely from the perspective of multilevel IP? Perhaps not solely, at least to date, but in what follows, we offer seven general recommendations for practice that are consistent with theory and research on IP as it relates to both grammar and vocabulary. These are also consistent with what we know about multilevel IP to date. Each recommendation is followed by a brief explanation, commentary, or both.

1.  []{#ch2.xhtml_p25 .pagebreak aria-label=" page 25. " role="doc-pagebreak"}Remember the primacy of input.

    While many different factors can affect how well or how quickly one might acquire a language, input is in a class of its own because it is a *necessary ingredient* for language acquisition, as Krashen (e.g., [1985](#ch2.xhtml_ref2_13){#ch2.xhtml_R_ref2_13 .biblioref role="doc-biblioref"}) pointed out decades ago. If a learner wants to complete a task and does not already have the vocabulary or a grammatical structure needed to do it, where is the learner going to get it? From the input. There is no avoiding it.

2.  Keep meaning in focus.

    One of the earliest findings about sentence-level input is that learners seek to comprehend the input to which they are exposed; they seek meaning. From a language acquisition perspective, meaning is essential because without meaning from sufficiently comprehensible input, learners have nothing to which to map the novel linguistic forms they encounter. The output that learners produce also should convey meaning. Research indicates, for example, that opportunities to retrieve vocabulary on one's own increases vocabulary learning, but output without access to meaning such as choral repetition of words does not ([Wong & Barcroft, 2020](#ch2.xhtml_ref2_27){#ch2.xhtml_R_ref2_27 .biblioref role="doc-biblioref"}).

3.  Respect limits on processing capacity.

    Learners are limited when it comes to their processing capacity, and IP and language acquisition can be taxing when it comes to the need to make processing resources available. The perspective of multilevel IP only further emphasizes this point by reminding us of how complex and multicomponential IP and language acquisition are; not just one level but all levels of IP exact processing resources from the learner over time.

4.  Respect differences in types of processing.

    As the TOPRA model illustrates, one should not expect increases in one type of processing to lead to increases in learning of a different nature. Tasks that increase semantic processing for target vocabulary may improve semantically oriented learning when there is something novel to be learned on that front, but we should not expect them to increase word form learning.

5.  Make best use of frequency.

    While the input provided to language learners should be meaningful and sufficiently comprehensible, it can also include repetition of target vocabulary or target grammatical structures in ways that increases the likelihood that they will be acquired. For grammatical structures, the structured input component of processing instruction includes multiple repetitions of target items, as does input-based incremental (IBI) vocabulary instruction when it comes to target vocabulary. Repetition can be increased while still focusing on meaning.

6.  Avoid getting in the learner's way.

    One overarching research finding from studies on lexical IP over the past two decades or so concerns how tasks such as sentence writing or word copying or addressing questions about word meaning that one might imagine to be "good vocabulary learning" techniques do not actually increase, and oftentimes can decrease, vocabulary learning. Language instructors can provide more effective instruction if they are aware of what research indicates regarding the effects of tasks of this nature and other types of tasks.

7.  Consider structuring and enhancing your input.

    Studies on IP indicate that the ways in which input is presented to a learner can impact upon the acquisition of grammatical structures and vocabulary. At the sentence level, structured input components can encourage and require learners to rely upon the meaning conveyed by target grammatical items (by removing lexical items that convey the same meaning, for example). Input enhancement, such as textual enhancement and input flood, may also be beneficial when targeting grammatical structures (see [Wong, 2005](#ch2.xhtml_ref2_26){#ch2.xhtml_R_ref2_26 .biblioref role="doc-biblioref"}). At the word level, increasing frequency []{#ch2.xhtml_p26 .pagebreak aria-label=" page 26. " role="doc-pagebreak"}and utilizing enhancement techniques such as increasing talker, speaking-style, and speaking-rate variability also have been found to improve vocabulary learning. Therefore, structuring and enhancing input are useful techniques that are regularly available to language instructors.
:::

::: {.section aria-labelledby="sec2_7"}
## Future Directions {#ch2.xhtml_sec2_7}

Future studies conducted within the framework of multilevel IP can address, as a point of departure, each of the questions discusses in the Critical Issues section to a further degree overall and in new and innovative ways. Recall the five questions were the following:

1.  How are processing resources allocated across different levels of IP?
2.  How are processing resources allocated within different levels of IP?
3.  How does the meaning of *intake* change across different levels of IP?
4.  How do different levels of IP interact with one another?
5.  How do different levels of intake inform linguistic systems?

For Question 1, new studies can investigate the nature of the resource allocator and how different variables, such as knowledge versus lack of knowledge of key lexical items, may affect it. For Question 2, new studies can continue to investigate linguistic subsystems such as phonology and pragmatics that to date have been underinvestigated from an IP perspective. For Question 3, new studies can expand our understanding of intake at different levels of IP. Online measures such as EEG and neuroimaging may be helpful in this regard. For Question 4, new studies can manipulate variables that pertain to more than one level of IP. The study described above on different amounts of informational content in sentences may serve as an example upon which to expand. Finally, for Question 5, new studies can specify further the nature of different developing linguistic systems as they relate to both IP and the type of intake needed for each developing system to develop and how each system accommodates or incorporates intake in ways that allow it to change over time.

As new research is conducted in these five key areas, our understanding of multilevel IP should increase, making way for new models and theoretical proposals that go beyond any one single level of IP. Given the complex nature of multilevel IP and its relationship to different aspects of language acquisition, perspectives and tools associated with dynamic systems theory may prove useful as research progresses. Connectionist simulations and computer modeling can also be incorporated in the effort to depict and understand how different levels of IP interact and how limited processing resources are allocated within and across levels over time.
:::

::: {.section .appendix role="doc-appendix"}
## Appendix {#ch2.xhtml_app2_1}

Thirty Observations about Lexical Input Processing (adapted from [Barcroft, 2015](#ch2.xhtml_ref2_1){.biblioref role="doc-biblioref"})

1.  *Lexical IP (lex-IP) within multilevel input processing*

    a.  Lex-IP is one of many levels of input processing.
    b.  Processing resource availability for lex-IP is tied to other levels of IP (and vice versa).
    c.  Lex-IP takes place with isolated lexical items or sentence-and discourse-level input.

2.  *Basics about vocabulary learning and lex-IP*
    a.  Vocabulary learning concern encoding, storage, and retrieval within mental networks.
    b.  Lex-IP and vocabulary learning involve subcomponents: form, meaning, and mapping.
    c.  Lex-IP and vocabulary learning occur in both intentional and incidental contexts.

3.  []{#ch2.xhtml_p27 .pagebreak aria-label=" page 27. " role="doc-pagebreak"}*Specificity in type of processing*

    a.  Different tasks result in increases and decreases in different types of processing.
    b.  Semantic and structural processing are (at least largely) dissociable.
    c.  Semantic and mapping-oriented processing are (at least largely) dissociable.
    d.  Structural and mapping-oriented processing are (at least largely) dissociable.

4.  *Processing resource allocation for subcomponents of vocabulary learning*

    a.  Processing for one subcomponent of vocabulary learning exhausts processing resources.
    b.  Increased processing for one subcomponent can increase learning for that subcomponent.
    c.  Increased processing for one subcomponent can decrease processing/learning for others.

5.  *Attention-drawing and resource-depleting potential of tasks in incidental contexts*

    a.  Tasks increase different types of processing in incidental contexts.
    b.  Tasks have both attention-drawing and resource-depleting potential in these contexts.
    c.  Resource depletion from semantic task can decrease word form processing and learning.

6.  *Output with versus without access to meaning*

    a.  Output with and without access to meaning affect lex-IP differently.
    b.  Output without access to meaning can detract from lex-IP and vocabulary learning.
    c.  Output with access to meaning (retrieval) increases vocabulary learning.

7.  *Partial word form learning*

    a.  Word forms are processed and learned incrementally in segments.
    b.  Word-initial and word-final segments are privileged over word-medial segments.
    c.  Word-initial privileging is higher in shorter words; word-final in longer words.

8.  *Increased and spaced exposure*

    a.  Increased exposure to vocabulary increases lex-IP and vocabulary learning.
    b.  Spaced (over massed) exposure^5^ to vocabulary increases lex-IP and vocabulary learning.

9.  *Semantic processing, lex-IP, and vocabulary learning*

    a.  Semantic properties of words are processed and learned incrementally.
    b.  Semantic properties learned for L1 words transfers to novel L2 words.
    c.  Excessive overlap in the semantic properties can cause interference during lex-IP.

10. *Input enhancement (IE), lex-IP, and vocabulary learning*

    a.  Sufficiently distinctive IE can increase focus on and processing/learning of vocabulary.
    b.  Some types of IE increase focus and time allocated to lex-IP.
    c.  Some types of IE (e.g., acoustic variability) affect the quality of lexical representations.
:::

::: {.section .reference aria-labelledby="r2_1"}

- [[Barcroft, J., & Rott, S.](#ch2.xhtml_R_ref2_9){.backlink aria-label=" This link returns to its first in-text citation. " role="doc-backlink"} (2010).
