[]{#ch3.xhtml}

::: {}
# []{#ch3.xhtml_p29 .pagebreak aria-label=" page 29. " role="doc-pagebreak"}[3 ]{.chnum .ordinal}[Input Processing in Spoken Versus Written Language]{.chtitle} {#ch3.xhtml_h1}

Ronald P. Leow

Georgetown University

Fei Li

China University of Geosciences (Wuhan)

DOI: [10.4324/9781003041412-4](https://dx.doi.org/10.4324/9781003041412-4){aria-label="D.O.I. link to this document."}
:::

::: {.section .introduction role="doc-introduction"}
## Introduction and Definitions {#ch3.xhtml_sec3_1}

Among a series of stages that comprise the second language (L2) learning process, how input is processed during the initial stage in particular has been of keen theoretical interest in the field of (instructed) second language acquisition (I)SLA (e.g., [Gass, 1997](#ch3.xhtml_ref3_5){#ch3.xhtml_R_ref3_5 .biblioref role="doc-biblioref"}; [Leow, 2015](#ch3.xhtml_ref3_18){#ch3.xhtml_R_ref3_18 .biblioref role="doc-biblioref"}; [McLaughlin, 1987](#ch3.xhtml_ref3_26){#ch3.xhtml_R_ref3_26 .biblioref role="doc-biblioref"}; [Robinson, 1995](#ch3.xhtml_ref3_32){#ch3.xhtml_R_ref3_32 .biblioref role="doc-biblioref"}; [Schmidt, 1990](#ch3.xhtml_ref3_33){#ch3.xhtml_R_ref3_33 .biblioref role="doc-biblioref"}; [VanPatten, 2007](#ch3.xhtml_ref3_35){#ch3.xhtml_R_ref3_35 .biblioref role="doc-biblioref"}). Input may be processed from both spoken and written language, which in itself may impact how such L2 data are processed by L2 learners, especially during the early stages, namely, at the input-to-intake[^2] stage (see, e.g., [Broadbent et al., 1978](#ch3.xhtml_ref3_3){#ch3.xhtml_R_ref3_3 .biblioref role="doc-biblioref"}; [Penney, 1989](#ch3.xhtml_ref3_30){#ch3.xhtml_R_ref3_30 .biblioref role="doc-biblioref"} in cognitive psychology). It is also well established that there are differences between the modality of language (aural vs. written) to which L2 learners are exposed and these include, for example, different biological functions, separate subsystems of the brain, which vary in properties and capacities of processing the information, the nature of the input (visual shapes vs. sound), processing time (longer vs. shorter), exposure to previous input (availability of backtracking vs. no backtracking), and amount of exposure (untimed vs. timed). Indeed, research in several fields has revealed that the mode of presentation may impact learners' recall (e.g., [Lund, 1991](#ch3.xhtml_ref3_24){#ch3.xhtml_R_ref3_24 .biblioref role="doc-biblioref"}; [Park, 2004](#ch3.xhtml_ref3_29){#ch3.xhtml_R_ref3_29 .biblioref role="doc-biblioref"}; [Penney, 1989](#ch3.xhtml_ref3_30){.biblioref role="doc-biblioref"}; [Penney & Burr, 1986](#ch3.xhtml_ref3_31){#ch3.xhtml_R_ref3_31 .biblioref role="doc-biblioref"}). For example, main ideas after listening to a narrative are better recalled by L2 learners when compared to after reading it. On the other hand, more details are remembered after reading than listening ([Lund, 1991](#ch3.xhtml_ref3_24){.biblioref role="doc-biblioref"}; [Park, 2004](#ch3.xhtml_ref3_29){.biblioref role="doc-biblioref"}).

Studies in (I)SLA associated with the role of modality (written vs. spoken language) in input processing (IP) have been conducted primarily from two perspectives: (1) theoretically within the metaphor of adult learners as limited capacity processors of information (e.g., [McLaughlin, 1987](#ch3.xhtml_ref3_26){.biblioref role="doc-biblioref"}), dependent upon the amount of attentional resources or processing capacity (e.g., [Kahneman, 1973](#ch3.xhtml_ref3_11){#ch3.xhtml_R_ref3_11 .biblioref role="doc-biblioref"}; [Wickens, 1980](#ch3.xhtml_ref3_36){#ch3.xhtml_R_ref3_36 .biblioref role="doc-biblioref"}) available to be deployed to attend to and process incoming L2 data, and (2) pedagogically with respect to the role of instruction and exposure in subsequent L2 development. In these two perspectives, studies have been conducted largely from two different methodological approaches, namely, (1) in separate studies or research designs (see, e.g., [Greenslade et al., 1999](#ch3.xhtml_ref3_7){#ch3.xhtml_R_ref3_7 .biblioref role="doc-biblioref"}; [Leow, 1995](#ch3.xhtml_ref3_16){#ch3.xhtml_R_ref3_16 .biblioref role="doc-biblioref"}) versus studies that have included both modalities within one research design (e.g., [Cintrón-Valentín & Ellis, 2016](#ch3.xhtml_ref3_4){#ch3.xhtml_R_ref3_4 .biblioref role="doc-biblioref"}; [Ito & Wong, 2019](#ch3.xhtml_ref3_10){#ch3.xhtml_R_ref3_10 .biblioref role="doc-biblioref"} and [Wong & Ito, 2018](#ch3.xhtml_ref3_38){#ch3.xhtml_R_ref3_38 .biblioref role="doc-biblioref"} combined; [Kim & Godfroid, 2019](#ch3.xhtml_ref3_12){#ch3.xhtml_R_ref3_12 .biblioref role="doc-biblioref"}; [Morgan-Short et al., 2018](#ch3.xhtml_ref3_28){#ch3.xhtml_R_ref3_28 .biblioref role="doc-biblioref"}; []{#ch3.xhtml_p30 .pagebreak aria-label=" page 30. " role="doc-pagebreak"}[Wong, 2001](#ch3.xhtml_ref3_37){#ch3.xhtml_R_ref3_37 .biblioref role="doc-biblioref"}) and (2) a product-oriented (e.g., [Wong, 2001](#ch3.xhtml_ref3_37){.biblioref role="doc-biblioref"}) versus a process-oriented perspective (e.g., [Wong & Ito, 2018](#ch3.xhtml_ref3_38){.biblioref role="doc-biblioref"}) that gathers concurrent data during exposure to the L2 data.

To better understand the role of modality during IP and in subsequent L2 development, this chapter provides (1) a theoretical perspective of the roles of input and IP in language learning followed by a review of early modality and IP studies, (2) the identification of critical issues derived from these studies, (3) a critical report of current pertinent research of IP in the aural and written modalities including process-oriented studies employing concurrent data elicitation procedures, (4) pedagogical implications, and (5) future directions.
:::

:::::: {.section aria-labelledby="sec3_2"}
## Historical Perspectives {#ch3.xhtml_sec3_2}

::: {.section aria-labelledby="sec3_2_1"}
### L2 Input and Input Processing {#ch3.xhtml_sec3_2_1}

There is no debate that input, usually defined as L2 data (whether spoken or written) to which learners are exposed, is critical to SLA. What has, however, drawn much theoretical, empirical, and pedagogical interest is *how* such input is processed. IP refers to the allocation of attentional resources and cognitive processes to extract linguistic data from input. This type of processing is postulated to represent the initial or input-to-intake stage of the learning process (e.g., [Leow, 2015](#ch3.xhtml_ref3_18){.biblioref role="doc-biblioref"}; [Gass, 1997](#ch3.xhtml_ref3_5){.biblioref role="doc-biblioref"}; [VanPatten, 2007](#ch3.xhtml_ref3_35){.biblioref role="doc-biblioref"}).

Early studies that have addressed the role of modality in IP, namely, in the strands of research on input simplification ([Leow, 1995](#ch3.xhtml_ref3_16){.biblioref role="doc-biblioref"}) and the role of simultaneous attention to form and meaning (e.g., [Greenslade et al., 1999](#ch3.xhtml_ref3_7){.biblioref role="doc-biblioref"}; [Wong, 2001](#ch3.xhtml_ref3_37){.biblioref role="doc-biblioref"}), were theoretical in nature and situated within capacity theories, which viewed L2 learners as limited capacity processors of information (e.g., [Kahneman, 1973](#ch3.xhtml_ref3_11){.biblioref role="doc-biblioref"}; [Wickens, 1980](#ch3.xhtml_ref3_36){.biblioref role="doc-biblioref"}). Capacity theories postulate that (1) there is competition for attentional resources to be paid to incoming information, (2) what is paid attention to may depend on the amount of mental effort required to process incoming information, and (3) the allocation of attentional resources to incoming information may come from a pool of cognitive resources.
:::

::: {.section aria-labelledby="sec3_2_2"}
### Modality, Simplification, and Input Processing {#ch3.xhtml_sec3_2_2}

Leow ([1993](#ch3.xhtml_ref3_15){#ch3.xhtml_R_ref3_15 .biblioref role="doc-biblioref"}, [1995](#ch3.xhtml_ref3_16){.biblioref role="doc-biblioref"}) addressed whether mode of presentation (written vs. aural) played a role in how L2 learners process simplified L2 input for intake (operationalized as immediate recognition of target items in L2 input) in addition to the roles that type of linguistic item (Spanish present perfect vs. present subjunctive), and language experience (first year vs. fourth year) might play. Leow hypothesized that textual simplification of input resulting in a more comprehensible text should reduce processing demands on adult L2 learners thereby facilitating their intake of target linguistic items. In both the written ([Leow, 1993](#ch3.xhtml_ref3_15){.biblioref role="doc-biblioref"}) and aural ([Leow, 1995](#ch3.xhtml_ref3_16){.biblioref role="doc-biblioref"}) modes, results were the same: Simplification in either mode did not appear to facilitate significant intake of targeted forms in input, that is, increased comprehensibility of L2 input did not appear to reduce learners' processing demands or reallocate attentional resources that might have permitted them to take in more linguistic information while exposed to aural or written input. [Leow (1995)](#ch3.xhtml_ref3_16){.biblioref role="doc-biblioref"} suggested that "external manipulation of input does not necessarily affect what in the input is taken in by the learner" (p. 84). At the same time, with respect to type of linguistic item, Leow reported superior recognition of the present perfect form when exposed to aural input but not with written input. One plausible explanation provided was the saliency or noticeability of the present perfect form when compared to the present subjunctive form (see similar arguments from [Issa & Morgan-Short, 2019](#ch3.xhtml_ref3_9){#ch3.xhtml_R_ref3_9 .biblioref role="doc-biblioref"}, and [Cintrón-Valentín & Ellis, 2016](#ch3.xhtml_ref3_4){.biblioref role="doc-biblioref"}). Additionally, Leow []{#ch3.xhtml_p31 .pagebreak aria-label=" page 31. " role="doc-pagebreak"}found an effect for proficiency level: In both modalities, fourth semester participants' intake was superior to that of first semester participants.
:::

::: {.section aria-labelledby="sec3_2_3"}
### Modality, Simultaneous Attention to Form and Meaning, and IP {#ch3.xhtml_sec3_2_3}

[VanPatten (1990)](#ch3.xhtml_ref3_34){#ch3.xhtml_R_ref3_34 .biblioref role="doc-biblioref"} examined whether L2 learners could simultaneously pay attention to form and meaning in the aural mode. [Greenslade et al. (1999)](#ch3.xhtml_ref3_7){.biblioref role="doc-biblioref"} sought to address the role of modality with their conceptual replication of [VanPatten (1990)](#ch3.xhtml_ref3_34){.biblioref role="doc-biblioref"} in the written mode. They hypothesized that attending to both form and meaning simultaneously would result in a cognitive overload that would have negative effects on processing for comprehension. Like [VanPatten's (1990)](#ch3.xhtml_ref3_34){.biblioref role="doc-biblioref"} study, participants were exposed to L2 Spanish written input in one of four conditions: condition 1 required participants to read for meaning only, conditions 2, 3, and 4 required them to pay attention to the word "*inflación*" (inflation), the definite article "*la*", or the third-person plural morpheme -*n*, respectively. Attention was operationalized by asking participants to mark on a sheet of paper every instance targeted linguistic forms were attended to in the input. VanPatten reported an overall decrease of comprehension when participants attended to *la* and -*n* (less communicative value) when compared to attention paid to *inflación* (more communicative value). Additionally, VanPatten also found superior performance at the advanced level when compared to the less advanced level prompting him to postulate that "only when input is easily understood can learners attend to form as part of the intake process" ([1990](#ch3.xhtml_ref3_34){.biblioref role="doc-biblioref"}, p. 296). In other words, there may be a tendency at early stages of language learning to process only for meaning due to limited attentional resources.

Greenslade et al.'s written mode results almost paralleled those in [VanPatten's (1990)](#ch3.xhtml_ref3_34){.biblioref role="doc-biblioref"} study in the aural mode with one major difference: No significant difference in comprehension was found between the lexical item *inflación* and verbal morpheme -*n* groups. These two linguistic items are assumed to represent opposite ends of a form continuum with respect to their level of communicative value or even saliency (see below).

[Wong (2001)](#ch3.xhtml_ref3_37){.biblioref role="doc-biblioref"} conducted a conceptual replication of [VanPatten (1990)](#ch3.xhtml_ref3_34){.biblioref role="doc-biblioref"} and [Greenslade et al. (1999)](#ch3.xhtml_ref3_7){.biblioref role="doc-biblioref"} to address, with more robust methodology, the role of modality in this theoretical strand of research. Wong's research design was the first to directly compare both spoken and written input within the same participant pool, albeit with a between-subjects design, and sought to explore whether similar results would hold across different modes. In the aural mode, [Wong (2001)](#ch3.xhtml_ref3_37){.biblioref role="doc-biblioref"} reported relatively similar findings to [VanPatten (1990)](#ch3.xhtml_ref3_34){.biblioref role="doc-biblioref"} but differing from VanPatten, no significant difference in comprehension was found between *inflation* and the definite article *the* groups. In the written mode, Wong reported, like [Greenslade et al. (1999)](#ch3.xhtml_ref3_7){.biblioref role="doc-biblioref"}, no significant difference in comprehension between the "read for content only" group and the *inflation* group but did find differences in two other comparisons, namely, between the control and the definite article *the* groups and between the *inflation* and the definite article *the* groups). Wong concluded that these findings suggest that "learners' limited attentional capacity is not constrained in the same way during input processing in the aural and written modes" (p. 358). The next section identifies critical issues derived from these early studies addressing the role of modality in IP.
:::
::::::

::::::: {.section aria-labelledby="sec3_3"}
## Critical Issues {#ch3.xhtml_sec3_3}

Primarily due to the paucity of studies, there are several critical issues or questions that remain to be addressed with respect to the role of modality in IP. First, when viewed from a theoretical perspective framed within the conceptualization of adult L2 learners as limited capacity processors of information, are L2 learners' attentional resources/processing capacity constrained []{#ch3.xhtml_p32 .pagebreak aria-label=" page 32. " role="doc-pagebreak"}differentially during exposure to aural or written input? Second, what are the relationships between modality, input processing, and subsequent L2 development? Third, what factors affect IP during exposure to type of mode (aural or written)? Finally, what are appropriate methodological approaches to conducting research on the role of modality and IP?

::: {.section aria-labelledby="sec3_3_1"}
### Are L2 Learners' Attentional Resources/Processing Capacity Constrained Differentially During Exposure to Aural or Written Input? {#ch3.xhtml_sec3_3_1}

Three studies conducted within this strand of research revealed inconsistent findings. [Leow (1993)](#ch3.xhtml_ref3_15){.biblioref role="doc-biblioref"} suggested that learners' intake may depend, to some degree, on cognitive constraints and different cognitive strategies employed in different modes. In other words, readers are generally less cognitively constrained by their exposure to L2 data than are listeners. The findings of both [Leow's (1993)](#ch3.xhtml_ref3_15){.biblioref role="doc-biblioref"} study and the 1995 replication study in the aural mode, premised on improved comprehension leading to attentional resources reallocated to paying attention to form and potentially more intake, did not appear to support this assumption (i.e., the assumption that learners are less cognitively constrained in the written mode) based on differential performances due to modality. Findings of two other studies ([Greenslade et al., 1999](#ch3.xhtml_ref3_7){.biblioref role="doc-biblioref"}; [Wong, 2001](#ch3.xhtml_ref3_37){.biblioref role="doc-biblioref"}), both conceptual replications of [VanPatten (1990)](#ch3.xhtml_ref3_34){.biblioref role="doc-biblioref"}, reported findings that appear to support the detrimental effect of simultaneously processing for form and meaning in both modalities on learners' comprehension, that is, learners' processing capacity may be constrained when exposed to aural or written input. However, these studies did not use concurrent data elicitation procedures such as think-aloud protocols or eye-tracking to gather data on L2 learners' attentional resources or processing capacity. To this end, whether such cognitive processes are differentially impacted by type of mode clearly warrants further investigation.
:::

::: {.section aria-labelledby="sec3_3_2"}
### What Are the Relationships Between Modality, IP, and Subsequent L2 Development? {#ch3.xhtml_sec3_3_2}

As discussed above, the issue of L2 learners' attentional resources or processing capacity being constrained differentially during exposure to type of mode remains to be empirically supported. However, arguably a more overarching issue is the relationships between modality, IP, and L2 development, especially in ISLA research. Previous studies on modality and IP have only addressed the initial stage of the L2 learning process, namely, intake ([Leow, 1995](#ch3.xhtml_ref3_16){.biblioref role="doc-biblioref"}) and comprehension ([Greenslade et al., 1999](#ch3.xhtml_ref3_7){.biblioref role="doc-biblioref"}; [Wong, 2001](#ch3.xhtml_ref3_37){.biblioref role="doc-biblioref"}). Whether modality also plays an important role in IP that leads to robust L2 development, a later stage of the L2 learning process, is clearly an area of investigation to be pursued.
:::

::: {.section aria-labelledby="sec3_3_3"}
### What Factors Affect IP During Exposure to Type of Mode (Aural or Written)? {#ch3.xhtml_sec3_3_3}

Two factors are provided in these early studies that may or may not potentially affect IP during exposure to spoken or written language, namely, simplification and saliency. Leow's ([1993](#ch3.xhtml_ref3_15){.biblioref role="doc-biblioref"}, [1995](#ch3.xhtml_ref3_16){.biblioref role="doc-biblioref"}) studies on simplification (undertaken to enhance overall comprehension) do not appear to have a facilitating effect on learners' intake of linguistic items contained in the input, irrespective of mode. In other words, it appears that simplification does not significantly reduce learners' processing demands to an extent that may permit them to take in more linguistic information while interacting with aural or written input.

With regard to the effect of saliency on IP during exposure to type of mode, the findings are inconclusive. [Leow (1995)](#ch3.xhtml_ref3_16){.biblioref role="doc-biblioref"} reported that saliency of target item (present perfect vs. present []{#ch3.xhtml_p33 .pagebreak aria-label=" page 33. " role="doc-pagebreak"}subjunctive form) appeared to have played a role in IP for intake only in the aural mode. [Greenslade et al. (1999)](#ch3.xhtml_ref3_7){.biblioref role="doc-biblioref"}, [Wong (2001)](#ch3.xhtml_ref3_37){.biblioref role="doc-biblioref"}, and the original study ([VanPatten, 1990](#ch3.xhtml_ref3_34){.biblioref role="doc-biblioref"}) all report conflicting findings. If the item *inflación* or *inflation* is viewed as more salient when compared to the verbal morpheme *-n*, and the definite articles *-la* and *the*, then conflicting findings were reported for the written mode (Greenslade et al.'s *inflación \> la* vs. Wong's *inflation = the*), for the aural mode (Wong's *inflation* = *the* vs. VanPatten's original study *inflación \> la*), and for the written versus aural modes (Greenslade et al.'s *inflación = -n* vs. VanPatten's *inflación \> -n*). Like the role of simplification, whether saliency plays a role in IP during exposure to type of mode remains to be empirically supported.
:::

::: {.section aria-labelledby="sec3_3_4"}
### What Are Appropriate Methodological Approaches to Conducting Research on the Role of Modality and IP? {#ch3.xhtml_sec3_3_4}

Arguably the most critical issue regarding the role of modality in IP lies with the operationalization of IP. All three studies reported above approached its operationalization and measurement from a product-oriented perspective, that is, they used participants' performances on immediate posttests to make assumptions on whether they actually processed the target items in the L2 input or reallocated their attentional resources during exposure. In Leow ([1993](#ch3.xhtml_ref3_15){.biblioref role="doc-biblioref"}, [1995](#ch3.xhtml_ref3_16){.biblioref role="doc-biblioref"}), IP was operationalized and measured offline by learners' performances on a post-exposure multiple-choice recognition task. However, whether learners re-allocated their attention to form when processing demands to process for meaning were assumed to have been significantly reduced remains unknown given the lack of concurrent data. A similar critique can be made of [Greenslade et al.'s (1999)](#ch3.xhtml_ref3_7){.biblioref role="doc-biblioref"} and [Wong's (2001)](#ch3.xhtml_ref3_37){.biblioref role="doc-biblioref"} studies. IP was operationalized by participants making a minimum of six check marks on a paper while exposed to the L2 data. This operationalization may be arguably associated more with attention than processing as in whether attentional resources were indeed divided in processing for grammatical information or content or both. Whether modality played a role in IP, then, remains unclear given that these studies did not provide concurrent data on such processing, that is, data on whether modality did indeed impact *how* L2 learners processed L2 data.

It is of interest to note the findings of two process-oriented studies ([Leow et al., 2008](#ch3.xhtml_ref3_19){#ch3.xhtml_R_ref3_19 .biblioref role="doc-biblioref"}; [Morgan-Short et al., 2012](#ch3.xhtml_ref3_27){#ch3.xhtml_R_ref3_27 .biblioref role="doc-biblioref"}) conducted within this strand of research (written mode only) that sought to address specifically *how* L2 learners were processing incoming L2 input. Both studies employed think-aloud protocols to first establish the baseline of the research design, that is, whether participants did indeed pay attention to both form and meaning as instructed while gathering online data on how L2 learners were processing L2 input. Leow et al. (2008) made several modifications in [VanPatten's (1990)](#ch3.xhtml_ref3_34){.biblioref role="doc-biblioref"} original research design to address methodological issues with previous studies. Similar to the results for the written passage in [Wong (2001)](#ch3.xhtml_ref3_37){.biblioref role="doc-biblioref"}, Leow et al. found no differences in the level of comprehension between the experimental and control conditions. Based on the evidence gleaned from the think-aloud protocols that revealed that all experimental groups evidenced a low level of processing, they suggested that such low depth of processing could have contributed to the non-significant performances between the experimental groups. Depth of processing is defined as "the relative amount of cognitive effort, level of analysis, and elaboration of intake, together with the usage of prior knowledge, hypothesis testing, and rule formation employed in decoding and encoding some grammatical or lexical item in the input" ([Leow, 2015](#ch3.xhtml_ref3_18){.biblioref role="doc-biblioref"}, p. 204). [Morgan-Short et al. (2012)](#ch3.xhtml_ref3_27){.biblioref role="doc-biblioref"}, a conceptual replication of [Leow et al. (2008)](#ch3.xhtml_ref3_19){.biblioref role="doc-biblioref"}, provided empirical support for the external validity of the original study by reporting similar findings together with positive correlations between higher depth of processing and superior comprehension performances.

[]{#ch3.xhtml_p34 .pagebreak aria-label=" page 34. " role="doc-pagebreak"}As can be seen, any study purporting to address the role of modality in IP or how L2 learners process L2 data during exposure to aural or written input must approach this issue from a process-oriented perspective by employing concurrent data elicitation procedures (e.g., eye-tracking, think-aloud protocols) to gather data on learners' processing and processes employed during exposure to aural or written input (see also Jegerski, this volume, and Marijuan, this volume, for more information about concurrent data elicitation procedures). Additionally, like [Wong (2001)](#ch3.xhtml_ref3_37){.biblioref role="doc-biblioref"}, a more robust research design should address this issue by directly comparing aural and written modes within the same participant pool and research design. A within-subjects design would also improve the research methodology.
:::
:::::::

:::::::: {.section aria-labelledby="sec3_4"}
## Current Contributions and Research {#ch3.xhtml_sec3_4}

Since 2001, there have been only four studies that have investigated the role of modality in IP, albeit from both a product-oriented and process-oriented perspective.[^3] One study, [Morgan-Short et al., 2018](#ch3.xhtml_ref3_28){.biblioref role="doc-biblioref"}, contributed to the theoretical strand of research on simultaneous attention to form and meaning while the other three approached the role of modality in IP from a pedagogical or instructional perspective (e.g., [Cintrón-Valentín & Ellis, 2016](#ch3.xhtml_ref3_4){.biblioref role="doc-biblioref"} \[aural, written\]; [Kim & Godfroid, 2019](#ch3.xhtml_ref3_12){.biblioref role="doc-biblioref"} \[aural, written\]; [Ito & Wong, 2019](#ch3.xhtml_ref3_10){.biblioref role="doc-biblioref"} \[aural\] and [Wong & Ito, 2018](#ch3.xhtml_ref3_38){.biblioref role="doc-biblioref"} \[written\] combined). We wish to direct readers' attention to the methodological efforts of these studies to gather concurrent data on how L2 learners were processing the L2 data (e.g., eye-tracking by [Ito & Wong, 2019](#ch3.xhtml_ref3_10){.biblioref role="doc-biblioref"}, and [Wong & Ito, 2018](#ch3.xhtml_ref3_38){.biblioref role="doc-biblioref"}, and think-aloud protocols by [Martin et al., 2019](#ch3.xhtml_ref3_25){#ch3.xhtml_R_ref3_25 .biblioref role="doc-biblioref"}) instead of only relying on post-exposure assessment tasks to make assumptions about processing.

::: {.section aria-labelledby="sec3_4_1"}
### Modality and Input Processing Research: Theoretical Perspective (Update) {#ch3.xhtml_sec3_4_1}

Given the inconclusive results of both aural and written modes in the earlier modality studies, an international multi-site replication study ([Morgan-Short et al., 2018](#ch3.xhtml_ref3_28){.biblioref role="doc-biblioref"}) on both modes within the same research design was conducted following [Leow et al.'s (2008)](#ch3.xhtml_ref3_19){.biblioref role="doc-biblioref"} research design, including the multiple-choice comprehension assessment task whose reliability level was reported to be relatively low, and a change in how the written text was presented (via computer with rapid serial visual presentation). The authors reported findings of no effect of attending to lexical or grammatical form on comprehension, consistent with findings reported in [Leow et al. (2008)](#ch3.xhtml_ref3_19){.biblioref role="doc-biblioref"}, [Morgan-Short et al. (2012)](#ch3.xhtml_ref3_27){.biblioref role="doc-biblioref"} and [Wong (2001)](#ch3.xhtml_ref3_37){.biblioref role="doc-biblioref"} for the written mode but contradictory to those reported for the aural mode ([VanPatten, 1990](#ch3.xhtml_ref3_34){.biblioref role="doc-biblioref"}; [Wong, 2001](#ch3.xhtml_ref3_37){.biblioref role="doc-biblioref"}). With regard to the role of modality, they reported that a significant main effect was found (written \> aural) together with a marginal interaction of condition by modality. After further analyses, the authors concluded that their results "did not provide evidence that there were differences in comprehension between experimental conditions and control, regardless of modality" ([Morgan-Short et al., 2018](#ch3.xhtml_ref3_28){.biblioref role="doc-biblioref"}, p. 416). (See also discussion, in Sanz, this volume.)

Overall, whether modality plays a role in L2 learners' comprehension due to cognitive constraints or processing capacity remains to be robustly supported by empirical evidence. Differences in results may be due to different paradigms, L2 learners' level of L2 comprehension, mode of presentation (written passage vs. rapid serial visual presentation), passage length (275 vs. 358 words), and testing procedures for comprehension (recall vs. multiple-choice). At the same time, findings gleaned from concurrent data gathered while L2 learners were processing written input also appear to indicate some role of depth of processing in accounting for non-significant differences in performances between experimental conditions, a role that is also reported in the pedagogical research strand. In sum, given that the dependent variables of these []{#ch3.xhtml_p35 .pagebreak aria-label=" page 35. " role="doc-pagebreak"}studies were intake and comprehension, whether modality plays a role in how L2 learners process incoming L2 input for subsequent knowledge remains to be addressed in this theoretical strand of research.
:::

::: {.section aria-labelledby="sec3_4_2"}
### Modality and IP Research: Pedagogical Perspectives {#ch3.xhtml_sec3_4_2}

The role of modality in IP has also recently been addressed from minimally three approaches within a pedagogical perspective, namely, processing instruction (PI) (e.g., [Ito & Wong, 2019](#ch3.xhtml_ref3_10){.biblioref role="doc-biblioref"} \[aural\]; [Wong & Ito, 2018](#ch3.xhtml_ref3_38){.biblioref role="doc-biblioref"} \[written\]), saliency within form-focused instruction ([Cintrón-Valentín & Ellis, 2016](#ch3.xhtml_ref3_4){.biblioref role="doc-biblioref"}), and integrated practice during exposure ([Kim & Godfroid, 2019](#ch3.xhtml_ref3_12){.biblioref role="doc-biblioref"}).
:::

::: {.section aria-labelledby="sec3_4_3"}
### Processing Instruction (PI) {#ch3.xhtml_sec3_4_3}

[Ito and Wong (2019)](#ch3.xhtml_ref3_10){.biblioref role="doc-biblioref"} make a contribution to research on modality and IP from two perspectives: (1) they are the first PI study to address the role of modality in IP and subsequent L2 development in relation to their previous (2018) study and (2), together with [Wong and Ito (2018)](#ch3.xhtml_ref3_38){.biblioref role="doc-biblioref"}, they are also the first PI study to use eye-tracking to directly address attentional allocation before and after exposure to PI (compared to traditional instruction \[TI\]) in the written ([Wong & Ito, 2018](#ch3.xhtml_ref3_38){.biblioref role="doc-biblioref"}) and aural ([Ito & Wong, 2019](#ch3.xhtml_ref3_10){.biblioref role="doc-biblioref"}) mode. [Wong and Ito (2018)](#ch3.xhtml_ref3_38){.biblioref role="doc-biblioref"} conducted two experiments (one without explicit instruction \[EI\] and the other with EI of the target French causative structure, e.g., *Pierre fait faire du sushi à Marie* "Pierre makes Marie make sushi."). They assessed changes in eye movement patterns and picture selection (2 options) accuracy (mouse click responses). Accuracy and eye-tracking data obtained after instructional training revealed that not only was the PI group's performance statistically superior to TI but also demonstrated differences in IP. Wong and Ito reported, however, that the timing of the shift to the correct picture was not as early as predicted where *faire* after *fait* is the first critical grammatical cue that differentiates the causative structure from the non-causative structure. Instead of responding to the infinitive verb and shifting their gazes to the earlier cue, their gazes were shifted to the correct picture during the sentence-final prepositional phrase (à Marie), that is, at the onset of the final prepositional phrase (à Marie/Pierre). Experiment 2, which included the provision of EI, revealed in both groups more substantial improvement in both accuracy performances and gaze shifts, underscoring the role of prior grammatical knowledge gleaned from the instructional training session, but did not result in an earlier shift to processing the anticipated grammatical cue in either group.

[Ito and Wong (2019)](#ch3.xhtml_ref3_10){.biblioref role="doc-biblioref"} directly addressed the role of modality in the instructional training session by replicating [Wong and Ito (2018)](#ch3.xhtml_ref3_38){.biblioref role="doc-biblioref"} with PI training using auditory input instead of written input. They reported no effect of modality on offline interpretational judgments, but the eye-movement patterns revealed a resilient first-noun bias and an overall slowness in the increase in looks to the correct picture when compared to the written PI groups' responses reported in [Wong and Ito (2018)](#ch3.xhtml_ref3_38){.biblioref role="doc-biblioref"}. Ito and Wong suggested that the slower processing from aural input supported the findings of [VanPatten (1990)](#ch3.xhtml_ref3_34){.biblioref role="doc-biblioref"} and [Wong (2001)](#ch3.xhtml_ref3_37){.biblioref role="doc-biblioref"} whose participants found it challenging to attend to surface grammatical forms while processing auditory L2 input (see [Martin et al., 2019](#ch3.xhtml_ref3_25){.biblioref role="doc-biblioref"} for another plausible explanation). However, such comparison may be challenged given that VanPatten and Wong both addressed the aural mode *during* exposure while their study gathered data *after* an instructional training session. Furthermore, the former studies addressed global comprehension while the current ones addressed specific interpretation of a picture selection test comprising two options.

Five other PI studies employing similar research designs and eye-tracking ([Issa, 2019](#ch3.xhtml_ref3_8){#ch3.xhtml_R_ref3_8 .biblioref role="doc-biblioref"}; [Issa & Morgan-Short, 2019](#ch3.xhtml_ref3_9){.biblioref role="doc-biblioref"}; Lee & Doherty, [2019a](#ch3.xhtml_ref3_13){#ch3.xhtml_R_ref3_13 .biblioref role="doc-biblioref"}, [2019b](#ch3.xhtml_ref3_14){#ch3.xhtml_R_ref3_14 .biblioref role="doc-biblioref"}) as well as think-aloud protocols ([]{#ch3.xhtml_p36 .pagebreak aria-label=" page 36. " role="doc-pagebreak"}[Martin et al., 2019](#ch3.xhtml_ref3_25){.biblioref role="doc-biblioref"}) may provide some additional insights into the potential role of modality. Almost all of these studies ([Issa, 2019](#ch3.xhtml_ref3_8){.biblioref role="doc-biblioref"}; [Issa & Morgan-Short, 2019](#ch3.xhtml_ref3_9){.biblioref role="doc-biblioref"}; [Lee & Doherty, 2019a](#ch3.xhtml_ref3_13){.biblioref role="doc-biblioref"}; [Martin et al., 2019](#ch3.xhtml_ref3_25){.biblioref role="doc-biblioref"}) referenced some role for depth of processing (see also [Leow, 2015](#ch3.xhtml_ref3_18){.biblioref role="doc-biblioref"}; [Leow et al., 2008](#ch3.xhtml_ref3_19){.biblioref role="doc-biblioref"}; [Morgan-Short et al., 2012](#ch3.xhtml_ref3_27){.biblioref role="doc-biblioref"}) during the instructional treatment or for previous instruction ([Issa & Morgan-Short, 2019](#ch3.xhtml_ref3_9){.biblioref role="doc-biblioref"}), leading to gains on the posttest (see [Wong and Ito, 2018](#ch3.xhtml_ref3_38){.biblioref role="doc-biblioref"} for improvement due to instruction in both their PI and TI groups, Experiment 2). Indeed, the think-aloud protocols of the PI participants in [Martin et al. (2019)](#ch3.xhtml_ref3_25){.biblioref role="doc-biblioref"} reported a relatively high level of processing evidenced by their activation of recent prior grammatical knowledge and metacognition while employing four specific strategies of interpreting the target structure (the pre-verbal Spanish accusative clitics), namely, identifying the verb form to assign agency and using the object pronoun, the redundant object pronoun, and the preposition *a* to identify the object of the verb. The focus on the preposition in the prepositional phrase (e.g., a María) appears to parallel [Wong and Ito's (2018)](#ch3.xhtml_ref3_38){.biblioref role="doc-biblioref"} and [Ito and Wong's (2019)](#ch3.xhtml_ref3_10){.biblioref role="doc-biblioref"} eye-tracking finding that the PI participants started to shift their gazes at the beginning of the same prepositional phrase (à Marie/Pierre) before they processed the entire sentence, indicating the strategy of processing the preposition to interpret the sentences. More importantly, although a high level of processing was established, many of the PI participants did not achieve awareness at the level of understanding ([Schmidt, 1990](#ch3.xhtml_ref3_33){.biblioref role="doc-biblioref"}) resulting in their overall lower performances. This finding may explicate [Wong and Ito's (2018)](#ch3.xhtml_ref3_38){.biblioref role="doc-biblioref"} and [Ito and Wong's (2019)](#ch3.xhtml_ref3_10){.biblioref role="doc-biblioref"} participants' performances regarding the late timing of the shift to the second critical grammatical cue. While participants did improve their ability to interpret the target structure in those studies and the present one, they did not appear to have adequately internalized the underlying rule.
:::

::: {.section aria-labelledby="sec3_4_4"}
### Modality and Form-Focused Instruction {#ch3.xhtml_sec3_4_4}

[Cintrón-Valentín and Ellis (2016)](#ch3.xhtml_ref3_4){.biblioref role="doc-biblioref"} extended previous research on salience and learned attention in ISLA by exploring and comparing, in two experiments, the degree to which implicit and explicit methods of form-focused instruction (FFI) might serve "to contrast and illuminate the processes by which these different methods help learners refocus their attention to non-salient forms in the input" (p. 5), and by comparing their effects in aural and visual modalities of language. Eye-tracking was used in this study to gather concurrent attentional data during the instructional phase. Regarding the role of modality, the authors reported that all forms of FFI were effective in increasing attention to verbal morphology in the visual modality, although verb pretraining resulted in balanced attention to both cues. However, attending to the morphological cues was considerably more difficult under aural than under visual presentation. Only grammar instruction (verb pretraining) was successful in reattuning learners' attention to the non-salient morphological cues in both modalities (see also, the role of instruction in [Issa & Morgan-Short, 2019](#ch3.xhtml_ref3_9){.biblioref role="doc-biblioref"}; [Martin et al., 2019](#ch3.xhtml_ref3_25){.biblioref role="doc-biblioref"}; [Wong & Ito, 2018](#ch3.xhtml_ref3_38){.biblioref role="doc-biblioref"}, Experiment 2). Textually enhancing verb morphology promoted attention to and analysis of these cues during sentence exposure and even after these cues were no longer made salient. In contrast, with aural input presentation, the emphasized inflectional forms were attended to, but not analyzed, so once the emphasis was removed, sustained use of the cues was curtailed. This finding of attention without deeper processing lends support to [Leow and Martin (2017)](#ch3.xhtml_ref3_20){#ch3.xhtml_R_ref3_20 .biblioref role="doc-biblioref"} who provided a critical overview of textual enhancement (TE) research revealing that despite documented larger amounts of attention paid to more salient forms, an overall lack of benefit was attributed to the role of TE. From a theoretical perspective, they pointed out that perhaps it is not attention that drives learning but *how* L2 data are processed given that intake not further processed may disappear from working memory ([Leow, 2015](#ch3.xhtml_ref3_18){.biblioref role="doc-biblioref"}).
:::

::: {.section aria-labelledby="sec3_4_5"}
### []{#ch3.xhtml_p37 .pagebreak aria-label=" page 37. " role="doc-pagebreak"}Modality within Integrated Practice {#ch3.xhtml_sec3_4_5}

[Kim and Godfroid (2019)](#ch3.xhtml_ref3_12){.biblioref role="doc-biblioref"} examined language exposure to a semi-artificial language and integrated practice. In two experiments, they investigated whether modality (aural input vs. visual word-by-word input via rapid serial visual presentation) plays a role in learning an L2, measured on a grammaticality judgment task (GJT) in which each GJT decision was immediately followed by a request to provide the source of their judgment (source attribution: "guess," "intuition," "recollection," or "rule") to address different types of L2 knowledge (implicit vs. explicit). In both experiments, participants engaged in integrated L2 practice, which combined meaning-focused exposure to L2 input with opportunities to practice the L2 and provided the learners with implicit feedback. Based on performance obtained after the experimental practice, Kim and Godfroid reported no significant role for modality on the GJT task. Furthermore, only the natural visual group acquired both explicit and implicit knowledge. The authors posited that the differential performance based on source attribution data may indicate that modality effects may depend on "the complexity of the linguistic system, not only in terms of its rule complexity, but also the actual amount of phonological space that the different units in the language occupy" (p. 661). However, whether these variables played a role in input processing remains unknown due to a lack of concurrent data.

In sum, recent modality studies have begun to address L2 development in addition to an uptick in employing eye-tracking to directly gather concurrent attentional data. Similarly, due to the paucity of studies addressing L2 learners' limited attentional resources or processing capacity, the role of modality in the pedagogical strand of research remains to be robustly supported by empirical evidence. At the same time, it may be instructive to note the variables that appear to contribute to some robust performances in the literature reported above. For example, L2 learners appear to pay attention to or process more salient linguistic items when compared to less salient ones (e.g., lexical vs. morphological), which may play a role in comprehension in the aural mode ([Cintrón-Valentín & Ellis, 2016](#ch3.xhtml_ref3_4){.biblioref role="doc-biblioref"}; [Leow, 1995](#ch3.xhtml_ref3_16){.biblioref role="doc-biblioref"}; [VanPatten, 1990](#ch3.xhtml_ref3_34){.biblioref role="doc-biblioref"}; [Wong, 2001](#ch3.xhtml_ref3_37){.biblioref role="doc-biblioref"}). At the same time, attention to morphological cues also appears to be more effective from visual than from aural input, especially after instruction ([Cintrón-Valentín & Ellis, 2016](#ch3.xhtml_ref3_4){.biblioref role="doc-biblioref"}) but not for syntactic cues ([Ito & Wong, 2019](#ch3.xhtml_ref3_10){.biblioref role="doc-biblioref"} and [Wong & Ito, 2018](#ch3.xhtml_ref3_38){.biblioref role="doc-biblioref"}). Similarly, L2 learners appear to explicitly process grammatical information via cognitive strategies (e.g., [Martin et al., 2019](#ch3.xhtml_ref3_25){.biblioref role="doc-biblioref"}) at a relatively high level *during* (e.g., [Cintrón-Valentín & Ellis, 2016](#ch3.xhtml_ref3_4){.biblioref role="doc-biblioref"}; [Lee & Doherty, 2019a](#ch3.xhtml_ref3_13){.biblioref role="doc-biblioref"}; [Martin et al., 2019](#ch3.xhtml_ref3_25){.biblioref role="doc-biblioref"}) and *after* (e.g., [Issa & Morgan-Short, 2019](#ch3.xhtml_ref3_9){.biblioref role="doc-biblioref"}; [Ito & Wong, 2019](#ch3.xhtml_ref3_10){.biblioref role="doc-biblioref"}; [Lee & Doherty, 2019b](#ch3.xhtml_ref3_14){.biblioref role="doc-biblioref"}; [Wong & Ito, 2018](#ch3.xhtml_ref3_38){.biblioref role="doc-biblioref"}) instruction. However, what appears to play perhaps a more important role, regardless of modality, is how L2 learners process the grammatical information (e.g., [Martin et al., 2019](#ch3.xhtml_ref3_25){.biblioref role="doc-biblioref"}), which may potentially make the target information more salient ([Cintrón-Valentín & Ellis, 2016](#ch3.xhtml_ref3_4){.biblioref role="doc-biblioref"}; [Issa & Morgan-Short, 2019](#ch3.xhtml_ref3_9){.biblioref role="doc-biblioref"}) while allowing the potential for further processing of the grammatical information ([Leow, 2015](#ch3.xhtml_ref3_18){.biblioref role="doc-biblioref"}; [Martin et al., 2019](#ch3.xhtml_ref3_25){.biblioref role="doc-biblioref"}). [Leow et al. (2019)](#ch3.xhtml_ref3_22){#ch3.xhtml_R_ref3_22 .biblioref role="doc-biblioref"} provided further empirical evidence that L2 learners who processed more deeply (as established via think-aloud protocols) produced superior performances when compared to those who processed at a lower depth (see also [Cintrón-Valentín & Ellis, 2016](#ch3.xhtml_ref3_4){.biblioref role="doc-biblioref"}; [Issa & Morgan-Short, 2019](#ch3.xhtml_ref3_9){.biblioref role="doc-biblioref"}).
:::
::::::::

::: {.section aria-labelledby="sec3_5"}
## Recommendations for Practice {#ch3.xhtml_sec3_5}

As observed above, the paucity of studies that have investigated the role of modality in one research design does not permit us to offer conclusive pedagogical recommendations. At the same time, the information gleaned from concurrent data elicitation procedures on L2 learners' attention to and subsequent processing of L2 data in the two modes suggests that teachers []{#ch3.xhtml_p38 .pagebreak aria-label=" page 38. " role="doc-pagebreak"}should be aware that visually highlighting less salient morphological items in the written mode may be more beneficial than enhancing forms in the aural mode (e.g., via intonation). Instructors may also want to ensure that they prime their students before exposure to new lexical or grammatical information in the L2 input by providing some grammatical explanation and/or the provision of focused or directed attention to target items in the L2 input. This priming, in turn, may allow learners to more efficiently employ their cognitive resources to facilitate their processing of incoming input in either mode. Type of mode does not appear to play a role during this type of processing given that it is more learner-centered than dependent upon external manipulation (e.g., [Issa & Morgan-Short, 2019](#ch3.xhtml_ref3_9){.biblioref role="doc-biblioref"}; [Leow, 1995](#ch3.xhtml_ref3_16){.biblioref role="doc-biblioref"}). In other words, instructors should be aware that not all learners process the L2 input they are exposed to in the same way.
:::

::: {.section aria-labelledby="sec3_6"}
## Future Directions {#ch3.xhtml_sec3_6}

The four critical issues raised earlier in the chapter have been partially addressed. While the paucity of studies makes it challenging to provide a clear understanding of the role that modality plays in IP, there has been an appreciable uptick toward a more process-oriented approach to this strand of research in addressing the relationship between modality, IP, and L2 development. Studies that have employed both modes in one design (e.g., [Cintrón-Valentín & Ellis, 2016](#ch3.xhtml_ref3_4){.biblioref role="doc-biblioref"}; [Ito & Wong, 2019](#ch3.xhtml_ref3_10){.biblioref role="doc-biblioref"} and [Wong & Ito, 2018](#ch3.xhtml_ref3_38){.biblioref role="doc-biblioref"}; [Kim & Godfroid, 2019](#ch3.xhtml_ref3_12){.biblioref role="doc-biblioref"}; [Morgan-Short et al., 2018](#ch3.xhtml_ref3_28){.biblioref role="doc-biblioref"}) have produced conflicting results in offline performances most likely due to, for example, different paradigms, learning conditions, or populations. At the same time, the increasing number of studies employing concurrent data elicitation procedures (eye-tracking and think-aloud protocols) provides more insight into the allocation of attention during IP (via eye-tracking data) and what cognitive processes or strategies L2 learners employ while processing L2 input (via think-aloud protocols). The concurrent data appears to indicate that how L2 learners process incoming L2 data during initial exposure (e.g., [Cintrón-Valentín & Ellis, 2016](#ch3.xhtml_ref3_4){.biblioref role="doc-biblioref"}; [Lee & Doherty, 2019a](#ch3.xhtml_ref3_13){.biblioref role="doc-biblioref"}; [Martin et al., 2019](#ch3.xhtml_ref3_25){.biblioref role="doc-biblioref"}) or after instruction (e.g., [Issa & Morgan-Short, 2019](#ch3.xhtml_ref3_9){.biblioref role="doc-biblioref"}; [Lee & Doherty, 2019b](#ch3.xhtml_ref3_14){.biblioref role="doc-biblioref"}; [Ito & Wong, 2019](#ch3.xhtml_ref3_10){.biblioref role="doc-biblioref"}; [Wong & Ito, 2018](#ch3.xhtml_ref3_38){.biblioref role="doc-biblioref"}) may play a more important role than that of modality. However, it is also noted that arguably only one study ([Wong, 2001](#ch3.xhtml_ref3_37){.biblioref role="doc-biblioref"}) has addressed the role of modality during the initial exposure without any prior experimental preparation or in combination with other variables, However, this study only addressed the effect of modality on comprehension and not L2 development.

Future research on the role of modality in IP, then, should first be process-oriented to gather concurrent data on how L2 learners actually process L2 input. Second, L2 data needs to be gathered *during* the initial stage of exposure to truly address the role of modality in initial IP. Third, to elicit clear answers to the role of modality in IP, future research needs to go beyond L2 learners' allocation of attention to capturing how the L2 data are processed during exposure to aural or written input. For example, while it appears that with prior grammatical knowledge of specific L2 structures, L2 learners demonstrate a similar processing strategy across modality in order to interpret incoming L2 data ([Ito & Wong, 2019](#ch3.xhtml_ref3_10){.biblioref role="doc-biblioref"}; [Wong & Ito, 2018](#ch3.xhtml_ref3_38){.biblioref role="doc-biblioref"}), think-aloud protocols also reveal several other strategies in play while processing the input ([Martin et al., 2019](#ch3.xhtml_ref3_25){.biblioref role="doc-biblioref"}). Fourth, given the challenge of gathering concurrent data on how L2 learners process input during exposure to spoken language and not simply whether or not they allocate attention to target L2 data, the need for triangulation of data is of paramount importance ([Godfroid & Uggen, 2013](#ch3.xhtml_ref3_6){#ch3.xhtml_R_ref3_6 .biblioref role="doc-biblioref"}; [Leow et al., 2014](#ch3.xhtml_ref3_23){#ch3.xhtml_R_ref3_23 .biblioref role="doc-biblioref"}; Marijuan, this volume). To this end, the potential usefulness of reaction times in this strand of research may be an additional source of data to be considered in triangulation with those of other concurrent procedures.[^4] Fifth, given the preponderance of replication studies in the modality strand, one fruitful area of research may be to continue the exploration of multi-site studies (e.g., [Morgan-Short et al., 2018](#ch3.xhtml_ref3_28){.biblioref role="doc-biblioref"}) that should provide more robust findings []{#ch3.xhtml_p39 .pagebreak aria-label=" page 39. " role="doc-pagebreak"}regarding the role of modality in IP.[^5] Sixth, studies also need to measure comprehension, interpretation, and production followed by delayed posttests to address retention. Finally, important variables that future research may want to consider include L2 learners' levels of comprehension, language experience, length of exposure, type of linguistic item, and individual differences given that they may potentially play some role in how input is processed in different modes.
:::

::: {.section .conclusion role="doc-conclusion"}
## Conclusion {#ch3.xhtml_sec3_7}

Surprisingly, the role of modality in IP has not attracted much attention in (I)SLA research although learners are exposed to both spoken and written languages in the L2 learning process. This chapter has provided a critical review of the role of modality during IP (intake and comprehension) and in subsequent L2 development and has raised several critical issues. We discussed that the role of modality in IP in (I)SLA has been approached from two major perspectives, namely, theoretical with regard to the learner viewed as a limited capacity processor of information and pedagogical in relation to the effect of instruction on IP and subsequent L2 development. However, whether modality plays a role in IP remains generally unexplored due to the paucity of studies that have addressed this variable within one research design and, within the pedagogical perspective, during the instructional phase. Indeed, it was noted that within both the theoretical and pedagogical perspectives on how L2 learners process the L2 data to which they are exposed may be independent from mode of presentation. Based on the concurrent data, limited pedagogical implications were provided.

Studies on the role of modality in IP can add a much-needed dimension to (I)SLA research that can broaden our understanding of learners' cognitive processes while interacting with L2 input in different modes. By addressing the effects of type of mode to which learners are exposed, we not only arrive at a clearer understanding of adult L2 learners' cognitive processes while interacting with L2 data, but we also increase our efforts to avoid the pitfalls of deduction or extrapolation from one mode to another. Moreover, as we improve our understanding of the role of modality in IP in (I)SLA, we can begin to propose more robust instructional approaches and teaching materials that take such findings into account.
:::

::: {.endnotes role="doc-endnotes"}
## Notes {#ch3.xhtml_h2}
:::

::: {.section .reference aria-labelledby="r3_1"}
## Further Reading {#ch3.xhtml_r3_1}

Given the limited amount of investigation on the role of modality in input processing, we recommend readers revisit the following studies that employed appropriate research designs to address such a role.

- [Morgan-Short, K., Marsden, E., Heil, J., Issa II, B. I., Leow, R. P., Mikhaylova, A., & Szudarski, P. (2018). Multisite replication in second language acquisition research: Attention to form during listening and reading comprehension. `<cite>`{=html}Language Learning`</cite>`{=html}, *68*, 392--437.]{#ch3.xhtml_ref3_1}
- [Wong, W. (2001). Modality and attention to meaning and form in the input. `<cite>`{=html}Studies in Second Language Acquisition`</cite>`{=html}, *23*, 345--368.]{#ch3.xhtml_ref3_2}
:::
