[]{#ch4.xhtml}

::: {}
# []{#ch4.xhtml_p42 .pagebreak aria-label=" page 42. " role="doc-pagebreak"}[4 ]{.chnum .ordinal}[Input Processing in L1 Acquisition and Simultaneous Bilingualism]{.chtitle} {#ch4.xhtml_h1}

John W. Schwieter

Wilfrid Laurier University / McMaster University

Alessandro Giovanni Benati

University College Dublin

DOI: [10.4324/9781003041412-5](https://dx.doi.org/10.4324/9781003041412-5){aria-label="D.O.I. link to this document."}
:::

:::: {.section .introduction role="doc-introduction"}
## Introduction and Definitions {#ch4.xhtml_sec4_1}

::: {.section aria-labelledby="sec4_1_1"}
### What Is Input and Why Is It Essential for Language Acquisition? {#ch4.xhtml_sec4_1_1}

Exposure to input is both necessary and sufficient for children to acquire all the components of their native language. Input is argued to be among the strongest predictors of the rate of acquisition in both monolingual and bilingual children ([Huttenlocher et al., 1991](#ch4.xhtml_ref4_52){#ch4.xhtml_R_ref4_52 .biblioref role="doc-biblioref"}; [Pearson, 2007](#ch4.xhtml_ref4_77){#ch4.xhtml_R_ref4_77 .biblioref role="doc-biblioref"}). At the same time, mere access to input is not the only thing that children need to learn their first language (L1). Empirical studies on the role of input in L1 acquisition have demonstrated that children are sensitive to sounds from the earliest days of life and interact with environmental and parental input. Caregiver speech is the input that children are exposed to prior to attending school. It is a style of language that is characterized by restricted vocabulary, short and simple sentences, slow and repetitive speech, high pitch, and exaggerated intonation ([O'Grady, 1997](#ch4.xhtml_ref4_70){#ch4.xhtml_R_ref4_70 .biblioref role="doc-biblioref"}). L1 learners need this form of speech to acquire both the lexical and the syntactic system of a language.

Empirical evidence has demonstrated that L1 learners are also sensitive to frequency effects in language processing ([Lieven, 2010](#ch4.xhtml_ref4_58){#ch4.xhtml_R_ref4_58 .biblioref role="doc-biblioref"}). This is the case for both children's production and comprehension. Experimental studies ([Bates & MacWhinney, 1987](#ch4.xhtml_ref4_7){#ch4.xhtml_R_ref4_7 .biblioref role="doc-biblioref"}; [MacWhinney, 1987](#ch4.xhtml_ref4_62){#ch4.xhtml_R_ref4_62 .biblioref role="doc-biblioref"}) and connectionist and computational simulations (e.g., [Chang et al., 2006](#ch4.xhtml_ref4_22){#ch4.xhtml_R_ref4_22 .biblioref role="doc-biblioref"}; [Chater & Manning, 2006](#ch4.xhtml_ref4_23){#ch4.xhtml_R_ref4_23 .biblioref role="doc-biblioref"}; [Morris et al., 2000](#ch4.xhtml_ref4_67){#ch4.xhtml_R_ref4_67 .biblioref role="doc-biblioref"}) can provide considerable insight into the ways in which frequencies of forms and constructions may interact to produce learning outcomes. From a usage-based perspective, children build their grammars initially out of the phonological--lexical strings that they learn from the input. Many naturalistic studies of children's speech have found that the more frequently children hear a particular word or construction, all things being equal, the earlier they acquire it. For example, [Theakston et al. (2004)](#ch4.xhtml_ref4_94){#ch4.xhtml_R_ref4_94 .biblioref role="doc-biblioref"} have shown that order of emergence of particular verbs is significantly correlated with the frequency of use in language addressed to children.

An additional element to frequency, as highlighted by [Slobin (1985)](#ch4.xhtml_ref4_92){#ch4.xhtml_R_ref4_92 .biblioref role="doc-biblioref"}, is the ability of L1 learners to make a one-to-one mapping between a form and its function. In the competition model, [Bates and MacWhinney (1989)](#ch4.xhtml_ref4_8){#ch4.xhtml_R_ref4_8 .biblioref role="doc-biblioref"} demonstrated that frequency interacts with consistency []{#ch4.xhtml_p43 .pagebreak aria-label=" page 43. " role="doc-pagebreak"}in form-function mapping, and they developed methods for counting the strength of different cues in the input.

[Pinker (1995)](#ch4.xhtml_ref4_82){#ch4.xhtml_R_ref4_82 .biblioref role="doc-biblioref"} and [Klein (1996)](#ch4.xhtml_ref4_56){#ch4.xhtml_R_ref4_56 .biblioref role="doc-biblioref"} have argued that children are not born with a specific language in their head. They can't speak the language because they have not been exposed to language input yet. An individual's capacity to talk and understand a particular language depends on the exposure to linguistic input. In contrast to the view that sufficient linguistic input plays a key role in assisting children to acquire their L1, is the view that L1 acquisition is predominantly possible by the existence of an innate capacity. [Chomsky (1965)](#ch4.xhtml_ref4_24){#ch4.xhtml_R_ref4_24 .biblioref role="doc-biblioref"} claimed that children would never acquire the tools needed for processing an infinite number of sentences if the language acquisition mechanism was dependent on language input alone. [Chomsky (1965, p. 56)](#ch4.xhtml_ref4_24){.biblioref role="doc-biblioref"} coined the term "language faculty" as one of cognitive faculties that facilitate language acquisition. This internalized grammar allows learners to go beyond particular sentences fed by the input.

These two views are not mutually exclusive to each other but rather, are complementary because the absence of one will significantly inhibit the acquisition process. Innate capacity and linguistic input are playing different roles with the same objective. Innate capacity provides a platform for the child to develop, test, and acquire the grammatical rules fed to them through exposure to the language input. In doing so, children require linguistic inputs to be processed without which innate capacity will be simply wasted. Therefore, both views are in a complementary collaboration in the process of L1 acquisition.

In this chapter, we will first define input and why it is essential in L1 acquisition. Throughout our discussions, we will use the term *simultaneous bilinguals* to refer to individuals who have learned two languages from birth or very early childhood. We use the term *sequential bilinguals* for individuals who began learning their second language (L2) several years after birth. Following this, we discuss input processing (IP) ([VanPatten, 2015](#ch4.xhtml_ref4_104){#ch4.xhtml_R_ref4_104 .biblioref role="doc-biblioref"}) and other prominent hypotheses from L2 acquisition that can further explain the role of input in L1 acquisition and early bilingualism. In doing so, we review studies comparing the differential effects of the quantity and quality of input on vocabulary and grammar development in two languages. We conclude the chapter by discussing some pedagogical implications.
:::
::::

::::: {.section aria-labelledby="sec4_2"}
## Historical Perspectives {#ch4.xhtml_sec4_2}

In this section we provide an overview of the role and effects of the quantity and quality of input in language for both monolinguals and bilinguals. We then introduce a few prominent theoretical frameworks that have had a foundational impact on our understanding of input, language acquisition, and how this knowledge can inform teaching and learning.

::: {.section aria-labelledby="sec4_2_1"}
### Quantity and Quality of Input in L1 Acquisition and Early Bilingualism {#ch4.xhtml_sec4_2_1}

We can learn a great deal about the role of input in monolingual and bilingual children by examining the quantity and quality of the language they are exposed to. Children growing up learning one language, two languages from birth, or an L2 from a very early age all have distinct experience with input ([De Houwer, 2018](#ch4.xhtml_ref4_32){#ch4.xhtml_R_ref4_32 .biblioref role="doc-biblioref"}). For instance, while children growing up in monolingual contexts likely receive all of their language input in the same language, the input to which bilingual children are exposed is divided to varying degrees between two languages. De Houwer states that:

> For \[simultaneous bilingual children\], large linguistic variation in the input is present from the outset, and children have been used to people speaking in fundamentally different []{#ch4.xhtml_p44 .pagebreak aria-label=" page 44. " role="doc-pagebreak"}ways from birth. \[They\] gradually learn to understand two languages at the same time and can gradually learn to make sense of their bilingual world in an environment that supports their early understanding through specific infant-directed speech embedded in familiar contexts.
>
> [(pp. 602--603)]{.credit role="doc-credit"}

The situation is quite different for early bilinguals who are first exposed to their L2 a few years after they have already started learning their L1. For these individuals---who have seemingly been monolinguals up until this point---they often are suddenly faced with complex linguistic input in an unfamiliar language. And for the first few months in which they do not understand the language around them (e.g., in a school where the L2 is only spoken), this can be an upsetting experience that can have negative consequences for their psychosocial behaviour ([De Houwer, 2017](#ch4.xhtml_ref4_31){#ch4.xhtml_R_ref4_31 .biblioref role="doc-biblioref"}). Furthermore, this first exposure typically may occur in group settings in which little one-on-one interaction and caretaker speech are present. In sum, the unique experiences with language input for monolinguals and early bilinguals will lead to different learning trajectories which we will discuss in the upcoming sections.
:::

::: {.section aria-labelledby="sec4_2_2"}
### Theoretical Approaches {#ch4.xhtml_sec4_2_2}

Theories and empirical studies in L2 acquisition have addressed the role of input in L2 acquisition ([VanPatten et al., 2020](#ch4.xhtml_ref4_105){#ch4.xhtml_R_ref4_105 .biblioref role="doc-biblioref"}). There is general agreement among scholars that input is a necessary and vital source for L2 acquisition as it provides the primary linguistic data for the development of the internal linguistic system ([Schwartz, 1993](#ch4.xhtml_ref4_90){#ch4.xhtml_R_ref4_90 .biblioref role="doc-biblioref"}). No theoretical model of L2 acquisition ignores input while explaining how learners create an internal linguistic system ([Gass & Mackey, 2015](#ch4.xhtml_ref4_39){#ch4.xhtml_R_ref4_39 .biblioref role="doc-biblioref"}; [VanPatten, 2015](#ch4.xhtml_ref4_104){.biblioref role="doc-biblioref"}). When L2 learners are exposed to language input, they process that input to construct a grammatical system. Despite the consensus that input provides the raw data for L2 acquisition, theoretical perspectives may differ on what exactly happens to the input as L2 learners interact with it.

From a Universal Grammar perspective ([White, 2003](#ch4.xhtml_ref4_106){#ch4.xhtml_R_ref4_106 .biblioref role="doc-biblioref"}), L2 learners possess a series of innate principles regarding language. These innate principles interact with the language input and this interaction results in language acquisition. Opposite to this perspective is the connectionist perspective ([Ellis, 2012](#ch4.xhtml_ref4_35){#ch4.xhtml_R_ref4_35 .biblioref role="doc-biblioref"}) according to which there is no interaction with any innate knowledge. In other words, connectionist theories hold that there are no special internal and innate mechanisms containing linguistic information about language. Instead, L2 learners simply process linguistic information in the input they receive, and acquisition is affected by the examples to which they are exposed in the input. Empirical research demonstrates that acquisition is sensitive to frequency effects at all levels of language processing ([Bybee & Hopper, 2001](#ch4.xhtml_ref4_17){#ch4.xhtml_R_ref4_17 .biblioref role="doc-biblioref"}; [Ellis, 2002](#ch4.xhtml_ref4_34){#ch4.xhtml_R_ref4_34 .biblioref role="doc-biblioref"}).

From an autonomous induction perspective ([Carroll, 2000](#ch4.xhtml_ref4_20){#ch4.xhtml_R_ref4_20 .biblioref role="doc-biblioref"}) a distinction is made between processing for parsing and processing for acquisition. Parsing, also called syntactic analysis, is the procedure of analyzing input and verifying that it conforms to the grammatical rules of the language. It is exactly when parsing fails that the acquisition mechanisms are triggered. During successful parsing, rules are activated in each processor to categorize and combine representations. Failures occur when the rules are inadequate or missing. Consequently, the rule that comes closest to successfully parse the specific unit would be selected and would undergo the most economical and incremental revision. This process is repeated until parsing succeeds or is at least passable at that given level. This procedure explains the process of acquisition, where the impetus for acquisition is parsing incomprehensible input.

[]{#ch4.xhtml_p45 .pagebreak aria-label=" page 45. " role="doc-pagebreak"}According to the input processing (IP) perspective, [VanPatten (2015)](#ch4.xhtml_ref4_104){.biblioref role="doc-biblioref"} has addressed the issue as to why L2 learners successfully process only some elements in the input and not others. This is due to a series of basic processing strategies affecting how L2 learners make appropriate form--meaning connections and parse syntactic structures in a sentence. IP is about the moment-by-moment connection of surface form with meaning and the moment-by-moment computation of sentence structure during comprehension ([VanPatten, 2015](#ch4.xhtml_ref4_104){.biblioref role="doc-biblioref"}). In the first case, one of the basic processing strategies called the lexical preference principle indicates that L2 learners will process lexical items for meaning before grammatical forms when both encode the same semantic information. In the second case, the first-noun principle states that L2 learners tend to process the first noun or pronoun they encounter in a sentence as the subject/agent.

It is clear that regardless of the theoretical approach, input is a key element for L2 acquisition. However, innate mechanisms and internal processing strategies might interact with input to drive L2 acquisition. Furthermore, input alone is likely insufficient to fully acquire an L2.
:::
:::::

::::: {.section aria-labelledby="sec4_3"}
## Critical Issues and Topics {#ch4.xhtml_sec4_3}

::: {.section aria-labelledby="sec4_3_1"}
### Is the Role of Input and Developmental Trajectories the Same in L1 Acquisition and Early Bilingualism? {#ch4.xhtml_sec4_3_1}

Many studies examining how input affects bilingual language development have focused on exposure within the home as it often provides the primary source of input in the language not spoken in the community (e.g., [Gathercole & Thomas, 2009](#ch4.xhtml_ref4_42){#ch4.xhtml_R_ref4_42 .biblioref role="doc-biblioref"}; [Paradis et al., 2011](#ch4.xhtml_ref4_75){#ch4.xhtml_R_ref4_75 .biblioref role="doc-biblioref"}; [Thomas et al., 2014](#ch4.xhtml_ref4_95){#ch4.xhtml_R_ref4_95 .biblioref role="doc-biblioref"}). The findings suggest that more input generally leads to faster acquisition, although there are modulating factors to this such as the proficiency level of those providing the input ([Chondrogianni & Marinis, 2011](#ch4.xhtml_ref4_25){#ch4.xhtml_R_ref4_25 .biblioref role="doc-biblioref"}), the variety of input sources ([Place & Hoff, 2011](#ch4.xhtml_ref4_83){#ch4.xhtml_R_ref4_83 .biblioref role="doc-biblioref"}), and whether there are other school-age siblings in the home ([Bridges & Hoff, 2014](#ch4.xhtml_ref4_15){#ch4.xhtml_R_ref4_15 .biblioref role="doc-biblioref"}). When comparing bilingual and monolingual children, many studies have demonstrated that the frequency of input has a direct impact on the rate of acquisition. For instance, [Chan (2010)](#ch4.xhtml_ref4_21){#ch4.xhtml_R_ref4_21 .biblioref role="doc-biblioref"} compared the acquisition of Cantonese double object constructions among Cantonese-English bilingual children and Cantonese monolingual children. When analyzing the amount of input for the two groups, Chan found that because the bilingual children were exposed to fewer instances of double object constructions (7%) compared to monolingual children (14%), they were significantly slower to acquire them.
:::

::: {.section aria-labelledby="sec4_3_2"}
### How Does Input in One Versus Two Languages Affect the Processing of Those Languages? {#ch4.xhtml_sec4_3_2}

[Hurtado et al. (2008)](#ch4.xhtml_ref4_51){#ch4.xhtml_R_ref4_51 .biblioref role="doc-biblioref"} studied vocabulary development among toddlers aged 18--24 months in a monolingual (Spanish-only) context. The results showed that children whose mothers gave more input at 18 months not only had more vocabulary knowledge at 24 months, but they also demonstrated a processing advantage such that they were faster in word recognition. Hurtado et al. argue that "input shapes \[monolingual\] children's lexical processing efficiency and that vocabulary growth and increasing facility in spoken word comprehension work together to support the uptake of the information that rich input affords the young language learner" (p. 31). These processing effects of input are similarly seen in cases of simultaneous bilingualism ([Byers-Heinlein & Lew-Williams, 2013](#ch4.xhtml_ref4_18){#ch4.xhtml_R_ref4_18 .biblioref role="doc-biblioref"}). As in Hurtado et al.'s study, several bilingual studies have shown that children who are exposed to more input in one or both of their languages demonstrate better processing techniques in that/those languages ([Conboy & Mills, 2006](#ch4.xhtml_ref4_28){#ch4.xhtml_R_ref4_28 .biblioref role="doc-biblioref"}; [Hurtado et al., 2014](#ch4.xhtml_ref4_50){#ch4.xhtml_R_ref4_50 .biblioref role="doc-biblioref"}; [Marchman et al., 2010](#ch4.xhtml_ref4_63){#ch4.xhtml_R_ref4_63 .biblioref role="doc-biblioref"}).

[]{#ch4.xhtml_p46 .pagebreak aria-label=" page 46. " role="doc-pagebreak"}The consequences of input for the development of various linguistic domains have been of interest to researchers (see [Unsworth, 2016](#ch4.xhtml_ref4_102){#ch4.xhtml_R_ref4_102 .biblioref role="doc-biblioref"} for a review). Although a few of these studies examine the effects on semantic fluency ([Bohman et al., 2010](#ch4.xhtml_ref4_14){#ch4.xhtml_R_ref4_14 .biblioref role="doc-biblioref"}), narrative abilities ([Paradis & Kirova, 2014](#ch4.xhtml_ref4_74){#ch4.xhtml_R_ref4_74 .biblioref role="doc-biblioref"}), and phonological memory ([Parra et al., 2011](#ch4.xhtml_ref4_76){#ch4.xhtml_R_ref4_76 .biblioref role="doc-biblioref"}; [Thordardottir, 2014](#ch4.xhtml_ref4_97){#ch4.xhtml_R_ref4_97 .biblioref role="doc-biblioref"}), the majority have focused on the development of vocabulary (Cobo-Lewis et al., [2002](#ch4.xhtml_ref4_26){#ch4.xhtml_R_ref4_26 .biblioref role="doc-biblioref"}; [Gathercole & Thomas, 2009](#ch4.xhtml_ref4_42){.biblioref role="doc-biblioref"}; [Oller et al., 2007](#ch4.xhtml_ref4_72){#ch4.xhtml_R_ref4_72 .biblioref role="doc-biblioref"}; [Pearson et al., 1997](#ch4.xhtml_ref4_79){#ch4.xhtml_R_ref4_79 .biblioref role="doc-biblioref"}; [Scheele et al., 2010](#ch4.xhtml_ref4_89){#ch4.xhtml_R_ref4_89 .biblioref role="doc-biblioref"}; [Thordardottir, 2011](#ch4.xhtml_ref4_96){#ch4.xhtml_R_ref4_96 .biblioref role="doc-biblioref"}; [Thordardottir et al., 2006](#ch4.xhtml_ref4_100){#ch4.xhtml_R_ref4_100 .biblioref role="doc-biblioref"}) and grammar, particularly morphosyntax ([Blom, 2010](#ch4.xhtml_ref4_13){#ch4.xhtml_R_ref4_13 .biblioref role="doc-biblioref"}; [Gathercole & Thomas, 2005](#ch4.xhtml_ref4_41){#ch4.xhtml_R_ref4_41 .biblioref role="doc-biblioref"}; [Itani-Adams et al., 2017](#ch4.xhtml_ref4_53){#ch4.xhtml_R_ref4_53 .biblioref role="doc-biblioref"}; [Lemmerth & Hopp, 2019](#ch4.xhtml_ref4_57){#ch4.xhtml_R_ref4_57 .biblioref role="doc-biblioref"}; [Montrul & Potowski, 2007](#ch4.xhtml_ref4_66){#ch4.xhtml_R_ref4_66 .biblioref role="doc-biblioref"}; [Nicoladis et al., 2007](#ch4.xhtml_ref4_69){#ch4.xhtml_R_ref4_69 .biblioref role="doc-biblioref"}; [Thordardottir, 2015](#ch4.xhtml_ref4_98){#ch4.xhtml_R_ref4_98 .biblioref role="doc-biblioref"}). In the next section, we will discuss a few key studies that form part of this growing body of literature.
:::
:::::

:::::: {.section aria-labelledby="sec4_4"}
## Current Research {#ch4.xhtml_sec4_4}

It is generally accepted that simultaneous bilinguals resemble their monolingual counterparts in language development and ultimate attainment ([De Houwer, 2009](#ch4.xhtml_ref4_29){#ch4.xhtml_R_ref4_29 .biblioref role="doc-biblioref"}; [Genesee & Nicoladis, 2007](#ch4.xhtml_ref4_43){#ch4.xhtml_R_ref4_43 .biblioref role="doc-biblioref"}; [Grüter & Paradis, 2014](#ch4.xhtml_ref4_46){#ch4.xhtml_R_ref4_46 .biblioref role="doc-biblioref"}; [Meisel, 2011](#ch4.xhtml_ref4_64){#ch4.xhtml_R_ref4_64 .biblioref role="doc-biblioref"}). However, because simultaneous bilingual children are exposed to input in both languages, the notion has been entertained that they consequently have a reduced amount of language input in each language ([Hoff, 2018](#ch4.xhtml_ref4_48){#ch4.xhtml_R_ref4_48 .biblioref role="doc-biblioref"}; although see De Houwer, [2009](#ch4.xhtml_ref4_29){.biblioref role="doc-biblioref"}, [2014](#ch4.xhtml_ref4_30){#ch4.xhtml_R_ref4_30 .biblioref role="doc-biblioref"} for a discussion on how some children exposed to two languages hear as much speech in each as their monolingual peers). Advocates for nativism---the theory suggesting that language acquisition depends on inherent knowledge of language properties---argue that even with a quantitative reduction of input, the speed and ease of acquiring both languages support the innate ability to learn language(s) that is independent from the effects of variation in input ([Gleitman & Newport, 1995](#ch4.xhtml_ref4_44){#ch4.xhtml_R_ref4_44 .biblioref role="doc-biblioref"}; [Petitto & Kovelman, 2003](#ch4.xhtml_ref4_80){#ch4.xhtml_R_ref4_80 .biblioref role="doc-biblioref"}). However, input-based accounts claim that due to less single-language input, simultaneous bilinguals will require more time to acquire two languages than one ([Gathercole & Hoff, 2007](#ch4.xhtml_ref4_40){#ch4.xhtml_R_ref4_40 .biblioref role="doc-biblioref"}; [Oller & Eilers, 2002](#ch4.xhtml_ref4_71){#ch4.xhtml_R_ref4_71 .biblioref role="doc-biblioref"}). As mentioned above, key topics in this vein of research have predominantly focused on input and its consequences for vocabulary and grammatical development. Here we will discuss recent findings on these topics.

::: {.section aria-labelledby="sec4_4_1"}
### The Nature of and Exposure to Input in L1s and L2s {#ch4.xhtml_sec4_4_1}

The type of input to which children are exposed is reflected in the language they use. In a study by [Bialystok et al. (2010)](#ch4.xhtml_ref4_11){#ch4.xhtml_R_ref4_11 .biblioref role="doc-biblioref"}, primary school, English-speaking monolingual children were compared to age-matched bilingual children on a test of vocabulary. The results revealed no differences between the two groups in terms of their "school vocabulary" in English (e.g., desk, hallway, net, etc.). However, the bilingual children knew significantly less "home vocabulary" in English suggesting that while the monolingual children have ample exposure to English input at home, for bilingual children, whose input at home is in another language, home-related concepts are learned in their heritage language.

There are also a few studies that have examined whether non-native-like input (e.g., grammatical errors or "foreign accent") negatively affects language acquisition. Indeed, there are many individuals who grow up learning a language that is not the L1 of their caregivers. As such, the input provided by the caregivers may have various degrees of erroneous language patterns. However, the answer to this is unclear. While [Hoff et al. (2012)](#ch4.xhtml_ref4_49){#ch4.xhtml_R_ref4_49 .biblioref role="doc-biblioref"} and [Place and Hoff (2016)](#ch4.xhtml_ref4_84){#ch4.xhtml_R_ref4_84 .biblioref role="doc-biblioref"} found that non-native input given by parents and caregivers was less supportive of L2 acquisition than native input, [Dulay et al. (2017)](#ch4.xhtml_ref4_33){#ch4.xhtml_R_ref4_33 .biblioref role="doc-biblioref"} reported that non-native input was actually beneficial to L2 development among older sequential bilinguals. Unfortunately, many of these studies often categorize input as either native or non-native and at times assume that non-native input []{#ch4.xhtml_p47 .pagebreak aria-label=" page 47. " role="doc-pagebreak"}implies erroneous language use ([Arom-Lotem & Meir, 2019](#ch4.xhtml_ref4_6){#ch4.xhtml_R_ref4_6 .biblioref role="doc-biblioref"}). However, one study examining the actual input given to a bilingual child at home showed that the morphosyntactic errors spoken in Japanese by a Thai mother were not acquired by the child ([Nakamura, 2015](#ch4.xhtml_ref4_68){#ch4.xhtml_R_ref4_68 .biblioref role="doc-biblioref"}). In fact, the errors committed by the mother and those of the child had different characteristics. Non-native input, however, may have a negative effect for deaf children acquiring sign language. In a study by [Lu et al. (2016)](#ch4.xhtml_ref4_60){#ch4.xhtml_R_ref4_60 .biblioref role="doc-biblioref"}, deaf children who received signed input from non-native, beginning signing parents had less-developed sign and phonological handshape repertoires than deaf children who received native signed input from deaf parents (see [Tang & Sze, 2019](#ch4.xhtml_ref4_93){#ch4.xhtml_R_ref4_93 .biblioref role="doc-biblioref"} for a review of bilingualism and sign language research).
:::

::: {.section aria-labelledby="sec4_4_2"}
### Amount of Input and Vocabulary Development {#ch4.xhtml_sec4_4_2}

With respect to the rate of vocabulary development, early studies found no differences between simultaneous bilingual children and monolingual children: both produced their first word and achieved the 50-word vocabulary milestone at the same time ([Petitto et al., 2001](#ch4.xhtml_ref4_81){#ch4.xhtml_R_ref4_81 .biblioref role="doc-biblioref"}; [Petitto & Kovelman, 2003](#ch4.xhtml_ref4_80){.biblioref role="doc-biblioref"}) and both displayed similar ranges of variation in vocabulary ([Pearson et al., 1993](#ch4.xhtml_ref4_78){#ch4.xhtml_R_ref4_78 .biblioref role="doc-biblioref"}). However, these studies were based on very small samples and subsequent work has reported compelling, differential findings (e.g., [Bialystok & Feng, 2011](#ch4.xhtml_ref4_12){#ch4.xhtml_R_ref4_12 .biblioref role="doc-biblioref"}; [Bialystok et al., 2010](#ch4.xhtml_ref4_11){.biblioref role="doc-biblioref"}). [Hoff et al. (2012)](#ch4.xhtml_ref4_49){.biblioref role="doc-biblioref"} compared the vocabulary development of simultaneous English--Spanish bilinguals and English monolinguals at ages 1;10, 2;1, and 2;6 using the English MacArthur-Bates Communicative Development Inventory ([Fenson et al., 1993](#ch4.xhtml_ref4_37){#ch4.xhtml_R_ref4_37 .biblioref role="doc-biblioref"}) and its Spanish equivalent, *El Inventario del Desarrollo de Habilidades Comunicativas* ([Jackson-Maldonado et al., 2003](#ch4.xhtml_ref4_54){#ch4.xhtml_R_ref4_54 .biblioref role="doc-biblioref"}). Overall, the results were two-fold: 1) monolingual children acquired vocabulary faster than children exposed to two languages; and 2) simultaneous bilingual children exhibited a rate of development in both languages that was a function of the relative amount of input received in each.

While [Hoff et al.'s (2012)](#ch4.xhtml_ref4_49){.biblioref role="doc-biblioref"} study confirmed findings from previous studies (i.e., that simultaneous bilingual children acquire each language within the normal range of variation as monolingual children; that the variation for the rate of language development is large; and that the distributions of single-language skills overlap for both monolingual and bilingual children) it did not support the notion that simultaneous bilingual children acquire both languages at the same pace as one. Partly explaining this finding were additional analyses examining the degree to which language input at home is equal or skewed toward one or the other language. Hoff et al. split the simultaneous bilingual group into three smaller groups, those whose input in English was 30% or less, between 50% and 60%, or 70% or more, and compared these to the monolingual group. The results showed that the monolingual group consistently outperformed the bilingual groups, including those who were exposed to 70% or more English input at home. These findings also suggest that balanced language input, at least for these very young children, does not imply that there is a similar rate of vocabulary acquisition as in monolingual children. Taken as a whole, Hoff et al.'s study demonstrates that vocabulary development is a predictable function of the relative amount of input in the given language(s).

[Hoff et al.'s (2012)](#ch4.xhtml_ref4_49){.biblioref role="doc-biblioref"} study suggested that for one- and two-year-old children, a balanced amount of input in two languages is not sufficient for vocabulary acquisition to match the rate of monolingual children. However, it has been argued that by the age of five, simultaneous bilinguals are able to perform within the range of their monolingual peers if they are exposed to 40--60% of input (Thordardottir, [2011](#ch4.xhtml_ref4_96){.biblioref role="doc-biblioref"}, [2015](#ch4.xhtml_ref4_98){.biblioref role="doc-biblioref"}). More recently, it was asked whether this requirement still holds at later ages. In other words, is dual input sufficient to sustain this equal performance over the years? In [Thordardottir (2019)](#ch4.xhtml_ref4_99){#ch4.xhtml_R_ref4_99 .biblioref role="doc-biblioref"}, the author compared a group of first-grade children to third-grade children who were enrolled in French-speaking schools in Montreal. []{#ch4.xhtml_p48 .pagebreak aria-label=" page 48. " role="doc-pagebreak"}All children were either simultaneous or sequential French--English bilinguals or French monolinguals. A battery of receptive and expressive vocabulary tasks was administered in French and English. The findings demonstrated that the amount of input (rather than the onset of exposure) was significantly related to vocabulary development. Simultaneous bilinguals performed slightly better than sequential bilinguals in the first grade, but this difference was less in third grade. Upon closer examination it was found that sequential bilinguals in first grade had a faster rate of vocabulary acquisition than sequential bilingual third graders. The implications of [Thordardottir's (2019)](#ch4.xhtml_ref4_99){.biblioref role="doc-biblioref"} study "call into question the traditional separation between simultaneous and sequential bilinguals and show that an early start of bilingualism does not in and of itself predict better performance or performance within the monolingual range" (p. 236) (see also [Unsworth, 2016](#ch4.xhtml_ref4_102){.biblioref role="doc-biblioref"}). Instead, it is the amount of input that drives the rate of acquisition (see also [Kaltsa et al., 2020](#ch4.xhtml_ref4_55){#ch4.xhtml_R_ref4_55 .biblioref role="doc-biblioref"} which discusses how syntactic development is affected by the quantity of input).
:::

::: {.section aria-labelledby="sec4_4_3"}
### Amount of Input and Grammatical Development {#ch4.xhtml_sec4_4_3}

The acquisition of grammatical gender has been among the most studied areas of language development in simultaneous bilinguals. As we saw with vocabulary, the amount of input is a key predictor of acquiring grammatical gender. [Unsworth (2013)](#ch4.xhtml_ref4_101){#ch4.xhtml_R_ref4_101 .biblioref role="doc-biblioref"} conducted a study investigating whether acquisition of grammatical gender in 136 simultaneous bilinguals, aged 3--17, is affected by current and prior amount of input. The results from the elicited production and grammaticality judgement tasks revealed a significant effect for amount of input on gender-marking performance. Furthermore, when matching this performance to cumulative length of exposure to input, there were no longer observable differences between the simultaneous bilinguals and a monolingual comparison group. The results underscore the importance of continuous input throughout childhood and adolescence. We will return to discuss this further in the section on heritage language and bilingual education.

In another study by [Unsworth et al. (2014)](#ch4.xhtml_ref4_103){#ch4.xhtml_R_ref4_103 .biblioref role="doc-biblioref"}, the authors examined the effects of the amount of input on the acquisition of grammatical gender in English--Greek and English--Dutch simultaneous bilinguals. Because of differences in how Greek and Dutch mark grammatical gender (i.e., Dutch lacks systematic cues while Greek's cues are abundant and transparent), the authors hypothesized that the amount of input would affect the two groups of simultaneous bilinguals to different degrees. This prediction was accurate: there were clear effects for quantity of input for both groups, but this was significantly less for English--Greek bilinguals. The authors argue that because grammatical gender in Dutch is opaquer than Greek, more input is required to acquire it. Similar findings underscoring the fact that the role of input in acquiring grammatical gender is not equal in all languages have been reported in other studies ([Ågren et al., 2014](#ch4.xhtml_ref4_5){#ch4.xhtml_R_ref4_5 .biblioref role="doc-biblioref"}; [Rodina & Westergaard, 2013](#ch4.xhtml_ref4_88){#ch4.xhtml_R_ref4_88 .biblioref role="doc-biblioref"}).

In a longitudinal study, [Granfeldt (2018)](#ch4.xhtml_ref4_45){#ch4.xhtml_R_ref4_45 .biblioref role="doc-biblioref"} examined how quantity and quality of input affected French gender assignment and gender concord among Swedish--French simultaneous and sequential bilinguals. Monolingual French children (N = 4), Swedish--French simultaneous bilingual children (N = 4), and Swedish--French sequential bilingual children (N = 4) were tested on several occasions during the period of two, and for some participants, three years. Individual input profiles that estimated the amount of input in French outside of school were created based on parental interviews. The results showed that the amount of input significantly correlated with the rate of development of gender assignment for monolinguals and simultaneous bilinguals but not for sequential bilinguals. Regarding gender concord, the results showed no differences between the monolinguals and the simultaneous bilingual groups, but error patterns were []{#ch4.xhtml_p49 .pagebreak aria-label=" page 49. " role="doc-pagebreak"}significantly different for the simultaneous and sequential bilinguals. Granfeldt argued that although the acquisition of gender assignment depends on the amount of input to which bilinguals are exposed, gender concord is modulated by a combination of factors including age of onset, the typical L1 acquisition timetable, and input.
:::
::::::

::::: {.section aria-labelledby="sec4_5"}
## Recommendations for Practice {#ch4.xhtml_sec4_5}

::: {.section aria-labelledby="sec4_5_1"}
### Caregivers {#ch4.xhtml_sec4_5_1}

Firstly, beyond the educational setting, parents and caregivers should be encouraged to be actively involved in their children's bilingual development. Often it is the case that one parent/caregiver provides input in Language X and the other provides input in Language Y. Unfortunately, this does not seem to be all that beneficial to bilingual development, particularly in families who live in a subtractive bilingual context and/or both parents/caregivers work full time ([MacLeod et al., 2013](#ch4.xhtml_ref4_61){#ch4.xhtml_R_ref4_61 .biblioref role="doc-biblioref"}). Instead, when possible, families should: 1) use the majority language less; 2) use the minority language more; 3) encourage siblings to continue using the minority language; and 4) increase reading and media exposure in the minority language ([MacLeod et al., 2013](#ch4.xhtml_ref4_61){.biblioref role="doc-biblioref"}).

The important role of the linguistic input given by caregivers to monolingual children is well documented ([Pungello et al., 2009](#ch4.xhtml_ref4_87){#ch4.xhtml_R_ref4_87 .biblioref role="doc-biblioref"}). In this large body of work, an often-studied variable, in addition to maternal educational level, that has been shown to modulate this input is socioeconomic status ([Ensminger & Fothergill, 2003](#ch4.xhtml_ref4_36){#ch4.xhtml_R_ref4_36 .biblioref role="doc-biblioref"}). For instance, a study by [Pan et al. (2005)](#ch4.xhtml_ref4_73){#ch4.xhtml_R_ref4_73 .biblioref role="doc-biblioref"} showed that caregiver socioeconomic status and vocabulary skills significantly predicted vocabulary development in monolingual children. For simultaneous bilingual children, the effects of caregiver speech have also been documented ([Hammer et al., 2012](#ch4.xhtml_ref4_47){#ch4.xhtml_R_ref4_47 .biblioref role="doc-biblioref"}). However, there may be differential effects from L1 vs. L2 input. This was shown in a study by [Buac et al. (2014)](#ch4.xhtml_ref4_16){#ch4.xhtml_R_ref4_16 .biblioref role="doc-biblioref"}, who examined vocabulary development in L1 Spanish and L2 English children ages 5--7. The results showed that caregivers' vocabulary knowledge and socioeconomic status were robust predictors of the children's L2, but not L1, vocabulary skills. These findings underscore just how instrumental input is *in both languages* for children growing up in bilingual situations. Caregivers should be encouraged to read *with* their children and not *to* their children in both languages whenever possible in an effort to foster development of literacy skills among both parties.
:::

::: {.section aria-labelledby="sec4_5_2"}
### The Heritage Language Classroom {#ch4.xhtml_sec4_5_2}

Many simultaneous and sequential bilinguals can be classified as *heritage learners* if their minority language is "part of a person's family or cultural heritage, regardless of the person's functional proficiency in the language and whether the language may have been spoken in the home" ([Potowski & Shin, 2019](#ch4.xhtml_ref4_86){#ch4.xhtml_R_ref4_86 .biblioref role="doc-biblioref"}, p. 673). Heritage learners are speakers of "ethnolinguistically minority languages who were exposed to the language in the family since childhood and as adults wish to learn, relearn, or improve their current level of linguistic proficiency in their family language" ([Montrul, 2010](#ch4.xhtml_ref4_65){#ch4.xhtml_R_ref4_65 .biblioref role="doc-biblioref"}, p. 3). Heritage language acquisition has recently been argued to have the potential to inform linguistic theories ([Scontras et al., 2015](#ch4.xhtml_ref4_91){#ch4.xhtml_R_ref4_91 .biblioref role="doc-biblioref"}), particularly those that are interested in the *poverty of the stimulus* question ([Lohndal et al., 2019](#ch4.xhtml_ref4_59){#ch4.xhtml_R_ref4_59 .biblioref role="doc-biblioref"}).

The uniqueness of their experience with language acquisition is clear when considering the fact that one language (i.e., the heritage one) is often the "first in order of acquisition but was not completely acquired because of the individual's switch to another dominant language" ([Polinsky & Kagan, 2007](#ch4.xhtml_ref4_85){#ch4.xhtml_R_ref4_85 .biblioref role="doc-biblioref"}, p. 369). Furthermore, heritage speakers often lack literacy skills in the []{#ch4.xhtml_p50 .pagebreak aria-label=" page 50. " role="doc-pagebreak"}heritage language. If a simultaneous or sequential bilingual is a *heritage language learner*, the pedagogical considerations should build on the unique strengths and challenges that may not be apparent in L2 learners. We have already mentioned that because input in each language is quantitatively reduced compared to L1 speakers, the role of input is especially important. [Benati and Schwieter (2019)](#ch4.xhtml_ref4_10){#ch4.xhtml_R_ref4_10 .biblioref role="doc-biblioref"} discuss the benefits of input flooding in the target language. An input flood provides comprehensible input that does not interrupt the flow of communication. Ideally for heritage language learners, this input should include syntactically complex utterances and a wide range of vocabulary ([Gámez & Levine, 2013](#ch4.xhtml_ref4_38){#ch4.xhtml_R_ref4_38 .biblioref role="doc-biblioref"}). A drawback of input flooding is that it may not be effective in increasing learners' knowledge of what is *not* possible in the target language. However, this should not be an issue for heritage language learners who have already developed the ability to naturally differentiate between what is and is not allowed in the target language.

Alongside this critical input should be opportunities to explore what has traditionally been defined as *language arts*. Language arts encompasses activities such as reading (non)fiction, writing in a range of genres, and visual literacy (see [Carreira & Chik, 2018](#ch4.xhtml_ref4_19){#ch4.xhtml_R_ref4_19 .biblioref role="doc-biblioref"}; [Potowski & Shin, 2019](#ch4.xhtml_ref4_86){.biblioref role="doc-biblioref"}; for further discussion). Activities with literacy can also help to compensate for lack of input outside of the classroom ([Kaltsa et al., 2020](#ch4.xhtml_ref4_55){.biblioref role="doc-biblioref"}). Ideally, the curriculum should be designed to meet heritage leaners' affective and academic needs through critical pedagogies and ethnographic community self-study ([Beaudrie et al., 2014](#ch4.xhtml_ref4_9){#ch4.xhtml_R_ref4_9 .biblioref role="doc-biblioref"}).

Regarding curriculum design, ongoing conversations should take place between teachers and administrators that bring to light the critical role of dual language input. [Hoff et al. (2012)](#ch4.xhtml_ref4_49){.biblioref role="doc-biblioref"} underscore the importance that educators realize that it is *typical* that language development for simultaneous bilingual children lags behind monolingual children and that with respect to assessment "a skill level in a single language is not the same indicator of ability for bilingual children that it is for monolingual children" (p. 15). Admittedly, however, many studies comparing monolingual and bilingual children with continuous vs. disrupted exposure to input have not fully accounted for the age ranges of the learners, a detail which should be considered in future research.
:::
:::::

::: {.section aria-labelledby="sec4_6"}
## Future Directions {#ch4.xhtml_sec4_6}

There is a disconnect between some of the theories of L2 acquisition we discussed in this chapter and their application to L1 acquisition and early bilingualism. Admittedly, there is very little research investigating these early forms of bilingual acquisition in the context of the IP framework. However, this lacuna should not exist: the role of input is essential in all forms of language acquisition and the question of how input is similarly or differently processed when acquiring one L1 vs. multiple languages from birth or soon after, is a critical issue to study. Ideally, although harder to come by, longitudinal studies should be conducted during a multiple-year period with the aim of comparing the role of input, how it is processed, and how these processes develop for different learning experiences (learning only L1 vs. simultaneous bilingualism vs. (early) sequential bilingualism vs. adult L2 acquisition).

There is still much to learn about the effects of the quantity, and particularly the quality of input on the linguistic development of one or more languages. At least two issues merit further research: how do the age ranges of monolingual and bilingual children modulate these effects? and how does continuous exposure vs. disruptions in exposure to input affect language acquisition? Furthermore, longitudinal studies are critically needed to understand how caregiver input changes over time as children grow older and their communicative demands change. Finally, an in-depth analysis is needed of the input that bilingual children receive in a heritage language compared to input available where that language is spoken as an L1. We hope that this chapter will serve as a spark to initiate these important enterprises.
:::

::: {.section .conclusion role="doc-conclusion"}
## []{#ch4.xhtml_p51 .pagebreak aria-label=" page 51. " role="doc-pagebreak"}Conclusion {#ch4.xhtml_sec4_7}

In this chapter we have reviewed the role of input and IP in L1 acquisition and early bilingualism. We have seen that not only does language acquisition crucially depend on input, but it is sensitive to quality and quantity characteristics. We discussed some prominent theories of IP that can inform our examination of input in language acquisition in general. Unfortunately, researchers focusing primarily on adult L2 sentence-level processing (as in the case of VanPatten's model of IP) do not typically address issues of IP in L1 and simultaneous bilingualism, and those working in L1 contexts do not tend to investigate L1 acquisition from an IP perspective. Those focusing on sentence-level L2 input processing (and any level of IP in L2 for that matter) can gain from a better understanding and an up-to-date review of research and theoretical advances on L1 IP among children. We believe that these field-related limitations related to scope highlight the value of the issues discussed in this chapter and hope that future research can bridge this gap.[^6]
:::

::: {.endnotes role="doc-endnotes"}
## Note {#ch4.xhtml_h2}
