::: {}
# []{#ch5.xhtml_p59 .pagebreak aria-label=" page 59. " role="doc-pagebreak"}[5 ]{.chnum .ordinal}[Input Processing in Generative Second Language Acquisition]{.chtitle} {#ch5.xhtml_h1}

Laurent Dekydtspotter

Indiana University

DOI: [10.4324/9781003041412-7](https://dx.doi.org/10.4324/9781003041412-7){aria-label="D.O.I. link to this document."}
:::

::: {.section .introduction role="doc-introduction"}
## Introduction and Definitions {#ch5.xhtml_sec5_1}

Input processing (IP) in second language acquisition (SLA) (VanPatten, [1996](#ch5.xhtml_ref5_46){#ch5.xhtml_R_ref5_46 .biblioref role="doc-biblioref"}, [2002](#ch5.xhtml_ref5_47){#ch5.xhtml_R_ref5_47 .biblioref role="doc-biblioref"}, [2004](#ch5.xhtml_ref5_48){#ch5.xhtml_R_ref5_48 .biblioref role="doc-biblioref"}, [2020](#ch5.xhtml_ref5_49){#ch5.xhtml_R_ref5_49 .biblioref role="doc-biblioref"}; [VanPatten & Cadierno, 1993](#ch5.xhtml_ref5_50){#ch5.xhtml_R_ref5_50 .biblioref role="doc-biblioref"}; [VanPatten & Wong, 2004](#ch5.xhtml_ref5_51){#ch5.xhtml_R_ref5_51 .biblioref role="doc-biblioref"}; inter alia) characterizes the real-time processing of nonnative language input leading to the acquisition of the specifications for the second language (L2) grammar. IP relies on the notion of intake as "that subset of the input that has been processed in working memory and made available to further processing (i.e., possible incorporation into the developing system)" ([VanPatten, 2004](#ch5.xhtml_ref5_48){.biblioref role="doc-biblioref"}, 6--7). [VanPatten (1996)](#ch5.xhtml_ref5_46){.biblioref role="doc-biblioref"} presents IP as complementary to the goal of developing a theory of L2 representations in generative SLA, with the first language (L1) grammar determining initial assumptions and general grammatical constraints guiding structuring and reanalysis, as in Schwartz and Sprouse ([1994](#ch5.xhtml_ref5_42){#ch5.xhtml_R_ref5_42 .biblioref role="doc-biblioref"}, [1996](#ch5.xhtml_ref5_43){#ch5.xhtml_R_ref5_43 .biblioref role="doc-biblioref"}). [VanPatten (1996)](#ch5.xhtml_ref5_46){.biblioref role="doc-biblioref"} discusses three sets of processes in SLA: i) extraction of intake from input, ii) accommodation of intake in grammatical development, and iii) access to the interlanguage knowledge in nonnative language use (see Leeser, this volume, for a detailed discussion of VanPatten's IP model).

Both *Universal Grammar (UG)*, which refers to the built-in capacity or knowledge source specific to language, and the L1 grammar constitute sources of information for IP in extracting intake from input but also for accommodating intake. As [VanPatten (2002, p. 763)](#ch5.xhtml_ref5_47){.biblioref role="doc-biblioref"} noted, "Future research will no doubt add to the current model of IP or push for alterations in it, especially as parsing is incorporated into any account of IP." This chapter argues that L2 grammar acquisition as *implicit learning* or learning below any level of awareness as a by-product of incremental comprehension, directly involves the *parser*, which is the component of the system that processes the structure of the input. Implicit learning in SLA arises as a by-product of the real-time analysis of input when UG-constrained specifications beyond those licensed by the current L1-based grammar are called upon to analyze the L2 input. In the most economical model, parsing involves the direct use of the grammatical system in real-time structure construction. Thus, SLA arises during IP when parsing induces updates to the grammatical specifications deployed in real-time structure construction.

This model places intake extraction, accommodation, and access within the parsing procedure in the face of target language (TL) input not licensed by current L1-based specifications. []{#ch5.xhtml_p60 .pagebreak aria-label=" page 60. " role="doc-pagebreak"}It fits well with minimalist language design in which grammatical processing involves the selection of basic grammatical elements into a temporary working memory space where these are combined. UG-based updates to stored specifications for the TL constitute the most minimal approach to learning. Greater efficiency is achieved when the updated specifications can be quickly selected into the working memory space where they are efficiently combined into a grammatical structure that fully represents the input. Implicit language learning as parser update is consistent with grammatical acquisition as a complex cognitive process in which the data provided by the input select specifications among the possibilities determined by UG ([Chomsky, 1975](#ch5.xhtml_ref5_8){#ch5.xhtml_R_ref5_8 .biblioref role="doc-biblioref"}). Hence, language acquisition can be reduced to the selection of those UG options that best characterize the input during sentence processing. This is also the case in SLA, except that the specifications for the L1 constitute the starting point of language learning (Schwartz & Sprouse, [1994](#ch5.xhtml_ref5_42){.biblioref role="doc-biblioref"}, [1996](#ch5.xhtml_ref5_43){.biblioref role="doc-biblioref"}).

[VanPatten's (1996)](#ch5.xhtml_ref5_46){.biblioref role="doc-biblioref"} IP-induced acquisition model is reconsidered in light of the parser as language acquisition device ([Fodor, 1998](#ch5.xhtml_ref5_22){#ch5.xhtml_R_ref5_22 .biblioref role="doc-biblioref"}). Implicit L2 learning is discussed as an update to L1-based stored inventories for semantics, phonology, and syntax. Computational principles make it the smallest update to account for the input at hand. Language acquisition as parser update requires i) structuring based on specifications stored in inventories together with access to a larger universal store when these fail, ii) updating these inventories with the representations that enable an analysis of the TL input, and iii) accessing these representations within a narrow processing window. Approaches to implicit learning might, however, be as elegant as possible, deploying the most economical system consistent with the evidence. SLA arises as a by-product of the real-time analysis of input. The remainder of this chapter documents acquisition as the selection of UG-licensed options as part of the parsing procedure within the limits of working memory. It argues that the parsing procedure alone suffices to deliver grammatical learning and highlights the repercussions of this view of learning in IP to enhance SLA.
:::

::: {.section aria-labelledby="sec5_2"}
## Historical Overview {#ch5.xhtml_sec5_2}

[VanPatten's (1996)](#ch5.xhtml_ref5_46){.biblioref role="doc-biblioref"} seminal work on IP coincided with the development of a body of research on nonnative language processing within the generative paradigm (Juffs & Harrington, [1995](#ch5.xhtml_ref5_32){#ch5.xhtml_R_ref5_32 .biblioref role="doc-biblioref"}, [1996](#ch5.xhtml_ref5_33){#ch5.xhtml_R_ref5_33 .biblioref role="doc-biblioref"}). This early research identified processing factors as potential explanations for nonnative behavior. Juffs and Harrington ([1995](#ch5.xhtml_ref5_32){.biblioref role="doc-biblioref"}, [1996](#ch5.xhtml_ref5_33){.biblioref role="doc-biblioref"}) demonstrated that nonnative users processed more slowly and had trouble with revisions whereby parsing may not always lead to native-like representations. The ability of nonnative speakers to analyze input in real-time has since been put into question. Nonnative speakers' real-time processing has been claimed to lack syntactic details and to over-rely on lexical and semantic information (Clahsen & Felser, [2006a](#ch5.xhtml_ref5_9){#ch5.xhtml_R_ref5_9 .biblioref role="doc-biblioref"}, [2006b](#ch5.xhtml_ref5_10){#ch5.xhtml_R_ref5_10 .biblioref role="doc-biblioref"}, [2018](#ch5.xhtml_ref5_11){#ch5.xhtml_R_ref5_11 .biblioref role="doc-biblioref"}). However, this characterization has also been put into question. Cunnings ([2017a](#ch5.xhtml_ref5_13){#ch5.xhtml_R_ref5_13 .biblioref role="doc-biblioref"}, [2017b](#ch5.xhtml_ref5_14){#ch5.xhtml_R_ref5_14 .biblioref role="doc-biblioref"}) argues for a memory-based account of L2 processing difficulty, claiming detailed structuring. In parsing-induced acquisition as in IP, the failure to attend to structure in L2 input beyond L1 specifications would not result in the intake of new grammatical properties. Whenever lexically dependent processing rather than a structurally dependent processing for interpretation is primary, the triggers in the form of UG-guided detailed representations of input (i.e., the intake requiring accommodation) for grammatical learning might not be present. The ability to extract intake relies on nonnative speakers' ability to build structural representations of the input.

The significance of parsing in grammatical development has long been acknowledged in the theoretical literature ([Clark & Roberts 1993](#ch5.xhtml_ref5_12){#ch5.xhtml_R_ref5_12 .biblioref role="doc-biblioref"}), but parser-grammar relations can be instantiated in many ways. The notion of parser as language acquisition device also arose within a similar []{#ch5.xhtml_p61 .pagebreak aria-label=" page 61. " role="doc-pagebreak"}time frame. [Fodor (1998)](#ch5.xhtml_ref5_22){.biblioref role="doc-biblioref"} identified the parser as the learning mechanism. The parser involves both a universal store and a local store for grammatical specifications. Structures are selected from the universal store entering the local store when the structures specified for the current grammar fail. This mechanism allows the parser to function as the learning device. [Dekydtspotter and Renaud (2014)](#ch5.xhtml_ref5_18){#ch5.xhtml_R_ref5_18 .biblioref role="doc-biblioref"} then introduced the *parser as language acquisition device in SLA* as the reassembly of features into new specifications for the L2 ([Lardiere, 2009](#ch5.xhtml_ref5_34){#ch5.xhtml_R_ref5_34 .biblioref role="doc-biblioref"}) when L1-based specifications fail instantiating the full transfer/full access model (Schwartz & Sprouse, [1994](#ch5.xhtml_ref5_42){.biblioref role="doc-biblioref"}, [1996](#ch5.xhtml_ref5_43){.biblioref role="doc-biblioref"}). In this approach, the extraction of (currently unlicensed) structures from L2 input, the accommodation of these new structures through updated specifications for the language, and access to these specifications in processing all involve the parsing procedure. These steps (intake extraction, accommodation, and access) arise as the parser encounters TL input containing learning triggers. Crucially, parsing-induced acquisition must also involve a narrow processing window corresponding to the real-time activation of specifications for the language into a working memory space for processing. Retrieving information for a nonnative language is widely thought as more effortful (i.e., requiring greater neuronal resources than is the case for L1). As a result, weakly activated representations for the nonnative language input are lost more quickly as activation decays ([Lim & Christianson, 2013](#ch5.xhtml_ref5_35){#ch5.xhtml_R_ref5_35 .biblioref role="doc-biblioref"}). The ability to retrieve and then maintain recently acquired L2 representations affects performance in SLA. Mitigating memory trace decay should therefore be of central interest for the purpose of enhancing performance.
:::

:::::: {.section aria-labelledby="sec5_3"}
## Critical Issues and Topics {#ch5.xhtml_sec5_3}

::: {.section aria-labelledby="sec5_3_1"}
### Can Learners Process Input Deeply? {#ch5.xhtml_sec5_3_1}

Input-driven processes in SLA assumes the ability to structure nonnative language input in real time, which is still highly contentious (Clahsen & Felser, [2006a](#ch5.xhtml_ref5_9){.biblioref role="doc-biblioref"}, [2006b](#ch5.xhtml_ref5_10){.biblioref role="doc-biblioref"}, [2018](#ch5.xhtml_ref5_11){.biblioref role="doc-biblioref"}). Experimental research on non-native processing reveals that different tasks affect outcomes in this regard (see [Lim & Christianson, 2013](#ch5.xhtml_ref5_35){.biblioref role="doc-biblioref"}). Hence, tasks that focus resources on structural properties may divert resources to structuring or, assuming structuring to be a basic process, the maintenance of structural representations in working memory. In [Lim and Christianson's (2013)](#ch5.xhtml_ref5_35){.biblioref role="doc-biblioref"} model, sentence processing involves two separate processing streams reconciled in real time. The first processing stream deploys heuristic strategies such as a linear N--V--N sequence together with an agent interpretation of the first noun phrase. The second processing stream fully deploys structural knowledge, which enables a check of the interpretation obtained heuristically. Native and nonnative processing differences are due to a nonnative speakers' diminished ability to reconcile the two streams in real time. Hence, according to this view, structural construction constitutes a basic process that is always present, but the task affects the reconciliation of structural and heuristic processing streams. This is because a task may require a greater focus on structure. Effects of tasks in processing outcomes are of potential great significance for IP and for the manipulation of input to enhance learning. In support of a basic structuring process in SLA, Cunnings ([2017a](#ch5.xhtml_ref5_13){.biblioref role="doc-biblioref"}, [2017b](#ch5.xhtml_ref5_14){.biblioref role="doc-biblioref"}) also argues that native and nonnative speakers (NNS) have a similar ability to generate structures, but NNS exhibit greater sensitivity to interference in the retrieval of information from working memory. Issues of lexical access and retrieval have become central to the debate on nonnative processing. Effortful lexical access for a nonnative language can have downstream effects on processing capacity ([Miller, 2014](#ch5.xhtml_ref5_38){#ch5.xhtml_R_ref5_38 .biblioref role="doc-biblioref"}; [Hopp, 2016](#ch5.xhtml_ref5_31){#ch5.xhtml_R_ref5_31 .biblioref role="doc-biblioref"}). As a result, learners' ability to maintain weakly activated representations after effortful lexical access constitutes a fundamental issue in nonnative language processing.
:::

::: {.section aria-labelledby="sec5_3_2"}
### []{#ch5.xhtml_p62 .pagebreak aria-label=" page 62. " role="doc-pagebreak"}What Is the Role of UG? {#ch5.xhtml_sec5_3_2}

In IP, UG and the L1 grammar constitute information sources for deriving intake from input and for accommodating intake. In minimalist approaches to grammar, UG refers to the computational capacity for language. The brain has the general capacity to generate, search, access, retrieve, hold, and match features (i.e., basic cognitive properties) across domains. These domain-general processes apply to basic grammatical elements according to general principles to produce domain-specific language representations. This defines an epistemological space for language. Crucially, the computational capacity for language enables analyses of the TL input beyond what L1 specifications license. The failure of current grammatical specifications to license a necessary analysis of the TL input in sentence processing, therefore, induces acquisition ([Clark & Roberts 1993](#ch5.xhtml_ref5_12){.biblioref role="doc-biblioref"}; inter alia). Failure-induced learning is compatible with any IP-based model of language development. However, seeking the most economical approach, [Dekydtspotter and Renaud (2014)](#ch5.xhtml_ref5_18){.biblioref role="doc-biblioref"} discuss L2 acquisition as a direct byproduct of UG-constrained parsing. Irrespective of whether language acquisition arises during parsing, the real-time processing of input with access to UG-constrained representations plays a significant part in grammatical acquisition.

The evidence for UG guidance in SLA is significant: constraints on interpretations and structures arise in L2 learners when these constraints are neither found in the L1 grammar nor directly instantiated in the input or instructed ([White, 2003](#ch5.xhtml_ref5_53){#ch5.xhtml_R_ref5_53 .biblioref role="doc-biblioref"}). Such constraints must thus be inherent in the implicit learning mechanism. In IP-based SLA, learning arises as L2 input is processed for its propositional content. IP extracts meaning-carrying structural representations. And indeed, implicit knowledge of possible meanings develops despite impoverished speech situations. L1-English L2-French learners acquire restrictions on the meaning of discontinuous vs. continuous interrogative structures. In a discontinuous structure, a question word is separated from its nominal restriction as in *combien...de livres* 'how many...(of) books' ([Dekydtspotter et al., 2001](#ch5.xhtml_ref5_16){#ch5.xhtml_R_ref5_16 .biblioref role="doc-biblioref"}). These discontinuous interrogatives do not have the same set of interpretations as English-like continuous interrogatives *combien de livres* 'how many (of) books.' When the question word is separated from its nominal restriction, a more limited set of interrogative meanings is possible. To learn the set of possible meanings through observation of interrogative--response pairs, a learner would need to know what question was intended by a particular interrogative string and whether the response constituted a true answer to this question. This procedure would require knowing everything about the speech situation including the interlocutors' states of mind. This constitutes an impossible task: information questions are asked when there is lack of knowledge. The meanings of questions, therefore, are much more likely to inhere in an implicit ability to assign an interpretation to language structures in real time rather than to be inferred from observations of interrogative--answer pairs.

In IP-based SLA, learning arises as language input is structurally processed. Implicit constraints on processes not found in the native language support the view of a basic UG-guided structuring process. L2 learners of English across L1s backgrounds show knowledge of contrasts between weak island violations (e.g.,? *\*Which book did John wonder when Bill bought*) and strong islands that are experienced as more severe violations (e.g., *\*\*Which book did John laugh when Bill read?*) ([Martohardjono, 1993](#ch5.xhtml_ref5_36){#ch5.xhtml_R_ref5_36 .biblioref role="doc-biblioref"}). These contrasts in ungrammaticality found across native and nonnative groups are not in the TL input and they are not taught. They rather inhere in the learner's ability to structure input and appear when a language deploys movement.

L2 learners also demonstrate knowledge of constraints on sound structure despite a lack of orthographic cues, explicit instruction, and reduced input. Thus, L1-English L2-Turkish learners attune to the canonical distribution of lateral sounds. In this distribution, light, non-velarized []{#ch5.xhtml_p63 .pagebreak aria-label=" page 63. " role="doc-pagebreak"}/l/ as in *il-e* \[ile\] 'city.DAT' and dark, velarized /ɫ/ laterals as in *kol-a* \[koɫa\] 'arm-dat.' harmonize with the front and back vowels of word stems and suffixes. Crucially, hearing very few non-canonical words such as *petrol* 'petroleum' with a light /l/ and a back vowel /o/ in the word, learners know that the dative affix added to *petrol* must be -*e* and not -*a*. Knowledge that /l/ following a back vowel blocks vowel harmony extends to newly created pseudo-words ([Özcelik & Sprouse, 2017](#ch5.xhtml_ref5_39){#ch5.xhtml_R_ref5_39 .biblioref role="doc-biblioref"}). Such pattern knowledge without direct evidence also supports the implicit extraction of abstract representations. This again seems best characterized by a model in which IP in SLA follows L1 patterns while adapting to nonnative language input.
:::

::: {.section aria-labelledby="sec5_3_3"}
### What Is Intake /How Is It Extracted / When Is It Accommodated? {#ch5.xhtml_sec5_3_3}

Generative SLA offers a precise characterization of intake and accommodation in terms of the ability to build structural representations. *Intake* is the abstract representation of the input obtained in parsing. It consists of syntactic, semantic, and morpho-phonological representations. These are constituted by sets of syntactic, semantic, and phonological features, basic cognitive properties relevant to each subdomain of language. Grammatical features include Person, Gender, Number, Animacy, Tense, Aspect, Mood, among many others. Intake includes learning triggers. These consist of those feature sets necessary to represent the input that are not already part of the syntactic, semantic, or morpho-phonological inventories for the L2. Implicit learning thus relies on the extraction of basic grammatical representations constituted of feature sets during the real-time analysis of nonnative input. Without the ability to fully represent the TL, there is no relevant intake that can trigger implicit learning of all aspects of the TL as in IP. The L2 learner would be insensitive to parts of the TL input. This is true whether the property in question consists of a double articulation on a consonant, a tone on a vowel, or an agreeing gender feature on a verb. Hence, sound structure acquisition can be seen as consisting in an update to stored abstract phonological specifications for the L2, as new feature sets must be accommodated when necessary. Crucially, not all intake opportunities induce processing system update in IP.

Learning triggers require the identification of properties that the current system does not license. However, not all input provides learning triggers. The general problem of the extraction of learning triggers is very well illustrated by the problem of assimilation of distinct L2 sounds to a single phoneme in categorical perception ([Best, 1995](#ch5.xhtml_ref5_3){#ch5.xhtml_R_ref5_3 .biblioref role="doc-biblioref"}). Categorical perception involves the ability to view certain items but not others as part of the same set, so that these specific items are treated as tokens of a type. It is established very early in life and difficult to change later in life. However, [Brown (2000)](#ch5.xhtml_ref5_5){#ch5.xhtml_R_ref5_5 .biblioref role="doc-biblioref"} argues that phonological acquisition is best captured in the mapping from acoustics to the feature organization, rather than purely phonemic categorization.

[Darcy et al. (2012)](#ch5.xhtml_ref5_15){#ch5.xhtml_R_ref5_15 .biblioref role="doc-biblioref"} consider the ability to extract feature-based round vowel contrasts from the input in the acquisition of lexical items. They examined the processing of lexical items contrasting front-rounded /y/ vs. back-rounded /u/ (e.g., *la rue* 'the street' vs. *la roue* 'the wheel') and front-rounded /œ/ vs. back-rounded /ɔ/ contrasts (e.g., *le cœur* /lœ kœʁ/ 'the heart' vs. *le corps* /lœ kɔʁ/ 'the body') in the absence of robust categorization. Thus, the front-rounded /y/ vowel can be represented with the set: {\[vocalic\], \[labial\], \[dorsal\], \[high\]}, with labial accounting for lip-rounding, \[dorsal\] accounting for constriction due to the back of the tongue, and \[high\] accounting for the high position of the tip of the tongue. [Darcy et al. (2012)](#ch5.xhtml_ref5_15){.biblioref role="doc-biblioref"} showed L1-English L2-French learners merge \[y\] and \[u\] into a single {\[dorsal\], \[high\]} feature representation in lexical retrieval in early to intermediate stages of acquisition. /y/ vs. /u/ lexical contrasts are acquired by advanced learners of French. Intermediate learners of French robustly process mid rounded []{#ch5.xhtml_p64 .pagebreak aria-label=" page 64. " role="doc-pagebreak"}vowel contrasts in lexical retrieval of *le cœur* /lœ kœʁ/ 'the heart' vs. *le corps* /lœ kɔʁ/ 'the body'. Thus, acoustic properties mapping onto {\[labial\], \[coronal\]} feature combinations trigger a phonemic inventory update in L1-English L2-French acquisition. However, a feature-based account predicts mid vowels lexical contrasts to arise before high vowel lexical contrasts: the feature set {\[labial\], \[coronal\]} for mid vowel /œ/ (without specification for tongue height) is established sooner than the feature set{\[labial\], \[coronal\], \[high\]} for high vowel /y/ ([Darcy et al., 2012](#ch5.xhtml_ref5_15){.biblioref role="doc-biblioref"}). Updated feature-set contrasts {\[labial\], \[coronal\]} for /œ/ vs. {\[labial\], \[dorsal\]} for /ɔ/ and {\[labial\], \[coronal\], \[high\]} for /y/ vs. {\[labial\], \[dorsal\], \[high\]} for /u/ determine a new phonemic inventory with mid and high rounded vowels for the L2. These phonemic contrasts require a perceptual category space that reflects lexical distinctions. Learner's categorization of sounds in nonwords, however, minimally changed in response to phonological development.

If the L1-English feature representations and sound categorization completely determined the set of detectable representations (i.e., the intake), L1-English L2-French learners would experience an irrecoverable mental insensitivity to the lexically encoded phonological /y/ vs. /u/ contrast (cf. [Brown (2000)](#ch5.xhtml_ref5_5){.biblioref role="doc-biblioref"}). Lexical distinctions that carry meaning differences through sounds would not be learnable. Intake, therefore, consists in extracted feature sets. Phonological features have stable acoustic properties in the input and enable word learning. Still, not all encounters with the TL provide triggers for learning. French input such as *la rue* 'the street' provides for a {\[labial\], \[coronal\], \[high\]} feature representation for \[y\] together with the \[dorsal\] sound /ʁ/. In such cases, the anteriority of the rounded \[y\] phone cannot be due to its \[dorsal\] immediate context. Hence, detecting \[labial\]+\[coronal\] in a \[dorsal\] context requires accessing the {\[labial\], \[coronal\], \[high\]} representation for the vowel. The English phonemic inventory excludes this representation, because \[labial\] is not a contrastive feature on English vowels. This representation in the right context constitutes a trigger. The processing of *la rue* 'the street' for instance therefore generates a \[labial\]+\[coronal\] trigger for the acquisition of a new phoneme inventory that licenses both \[labial\]+\[coronal\] vs. \[labial\]+\[dorsal\] representations. The nonnative acquisition of rounded vowels lexical contrast makes it clear that features constitute the ingredients of language processing and acquisition. Crucially, feature set: {\[labial\], \[coronal\]} constitutes a trigger only when \[coronal\] cannot be due to the environment but must inhere in the representation of the vowel.
:::
::::::

::::::::: {.section aria-labelledby="sec5_4"}
## Current Contributions and Research {#ch5.xhtml_sec5_4}

::: {.section aria-labelledby="sec5_4_1"}
### The Parser as the Learning Mechanism {#ch5.xhtml_sec5_4_1}

Syntactic processing involves grammatical features, principles guiding structure generation, and inventoried specifications for the language. Syntactic processing involves selecting basic grammatical elements from the lexicon into a working memory space for syntactic processing. When L1-based grammatical specifications fail to allow the structuring of the L2 input, grammatical specifications enabling the representation of TL input are selected among UG-licensed possibilities into the syntactic working memory space. The least change of specifications consistent with the representation of the input at hand is made by retrieving relevant features and/or feature values from the UG store to be combined. The specifications enabling a parse immediately enter the L2 lexicon updating the parser.

However, efficient target-like grammatical processing for the L2 becomes achievable only when the newly stored updated specifications can be efficiently selected out of the L2 lexicon into the working memory space for syntactic processing. Indeed, laboratory evidence shows that cognate and frequent vocabulary enhances nonnative grammatical processing ([Miller, 2014](#ch5.xhtml_ref5_38){.biblioref role="doc-biblioref"}; []{#ch5.xhtml_p65 .pagebreak aria-label=" page 65. " role="doc-pagebreak"}[Hopp, 2016](#ch5.xhtml_ref5_31){.biblioref role="doc-biblioref"}) consistent with the model. [Paradis (2004)](#ch5.xhtml_ref5_40){#ch5.xhtml_R_ref5_40 .biblioref role="doc-biblioref"} notes that activation requires "a sufficient amount of positive neural impulses" (p. 28), lowered and maintained by continued stimulation. In coactivation of grammatical specifications, those specifications that require the least neurocognitive resources are advantaged. However, as the TL input is parsed, adequate specifications are strengthened and inadequate specifications weakened, so that they fall out of consideration. Strengthening requires multiple access to new specifications, lowering the resources needed for retrieval.

This view of implicit learning as UG-based parser update in the task of processing fits within the minimalist approach to the language faculty. The universal processing capacity for language and inventoried specifications for the language are necessary for parsing to effect language learning. This view of learning as parser update inherent in the parsing procedure contrasts with coalition-based approaches to L2 learning ([Carroll, 1999](#ch5.xhtml_ref5_6){#ch5.xhtml_R_ref5_6 .biblioref role="doc-biblioref"}, [2001](#ch5.xhtml_ref5_7){#ch5.xhtml_R_ref5_7 .biblioref role="doc-biblioref"}; [Herschensohn, 2003](#ch5.xhtml_ref5_29){#ch5.xhtml_R_ref5_29 .biblioref role="doc-biblioref"}), but fits the IP-approach to implicit learning.
:::

::: {.section aria-labelledby="sec5_4_2"}
### Input Structuring and the Development of L2 Grammars {#ch5.xhtml_sec5_4_2}

Learning as parser update addresses the delayed growth of grammatical knowledge relative to the immediacy of parsing. Thus, L2 grammatical acquisition suggests periods of relative stability in, for instance, word order, punctuated by abrupt changes. [Schwartz and Sprouse (1994)](#ch5.xhtml_ref5_42){.biblioref role="doc-biblioref"} document stages in L1-Turkish L2-German acquisition, Turkish is a subject (S)--object (O)--verb (V) language. In German, finite verbs appear sentence finally in embedded clauses, but in second position in matrix clauses. Examining the acquisition of German by Cevdet, a Turkish migrant worker, Schwartz and Sprouse highlighted three stages in verb placement beyond the initial Turkish verb-final placement: "At stage 1 \[22 October 1982 through 10 February 1983\], Cevdet's grammar does not allow for post-verbal subjects at all. At stage 2 \[9 June 1983 through 16 May 1984\], post-verbal subjects are permitted, but only if they are pronominal. At stage 3 \[by 5 August 1984\], post-verbal subject may be pronominal or nonpronominal" (p. 340). Cevdet did not generalize to the (entire) class of subjects, nominative case-marked expressions. A stage 2 grammar reveals a XVS pattern exhibiting verb movement past the subject into the left-edge of the clause, limited to subject pronouns. Schwartz and Sprouse note that the VS\[+pronominal\] order in the absence of VS\[-pronominal\] order is found in French: *Vient-elle?* 'comes-she' for "Is she coming?" vs. *\*Vient Marie?* 'comes Marie' for "Is Marie coming?".

This change in L2 grammar is surprising if learning involves a goodness-of-fit generalization over stored instances given that the class of subjects constitutes the best fit for the XVS pattern in German input to account for all cases. From the standpoint of parsing guided by derivational economy and the frequency of pronouns, however, the \[± pronominal\] distinction makes perfect sense. A French-like pronoun cliticization analysis, whenever a postverbal pronoun is encountered, constitutes the smallest change from Stage 1 specifications, even if the change is globally non-target-like. This change updates the prior grammatical state to account for a sizable portion of the verb-subject word order input, but not all. Crucially, the change in grammatical state occurs only when the new specifications for the TL can be efficiently selected for structuring within a narrow processing window. The cliticization specification for more frequent pronouns achieves a lower deployment threshold prior to the less frequent input for noun phrases. However, VS\[-pronominal\] input requires a target-like verb-second specifications. Once its deployment is robustly enabled, it is supported by all VS input. The effect of IP on L2 production is, therefore, not immediately visible. It reflects activation thresholds in the deployment of stored knowledge, but the effect of the processing system is nevertheless reflected in acquisition order.
:::

::: {.section aria-labelledby="sec5_4_3"}
### []{#ch5.xhtml_p66 .pagebreak aria-label=" page 66. " role="doc-pagebreak"}The Significance of Contrast {#ch5.xhtml_sec5_4_3}

In IP, contrasts between forms that convey distinct meanings play a significant role. Learning requires encoding new contrasts and letting go of others. Grammatical knowledge is encoded in contrastive features. Therefore, contrasts encoded in feature sets are indeed central to IP. Thus, in French for instance, the gender system is maintained by contrasting determiners. The presentation of French input to enhance contrasts between determiners facilitates acquisition in IP. [Dekydtspotter and Renaud (2014)](#ch5.xhtml_ref5_18){.biblioref role="doc-biblioref"} discussed the acquisition of grammatical gender contrast using the distribution of definite determiners in L1-English L2-French acquisition. Given structural licensing by the lexical specifications for the language in parsing, L1-English learners expected a single \[definite\] determiner in French in advance of exposure as a counterpart to English *the* in a relabeling step ([Sprouse, 2006](#ch5.xhtml_ref5_45){#ch5.xhtml_R_ref5_45 .biblioref role="doc-biblioref"}). The French article *le* "the-masc" of category D(eterminer) might first be identified as {D, \[definite\]} without further specification. As article *la* "the-fem" is encountered in its function as a definite determiner, the search for the feature \[definite\] finds the specifications for *le*. This brings about the alternation between *le* "the-masc" and *la* "the-fem" into the focus of attention. Parsing seeks a characterization of the *la* vs. *le* contrast as alternative realizations of \[definite\], with the default (\[-Plural\]) value underspecified as \[Number: Ø\]. The search for values is mediated by a local search in syntax guided by a feature hierarchy including Animacy with \[± Human\] values and/or Gender with \[±Feminine\] values. \[±Feminine\] is a necessary feature of French nouns. Relatively few vocabulary items are required to establish \[±Feminine\] gender values, in which \[−Feminine\] is underspecified as \[Gender: Ø\] with no further specifications by representational economy. French input with inanimate gendered nouns such as *la table* "the-fem table-fem" demands decoupling \[Gender\] from \[Animacy\] in the interpretation of nouns, since the referent for *la table* "the-fem table-fem" must be referred to with a feminine pronoun, indicating a feminine-marked referent. Crucially, gender agreement as in *la table* "the-fem table-fem" also requires an uninterpretable *u*Gender feature on the determiner receiving values via local search, abstracting away from number. As highlighted in the processing instruction literature, the notion of contrast is crucial. In the proposed model of IP, the licensing requirement will establish a contrast between the bundle {D, \[definite\], \[*u*Gender\]} for the masculine determiner *le* "the-masc", where the absence of Feminine value is construed as \[-Feminine\], and the bundle {D, \[definite\], \[*u*Gender: Feminine\]} for the feminine determiner *la* "the-fem". Grammatical contrast is central to the establishment of target-like specifications.

The acquisition of new feature bundles does not guarantee target-like production. Staying with grammatical gender, gender assignment to nominal concepts is influenced by certain phonological biases with incorrect results for specific words. Notwithstanding the need for deactivation of the coactivated L1 in SLA ([Blumenfeld & Marian, 2013](#ch5.xhtml_ref5_4){#ch5.xhtml_R_ref5_4 .biblioref role="doc-biblioref"}), more onerous access to lexical specifications for the nonnative language induces weakly activated representations, given limited resources. Processing capacity is reduced as the processing system maintains weakly activated representations based on lexical specifications at the possible expense of additional processes of interpretation ([Dekydtspotter et al., 2018](#ch5.xhtml_ref5_17){#ch5.xhtml_R_ref5_17 .biblioref role="doc-biblioref"}). Hence, in the externalization of abstract syntactic computations for French, the underspecified masculine form *le* with specifications {D, \[definite\], \[*u*Gender\]} may still often be associated with (abstract) syntactic computations involving \[Feminine\]. The specification {D, \[definite\], \[*u*Gender\]} for the underspecified masculine form *le* does not directly clash with specifications {D, \[definite\], \[*u*Gender: Feminine\]} in syntactic computations ([McCarthy, 2008](#ch5.xhtml_ref5_37){#ch5.xhtml_R_ref5_37 .biblioref role="doc-biblioref"}).
:::

::: {.section aria-labelledby="sec5_4_4"}
### []{#ch5.xhtml_p67 .pagebreak aria-label=" page 67. " role="doc-pagebreak"}Computational Principles in Parsing and Learning {#ch5.xhtml_sec5_4_4}

Parsing strategies in IP result from the interaction of general principles with specifications for each language ([Fodor, 1998](#ch5.xhtml_ref5_22){.biblioref role="doc-biblioref"}; [Fodor & Inoue, 2000](#ch5.xhtml_ref5_23){#ch5.xhtml_R_ref5_23 .biblioref role="doc-biblioref"}). Parsing is guided by computational economy: The solution to the parsing problem resides in an appropriately local domain. The solution is the most economical. Hence, the path that is taken involves a minimal change necessitated by the input, even if not fully target convergent ([Schwartz & Sprouse, 1994](#ch5.xhtml_ref5_42){.biblioref role="doc-biblioref"}). This parsing path is echoed in L2 learning: the analysis of input follows steps that guide learning. Learning is, therefore, tied to the licensing of structures by inventoried specifications in parsing. Inventoried specifications for grammar and vocabulary that are more frequent will be more efficiently retrieved. In IP, it is important to recognize the problem of imperfections---including speech errors such as mixed structures, such as *many people often thought...* to mean "many people thought..." or "people often thought...," (Frazier & Clifton, [2011](#ch5.xhtml_ref5_24){#ch5.xhtml_R_ref5_24 .biblioref role="doc-biblioref"}). Such strings are not treated as a new grammatical construction but as blends (Frazier & Clifton, [2011](#ch5.xhtml_ref5_24){.biblioref role="doc-biblioref"}). They will not be mistaken for new ways of expressing quantification by learners.
:::

::: {.section aria-labelledby="sec5_4_5"}
### Analysis Across Grammars and Learning {#ch5.xhtml_sec5_4_5}

Ambiguity is pervasive in language. The input *flying pigeons can be frightening* is ambiguous between an analysis in which *flying* heads the phrase, so that the activity of flying pigeons can be frightening (perhaps because of the hawks that visit the neighborhood) and an analysis in which *flying* modifies *pigeons*, so that the animal in flight can be frightening to people with bird phobias. Ambiguity can also arise across the grammaticality boundary. Input strings such as *Tom draws slowly jumping monkeys* receive distinct analyses depending on the grammar. Hence, the verb *draws* may raise past adverbs as in French or Spanish, leaving a silent copy or trace marked, as in \[Tom \[draws \[slowly \[jumping monkeys\]\]\]\] so that *slowly* modifies drawing. In English or Chinese, the verb remains in situ as \[Tom \[0 \[ draws \[ slowly jumping monkeys\]\]\]\] so that *slowly* modifies *jumping*. [Eubank et al. (1997)](#ch5.xhtml_ref5_21){#ch5.xhtml_R_ref5_21 .biblioref role="doc-biblioref"} reported that, contrary to expectation, L1-Chinese learners of English allow French-like interpretations in processing *Tom draws slowly jumping monkeys* even though neither Chinese nor English grammar does so. The authors assumed a grammatical impairment of mechanisms determining whether finite verbs should or should not raise.

However, as noted by [Dekydtspotter and Sprouse (2003)](#ch5.xhtml_ref5_20){#ch5.xhtml_R_ref5_20 .biblioref role="doc-biblioref"}, the ability to raise finite verbs in processing across proficiency and native vs. nonnative language status can be uniformly explained when it is recognized that the ability to parse goes beyond specifications for the current grammar. For a Chinese or English speaker to acquire verb raising at all, verb-raising analyses need to be accessible in parsing. [Dekydtspotter and Sprouse (2003)](#ch5.xhtml_ref5_20){.biblioref role="doc-biblioref"} proposed that English NSs and L1-Chinese learners of English first assign a non-verb raising representation \[Tom \[0 \[ draws \[ slowly jumping monkeys\]\]\]\] licensed by their (L1-based) interlanguage grammar. This interpretation, however, fails to be contextually integrated into the context where the drawing is very slow, but the monkeys jump very fast. It is responsible for the overwhelming 'false' responses: 81.7% for higher-proficiency learners and 69.5% for lower-proficiency learners in [Eubank et al.'s (1997)](#ch5.xhtml_ref5_21){.biblioref role="doc-biblioref"} experiment. However, the parser striving for integration with the context in comprehension revises the unintegrated analysis to a verb-raising analysis. This analysis allows for contextual integration; however, the grammatical inventory fails to license it. L1-Chinese L2-English learners provided a verb-raising response at 18.3% (higher-proficiency) and []{#ch5.xhtml_p68 .pagebreak aria-label=" page 68. " role="doc-pagebreak"}30.4% (lower-proficiency), and English NSs at a rate of 9.0%. Access to a verb-raising analysis was in evidence across groups. [Dekydtspotter and Sprouse (2003)](#ch5.xhtml_ref5_20){.biblioref role="doc-biblioref"} also noted that, given the slower pace of L2 processing, parses may fade more quickly in a narrow processing window. They suggested: "In even highly proficient L2 learners (...) the ungrammaticality message subsides as the representation fades quickly, whereas the message of contextual integration subsists (i.e., the contextually intended message has been carried)" (p. 52). Nonnative behavior highlights the significance of representations that fade quickly in response to higher activation thresholds for a nonnative language resulting in diminished grammatical intuitions in SLA ([Lim & Christianson, 2013](#ch5.xhtml_ref5_35){.biblioref role="doc-biblioref"}), given that inventoried specifications must license analyses within a narrow time window.

The ability to generate representations that the current grammatical state does not license is essential for learning, but it creates structural ambiguity where there is none within the grammar. This phenomenon should therefore be an area of interest in IP because of its consequences for learner behavior. The parsing mechanisms that enable nonnative language acquisition in IP must also therefore affect nonnative language use.
:::

::: {.section aria-labelledby="sec5_4_6"}
### On the Sources of IP Strategies {#ch5.xhtml_sec5_4_6}

In learning as parser update, IP strategies are rooted in the design of the language faculty. The first-noun phrase strategy has been the object of much scrutiny ([VanPatten, 2004](#ch5.xhtml_ref5_48){.biblioref role="doc-biblioref"}, among many others). There is indeed a bias for treating a noun phrase as a thematic subject: the agent of an event or the experiencer of a state. It is often treated as a heuristic with an N--V--N sequence mapping to Agent Verb Patient ([Lim & Christianson, 2013](#ch5.xhtml_ref5_35){.biblioref role="doc-biblioref"}). This strategy need not be stipulated: It follows from a general local search constraint. Thus, sentence structure involves both a functional domain for propositional force, tense/aspect and a thematic domain in which event-relations are expressed. Sentence-initial noun phrases must be linked to gapped thematic positions for the purpose of interpretation with respect to the event relation characterized by the verb. Interpretation thus involves an active gap filling procedure in local search. Hence, *the horse that bit the donkey* is more easily processed than *the donkey that the horse bit*. In processing *the horse that*, a filler-gap dependency for the relative clause is immediately anticipated. The parser seeks to resolve it at the first opportunity. The filler *the horse* is first associated with the sentential subject position, linked to the thematic subject/agent position, before entertaining an object position. In sum, in resolving filler-gap dependencies (i.e., interrogatives, clefts, topicalizations, and relative clauses) processing loads reflect an active local search with additional costs for objects due to revisions. Similarly, a first noun phrase at the clause edge is automatically associated with the closest thematic subject argument position in incremental interpretation. The ubiquitous local search constraint in language has a significant effect in explaining subject vs. object extraction asymmetries in L2 learners ([Juffs & Harrington, 1995](#ch5.xhtml_ref5_32){.biblioref role="doc-biblioref"}) and plays a role in first-noun strategy errors. It also accounts for certain processing loads after intransitive verbs in language use, modulated by intransitivity type as in *As the journalist arrived the editor postponed the meeting* in contrast to intransitive prepositional predicates *As the journalist was on air the editor postponed the meeting* ([Dekydtspotter & Seo, 2017](#ch5.xhtml_ref5_19){#ch5.xhtml_R_ref5_19 .biblioref role="doc-biblioref"}). [Dekydtspotter and Seo (2017)](#ch5.xhtml_ref5_19){.biblioref role="doc-biblioref"} reported greater processing load on the verb *postponed* after intransitive verbs like *arrived* than after copular predicates like *be on air* in Korean learners of English as well as native speakers of English as evidenced in reading times. Significantly greater processing load asymmetries were driven by intransitive verbs with theme arguments (unaccusative verbs like *arrived*) than after copular predicates and intransitive verbs with agent arguments (unergative verbs like *spoke*). Processing load effects after (unaccusative) intransitive verbs in nonnative speakers were first noticed in Juffs (2004).

[]{#ch5.xhtml_p69 .pagebreak aria-label=" page 69. " role="doc-pagebreak"}Processing loads for intransitive predicates are unexpected based on the lexical information for both verbal and copular predicates, because predicates with one argument such as *arrived* or *be on air* should completely rule out a second noun phrase argument. However, processing load asymmetries on the verb *postponed* are explained if the noun phrase automatically receives an interpretation as the thematic internal subject/agent of a verb phrase in advance of encountering the lexically encoded intransitivity information for the verb in active structural processing. This first noun as agent induces a transitivity expectation that is revised when the lexical information for the intransitive verb is processed. This induces processing load effects on the verb *postponed* as the structure is revised. The revision is greatest as the internal subject/agent of a verb phrase must be reanalyzed as a theme, explaining the strength of effects with unaccusative verbs. These findings do not deny strong lexical effects in parsing but show more complex interactions than a lexis-to-syntax information flow ([Hopp, 2013](#ch5.xhtml_ref5_30){#ch5.xhtml_R_ref5_30 .biblioref role="doc-biblioref"}). Crucially for IP, the strategy induces misinterpretations with unaccusative verbs. In processing *As the journalist arrived the editor postponed the meeting*, the initial mapping to *the journalist* to an internal subject induces the interpretation that the journalist caused the arrival of the editor, even though the structure is not lexically unlicensed requiring a revision. Errors in comprehension questions show this misinterpretation to persist together with the lexically licensed interpretation in which the journalist arrived ([Seo, 2014](#ch5.xhtml_ref5_44){#ch5.xhtml_R_ref5_44 .biblioref role="doc-biblioref"}), so that IP strategies affect not only L2 grammar acquisition but also L2 interpretation.

The primacy of content words principle in VanPatten's IP model postulates that learners process input for meaning before anything else (e.g., grammatical form) ([VanPatten, 2020](#ch5.xhtml_ref5_49){.biblioref role="doc-biblioref"}; Leeser, this volume). Both the interpretation and the externalization of language require grammatical structure. However, certain elements of grammatical structure feed interpretation and others feed externalization in structure-to-sound mappings. The primacy of meaning principle of VanPatten's IP model claims a focus on meaning at the possible expense of functional morphological form in L2 acquisition. The irretrievability of forms in SLA leading to morphological variability is widely indeed recognized as a major characteristic of nonnative language. Morphological variability has been attributed to a mapping problem between feature specifications in syntactic representations and the forms that realize these feature specifications in spoken language ([Prévost & White, 2000](#ch5.xhtml_ref5_41){#ch5.xhtml_R_ref5_41 .biblioref role="doc-biblioref"}). Hence, when there are several possible candidates, nonnative language users often have difficulty selecting the best matching form. Still, how the selection of morphology interacts with the resources attending to the construction of the sentence meaning has not been fully addressed until recently in native ([Frimu & Dekydtspotter, 2021](#ch5.xhtml_ref5_25){#ch5.xhtml_R_ref5_25 .biblioref role="doc-biblioref"}) and nonnative ([Frimu & Dekydtspotter, 2022](#ch5.xhtml_ref5_26){#ch5.xhtml_R_ref5_26 .biblioref role="doc-biblioref"}) processing research. This research experimentally shows, for the French simple future tense, that the selection of forms in native and nonnative speakers alike relies on real-time processes of inference in the conceptual system. Thus, in person and number subject--verb agreement in *les enfants mangeront* 'the children will eat', the features \[Person, Number: Plural\] of the subject *les enfants* 'the children' activate verbal agreement forms with these features. These forms include *-a* (3^rd^ person singular) realizing the features \[Person, Number\] (with 3^rd^ person and singular default values) and *-ont* (3^rd^ person plural) realizing \[Person, Number: Plural\]. In a context where the more specified -*ont* is activated, the informationally simpler, less-specified default -*a* form is inferred to be \[- Plural\] (i.e., singular). This makes the default -*a* form unsuitable in a plural context, so that -*ont* is selected as the default form is eliminated. The exclusion of the less-specified -*a* enables the retrieval of the more specified *-ont*, which offers the best match. Form selection, therefore, involves basic conceptual-structure processes that compete with the processing of the ongoing sentence meaning. This enables an interaction between semantic processes and basic conceptual-structure processes enlisted in form selection. In language learners, the inferred value \[- Plural\] may fade from []{#ch5.xhtml_p70 .pagebreak aria-label=" page 70. " role="doc-pagebreak"}working memory. Nothing then prevents the selection of informationally simpler -*a*, which blocks the retrieval of the more specified -*ont*. Default form would, therefore, arise in nonnative speakers as inferences fail to be maintained in the focus of attention. Major IP strategies seem therefore to be rooted in language design.
:::
:::::::::

::: {.section aria-labelledby="sec5_5"}
## Future Directions {#ch5.xhtml_sec5_5}

As [VanPatten (2002, p. 763)](#ch5.xhtml_ref5_47){.biblioref role="doc-biblioref"} noted: "Future research will no doubt add to the current model of IP or push for alterations in it, especially as parsing is incorporated into any account of IP." The chapter highlighted the role of the parser in SLA, incorporating implicit learning as parser update into IP. Intake extraction, accommodation, and access in SLA all involve an automatic parsing procedure building representations based on stored specifications for the L2. Accommodation involves updating these specifications. Access involves lexical retrieval and assembly within a narrow time window. Repeated retrieval in TL processing leads to more robust TL-like deployment of knowledge. Language learning as parser update provides a source for the primacy of implicit learning in SLA ([VanPatten & Smith, 2022](#ch5.xhtml_ref5_52){#ch5.xhtml_R_ref5_52 .biblioref role="doc-biblioref"}). The development of research on the neurocognitive mechanisms for storage, retrieval, and deployment of grammatical specifications and lexis for the nonnative language should considerably advance understanding of IP within the language system.

IP-based processing instruction helps "L2 learners derive richer intake from input by having them engage in structured input activities that push them away from the strategies that they normally use to make form-meaning connections" ([Wong, 2004](#ch5.xhtml_ref5_2001){#ch5.xhtml_R_ref5_2001 .biblioref role="doc-biblioref"}). The integration of learning within IP as parser update invites detailed investigations of the input, with a specific focus on the identification of triggers and the best rate of iteration for optimal learning of the learner with typical implicit cognitive abilities. Addressing implicit learning in SLA ([VanPatten & Smith, 2022](#ch5.xhtml_ref5_52){.biblioref role="doc-biblioref"}), research could examine whether variation in the implicit cognitive abilities sustaining language processing (categorization, memory, priming, and selective attention ([Gramena, 2020](#ch5.xhtml_ref5_27){#ch5.xhtml_R_ref5_27 .biblioref role="doc-biblioref"})) explains individual speed of growth and TL attainment in response to processing instruction ([Gramena & Yilmaz, 2019](#ch5.xhtml_ref5_28){#ch5.xhtml_R_ref5_28 .biblioref role="doc-biblioref"}).
:::

:::
