[]{#ch6.xhtml}

::: {}
# []{#ch6.xhtml_p73 .pagebreak aria-label=" page 73. " role="doc-pagebreak"}[6 ]{.chnum .ordinal}[Input Processing and Usage-Based Approaches]{.chtitle} {#ch6.xhtml_h1}

Alfonso Morales-Front

Georgetown University

Joe Barcroft

Washington University in Saint Louis

DOI: [10.4324/9781003041412-8](https://dx.doi.org/10.4324/9781003041412-8){aria-label="D.O.I. link to this document."}
:::

::::: {.section .introduction role="doc-introduction"}
## Introduction {#ch6.xhtml_sec6_1}

Input processing (IP) during language acquisition is a cognitive phenomenon that is largely bottom-up in nature. It provides mechanisms in our brain with linguistic data across all levels of linguistic analysis as we attend to samples of a target language (*input*) during communication. Therefore, what is needed to advance our understanding of IP are approaches that focus on bottom-up processing and its relationship with memory and learning. Neurally inspired and gradient in nature, usage-based (UB) approaches to language acquisition fit this bill. UB approaches, in all of their historical varieties---including functionalism, connectionism, and emergentism as applied to language, the competion model, cognitive linguistics, and complexity theory as applied to language---focus on processing that is bottom-up, interactive, and distributed in nature. As such, these approaches are well equipped to advance theory and promote new research on IP. In this chapter, we assess the relationship between UB perpectives and issues in research on IP for the acquisition of morphosyntax and IP for the acquisition of other linguistic subsystems, such as lexis.

::: {.section aria-labelledby="sec6_1_1"}
### What Are Usage-Based Approaches? {#ch6.xhtml_sec6_1_1}

Usage-based (UB) approaches are a coalition of frameworks, theories, and models that share assumptions such as the following: (a) language use is central to explaining language acquisition; (b) a limited set of cognitive mechanisms involving association and chunking underlie language acquisition; (c) language acquisition is tied to statiscally related properties of the input, such as type and token frequency, prototypicality, and contingency; and (d) grammar is an emergent property of exposure and use of language over time. Among the many lines of UB theory are dynamic systems theory ([Thelen & Smith, 1994](#ch6.xhtml_ref6_46){#ch6.xhtml_R_ref6_46 .biblioref role="doc-biblioref"}), functionalism ([Givón, 1979](#ch6.xhtml_ref6_27){#ch6.xhtml_R_ref6_27 .biblioref role="doc-biblioref"}), the competition model ([Bates & MacWhinney, 1981](#ch6.xhtml_ref6_15){#ch6.xhtml_R_ref6_15 .biblioref role="doc-biblioref"}), cognitive grammar ([Langacker, 1988](#ch6.xhtml_ref6_33){#ch6.xhtml_R_ref6_33 .biblioref role="doc-biblioref"}), construction grammar ([Kay & Fillmore, 1994](#ch6.xhtml_ref6_31){#ch6.xhtml_R_ref6_31 .biblioref role="doc-biblioref"}; [Goldberg, 2006](#ch6.xhtml_ref6_28){#ch6.xhtml_R_ref6_28 .biblioref role="doc-biblioref"}), conceptual metaphor theory ([Lakoff & Johnson, 1980](#ch6.xhtml_ref6_32){#ch6.xhtml_R_ref6_32 .biblioref role="doc-biblioref"}), parallel distributed processing ([Rumelhart & McClelland, 1986a](#ch6.xhtml_ref6_41){#ch6.xhtml_R_ref6_41 .biblioref role="doc-biblioref"}), and embodied cognition ([Valera et al., 1991](#ch6.xhtml_ref6_50){#ch6.xhtml_R_ref6_50 .biblioref role="doc-biblioref"}). The UB approaches we assume in this chapter are essentially (a) *connectionist* (i.e., they assume that the mental representation of information is []{#ch6.xhtml_p74 .pagebreak aria-label=" page 74. " role="doc-pagebreak"}distributed in neural networks); (b) *constructivist* (i.e. that learners construct knowledge from experiences); and (c) *functionalist* (i.e. that language is essentially a tool for communication).

These three dimensions converge in one key word, which is *usage*. In the case of language, usage in social interactions in different contexts is what creates the experiences from which knowledge emerges, leading to the development and constant refinement of linguistic ability based on mental associations that emerge in extensive networks of memory and knowledge. Usage results in the development of complex systems that are constantly adapting and evolving. Usage provides multiple opportunities for exposure to input as IP promotes development of competence across all linguistic subsystems. Usage also implies involvement of interaction and output, both of which interact with and affect IP and language acquisition. Given the pivotal role of usage, from a UB perspective, language learning is driven by associations that can be strengthened or attenuated and that are paired with the social dimension as use in social interactions ensues. Additionally, the unmistakable multidisciplinary nature of UB approaches has attracted researchers from various backgrounds who have coalesced around the idea that (a) usage is central and that (b) grammar is emergent, usage being essential and grammar being epiphenomenal ([Tomasello, 2001](#ch6.xhtml_ref6_48){#ch6.xhtml_R_ref6_48 .biblioref role="doc-biblioref"}). This approach views language acquisition as the entrenchment into categories and constructions of tokens of experience. As [Hopper (1998, p. 161)](#ch6.xhtml_ref6_30){#ch6.xhtml_R_ref6_30 .biblioref role="doc-biblioref"} writes, "emergent regularities are aggregations; they are the sediment of frequency," the idea being that in time, through usage in appropriate conditions, complex structures can emerge from the interaction of simple elements.
:::

::: {.section aria-labelledby="sec6_1_2"}
### Overview of the Chapter {#ch6.xhtml_sec6_1_2}

The rest of this chapter focuses on what UB approaches have done and can do to advance our understanding of IP. The chapter begins by reviewing important historical trajectories in the development of UB approaches, including early work on parallel distributed processing (PDP) and connectionist simulations of language learning ([Rumelhart & McClelland, 1986a](#ch6.xhtml_ref6_41){.biblioref role="doc-biblioref"}, [b](#ch6.xhtml_ref6_42){#ch6.xhtml_R_ref6_42 .biblioref role="doc-biblioref"}); the competition model ([Bates & MacWhinney 1981](#ch6.xhtml_ref6_15){.biblioref role="doc-biblioref"}) as a model of IP and language processing; and the application of tools from complexity theory to second language (L2) acquisition. We focus on critical issues and current contributions related to UB approaches to IP, including how UB approaches and research on IP have been compatible and mutually informative. Importantly, our view of IP here is not limited to IP for morphosyntax but also includes IP for all aspects of language, such as lexis and interactions between linguistic subsystems, such as between phonetics and lexis. We consider how UB tenets such as associative learning, exemplar-based learning, type and token frequency, and contingency are useful when investigating and advancing theory on all levels/types of IP and interactions between them. Finally, we review some existing research on IP conducted from UB persectives and conclude by addressing instructional issues and possible future directions for research in this area.
:::
:::::

::::::: {.section aria-labelledby="sec6_2"}
## Historical Perspectives {#ch6.xhtml_sec6_2}

::: {.section aria-labelledby="sec6_2_1"}
### 1932 to 1987: UB Approaches Have Been Considering IP for a Long Time {#ch6.xhtml_sec6_2_1}

In [1932](#ch6.xhtml_ref6_47){#ch6.xhtml_R_ref6_47 .biblioref role="doc-biblioref"}, the term *connectionism* was utilized by Thorndike to refer to how learning can occur based on connections between a stimuli and responses. In the 1950s, Rosenblatt (e.g., [1958](#ch6.xhtml_ref6_38){#ch6.xhtml_R_ref6_38 .biblioref role="doc-biblioref"}) further advanced the theory by developing the Perceptron, which is considered to be the first modern neural network. In the 1980s, work continued on *connectionism*---a theory based on the idea that mental processes, including IP, occur in networks of low-level nodes that change in their []{#ch6.xhtml_p75 .pagebreak aria-label=" page 75. " role="doc-pagebreak"}activation levels. Within linguistics in particular, the work of Greenberg can be viewed as an important precursor to later work on UB theory, as [Bybee (2023, p. 10)](#ch6.xhtml_ref6_16){#ch6.xhtml_R_ref6_16 .biblioref role="doc-biblioref"} has explained, noting, for example, [Greenberg's (1966)](#ch6.xhtml_ref6_29){#ch6.xhtml_R_ref6_29 .biblioref role="doc-biblioref"} "reference to usage in terms of frequency effects, which he cited along with other criteria in his discussion of markedness." [Bybee (2023)](#ch6.xhtml_ref6_16){.biblioref role="doc-biblioref"} traces this work and other work of "holdouts from the generative revolution" (such as Comrie) toward a consolidation of UB theory and the coining of the term "usage-based" by Langacker in 1987 (p. 10).

Also during the 1980s, in cognitive science, [Rumelhart and McClelland's (1986a)](#ch16.xhtml_ref16_41) groundbreaking two-volume set *Parallel Distributed Processing* had illustrated how the use of three-level networks with input, hidden, and output layers (*connectionist models* or *simulations*) or sometimes two-level networks could be used to account for and simulate different cognitive phenomena, including IP (IP broadly conceived not with specific reference to VanPatten's model of IP) and the acquisition of different aspects of language. MacWhinney's early work on UB approaches ([1978](#ch6.xhtml_ref6_34){#ch6.xhtml_R_ref6_34 .biblioref role="doc-biblioref"}, [1982](#ch6.xhtml_ref6_35){#ch6.xhtml_R_ref6_35 .biblioref role="doc-biblioref"}) also addressed different aspects of IP. It concerned language learning as the creation of *form--meaning connections*; morphology and word order; different types of IP strategies and principles; notions of semantic superfluity and redundancy; processing of particles, copula, and quantifiers; and the *first come--first served principle*. These concepts and foci are tied to sentence-level IP and beyond. Indeed, for a very long time now, UB approaches "are input driven, emphasizing associative learning of constructions from the input ([Ellis & Wulff, 2020](#ch6.xhtml_ref6_24){#ch6.xhtml_R_ref6_24 .biblioref role="doc-biblioref"}, p. 75).
:::

::: {.section aria-labelledby="sec6_2_2"}
### The 1980s and 1990s: Connectionist Models Simulate IP and Language Acquisition {#ch6.xhtml_sec6_2_2}

The 1980s and 1990s witnessed many publications of connectionist models (simulations) related to IP and different aspects of language acquisition. Rumelhart and McClelland published a simulation of IP and the acquisition of both regular and irregular verbs in a connectionist model with low-level nodes at various activation levels based upon a simple type of feedback loop, leading to subsequent debates about the extent to which the learning of regular and irregular forms is connectionist versus symbolic in nature. A number of other connectionist models were developed for verbal morphology, including [Plunkett and Juola's (1999)](#ch6.xhtml_ref6_37){#ch6.xhtml_R_ref6_37 .biblioref role="doc-biblioref"} model of English past tense and plural morphology. [Elman (1993)](#ch6.xhtml_ref6_25){#ch6.xhtml_R_ref6_25 .biblioref role="doc-biblioref"} also developed an influential connectionist model for learning that, when tweaked in a way that limited the processing span of the model (how much information it could process at a given time), demonstrated more complex learning of a "grammatical" nature for shorter processing spans, highlighting "the importance of starting small" as a potential mechanistic account of how age of onset affects language acquisition. These and other connectionist models have important implications for IP as it relates to SLA. The reader is referred to [Shirai's (2019)](#ch6.xhtml_ref6_43){#ch6.xhtml_R_ref6_43 .biblioref role="doc-biblioref"} book *Connectionism and second language acquisition* for a more full review of connectionist models pertinent to IP and SLA whereas other examples of models pertinent to IP and SLA are discussed in the Current Contributions section of this chapter.

Finally, during this same time period, [Bates and MacWhinney (1981)](#ch6.xhtml_ref6_15){.biblioref role="doc-biblioref"} proposed the UB-inspired *competition model* (CM), which also has implications for IP and SLA given its focus on the relative weight given to different features in the input as learners try to extract meaning. The CM is a lexico-functional model of processing in which meaning is derived from the competition of linguistic cues. Interpretations with the highest probability, given the available cues and their relative weights, prevail. A key notion for processing in the CM is identification and proper usage of relevant properties or patterns in the input, such as culmination (stress or intonation), constituent edges (beginning or end), word order, inflexion, and so forth. These are cues that []{#ch6.xhtml_p76 .pagebreak aria-label=" page 76. " role="doc-pagebreak"}compete during real-time processing to yield a particular meaning. Cues may have different strengths in different languages which need to be learned in L2 contexts.
:::

::: {.section aria-labelledby="sec6_2_3"}
### The 1990s to Present: UB Approaches Help to Explain Different Aspects of SLA {#ch6.xhtml_sec6_2_3}

Throughout the 1990s to the present, work by researchers such as Nick Ellis (e.g., [1996](#ch6.xhtml_ref6_19){#ch6.xhtml_R_ref6_19 .biblioref role="doc-biblioref"}, [2002](#ch6.xhtml_ref6_20){#ch6.xhtml_R_ref6_20 .biblioref role="doc-biblioref"}, [2019](#ch6.xhtml_ref6_21){#ch6.xhtml_R_ref6_21 .biblioref role="doc-biblioref"}) has applied connectionism, emergentistm, and other lines of UB approaches to issues in SLA, including the role of IP. From this work we appreciate the following:

a.  how variables such as frequency (e.g., type versus token frequency) impact upon IP and SLA;
b.  how IP and SLA are based largely upon processing formulaic (lexicalized) language and making use of the *principle of idiom*, which refers to how "a language user has available ... a large number of semi-preconstructed phrases that constitute single choices, even though they might appear to be analyzable into segments" ([Sinclair, 1991](#ch6.xhtml_ref6_44){#ch6.xhtml_R_ref6_44 .biblioref role="doc-biblioref"}, p. 110);
c.  how our linguistic competence reflects precisely what we have been exposed to in the input over long periods of time, including sensitivities to what "sounds right" or "sort of sounds right but not really" that are implicit in nature.

In doing so, Ellis has helped to merge psycholinguistics and SLA in important ways. Also during this time period, [VanPatten's (1996)](#ch6.xhtml_ref6_51){#ch6.xhtml_R_ref6_51 .biblioref role="doc-biblioref"} model of IP was proposed and has developed, including tenets such as how learners push to get meaning, how limited processing resources are allocated during sentence-level IP, and how learners tend to rely on lexical items when available to get meaning.
:::

::: {.section aria-labelledby="sec6_2_4"}
### The 2000s to Present: Lexical IP and Multilevel IP Develop within a UB Perspective {#ch6.xhtml_sec6_2_4}

Since 2000, research and theory on IP has expanded beyond sentence-level IP to focus on lexical IP, leading to UB-consistent observations about how different input-and task-based manipulations affect vocabulary learning. Related models have been proposed, such as [Barcroft and Sommers' (2005)](#ch6.xhtml_ref6_13){#ch6.xhtml_R_ref6_13 .biblioref role="doc-biblioref"} distributed model to account for the benefits of acoustically varied input on vocabulary learning. New theoretical advances and foci have also emerged, including the need to understand *multilevel input processing*, or how learners process input with regard to the data it provides across multiple levels of linguistic analysis. In this regard, [Barcroft (2015)](#ch6.xhtml_ref6_11){#ch6.xhtml_R_ref6_11 .biblioref role="doc-biblioref"} proposed that the "executive" resource allocator that learners use to allocate resources among multiple levels of IP is *distributed* in nature, based on the neurally inspired connections among many low-level nodes and patterns of interactions among them, which is consistent with UB theory.
:::
:::::::

::::::: {.section aria-labelledby="sec6_3"}
## Critical Issues and Topics {#ch6.xhtml_sec6_3}

::: {.section aria-labelledby="sec6_3_1"}
### What Is the Nature of IP from a UB Perspective? {#ch6.xhtml_sec6_3_1}

As [Ellis and Wulff (2020, p. 75)](#ch6.xhtml_ref6_24){.biblioref role="doc-biblioref"} have stated, UB approaches "are input driven, emphasizing the associative learning of different types of form--meaning mappings, or constructions, from input. As with other statistical estimations, a large and representative sample of language is required for the learner to abstract a rational model that is a good fit to the language data." In other words, while UB approaches concur with other theoretical approaches on the need for and []{#ch6.xhtml_p77 .pagebreak aria-label=" page 77. " role="doc-pagebreak"}critical role of *input* (samples of a target language to which a learner is exposed) in language acquisition, UB approaches pay particular attention to statistical properties related to the input, such as type and token frequency of grammatical forms, and how these lead to language-focused associative learning that lead to *constructions*, which again, are different types of form--meaning pairings. For example, a word such as *leaf* is associated with trees, branches, and other aspects of its meaning whereas an inflectional morpheme such as -*ed* is associated with pastness in English. Constructions such as these are central during both IP and language acquisition in general. As such, IP from a UB perspective is the process by which learners make such associations based not only on mappings between different types of linguistic forms in the input and their meanings but also on statistical properties of how and when they appear in the input. From this perspective, IP over long periods of time "tunes" language learners so that they become sensitive to these statistical properties. Additionally, from a UB perspective, IP depends on other cognitive abilities such as memory, categorization, and analogy and does not take place in a vacuum given that language usage is embodied and situated.

Working within a UB perspective, [Barcroft (2015)](#ch6.xhtml_ref6_11){.biblioref role="doc-biblioref"} proposed that we expand the scope of inquiry of IP to include lexis (lexical IP) and other domains, that IP should also expand to consider phonological IP, sociolinguistic IP, pragmatic IP, and so forth. Inclusion of all linguistic subsystems and other cognitive domains is something that UB approaches naturally encourage and that can be complemented by focusing more on the distributed and statistically guided nature of IP and language acquisition. Observations to emerge from research in this area include, for example, that *lexical IP is one of many levels of IP; semantic and structural processing are (at least largely) dissociable; processing for one subcomponent of vocabulary learning can decrease processing and learning for others; output with and without access to meaning affect lexical IP differently*; and *word-initial and word-final segments are privileged over word-medial segments*. This area of research along with new work on IP for the acquisition of other linguistic subsystems (see Tremblay, this volume, on phonology; see Fichtner, this volume, on pragmatics) continues to raise new questions about how UB approaches can help to advance our understanding of IP overall. Additionally, and also at the lexical level, theories such as cross-situational word learning ([Yu & Smith, 2007](#ch6.xhtml_ref6_53){#ch6.xhtml_R_ref6_53 .biblioref role="doc-biblioref"}) may predict that learners track co-occurrence statistics of form--meaning pairings across different situations and experiences.
:::

::: {.section aria-labelledby="sec6_3_2"}
### What Is the Nature of Intake from a UB Perspective? {#ch6.xhtml_sec6_3_2}

Given the extent to which statistical properties of input are of interest during IP from a UB perspective, the notion of *intake*, which is a subset of the input, might also be defined with regard to these properties as well. Arguably, the notion of intake may not even be needed from the UB perspective as associative learning is viewed as a seamless flow from attending to input to the gradual development of making form--meaning connections (constructions) over time, but if it is to be maintained, it should reflect different types of stastistical properties, such as frequency of co-ocurrence of different types of linguistic forms. For example, associations between a word like *blue* and other words like *sky*, *ocean*, *baby* (as in *baby blue*) in the input would not only reflect co-ocurrence but also the frequency of co-ocurrence relative to all co-ocurrences, such as the number of exemplars of *blue sky* versus *blue ocean* that have appeared up to any point in time in the input. Weightings for the different types of word combinations is going to be encoded as learners "take statistics" based on the input to which they are exposed. Intake from a UB perspective should therefore reflect statistical information of this nature. As always, however, if input is not attended to sufficiently by the learner, input still may not lead to intake.

[]{#ch6.xhtml_p78 .pagebreak aria-label=" page 78. " role="doc-pagebreak"}As should be clear from this discussion of the nature of intake, as is the case with many approaches to IP and language acquisition, form--meaning mappings (or form--meaning connections) are important in UB approaches. From a UB perspective, such mappings can be very complex and dynamic in nature. Multiple meanings can map onto multiple forms, and vice versa. As [Goldberg (2006)](#ch6.xhtml_ref6_28){.biblioref role="doc-biblioref"} points out, form--meaning correspondences can be observed at all linguistic levels, from morphemes or words to sentences and discourse, and the meanings of linguistic units are based on complex networks of form--meaning associations. UB approaches also emphasize that forms, meanings, and mappings are not static; instead, they change over time in response to changing circumstances at the individual or societal level. In our view, this multifaceted conception of form--meaning connections is not inconsistent with existing models of sentence-level IP or lexical IP as the complexity and evolving nature of the mappings need not be drawn into question.
:::

::: {.section aria-labelledby="sec6_3_3"}
### What Is the Nature of a Developing Linguistic System from a UB Perspective? {#ch6.xhtml_sec6_3_3}

UB proponents also often argue that *language competence*, the implicit ability to use grammar and all aspects of language, is the result of general cognitive abilities. In UB approaches, developing linguistic systems are dynamic interactive networks of constructions (as explained above) over time. The lexicon, for example, should be viewed as a large array of constructions interconnected at many levels, all form--meaning mappings with different levels of specificity or schematicity ([Langacker, 1988](#ch6.xhtml_ref6_33){.biblioref role="doc-biblioref"}). As such, sentence-level IP researchers may consider focusing more on the acquisition and development of constructions instead of grammatical forms. From this perspective, as [Tomasello (2006, p. 263)](#ch6.xhtml_ref6_49){#ch6.xhtml_R_ref6_49 .biblioref role="doc-biblioref"} states, children initially learn "concrete pieces of language---words (e.g., cat), complex expressions (e.g., I-wanna-do-it), or mixed constructions (e.g., Where's-the\_\_\_\_\_)" prior to extracting the rules and constraints of grammar from constructions.
:::

::: {.section aria-labelledby="sec6_3_4"}
### What Information Sources Guide Language Acquisition from a UB Perspective? {#ch6.xhtml_sec6_3_4}

Beyond low-level mechanisms of associative learning and all of the information provided by input, UB approaches to language acquisition avoid positing any additional information source, such as Universal Grammar, that might inform and constrain the process in any way. While this position contrasts with the generative view of language acquisition, some researchers may support at once a theory of IP that is UB in nature and a view of language acquisition that is generative in nature. While VanPatten's model of IP may not assume a generative view of language building at the IP stage, VanPatten's position on language acquisition does assume a generative view (cf. Harrington, 2004, for the view that several of VanPatten's IP principles imply access to information that is generated outside of the initial processing module). [VanPatten and Jegerski (2010](#ch6.xhtml_ref6_52){#ch6.xhtml_R_ref6_52 .biblioref role="doc-biblioref"}, p. 5) have stated the following: "From a linguistic and psycholinguistic perspective, we see acquisition as basically consisting of three elements (everything else being equal): input, Universal Grammar (and any other language-oriented mechanisms), and the processing that mediates between input and UG." UB approaches to language acquisition would not include UG as a part of this view of language acquisition.
:::
:::::::

::::::: {.section aria-labelledby="sec6_4"}
## Current Contributions and Research {#ch6.xhtml_sec6_4}

Studies focused specifically on the intersection of UB theory and sentence-level IP are limited in number. Nevertheless, in what follows we highlight four areas of research that we view as being []{#ch6.xhtml_p79 .pagebreak aria-label=" page 79. " role="doc-pagebreak"}mutually beneficial with regard to this particular intersection or with regard to the intersection between UB approaches and lexical IP or multilevel IP: (a) research on connectionist simulations of different aspects of L2 learning that highlight the role of input in profitable ways; (b) research on how input-related factors such as frequency, prototypicality, and contingency affect L2 learning over time; (c) research on processing resource allocation within and across different linguistic subsystems; and (d) research on distributed models designed to account for phenoma related to lexical IP and L2 vocabulary learning. Here we discuss each of these in turn.

::: {.section aria-labelledby="sec6_4_1"}
### Connectionist Simulations in SLA {#ch6.xhtml_sec6_4_1}

Whereas early connectionist simulations tended to focus on L1 acquisition, a number of researchers have developed simulations for different types of phenomena in L2. In one study, for example, [Sokolik and Smith (1992)](#ch6.xhtml_ref6_45){#ch6.xhtml_R_ref6_45 .biblioref role="doc-biblioref"} used a simple two-layer (input, output) network to simulate the acquisition of the French gender system. The simulation involved presenting the network with 450 French nouns of different genders, including both regulars and irregulars. They first modeled L1 acquisition of the gender system, and the simple network performed quite well. They also wished to simulate L2 acquisition of the gender system, however, so they changed to a slower learning mechanism and had the network begin with different weights, both features that the researchers viewed as paralling SLA. This second network took longer to learn the gender system, which arguably parallels acquiring a gender system as an adult L2 learner.

In another study, which [Shirai (2019)](#ch6.xhtml_ref6_43){.biblioref role="doc-biblioref"} has pointed out as not simulating SLA per se, [Ellis and Schmidt (1998)](#ch6.xhtml_ref6_23){#ch6.xhtml_R_ref6_23 .biblioref role="doc-biblioref"} compared the performance of L2 English learners with performance of a three-layer (input, hidden, and output) connectionist simulation designed to process lexical items and their plurals as input in an artificial language. Frequency (more frequent forms were presented five times more) and regularity (regular, irregular) of the plural forms were maninpulated as variables. The behavioral findings demonstrated a frequency effect for regular forms at the initials stages of learning with regular forms being learned quickly at reaching ceiling-level performance quickly. The connectionist simulation paralleled this performance generally well, providing strong evidence in favor of the acquisition of both regular and irregular forms being predicted by the frequency with which the forms appear in the input.

In what ways might connectionist simulations such as these inform us about IP? To begin, it is important to clarify that such simulations speak mostly to acquisition overall as opposed to IP in particular. They do help to confirm, however, that IP can be viewed as involving a large amount of simple associative mapping. Second, they confirm that the power of repetition in memory and learning is alive and well in L2 IP and SLA. Third, they suggest that the performance of L2 learners can be modelled by adjusting features of networks, such as the rate at which they can learn and the initial activation weights assigned to the network. Fourth, they speak to what we might expect from L2 learners when they are presented with input containing morphosyntactic features that vary with regard to their frequency and their regularity. Insights such as these should be of interest to IP researchers, regardless of the areas in which they tend to focus and regardless of whether they consistently work within a UB approach or not.
:::

::: {.section aria-labelledby="sec6_4_2"}
### How Input-Related Factors Affect L2 Learning {#ch6.xhtml_sec6_4_2}

Research on learner performance from UB perspectives often yields results that are very intuitive, such as with regard to frequency. It is intuitive that increasing the number of times a word appears in the input should increase the likelihood that the word in question will be learned, regardless of whether it is via intentional or incidental learning (see, e.g., [Rott, 1999](#ch6.xhtml_ref6_39){#ch6.xhtml_R_ref6_39 .biblioref role="doc-biblioref"}). So is the []{#ch6.xhtml_p80 .pagebreak aria-label=" page 80. " role="doc-pagebreak"}idea that there should be correlations between the overall frequency of morphological and syntactic structures and the degree to which they are acquired by learners. All else being equal, we would expect that more frequently appearing grammatical structures should be learned more rapidly provided that the structures in question are sufficiently acquirable given the stage of the linguistic development of the learner. Regardless of how intuitive such findings may be, we need systematic investigation on the effects of frequency at different levels of linguistic analysis. The same holds true for other variables such as contingency, and prototypicality.

[Ellis et al. (2014)](#ch6.xhtml_ref6_22){#ch6.xhtml_R_ref6_22 .biblioref role="doc-biblioref"} conducted one such study that assessed the impact of frequency, contingency, and prototypicality on L2 English learners' ability to process verb-argument constructions (VACs) such as V(erb) *across* N(oun) or *it* V *of the* Noun, in L2 English. *Frequency* refers to the number of times exemplars of a form appear in a language. *Contingency* refers to when the presence of one form depends to a certain degree (high, low, or somewhere in between) on another, as in the case of *spontaneous \> combustion. Prototypicality* refers to the degree to which an item is prototypical of a category, such as how *sparrow* is more typical of the category bird than *ostrich* or *takahe*. The researchers consulted the British National Corpus to determine verb type frequencies, used an association measure (DeltaP) to determine association strengths, and consulted a lexical database (WordNet) to establish what degree of prototypicality the verbs of interest were. Participants completed a free association task in which they filled in a verb slot with the first word that came to their mind. As such, IP of the VACs happened over long periods of time well before the study was initiated. The findings of the study might be best summarized by the title of the article in which they appear: "The processing of second language verb-argument constructions is sensitive to form, function, frequency, contingency, and prototypicality." While these findings and the nature of the study may not have investigated IP directly, they help to confirm the pivotal roles of factors such as frequency, contingency, and prototypicality of which IP researchers should be aware even if they may not factor into the concept of structure building during processing on every occasion.
:::

::: {.section aria-labelledby="sec6_4_3"}
### Processing Resource Allocation During Sentence-Level and Lexical IP {#ch6.xhtml_sec6_4_3}

Another area of research consistent with UB perspectives concerns *processing resource allocation*, or how learners distribute their limited processing resources in different ways, during IP. Learners need to allocate their limited processing resources in different ways. For example, when learners allocate processing resources toward a semantically oriented task such as *pleasantness ratings* (e.g., "On a scale of 1 to 7, how pleasant is the meaning of this word?"), their increased processing of a semantic nature can decrease their processing of structural or form-oriented nature, leading to decreased novel word form learning, a phenomenon that is accounted for visually in the type of processing -- resource allocation (TOPRA) model ([Barcroft, 2002](#ch6.xhtml_ref6_9){#ch6.xhtml_R_ref6_9 .biblioref role="doc-biblioref"}). Research on IP is replete with studies on the effects of processing resource allocation. This research should be and often is informed by UB principles because they concern how learners allocate resources from pools of cognitive resources in varying degrees in real time. As such, they concern parallel distributed processing. Moreover, and critically, this research can help to establish which pools of resources are separable from others. In essence, where are the borders? Research on lexical IP and the TOPRA model has demonstrated how (a) semantic processing, (b), structural (form-oriented) processing, and (c) processing for mapping are at least in large part separable (dissociable) from one another, something which is of interest to researchers focused on IP, UB approaches, or both.
:::

::: {.section aria-labelledby="sec6_4_4"}
### []{#ch6.xhtml_p81 .pagebreak aria-label=" page 81. " role="doc-pagebreak"}Distributed Models in Lexical IP {#ch6.xhtml_sec6_4_4}

Fourth and finally, another area of research at the intersection of UB approaches and IP are models that have been developed in the study of lexical IP to account for different types of research findings. Here we consider two models: (a) one proposed by [Barcroft and Sommers (2005)](#ch6.xhtml_ref6_13){.biblioref role="doc-biblioref"} to account for how increasing amounts of acoustic variability (based on indexical features of speech, such as talker, speaking style, speaking rate, and so forth) improves L2 vocabulary learning (based on measures of accuracy and retrieval speed) ([Figure 6.1](#ch6.xhtml_fig6_1){#ch6.xhtml_R_fig6_1}) and (b) one designed by [Barcroft and Sommers (2014)](#ch6.xhtml_ref6_14){#ch6.xhtml_R_ref6_14 .biblioref role="doc-biblioref"} to account for the effects of acoustic variability across the lifespan, including both the positive effects of acoustically varied input on the early stages of word learning and the costs of acoustic variability when processing known lexical items as input during speech processing when measured as speed of word identification ([Figure 6.2](#ch6.xhtml_fig6_2){#ch6.xhtml_R_fig6_2}). Both of these UB models are distributed, gradated, and dynamic in nature, with different sizes and amounts of shading depicting different states of the models as they are in flux over time.

![[[Figure]{.label} [6.1 ]{.ordinal}A distributed model of the benefits of acoustic variability on vocabulary learning](#ch6.xhtml_R_fig6_1){.backlink aria-label=" This link returns to its first in-text citation. " role="doc-backlink"} [(adapted from [Barcroft & Sommers, 2005](#ch6.xhtml_ref6_13){.biblioref role="doc-biblioref"}).]{.source .credit role="doc-credit"}](images/fig6_1_B.jpg){#fig6_1 alt="A diagram with three panels that shows three types of acoustic variability. Each panel shows six ovals. The first panel is labeled “No variability” and shows how the lack of variability results in acoustic input is concentrated in one of the six ovals. The second panel is labeled “Moderate variability” and show how moderate acoustic variability is distributed in three of the six ovals. The third panel is called “High variability” and shows how the high acoustic variability is distributed across all six ovals."}

![[[Figure]{.label} [6.2 ]{.ordinal}A dynamic model of the effects of acoustic variability across the lifespan](#ch6.xhtml_R_fig6_2){.backlink aria-label=" This link returns to its first in-text citation. " role="doc-backlink"} [(from [Barcroft & Sommers, 2014](#ch6.xhtml_ref6_14){.biblioref role="doc-biblioref"}).]{.source .credit role="doc-credit"}](images/fig6_2_B.jpg){#fig6_2 alt="How three different levels of acoustic variability, that is, no variability, moderate variability, and high variability, impact word learning and word identification."}

[Figure 6.1](#ch6.xhtml_fig6_1) illustrates the finding by [Barcroft and Sommers (2005)](#ch6.xhtml_ref6_13){.biblioref role="doc-biblioref"} that presenting target L2 words six times in the input in the recorded voice of only one talker (no variability) led to much less vocabulary learning than when they were once each by six different talkers (high variability) with a condition of moderate variability (three speakers, two repetitions each) falling somewhere in between the no and high-variability conditions. As one can see in the model, increasing amounts of acoustic variability lead to a more robust distribution of the developing lexical forms, increasing the likelihood of a learner having "hits" that lead to more successful and faster retrieval of the developing word forms.

[]{#ch6.xhtml_p82 .pagebreak aria-label=" page 82. " role="doc-pagebreak"}The circles in the model on the left side of [Figure 6.2](#ch6.xhtml_fig6_2) depict the three degrees of robustness established in [Figure 6.1](#ch6.xhtml_fig6_1). These downward arrows passing through the circles with varied success illustrate how when a learner activates the semantic representation for a word (such as when presented with a picture of a word referent), there is greater chance that the learner will retrieve the target word form when the developing lexical form is more robust (a larger circle). The circles in the model on the right side of [Figure 6.2](#ch6.xhtml_fig6_2), illustrate how, after spoken word forms have been incorporated into one's linguistic system over long periods of time, more acoustically varied input, represented by greater dispersion of arrows moving upward, is less likely to "meet up with" canonical representations, leading to slower word identification performance. The dotted circles indicate how the acoustically varied input continues to affect the nature of the canonical forms, albeit to a very minimal degree based on one instance only.
:::
:::::::

::: {.section aria-labelledby="sec6_5"}
## Recommendations for Practice {#ch6.xhtml_sec6_5}

With regard to L2 pedagogy, VanPatten's model of sentence-level IP has a well-designed instructional arm that parallels and complements its theoretical side: processing instruction (PI) (see Wong, this volume). [Ortega and Tyler (2018)](#ch6.xhtml_ref6_36){#ch6.xhtml_R_ref6_36 .biblioref role="doc-biblioref"} also have highlighted types of instruction that they view as being usage-inspired, including Systemic Theoretical Instruction ([Gal'perin, 1989](#ch6.xhtml_ref6_26){#ch6.xhtml_R_ref6_26 .biblioref role="doc-biblioref"}) and the Dynamic Usage-Based Approach ([Rousse-Malpat & Verspoor, 2018](#ch6.xhtml_ref6_40){#ch6.xhtml_R_ref6_40 .biblioref role="doc-biblioref"}). PI can be viewed, at least arguably, as being compatible with the Dynamic Usage-Based Approach given that both focus on form--meaning mappings, view input as primordial, and advocate for []{#ch6.xhtml_p83 .pagebreak aria-label=" page 83. " role="doc-pagebreak"}manipulations of the input to help the learner process it more efficiently. Because both of these are well described elsewhere, in light of present purposes, we simply identify them here and provide reference for the interested reader.

From a more general perspective, we also note that the implications of research to date on IP are consistent with UB approaches from an instructional standpoint in that language competence develops gradually over time with both input and usage. Language instructors should make use of the classroom for communicative, task-based, and content-oriented activities, following principles of communicative language teaching, including facilitating real interactions in which meaning is negotiated. PI is an instructional technique that can complement this general approach if and when one may wish to target particular grammatical structures. The *input-based incremental* (IBI) approach to L2 vocabulary development ([Barcroft, 2012](#ch6.xhtml_ref6_10){#ch6.xhtml_R_ref6_10 .biblioref role="doc-biblioref"}; [Barcroft & Muñoz Basols, 2021](#ch6.xhtml_ref6_12){#ch6.xhtml_R_ref6_12 .biblioref role="doc-biblioref"}), an approach to language teaching that emphasizes how input may be presented and tasks may be designed in ways that improve vocabulary learning, also may be incorporated to complement this overall view of meaning-oriented instruction.
:::

::: {.section aria-labelledby="sec6_6"}
## Future Directions {#ch6.xhtml_sec6_6}

How might UB theory benefit from future IP research and vice versa? With regard to the first half of this question, UB approaches to language acquisition, including recent ones related to the application of dynamic systems theory to SLA, can benefit from existing IP research and potential future research on IP from UB perspectives. Research on sentence-level IP has demonstrated, for example, how learners have a preference for lexical items over grammatical items in their push to comprehend input (see White & Wong, this volume) and how input can be structured in ways that require them to process target grammatical items instead (see Wong, this volume). Research on lexical IP has demonstrated how semantic and structurally oriented processing are dissociable during IP ([Barcroft, 2002](#ch6.xhtml_ref6_9){.biblioref role="doc-biblioref"}) and how augmenting acoustic variability in the input can increase L2 vocabulary learning by 68% without changing anything else ([Barcroft & Sommers, 2005](#ch6.xhtml_ref6_13){.biblioref role="doc-biblioref"}). Research findings such as these improve our understanding of cognitive aspects of SLA in concrete ways that go beyond general explanations of tenets of UB theory that have been proposed and imply new questions that can be addressed from UB perspectives. The questions oftentimes are related to timing and quantity, such as *Is there an ideal time at which to decrease redundancy between lexical items and morphosyntactic features to promote the acquisition of a given morphosyntactic feature?* and *Is there an ideal amount of acoustic variability to include in spoken input for optimizing vocabulary learning?* Questions such as these may be addressed profitably from a UB perspective.

With regard to the second half of the question above, there are also a number of ways in which future research on IP can benefit from UB theory. To begin, cognitive models inspired by UB approaches might address principles of VanPatten's model of sentence-level IP more directly. Work on learned attention by Ellis and Sagarra ([2010](#ch6.xhtml_ref6_17){#ch6.xhtml_R_ref6_17 .biblioref role="doc-biblioref"}, [2011](#ch6.xhtml_ref6_18){#ch6.xhtml_R_ref6_18 .biblioref role="doc-biblioref"}) has already broken ground in this area. Might a connectionist simulation be able to account for the lexical preference principle or the combination of several of VanPatten's IP principles within a single model? Questions about the nature of processing resource allocation might also be quantified and tested in new ways, both with regard to sentence-level and lexical IP, such as with regard to quantifying predictions of the TOPRA model. UB perspectives might also be beneficial in advancing theory related to other levels of IP as well. Might UB models be designed to account for different phenomena during phonological IP (see Tremblay, this volume) or IP for the development of pragmatic competence (see Fichtner, this volume) over extended periods of time? In addition to novel []{#ch6.xhtml_p84 .pagebreak aria-label=" page 84. " role="doc-pagebreak"}questions such as these, new research also can be conducted to continue to address "age-old" questions about the role of explicit instruction in SLA (cf. Henry, this volume), questions such as how is it that structured input is still effective in the absence of explicit instruction with regard to IP for at least some types of L2 morphosyntactic structures? UB approaches and VanPattten's model of IP both focus primarily on implicit language learning, but for many years both also have addressed the potential role of explicit instruction from different angles, which should continue to inspire new research at the intersection of IP and UB perspectives.
:::

::: {.section .conclusion role="doc-conclusion"}
## Conclusion {#ch6.xhtml_sec6_7}

This chapter has addressed the theoretical positioning of different areas of research on IP with respect to UB approaches. While existing models of IP have been successful with their current scope and theoretical alignments, pursuing new avenues of development informed by UB perspectives could afford expansion in new ways, including with regard to new research on other levels of IP, such as lexical IP, and how all levels of IP co-occur as part of multilevel (multitype) IP. We also have considered areas of mutual compatibility between research and theory on IP and UB approaches, asking and discussing four central questions in the Critical Issues and Topics section. We also highlighted four areas in which research conducted from UB perspectives has increased our understanding of IP and research on IP has increased our understanding of issues related to UB approaches. At the end of the day, both areas of focus are complementary and mutually informative, a welcome state of affairs as we look toward the future.
:::

::: {.section .reference aria-labelledby="r6_1"}
## Further Reading {#ch6.xhtml_r6_1}
