[]{#ch7.xhtml}

::: {}
# []{#ch7.xhtml_p87 .pagebreak aria-label=" page 87. " role="doc-pagebreak"}[7 ]{.chnum .ordinal}[Input Processing in Conceptual Semantics]{.chtitle} {#ch7.xhtml_h1}

Susanne E. Carroll and Lindsay Hracs

University of Calgary

DOI: [10.4324/9781003041412-9](https://dx.doi.org/10.4324/9781003041412-9){aria-label="D.O.I. link to this document."}
:::

:::: {.section .introduction role="doc-introduction"}
## Introduction and Definitions {#ch7.xhtml_sec7_1}

*Meanings* play a crucial role in the discovery of words and word classes in second language acquisition (SLA).[^7] At the initial stage of SLA, it is generally assumed meanings must be inferred from sensory information. This puts *ab initio* L2 learners in a situation analogous to child first language (L1) learners, having to "bootstrap" their way into a novel grammar ([Gleitman & Gleitman, 2001](#ch7.xhtml_ref7_26){#ch7.xhtml_R_ref7_26 .biblioref role="doc-biblioref"}; [Pinker, 1984](#ch7.xhtml_ref7_53){#ch7.xhtml_R_ref7_53 .biblioref role="doc-biblioref"}). At the initial stage of learning, learners cannot find meaning in language itself; they have none. They must rely on non-linguistic information inferred from percepts. Much SLA research discusses only *reference*, a "word-to-world" relation that will not support the creation of an L2 grammar. We explore the hypothesis that visual stimuli make *ontological categories* available for representation in conceptual representations. Ontological categories are the means by which we understand the world (including the mental world) in perception, thought, and language (see papers in [Munitz, 1973](#ch7.xhtml_ref7_50){#ch7.xhtml_R_ref7_50 .biblioref role="doc-biblioref"}). We provide evidence for this claim, our data coming from controlled exposure learning studies with adults of a variety of Lab-languages.[^8] Our contribution is both theoretical and empirical, shedding light on how pictures and videos serve as input to initial-stage L2 learning. Since the role of visual stimuli in SLA is largely unexplored, our research makes an important contribution to the study of input processing.

::: {.section aria-labelledby="sec7_1_1"}
### Conceptual Semantics: A Brief Overview {#ch7.xhtml_sec7_1_1}

Conceptual Semantics is a theory of meaning embedded in a "tri-partite architecture" of the language faculty (Jackendoff, [2010](#ch7.xhtml_ref7_34){#ch7.xhtml_R_ref7_34 .biblioref role="doc-biblioref"}, [2015](#ch7.xhtml_ref7_35){#ch7.xhtml_R_ref7_35 .biblioref role="doc-biblioref"}). When someone knows a language, by hypothesis, they encode linguistic units as 'mental descriptions' in three autonomous levels of representation: Phonological Structures (PSs), Morpho-syntactic Structures (MsSs), and Conceptual Semantic Structures (CSSs). These representations are constructed or 'projected' by computational processes (e.g., unification) from primitive units that are unique to each level of representation and are subject to structural constraints that are also unique to each level (Jackendoff, [2010](#ch7.xhtml_ref7_34){.biblioref role="doc-biblioref"}, [2015](#ch7.xhtml_ref7_35){.biblioref role="doc-biblioref"}). The representations are processed in a modular fashion; the three types of structures are linked by "correspondence rules".[^9] We illustrate in (1), using a sound form from our Lab-Persian study (Carroll & Hracs, [2021](#ch7.xhtml_ref7_14){#ch7.xhtml_R_ref7_14 .biblioref role="doc-biblioref"}, [2022](#ch7.xhtml_ref7_15){#ch7.xhtml_R_ref7_15 .biblioref role="doc-biblioref"}), showing that a sound form expressing a []{#ch7.xhtml_p88 .pagebreak aria-label=" page 88. " role="doc-pagebreak"}PHYSICAL-ACTION concept maps to a verb word class in an MsS. The mappings between structures result from the operation of associative learning mechanisms.

``` {#ch7.xhtml_preformat7_1}

(1) a.  If a unit P in a PS expresses an ACTION PREDICATE (x acts on y) in a CSS, then P instantiates a verb in a related MsS.
    b.  [læms.kærd]i     ↔     [TOUCH (x) (y)]ACTION-i     ↔     Verbi
```

Example (1b) expresses a non-derivational relationship between the sound form *P* \[læms.kærd\] and the conceptual structure CSS which shows that it expresses an ACTION concept involving two arguments which will be fleshed out in the syntax as the subject and the direct object of the verb, as shown on the right edge of (1b). The double-headed arrows show that the relationship can work bidirectionally. The associative linkage is formally expressed in terms of the sub-index *i* which appears at the lower right edge of each structure. The representation shows that the "word" consists of all three representations and not (as one might assume from much writing on language acquisition) just the sound form on the left.

Conceptual Semantics is intended to be compatible with data from language processing and language acquisition and captures a broad range of meanings, including aspects that result from visual processing of Spatial Structures (SSs). SSs are non-linguistic representations characterizing our grasp of how objects relate to each other in space, their shape, (relative) size, subitized quantities, how they move in space, etc. (Jackendoff, [1987](#ch7.xhtml_ref7_32){#ch7.xhtml_R_ref7_32 .biblioref role="doc-biblioref"}, [1990](#ch7.xhtml_ref7_33){#ch7.xhtml_R_ref7_33 .biblioref role="doc-biblioref"}, pp. 32--35, 2015, pp. 11--14; [Landau & Jackendoff, 1993](#ch7.xhtml_ref7_41){#ch7.xhtml_R_ref7_41 .biblioref role="doc-biblioref"}).
:::
::::

::: {.section aria-labelledby="sec7_2"}
## Historical Perspectives {#ch7.xhtml_sec7_2}

Much research using *ab initio* learners has made use of artificial languages, focussing on how learners might use *distributional analysis* to induce a grammar. Such research tends to reify linguistic constructs: learners are exposed to sounds but it is claimed they learn "words" and "markers" (as in, functional categories like agreement). However, if learners hear auditory stimuli, distributional analysis can only operate on segmented shapes of words and their position in a linear sequence of sounds (see [Saffran et al., 1996](#ch7.xhtml_ref7_55){#ch7.xhtml_R_ref7_55 .biblioref role="doc-biblioref"}, on segmentation; Braine, [1963](#ch7.xhtml_ref7_9){#ch7.xhtml_R_ref7_9 .biblioref role="doc-biblioref"}, [1987](#ch7.xhtml_ref7_10){#ch7.xhtml_R_ref7_10 .biblioref role="doc-biblioref"}; [Chemla et al., 2009](#ch7.xhtml_ref7_19){#ch7.xhtml_R_ref7_19 .biblioref role="doc-biblioref"}; [Mintz, 2002](#ch7.xhtml_ref7_48){#ch7.xhtml_R_ref7_48 .biblioref role="doc-biblioref"}; [Valian & Coulson, 1988](#ch7.xhtml_ref7_62){#ch7.xhtml_R_ref7_62 .biblioref role="doc-biblioref"}, on distributional learning). At best, such distributional learning can result in *form classes*. Calling form classes "word" classes prematurely concludes what must be demonstrated, namely that learners cognize what it is that the forms are doing semantically and syntactically. In other words, it presupposes that learners have represented words with the CSSs and MsSs seen in the middle and on the right of (1b). Nouns or Verbs are not merely words occurring in a given position in a string, nor are they merely forms linkable to Person- and Number-agreement markers as "non-adjacent" expressions (as, e.g., [Gómez, 2002](#ch7.xhtml_ref7_27){#ch7.xhtml_R_ref7_27 .biblioref role="doc-biblioref"}; [Mintz, 2002](#ch7.xhtml_ref7_48){.biblioref role="doc-biblioref"}; [Perruchet et al., 2004](#ch7.xhtml_ref7_51){#ch7.xhtml_R_ref7_51 .biblioref role="doc-biblioref"}, inter alia, suggest). Auditory stimuli are what learners are exposed to, but the knowledge that is abstracted or inferred as PSs, CSSs, and/or MsSs results in types of 'input' that will serve as the foundation for additional kinds of language learning. Input, in other words, is abstract (see [Carroll, 2001](#ch7.xhtml_ref7_11){#ch7.xhtml_R_ref7_11 .biblioref role="doc-biblioref"}; [Gagliardi, 2013](#ch7.xhtml_ref7_21){#ch7.xhtml_R_ref7_21 .biblioref role="doc-biblioref"}; [Gagliardi et al., 2017](#ch7.xhtml_ref7_22){#ch7.xhtml_R_ref7_22 .biblioref role="doc-biblioref"}; [Hracs 2021](#ch7.xhtml_ref7_30){#ch7.xhtml_R_ref7_30 .biblioref role="doc-biblioref"}, p. 153).[^10] Therefore, we must not assume that just because learners have been exposed to sounds that they have necessarily abstracted from them target-like PSs. Nor should we assume that just because they have learned to successfully associate sound forms to pictures, they have acquired predicate-argument structures, semantic roles, or semantic features. Additionally, appropriate use of a form by a learner in spontaneous production does not necessarily mean that they have acquired target-like representations, a fact long-established in first language acquisition ([Bowerman, 1982](#ch7.xhtml_ref7_7){#ch7.xhtml_R_ref7_7 .biblioref role="doc-biblioref"}). Since input is abstract, it cannot be studied by examination of learner-directed speech or written texts. It must be inferred from learner behaviours.
:::

::::: {.section aria-labelledby="sec7_3"}
## []{#ch7.xhtml_p89 .pagebreak aria-label=" page 89. " role="doc-pagebreak"}Critical Issues and Topics {#ch7.xhtml_sec7_3}

::: {.section aria-labelledby="sec7_3_1"}
### Ontological Categories, Word Classes, and Word Learning {#ch7.xhtml_sec7_3_1}

How do first exposure learners interpret speech when they have no word forms, no word classes, nor knowledge of how words combine in the L2 grammar to form phrases and sentences? Our answer is (partly) that visual (and possibly other types of sensory) information plays a crucial role in inferring 'meanings' and, moreover, meanings take the shape of CSSs that express at least ontological categories. To fully understand the descriptive notation, we introduce here for CSSs, three things must be understood: (i) CSSs are algebraic: concepts combine in precise ways dependent on the logical structure of a CSS, not the linear order of PSs. Predicates appear to the left of their arguments; arguments are then "in the scope of" their predicates.[^11] Combinatorial operations that put predicates and arguments together are universal (hence unlearned and independent of linguistic experience) and reflect the nature of the human mind; (ii) Despite this left-to-right display, CSSs are (unlike PSs) non-linear. This follows from the instantaneous way that we perceive events visually or through other senses; (iii) CSSs are multi-tiered representations. Each tier is a domain in which different types of meanings are simultaneously encoded.

In the descriptive tier, individual predicates, arguments, and propositions are marked as members of ontological categories such as THING-, ACTION-, PATH-, PLACE-, STATE-, and EVENT-concepts. Such concepts arise in perception and are available to prelinguistic infants and even other species ([Gelman & Spelke, 1981](#ch7.xhtml_ref7_23){#ch7.xhtml_R_ref7_23 .biblioref role="doc-biblioref"}; [Seed & Tomasello, 2010](#ch7.xhtml_ref7_56){#ch7.xhtml_R_ref7_56 .biblioref role="doc-biblioref"}; [Spelke et al., 1992](#ch7.xhtml_ref7_61){#ch7.xhtml_R_ref7_61 .biblioref role="doc-biblioref"}). In Conceptual Semantics, ontological categories are the main "building blocks" of the CSSs, with Jackendoff making an explicit analogy to the "parts of speech" or word classes of morpho-syntax to capture hypotheses about the role of ontological categories in the discovery of early word classes ([Grimshaw, 1981](#ch7.xhtml_ref7_28){#ch7.xhtml_R_ref7_28 .biblioref role="doc-biblioref"}; [Macnamara, 1982](#ch7.xhtml_ref7_44){#ch7.xhtml_R_ref7_44 .biblioref role="doc-biblioref"}; [Pinker, 1984](#ch7.xhtml_ref7_53){.biblioref role="doc-biblioref"}).

It would be a mistake to claim that first exposure adult L2 learners are in exactly the same position as infants at the initial stage of exposure. Adults have rich and mature conceptual systems, detailed knowledge of L1 phonology and morpho-syntax, and (often) large L1 vocabularies. They nonetheless face the challenge of learning language-specific mappings between concepts and forms. Children use many early words for THINGs, INDIVIDUALs, ACTIONs, and PATHs (L. Bloom, [1970](#ch7.xhtml_ref7_4){#ch7.xhtml_R_ref7_4 .biblioref role="doc-biblioref"}, [1973](#ch7.xhtml_ref7_5){#ch7.xhtml_R_ref7_5 .biblioref role="doc-biblioref"}; P. [Bloom, 2001](#ch7.xhtml_ref7_6){#ch7.xhtml_R_ref7_6 .biblioref role="doc-biblioref"}; [Bowerman, 1989](#ch7.xhtml_ref7_8){#ch7.xhtml_R_ref7_8 .biblioref role="doc-biblioref"}; [Smiley & Huttenlocher, 1995](#ch7.xhtml_ref7_59){#ch7.xhtml_R_ref7_59 .biblioref role="doc-biblioref"}; inter alia). Since these concepts are expressed already at the one-word stage by language learners whose conceptual systems are immature in comparison to the adult systems they must ultimately attain, we expect that adult L2ers will also show early use of the same concepts. Specifically, we predict that first exposure adult L2ers exploit the following mappings in their initial word learning:[^12]

``` {#ch7.xhtml_preformat7_2}

(2) The semantic bootstrapping hypothesis
    a.  THING-concepts         ↔        Noun
    b.  ACTION-concepts        ↔        Verb
    c.  PLACE-concepts         ↔        Noun
    d.  PATH-concepts          ↔        Preposition/Post-position
    e.  MANNER-concepts        ↔        Adverb
    f.  SIZE-concepts          ↔        Adjective
```

Only Nouns and Verbs are uncontroversially universal word classes, thus only (2a--c) are clearly cross-linguistically motivated (for detailed discussion see [Baker, 2003](#ch7.xhtml_ref7_3){#ch7.xhtml_R_ref7_3 .biblioref role="doc-biblioref"}; [Chafe, 2012](#ch7.xhtml_ref7_18){#ch7.xhtml_R_ref7_18 .biblioref role="doc-biblioref"}; [Dixon, 1982](#ch7.xhtml_ref7_20){#ch7.xhtml_R_ref7_20 .biblioref role="doc-biblioref"}; [Lu, 2018](#ch7.xhtml_ref7_42){#ch7.xhtml_R_ref7_42 .biblioref role="doc-biblioref"}).[^13] Jackendoff ([1983](#ch7.xhtml_ref7_31){#ch7.xhtml_R_ref7_31 .biblioref role="doc-biblioref"}, pp. 48--56), however, has argued that no interpretation of (3) would be possible if the ontological categories shown in brackets were not already representable in CSSs.[]{#ch7.xhtml_p90 .pagebreak aria-label=" page 90. " role="doc-pagebreak"}

``` {#ch7.xhtml_preformat7_3}

(3) a.  Your shoes are over there.     [pointing to a spatial area = PLACE]
    b.  We have to put your dress on this way.     [pulling the dress on over the arms and head = MANNER]
    c.  It was this big.    [demonstrating a SIZE using the hands to display a dimension]
    d.  Can you do this?      [demonstrating an ACTION]
```

By rooting the emergence of ontological categories in SSs and linking those ontological categories to the building blocks of CSSs, as well as investigating in detail the words addressed to children in child-directed speech and the meanings contexts might make available, a more detailed story about how not only individual words but also word classes emerge can be proposed in first language acquisition (*pace* P. [Bloom, 2001](#ch7.xhtml_ref7_6){.biblioref role="doc-biblioref"}). By pursuing the same logic in our controlled exposure studies, we will ultimately be in a better position to compare the functioning of learning mechanisms in L1 and L2 populations, permitting us to identify similarities and differences.
:::

::: {.section aria-labelledby="sec7_3_2"}
### Basic Predicates and Verb Learning {#ch7.xhtml_sec7_3_2}

MOTION-EVENTs are a subset of ACTION-EVENTs. In (4a), an intransitive verb expresses a MOTION-EVENT requiring a THEME argument (expressed by the subject noun phrase \[NP\]) and a PATH argument overtly expressed via prepositional phrases.

``` {#ch7.xhtml_preformat7_4}
(4) a.  John went from London to Peterborough (ambled, cycled, drove, …)
    b.  [GO ([JOHN]INDIVIDUAL-i ), (FROM [LONDON]PLACE-j, TO [PETERBOROUGH]PLACE-k)PATH-l]EVENT-m 
    c.  [GO (ARGUMENT), (PATH)  ]           
             MANNER: SLOWLY/ON FOOT/with INSTRUMENT: BICYCLE, …     EVENT
    d.  Theme Tier:  THEME = i, SOURCE = j, GOAL = k
    e.  Agency Tier:  AGENT = i
```

The motion concept is captured by a universal predicate GO.[^14] The Prepositional Phrases in (4a) map to a BOUNDED PATH in (4b). The semantic roles AGENT, THEME, SOURCE, GOAL are shown in (4d, e) on two separate tiers. The Theme Tier captures displacements in space: THEMEs are entities that move in space or are located in space; the boundaries of the PATH are expressed here. The Agency Tier captures the fact that certain kinds of ACTION-concepts can involve intentional AGENTs, ACTORs (to whom we do not impute intentions), and PATIENTs ([Jackendoff, 1983](#ch7.xhtml_ref7_31){.biblioref role="doc-biblioref"}).

There are many verbs that can appear in the syntactic frame of (4a) {*go*, *amble*, *cycle*, *drive*, ... } because this construction defines a semantic field MOTION (cf. [Lyons, 1977](#ch7.xhtml_ref7_43){#ch7.xhtml_R_ref7_43 .biblioref role="doc-biblioref"}, pp. 250--261). Different verbs can incorporate different MANNERS-OF-MOTION (as in (4c)) ([Jackendoff, 1990](#ch7.xhtml_ref7_33){.biblioref role="doc-biblioref"}, pp. 25--27). In order to grasp the difference between the basic motion verb *go* and verbs like *walk*, *drive*, *fly*, ... learners presumably scan perceptual stimuli for evidence of MANNER differences expressed by these lexemes. SSs will provide critical information for encoding such differences as type of gait or type of foot motion (*amble* vs. *walk*, *walk* vs. *peddle*), speed of gait (*trot* vs. *canter* vs. *gallop*) and use of instruments (*cycle* vs. *drive* vs. *fly*). It is linguistic cognition, however, that decides whether conceptual differences are expressed as MANNER differences on lexemes or as aspectual/temporal differences on functional categories.

[Premack (1990)](#ch7.xhtml_ref7_54){#ch7.xhtml_R_ref7_54 .biblioref role="doc-biblioref"} argues that there is an innate bias to discern MOTION-EVENTs in visual perception, and that they are also crucial to the discernment of causation. Consider (5):[]{#ch7.xhtml_p91 .pagebreak aria-label=" page 91. " role="doc-pagebreak"}

``` {#ch7.xhtml_preformat7_5}

(5) a. Pingu rolled over the ice.
    b. Pingu pushed the barrel over the ice.
```

Since Pingu can self-propel, (5a) will evoke a CSS similar to (4b) with rolling as a MANNER-OF-MOTION. Since the barrel cannot self-propel, if it moves and we see Pingu acting on it with physical contact, we impute intentions to *Pingu* to get the barrel to move through space by exerting force on it. The NP *Pingu* then expresses CAUSER and AGENT roles of one event, the NP *the barrel* expresses PATIENT role of one event and THEME role of a second event. See (6) and (7):

``` {#ch7.xhtml_preformat7_6}

(6) [CAUSE (ARGUMENTi), ([GO (ARGUMENTj), (PATH)]EVENT )]EVENT 
(7) [CAUSE ([PINGU]INDIVIDUAL/PENGUIN)i  ([GO ([BARREL]THING)j, ([OVER ([ICEDEF]PLACE-l )]ROUTE-PATH-k)]EVENT-m)]EVENT-n   
    Agency Tier Event-n: AGENT = i, PATIENT = j
    Theme Tier Event-n:
    Agency Tier Event-m: 
    Theme Tier Event-m: THEME = j, PATH = k
    Referential Tier: i, j, k, l, m, n
```

In our Lab-Persian study (Carroll & Hracs, [2021](#ch7.xhtml_ref7_14){.biblioref role="doc-biblioref"}, [2022](#ch7.xhtml_ref7_15){.biblioref role="doc-biblioref"}), we exposed participants to MOTION-EVENTs involving self-propulsion and MOTION-CAUSATION involving force-physics.

Our studies introduce referents into a discourse by relying on pragmatic anaphora typical of ostensive definitions of INDIVIDUAL-, THING-, or SUBSTANCE-concepts. By hypothesis, we assume participants infer a CSS with a universal predicate BE that takes two arguments, one for the ostensively defined referent, the second for the NAME. See (8):

``` {#ch7.xhtml_preformat7_7}

(8) a.  This (pointing to picture of Pingu the penguin) is Pingu.
    b.  [BE ([visual image goes here]INDIVIDUAL-TOKEN-i), ([PINGU]INDIVIDUAL TOKEN-j )]STATE-k 
        Referential Tier: i, j, k
```

Notice that such "naming" events involve the attribution of the referent as 'in' a STATE. This is shown as the ontological category of the proposition in (8b). Furthermore, we adopt the quotational theory of proper names ([Kneale, 1962](#ch7.xhtml_ref7_40){#ch7.xhtml_R_ref7_40 .biblioref role="doc-biblioref"}; Katz, [1977](#ch7.xhtml_ref7_37){#ch7.xhtml_R_ref7_37 .biblioref role="doc-biblioref"}, [2001](#ch7.xhtml_ref7_38){#ch7.xhtml_R_ref7_38 .biblioref role="doc-biblioref"}; [Geurts, 1997](#ch7.xhtml_ref7_24){#ch7.xhtml_R_ref7_24 .biblioref role="doc-biblioref"}) which claims that proper names express the concept that the referent of the proper name bears the second argument of (8a) as its moniker. For the post-copula and post-verbal NPs of (9a,b,c), we have the concepts loosely paraphrased in (10):

``` {#ch7.xhtml_preformat7_8}
(9) a.  This (pointing to a photo of Jon) is Jon.
    b.  That one (pointing to a dog in the park) is Fido.
    c.  There you see the Ponte Vecchio.
```

``` {#ch7.xhtml_preformat7_118}
(10) a.  the TOKENHUMAN which is the bearer of [dʒɑ:n]   (spelling Jon)
     b.  the TOKENDOG which is the bearer of [faj.do]   (spelling Fido)
     c.  the TOKENBRIDGE which is the bearer of [pɔn.te.vɛk.jo]   (spelling Ponte Vecchio)
```

Proper names differ from class names in the inferences they license. Thus, from the fact that one person we know who bears the name *Jon* is 1.95 meters tall and plays baseball, we do not infer that other *Jons* will have the same traits. Notice too that the same line drawing of a baseball []{#ch7.xhtml_p92 .pagebreak aria-label=" page 92. " role="doc-pagebreak"}player ([Figure 7.1](#ch7.xhtml_fig7_1){#ch7.xhtml_R_fig7_1}) that licenses the CSS appropriate for *This is Jon* would also license the inference *This is a baseball player* or *Jon is a baseball player*, the latter two sentences being "classification" sentences.

![[[Figure]{.label} [7.1 ]{.ordinal}Line drawing of baseball player (Carroll,](#ch7.xhtml_R_fig7_1){.backlink aria-label=" This link returns to its first in-text citation. " role="doc-backlink"} [2012](#ch7.xhtml_ref7_12){#ch7.xhtml_R_ref7_12 .biblioref role="doc-biblioref"}, [2014](#ch7.xhtml_ref7_13){#ch7.xhtml_R_ref7_13 .biblioref role="doc-biblioref"}). [Image created by Danny Kirk.]{.credit role="doc-credit"}](images/fig7_1_B.jpg){#fig7_1 alt="Line art of a baseball player who is holding a baseball bat."}

As Katz ([1977](#ch7.xhtml_ref7_37){.biblioref role="doc-biblioref"}, [2001](#ch7.xhtml_ref7_38){.biblioref role="doc-biblioref"}) insisted, a compositional semantics for a language (as opposed to a theory of reference) must explain how NPs like *Pingu* compose with the rest of the sentence in (8a) to give, we say, the CSS in (8b). Katz also insisted that a theory of linguistic meaning must explain the interpretation of "improper" proper names like *the Centre Street Bridge*, *the Rotman Building*, *221B Baker Street*, and other compound names where the head noun is a common noun. In fact, as serious linguistic analysis has shown ([Jonasson, 1994](#ch7.xhtml_ref7_36){#ch7.xhtml_R_ref7_36 .biblioref role="doc-biblioref"}; [Kleiber, 2004](#ch7.xhtml_ref7_39){#ch7.xhtml_R_ref7_39 .biblioref role="doc-biblioref"}), there is no clear break in the distribution of common nouns and proper names. Moreover, presentational sentences like (8a) and (9) have in some languages a similar syntactic shape as classification sentences like (11) and (12):

``` {#ch7.xhtml_preformat7_9}

(11) a. That one (pointing to a dog in the park) is a dog.
     b. Da (pointing to a picture of a dog)         sehen    Sie         einen                 Hund.
        There                                       see      you         a.Masc.Acc.           dog
       ‘There you see a dog.’ (German)
     c. Da (pointing to a picture
        of a football player)   steht                        ein                Fußballer.
        There                   stand.3rdP.present           a.Masc.Nom         football player
        ‘There stands a football player.’ (German)
```

``` {#ch7.xhtml_preformat7_19}
(12) a. Ini adalah Nina.
        ‘There is Nina.’ (Indonesian)
     b. Ini adalah roti.
        ‘There is a loaf of bread/are loaves of bread.’ (Indonesian)
```

Finally, the same distributional evidence might involve classification, not reference.[]{#ch7.xhtml_p93 .pagebreak aria-label=" page 93. " role="doc-pagebreak"}

``` {#ch7.xhtml_preformat7_10}
 
(13) a. This (pointing) is Pingu.
     b. This (pointing) is a penguin.
     c. Penguins (pointing to some penguins in a zoo pool) are birds.
```

In short, ostensive definition and other gestures will not tell us what the speaker's communication intention was (to name vs. to classify). Thus, it is the inference the learner draws about lexical semantics, not the picture-to-NP sound form mapping, nor distributional analysis of the sound forms, that gives us a naming interpretation (13a) versus a classification interpretation (13b) versus a generic interpretation (13c).

Example (13c) is designed to emphasize that visual stimuli will not express the TOKEN/TYPE distinction crucial for classification assertions ([Jackendoff, 1983](#ch7.xhtml_ref7_31){.biblioref role="doc-biblioref"}). SSs, however, present language learners with rich information for building an IDENTITY of an INDIVIDUAL, for deciding if an entity is a PENGUIN or an OSTRICH, and even for inferring the role of functional categories like Classifiers which often are used to focus our attention on the SIZE and SHAPE of entities ([Senft, 2000](#ch7.xhtml_ref7_57){#ch7.xhtml_R_ref7_57 .biblioref role="doc-biblioref"}). The line drawing of the baseball player, used in our Lab-German studies, licenses the inference that the individual depicted plays baseball in some organized fashion (requiring a uniform). The picture necessarily presents us with a TOKEN of an individual with specific skin and hair colour, precise facial and body features, a given pose or posture, and so on. All of these traits can be taken in at a glance and the participants in our Lab-German studies (Carroll, [2012](#ch7.xhtml_ref7_12){.biblioref role="doc-biblioref"}, [2014](#ch7.xhtml_ref7_13){.biblioref role="doc-biblioref"}) did not hesitate to invoke them in debriefing sessions where they talked in English about "the people" they had seen. Adult L2ers, even at the initial stage of learning, cognize "naming" situations and the ontological category of specific proper names. When used as arguments of verbs expressing, e.g., MOTION-EVENTs or CAUSATION-EVENTs, they will cognize that names express a semantic role that emerges as part of the meaning of that verb (AGENT, ACTOR, THEME, EXPERIENCER, ...). All of these functions find expression in CSSs.
:::
:::::

:::::::: {.section aria-labelledby="sec7_6"}
## Current Contributions and Research {#ch7.xhtml_sec7_6}

::: {.section aria-labelledby="sec7_6_1"}
### Ontological Categories Are Input to *ab initio* Learners {#ch7.xhtml_sec7_6_1}

In each study presented in this section, adult participants were exposed to an L2 for the first time, using auditory and visual stimuli. Designs and methods were similar across all studies, starting with training phases, followed by test phases. Target forms appeared in multiple syntactic distributions involving pragmatic anaphora. Input was determined by the different goals of each study: investigating the role of cognate forms in facilitating segmentation and form-picture mappings (Carroll, [2012](#ch7.xhtml_ref7_12){.biblioref role="doc-biblioref"}, [2014](#ch7.xhtml_ref7_13){.biblioref role="doc-biblioref"}), transfer of phonological knowledge as measured on the pronunciation of target words ([Carroll & Windsor, 2015](#ch7.xhtml_ref7_17){#ch7.xhtml_R_ref7_17 .biblioref role="doc-biblioref"}), the acquisition of grammatical Number (Carroll & Hracs, [2021](#ch7.xhtml_ref7_14){.biblioref role="doc-biblioref"}, [2022](#ch7.xhtml_ref7_15){.biblioref role="doc-biblioref"}; [Carroll & Widjaja, 2013](#ch7.xhtml_ref7_16){#ch7.xhtml_R_ref7_16 .biblioref role="doc-biblioref"}), means of expressing concepts via count/mass syntax (Carroll & Hracs, [2021](#ch7.xhtml_ref7_14){.biblioref role="doc-biblioref"}, [2022](#ch7.xhtml_ref7_15){.biblioref role="doc-biblioref"}), learning grammatical Classifier constructions ([Carroll & Widjaja, 2013](#ch7.xhtml_ref7_16){.biblioref role="doc-biblioref"}). The stimuli chosen for exposure purposes were selected based on a careful linguistic-theoretically motivated analysis of the "learning problem" learners had to solve, a unique feature of our research.
:::

::: {.section aria-labelledby="sec7_3_4"}
### INDIVIDUALs and Proper Names {#ch7.xhtml_sec7_3_4}

In our Lab-German studies (Carroll, [2012](#ch7.xhtml_ref7_12){.biblioref role="doc-biblioref"}, [2014](#ch7.xhtml_ref7_13){.biblioref role="doc-biblioref"}), participants heard randomized recordings of continuous speech while looking simultaneously at colourful line drawings of people (like the baseball player above). Participants were asked in English to "learn their names". In the training []{#ch7.xhtml_p94 .pagebreak aria-label=" page 94. " role="doc-pagebreak"}phase, participants saw 20 visual stimuli while they simultaneously heard 20 German utterances. In the Lab-German study contrasting cognate vs. non-cognate first names, participants heard, e.g., *[Das]{.underline} ist Sonja* 'That is Sonja' (cognate), *[Hier]{.underline} ist Femke* 'Here is Femke' (non-cognate), *[Hier]{.underline} sehen Sie Frank* 'Here see you Frank' (cognate), *[Da]{.underline} steht Sönke* 'There stands Sönke' (non-cognate), matched to unique line drawings. Target names were always utterance-final, a position shown to facilitate segmentation on first exposure ([Shoemaker & Rast, 2013](#ch7.xhtml_ref7_58){#ch7.xhtml_R_ref7_58 .biblioref role="doc-biblioref"}). Immediately after the final utterance of a training trial, participants were tested on a binary forced-choice task where they heard the question corresponding to the syntax of the original presentational sentences. In the test utterances, the target noun could be the first or the second noun in a NP conjunction, e.g., *Sehen Sie hier Frank oder Franz?* 'See you here Frank or Franz?'

We were looking to see if L2 sound forms similar to L1 words (L1 words likely to be familiar to our participants) would affect L2 segmentation and word recognition in ways similar to "lexical effects" in L1 segmentation and word recognition ([Mattys & Melhorn, 2007](#ch7.xhtml_ref7_45){#ch7.xhtml_R_ref7_45 .biblioref role="doc-biblioref"}; [Mattys et al., 2005](#ch7.xhtml_ref7_46){#ch7.xhtml_R_ref7_46 .biblioref role="doc-biblioref"}). Study 1 provided baseline data in English (*Anna* \[\'æ. nə\] and *Frank* \[\'fræŋk\]) versus German cognate names (like *Anna* \[\'a.nə\] and *Frank* \[\'fʁaŋk\]). Study 2 compared the same German cognate names to non-cognate names like *Lutz* \[lʋts\] and *Femke* \[\'fem.kə\]. Study 3 compared single cognate names that were one- to four-syllables long with first + last names that were four- to seven-syllables long. We also compared the performance of first exposure learners to Anglophones who had had one to two semesters of German. Across the various studies, results strongly suggest that cognates automatically activated L1 lexical entries as measured by significantly faster response latencies on the cognate items. Nevertheless, our participants also successfully learned non-cognate single names and much longer first and last names (names like *Martina Pfannenschmidt* and *Hannelore Fleischhauer*). Interestingly, once a form was learned, its cognate/non-cognate status or length made no difference to accuracy scores.
:::

::::: {.section aria-labelledby="sec7_3_5"}
### THING-Concepts and Learning Grammatical Number/Classifier Constructions {#ch7.xhtml_sec7_3_5}

::: {.section aria-labelledby="sec7_3_5_1"}
#### The Lab-Indonesian Study {#ch7.xhtml_sec7_3_5_1}

Our Lab-Indonesian study ([Carroll & Widjaja, 2013](#ch7.xhtml_ref7_16){.biblioref role="doc-biblioref"}) asked if first exposure learners could learn typologically novel morpho-syntactic phenomena. One learning problem we studied was the learning of a novel Plural marker expressed as a reduplicative morpheme. A second learning problem was the learning of Numeral + Classifier constructions. Lab-Indonesian, like real Indonesian, lacks determiners; bare NPs express both singleton and multiple entities in indefinite reference contexts. Lab-Indonesian, like real Indonesian, has a Plural-marker in the form of an abstract templatic morpheme based on the bare noun (CV.CV or CV.CVC).

``` {#ch7.xhtml_preformat7_11}

(14) a. Ini adalah roti.
        ‘This is/These are bread(s).’
     b. Ini adalah roti-roti.
        ‘These are breads.’ (= loaves of bread, not types of breads)
     c. Ini adalah buku.
        ‘This is/These are book(s).’
     d. Ini adalah buku-buku.
        ‘These are books.’
```

Lab-Indonesian also has Numeral Classifier constructions: a Classifier denoting ANIMALs, a second Classifier denoting THINGs, and a third Classifier denoting PORTIONS OF WHOLE []{#ch7.xhtml_p95 .pagebreak aria-label=" page 95. " role="doc-pagebreak"}THINGs. Visual stimuli consisted of pictures of animals or toys depicting animals (like chickens, zebras, and pandas), of entities (like playing cards, books, and bottles of water), and of foodstuffs (like lemons, apples, and bread) either intact or as slices or portions (see [Widjaja (2010)](#ch7.xhtml_ref7_64){#ch7.xhtml_R_ref7_64 .biblioref role="doc-biblioref"} for details). We tested participants' interpretations and recall of linguistic stimuli and ability to generalize what they had learned to novel (untrained) sound forms and new visual stimuli.

Results showed excellent, rapid learning of bare nouns: 29/29 participants segmented and learned them starting from the first utterances of the first training trial and learned to criterion within an average of 2 training trials. Somewhat fewer participants (22/29) learned the Plural and Classifier Constructions to criterion. More training was required for those that did: 3.27 mean training trials for the Plural morpheme and 4.09 for the Numeral Classifier constructions. Still, accuracy scores on training trial 1 were all significantly above chance for all three constructions. To learn the correct interpretation of the bare NP, our participants had to observe the contingency between single entities depicted in visual stimuli and the bare NP as well as multiple entities and the bare NP. To learn the Plural, our participants had to attend to the presence of multiple THINGs in the pictures and observe the contingency between the reduplicative form and the multiple entities in the pictures. To learn the Numeral Classifier construction, participants had to attend in training to the Arabic numerals "2" and "3" printed on the pictures and note the correspondence between "2" and the sound form /du/ and "3" and the sound form /ta/. Equally important, they had to attend to and observe that the entities depicted belonged to specific conceptual classes (THING, ANIMAL, and PORTIONS OF WHOLEs) and observe the contingency between these conceptual classes and the presence in the linguistic stimuli of the words *kar* (for ANIMALs) or *pak* (for THING-concepts, including ARTEFACTs and FOODSTUFFs) or *sim* (for slices or portions of FOODSTUFFs). Ontological categories were relevant not only for learning specific Lab-Indonesian words like *kuda* 'horse', *zebra* 'zebra', *balon* 'balloon', *koin* 'coin', *keju* 'cheese', and *roti* 'bread', they were also relevant for learning functional categories (the Classifiers). On the generalization test (a free recall test), participants preferred the bare noun construction over the other two in terms of frequency of response (53.1% bare nouns, 36.1% Numeral Classifier constructions, 10.9% Plural). They were accurate (above 80%) on the bare and Numeral Classifier construction. Participants were less accurate on the Plural because they performed poorly on (irregular) partial reduplications, showing that they had internalized an abstract PS and were not merely imitating (acoustic) wave forms. Shown pictures where any of the constructions would result in a correct response, participants produced all three constructions, suggesting that they had internalized at least some aspects of the semantics of each construction. Taking results together, we conclude that the visual stimuli provided a basis for activating known conceptual categories. These concepts, we believe, supported distributional learning mechanisms which made it possible for participants to attend to the recurrent locations of segmented prosodic forms and to learn a novel form of Plural-marking and, as well, Classifier constructions, neither of which are part of the L1.
:::

::: {.section aria-labelledby="sec7_3_5_2"}
#### The Lab-Persian Study {#ch7.xhtml_sec7_3_5_2}

On the basis of our previous studies, we had become more aware of the problematic assumption that pictures would make meanings available. The question is not: "Do they?", but rather "Which meanings?" and "Why just those meanings and not others?" Accordingly, we wanted to examine sensitivity to the contingencies of pictures that contained many individuals, entities, complex backgrounds and foregrounds. We also wanted to study the learning of grammatical paradigms. Accordingly, our Lab-Persian stimuli were designed to offer complex *contrasts*: Singular, Dual, Plural, Collective, and Mass. Visual stimuli were also more complex. In natural []{#ch7.xhtml_p96 .pagebreak aria-label=" page 96. " role="doc-pagebreak"}situations, a learner might be looking at multiple objects, but the speaker might choose to refer to one, a pair, a subset of the total, or none at all. Similarly, the learner might be looking at a bowl of soup and the speaker might refer to the bowl (THING-concept) or to the soup it contains (SUBSTANCE-concept), to the colour of the soup (PROPERTY) or to the material the bowl is made of (SUBSTANCE).

The Lab-Persian study was divided into phases with plenty of opportunity to hear nouns and verbs. We elicited L1-descriptions of the visual stimuli to have baseline data on what concepts learners were attending to and then trained them on Lab-Persian linguistic stimuli in the presence of still pictures or videos (15b, c).

``` {#ch7.xhtml_preformat7_12}

(15) a. English-language description of video clips (no Lab-Persian stimuli)
     b. Noun-learning phase (24 unique bare nouns heard in three different positions in utterances in three different constructions containing pragmatic anaphors; visual stimuli = static images)
     c. Verb-learning phase (5 verbs presented 3 times each in 3 different positions in utterances; nouns were drawn from the Noun-learning phase; visual stimuli = video clips)
```

To create suitable visual stimuli, we searched *Pingu* videos ([Gutmann & Brueggemann, 1990](#ch7.xhtml_ref7_29){#ch7.xhtml_R_ref7_29 .biblioref role="doc-biblioref"}--2006) for multiple instances of images of THINGs, INDIVIDUALs, and SUBSTANCEs occurring as single entities, pairs of entities, groups of three or more entities, and as parts of sets or collections (e.g., AGGREGATEs such as furniture or dishes). The single occurrences would co-occur with linguistic stimuli containing a Singular suffix, the pairs would co-occur with the Dual suffix, groups of entities from the same conceptual category would co-occur with the Plural suffix, and the sets or collections (entities drawn from different conceptual categories) would co-occur with the Collective suffix. The process was repeated to find instances of SUBSTANCEs. Then we went through the videos looking for actions involving the displacement in space of the animated penguins (whole body movement) to evoke MOTION-EVENT type propositions, e.g., Pingu moving towards his family, or Pingu moving towards a small water hole in the ice landscape. We sought video segments where a penguin was causing an object to move (e.g., by pushing it). These were intended to evoke \[CAUSE (x) (\[GO (y), (\[PATH\]) \]~EVENT)~ \]~EVENT~ propositions. We sought other actions that required participants to attend more closely to body part motions (a penguin is touching an object and/or manipulating it). Finally, we looked for actions that involved orienting the face to license an inference to a mental state (SEE).

Reported below for each of the learning phases are mean accuracy scores and residual reaction times on initial trials (i.e., on first exposure) as well as the total number of trials needed to reach criterion (100%). A summary of the means for the Noun-learning phase can be seen in [Table 7.1](#ch7.xhtml_table7_1){#ch7.xhtml_R_table7_1}.

+-------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------+
| Noun-Learning Phase                                                                                                                             |
+------------------+------------------------------------------+----------------------------------------+------------------------------------------+
| Conceptual Class | Mean Accuracy Score on Initial Trial (%) | Mean Residual RT on Initial Trial (ms) | Mean Number of Trials to Reach Criterion |
+==================+==========================================+========================================+==========================================+
| THING            | 59.85                                    | 981.49                                 | 2.28                                     |
+------------------+------------------------------------------+----------------------------------------+------------------------------------------+
| INDIVIDUAL       | 57.58                                    | 1114.55                                | 2.05                                     |
+------------------+------------------------------------------+----------------------------------------+------------------------------------------+
| AGGREGATE        | 56.82                                    | 1364.23                                | 2.63                                     |
+------------------+------------------------------------------+----------------------------------------+------------------------------------------+
| SUBSTANCE        | 63.64                                    | 1337.62                                | 2.08                                     |
+------------------+------------------------------------------+----------------------------------------+------------------------------------------+

: [[Table ]{.label}[7.1 ]{.ordinal}Accuracy Scores, Reaction Times, and Trials-to-Criterion for the Noun-Learning Phase](#ch7.xhtml_R_table7_1){.backlink aria-label=" This link returns to its first in-text citation. " role="doc-backlink"} {#table7_1}

[]{#ch7.xhtml_p97 .pagebreak aria-label=" page 97. " role="doc-pagebreak"}Out of 22 participants, five performed significantly above chance on the first training trial. There was no significant difference in mean accuracy scores among the conceptual classes, however, stimuli items expressing AGGREGATE-concepts showed significantly higher residual reaction times and required more exposures to learn. In our visual stimuli, we used red arrows to visually highlight the different objects forming an AGGREGATE-concept. The higher reaction times suggest that the visual search or the inference (or both) were taking longer to execute than searching for referents of THING-, INDIVIDUAL-, or SUBSTANCE-concepts.

In the Verb-learning phase, one participant performed significantly above chance, one performed significantly below chance, and 20/22 participants did not perform significantly different from chance on the initial trial. This independently confirms, with a superior methodology, the claims of [Gillette et al. (1999)](#ch7.xhtml_ref7_25){#ch7.xhtml_R_ref7_25 .biblioref role="doc-biblioref"}, [Medina et al. (2011)](#ch7.xhtml_ref7_47){#ch7.xhtml_R_ref7_47 .biblioref role="doc-biblioref"}, and [Snedeker and Gleitman (2004)](#ch7.xhtml_ref7_60){#ch7.xhtml_R_ref7_60 .biblioref role="doc-biblioref"} that the perceptual input needed to figure out the concepts that verbs express is hard to identify. Means for the Verb-learning phase are shown in [Table 7.2](#ch7.xhtml_table7_2){#ch7.xhtml_R_table7_2}.

+-------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------+
| Verb-learning Phase                                                                                                                             |
+------------------+------------------------------------------+----------------------------------------+------------------------------------------+
| Conceptual Class | Mean Accuracy Score on Initial Trial (%) | Mean Residual RT on Initial Trial (ms) | Mean Number of Trials to Reach Criterion |
+==================+==========================================+========================================+==========================================+
| MOTION-EVENT     | 57.58                                    | 1124.93                                | 2.94                                     |
+------------------+------------------------------------------+----------------------------------------+------------------------------------------+
| CAUSE.GO-EVENT   | 48.48                                    | 1379.37                                | 3.06                                     |
+------------------+------------------------------------------+----------------------------------------+------------------------------------------+
| ACTION-hand      | 39.39                                    | 1389.66                                | 3.14                                     |
+------------------+------------------------------------------+----------------------------------------+------------------------------------------+
| EXPERIENCE       | 53.03                                    | 1206.15                                | 2.85                                     |
+------------------+------------------------------------------+----------------------------------------+------------------------------------------+

: [[Table ]{.label}[7.2 ]{.ordinal}Accuracy Scores, Reaction Times, and Trials-to-Criterion for the Verb-Learning Phase](#ch7.xhtml_R_table7_2){.backlink aria-label=" This link returns to its first in-text citation. " role="doc-backlink"} {#table7_2}

Although there are no significant differences among the conceptual classes, results show that participants had greater difficulty with verbs /læms_kærd/ 'touch' and /ɛs.tɛ.fæ.\'dɛ_kærd/ 'handle/use'. One possible explanation for this is that there is little to distinguish the two verbs in terms of visual cues, for example, when a penguin is touching an object with its flipper vs. when it is handling an object with its flipper. Such verb meanings may be easier to learn when the INDIVIDUALs inferable from visual stimuli have fingers and hands and can be seen to be holding an object within a hand. The data do confirm that whole-body MOTION-EVENTs are easier to visually detect than ACTION-EVENTs requiring attention to body-part motions.
:::
:::::
::::::::

::: {.section aria-labelledby="sec7_4"}
## Main Research Methods {#ch7.xhtml_sec7_4}

Distinguishing between "exposure" and "input" allows us to make precise claims about learning on the basis of specific properties of the target language and visual stimuli. In particular, our first exposure studies provide preliminary evidence that when visual stimuli are carefully chosen, they lead learners to encode ontological categories like THING, SUBSTANCE, INDIVIDUAL, MOTION-ACTION, PHYSICAL-ACTION, and even perceptual states like SEEing. These meanings are extracted from visual stimuli via SSs and linked to CSSs. Our Lab-German studies show adults readily learn to name persons. Our Lab-Indonesian study showed that participants can see tokens of single objects in pictures but infer that a reduplicative form denotes plurality. It also showed that participants can infer PART/WHOLE relations and map that meaning to a given Classifier form. Our Lab-Persian project provides independent evidence, supporting the []{#ch7.xhtml_p98 .pagebreak aria-label=" page 98. " role="doc-pagebreak"}findings of the Human Simulation Project ([Gillette, et al., 1999](#ch7.xhtml_ref7_25){.biblioref role="doc-biblioref"}; [Piccin & Waxman, 2007](#ch7.xhtml_ref7_52){#ch7.xhtml_R_ref7_52 .biblioref role="doc-biblioref"}, inter alia), that the concepts that map to verbs are harder to discern in visual stimuli than the concepts that map to nouns even when the EVENTs we see are highly salient. Essentially, we demonstrate that adult learners successfully solve complex language learning problems that are not normally studied in Romance and Germanic L2s, on which we have the most L2 data, and they do so on the basis of limited exposure. In short, our research does more than document rapid segmentation and form-meaning mappings; it provides the beginnings of an explanation of the observations, rooted in the ability of learners to 'parse' both still pictures and dynamic videos into ontological categories and to map those categories to word classes and even grammatical functions.
:::

::: {.section .conclusion role="doc-conclusion"}
## Conclusions and Future Directions {#ch7.xhtml_sec7_5}

Our studies provide independent support for Conceptual Semantics, its meaning analyses, and its use of ontological categories and Spatial Structures. Given the widespread reliance on pictures in L2 research, testing, and teaching, we assume there is consensus that beginners rely on non-linguistic perceptual stimuli at initial stages of L2 learning. However, much discussion of early word learning fixes on reference, both with absolute beginners and with later early-stage learners. Reference ties form-meaning mappings to the context, but it is a blunt tool, incapable of telling us how learners distinguish associative mappings to token concepts from associative mappings to type concepts. It fails to tell us how word meanings combine to create the meanings of sentences. It fails to tell us how word classes map to concepts. Invoking ontological categories as major building blocks of semantic representations, we believe, moves us substantially forward. Obviously, we have just scratched the surface in exploring visual input in SLA, and the way in which adult L2 learners use ontological categories as kinds of concepts both in learning individual words and in mapping concepts to lexical and functional classes. We encourage others to explore this fascinating topic.
:::
