::: {.section .part role="doc-part"}
# []{#pt3.xhtml_p103 .pagebreak aria-label=" page 103. " role="doc-pagebreak"}[Part III ]{.chnum .ordinal}[Issues in Other Theories and Models[]{#pt3.xhtml_p104 .pagebreak aria-label=" page 104 is blank. " role="doc-pagebreak"}]{.chtitle} {#pt3.xhtml_h1}
:::

[]{#ch8.xhtml}

::: {}
# []{#ch8.xhtml_p105 .pagebreak aria-label=" page 105. " role="doc-pagebreak"}[8 ]{.chnum .ordinal}[VanPatten's Theory of Input Processing in L2 Acquisition]{.chtitle} {#ch8.xhtml_h1}

Michael J. Leeser

Florida State University

DOI: [10.4324/9781003041412-11](https://dx.doi.org/10.4324/9781003041412-11){aria-label="D.O.I. link to this document."}
:::

:::: {.section .introduction role="doc-introduction"}
## Introduction and Definitions {#ch8.xhtml_sec8_1}

Since the mid-l970s, the role of input has occupied a central role in second language acquisition (SLA) theory and research, and all major theories concur that learners cannot create a second language (L2) linguistic system without processing copious amounts of input---samples of language that communicate a message. Those messages in the input are encoded linguistically in lexical items, affixes attached to lexical items, and sentence structure, all of which serve as data for acquisition when learners are engaged in the comprehension of messages. For this reason, acquisition is (in some ways) a by-product of comprehension. At the same time, however, linguistic items are not all that learners use to understand messages. According to models of comprehension (e.g., [Kintsch, 1998](#ch8.xhtml_ref8_19){#ch8.xhtml_R_ref8_19 .biblioref role="doc-biblioref"}), comprehenders construct meaning using information from knowledge sources stored in long-term memory in addition to the lexical and morpho-syntactic forms present in the input. Furthermore, due to L2 learners' limited lexical and morphosyntactic knowledge and the resulting demands placed on processing resources during comprehension at early stages of acquisition, learners only understand meaning via certain kinds of linguistic elements in the input. Input processing (IP) is a theory[^15] in SLA developed by VanPatten (e.g., VanPatten, [1996](#ch8.xhtml_ref8_39){#ch8.xhtml_R_ref8_39 .biblioref role="doc-biblioref"}, [2004](#ch8.xhtml_ref8_42){#ch8.xhtml_R_ref8_42 .biblioref role="doc-biblioref"}, [2020](#ch8.xhtml_ref8_43){#ch8.xhtml_R_ref8_43 .biblioref role="doc-biblioref"}) that lays out a series of principles that guide how learners process these linguistic elements (i.e., connect meaning to surface features of language) and parse sentences during comprehension. More specifically, IP addresses the following questions ([VanPatten, 2020](#ch8.xhtml_ref8_43){.biblioref role="doc-biblioref"}):

1.  How do learners make initial form--meaning connections during comprehension?
2.  Why do they make some form--meaning connections and not others?
3.  What strategies do learners use to assign semantic and syntactic roles to nouns in a sentence (i.e., determine "who does what to whom"), and what are the consequences of these strategies for acquisition?

This chapter provides a brief historical perspective on IP and then discusses the principles of IP theory, the relationship of IP to other L2 theoretical frameworks, and some of the foundational studies and methodologies within IP research. Before doing so, however, it is worth highlighting the processes involved in comprehension itself given that IP is concerned with form--meaning connections and semantic/syntactic role assignment to nouns *during comprehension*.

::: {.section aria-labelledby="sec8_1_1"}
### []{#ch8.xhtml_p106 .pagebreak aria-label=" page 106. " role="doc-pagebreak"}Comprehension and IP {#ch8.xhtml_sec8_1_1}

Comprehension of spoken, written, or signed language involves a complex set of processes for understanding and constructing meaning. These processes are generally categorized according to lower and higher-level processes. Lower-level processes refer to "basic language understanding" ([McNamara & Magliano, 2009](#ch8.xhtml_ref8_28){#ch8.xhtml_R_ref8_28 .biblioref role="doc-biblioref"}, p. 302) and include word recognition and syntactic parsing (i.e., determining "who does what to whom"). Both word recognition and syntactic parsing are characterized as predominantly automatic bottom-up L1 processes due to the rich lexical knowledge and complete syntactic information that adult comprehenders possess in their native language. Higher-level processes include, at a minimum, the connecting of ideas, understanding the gist, and generating inferences about what is not explicitly stated based on background knowledge and previous experience. Thus, even though basic language understanding is essential for the construction of meaning, it is not all that comprehenders use. Rather, comprehension involves "connections between things that were previously disparate: the ideas expressed in the text and relevant prior knowledge" ([Kintsch, 1998](#ch8.xhtml_ref8_19){.biblioref role="doc-biblioref"}, p. 93).

In L2 comprehension, learners possess an incomplete L2 system, and ample evidence exists that L2 word recognition is slower and incurs greater processing costs than in the L1 (e.g., [Van Heuven et al., 1998](#ch8.xhtml_ref8_37){#ch8.xhtml_R_ref8_37 .biblioref role="doc-biblioref"}). Slower word recognition, lack of lexical knowledge, and L1--L2 differences in syntactic and semantic cues that determine "who does what to whom" clearly have consequences for how L2 learners might (not) parse sentences. Additionally, findings from a number of L2 sentence processing studies reveal that even advanced L2 learners "tend to have problems building or manipulating abstract syntactic representations in real time" ([Clahsen & Felser, 2018](#ch8.xhtml_ref8_7){#ch8.xhtml_R_ref8_7 .biblioref role="doc-biblioref"}, p. 694). For this reason, it cannot be assumed that the lower-level comprehension processes of L2 word recognition and syntactic parsing proceed automatically in the L2 or in the same way as they do in the L1, particularly for lower proficiency learners. Consequently, L2 learners may be even more likely to rely on extralinguistic information (i.e., schematic and contextual knowledge) in order to achieve some kind of coherent understanding of the input, potentially circumventing morphosyntactic analyses altogether ([Swain, 1995](#ch8.xhtml_ref8_35){#ch8.xhtml_R_ref8_35 .biblioref role="doc-biblioref"}). For example, consider the utterance in (1):

``` {#ch8.xhtml_preformat8_1}

(1)  No dormí nada anoche.         ‘I didn’t sleep at all last night.’
```

An L2 learner of Spanish might hear (1) and understand the meaning of the utterance via knowledge of lexical items such as *no*, *dormir* ('to sleep'), and *anoche* ('last night'). In addition, the learner may possess background information and know that the speaker was working all night or be able to tell from the speaker's face that they are extremely tired and most likely did not sleep much the night before. Furthermore, the learner may comprehend the semantic notion of PAST either from (a) the lexical item *anoche*, (b) contextual/background information (i.e., how tired the speaker looks, previous knowledge that the speaker was working the night before), and/or (c) the preterite suffix *-í* attached to *dorm-*. The point to be made is that comprehension can be achieved regardless of whether the learner relied on linguistic or extralinguistic information.

From this brief overview of comprehension processes and the differences between L1 and L2 comprehension, the following points should be clear:

- Understanding/Construction of meaning is central to comprehension.
- Comprehension is achieved via linguistic and non-linguistic means.
- L2 learners do not possess the same linguistic knowledge (lexical, morphosyntactic, etc.) in order for comprehension to proceed automatically as it does in L1.
- L2 comprehension is more effortful and consuming of processing resources and working memory.

[]{#ch8.xhtml_p107 .pagebreak aria-label=" page 107. " role="doc-pagebreak"}A result of the resource consuming processes of L2 comprehension is that learners only process a subset of the actual input to which they are exposed. Crucially, the term *process* refers to the linking between linguistic forms and their meanings and/or functions. For instance, when L2 English learners hear the word *dog*, they must (minimally) link a specific piece of phonology /dɔg/ to a concept in semantics of a furry, four-legged animal of a specific type. In a similar vein, when they encounter words like *helped*, *studied*, and *painted* they must associate /-t/, /-d/, and /-əd/ with a suffix attaching to a verb and the meaning PAST. This filtered, processed input or *intake* is then made available to be accommodated within the network of lexical, semantic, and syntactic relationships that comprise the learner's developing language system ([VanPatten, 2003](#ch8.xhtml_ref8_41){#ch8.xhtml_R_ref8_41 .biblioref role="doc-biblioref"}), as shown in [Figure 8.1](#ch8.xhtml_fig8_1){#ch8.xhtml_R_fig8_1}.

![[[Figure]{.label} [8.1 ]{.ordinal}The scope of IP.](#ch8.xhtml_R_fig8_1){.backlink aria-label=" This link returns to its first in-text citation. " role="doc-backlink"}](images/fig8_1_B.jpg){#fig8_1 alt="Details that show that input processing is the process between input and the creation of intake. Three components are shown: input, intake, and developing system. The figure shows that input processing happens in between the input and intake stage."}

The focus of IP is on the first set of processes in [Figure 8.1](#ch8.xhtml_fig8_1)---how learners attach meaning to form and parse sentences during moment-to-moment comprehension (i.e., the conversion of input to intake).
:::
::::

::: {.section aria-labelledby="sec8_2"}
## Historical Perspectives {#ch8.xhtml_sec8_2}

The distinction between the language to which learners are exposed (input) and the linguistic data that learners actually process (intake) has been attributed to [Corder (1967)](#ch8.xhtml_ref8_8){#ch8.xhtml_R_ref8_8 .biblioref role="doc-biblioref"}. In this seminal paper, "input is 'what goes in' not what is *available* for going in, and we may reasonably suppose that it is the learner who controls the input, or more properly his intake" (p. 165, emphasis original). As [Leeser et al. (2021)](#ch8.xhtml_ref8_25){#ch8.xhtml_R_ref8_25 .biblioref role="doc-biblioref"} point out, Corder's statement was critical for two reasons. The first was the acknowledgment that learners process (and acquire) only a subset of the linguistic data in the input. The second is that learners themselves control this process, not teachers.

The 1980s and early 1990s witnessed a marked increase in the application of information processing perspectives to address learner factors that control the conversion of input to intake, drawing upon research in cognitive psychology related to limitations on both focal attention during dual task performance (e.g., [Wickens, 1984](#ch8.xhtml_ref8_48){#ch8.xhtml_R_ref8_48 .biblioref role="doc-biblioref"}) and working memory capacity (e.g., [Just & Carpenter, 1992](#ch8.xhtml_ref8_17){#ch8.xhtml_R_ref8_17 .biblioref role="doc-biblioref"}). Regarding attention, an issue of considerable importance was the role of conscious attention to formal features of language during comprehension in order to incorporate them into their developing L2 systems. For instance, [Schmidt (1990)](#ch8.xhtml_ref8_31){#ch8.xhtml_R_ref8_31 .biblioref role="doc-biblioref"} concluded that only linguistic forms that learners consciously notice in the input can become intake (see, e.g., [Simard and Wong (2001)](#ch8.xhtml_ref8_34){#ch8.xhtml_R_ref8_34 .biblioref role="doc-biblioref"} for a critical overview of attentional functions in L2 acquisition research prior to 2000).

The concept of noticed input became an important feature of an early model of L2 acquisition proposed by [Gass (1988)](#ch8.xhtml_ref8_12){#ch8.xhtml_R_ref8_12 .biblioref role="doc-biblioref"} and further developed in [Gass (1997)](#ch8.xhtml_ref8_13){#ch8.xhtml_R_ref8_13 .biblioref role="doc-biblioref"}. This model posited four intermediary stages between the input learners receive and their L2 output: apperceived input---input that has been noticed due to a form's frequency, affective factors (e.g., motivation), prior knowledge, and/or focused attention; comprehended input---input for which meaning has been assigned to a particular form; intake---matching newly comprehended input to existing linguistic knowledge; and integration---the storage of and/or incorporation of new linguistic structures []{#ch8.xhtml_p108 .pagebreak aria-label=" page 108. " role="doc-pagebreak"}into the learner's developing grammar. Of relevance to the present chapter is that comprehended input in Gass' model most closely resembles VanPatten's notion of intake or processed input ([Figure 8.1](#ch8.xhtml_fig8_1)).

Within discussions of learners' limited processing capacity and the role of conscious attention to form, [VanPatten (1990)](#ch8.xhtml_ref8_38){#ch8.xhtml_R_ref8_38 .biblioref role="doc-biblioref"} investigated whether L2 learners were able to consciously attend to both form (i.e., grammatical morphology) and meaning during comprehension. Groups of L2 Spanish learners of three proficiency levels listened to the same passage in Spanish, but they were told to focus on different things. Besides listening for content, participants indicated how many times they heard one of the following: a) the lexical item *inflación*, b) the definite article *la*, or c) the bound morpheme *-n*, marking 3rd person plural. A control group listened for content only. After listening to the passage, participants completed a comprehension recall, in which they wrote down everything they understood from the passage, and their recall protocols were analyzed according to the correct number of idea units produced. The results revealed that the groups that listened for content alone or content plus a lexical item recalled significantly more idea units than the groups that listened for the grammatical forms in addition to content. Therefore, VanPatten concluded that "conscious attention to form in the input competes with conscious attention to meaning, and, by extension, that only when input is easily understood can learners attend to form as part of the intake process" (p. 296). Furthermore, the finding that the processing of the content word *inflación* did not negatively affect learners' comprehension suggested the importance of content words during early stages of L2 input processing. The results of VanPatten's study were foundational for his theory of IP, and this study continues to be one of the most cited and replicated experiments in L2 research ([Sanz & McCormick, 2021](#ch8.xhtml_ref8_33){#ch8.xhtml_R_ref8_33 .biblioref role="doc-biblioref"}; see also Sanz, this volume).
:::

::::::: {.section aria-labelledby="sec8_3"}
## Critical Issues and Topics {#ch8.xhtml_sec8_3}

:::::: {.section aria-labelledby="sec8_3_1"}
### The Principles of IP {#ch8.xhtml_sec8_3_1}

IP has undergone revisions over the years concerning the principles that make up the theory and how they are stated (see White & Wong, this volume, for a discussion on the evolution of the principles of IP). [Table 8.1](#ch8.xhtml_table8_1){#ch8.xhtml_R_table8_1} presents the most recent version of the IP principles ([VanPatten, 2020](#ch8.xhtml_ref8_43){.biblioref role="doc-biblioref"}), and the discussion of the principles draws from discussions in VanPatten ([1996](#ch8.xhtml_ref8_39){.biblioref role="doc-biblioref"}, [2004](#ch8.xhtml_ref8_42){.biblioref role="doc-biblioref"}, [2020](#ch8.xhtml_ref8_43){.biblioref role="doc-biblioref"}).

  Principle                      Description
  ------------------------------ -----------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------
  Primacy of content words       Learners process content words in the input before anything else.
  Lexical preference             Learners process lexical items for meaning before grammatical forms when both encode the same semantic information.
  Revised lexical preference     If grammatical forms express a meaning that can also be encoded lexically (i.e., the grammatical marker is redundant), then learners will not initially process those grammatical forms until they have lexical forms to which they can match them.
  Preference for nonredundancy   Learners are more likely to process non-redundant meaningful grammatical markers before they process redundant meaningful markers.
  Meaning before nonmeaning      Learners are more likely to process meaningful grammatical markers before nonmeaningful grammatical markers.
  First-noun                     Learners tend to process the first noun or pronoun they encounter in a sentence as the subject.
  L1 transfer                    Learners begin acquisition with L1 parsing procedures.
  Event probability              Learners may rely on event probabilities, where possible, instead of the first-noun principle to interpret sentences.
  Lexical semantics              Learners may rely on lexical semantics, where possible, instead of the first-noun principle (or an L1 parsing procedure) to interpret sentences.
  Contextual constraint          Learners may rely less on the first-noun principle (or L1 transfer) if preceding context constrains the possible interpretation of a clause or sentence.
  Sentence location              Learners tend to process items in sentence initial position before those in final position and those in medial position.

  : [[Table ]{.label}[8.1 ]{.ordinal}The Principles of Input Processing](#ch8.xhtml_R_table8_1){.backlink aria-label=" This link returns to its first in-text citation. " role="doc-backlink"} {#table8_1}

<aside aria-label="Notes for Table 8.1" class="tfoot">

[VanPatten, 2020](#ch8.xhtml_ref8_43){.biblioref role="doc-biblioref"}

</aside>

The principles in [Table 8.1](#ch8.xhtml_table8_1) can be organized around three main issues: form--meaning connections, syntactic parsing, and, lastly, sentence position. Before discussing each of these issues and the related principles, two clarifications are worth mentioning. The first is a reminder that the term *process* in IP refers to attaching meaning to morpho-phonological units in the input (i.e., content and function words, as well as inflectional morphemes encoding person, number, tense, mood, and so forth). The second is that the word *before* as in "learners process X before Y" does not refer to the time course of processing an individual utterance. That is, learners do not search for content words to process (or lexical items, meaningful markers, etc.) and then search for something else. Instead, they only process content words at early stages of acquisition. Processing different types of grammatical markers emerges at later stages.

::: {.section aria-labelledby="sec8_3_1_1"}
#### Form--Meaning Connections {#ch8.xhtml_sec8_3_1_1}

A consequence of learners' drive to understand meaning during comprehension is that they process those elements that carry the most meaning, namely content words. This is captured by the **primacy of content words principle**. For instance, if L2 English learners hear *Yesterday Jeff worked til late*, the forms to which they are most likely to assign meaning are YESTERDAY, []{#ch8.xhtml_p109 .pagebreak aria-label=" page 109. " role="doc-pagebreak"}JEFF, WORK, and LATE. They may perceive or "notice" a suffix /-t/ attached to the verb and/or the function word (preposition) *til*, but these elements are less likely to be linked to meaning and/or function because learners are focused on getting meaning from content words.

This preference for processing content words before anything else in the input has further implications for the processing of grammatical markers that encode the same information as content words, as is often the case with verbal inflections for person/number, tense, and others. For instance, tense morphology often co-occurs with adverbial expressions of time (*yesterday*, *today*, *tomorrow*), as in the Spanish sentence *iré contigo mañana* (I'll go with you tomorrow).

Lexical items like *mañana* ('tomorrow') are undoubtedly more transparent and reliable cues to meaning than inflectional morphology because verbal inflection in many languages often encodes more than one concept (e.g., [Ellis & Wulff, 2020](#ch8.xhtml_ref8_10){#ch8.xhtml_R_ref8_10 .biblioref role="doc-biblioref"}). In the Spanish example, the morpheme *-ré* not only encodes futurity, but it also encodes person (first), number (singular), and mood (indicative), rendering it a much less transparent marker of future time reference than the adverb *mañana*. Therefore, given learners' reliance on content words to get meaning, L2 Spanish learners will link the semantic concept of FUTURE with *mañana* before linking it with the morpheme *-ré*. This preference for processing lexical items before grammatical forms is captured in the **lexical preference principle**.

In more recent versions of IP, VanPatten ([2004](#ch8.xhtml_ref8_42){.biblioref role="doc-biblioref"}, [2020](#ch8.xhtml_ref8_43){.biblioref role="doc-biblioref"}) has included an alternative to the original lexical preference principle. That is, it is possible that learners will not process inflectional morphemes encoding time, person, number, and so forth until they have actually acquired []{#ch8.xhtml_p110 .pagebreak aria-label=" page 110. " role="doc-pagebreak"}lexical items expressing the same semantic information. For this reason, the **revised lexical preference principle** has been added to capture this possibility.

Both versions of the lexical preference principle illustrate the fact that a good deal of inflectional morphology is redundant. Thus, the redundant inflectional morphemes in (2) do not uniquely contribute to the meaning of those utterances because of the presence of temporal adverbs *yesterday* and *mañana* ('tomorrow'). However, some forms do not uniquely contribute to overall sentence meaning because they lack semantic information. Linguistic items such as agreement markers for gender on adjectives, vowels representing conjugation classes in Romance languages, differential object marking in Spanish (i.e., personal *a*), and the complementizer *that* in English all have grammatical functions, but they lack meaning. In earlier versions of IP (e.g., VanPatten, [1996](#ch8.xhtml_ref8_39){.biblioref role="doc-biblioref"}, [2000](#ch8.xhtml_ref8_40){#ch8.xhtml_R_ref8_40 .biblioref role="doc-biblioref"}), a form's unique contribution to meaning in an utterance was a reflection of its *communicative value*, which was determined by whether a form expressed meaning (i.e., +/- semantic value) and whether it was redundant (+/- redundant) ([VanPatten, 2000](#ch8.xhtml_ref8_40){.biblioref role="doc-biblioref"}). A form was higher in communicative value if its characteristics were \[-redundancy, +semantic value\]. And a form would have lower communicative value if it had the characteristics of \[+redundancy, +semantic value\] and no communicative value with the characteristic \[-semantic value\]. Since 2004, however, *communicative value* has not been part of the IP principles. Instead, two different principles have been proposed---**preference for nonredundancy** and **meaning before nonmeaning**---in order to separate (a) redundant forms that encode meaning from (b) nonmeaningful forms that may or may not be redundant.
:::

::: {.section aria-labelledby="sec8_3_1_2"}
#### Syntactic and Semantic Roles in Sentences {#ch8.xhtml_sec8_3_1_2}

As previously noted, IP is also about sentence parsing or determining "who does what to whom". Consider, for example, the English utterance *the girl helps the boy*. Native speakers of English immediately and effortlessly understand that the grammatical subject *the girl* performs the action of helping, and that the grammatical object *the boy* receives the help based on their implicit knowledge that English follows a strict subject--verb--object word order and that English uses word order to encode grammatical relationships among words. Competent English speakers, therefore, consistently rely on the preverbal positioning of a noun phrase to identify the subject of a sentence. Many other languages, however, possess much more flexible word order than English. As a result, speakers of languages like German (1) and Russian (2) identify subjects and objects by means of case markings:

``` {#ch8.xhtml_preformat8_2}

(1) German:   a. Das Mädchen hilft dem Jungen.
              ‘[the girl]NOM helps [the boy]DAT’
              b. Dem Jungen hilft das Mädchen.
              ‘[the boy]DAT helps [the girl]NOM’
```

``` {#ch8.xhtml_preformat8_3}

(2) Russian:   a. Девушка помогает мальчику
               Devochka pomogayet mal’chiku
               ‘[the girl]NOM helps [the boy]DAT’
               b. Mальчику помогает девочка
               Mal’chiku pomogayet devochka
               ‘[the boy]DAT helps [the girl]NOM’
```

[]{#ch8.xhtml_p111 .pagebreak aria-label=" page 111. " role="doc-pagebreak"}Although the word orders differ between the (a) and (b) versions of (1) and (2), native speakers of German and Russian automatically understand "the girl helps the boy" regardless of word order ([MacWhinney, 2008](#ch8.xhtml_ref8_26){#ch8.xhtml_R_ref8_26 .biblioref role="doc-biblioref"}).

Adult native speakers parse sentences incrementally, or on a word-by-word basis, in which each word is assigned to a position within the phrase structure of the sentence ([Just & Carpenter, 1980](#ch8.xhtml_ref8_16){#ch8.xhtml_R_ref8_16 .biblioref role="doc-biblioref"}). Furthermore, as part of this sentence structure building, native speakers automatically process agreement features (e.g., number, person, case, gender) associated with a given word based on the reader/listener's implicit linguistic knowledge (e.g., [Weinberg, 1999](#ch8.xhtml_ref8_46){#ch8.xhtml_R_ref8_46 .biblioref role="doc-biblioref"}). Once again, these lower-level comprehension processes occur effortlessly for adult L1 speakers. L2 learners, on the other hand, do not possess the same level of lexical and syntactic knowledge as native speakers. Thus, it cannot be assumed that they parse and interpret sentences in the same way that native speakers do ([VanPatten, 2004](#ch8.xhtml_ref8_42){.biblioref role="doc-biblioref"}). How, then, does parsing develop in a second language? What kinds of information (linguistic and otherwise) do L2 learners use to understand sentences, particularly when the encoding of grammatical relationships in the native language (e.g., word order in English) differs from that of the second language (e.g., case markings in German and Russian)?

IP considers two possibilities for what guides L2 learners parsing or determining "who does what to whom". One possibility is that learners' parsing is determined by universal strategies regardless of their L1. Under this scenario, L2 learners tend to identify the first noun in an utterance as the subject/agent, regardless of the predominant word order of their L1, as stated in the **first-noun principle** (FNP). L2 learners making use of the FNP correctly interpret agent-first, SVO sentences; however, they are more likely to misinterpret passives and object-first utterances. For instance, lower level L2 Spanish learners will misinterpret *lo besa María* as "he kisses María" instead of "María kisses him", even if their L1 allows for preverbal objects or clitics (see Simonsen & VanPatten, this volume).

The other possibility is that L2 learners begin parsing utterances based on L1 parsing procedures, which is proposed in the **L1 transfer principle**. In this alternative, L2 learners whose L1 follows a strict SVO word order would be more likely to misinterpret OV and OVS sentences than those whose L1 allows flexible word order. In other words, the misinterpretation of *lo besa María* would be more prevalent among L1 English than L1 Russian speakers.

Both the universal position (FNP) and L1 transfer make the same prediction for L1 English speakers processing L2 sentences with OV and OVS word order. However, VanPatten has argued for the universal position given that first noun=subject/agent seems to be the predominant word order in more than 80% of the world's languages (e.g., [Tomlin, 1986](#ch8.xhtml_ref8_36){#ch8.xhtml_R_ref8_36 .biblioref role="doc-biblioref"}), suggesting that the human mind may be predisposed to assign the role of subject/agent to the first noun in a sentence ([VanPatten, 2004](#ch8.xhtml_ref8_42){.biblioref role="doc-biblioref"}). Additional arguments for a universal position can be made based on the widespread finding of first noun=subject/agent in pidgins ([Givón, 1995](#ch8.xhtml_ref8_14){#ch8.xhtml_R_ref8_14 .biblioref role="doc-biblioref"}) and basic variety second language grammars ([Klein & Perdue, 1997](#ch8.xhtml_ref8_20){#ch8.xhtml_R_ref8_20 .biblioref role="doc-biblioref"}).

The issue of whether a first noun=subject/agent strategy is the result of a universal principle like the FNP or L1 transfer may ultimately be resolved by experimental studies of L2 learners whose L1 is OVS, although strictly OVS languages are extremely rare. That said, IP also accounts for factors that may attenuate or even override reliance on a default first noun=subject/agent strategy. For example, consider the sentences in (6)--(8):

``` {#ch8.xhtml_preformat8_4}

(3) The house was built by my neighbor.
(4) The student was corrected by the teacher.
(5) Mary is furious because she was lied to by Jane.
```

[]{#ch8.xhtml_p112 .pagebreak aria-label=" page 112. " role="doc-pagebreak"}The FNP predicts that L2 English learners are likely to assign an agent role to *the house* (3), *the student* (4) and *she* (5) in each of the passive structures above. Obviously, this would yield interpretations of these sentences that most speakers would find highly unlikely at best. For this reason, three principles of IP stipulate how meaning expressed in the sentence itself can potentially avoid or override a misinterpretation based on a first noun=subject/agent strategy (due to FNP or L1 transfer). For example, in (3), an interpretation of "the house built my neighbor" makes no sense. The semantics of the verb *build* requires an argument that initiates the action of building, and that argument must be animate. The only noun that meets the requirement is *neighbor*. Therefore, as the **lexical semantics principle** proposes, learners may rely on the meaning of the verb and the semantic requirements that verbs place on nouns in a sentence to arrive at an interpretation.

In (4), it is certainly possible for students to correct teachers, such as when teachers mispronounce students' names or make other mistakes. However, it is much more likely for teachers to correct students. Background knowledge, including knowledge of events, plays a vital role in how language users arrive at an understanding of utterances and discourse. Thus, speakers possess knowledge of the likelihood or probability that a particular *who* does something to a particular *whom*. This is captured in the **event probabilities principle**: learners may rely on their knowledge of events (and their likelihood) instead of a default strategy to interpret utterances.

Finally, contextual information in another part of the sentence may also override or attenuate learners' reliance on a first noun=subject/agent strategy. Considering the example in (5), how likely is it that Mary is furious because she lied to someone? Or is it more likely that she is furious because someone lied to her? In (5), learners may rely on the context established in the first part of the sentence (*Mary is furious*) to determine who lied to whom in the second part of the sentence, as captured by the **contextual constraint principle**.

To summarize, IP proposes that beginning L2 learners adopt default strategies that are either universal or based on their L1 to assign syntactic and semantic roles to nouns in a sentence. However, their ultimate interpretation of an utterance may be determined by the meaning of verbs, learners' own knowledge of events, and contextual cues located elsewhere in a sentence.
:::

::: {.section aria-labelledby="sec8_3_1_3"}
#### Sentence Location {#ch8.xhtml_sec8_3_1_3}

The last principle in [Table 8.1](#ch8.xhtml_table8_1) says that learners are more likely to process certain linguistic items depending on their position in the sentence. More specifically, the **sentence location principle** states that learners are more likely to process items that appear in sentence initial position, followed by those in sentence final position, and lastly, those in sentence medial position. The effect of the serial position of words and numbers on subsequent recall has been documented in the cognitive psychology literature for decades (e.g., [Murdock, 1962](#ch8.xhtml_ref8_29){#ch8.xhtml_R_ref8_29 .biblioref role="doc-biblioref"}). In addition, a rationale for a serial position effect in L2 sentence processing lies in the effortful and resource demanding nature of comprehension at early stages ([Barcroft & VanPatten, 1997](#ch8.xhtml_ref8_5){#ch8.xhtml_R_ref8_5 .biblioref role="doc-biblioref"}). These resources are obviously applied first to a sentence initial item and may soon be consumed by the middle of an utterance. If a pause follows the end of an utterance, additional processing resources may be available again to process a sentence final element.

At first glance, it may seem that the sentence location principle contradicts the first-noun principle. That is, if learners are most likely to process elements that regularly appear in sentence initial position, then why would they misinterpret preverbal object pronouns if they appear first in a sentence? To answer this question, it is important to keep in mind that the term *process* means linking meaning with form. It does not mean that the correct meaning is linked to form. Learners certainly can and do make incorrect form--meaning connections. Thus, these two principles would not contradict each other. Instead, they can operate together. For example, when preverbal object []{#ch8.xhtml_p113 .pagebreak aria-label=" page 113. " role="doc-pagebreak"}pronouns appear in sentence initial position (e.g., *la besó en la frente* '\[someone\] kissed her on the forehead'), the first noun and sentence location principles may reinforce an incorrect interpretation at beginning levels. However, once learners begin to connect *la* with 'her' instead of 'she', they may be more likely to overcome a first noun=subject/agent strategy if they encounter preverbal object pronouns in sentence initial position much more often than if they appear later in the sentence (*en la frente la besó*). Therefore, the point here is that IP principles do not operate in isolation.
:::
::::::
:::::::

:::: {.section aria-labelledby="sec8_4"}
## Current Contributions {#ch8.xhtml_sec8_4}

::: {.section aria-labelledby="sec8_4_1"}
### The Relationship of IP to other L2 Theories and Processes {#ch8.xhtml_sec8_4_1}

Since its inception, the focus of IP has always been on the first set of processes illustrated in [Figure 8.1](#ch8.xhtml_fig8_1): the conversion of input to intake. Thus, IP was never intended to be a comprehensive model or theory of L2 acquisition. In fact, given the multi-dimensional nature of acquisition, IP is largely compatible with and complements other major theoretical frameworks that focus on other aspects of L2 acquisition. To illustrate, [Figure 8.2](#ch8.xhtml_fig8_2){#ch8.xhtml_R_fig8_2} expands [Figure 8.1](#ch8.xhtml_fig8_1) and indicates which processes are accounted for by IP and by a few other well-known frameworks.

![[[Figure]{.label} [8.2 ]{.ordinal}The scope of four theoretical frameworks for L2 acquisition.](#ch8.xhtml_R_fig8_2){.backlink aria-label=" This link returns to its first in-text citation. " role="doc-backlink"}](images/fig8_2_B.jpg){#fig8_2 alt="The four stages of second language acquisition: input, intake, developing system, and output. The figure shows how different theories, that is, usage-based approaches, IP principles, generative approaches, and processability theory fall within these four stages."}

As [Figure 8.2](#ch8.xhtml_fig8_2) suggests, generative perspectives are specifically concerned with characterizing L2 learners' developing system or (implicit) knowledge of abstract syntactic properties ([White, 2020](#ch8.xhtml_ref8_47){#ch8.xhtml_R_ref8_47 .biblioref role="doc-biblioref"}). The developing system, however, is dependent on intake data. That is, internal mechanisms like Universal Grammar (UG) operate on intake data and not on input for the construction of the developing system ([Carroll, 2001](#ch8.xhtml_ref8_6){#ch8.xhtml_R_ref8_6 .biblioref role="doc-biblioref"}; [VanPatten, 1996](#ch8.xhtml_ref8_39){.biblioref role="doc-biblioref"}). In fact, if learners fail to process grammatical forms (e.g., agreement markers) due to a reliance on lexical items, then this "impoverished intake" deprives UG of important data for the syntactic component of the developing system.[^16] Thus, intake is a point of contact between IP and generative approaches.

Processability Theory (PT) accounts for stages in learners' speech production and posits a universal hierarchy of processing procedures ([Pienemann & Lenzig, 2020](#ch8.xhtml_ref8_30){#ch8.xhtml_R_ref8_30 .biblioref role="doc-biblioref"}). For example, PT predicts that L2 learners' output initially consists of content words and formulaic utterances, followed by inflections on individual lexical items (*book[s]{.underline}*), and later morphemes that require matching information between two items within a phrase (*these books*). Because PT's focus has been primarily on the stages of development of speech production, its scope obviously differs considerably from that of IP. However, both frameworks posit an initial importance on lexical items and a later emergence of certain kinds of grammatical items in comprehension (IP) and production (PT).

Usage-based approaches encompass a wider range of processes, including a shared focus with IP on the conversion of input to intake. In fact, both perspectives make similar predictions regarding which forms learners process. For example, both predict an initial reliance on adverbs []{#ch8.xhtml_p114 .pagebreak aria-label=" page 114. " role="doc-pagebreak"}over redundant inflectional tense morphology, whether due to a phenomenon like "blocking" (in usage-based approaches) or the lexical preference principle (in IP) (see White & Wong, this volume). Furthermore, both predict that L1 English speakers will use a first noun=subject/agent strategy to interpret sentences, albeit for somewhat different reasons. A usage-based framework like the competition model (e.g., [MacWhinney, 2008](#ch8.xhtml_ref8_26){.biblioref role="doc-biblioref"}) hypothesizes that L2 learners use the most dominant L1 *cues* (canonical word order, noun animacy, case markings, etc.) to initially interpret utterances in the L2. Thus, because word order is a frequent and reliable cue to agent assignment in English, L1 English speakers will rely on preverbal positioning of a noun to determine "who does what to whom" in any L2. This reliance on L1 cues contrasts with the universal position of the FNP in IP but not the L1 transfer principle.[^17]

Usage-based approaches are also concerned with what happens to processed input (or *intake*). For instance, usage-based frameworks posit general cognitive mechanisms that induce patterns, regularities, and relationships among elements that have been processed in some way ([Ellis & Wulff, 2020](#ch8.xhtml_ref8_10){.biblioref role="doc-biblioref"}). In other words, domain-general processes like categorization, chunking, association, and so forth operate on intake data, not on ambient input. A theory like IP, then, can be informative to usage-based frameworks (see Morales-Front & Barcroft, this volume) and to generative approaches (see Dekydtspotter, this volume) particularly in terms the kind of information made available to internal mechanisms (whether UG or domain general) and why the acquisition of certain constructions may be delayed due to issues of (lack of) meaningfulness and redundancy.

To be sure, the connections made here with other frameworks in no way imply that IP should extend its scope and account for all processes involved in L2 acquisition. Instead, the fact that IP relates to and is consistent with aspects of other frameworks, while still maintaining its core focus on the conversion on input to intake, can be viewed as strength of the theory ([Leeser et al., 2021](#ch8.xhtml_ref8_25){.biblioref role="doc-biblioref"}).
:::
::::

:::::: {.section aria-labelledby="sec8_5"}
## Main Research Methods {#ch8.xhtml_sec8_5}

Research on IP spans more than three decades, and it is impossible to discuss adequately the wide range of studies conducted. Instead, this section focuses on a handful of foundational studies that exemplify the kinds of tasks and methodologies used to investigate questions related to IP.

::: {.section aria-labelledby="sec8_5_1"}
### Discourse-level Comprehension Tasks {#ch8.xhtml_sec8_5_1}

One strand of foundational IP research has utilized discourse-level comprehension tasks that have examined either (a) the impact of attention to form versus meaning on comprehension or (b) the kinds of forms learners used to understand meaning (e.g., adverbs versus inflectional morphology for temporal reference). These studies follow a methodology similar to [VanPatten (1990)](#ch8.xhtml_ref8_38){.biblioref role="doc-biblioref"} discussed in the section on historical perspectives. For example, [Lee et al. (1997)](#ch8.xhtml_ref8_24){#ch8.xhtml_R_ref8_24 .biblioref role="doc-biblioref"} reported that L2 Spanish learners who heard a narrative with temporal adverbs reconstructed significantly more past time references on a written recall task than learners who heard the same passage without the temporal adverbs. Furthermore, [Lee (2002)](#ch8.xhtml_ref8_21){#ch8.xhtml_R_ref8_21 .biblioref role="doc-biblioref"} reported similar findings in a study examining future tense morphology and future-oriented adverbial expressions in L2 reading. Both studies provide evidence for learners' preference of using lexical cues to understand temporal information in aural and written input as predicted by the lexical preference principle (see White & Wong, this volume).
:::

::: {.section aria-labelledby="sec8_5_2"}
### Sentence Interpretation Tasks {#ch8.xhtml_sec8_5_2}

Tasks involving sentence interpretation have been the mostly widely used task in IP research. Generally, these tasks involve listening to or reading a sentence and then selecting the agent of []{#ch8.xhtml_p115 .pagebreak aria-label=" page 115. " role="doc-pagebreak"}sentence (to examine the FNP) or the time frame in which an event in the sentence occurred (to examine lexical preference).

Evidence for the FNP in sentence interpretation tasks can be found in dozens of studies that have investigated the effects of processing instruction (PI). PI is an instructional intervention designed to push learners away from non-optimal processing strategies, such as the FNP and lexical preference, so that they are more likely to make correct form--meaning connections and correctly interpret sentences during comprehension (see Wong, this volume). Of relevance to the present discussion on IP are the assessment measures used as pre- and post-tests that show whether learners are actually using the FNP and misinterpreting utterances. For example, [VanPatten and Cadierno's (1993)](#ch8.xhtml_ref8_44){#ch8.xhtml_R_ref8_44 .biblioref role="doc-biblioref"} landmark study reported an accuracy rate of less than 20% for fourth semester L2 Spanish learners' interpretation of OVS sentences, which clearly suggests that they were using a first noun=subject/agent strategy. This finding has been replicated on pre-tests in more than 40 studies on the effects of PI in altering the FNP.

In terms of learners' preference for adverbs over morphological tense inflections, [Ellis and Sagarra (2011, experiment 2)](#ch8.xhtml_ref8_9){#ch8.xhtml_R_ref8_9 .biblioref role="doc-biblioref"} examined how beginning L2 learners of Latin of various L1s (Chinese, English, Russian, or Spanish) assigned temporal reference to sentences containing adverb--verb morphology mismatches, such as *yesterday I think*\*. They found that all learners were more influenced by the adverb than verb morphology in assigning temporal reference, but the degree to which they relied on adverbs depended on their L1. Learners whose L1 contained rich verbal morphology (i.e., Russian and Spanish) were more likely to make use of tense inflections to resolve incongruities.

Although Ellis and Sagarra did not set out to specifically test IP principles, their results lend support to both the lexical preference and L1 transfer principles and underscore the idea that the IP principles do not operate in isolation ([VanPatten, 2020](#ch8.xhtml_ref8_43){.biblioref role="doc-biblioref"}). That is, participants showed a reliance on lexical cues to meaning over inflectional morphology regardless of their L1, as predicted by the lexical preference principle. However, learners whose L1 contained rich verbal morphology were able to make use of this cue under certain conditions, but they seem to default to a more universal one, such as lexical preference, when comprehension is more resource demanding at the beginning stages of acquisition.
:::

::: {.section aria-labelledby="sec8_5_3"}
### On-line Sentence Processing Studies {#ch8.xhtml_sec8_5_3}

On-line methods, such as self-paced reading/listening and eye-tracking, have gained considerable traction during the last decade in L2 processing research, and a handful of IP related studies employing on-line methods have begun to appear. Some of these studies are not motivated by IP per se; however, their findings are relevant to certain principles (e.g., [Sagarra & Ellis, 2013](#ch8.xhtml_ref8_32){#ch8.xhtml_R_ref8_32 .biblioref role="doc-biblioref"}). Others are motivated by a specific IP principle, particularly those that investigate whether PI alters learners' processing behavior (e.g., [Lee & Doherty, 2019](#ch8.xhtml_ref8_22){#ch8.xhtml_R_ref8_22 .biblioref role="doc-biblioref"}; [Wong & Ito, 2018](#ch8.xhtml_ref8_49){#ch8.xhtml_R_ref8_49 .biblioref role="doc-biblioref"}). Two eye-tracking studies, one examining reading ([Sagarra & Ellis, 2013](#ch8.xhtml_ref8_32){.biblioref role="doc-biblioref"}) and the other listening ([Wong & Ito, 2018](#ch8.xhtml_ref8_49){.biblioref role="doc-biblioref"}), illustrate how this on-line method can shed light on learners' moment-by-moment processing in IP research.

[Sagarra and Ellis (2013)](#ch8.xhtml_ref8_32){.biblioref role="doc-biblioref"} examined intermediate and advanced L2 Spanish learners' eye-movements while reading sentences in which temporal adverbs either matched verbal inflections (e.g., ...*yesterday the boy cooked...*) or conflicted with them (e.g., ...*yesterday the boy cooks\*...*). The L2 learners included L1 speakers of Romanian (a morphologically rich language) and English (a morphologically poor language). Among the many findings of this study, of particular interest is that there were no L1 differences found for the length of time learners spent looking at temporal adverbs. But among the advanced learners, L1 Romanian participants regressed longer to verbs than the L1 English participants in the adverb--verb mismatch condition. In []{#ch8.xhtml_p116 .pagebreak aria-label=" page 116. " role="doc-pagebreak"}other words, L2 learners of both proficiency levels and L1 backgrounds were consistent in their processing of adverbs as temporal reference cues. But at higher proficiency levels, learners whose L1 contained rich verbal morphology also spent more time looking at verbal morphology in their L2. The authors conclude that "the L1 affects later processing of lexical and morphological cues to temporal reference at high- but not low-proficiency levels" (p. 285). In line with [Ellis and Sagarra (2011)](#ch8.xhtml_ref8_9){.biblioref role="doc-biblioref"}, the findings indicate that learners use adverbs as a default cue to temporal meaning, as predicted by the lexical preference principle. However, the L1 can influence processing at higher proficiency levels when comprehension is less resource demanding. Once again, these findings suggest that IP principles do not operate in isolation.

Besides examining eye-movements during processing of written input, eye-tracking can also be used to investigate L2 learners' moment-by-moment processing of aural input using a visual world paradigm. For example, [Wong and Ito (2018)](#ch8.xhtml_ref8_49){.biblioref role="doc-biblioref"} compared the effects of processing instruction (PI) and traditional instruction on L2 French learners' processing of the French causative construction. They examined accuracy on a sentence--picture matching task as well as the changes in looks at the two pictures representing correct and incorrect interpretations of the sentence. This design allowed the researchers not only to determine learners' final interpretation of the sentence, but also to ascertain at what point in the sentence participants' looks converged on the picture representing one interpretation or the other. For instance, Wong and Ito reported that prior to instructional treatment, participants' looks converged on the picture showing the incorrect interpretation as soon as they heard the first noun phrase in causative sentences. In other words, learners seem to commit to a first-noun strategy almost immediately. After the treatment, however, learners who had received PI shifted their looks to the correct picture towards the end of the sentence. This finding suggests that PI helps learners overcome their reliance on the FNP at a certain point in the sentence when they arrive at specific cues to identify the agent in causative constructions (see Wong and Ito for additional discussion).
:::
::::::

::: {.section aria-labelledby="sec8_6"}
## Recommendations for Practice {#ch8.xhtml_sec8_6}

The clearest application of IP theory to L2 instruction is processing instruction (PI). PI is an instructional intervention designed to push learners to process linguistic forms and interpret sentences correctly during comprehension by helping them overcome non-optimal strategies such as the FNP, the lexical preference principle and others listed in [Table 8.1](#ch8.xhtml_table8_1). Both [Leeser et al. (2021)](#ch8.xhtml_ref8_25){.biblioref role="doc-biblioref"} and Wong (this volume) present a thorough examination of PI and PI research. In addition, examples of PI activities targeting various processing problems in different languages can be found in [Farley (2005)](#ch8.xhtml_ref8_11){#ch8.xhtml_R_ref8_11 .biblioref role="doc-biblioref"} and [Lee and VanPatten (2003)](#ch8.xhtml_ref8_23){#ch8.xhtml_R_ref8_23 .biblioref role="doc-biblioref"}.
:::

::: {.section .conclusion role="doc-conclusion"}
## Future Directions and Conclusion {#ch8.xhtml_sec8_7}

The sample studies in this chapter, as well as the vast majority of IP research, have centered on three of the IP principles: primacy of content words, first-noun, and lexical preference. A couple of studies have directly tested the contextual constraint principle (e.g., [Isabelli, 2021](#ch8.xhtml_ref8_15){#ch8.xhtml_R_ref8_15 .biblioref role="doc-biblioref"}; [Malovrh, 2006](#ch8.xhtml_ref8_27){#ch8.xhtml_R_ref8_27 .biblioref role="doc-biblioref"}; [VanPatten & Houston, 1998](#ch8.xhtml_ref8_45){#ch8.xhtml_R_ref8_45 .biblioref role="doc-biblioref"}) and the sentence location principle ([Barcroft & VanPatten, 1997](#ch8.xhtml_ref8_5){.biblioref role="doc-biblioref"}). However, contextual constraint, sentence location, and many others lack the same degree of empirical evidence as those dealing with content words, first noun, and lexical preference. Furthermore, very few studies have directly examined the ways in which different principles work together. Finally, as [Keating (2021)](#ch8.xhtml_ref8_18){#ch8.xhtml_R_ref8_18 .biblioref role="doc-biblioref"} has noted, most evidence for IP comes from offline tasks even though IP theory is concerned with moment-by-moment (i.e., real time) sentence comprehension. To stimulate new research in this area, Keating walks through various []{#ch8.xhtml_p117 .pagebreak aria-label=" page 117. " role="doc-pagebreak"}experimental techniques---eye-tracking, mouse tracking, cross-modal priming, self-paced reading, and event-related potentials)---and shows how they can be used to examine learners on-line processing of aural and written input and test several IP principles.

Given the fundamental role of input for L2 acquisition, the study of how learners process it should occupy a central avenue of research in SLA. This chapter explored VanPatten's theory of IP, which is an account of how learners derive intake from input during moment-to-moment comprehension. Although research spanning more than three decades has provided convincing support for core principles of IP, there are still several fruitful avenues of research (mentioned above) that can refine existing principles, shed light on how they interact, and even propose new ones that guide learners' processing of morpho-phonological forms and sentence parsing.
:::

::: {.endnotes role="doc-endnotes"}
## Notes {#ch8.xhtml_h2}
:::

::: {.section .reference aria-labelledby="r8_1"}
## Further Reading {#ch8.xhtml_r8_1}

- [Leeser, M. J., Keating, G. D., & Wong, W. (Eds.) (2021). `<cite>`{=html}Research on second language processing and processing instruction: Studies in honor of Bill VanPatten`</cite>`{=html}. John Benjamins.]{#ch8.xhtml_ref8_1}

  This volume provides a chapter on Bill VanPatten's research trajectory and contribution to SLA, chapters presenting original research on IP and PI, and a concluding chapter offering suggestions for further on-line research on IP and PI.

- [VanPatten, B. (1996). `<cite>`{=html}Input processing and grammar instruction: Theory and research`</cite>`{=html}. Ablex.]{#ch8.xhtml_ref8_2}

  This is the first book in which VanPatten presents the original principles of IP, the research motivating the principles, and an in-depth discussion of PI.

- [VanPatten, B. (Ed.). (2004). Processing instruction: Theory, research, and commentary. Erlbaum.]{#ch8.xhtml_ref8_3}

  This edited volume contains an earlier version of IP (chapter 1), and essay on PI (chapter 2), several chapters of research on PI, and critical commentaries on both IP and PI.

- [VanPatten, B. (2020). Input processing in adult L2 acquisition. In B. VanPatten, G. D. Keating, & S. Wulff (Eds.), `<cite>`{=html}Theories in second language acquisition: An introduction`</cite>`{=html} (3rd ed.) (pp. 105--127). Routledge.]{#ch8.xhtml_ref8_4}

  This chapter provides VanPatten's most recent elaboration of his theory of IP.
:::
