Explicit instruction and input processing. `<cite>`{=html}Studies in Second Language Acquisition`</cite>`{=html}, *15*, 225--243.]{#ch8.xhtml_ref8_44}
- [[VanPatten, B., & Houston, T.](#ch8.xhtml_R_ref8_45){.backlink aria-label=" This link returns to its first in-text citation. " role="doc-backlink"} (1998). Contextual effects in processing L2 input sentences. `<cite>`{=html}Spanish Applied Linguistics`</cite>`{=html}, *2*, 53--70.]{#ch8.xhtml_ref8_45}
- [[Weinberg, A.](#ch8.xhtml_R_ref8_46){.backlink aria-label=" This link returns to its first in-text citation. " role="doc-backlink"} (1999). A minimalist theory of human sentence processing. In Epstein, S. and Hornstein, N. (Eds.), `<cite>`{=html}Working minimalism`</cite>`{=html} (pp. 287--315). MIT Press.]{#ch8.xhtml_ref8_46}
- [[White, L.](#ch8.xhtml_R_ref8_47){.backlink aria-label=" This link returns to its first in-text citation. " role="doc-backlink"} (2020). Linguistic theory, universal grammar, and second language acquisition. In B. VanPatten, G. D. Keating, & S. Wulff (Eds.), `<cite>`{=html}Theories in second language acquisition: An introduction`</cite>`{=html} (3rd ed.) (pp. 19--39). Routledge.]{#ch8.xhtml_ref8_47}
- [[Wickens, C.](#ch8.xhtml_R_ref8_48){.backlink aria-label=" This link returns to its first in-text citation. " role="doc-backlink"} (1984). Processing resources in attention. In R. Parasuraman, & D. Davies (Eds.), `<cite>`{=html}Varieties of attention`</cite>`{=html} (pp. 63--98). Academic Press.]{#ch8.xhtml_ref8_48}
- [[Wong, W., & Ito, K.](#ch8.xhtml_R_ref8_49){.backlink aria-label=" This link returns to its first in-text citation. " role="doc-backlink"} (2018). The effects of processing instruction and traditional instruction on L2 online processing of the causative construction in French: An eye-tracking study. `<cite>`{=html}Studies in Second Language Acquisition`</cite>`{=html}, *40*, 241--268.]{#ch8.xhtml_ref8_49}
:::

[]{#ch9.xhtml}

::: {}
# []{#ch9.xhtml_p120 .pagebreak aria-label=" page 120. " role="doc-pagebreak"}[9 ]{.chnum .ordinal}[Input Processing as an Interaction Between Internal and External Context ]{.chtitle}[The Modular Cognition Framework]{.chsubtitle .subtitle role="doc-subtitle"} {#ch9.xhtml_h1}

Michael Sharwood Smith

Heriot-Watt University

John Truscott

National Tsing Hua University

DOI: [10.4324/9781003041412-12](https://dx.doi.org/10.4324/9781003041412-12){aria-label="D.O.I. link to this document."}
:::

::::::: {.section .introduction role="doc-introduction"}
## Introduction {#ch9.xhtml_sec9_1}

::: {.section aria-labelledby="sec9_1_1"}
### Definition {#ch9.xhtml_sec9_1_1}

Input processing (IP) refers to what is actually processed by language learners as opposed to all the evidence that is potentially available to the learner in the immediate environment. This includes specifically linguistic features as well as other kinds of evidence required for full comprehension and, by extension, for further linguistic development. This broader understanding of the term therefore allows us to view IP as involving an interaction between linguistic and non-linguistic input.
:::

::: {.section aria-labelledby="sec9_1_2"}
### Historical Perspective {#ch9.xhtml_sec9_1_2}

To explain more precisely the systems and mechanisms involved, the modular cognition framework (MCF) will be used. This will provide a means of resolving concerns raised in the literature about the precise nature of 'input' and specifying in detail what mechanisms are involved in its 'processing' in real time (see for example, [Carroll, 2001](#ch9.xhtml_ref9_7){#ch9.xhtml_R_ref9_7 .biblioref role="doc-biblioref"}; [Gass, 1997](#ch9.xhtml_ref9_14){#ch9.xhtml_R_ref9_14 .biblioref role="doc-biblioref"}; [Sharwood Smith, 1993](#ch9.xhtml_ref9_22){#ch9.xhtml_R_ref9_22 .biblioref role="doc-biblioref"}; [Sharwood Smith & Truscott 2014a](#ch9.xhtml_ref9_25){#ch9.xhtml_R_ref9_25 .biblioref role="doc-biblioref"}; [VanPatten, 2002](#ch9.xhtml_ref9_33){#ch9.xhtml_R_ref9_33 .biblioref role="doc-biblioref"}). The MCF, for a time known as MOGUL (modular online growth and use of language) has been in development since 2000 ([Truscott & Sharwood Smith, 2004](#ch9.xhtml_ref9_29){#ch9.xhtml_R_ref9_29 .biblioref role="doc-biblioref"}). It integrates contemporary ideas about the mind and language within a framework intended to guide research into, alongside many other things, the relationship between IP and language development in the individual. More recent refinements and further development of the framework have included affective processing, the distinction between internal and external context as well as the concepts of 'goal' and 'self' ([Truscott & Sharwood Smith, 2019](#ch9.xhtml_ref9_32){#ch9.xhtml_R_ref9_32 .biblioref role="doc-biblioref"}).
:::

::: {.section aria-labelledby="sec9_1_3"}
### []{#ch9.xhtml_p121 .pagebreak aria-label=" page 121. " role="doc-pagebreak"}Explaining Acquisition {#ch9.xhtml_sec9_1_3}

The MCF includes the all-important *Acquisition by Processing* (APT) principle which states that it is online processing that drives the establishment of any new cognitive representations including those relevant for the acquisition of languages ([Sharwood Smith & Truscott, 2014b](#ch9.xhtml_ref9_26){#ch9.xhtml_R_ref9_26 .biblioref role="doc-biblioref"}, pp. 93--102). This is a principle shared by a number of other approaches not always compatible with the MCF perspective such as, for example, O'Grady's emergentist model ([O'Grady, 2005](#ch9.xhtml_ref9_20){#ch9.xhtml_R_ref9_20 .biblioref role="doc-biblioref"}). The MCF version is based on the idea that the mind is composed of a set of functionally specialised systems that are part of our biological inheritance. This includes a couple of dedicated linguistic systems responsible for managing phonological and syntactic structure during the course of processing. What is termed 'the internal context' of language processing is defined as the current activity of other specialised systems that in one way or another influence the linguistic outcome. This yields a two-way input processing distinction between what is specifically *linguistic* (phonological and syntactic) and *language* processing taken as a whole.
:::

::: {.section aria-labelledby="sec9_1_4"}
### Outline {#ch9.xhtml_sec9_1_4}

The discussion will proceed as follows. First, a concise account of how MCF works will be provided as a necessary preliminary step after which the focus will be placed on input processing with reference to second language acquirers. The chapter will then conclude with a consideration of the perennial concern amongst teachers and applied linguists about how, given different types of intervention, L2 development might best be facilitated instead of hindered or simply unaffected. MCF architecture allows a particular refinement of responses to such questions. Analysing these problems by applying the MCF can in turn yield further insights for both theory and practice and define more precisely avenues for future research.
:::
:::::::

:::::::::::: {.section aria-labelledby="sec9_2"}
## The Modular Cognition Framework {#ch9.xhtml_sec9_2}

Probably the most critical issue regarding IP is the need to specify exactly how it works. Whatever outside observers can analyse as being relevant available evidence for acquisition to take place, what are the hidden, internal processes that lead to further development? To facilitate research that helps us answer such questions, a conceptual framework is needed that embeds such discussions within a more comprehensive, contemporary understanding about how the mind works in general.

As already mentioned above, this framework is about *any* kind of cognitive processing and representation. Accounts focused on language cognition are consequently embedded in this 'mind-wide' perspective. Since the MCF provides a much wider context for IP research, it is difficult to compare it straightforwardly as just one more alternative to other approaches. It is, however, possible to judge the relative *compatibility* of different proposals about how input is processed from an MCF perspective. For example, VanPatten's IP model is easily interpretable within the MCF, but [O'Grady's (2005)](#ch9.xhtml_ref9_20){.biblioref role="doc-biblioref"} emergentist explanations are only compatible in some respects (see related discussion in [Sharwood Smith et al., 2013](#ch9.xhtml_ref9_27){#ch9.xhtml_R_ref9_27 .biblioref role="doc-biblioref"}; see also [VanPatten, 2020](#ch9.xhtml_ref9_35){#ch9.xhtml_R_ref9_35 .biblioref role="doc-biblioref"}). This is because the VanPatten proposals, unlike O'Grady's or any other emergentist approach, depend on the crucial assumption that general knowledge-creating mechanisms by themselves are insufficient to explain human language acquisition. Any approach that, in a comprehensive account of language processing involving multiple systems, assumes the necessary presence of core elements that reflect our specifically *human* linguistic ability will in principle be compatible with the MCF. However, despite their association with a different view of language in the mind, emergentist ideas may still be found reinterpreted within this framework.

[]{#ch9.xhtml_p122 .pagebreak aria-label=" page 122. " role="doc-pagebreak"}As far as language is concerned, as already mentioned, *many* cognitive systems are involved in IP, not only those specialised in handling linguistic structure. Furthermore, all languages and dialects including first languages and any subsequently acquired language are efficiently handled by this network of interacting systems. Acquiring a new language is a matter of acquiring new associations, new connections within *the same network*. All features that distinguish one language from another can already be accommodated there. New linguistic representations are accordingly formed and develop within the same phonological and syntactic systems, respectively (Jackendoff, [1997](#ch9.xhtml_ref9_16){#ch9.xhtml_R_ref9_16 .biblioref role="doc-biblioref"}, [2002](#ch9.xhtml_ref9_17){#ch9.xhtml_R_ref9_17 .biblioref role="doc-biblioref"}).

::: {.section aria-labelledby="sec9_2_1"}
### Input or Inputs? {#ch9.xhtml_sec9_2_1}

The MCF interpretation of language 'input' has already been mentioned. Whatever the learner processes from the external environment must begin with stimuli impacting on the senses. For linguists, this especially includes the acoustic waves that resonate in the ears and the visual stimuli that impact upon the retina in each eye. The physical stimuli in the environment themselves are not what interests IP research because IP is more about what happens *afterwards* ([Carroll, 2001](#ch9.xhtml_ref9_7){.biblioref role="doc-biblioref"}). This is the proper focus for the MCF, and, now talking about what happens internally, input becomes a *multiple* concept. This is because mind-internal activity involves interactions between many different cognitive systems. Processing involves interaction between systems, each one responding to inputs from one or more other systems next to it. This idea of multiple inputs should become clearer in the following sections.
:::

::: {.section aria-labelledby="sec9_2_2"}
### Three Types of Relevant Context for the Study of Input Processing {#ch9.xhtml_sec9_2_2}

'External' context is context in the traditional sense that lies outside the learner. It consists in states and events existing alongside language activity (speech, writing or signing) which can be independently observed and analysed. However, from a processing perspective external context consists of patterns of raw physical stimuli impacting on the senses, patterns we deem relevant for the interpretation of some word, or construction or longer stretch of language. Interacting meaningfully with someone in a supermarket, an assistant, an angry shopper, or a security guard, for example, requires processing a whole range of sensory experiences that any of these particular scenarios may give rise to.

This brings us back to determining what parts of the external context are *actually* involved in a given act of processing. How does *ex*ternal context become recreated *in*ternally? Here input processing splits into two parallel streams:

> a.  processing of linguistic features present in the total input
> b.  the processing of the external context in which the linguistic features are embedded
>
> [*([Truscott & Sharwood Smith, 2019](#ch9.xhtml_ref9_32){.biblioref role="doc-biblioref"}, p. 55ff)*]{.credit role="doc-credit"}

Added to this immediate response to the context outside is the contribution of internal systems that supply from their own stores meanings and values. This is called the *inherently* internal context ([Truscott & Sharwood Smith, 2019](#ch9.xhtml_ref9_32){.biblioref role="doc-biblioref"}, p. 81ff). Its contributions will reflect an individual's sense of self, goals, attitudes, and emotions (Truscott & Sharwood Smith, p. 81ff). Faced with some problematic situation in a supermarket, it is therefore unsurprising that two individuals may often respond in different ways. They will have different attitudes and feelings about what is happening depending on their individual goals, personality, and sense of self. This will influence the use of whatever linguistic options they have at their disposal and this in turn, []{#ch9.xhtml_p123 .pagebreak aria-label=" page 123. " role="doc-pagebreak"}following APT, will impact on their continuing linguistic development. So, in sum, there is (1) the external context in the individual's environment, (2) the internally constructed version of that external world and then, in addition to this there is, (3) the inherently internal part, not directly related to what is outside and composed of internal systems that strongly influence any processing that takes place.
:::

::: {.section aria-labelledby="sec9_2_3"}
### The Key Features of MCF Architecture {#ch9.xhtml_sec9_2_3}

The MCF adopts a common assumption nowadays that the mind is a system of interacting systems, each one having a unique function and a unique set of operating principles ([Barrett & Kurzban, 2006](#ch9.xhtml_ref9_6){#ch9.xhtml_R_ref9_6 .biblioref role="doc-biblioref"}; [Carruthers, 2006](#ch9.xhtml_ref9_8){#ch9.xhtml_R_ref9_8 .biblioref role="doc-biblioref"}; [Cosmides & Tooby, 1997](#ch9.xhtml_ref9_10){#ch9.xhtml_R_ref9_10 .biblioref role="doc-biblioref"}). The MCF also adopts one of the major approaches within linguistics, namely that language cognition depends on specialised systems for creating, storing, maintaining and developing linguistic structure. Language processing covers much more than this but it is these linguistic systems that make language uniquely human. Although it is not the only approach of this sort that might be incorporated into the framework, the MCF, as it stands, follows Jackendoff in having independent phonological and syntactic systems responsible for creating linguistic structure ([Jackendoff, 1997](#ch9.xhtml_ref9_16){.biblioref role="doc-biblioref"}; cf. [Chomsky, 1972](#ch9.xhtml_ref9_9){#ch9.xhtml_R_ref9_9 .biblioref role="doc-biblioref"}).
:::

::: {.section aria-labelledby="sec9_2_4"}
### The Systems {#ch9.xhtml_sec9_2_4}

Each functionally specialised cognitive system is composed of a *processor* and a *store*. In each case, the processor incorporates the unique principles which characterise the creation of representations in its store. These principles are not shared by any other system hence the composition of its representations is different from those of any other system. *Visual* representations are coded differently from *auditory* representations which are coded differently from phonological representations and so on. This means that there is nothing special or unique about the idea of what many linguists refer to as 'Universal Grammar.' This is because *every specialised system in the mind has its own unique principles* so each type of representation can be distinguished from any other. In other respects, the storage and management of representations in the separate stores operate in exactly the same way.

The phonological and syntactic processors incorporate the special principles that define language as uniquely human. Since 'language' is a broad concept, IP will incorporate many characteristics that are not language-specific. One way or another, the use and acquisition of language involves the collaboration of *every* specialised system in the mind.

IP takes place within this network of cognitive systems which is composed of two groups, (a) the first sensory perceptual group and (b) those systems responsible for further, 'deeper' processing. All these systems can work, both between and within the two levels, in parallel, sequentially and bidirectionally:

- **Group I**. Perceptual systems that create representations from sensory input called 'perceptual output structures' (POpS). Example: the auditory system that responds to acoustic input. For example, hearing the utterance 'They swiftly left the building', the auditory system will construct auditory representations (or activate already existing ones) to match the acoustic input.
- **Group II**. Systems that account for further processing beyond POpS. Example: the conceptual system that supplies meanings like [swiftly]{.sc} (see the example below)

Systems are linked by *interfaces*. These allow representations in the store of one system to be associated with representations in the store of another so they can coactivate each other. []{#ch9.xhtml_p124 .pagebreak aria-label=" page 124. " role="doc-pagebreak"}For example, since the conceptual system is linked by an interface to the syntactic system, a conceptual (meaning) representation like [swiftly]{.sc}, taken from the earlier example, may get associated with a syntactic representation like *Adv*.[^18]

Once associated, the activation of one will always trigger coactivation of the other.

In the MCF, the association and coactivation of representations (structures) across different systems is enabled by the assignment of indices (indexes) for example *Adv*~*234*~⇔ [swiftly]{.sc}[^19]~*234*~. Obviously, the syntactic structure *Adv* will be associated with many other conceptual structures as well. It will also be associated with different phonological structures such as /swiftli/, including those belonging to other languages that will be coactivated with the conceptual structure [swiftly]{.sc} (e.g., Spanish /rapidamente/). In this way, input processing will involve the spreading activation of many representations that may still, although written in mutually incompatible codes, be coindexed and coactivated.

The more important systems that will concern us here are listed below. Note that the official label for any representation, following Jackendoff's terminology, contains the term 'structure' which is synonymous with 'representation'. Note that, purely for convenience, the 'S' in the abbreviations below can also, in an appropriate context, be taken to mean 'system'.

a.  auditory structure (AS) -- the generic auditory system for any kind of sound. (Group I)
b.  visual structure (VS) -- visual system. (Group I)
c.  **phonological structure (PS)** -- **phonological system**. (Group II)
d.  **syntactic structure (SS)** -- **syntactic system**. (Group II)
e.  conceptual system (CS) -- the generic conceptual system for any kind of meaning. (Group II)
f.  affective system (AfS) -- affective system. (Group II)

Items marked in boldface are the 'linguistic' systems so not, for example, including e), the conceptual system. While an enormous number of CS will be associated with given PS and SS, the conceptual system is directly linked to all other systems and hence it is possible for there to be meaning associations such as the meaning of a smell, a taste or a melody that cannot be expressed in any of the individual's languages (except, perhaps, imprecisely via paraphrasing).
:::

::: {.section aria-labelledby="sec9_2_5"}
### Acquisition by Processing (APT) {#ch9.xhtml_sec9_2_5}

Some further detail is required to explain how exactly APT works during IP since there will be continual competition between candidate representations for completing a particular task. Additional explanation with three examples will be provided later. Each system has a store functioning as its memory. The syntactic store for example will contain many structures, some of them lexical categories like *Adv*, *Adj* or *Noun*, for instance, and some of them features such as *Plural* or *Feminine, Agreement and Aspect*. Ultimately, they are all composed of primitives, the system's biological 'starter set' but mostly now linked up in combinations determined by the individual's processing history. Hence for an L2 acquirer, a PS/SS/CS set of associations for English PS /apl/ and English CS [apple]{.sc} might involve an SS *Noun (*or *Noun Phrase)* without a gender feature but [apple]{.sc} may also coactivate a particular combination of nominal features that *does* contain a gender feature when, for example, the associated PS is /mansana/ ('apple' in Spanish).[^20] Note here that the SS and PS processors always operate blindly irrespective of whichever language is involved ([Sharwood Smith & Truscott, 2014b](#ch9.xhtml_ref9_26){.biblioref role="doc-biblioref"}, pp. 188--191).

As the above bilingual 'apple' example shows that, when composing a linguistic utterance that expresses the apple concept, there will be competition between candidates for the word that []{#ch9.xhtml_p125 .pagebreak aria-label=" page 125. " role="doc-pagebreak"}will eventually be produced. Synonyms for apple in a single language may also compete with each other in just the same way. Clearly two things will be crucial for the resolution of such competition. Firstly, the relative accessibility of each competing candidate irrespective of the situation and secondly the situational context itself which may promote one candidate over the other as for example when the currently intended hearer or reader only knows Spanish. The question here is how does MCF explain these two processing options and what exactly does 'accessibility' mean? Part of the answer has to do with the nature of memory in general, but the affective system also plays a vital role as will be explained below.
:::

::: {.section aria-labelledby="sec9_2_6"}
### Memory {#ch9.xhtml_sec9_2_6}

Firstly, the distinction between working memory (WM) and long-term memory (LTM) is reconceptualised along lines similar to proposals by [Cowan (2005)](#ch9.xhtml_ref9_11){#ch9.xhtml_R_ref9_11 .biblioref role="doc-biblioref"} in the sense that WM simply refers to a *state of activation*. There is no separate WM system as such -- the representations that are currently active are by definition 'in working memory' (see [Truscott, 2017](#ch9.xhtml_ref9_28){#ch9.xhtml_R_ref9_28 .biblioref role="doc-biblioref"}). These include both representations that already exist in one of the stores and ones that are newly created as combinations of existing representations. When inactive, representations 'rest' at their current *resting level of activation*. Following APT, each act of processing involving the activation of a given representation will have the effect of *increasing* that representation's resting level. This will give it (sometimes minimally) an increased chance in any future competition with rival candidates. This is what lies behind the metaphor of 'accessibility.'

Whether a representation gets activated or not depends on ongoing activity in adjacent systems. In other words, relevant external input as observable from the outside environment will not necessarily bring about activation within a given system. Only internal inputs in the MCF sense can do that, i.e. via the activation of associated representations (if there are any such associations) in adjacent systems. For example, the syntactic system will respond to phonological input because the syntactic and phonological systems are interconnected. For this to happen, there must already be some activation in the phonological system in the first place. Neither of these two systems are directly connected with the incoming speech input so there has to be a chain of activation all the way from external speech activity to the syntactic system for any syntactic response to take place. This means that a measurement of environmental input frequency will not be a reliable indicator of how much a given representation, in principle relevant, will have *actually* been activated.

To take the previous example of 'They swiftly left the building' again, all of the component parts of this utterance including the sentence construction itself will have featured in previous input with very widely varying degrees of frequency. However, for any part of it to effectively influence the learner's current representations, the item in question has to be part of a series of events that leads up to the stores where given representations need to be modified or added. For example, assume this item is 'left' and the learner is currently treating 'leave' as a regular verb (past tense \**leaved*). To start the process whereby this learner develops a representation 'leave' as an irregular verb, the acoustic pattern that carries the sound of 'left' has to trigger the chain of events along the various connecting interfaces that may or may not be completed successfully: it needs to produce a response (an auditory structure) in the auditory store of sufficient quality to act as suitable input for the next stage, that is, for the phonological system to activate the PS /left/ in its store. All being well, this activated phonological structure then becomes input via its interface to the syntactic store. Here the syntax of 'leave' has to be changed such that the SS, V+Past, gets to be associated with the active PS, /left/. This cannot happen without input coming into []{#ch9.xhtml_p126 .pagebreak aria-label=" page 126. " role="doc-pagebreak"}syntax from the other side, i.e., from the conceptual system. This system is also responding to the situational as well as the linguistic contextual input it is getting: it may or may not be activating the crucial conceptual structure expressing the meaning 'leave in past time'. This, then, is the minimum set of events for the necessary change of representation to occur, leading, with further frequent inputs of the kind described above, to a situation when the learner can be said to have fully mastered the irregular status of the verb, 'leave'. Still, at each successive input stage, the quality of the input may not be sufficient or the receiving system may simply not be 'ready (the definition of which may be an empirical question). This whole process shows that 'mere' frequency in the environment is very far from the automatic trigger of change that some may think.
:::

::: {.section aria-labelledby="sec9_2_7"}
### Affect {#ch9.xhtml_sec9_2_7}

The other factor which was mentioned above concerning the matter of how competition may be resolved was the situational context as currently interpreted by the language user in question. The *affective* system (AfS) plays a particularly important role here. Affect is commonly associated with emotion, which is also in line with the MCF understanding of the term. However, at a deeper level, the affect system is about *value* representations ([Barrett & Russell, 1999](#ch9.xhtml_ref9_5){#ch9.xhtml_R_ref9_5 .biblioref role="doc-biblioref"}; [Damasio, 2010](#ch9.xhtml_ref9_12){#ch9.xhtml_R_ref9_12 .biblioref role="doc-biblioref"}). For instance, given representations in other systems may be associated with a strong or weak value (AfS) which will positively or negatively influence their current level of activation at any given moment. In code-switching for example, a change in the context may cause, say, a strongly activated Spanish-related network of associations to suddenly lose out to previously weakly coactivated English-related ones as when a speaker joins a Spanish conversation who is understood by the participants not to be able to understand Spanish ([Truscott & Sharwood Smith, 2017](#ch9.xhtml_ref9_31){#ch9.xhtml_R_ref9_31 .biblioref role="doc-biblioref"}). The changed context has the effect of increasing the current levels of any representations associated with English-associated representations: this change in relative value and therefore competitiveness between coactivated candidate representations causes the codeswitch to occur.

The current assumption is that at least the basic emotions like *fear*, *joy* and *disgust* are affective representations, each associated with a positive or a negative value. The more sophisticated emotions like pride and humility probably require associations with particular meanings (CS) as well. In any case the effect on processing will depend largely on the positive and negative features they contain (for a more detailed discussion on affect, see [Sharwood Smith, 2014b](#ch9.xhtml_ref9_24){#ch9.xhtml_R_ref9_24 .biblioref role="doc-biblioref"}; [Truscott & Sharwood Smith, 2019](#ch9.xhtml_ref9_32){.biblioref role="doc-biblioref"}, pp. 85--87, 159--162).
:::

::: {.section aria-labelledby="sec9_2_8"}
### Consciousness {#ch9.xhtml_sec9_2_8}

The MCF account of consciousness is based on the *activation hypothesis*: A representation becomes the object of consciousness if and only if its current activation level is sufficiently high. A representation that has an especially high activation level is one that can exert strong influence throughout the system, explaining the association between awareness and learning, and novelty in general: dealing with novelty requires stronger and more sustained levels of activity than does more automatic processing and therefore tends to involve awareness of relevant representations. High activation levels naturally occur within POpS (in Group I), due to the inherent strength of sensory stimulation and the connectedness of the perceptual systems with each other and with other systems. Conscious experience is thus dominated by perceptual representations. Conceptual representations have a more limited relation to consciousness. An abstract concept like CHAIR, for example, does not itself appear in consciousness, but images of chairs serve as stand-ins for it; we mentally see specific chairs while the abstract concept lurks in the background, giving meaning to the images. Representations that never reach the activation levels required for []{#ch9.xhtml_p127 .pagebreak aria-label=" page 127. " role="doc-pagebreak"}consciousness include those in SS and PS, due to their relative isolation within the system and the very specialised, UG-defined processing that they do.
:::

::: {.section aria-labelledby="sec9_2_9"}
### Internal Context Revisited {#ch9.xhtml_sec9_2_9}

Earlier on, we introduced different kinds of context with input processing being determined by internal context. We can now more precisely describe the internal context of what we have narrowly described as *linguistic* processing as opposed to language processing in general. Strictly linguistic processing is simply processing within the strictly linguistic portions of the system, SS and PS. The context of the linguistic processing is then any current activity elsewhere that has an impact on it, directly or indirectly. Having an impact means influencing the activation levels of linguistic representations and thereby helping to determine which SS and PS representations are used in current processing. The SS--CS interface brings this influence from CS to the syntactic processing and thence to phonological processing via the PS--SS interface. The crucial influence of affective factors occurs by way of CS, the main stage for internal context.
:::
::::::::::::

:::::: {.section aria-labelledby="sec9_3"}
## Three Examples of Acquisition by Processing {#ch9.xhtml_sec9_3}

The distinction between external context, the source of input in its traditional sense and the internal context of linguistic processing throws the spotlight on how the acquisition of language involves the interaction of many cognitive systems and not just those specialised in handling linguistic structure. In this section we look at three examples of how IP plays out with regard to different aspects of what we include in the term 'language'.

::: {.section aria-labelledby="sec9_3_1"}
### Acquiring Words, Stock Phrases and Constructions {#ch9.xhtml_sec9_3_1}

By 'word' is meant here single words like *station*. Stock phrases would include single words as well but also idioms and often frequently occurring sequences of words that are predictable from the situational context and are acquired as unanalysed chunks like *isn't*, *don't*, *thank you*, *not at all*, *I mean*, *as it were*, *that's fine*, *no way*, *know what I mean* and even longer, memorised sequences. By 'chunks' is meant, in MCF terms, sequences of PS that are associated with CS, i.e., as phonological/conceptual associations. Syntactic associations may not yet have developed. In fact, the syntactic system will automatically respond to any CS or PS input and may accordingly assign some kind of temporary, default structure like NP or VP.

The main point here is that the creation of (in this case) L2 PS/CS associations may already be sufficient for these 'syntactically deficient' chunks to be inserted into an utterance, even in apparently appropriate ways. Repeated IP, in a variety of situations, of utterances containing the item in question may eventually allow for the development of more L2-compliant syntactic structure. At an even more undeveloped stage, it is in principle possible for a chunk of auditory structure to be associated with a meaning (CS) without any intervening linguistic (PS or SS) associations. The resulting non-linguistic AS/CS (sound/meaning) association could under limited circumstances still allow the communication of meaning thereby creating a complete illusion of linguistic mastery. Again, in reality, the two linguistic systems will always attempt automatically to provide some kind of response of their own. The take-home message for teachers and applied linguists is that the appropriate use of words and constructions on a given occasion cannot be taken as necessarily reflecting an adequate state of phonological and syntactic []{#ch9.xhtml_p128 .pagebreak aria-label=" page 128. " role="doc-pagebreak"}development. Sooner or later, misuse of such structures may signal the presence of incomplete grammatical development (cf. [Klima & Bellugi, 1966](#ch9.xhtml_ref9_18){#ch9.xhtml_R_ref9_18 .biblioref role="doc-biblioref"}; [Hakuta, 1974](#ch9.xhtml_ref9_15){#ch9.xhtml_R_ref9_15 .biblioref role="doc-biblioref"}). More input processing is needed and hence more exposure to input from outside.
:::

::: {.section aria-labelledby="sec9_3_2"}
### Acquiring New Linguistic Structures (PS/SS) {#ch9.xhtml_sec9_3_2}

Phonological acquisition takes place as a result of *phonological* processing triggered by input from adjacent auditory and/or syntactic systems. Similarly, syntactic acquisition occurs as a result of *syntactic* processing triggered by phonological and/or conceptual input. To take a syntactic example, post-nominal adjectives as in L2 French 'bâton rouge' (*lit*. 'stick red') will not, in a uniformly *pre*nominal adjective language, have any L2-compliant SS available to match with the L2 phonological input. Assuming that /ʁuʒ/ -- the PS of 'rouge' -- has been assigned a nominal category, *Noun*, then /bɑtɔ̃/ -- the PS of 'bâton'-- will be assigned some highly activated syntactic category in conformity with the currently dominant L1, probably resulting in a CS apparently describing a shade of 'red', e.g. 'stick red'. There will of course be clues present in the linguistic and situational context as to the adjectival identity of 'rouge' but these clues may not yet get to influence the current syntactic system. Once the correct L2 syntactic status, *Adj*, is established, the new L2 syntactic structure still has to be activated for its resting level to attain levels that can outcompete the dominant L1 candidate. All other things being equal, the new *Adj* representation will start out with a low resting level and need repeated processing over time to have this built up sufficiently to stand a chance in the competition against the initially dominant L1 alternative. Exactly the same situation will obtain for new L2 phonological structures faced with their competing L2 candidates as it will for all the functionally specialised systems in the mind.
:::

::: {.section aria-labelledby="sec9_3_3"}
### Acquiring Pragmatics {#ch9.xhtml_sec9_3_3}

Anything related to meaning is located in the conceptual system. We may not have words to directly express certain meanings, poetic and musical ones for example, in any of the languages we use. However, they may still be represented in our conceptual system and be open to linguistic expression via imprecise paraphrases. In any case, meanings traditionally labelled 'semantic' or 'pragmatic' do not belong to any linguistic meaning system. They are, in both cases, *conceptual* representations (CS): They are language-related by association: that they happen to be associated with linguistic representations (PS or SS). In fact, in a vast number of cases, a CS will in fact be language-related. Like any representations, they can be more or less complex. A 'semantic' CS that defines a basic meaning to be associated with a noun, say, is relatively speaking the simplest type of CS that can appear in a given PS⇔SS⇔CS chain of associations. There is therefore no 'syntax/semantic' interface as such, only the interface obtaining between the ('linguistic') syntactic system and the conceptual system. However, wherever the input does not provide transparent clues, acquiring the right SS/CS associations will always be challenging from an IP perspective so establishing the right SS/CS associations can still be difficult even when only what would traditionally be labelled 'semantic' associations are involved. Examples include various uses of the English definite article, grammatical gender, and expressing Topic and Focus. In other words, even though pragmatic meaning appears to require more complex networks of association, when talking about processing complexity or learning difficulty, the difference between conceptual/semantic and conceptual/pragmatic processing is best described in relative terms.

In IP terms, then, and following APT principles, such multiple associations need to be built up via repeated experience with situations where all the necessary clues for establishing the pragmatic conditions of use are present. Depending on their salience, eventually the co-occurrence []{#ch9.xhtml_p129 .pagebreak aria-label=" page 129. " role="doc-pagebreak"}of these clues may register with the conceptual system so that all the necessary connections are made in the same way described in the previous section.

It is not surprising that pragmatic knowledge as well as those areas of semantic knowledge that depend on difficult-to-acquire associations are found lacking even in very advanced non-native speakers of a language. There may, however, be help at hand for learners which has to do with the potential of conceptual structure to influence conscious experience and which will be mentioned below.
:::
::::::

:::::::::: {.section aria-labelledby="sec9_4"}
## Recommendations for Practice {#ch9.xhtml_sec9_4}

Adopting the MCF leads to various implications about input processing and also suggestions as to how to help learners notice and process relevant input features more effectively. In all cases, more research is needed. First, we will consider the question of what 'noticing' actually means and then look at different types of input 'enhancement' (Sharwood Smith, [1993](#ch9.xhtml_ref9_22){.biblioref role="doc-biblioref"}, [2014a](#ch9.xhtml_ref9_23){#ch9.xhtml_R_ref9_23 .biblioref role="doc-biblioref"}; [Sharwood Smith & Truscott, 2014a](#ch9.xhtml_ref9_25){.biblioref role="doc-biblioref"}).

::: {.section aria-labelledby="sec9_4_1"}
### Noticing {#ch9.xhtml_sec9_4_1}

[Schmidt (1990)](#ch9.xhtml_ref9_21){#ch9.xhtml_R_ref9_21 .biblioref role="doc-biblioref"} proposed that acquisition of a feature requires that the learner be aware of that feature's presence in the input -- the *noticing hypothesis*. Learners who are to acquire the English verbal form -*s*, for example, must not only encounter forms like *talks* but also consciously recognize the presence of the *s*. The required awareness does not extend to the meaning of the item or to any other abstract knowledge; the requirement is simply that its presence must consciously register. Schmidt was also concerned about higher level awareness ('awareness at the level of understanding') but strictly separated it from noticing and made no claim that it is necessary for learning.

The noticing hypothesis has become prominent but has also received heavy criticism (e.g. [Truscott & Sharwood Smith, 2011](#ch9.xhtml_ref9_30){#ch9.xhtml_R_ref9_30 .biblioref role="doc-biblioref"}), especially regarding its lack of theoretical clarity. Interpreting the hypothesis within a general theoretical framework such as MCF may offer greater hope of fruitful development and testing.

First, to say that a learner is aware of a feature in the input is to say that (a) a perceptual (POpS) representation has been constructed specifically of that feature, and (b) this representation has acquired a high current activation level, making it the content of awareness, if only briefly. This is noticing. The (a) portion is a claim that something which is not represented at all during input processing will not be acquired. An issue for this claim is whether *talks*, for example, can be stored as a chunk and then successfully analysed, off-line, into its component parts. But discussions of noticing typically take (a) for granted, focusing on the second claim (b), that the feature must be *consciously* represented.

Importantly, there is no implication that the learner *understands* the feature that is being consciously represented or is able to say anything about it beyond the fact that it is there in the input. Meaning and all varieties of abstractions reside in CS; a perceptual representation is only meaningful by virtue of its connections to conceptual structures. Awareness at the level of understanding, a step beyond noticing, is thus about CS processing that is based on the perceptual representations. This processing can be conscious in the indirect manner, being reflected in active perceptual representations.

The noticing hypothesis thus claims that the presence of *talks* in the input is useful for acquisition of the -*s* affix only if the latter is itself perceptually represented and this representation has an activation level that is sufficient for awareness. Why would such a constraint exist? From an MCF perspective, the issue is this: How active does a perceptual representation have to be, []{#ch9.xhtml_p130 .pagebreak aria-label=" page 130. " role="doc-pagebreak"}and for how long, in order for an adjacent module to make use of the information it contains? The answer should depend on the relation between the adjacent module and the information in the perceptual representation. If the module can deal with that information in an automatic manner, then the thresholds should be quite low, and so the need for awareness is doubtful. But if there is no automaticity, the requirements should be much higher, in which case the noticing hypothesis appears quite plausible.

The issue now becomes the particular view of language one subscribes to. If language development relies on general purpose learning mechanisms, automaticity is unlikely and so the need for awareness appears natural. If, on the other hand, the linguistic system (PS and SS) is innately specialised to process linguistic input then it should be able to do so automatically and therefore unconsciously. Metalinguistic knowledge, in contrast, is acquired by non-specialist processes, centred in CS. The implication is that, given the assumption of an innate Universal Grammar, the noticing hypothesis probably applies to metalinguistic knowledge but not to development of the linguistic modules.

Finally, what is the role of internal context in noticing? Quite simply, it determines what perceptual representations are constructed and which of them are then noticed, by selectively raising activation levels within POpS. A classroom setting can translate to outside-in context that favours construction and strong activation of representations related to grammar, such as verbal -*s*. An active goal of learning grammar can have the same effect, as can the attachment of value to features of grammar.
:::

:::::::: {.section aria-labelledby="sec9_4_2"}
### Input Enhancement {#ch9.xhtml_sec9_4_2}

The term 'enhancement' reflects the ambitions of the teacher to actively facilitate internal learning processes. More precisely stated, it refers to the manipulation of the environmental stimuli with this aim in mind ([Sharwood Smith, 1993](#ch9.xhtml_ref9_22){.biblioref role="doc-biblioref"}; [Sharwood Smith & Truscott, 2014a](#ch9.xhtml_ref9_25){.biblioref role="doc-biblioref"}). The teacher can only work with what lies *outside* learners. Labelling of different types of enhancement will tend to be based on the teacher's *intended focus*. The *actual effects* are another matter. We can have some confidence in establishing what the teacher's intended target is. We can have much less confidence in knowing whether the required mental processing by the learner, conscious or otherwise, together with any intended outcomes have actually been achieved. Our lack of detailed knowledge about processing prevents us from knowing what the effects of this or that teacher intervention may be. Also, if a desired outcome is ultimately achieved, we cannot be sure if this was *because of* or *despite* the intervention.

::: {.section aria-labelledby="sec9_4_2_1"}
#### Perceptual Enhancement {#ch9.xhtml_sec9_4_2_1}

Ultimately all input enhancement will be based, at least initially, on sensory perception since the only means of communicating anything with another person is via the senses but the actual targets of enhancement may vary. What is called 'perceptual enhancement' here refers to attempts to make salient given acoustic and visual features of spoken, written and signed language in the hope that this will trigger the construction and further processing of representations of these features thereby leading to the further development of the leaner's proficiency in the language. With speech for example, this means providing good quality acoustic input to the auditory system. Resulting auditory representation will then, or so it is hoped, provide good quality input at the next stage, i.e., to the phonological system in order to optimise without any firm guarantees of success, the chances of phonological growth being promoted and in turn syntactic growth as well. In other words, the target of perceptual enhancement is to raise the []{#ch9.xhtml_p131 .pagebreak aria-label=" page 131. " role="doc-pagebreak"}sensory perceptual quality of selected parts of utterances and text with the aim, that at some later stage of processing the chances of bringing about changes in the current L2 system will thereby be improved.
:::

::: {.section aria-labelledby="sec9_4_2_2"}
#### Enhancement of Linguistic Structure (PS and SS) {#ch9.xhtml_sec9_4_2_2}

Following perceptual enhancement, any resulting phonological development may in turn provide better input to syntax. If the teacher's intended enhancement is to promote syntactic growth by highlighting a given grammatical construction, then the chain of inputs must get through to syntax in order to form new SS.

A simple example would be the English 3^rd^ person singular as in 'she run*s*'. The Chinese learner of English processing 'runs' may well perceive the 's' sound if it gets represented in the auditory system but the buck does not stop there. Consequent formation of a matching PS for the whole word will have strong competition from Chinese-based PS that do not allow for postsyllabic /s/ to occur with result that the final 's' will not be 'noticed': it will, as it were, be discounted as relevant for the formation of a new PS. If there is an inadequate response from PS, there will be little hope of triggering a 3^rd^ person singular feature in the syntactic system. Even if a new SS does get added representing 3^rd^ person singular, it is unlikely to impact fluent performance until its activation level has been built up over time. The teacher will continue to notice errors even if the appropriate representation is in place (see [Lardiere, 1998](#ch9.xhtml_ref9_19){#ch9.xhtml_R_ref9_19 .biblioref role="doc-biblioref"}).
:::

::: {.section aria-labelledby="sec9_4_2_3"}
#### Conceptual Enhancement {#ch9.xhtml_sec9_4_2_3}

There are two distinct ways in which influencing conceptual processing may assist development. The first is a more general type of 'form/meaning' enhancement simply by making the meaning of spoken and written as well as the linguistic and non-linguistic contextual evidence clear and salient in order to optimise all conceptual input to the syntactic system albeit without any firm guarantees. This is already a general strategy familiar to good language teachers, but it can be given greater focus when explicitly linked to relevant research as is already the case with VanPatten's processing instruction but also with research going back to the 1970s ([VanPatten, 2017](#ch9.xhtml_ref9_34){#ch9.xhtml_R_ref9_34 .biblioref role="doc-biblioref"}).

The second type of conceptual enhancement relies on 'meta' knowledge about language built up in the form of conceptual structures which can feature in conscious processing. This would cover explicit knowledge about phonology, grammar and pragmatics so it might be called metalinguistic conceptual enhancement. It involves making people consciously aware of target features to be acquired and, in more or less detail, the way in which they work. This certainly harks back to traditional ways of teaching formal grammar but, as already indicated, still requires caution in assuming where and when such explicit teaching is backed up by the science and consequently needs a lot more research to take place to suggest where, when and how explicit methods may be efficacious.
:::

::: {.section aria-labelledby="sec9_4_2_4"}
#### Affective Input Enhancement {#ch9.xhtml_sec9_4_2_4}

The prime target of affective enhancement is the attitudes, the emotions or values of the learner with regard to the L2. This can mean attributing positive value to particular linguistic items or it can enhance the input in more indirect, contextual ways by making the experience of using the L2 in and outside the classroom more positive. In the case of strictly linguistic input, as opposed to accompanying contextual input, this would be what Dulay, Burt, and Krashen would call lowering the 'affective filter' and allowing the learner to process L2 input more effectively ([Dulay et al., 1982](#ch9.xhtml_ref9_13){#ch9.xhtml_R_ref9_13 .biblioref role="doc-biblioref"}; [Sharwood Smith, 2014b](#ch9.xhtml_ref9_24){.biblioref role="doc-biblioref"}). According to whether negative or positive value is []{#ch9.xhtml_p132 .pagebreak aria-label=" page 132. " role="doc-pagebreak"}attached, various types of emotion may be experienced by the learner with consequences that still need to be properly explored.
:::

::: {.section aria-labelledby="sec9_4_2_5"}
#### Lexical Input Enhancement {#ch9.xhtml_sec9_4_2_5}

The question of what a 'word' is, in the current perspective, has already been dealt with in various places as part of a more general explanation of how processing works. As 'vocabulary' is regarded as an important focus of language teaching, a short, separate mention of word learning in this section on input enhancement seems appropriate. Apart from its metalinguistic status as a basic unit of language as commonly featured in numerous types of word list, most theoretical analyses will treat the word as actually being a combination of different units; for this reason, words are often treated in linguistics not just as carriers of meaning but as carriers of vital evidence concerning the grammatical system as a whole. In the MCF, a word in its minimal form is a piece of *phonology* associated with a piece of *syntax* plus, one might argue, an associated piece of *conceptual* structure as well providing it with a meaning. Whereas neither the PS nor the SS can be directly enhanced, so only indirectly via spreading activation, the CS definitely can be targeted more directly. So too, can the different perceptual structures associated with the PS and CS, in other words, the *sound* of the word and also its *visual* appearance, i.e. its written shape or, where appropriate, its shape in a sign language. This then would certainly seem to warrant trying out any teaching strategies that effectively exploit all these various conceptual and perceptual associations both separately and in combination, again where possible with affective enhancement as well. The resulting, frequent co-activation of all these various structures associated with a given word, affectively boosted as well, should create the optimal conditions for lexical growth to take place.
:::
::::::::
::::::::::

::: {.section aria-labelledby="sec9_5"}
## Future Directions {#ch9.xhtml_sec9_5}

The way forward can be briefly described as both clear and challenging. It should have become clear at least that IP cannot be studied in isolation. A wider perspective is needed. Some might restrict that to language acquisition, some to multilingualism in general but the intended take-home message has obviously cast the net even wider to include cognitive processing as a whole. A framework like the MCF is needed for researchers to guide their thinking and empirical research in response to this challenge. The most recent example of this to date is probably Agebjörn's MCF-based analysis, based on four separately published experimental studies, of the acquisition of definiteness by Belorussian and Russian learners ([Agebjörn, 2021](#ch9.xhtml_ref9_4){#ch9.xhtml_R_ref9_4 .biblioref role="doc-biblioref"}).
:::

::: {.endnotes role="doc-endnotes"}
## Notes {#ch9.xhtml_h2}
:::

Explaining change in transition grammars. In J. Herschensohn & M. Young-Scholten (Eds.), `<cite>`{=html}The Cambridge handbook of second language acquisition`</cite>`{=html} (pp. 560--580). Cambridge University Press.]{#ch9.xhtml_ref9_27}
- [[Truscott, J.](#ch9.xhtml_R_ref9_28){.backlink aria-label=" This link returns to its first in-text citation. " role="doc-backlink"} (2017). Modularity, working memory, and second language acquisition: A research program. `<cite>`{=html}Second Language Research`</cite>`{=html}, *33*, 313--324.]{#ch9.xhtml_ref9_28}
- [[Truscott, J., & Sharwood Smith, M.](#ch9.xhtml_R_ref9_29){.backlink aria-label=" This link returns to its first in-text citation. " role="doc-backlink"} (2004). Acquisition by processing: A modular perspective on language development. `<cite>`{=html}Bilingualism: Language and Cognition`</cite>`{=html}, *7*, 1--20.]{#ch9.xhtml_ref9_29}
- [[Truscott, J., & Sharwood Smith, M.](#ch9.xhtml_R_ref9_30){.backlink aria-label=" This link returns to its first in-text citation. " role="doc-backlink"} (2011). Input, intake and consciousness: The quest for a theoretical foundation. `<cite>`{=html}Studies in Second Language Acquisition`</cite>`{=html}, *33*, 297--328.]{#ch9.xhtml_ref9_30}
- [[Truscott, J., & Sharwood Smith, M.](#ch9.xhtml_R_ref9_31){.backlink aria-label=" This link returns to its first in-text citation. " role="doc-backlink"} (2017). Representation, processing and code-switching. `<cite>`{=html}Bilingualism: Language & Cognition`</cite>`{=html}, *20*, 903--916.]{#ch9.xhtml_ref9_31}
