[]{#ch10.xhtml}

::: {}
# []{#ch10.xhtml_p134 .pagebreak aria-label=" page 134. " role="doc-pagebreak"}[10 ]{.chnum .ordinal}[The Declarative / Procedural Model and Input Processing]{.chtitle} {#ch10.xhtml_h1}

Sarah Grey

Fordham University

DOI: [10.4324/9781003041412-13](https://dx.doi.org/10.4324/9781003041412-13){aria-label="D.O.I. link to this document."}
:::

::::::: {.section .introduction role="doc-introduction"}
## Introduction and Definitions {#ch10.xhtml_sec10_1}

The Declarative/Procedural model (Ullman, [2001](#ch10.xhtml_ref10_64){#ch10.xhtml_R_ref10_64 .biblioref role="doc-biblioref"}, [2004](#ch10.xhtml_ref10_65){#ch10.xhtml_R_ref10_65 .biblioref role="doc-biblioref"}, [2016](#ch10.xhtml_ref10_68){#ch10.xhtml_R_ref10_68 .biblioref role="doc-biblioref"}, [2020](#ch10.xhtml_ref10_69){#ch10.xhtml_R_ref10_69 .biblioref role="doc-biblioref"}) is a model of language that is grounded in independent knowledge of two well-studied memory systems in the brain: declarative and procedural. Knowledge of the two memory systems -- including how they function, the types of knowledge they sustain, and how they change over the lifespan -- has enabled the Declarative/Procedural (DP) model to make a number of neurocognitive predictions about language. The DP model and input processing (IP) are quite distinct from one another regarding both their scope and the aspects of language and language processing they seek to explain. IP refers to the ways in which language learners attend to and process linguistic input. Whereas the DP model theorizes about the neurobiology of language and language learning in general; that is, with specific neurobiological predictions for both native and non-native language across the lifespan (see e.g., [Buffington & Morgan-Short, 2019](#ch10.xhtml_ref10_10){#ch10.xhtml_R_ref10_10 .biblioref role="doc-biblioref"}; Ullman, [2016](#ch10.xhtml_ref10_68){.biblioref role="doc-biblioref"}, [2020](#ch10.xhtml_ref10_69){.biblioref role="doc-biblioref"}), IP theorizes focally about behavioral, (psycho)linguistic aspects of late-learned second language processing during comprehension, with the understanding that acquisition cannot proceed without comprehension ([VanPatten, 2020](#ch10.xhtml_ref10_73){#ch10.xhtml_R_ref10_73 .biblioref role="doc-biblioref"}).

This chapter provides an overview of the DP model with respect to adult second language (L2) learning and considers ways in which the model connects with aspects of IP. I first provide definitions and descriptions of the declarative and procedural memory systems followed by discussion of DP model predictions about language and language learning. Then, I consider areas of theoretical alignment between the DP model and IP. The second half of the chapter presents behavioral and neurocognitive research on the roles of declarative and procedural memory in L2 learning and discusses potential alignments between the DP model and IP within this evidence. I conclude with a discussion of future directions for research that can connect DP model and IP perspectives in studying L2 learning.

:::::: {.section aria-labelledby="sec10_1_1"}
### Declarative and Procedural Memory Systems {#ch10.xhtml_sec10_1_1}

The DP model is motivated by knowledge of the functioning of declarative and procedural memory, two long-term memory systems. Information about each system is provided here to facilitate understanding of the model, its predictions for L2 learning and processing, and relevant research.

::: {.section aria-labelledby="sec10_1_1_1"}
#### []{#ch10.xhtml_p135 .pagebreak aria-label=" page 135. " role="doc-pagebreak"}Declarative Memory {#ch10.xhtml_sec10_1_1_1}

The declarative memory system is involved in the encoding, storage, and retrieval of information about semantic, fact-based and episodic, event- and experience-based knowledge. For example, learning the fact that cats were worshipped by Ancient Egyptians is a declarative memory process. Declarative memory is a highly flexible system; learning is not confined to only semantic or episodic knowledge and declarative memory knowledge may be used with knowledge from other memory systems. Furthermore, at least some information in declarative memory can be consciously accessed and may be described as explicit, though declarative memory also supports the learning of implicit information ([Cabeza & Moscovitch, 2013](#ch10.xhtml_ref10_11){#ch10.xhtml_R_ref10_11 .biblioref role="doc-biblioref"}; [Henke, 2010](#ch10.xhtml_ref10_31){#ch10.xhtml_R_ref10_31 .biblioref role="doc-biblioref"}; [Squire & Dede, 2015](#ch10.xhtml_ref10_52){#ch10.xhtml_R_ref10_52 .biblioref role="doc-biblioref"}; Ullman, [2016](#ch10.xhtml_ref10_68){.biblioref role="doc-biblioref"}, [2020](#ch10.xhtml_ref10_69){.biblioref role="doc-biblioref"}). However, the declarative memory system appears to be the only learning/memory system that underlies explicit knowledge, so any acquired explicit knowledge must have been learned in the declarative memory system ([Ullman, 2020](#ch10.xhtml_ref10_69){.biblioref role="doc-biblioref"}). Learning within the declarative system can occur after a single exposure, although the system itself appears to be associative in nature and thus frequency of input and attention to input strengthen encoding ([Eichenbaum & Cohen, 2001](#ch10.xhtml_ref10_17){#ch10.xhtml_R_ref10_17 .biblioref role="doc-biblioref"}; [Foerde et al., 2006](#ch10.xhtml_ref10_21){#ch10.xhtml_R_ref10_21 .biblioref role="doc-biblioref"}; [Squire & Dede, 2015](#ch10.xhtml_ref10_52){.biblioref role="doc-biblioref"}). Finally, knowledge in declarative memory, or access to this knowledge, may be more prone to weakening, or forgetting, than knowledge in the procedural memory system ([Foerde, 2007](#ch10.xhtml_ref10_20){#ch10.xhtml_R_ref10_20 .biblioref role="doc-biblioref"}; [Takashima et al., 2006](#ch10.xhtml_ref10_61){#ch10.xhtml_R_ref10_61 .biblioref role="doc-biblioref"}).

Declarative memory ability varies within a single individual throughout the lifespan and across individuals depending on sex, genes, handedness, disease, or other biological variables ([Eichenbaum & Cohen, 2001](#ch10.xhtml_ref10_17){.biblioref role="doc-biblioref"}; Ullman, [2016](#ch10.xhtml_ref10_68){.biblioref role="doc-biblioref"}, [2020](#ch10.xhtml_ref10_69){.biblioref role="doc-biblioref"}). Enhanced declarative memory ability, whatever the origin, is indexed by superior performance on tasks that involve the conscious storage and retrieval of verbal and/or non-verbal information. Recently, declarative memory has been proposed as a potential component of L2 aptitude ([Buffington & Morgan-Short, 2019](#ch10.xhtml_ref10_10){.biblioref role="doc-biblioref"}).

Declarative memory processing is supported in the brain by medial temporal lobe regions -- the hippocampus and related structures -- with connections to temporal and parietal neocortical regions. Learning of new information involves all parts of the system whereas consolidation of this information (which typically occurs during sleep) appears to rely on the medial temporal lobe components ([Eichenbaum & Cohen, 2001](#ch10.xhtml_ref10_17){.biblioref role="doc-biblioref"}; [Squire & Knowlton, 2000](#ch10.xhtml_ref10_53){#ch10.xhtml_R_ref10_53 .biblioref role="doc-biblioref"}). Eventually, information becomes independent of these structures and reliance shifts to neocortical regions in the temporal lobes ([Squire, 2004](#ch10.xhtml_ref10_51){#ch10.xhtml_R_ref10_51 .biblioref role="doc-biblioref"}; Squire et al., [2001](#ch10.xhtml_ref10_56){#ch10.xhtml_R_ref10_56 .biblioref role="doc-biblioref"}, [2004](#ch10.xhtml_ref10_58){#ch10.xhtml_R_ref10_58 .biblioref role="doc-biblioref"}; [Squire & Knowlton, 2000](#ch10.xhtml_ref10_53){.biblioref role="doc-biblioref"}). Other brain regions have also been proposed to underlie declarative memory including the anterior ventral--lateral prefrontal cortex, areas of the frontal cortex corresponding to Brodmann's Areas 45 and 47, and the right cerebellum (Ullman, [2013](#ch10.xhtml_ref10_67){#ch10.xhtml_R_ref10_67 .biblioref role="doc-biblioref"}, [2016](#ch10.xhtml_ref10_68){.biblioref role="doc-biblioref"}, [2020](#ch10.xhtml_ref10_69){.biblioref role="doc-biblioref"}) as well as the cingulate cortex ([Eichenbaum, 2012](#ch10.xhtml_ref10_16){#ch10.xhtml_R_ref10_16 .biblioref role="doc-biblioref"}). Neurological insult to these regions impairs an individual's ability to create new memories (anterograde amensia) and also results in partial to severe loss of old memories (retrograde amnesia).
:::

::: {.section aria-labelledby="sec10_1_1_2"}
#### Procedural Memory {#ch10.xhtml_sec10_1_1_2}

Procedural memory refers to the skill and habit learning system involved in the learning and control of long-established motor and cognitive functions ([Ashby et al., 2010](#ch10.xhtml_ref10_4){#ch10.xhtml_R_ref10_4 .biblioref role="doc-biblioref"}; [Eichenbaum & Cohen, 2001](#ch10.xhtml_ref10_17){.biblioref role="doc-biblioref"}), such as learning to ride a bicycle or learning to read mirror-inverted text. It essentially underlies the "how" of task or action performance ([Beggs et al., 1999](#ch10.xhtml_ref10_9){#ch10.xhtml_R_ref10_9 .biblioref role="doc-biblioref"}) and may be specialized for the learning of sequences and rules ([Henke, 2010](#ch10.xhtml_ref10_31){.biblioref role="doc-biblioref"}; [Poldrack & Foerde, 2008](#ch10.xhtml_ref10_46){#ch10.xhtml_R_ref10_46 .biblioref role="doc-biblioref"}; [Squire & Schacter, 2002](#ch10.xhtml_ref10_54){#ch10.xhtml_R_ref10_54 .biblioref role="doc-biblioref"}). This system learns gradually and requires practice -- the representations that result from learning are processes or routines as opposed to the "items" stored in declarative []{#ch10.xhtml_p136 .pagebreak aria-label=" page 136. " role="doc-pagebreak"}memory, and such representations can be retained long-term, i.e., are less susceptible to being forgotten ([Christiansen et al., 2002](#ch10.xhtml_ref10_14){#ch10.xhtml_R_ref10_14 .biblioref role="doc-biblioref"}; H. [Eichenbaum, 2012](#ch10.xhtml_ref10_16){.biblioref role="doc-biblioref"}; Ullman, [2016](#ch10.xhtml_ref10_68){.biblioref role="doc-biblioref"}, [2020](#ch10.xhtml_ref10_69){.biblioref role="doc-biblioref"}). Procedural knowledge is not available to conscious awareness ([Squire & Zola, 1996](#ch10.xhtml_ref10_55){#ch10.xhtml_R_ref10_55 .biblioref role="doc-biblioref"}; [Tulving, 2000](#ch10.xhtml_ref10_63){#ch10.xhtml_R_ref10_63 .biblioref role="doc-biblioref"}) and learning in the procedural memory system does not require (and may in fact be inhibited by) attention ([Foerde et al., 2006](#ch10.xhtml_ref10_21){.biblioref role="doc-biblioref"}), though once learned, the knowledge, or access to the knowledge, applies automatically and reliably ([Morgan-Short & Ullman, 2011](#ch10.xhtml_ref10_36){#ch10.xhtml_R_ref10_36 .biblioref role="doc-biblioref"}). Unlike declarative memory, which is flexible, knowledge in procedural memory appears to be inflexible; that is, unavailable for use by other memory systems ([Buffington & Morgan-Short, 2019](#ch10.xhtml_ref10_10){.biblioref role="doc-biblioref"}).

Procedural memory ability, like declarative memory, varies as a function of individual differences such as sex, handedness, genes, and other biological variables (Ullman, [2005](#ch10.xhtml_ref10_66){#ch10.xhtml_R_ref10_66 .biblioref role="doc-biblioref"}, [2013](#ch10.xhtml_ref10_67){.biblioref role="doc-biblioref"}, [2016](#ch10.xhtml_ref10_68){.biblioref role="doc-biblioref"}, [2020](#ch10.xhtml_ref10_69){.biblioref role="doc-biblioref"}). Enhanced procedural memory ability has been measured using tasks such as motor sequence learning, serial reaction time, and probabilistic classification learning ([Buffington & Morgan-Short, 2019](#ch10.xhtml_ref10_10){.biblioref role="doc-biblioref"}; [Carpenter, 2008](#ch10.xhtml_ref10_13){#ch10.xhtml_R_ref10_13 .biblioref role="doc-biblioref"}) and procedural memory has also recently been suggested as a potential component of L2 aptitude ([Buffington & Morgan-Short, 2019](#ch10.xhtml_ref10_10){.biblioref role="doc-biblioref"}).

Procedural memory processing is supported in the brain by frontal, temporal, parietal brain regions, the cerebellum, and basal ganglia structures, including a striatal-thalamic-frontal loop that circulates information from the cortex to the striatum (a basal ganglia structure), then to the thalamus, and back to the frontal cortex ([Ashby et al., 2010](#ch10.xhtml_ref10_4){.biblioref role="doc-biblioref"}; [Eichenbaum, 2012](#ch10.xhtml_ref10_16){.biblioref role="doc-biblioref"}; [Gabrieli, 1998](#ch10.xhtml_ref10_23){#ch10.xhtml_R_ref10_23 .biblioref role="doc-biblioref"}; [Squire & Zola, 1996](#ch10.xhtml_ref10_55){.biblioref role="doc-biblioref"}; Ullman, [2004](#ch10.xhtml_ref10_65){.biblioref role="doc-biblioref"}, [2016](#ch10.xhtml_ref10_68){.biblioref role="doc-biblioref"}, [2020](#ch10.xhtml_ref10_69){.biblioref role="doc-biblioref"}). Neurological abnormalities within the procedural memory system have been shown to compromise language functions related to grammatical processing, as in non-fluent aphasia or children with specific language impairment ([Hedenius, Adi-Japha, et al., 2011](#ch10.xhtml_ref10_29){#ch10.xhtml_R_ref10_29 .biblioref role="doc-biblioref"}; [Hedenius, Persson, et al., 2011](#ch10.xhtml_ref10_30){#ch10.xhtml_R_ref10_30 .biblioref role="doc-biblioref"}; [Ullman & Pierpont, 2005](#ch10.xhtml_ref10_70){#ch10.xhtml_R_ref10_70 .biblioref role="doc-biblioref"}).
:::

::: {.section aria-labelledby="sec10_1_1_3"}
#### Compensation, Competition, and Cooperation {#ch10.xhtml_sec10_1_1_3}

Declarative and procedural memory are dissociated brain systems that carry out different cognitive functions, but the functioning of each memory system in relation to the other is quite dynamic ([Packard & Knowlton, 2002](#ch10.xhtml_ref10_43){#ch10.xhtml_R_ref10_43 .biblioref role="doc-biblioref"}; [Poldrack & Packard, 2003](#ch10.xhtml_ref10_47){#ch10.xhtml_R_ref10_47 .biblioref role="doc-biblioref"}; Ullman, [2016](#ch10.xhtml_ref10_68){.biblioref role="doc-biblioref"}, [2020](#ch10.xhtml_ref10_69){.biblioref role="doc-biblioref"}). For example, deficits in processing ability for one system can be compensated for by the other system. In a study on amnesiacs and matched controls, [Bayley and Squire (2002)](#ch10.xhtml_ref10_8){#ch10.xhtml_R_ref10_8 .biblioref role="doc-biblioref"} showed that when declarative-like information is involved in the learning task, but declarative memory is compromised, procedural memory can do the learning -- although it is slower and outside of awareness. Similar compensatory behavior by declarative memory in the face of procedural deficits has been found ([Eichenbaum, 2012](#ch10.xhtml_ref10_16){.biblioref role="doc-biblioref"}).

Declarative and procedural memory are also competitive and when either system can carry out the same task it is often the strength of representations or speed of processing that determine which system is engaged. Interestingly, when a particular system does become engaged for a certain learning task there appears to be concomitant impairment of the other system with regards to performance on that task ([Poldrack & Packard, 2003](#ch10.xhtml_ref10_47){.biblioref role="doc-biblioref"}).

Finally, the two memory systems are cooperative. For example, impaired declarative memory may compromise the ability to learn the cues relevant for implicit pattern learning in procedural memory (see [Knowlton et al., 1994](#ch10.xhtml_ref10_34){#ch10.xhtml_R_ref10_34 .biblioref role="doc-biblioref"} on the Weather Prediction Task, a procedural memory task). When considering the interplay between reliance on each of these memory systems, it is important to remember that shifts in reliance from one memory system to another do not constitute transfer of knowledge from one system to another. Declarative knowledge cannot *become* procedural knowledge or vice versa ([Morgan-Short & Ullman, 2011](#ch10.xhtml_ref10_36){.biblioref role="doc-biblioref"}; [Paradis, 1994](#ch10.xhtml_ref10_44){#ch10.xhtml_R_ref10_44 .biblioref role="doc-biblioref"}; [Paradis, 2009](#ch10.xhtml_ref10_45){#ch10.xhtml_R_ref10_45 .biblioref role="doc-biblioref"}).
:::
::::::
:::::::

:::: {.section aria-labelledby="sec10_2"}
## []{#ch10.xhtml_p137 .pagebreak aria-label=" page 137. " role="doc-pagebreak"}Historical Perspectives {#ch10.xhtml_sec10_2}

::: {.section aria-labelledby="sec10_2_1"}
### The Declarative / Procedural Model of Language {#ch10.xhtml_sec10_2_1}

The DP model is a dual-mechanism domain general model, that is, it proposes distinct cognitive and neural mechanisms for the separate linguistic components of lexicon and grammar but does not assume that the mechanisms subserving these components are dedicated exclusively to that type of processing, i.e., linguistic processing. As a model of language, it is informed by independent knowledge about the functioning of the declarative and procedural memory systems, which has already been presented in this chapter, and it makes specific predictions regarding the neurobiological correlates of language processing within these memory systems. For clarity and brevity, I do not discuss the specificities of the proposed neurobiological correlates of the model and instead refer readers to [Ullman (2020)](#ch10.xhtml_ref10_69){.biblioref role="doc-biblioref"} for such details.

The DP model posits that in a native language (L1) and late-learned L2, the declarative memory system is involved in the learning and processing of lexical/semantic information, such as the stored knowledge about words, their sounds, their meanings, and other semantic information, as well as some grammatical information such as irregular morphosyntactic forms (e.g., *catch-caught*) and "chunked" rule-governed grammatical forms (e.g., *the cat*; see e.g., Ullman, [2016](#ch10.xhtml_ref10_68){.biblioref role="doc-biblioref"}, [2020](#ch10.xhtml_ref10_69){.biblioref role="doc-biblioref"}). The procedural memory system is posited to be involved in the implicit learning and automatized use of aspects of grammatical processing of rule-like computations across syntax, non-lexical semantics, morphology, and phonology; it is also predicted to be involved in articulation and speech perception as well as word segmentation, and may be especially important for grammatical phrase-structure building and prediction in language. More complex structures, such as long-distance dependencies, are likely to require procedural memory processing and be particularly unsuitable for declarative memory.

It is with regards to aspects of L2 grammar processing that the model predicts differences between L1 and late L2: L2 grammar is predicted to rely more on declarative memory than L1 grammar, which should rely more on procedural memory. In particular, at lower levels of L2 proficiency (or experience), learners should process lexical/semantic and grammar information in declarative memory. This prediction is motivated by studies which suggest that declarative memory functioning improves through childhood and into early adulthood ([DiGiulio et al., 1994](#ch10.xhtml_ref10_15){#ch10.xhtml_R_ref10_15 .biblioref role="doc-biblioref"}; [Graf, 1990](#ch10.xhtml_ref10_24){#ch10.xhtml_R_ref10_24 .biblioref role="doc-biblioref"}; [Vaidya et al., 2007](#ch10.xhtml_ref10_71){#ch10.xhtml_R_ref10_71 .biblioref role="doc-biblioref"}). Conversely, procedural memory abilities seem to function better in childhood, with little to no improvement (and possible attenuation) up to early adulthood. Therefore, late L2 learners are expected to rely on their "good" declarative memory more than procedural memory for L2 grammar processing, particularly in learning contexts that emphasize explicit learning, such as instructed, explicit L2 classroom contexts.

However, reliance on declarative memory for aspects of L2 grammar processing is predicted to decrease as a function of L2 proficiency/experience or learning context. Specifically, with higher levels of L2 proficiency/experience, or in more naturalistic learning contexts such as immersion, L2 learners may come to rely less on declarative memory and more on procedural memory for aspects of grammar processing. This fits well with studies showing that increased practice or experience can facilitate processing in procedural memory ([Schacter & Tulving, 1994](#ch10.xhtml_ref10_50){#ch10.xhtml_R_ref10_50 .biblioref role="doc-biblioref"}; [Squire et al., 1993](#ch10.xhtml_ref10_57){#ch10.xhtml_R_ref10_57 .biblioref role="doc-biblioref"}).

In sum, the DP model posits that lexical/semantic processing in L1 and L2 (regardless of experience/proficiency) is supported by the declarative memory system. Grammar processing in L1 and higher proficiency and/or immersion-learned L2 should be supported by the procedural memory system whereas grammar processing at lower proficiency and or explicit, classroom-learned L2 is predicted to more likely be supported by declarative memory, with a possible shift to reliance on procedural memory with increasing L2 proficiency/experience.
:::
::::

:::: {.section aria-labelledby="sec10_3"}
## []{#ch10.xhtml_p138 .pagebreak aria-label=" page 138. " role="doc-pagebreak"}Critical Issues and Topics {#ch10.xhtml_sec10_3}

::: {.section aria-labelledby="sec10_3_1"}
### Input Processing and the Declarative / Procedural Model {#ch10.xhtml_sec10_3_1}

IP refers to the ways in which language learners attend to and process linguistic input. Via IP, learners can create intake, which has been described as the subset of input that provides data to the learner's developing linguistic system ([VanPatten, 1996](#ch10.xhtml_ref10_72){#ch10.xhtml_R_ref10_72 .biblioref role="doc-biblioref"}). As noted in the introduction, the DP model and IP are quite distinct from one another in their scopes and the areas of language and language processing that they address: the DP model discusses the neurobiology of language and language learning in general, with specific neurobiological predictions for both native and non-native language, whereas IP focuses on behavioral, (psycho)linguistic aspects of late-learned second language processing during comprehension. Although they are different in these respects, there is some commonality in aspects of the two theoretical perspectives. In this section, I will consider some of the IP claims, principles, and perspectives and how they might be reflected within the DP framework.

IP claims that comprehension for L2 learners is initially effortful and that L2 learners cannot process and store the same amount of information as L1 speakers during real-time comprehension ([VanPatten, 2020](#ch10.xhtml_ref10_73){.biblioref role="doc-biblioref"}, p. 107) and several of the IP principles center lexical/semantic processing as a driving factor in early L2 comprehension as learners seek to extract meaning from lexical items -- *The primacy of content words principle*, *the lexical preference principle*, and the *(revised) lexical preference principle*. (See also White & Wong, this volume.) This early focus on lexical/semantic processing impacts aspects of grammar processing. For example, IP predicts that learners focus on lexical/semantic processing to the detriment of grammatical markers, which are more likely to be processed as proficiency increases.

These aspects of IP resonate well with [Ullman (2020)](#ch10.xhtml_ref10_69){.biblioref role="doc-biblioref"}'s suggestion that lexical learning in declarative memory may constrain whether or not L2 grammar operates, as in L1, within procedural memory. In particular, Ullman discusses the possibility that, early in L2 learning, lexical access (a declarative memory process) is too slow to support new grammatical learning in procedural memory and in fact slow lexical access may impede procedural learning due to the slowdown of prediction processes (for a related idea, see the lexical bottleneck hypothesis (Hopp, [2014](#ch10.xhtml_ref10_32){#ch10.xhtml_R_ref10_32 .biblioref role="doc-biblioref"}, [2016](#ch10.xhtml_ref10_33){#ch10.xhtml_R_ref10_33 .biblioref role="doc-biblioref"})). IP and the DP model thus converge in describing early L2 as a slow, effortful process that is not carried out in an L1-like way due to processing limitations. Within the DP model this has consequences for whether or not grammatical learning can take place in procedural memory. Within IP this has consequences for the kinds of input learners will attend to/process (e.g., lexical/semantic vs. grammatical), which subsequently contributes to L2 acquisition mechanisms and processes (these are outside the scope of IP).

The foundations of IP theory and research have focused on sentence-level processing, but another area of theoretical alignment between IP and the DP pertains to the more recently developed multilevel input processing perspective (multilevel-IP, see e.g., Barcroft, [2015](#ch10.xhtml_ref10_6){#ch10.xhtml_R_ref10_6 .biblioref role="doc-biblioref"}, [2021](#ch10.xhtml_ref10_7){#ch10.xhtml_R_ref10_7 .biblioref role="doc-biblioref"}). From a multilevel-IP perspective, during comprehension learners must distribute their limited cognitive resources across all of the available levels of input -- levels described by [Barcroft (2015)](#ch10.xhtml_ref10_6){.biblioref role="doc-biblioref"} include sub-lexical phonetic/phonemic, morphological, syntactic, pragmatic, discourse, and sociolinguistic levels. In other words, during comprehension L2 learners must perform multilevel input processing, potentially via a resource allocator mechanism (Barcroft, [2015](#ch10.xhtml_ref10_6){.biblioref role="doc-biblioref"}, [2021](#ch10.xhtml_ref10_7){.biblioref role="doc-biblioref"}). As an example, Lex-IP is one of the multiple levels of IP and refers to "the manner in which learners process words and lexical phrases as input" ([Barcroft, 2015](#ch10.xhtml_ref10_6){.biblioref role="doc-biblioref"}, p. 14). Research on Lex-IP aims to uncover insights related to information processing following a theoretical flow of information expressed as:

> *[]{#ch10.xhtml_p139 .pagebreak aria-label=" page 139. " role="doc-pagebreak"}lex-[input]{.sc} \> lex-[input processing]{.sc}\> lex-[intake]{.sc} \> [developing lexicosemantic system]{.sc} \> lex-[output processing]{.sc} \> lex-[output]{.sc}*
>
> *[(Flowchart is from [Barcroft, 2015](#ch10.xhtml_ref10_6){.biblioref role="doc-biblioref"}; note that all linguistic levels are considered to have a similar underlying information flow structure)]{.credit role="doc-credit"}*

Aspects of multilevel-IP can be interpreted within declarative and procedural memory learning and processing. As noted by [Barcroft (2021)](#ch10.xhtml_ref10_7){.biblioref role="doc-biblioref"}, the processing of different inputs (e.g., lexical, morphology, syntax) follows the same information flow structure but the specific developing systems will differ among each other based on the types of knowledge that each developing system uses in order to develop linguistic competence. For example, he notes that, following the DP model, lexical competence resides in declarative memory and syntactic competence resides in procedural memory.

The linking of multilevel-IP and the DP perspectives can be further elaborated. As a starting point, recall the generalized division of lexical/semantics as being tied to declarative memory and grammar being tied to procedural memory, and the L2 proficiency-based and L2 learning context-based predictions within the DP model (see Historical Perspectives section). Framing multilevel-IP within these declarative and procedural memory predictions, declarative memory might underlie lex-IP and aspects of sem-IP (semantics) levels as well as aspects of morph-IP (morphology) and syn-IP (syntax), mostly likely for the *[input processing]{.sc}*, *[intake]{.sc}*, and *[developing system]{.sc}* stages across these linguistic levels, at lower levels of L2 proficiency and/or in explicit L2 instruction contexts. As proficiency increases (and/or within immersion-learning contexts), learners should come to rely more on procedural memory for aspects of morph-IP and syn-IP, again for the *[input processing]{.sc}*, *[intake]{.sc}*, and *[developing system]{.sc}* stages. The flowcharts below illustrate the proposed linking between multilevel-IP and DP perspectives, for lexical input processing and syntactic input processing as examples.

> a.  *At lower and higher L2 proficiency: lex-[input]{.sc} \> lex-[input processing]{.sc}*~***DEC\ MEM***~ *\> lex-[intake]{.sc}*~***DEC\ MEM***~ *\> [developing lexicosemantic system]{.sc}*~***DEC\ MEM***~ *\> lex-[output processing]{.sc} \> lex-[output]{.sc}*
> b.  *Lower L2 proficiency/Explicit L2 instruction: syn-[input]{.sc} \> syn-[input processing]{.sc}*~***DEC\ MEM***~ *\> syn-[intake]{.sc}*~***DEC\ MEM***~ *\> [developing syntactic system]{.sc}*~***DEC\ MEM***~ *\> syn-[output processing]{.sc} \> syn-[output]{.sc}*
> c.  *Higher L2 proficiency/L2 immersion context: syn-[input]{.sc} \> syn-[input processing]{.sc}*~***PROC\ MEM***~ *\> syn-[intake]{.sc}*~***PROC\ MEM***~ *\> [developing syntactic system]{.sc}*~***PROC\ MEM***~*\> syn-[output processing]{.sc} \> syn-[output]{.sc}*
>
> *[(Dec Mem refers to declarative memory, Proc Mem refers to procedural memory. Note that (b) and (c) predictions for proficiency and learning context would also apply to some morphological processing, i.e., morph-IP.)]{.credit role="doc-credit"}*

Note also that since the DP model incorporates aspects of phonology and articulation (see the Historical Perspectives section), declarative and procedural memory may also underlie *[input processing]{.sc}*, *[intake]{.sc}*, and *[developing system]{.sc}* stages for some features of phon-IP; and procedural memory may come into play for *[output]{.sc}* and *[output processing]{.sc}* stages in terms of articulation.

In sum, despite their distinct theoretical nuclei, IP and the DP model share areas of convergence that may help to promote a clearer understanding of some of the core characteristics of adult second language learning and processing. Recognizing how these frameworks can align also opens up interesting possibilities for future research that bridges aspects of DP and IP perspectives, which will be discussed at the end of this chapter.
:::
::::

:::::: {.section aria-labelledby="sec10_4"}
## []{#ch10.xhtml_p140 .pagebreak aria-label=" page 140. " role="doc-pagebreak"}Current Research and Research Methods {#ch10.xhtml_sec10_4}

:::: {.section aria-labelledby="sec10_4_1"}
### Linking Declarative and Procedural Memory in L2 Learning and Input Processing: Behavioral Research {#ch10.xhtml_sec10_4_1}

To my knowledge, no study has attempted to directly address or connect IP and DP claims. However, a growing set of behavioral research has examined the roles of declarative and procedural memory for aspects of L2 learning. The behavioral studies collectively provide compelling evidence that declarative and procedural memory play important roles in aspects of L2 learning. Namely, the recruitment of these memory systems during L2 learning is dynamic and sensitive to a number of factors, such as learning stage (e.g., [Morgan-Short et al., 2014](#ch10.xhtml_ref10_38){#ch10.xhtml_R_ref10_38 .biblioref role="doc-biblioref"}), linguistic complexity (e.g., [Antoniou et al., 2016](#ch10.xhtml_ref10_3){#ch10.xhtml_R_ref10_3 .biblioref role="doc-biblioref"}; [Ettlinger et al., 2014](#ch10.xhtml_ref10_18){#ch10.xhtml_R_ref10_18 .biblioref role="doc-biblioref"}; [Tagarelli et al., 2016](#ch10.xhtml_ref10_59){#ch10.xhtml_R_ref10_59 .biblioref role="doc-biblioref"}), and training/exposure type (e.g., [Ruiz et al., 2018](#ch10.xhtml_ref10_49){#ch10.xhtml_R_ref10_49 .biblioref role="doc-biblioref"}). Here, I will review a selection of the behavioral work that has examined declarative and procedural memory in L2 learning, with a focus on the work that may be relevant to the theoretical connections between DP and IP discussed in the previous section (for a full review of the research on declarative and procedural memory in L2 learning, see [Morgan-Short et al., 2022](#ch10.xhtml_ref10_37){#ch10.xhtml_R_ref10_37 .biblioref role="doc-biblioref"}). See [Table 10.1](#ch10.xhtml_table10_1){#ch10.xhtml_R_table10_1} for summary information of the work discussed in this section.

::: {.section .tscroll}

: [[Table ]{.label}[10.1 ]{.ordinal}Summary Information of Selected Studies That Have Examined Declarative and Procedural Memory in L2 Learning](#ch10.xhtml_R_table10_1){.backlink aria-label=" This link returns to its first in-text citation. " role="doc-backlink"} {#table10_1}

<aside aria-label="Notes for Table 10.1" class="tfoot">

Notes: Decl. = declarative; Proc. = procedural; dual-task WPT = Weather Prediction Task ([Foerde et al., 2006](#ch10.xhtml_ref10_21){.biblioref role="doc-biblioref"}); CVMT = Continuous Visual Memory Test ([Trahan & Larrabee, 1988](#ch10.xhtml_ref10_62){#ch10.xhtml_R_ref10_62 .biblioref role="doc-biblioref"}); (A)SRT = (Alternating) Serial Reaction Time ([Nissen & Bullemer, 1987](#ch10.xhtml_ref10_42){#ch10.xhtml_R_ref10_42 .biblioref role="doc-biblioref"}); MLAT-V = Modern Language Aptitude Test part 5 ([Carroll & Sapon, 1959](#ch10.xhtml_ref10_12){#ch10.xhtml_R_ref10_12 .biblioref role="doc-biblioref"}); See [Meara (2005)](#ch10.xhtml_ref10_35){#ch10.xhtml_R_ref10_35 .biblioref role="doc-biblioref"} for LLAMA-B, [Phillips et al. (1999)](#ch10.xhtml_ref10_48){#ch10.xhtml_R_ref10_48 .biblioref role="doc-biblioref"} for Tower of London, and [Woodcock, Mather, and McGrew (2001)](#ch10.xhtml_ref10_74){#ch10.xhtml_R_ref10_74 .biblioref role="doc-biblioref"} for Woodcock-Johnson III Tests of Cognitive Ability.

</aside>
:::

In one of the first studies to explicitly examine the roles of declarative and procedural memory in L2 learning, [Morgan-Short et al. (2014)](#ch10.xhtml_ref10_38){.biblioref role="doc-biblioref"} gathered measures of declarative and procedural memory ability and tested early and late stages of L2 syntactic word order learning during training in an artificial language, Brocanto2 ([Morgan-Short et al., 2010](#ch10.xhtml_ref10_39){#ch10.xhtml_R_ref10_39 .biblioref role="doc-biblioref"}; [Morgan-Short, Finger, et al., 2012](#ch10.xhtml_ref10_40){#ch10.xhtml_R_ref10_40 .biblioref role="doc-biblioref"}; [Morgan-Short, Steinhauer, et al., 2012](#ch10.xhtml_ref10_41){#ch10.xhtml_R_ref10_41 .biblioref role="doc-biblioref"}; adapted from Brocanto, [Friederici et al., 2002](#ch10.xhtml_ref10_22){#ch10.xhtml_R_ref10_22 .biblioref role="doc-biblioref"}). They found that declarative memory ability, but not procedural memory ability, predicted L2 syntactic learning early in learning whereas procedural memory ability, but not declarative memory ability, predicted L2 syntactic learning at the later stage of learning. These findings are consistent with DP model predictions. They can also be at least indirectly interpreted within the proposed multilevel-IP and DP linking that is depicted in flowcharts (b) and (c) in the preceding section. Specifically, the findings may point to syntactic input processing (syn-IP) taking place in declarative memory early in learning and in procedural memory later in learning.

[Hamrick (2015)](#ch10.xhtml_ref10_28){.biblioref role="doc-biblioref"} also tested the relationship between declarative and procedural memory ability and L2 syntactic learning. In the study, participants were tested on their syntactic knowledge of a semi-artificial language (a language that combined native language words with non-native language word order, e.g., English words with German word order) immediately after language exposure and following a 1 to 3 week period of no exposure or practice. The results showed that declarative memory positively related to syntactic knowledge immediately after exposure whereas procedural memory positively related to syntactic knowledge after the no-exposure period. This set of results provides evidence of a shift in reliance on declarative memory mechanisms to procedural memory mechanisms for L2 grammar, which is consistent with DP model predictions and would also be consistent with syn-IP taking place in declarative memory early in learning and in procedural memory later in learning.

Also using an artificial language approach, [Ettlinger et al. (2014)](#ch10.xhtml_ref10_18){.biblioref role="doc-biblioref"} tested learning of simple affixed and complex analogical morphophonological rules in relation to declarative and procedural memory abilities. In this study, learning the simple affixed rule was predicted to involve procedural memory, since learning the rule involved sequence learning; and learning the complex analogical rule was predicted to involve declarative memory, since learning the rule involved recall of words presented during training. Based on their learning performance, participants []{#ch10.xhtml_p141 .pagebreak aria-label=" page 141. Table on this page. " role="doc-pagebreak"}[]{#ch10.xhtml_p142 .pagebreak aria-label=" page 142. Table on this page. " role="doc-pagebreak"}[]{#ch10.xhtml_p143 .pagebreak aria-label=" page 143. Table on this page. " role="doc-pagebreak"}[]{#ch10.xhtml_p144 .pagebreak aria-label=" page 144. " role="doc-pagebreak"}were divided into three groups: (1) *learners*: participants who learned both the simple and complex rules; (2) *simplifiers*: participants who learned the simple rule and applied it, incorrectly, to cases that required the complex rule; and (3) *non-learners*: participants who did not learn the simple or complex rule. Regarding declarative and procedural memory abilities, the results showed that *learners* had high declarative and procedural memory, *simplifiers* had high procedural memory and low declarative memory, and *non-learners* had low declarative and procedural memory. As predicted, then, declarative memory played a role for learning the complex rule (as seen in the *learners* group) and procedural memory played a role for learning the simple rule (as seen in the *simplifiers* group). In relation to multilevel-IP, these results might suggest that learning the complex analogical rule was mainly at the lex-IP level and involved declarative memory. In contrast, learning the simple rule might be interpreted as a taking place at a morph-IP level and involving procedural memory. Note that in [Ettlinger et al. (2014)](#ch10.xhtml_ref10_18){.biblioref role="doc-biblioref"} procedural memory also played a nuanced role in learning the complex rule, with high procedural memory being related both to poor learning of the rule (as seen in the *simplifers* group) and successful learning (as seen in the *learners* group).

[Tagarelli et al. (2016)](#ch10.xhtml_ref10_59){.biblioref role="doc-biblioref"} also examined the learning of simple and complex rules, and under different training conditions. Using a semi-artificial language, they tested learning of a simple syntactic word order rule and two complex syntactic word order rules under an incidental, meaning-focused condition and an instructed condition that provided each syntactic rule. They additionally measured procedural memory (they did not measure declarative memory). In the results, procedural memory ability was negatively related to learning in the incidental condition, which appeared to be driven by performance on one of the complex syntactic rules. This result, as in [Ettlinger et al. (2014)](#ch10.xhtml_ref10_18){.biblioref role="doc-biblioref"}, highlights a nuanced role for procedural memory in learning complex syntactic information. These two sets of findings may relate to specific (yet unknown) functions of procedural memory at different stages during syn-IP (e.g., *[input processing]{.sc}*, *[intake]{.sc}*, and *[developing system]{.sc}*), though only research that directly tests the role of procedural memory within a syn-IP framework can confirm this possibility.

In contrast to focusing on procedural memory, [Ruiz et al. (2018)](#ch10.xhtml_ref10_49){.biblioref role="doc-biblioref"} targeted declarative memory in L2 learning of words and syntax in a semi-artificial language. [Ruiz et al. (2018)](#ch10.xhtml_ref10_49){.biblioref role="doc-biblioref"} trained participants under an incidental, meaning-focused condition with instructions to try to learn the meaning of the words or an intentional condition that instructed them to try to learn the meaning of the words as well as the syntax rule. The results for declarative memory indicated that, across both conditions, memory ability was positively related to grammar learning, but only for ungrammatical items. There was no relationship between declarative memory and grammatical items or word learning. The lack of effects for word learning run contrary to DP model predictions and the proposed multilevel-IP and DP linking for lex-IP (flowchart (a)), though the authors suggest that their word learning results may not have had sufficient variability to capture a relationship between word learning and declarative memory.
::::

::: {.section aria-labelledby="sec10_4_2"}
### Neurocognitive Research {#ch10.xhtml_sec10_4_2}

Related to research in the previous section, [Faretta-Stutenberg and Morgan-Short (2018)](#ch10.xhtml_ref10_19){.biblioref role="doc-biblioref"} examined declarative and procedural memory and L2 syntactic learning, but in the study, they gathered electrophysiological data as well as behavioral language measures. Specifically, they used the ERP technique. ERPs reflect naturally occurring, real-time brain activity elicited in response to an external event, such as a word in a sentence, and the excellent temporal resolution of ERPs enables the examination of aspects of language processing with high precision.

[]{#ch10.xhtml_p145 .pagebreak aria-label=" page 145. " role="doc-pagebreak"}[Faretta-Stutenberg and Morgan-Short (2018)](#ch10.xhtml_ref10_19){.biblioref role="doc-biblioref"} tested L2 syntactic performance and processing in a group of at-home classroom L2 Spanish learners and a group of L2 Spanish learners who had studied abroad in a Spanish-speaking country. Participants were tested at two points: (1) a baseline session at the beginning of the semester (at-home group) or approximately one week before their study abroad experience (study abroad group); and (2) a follow-up session at the end of the semester (at-home group) or approximately two weeks after their return from abroad (study abroad group). In the study, declarative memory was found to correlate positively with syntactic judgment performance at the baseline session for both groups. This supports the DP model's prediction that declarative memory should support L2 grammar learning at early stages/lower proficiency and it would also support my proposal linking IP and DP perspectives wherein syn-IP involves declarative memory at earlier learning stages/lower proficiency. Procedural memory was related both to gains in syntactic judgment performance and increased magnitude of learners' ERP responses, but only in the study abroad group. This finding is consistent with the DP model's prediction that aspects of L2 grammar should recruit procedural memory at later stages of learning (or higher proficiency/experience), or in more naturalistic learning contexts, such as an immersion, study abroad experience. The results also connect well to the idea of syn-IP involving procedural memory at higher proficiency or in L2 immersion contexts.

There is also a recent meta-analysis that sheds light on the neurobiological underpinnings of L2 learning and declarative and procedural memory. [Tagarelli et al. (2019)](#ch10.xhtml_ref10_60){#ch10.xhtml_R_ref10_60 .biblioref role="doc-biblioref"} conducted a meta-analysis of neuroimaging studies on lexical and grammar learning of "model languages", which included artificial grammars, artificial languages, natural miniature languages, and words, and tested the predictions of the DP model. The meta-analysis showed that lexical learning was related to activation in ventral temporal lobe (ventral stream) areas that are linked to declarative memory. Grammar learning was related to activation in basal ganglia structures (anterior caudate nucleus and putamen), consistent with a role for procedural memory. Also consistent with the DP model, learning conditions that are predicted to engage declarative memory learning (explicit, classroom-like contexts) showed hippocampal activation whereas learning conditions that are predicted to engage procedural learning (naturalistic, immersion-like contexts) showed anterior caudate/putamen activation. These findings also seem relevant for the proposed IP perspective, particularly in relation to Lex-IP and declarative memory (flowchart (a)) as well as syn-IP and morph-IP and the proposed shift from declarative to procedural memory during IP with increasing L2 proficiency and/or under different learning contexts (flowcharts (b) and (c)).

Overall, the behavioral and neurocognitive evidence generally supports the claims of the DP model and can be feasibly interpreted within a perspective that links IP and DP perspectives. However, more research is needed in order to achieve a fuller understanding of the roles of the two memory systems and gain additional insights into not only the explanatory power of the DP model but also how IP and DP model perspectives may align to promote a better understanding of L2 learning and processing.
:::
::::::

::: {.section aria-labelledby="sec10_5"}
## Future Directions {#ch10.xhtml_sec10_5}

This chapter has provided a detailed overview of the DP model, its predictions for L2, and research on the roles of declarative and procedural memory in L2 learning, with a focus on how aspects of the DP model and L2 research relating to the model resonate with IP perspectives. Though current research findings are compelling, future research will be critical for advancing our understanding of how the two memory systems relate to aspects of L2 learning and IP.

[]{#ch10.xhtml_p146 .pagebreak aria-label=" page 146. " role="doc-pagebreak"}First, it should be highlighted that nearly all of the research reviewed in this chapter used artificial or semi-artificial language paradigms (an exception is [Faretta-Stutenberg & Morgan-Short, 2018](#ch10.xhtml_ref10_19){.biblioref role="doc-biblioref"}). Artificial linguistic systems are highly useful in language learning research because they can be learned to high levels of proficiency in a relatively short period of time (hours-to-days) and they enable precise control over a number of language learning-related variables that are often uncontrolled in natural language contexts, such as amount and type of training and prior exposure or experience with the language (for detailed discussions of artificial language research methods, see e.g., Grey, [2020](#ch10.xhtml_ref10_25){#ch10.xhtml_R_ref10_25 .biblioref role="doc-biblioref"}, [2023](#ch10.xhtml_ref10_26){#ch10.xhtml_R_ref10_26 .biblioref role="doc-biblioref"}; [Grey & Tagarelli, 2018](#ch10.xhtml_ref10_27){#ch10.xhtml_R_ref10_27 .biblioref role="doc-biblioref"}). Nonetheless, the findings from these artificial and semi-artificial language studies should be extended to research that examines the contributions of declarative and procedural memory -- and their potential functions within an IP framework -- with respect to natural language learning.

Additionally, future research will benefit from expanding the linguistic structures that are examined in questions of L2 learning and declarative and procedural memory ability. All but one of the empirical studies tested only aspects of morphosyntax/morphophonology ([Ruiz et al., 2018](#ch10.xhtml_ref10_49){.biblioref role="doc-biblioref"}), and therefore the collective findings provide a rather limited view. Additional work on lexical learning is warranted as is research on lexical/semantic structures and phonology, for example. This work would be valuable not only for understanding the DP model in isolation, but also for continuing to uncover the potential connections between the IP and DP frameworks.

Relatedly, although IP and the DP model have different priorities in terms of the specific areas of language and language processing/learning they seek to explain, the two theoretical approaches may intersect in future research on lexical learning and processing. In terms of lex-IP and L2 vocabulary learning, Barcroft ([2012](#ch10.xhtml_ref10_5){#ch10.xhtml_R_ref10_5 .biblioref role="doc-biblioref"}, [2015](#ch10.xhtml_ref10_6){.biblioref role="doc-biblioref"}) discusses that promoting both incidental and intentional learning are important in L2 instruction, and in much of the DP individual differences L2 work, input has been presented within language training/exposure conditions along an incidental--intentional continuum. Future studies may be able to shed light on how declarative memory ability influences lex-IP (and L2 vocabulary learning) in incidental, intentional, and mixed instructional conditions, for example.

Indeed, it will be interesting to directly test the roles of declarative and procedural memory in various multilevel-IP areas, for instance by studying the roles of the two memory systems not only for lex-IP, but other levels such as sem-IP, morph-IP and syn-IP. This research area can be further expanded by examining the influence of the two memory systems during the trajectory of L2 learning from lower to higher L2 proficiency and/or in explicit classroom contexts compared to more naturalistic, immersion-learning contexts. As highlighted by [Barcroft (2021)](#ch10.xhtml_ref10_7){.biblioref role="doc-biblioref"} for multilevel-IP, between-level research that focuses on the intersection between two or more levels is needed, and this call for future research can be extended to examining the roles of declarative and procedural memory at these intersections. Finally, although I suggest in this chapter that declarative and procedural memory underlie the *[input processing]{.sc}*, *[intake]{.sc}*, and *[developing system]{.sc}* stages within multilevel-IP, this is a question that should be examined empirically across different linguistic levels, L2s, proficiency levels, and instructional contexts. There is thus an abundance of potential future work that can bridge DP and IP perspectives and enrich our understanding of adult second language learning and processing.
:::

::: {.section .reference aria-labelledby="r10_1"}
