[]{#ch11.xhtml}

::: {}
# []{#ch11.xhtml_p150 .pagebreak aria-label=" page 150. " role="doc-pagebreak"}[11 ]{.chnum .ordinal}[The Type of Processing -- Resource Allocation (TOPRA) Model]{.chtitle} {#ch11.xhtml_h1}

Shusaku Kida

Doshisha University, Kyoto, Japan

DOI: [10.4324/9781003041412-14](https://dx.doi.org/10.4324/9781003041412-14){aria-label="D.O.I. link to this document."}
:::

::: {.section .introduction role="doc-introduction"}
## Introduction and Definitions {#ch11.xhtml_sec11_1}

In principle, input processing (IP) can occur at any level of linguistic grain when we are exposed to a language; therefore, all aspects of a given language could be foci, such as discourse, sentence, lexis, and so on. Nonetheless, most studies on IP have focused on sentence-level IP and the acquisition of (morpho)syntactic features (see [VanPatten, 1996](#ch11.xhtml_ref11_41){#ch11.xhtml_R_ref11_41 .biblioref role="doc-biblioref"}, for a review). Considerably less attention has been paid to how IP occurs at the lexical level and how it affects the learning of relevant lexical items. Lexical input processing (lex-IP) refers to "the cognitive processes by which learners allocate processing resources when they are exposed to a new word or lexical phrase as input" ([Barcroft, 2015](#ch11.xhtml_ref11_7){#ch11.xhtml_R_ref11_7 .biblioref role="doc-biblioref"}, p. 165).

Similar to other IP levels, lex-IP concerns how second language (L2) learners process and acquire a given target lexical item by allocating their processing resources to subcomponents of lexical items---that is, word form, meaning, and form--meaning mapping---which, in turn, are processed and available for learners' developing linguistic systems ([Barcroft, 2015](#ch11.xhtml_ref11_7){.biblioref role="doc-biblioref"}). Because their processing resources are limited, they are unable to attend to and process all the subcomponents simultaneously without trade-offs. Therefore, L2 learners must allocate their resources based on the goal(s) of their cognitive activities. Numerous previous studies on L2 vocabulary learning have demonstrated that the ways in which L2 learners process new vocabulary affects their learning outcomes. From the lex-IP perspective, differences in learning outcomes between conditions can be attributed to differences in *processing -- resource allocation* in which learners are engaged when they are exposed to the target lexical items as input. The type of processing -- resource allocation (TOPRA) model (Barcroft, [2002](#ch11.xhtml_ref11_3){#ch11.xhtml_R_ref11_3 .biblioref role="doc-biblioref"}, [2003](#ch11.xhtml_ref11_4){#ch11.xhtml_R_ref11_4 .biblioref role="doc-biblioref"}) considers the role of processing -- resource allocation in the learning of new L2 words while they perform linguistic tasks that induce different types of IP. For example, consider a typical new L2 vocabulary learning situation in which a learner attempts to remember a new L2 word and its L1 translation. When the learner allocates processing resources to the meaning (L1 translation) of the word, IP promotes the later recalling of its meaning (L1 translation) but may simultaneously inhibit the processing and learning of its form (spelling).

This chapter describes the historical background and predictions of the TOPRA model and reviews some empirical studies that support this model's predictions in the contexts of intentional and incidental L2 vocabulary learning. Furthermore, the chapter discusses methodological issues []{#ch11.xhtml_p151 .pagebreak aria-label=" page 151. " role="doc-pagebreak"}associated with testing the TOPRA model, such as the types of pre- and posttest, data collection procedure, and orienting tasks. The chapter concludes with pedagogical implications and future directions for research.
:::

::::: {.section aria-labelledby="sec11_2"}
## Historical Perspectives {#ch11.xhtml_sec11_2}

::: {.section aria-labelledby="sec11_2_1"}
### The Levels-of-Processing Framework and L2 Vocabulary Learning {#ch11.xhtml_sec11_2_1}

Based on an information-processing perspective, L2 vocabulary learning is regarded as a cognitive activity in which L2 learners gradually acquire target lexical information, such as word forms and word meanings. Therefore, L2 vocabulary learning researchers have utilized theoretical frameworks that were originally proposed for human memory research (e.g., [Brown & Perry, 1991](#ch11.xhtml_ref11_9){#ch11.xhtml_R_ref11_9 .biblioref role="doc-biblioref"}; [Ellis & He, 1999](#ch11.xhtml_ref11_14){#ch11.xhtml_R_ref11_14 .biblioref role="doc-biblioref"}; [Fraser, 1999](#ch11.xhtml_ref11_16){#ch11.xhtml_R_ref11_16 .biblioref role="doc-biblioref"}; [Hulstijn, 1992](#ch11.xhtml_ref11_21){#ch11.xhtml_R_ref11_21 .biblioref role="doc-biblioref"}; Joe, [1995](#ch11.xhtml_ref11_26){#ch11.xhtml_R_ref11_26 .biblioref role="doc-biblioref"}, [1998](#ch11.xhtml_ref11_27){#ch11.xhtml_R_ref11_27 .biblioref role="doc-biblioref"}). The levels-of-processing (LOP) framework is one such major framework ([Craik & Lockhart, 1972](#ch11.xhtml_ref11_10){#ch11.xhtml_R_ref11_10 .biblioref role="doc-biblioref"}). Past research on human memory has demonstrated that different types of information processing can lead to different levels of memory performances ([Craik & Lockhart, 1972](#ch11.xhtml_ref11_10){.biblioref role="doc-biblioref"}; [Craik & Tulving, 1975](#ch11.xhtml_ref11_11){#ch11.xhtml_R_ref11_11 .biblioref role="doc-biblioref"}; [Paivio & Desrochers, 1980](#ch11.xhtml_ref11_40){#ch11.xhtml_R_ref11_40 .biblioref role="doc-biblioref"}). [Craik and Lockhart (1972)](#ch11.xhtml_ref11_10){.biblioref role="doc-biblioref"} proposed the LOP framework by stating that "the memory trace is better described in terms of depth of processing or degree of stimulus elaboration. Deeper analysis leads to a more persistent trace" ([Craik & Lockhart, 1972](#ch11.xhtml_ref11_10){.biblioref role="doc-biblioref"}, p. 677). This supposition is supported by how (a) semantic processing leads to better memory and retention of target information related to already known words (L1 word stimuli) than does structural (orthographic or phonological) processing ([Craik & Tulving, 1975](#ch11.xhtml_ref11_11){.biblioref role="doc-biblioref"}; [Hyde & Jenkins, 1969](#ch11.xhtml_ref11_25){#ch11.xhtml_R_ref11_25 .biblioref role="doc-biblioref"}) and how (b) memory performance is better explained by type of information processing than by number of rehearsal activities conducted ([Craik & Watkins, 1973](#ch11.xhtml_ref11_12){#ch11.xhtml_R_ref11_12 .biblioref role="doc-biblioref"}).

When it comes to memory for words that are not already known, however, previous studies had indicated somewhat mixed effects from semantic processing. Regarding the effect of imagery-based mnemonic learning (e.g., the keyword method), for example, studies have demonstrated its positive effect on the learning of L1 words (e.g., [Bower & Reitman, 1972](#ch11.xhtml_ref11_8){#ch11.xhtml_R_ref11_8 .biblioref role="doc-biblioref"}; [Johnson-Laird et al., 1978](#ch11.xhtml_ref11_28){#ch11.xhtml_R_ref11_28 .biblioref role="doc-biblioref"}; [McClelland et al., 1981](#ch11.xhtml_ref11_37){#ch11.xhtml_R_ref11_37 .biblioref role="doc-biblioref"}) and new L2 words (e.g., [Brown & Perry, 1991](#ch11.xhtml_ref11_9){.biblioref role="doc-biblioref"}; [Wang et al., 1992](#ch11.xhtml_ref11_42){#ch11.xhtml_R_ref11_42 .biblioref role="doc-biblioref"}) when compared to non-mnemonic learning. However, apart from the research on mnemonic learning, studies have not always found positive effect of semantic processing on the learning of new L2 words ([Barcroft, 2004](#ch11.xhtml_ref11_5){#ch11.xhtml_R_ref11_5 .biblioref role="doc-biblioref"}; [Wong & Pyun, 2012](#ch11.xhtml_ref11_43){#ch11.xhtml_R_ref11_43 .biblioref role="doc-biblioref"}). Therefore, the improved memory predicted by LOP was not emerging with regard to L2 word learning, and new theoretical frameworks became increasingly needed.
:::

::: {.section aria-labelledby="sec11_2_2"}
### The Type of Processing -- Resource Allocation Model {#ch11.xhtml_sec11_2_2}

The TOPRA model was proposed for the purpose of explaining the effects of different types of processing on the learning of new L2 vocabulary. The model's three assumptions are as follows: (a) one type of information processing (e.g., processing types A, B, C, and so on) corresponds to its learning/memorization (e.g., learning of A, B, C, and so on), and (b) our processing resources are limited, so utilizing one type of processing (e.g., processing type A) could deplete the remaining resources that would otherwise be used for other types of processing (e.g., processing type B, C, and so on) when overall processing demands are sufficiently high. Therefore, (c) one type of processing (e.g., processing type A) could potentially negatively affect the learning of other types of information (e.g., learning of B, C, and so on). This assumption is fundamentally different from the LOP because the LOP assumes that deeper (semantic) processing is potentially superior to shallower (structural) processing whereas the TOPRA model assumes that the []{#ch11.xhtml_p152 .pagebreak aria-label=" page 152. Figure on this page. " role="doc-pagebreak"}[]{#ch11.xhtml_p153 .pagebreak aria-label=" page 153. " role="doc-pagebreak"}superiority of one type of processing depends on type (semantic, structural, mapping) of processing and type of target information.[^21]

As shown in B in [Figure 11.1](#ch11.xhtml_fig11_1){#ch11.xhtml_R_fig11_1}, the TOPRA model assumes that these three fundamental components (L2 word form, meaning, and form--meaning mapping) are dissociable from one another. From a lex-IP perspective, this means that these components can be processed separately. For example, when L2 learners allocate their processing resources to the semantic aspect of a new L2 word by performing a semantic processing task, it becomes difficult for them to process other aspects of the word (e.g., form) if overall processing demands are sufficiently high.

![[[Figure]{.label} [11.1 ]{.ordinal}The type of processing -- resource allocation (TOPRA) model](#ch11.xhtml_R_fig11_1){.backlink aria-label=" This link returns to its first in-text citation. " role="doc-backlink"} [([Barcroft, 2002](#ch11.xhtml_ref11_3){.biblioref role="doc-biblioref"}).]{.source .credit role="doc-credit"}](images/fig11_1_B.jpg){#fig11_1 alt="The TOPRA model detailing types of processing and learning, components of lexical learning such as semantic, formal, and mapping, and the semantic and formal components of lexical learning."}
:::
:::::

:::::::: {.section aria-labelledby="sec11_3"}
## Critical Issues and Topics {#ch11.xhtml_sec11_3}

:::: {.section aria-labelledby="sec11_3_1"}
### L2 Vocabulary Learning Components and Their Dissociability {#ch11.xhtml_sec11_3_1}

Let us consider L2 vocabulary learning components in more detail. The TOPRA model assumes that word form, word meaning, and form--meaning mapping are the three fundamental components in L2 vocabulary learning. Word form refers to spelling (in the case of the written mode of new L2 vocabulary learning) and pronunciation (in the case of the spoken mode of new L2 vocabulary learning). For example, let us consider a context of learning Japanese as a foreign language; a learner wishing to learn the word for *book* will learn the word form *本* (*hon*) in Japanese (*livre* in French, *libro* in Spanish, كِتاب in Arabic, and so on). Learning L2 word meanings, on the other hand, involves conceptual reference and relationships to L1 meanings. Adult L2 learners commonly do not have to acquire L2 word meanings from scratch because they have access to L1-based meanings (e.g., L1 translations or already known equivalent concepts). Therefore, learners often establish one-to-one correspondences between L2 words and their L1 translations. However, such one-to-one meanings are not sufficient because most words are *polysemous*---that is, they tend to have several meanings depending on context of use. Therefore, L2 learners must add other meanings to the known forms.

To illustrate this point further, consider the following two examples. First, most people usually know what planets, moons, stars, comets, or asteroids are. However, people who are not familiar with astronomy may not know the word *nebula*. It refers to an interstellar mass of dust or gas that reflects light from its neighboring stars. A learner who does not know the word form and what a nebula is will have to learn both the word form and its meaning. Thus, in this case, learning the word *nebula* involves learning new word form as well as new meaning. Another type of new-meaning learning involves adding a new meaning to already known words (homograph learning). For example, most university students in a linguistics department should know the word *stop* for its general meaning; however, they soon learn that it has a special meaning in phonetics (plosive) and learn its meaning in this context. In this case, they already know the word form in question (stop) but must learn the new meaning to this word form. These two examples demonstrate that learners can learn new meanings they have not previously acquired.

Finally, *form--meaning mapping* refers to establishing the connection between a word's form and its meaning. Many people tend to intuitively believe that L2 vocabulary learning involves learning new word *meanings*. However, as described above, this is not usually the case. Learners typically must learn word *forms* for concepts that they commonly already know. They must also map L2 word forms onto their appropriate *meanings*. This process, known as *form--meaning mapping*, is regarded as the central and most important phase in L2 vocabulary learning ([Henriksen, 1999](#ch11.xhtml_ref11_20){#ch11.xhtml_R_ref11_20 .biblioref role="doc-biblioref"}), and the TOPRA model hightlights this component in L2 vocabulary learning.

[]{#ch11.xhtml_p154 .pagebreak aria-label=" page 154. " role="doc-pagebreak"}The combination of the acquisition level of the three components (from completely unknown to completely known) reflects the complex nature of L2 vocabulary learning. Although L2 vocabulary acquisition is not a dichotomous phenomenon, for the sake of simplicility, we categorize words as *novel* and *known* in [Table 11.1](#ch11.xhtml_table11_1){#ch11.xhtml_R_table11_1} which results in the eight possible L2 vocabulary learning situations. Among these eight situations, the four (2, 5, 6, 8) where form--meaning mapping is *known* are difficult to imagine. Therefore, most L2 vocabulary learning is assumed to happen in the remaining four situations (1, 3, 4, 7), some of which are reviewed in the following section.

::: {.section .tscroll}
+--------------+--------+-----------+-----------+-------------------------------------------------------------------------------+---------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------+
| *Situations* | *Form* | *Meaning* | *Mapping* | *Definition*                                                                  | *Example*                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                               |

| \(1\)        | novel  | novel     | novel     | Learn a novel L2 word form and meaning and create a new mapping between them. | A native English speaker who does not know the Japanese word "sugitama" learns it ("sugitama" refers to a traditional round symbol for a sake \[the traditional Japanese alcohol made with rice\] brewery that is made of cedar leaves and sticks); this shows the maturation process of the sake through its gradual color change from green to brown. |
+--------------+--------+-----------+-----------+-------------------------------------------------------------------------------+---------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------+
| \(2\)        | novel  | novel     | known     |                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                         |
+--------------+--------+-----------+-----------+-------------------------------------------------------------------------------+---------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------+
| \(3\)        | novel  | known     | novel     | Learn a novel L2 word and map it onto a known meaning.                        | A native English speaker learns the Japanese word "benkyo-suru," which means *to study* in English.                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                     |
+--------------+--------+-----------+-----------+-------------------------------------------------------------------------------+---------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------+
| \(4\)        | known  | novel     | novel     | Learn a novel meaning and map a known form onto the novel meaning.            | A native English speaker who already knows that a Japanese word "nou (能)" means *ability* learns its other meaning (a Japanese traditional art played on a stage).                                                                                                                                                                                     |
+--------------+--------+-----------+-----------+-------------------------------------------------------------------------------+---------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------+
| \(5\)        | novel  | known     | known     |                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                         |
+--------------+--------+-----------+-----------+-----------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------+
| \(6\)        | known  | novel     | known     |                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                         |
+--------------+--------+-----------+-----------+-------------------------------------------------------------------------------+---------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------+
| \(7\)        | known  | known     | novel     | Learn a novel mapping of a known form onto a known meaning.                   | A native English speaker who already knows the word "benkyo-suru" and its first meaning (*to study*) learns its secondary meaning (*to give a discount*).                                                                                                                                                                                               |
+--------------+--------+-----------+-----------+-------------------------------------------------------------------------------+---------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------+
| \(8\)        | known  | known     | known     |                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                         |
+--------------+--------+-----------+-----------+-----------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------+

: [[Table ]{.label}[11.1 ]{.ordinal}A Conceptual Framework for Possible L2 Vocabulary Learning Situations](#ch11.xhtml_R_table11_1){.backlink aria-label=" This link returns to its first in-text citation. " role="doc-backlink"} {#table11_1}
:::
::::

::: {.section aria-labelledby="sec11_3_2"}
### Orienting Tasks {#ch11.xhtml_sec11_3_2}

Selection of orienting tasks is one critical issue related to research on the TOPRA model. Because the model offers alternative predictions to those of the LOP framework, when testing its predictions, one must identify orienting tasks that direct learners' lexical IP in different ways. Thus far, two types of orienting tasks have been examined: (1) Semantic tasks require learners to attend to the semantic properties of new L2 words whereas (2) structural tasks require them to attend to structural properties. [Table 11.2](#ch11.xhtml_table11_2){#ch11.xhtml_R_table11_2} summarizes the tasks utilized by existing studies.

+-----------------------+----------------------------------+------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------+
| *Orientation*         | *Task*                           | *Reference*                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                |

| Semantic              | - Pleasantness ratings           | [Barcroft (2002)](#ch11.xhtml_ref11_3){.biblioref role="doc-biblioref"}; Kida ([2010a](#ch11.xhtml_ref11_30){#ch11.xhtml_R_ref11_30 .biblioref role="doc-biblioref"}, [2010b](#ch11.xhtml_ref11_31){#ch11.xhtml_R_ref11_31 .biblioref role="doc-biblioref"}, [2020](#ch11.xhtml_ref11_32){#ch11.xhtml_R_ref11_32 .biblioref role="doc-biblioref"}); [Kida & Barcroft (2018)](#ch11.xhtml_ref11_33){#ch11.xhtml_R_ref11_33 .biblioref role="doc-biblioref"} |
+-----------------------+----------------------------------+------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------+
|                       | - Asking questions about meaning | [Barcroft (2003)](#ch11.xhtml_ref11_4){.biblioref role="doc-biblioref"}                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                    |
+-----------------------+----------------------------------+------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------+
|                       | - Sentence writing               | [Barcroft (2004)](#ch11.xhtml_ref11_5){.biblioref role="doc-biblioref"}; [Wong & Pyun (2012)](#ch11.xhtml_ref11_43){.biblioref role="doc-biblioref"}                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                       |
+-----------------------+----------------------------------+------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------+
|                       | - Synonym generation             | [Barcroft (2009)](#ch11.xhtml_ref11_6){#ch11.xhtml_R_ref11_6 .biblioref role="doc-biblioref"}                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                              |
+-----------------------+----------------------------------+------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------+
|                       | - Synonym selection              | [Kida, Barcroft, & Sommers (2022)](#ch11.xhtml_ref11_34){#ch11.xhtml_R_ref11_34 .biblioref role="doc-biblioref"}                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                           |
+-----------------------+----------------------------------+------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------+
| Structural            | - Letter counting                | [Barcroft (2002)](#ch11.xhtml_ref11_3){.biblioref role="doc-biblioref"}, [Kida & Barcroft (2018)](#ch11.xhtml_ref11_33){.biblioref role="doc-biblioref"}                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                   |
+-----------------------+----------------------------------+------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------+
|                       | - Phonological encoding          | Kida ([2010a](#ch11.xhtml_ref11_30){.biblioref role="doc-biblioref"}, [2010b](#ch11.xhtml_ref11_31){.biblioref role="doc-biblioref"}, [2020](#ch11.xhtml_ref11_32){.biblioref role="doc-biblioref"})                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                       |
+-----------------------+----------------------------------+------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------+
|                       | - Neighborhood selection         | [Kida et al. (2022)](#ch11.xhtml_ref11_34){.biblioref role="doc-biblioref"}                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                |
+-----------------------+----------------------------------+------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------+

: [[Table ]{.label}[11.2 ]{.ordinal}The Types of Semantic and Structural Processing Tasks Used in Previous Studies](#ch11.xhtml_R_table11_2){.backlink aria-label=" This link returns to its first in-text citation. " role="doc-backlink"} {#table11_2}

Based on the TOPRA model predictions, semantic processing tasks (e.g., pleasantness rating, asking questions, sentence writing, synonym generation/selection) are assumed to inhibit the learning of new L2 word forms whereas structural processing (e.g., letter counting, phonological encoding, neighborhood selection) might promote it, at least under certain conditions and with the trade-off being potentially reduced processing and learning of the meaning and mapping components of word learning.

One may argue that the assumption of the dissociability of word form and meaning and the orienting tasks to examine this dissociability is somewhat artificial or not pedagogically applicable and does not reflect the process of actual L2 vocabulary learning. However, as shown in [Table 11.2](#ch11.xhtml_table11_2), the orienting tasks used in the TOPRA model studies are not limited to psychological tasks (e.g., pleasantness rating, letter counting) but include others that could and have been considered pedagogical in nature, such as sentence writing and synonym or neighborhood selection. Therefore, the assumption of the model and its vocabulary learning outcomes should be applicable not only to laboratory-based research but also to L2 classrooms.
:::

::: {.section aria-labelledby="sec11_3_3"}
### Types of Posttests {#ch11.xhtml_sec11_3_3}

Thus far, studies testing the TOPRA model have focused largely on two types of vocabulary posttests: free recall and cued recall. In *free recall*, the participants are asked to recall L2 word forms (L2 free recall) or their meanings in L1 (L1 free recall) in any order. It is considered free recall because no cue is provided to prompt participants to recall the target information. On the other hand, *cued recall* provides a prompt, such as a picture, that enables the participant to activate and recall target information. In *L1 (or picture)-to-L2 cued recall*, L1 words or pictures are shown to the participants, and they are asked to produce the corresponding L2 words, whereas in *L2-to-L1 cued recall*, L2 words are shown to the participants, and they are asked to produce the corresponding L1 words.

Vocabulary learning researchers often prefer cued recall because it measures form--meaning mapping (one needs to map a cued semantic representation to the representation of its corresponding word form), which is considered the most central phase of L2 vocabulary learning because learners need to know both word form and meaning during real communication. In a context involving the TOPRA model, however, it is not always appropriate because cued recall []{#ch11.xhtml_p155 .pagebreak aria-label=" page 155. Table on this page. " role="doc-pagebreak"}[]{#ch11.xhtml_p156 .pagebreak aria-label=" page 156. " role="doc-pagebreak"}measures both word-form knowledge and form--meaning mapping knowledge. If word form and meaning are dissociable to a certain degree, free recall can provide a relatively pure measurement of word form and meaning learning. [Table 11.3](#ch11.xhtml_table11_3){#ch11.xhtml_R_table11_3} from [Kida and Barcroft (2018, p. 483)](#ch11.xhtml_ref11_33){.biblioref role="doc-biblioref"} summarizes these relationships between recall type and what they reflect in terms of learning and cognition.

+------------------------------------------------------------------------------------+----------------------------------------------------------------------------------+
| *Recall Type*                                                                      | *It depends largely on ...*                                                      |
+====================================================================================+==================================================================================+
| For a known L1 word ...                                                            |                                                                                  |
+------------------------------------------------------------------------------------+----------------------------------------------------------------------------------+
| 1.  L1 free recall                                                                 | semantic activation within L1 lexicosemantic space.                              |
+------------------------------------------------------------------------------------+----------------------------------------------------------------------------------+
| For a novel L2 word form with a known L1 meaning ...                               |                                                                                  |
+------------------------------------------------------------------------------------+----------------------------------------------------------------------------------+
| 1.  L2 free recall                                                                 | learning L2 word form.                                                           |
+------------------------------------------------------------------------------------+----------------------------------------------------------------------------------+
| 1.  L1 (or picture)-to-L2 cued recall                                              | learning L2 word form and form--meaning mapping.                                 |
+------------------------------------------------------------------------------------+----------------------------------------------------------------------------------+
| 1.  L2-to-L1 cued recall                                                           | learning L2 word form (but to a lesser degree than 3) and form--meaning mapping. |
+------------------------------------------------------------------------------------+----------------------------------------------------------------------------------+
| For a novel L2 homograph with a known L2 word from and a known L1 meaning[^22] ... |                                                                                  |
+------------------------------------------------------------------------------------+----------------------------------------------------------------------------------+
| 1.  L1-to-L2 cued recall                                                           | mapping known L2 words to known meaning.                                         |
+------------------------------------------------------------------------------------+----------------------------------------------------------------------------------+
| 1.  L2-to-L1 cued recall                                                           | mapping within L2 words to known meaning.                                        |
+------------------------------------------------------------------------------------+----------------------------------------------------------------------------------+

: [[Table ]{.label}[11.3 ]{.ordinal}Recall Types and the Type of Memory/Learning on Which Each Depends](#ch11.xhtml_R_table11_3){.backlink aria-label=" This link returns to its first in-text citation. " role="doc-backlink"} {#table11_3}

<aside aria-label="Notes for Table 11.3" class="tfoot">

([Kida & Barcroft, 2018](#ch11.xhtml_ref11_33){.biblioref role="doc-biblioref"}, p. 483)

1.  [[\*](#ch11.xhtml_R_table-fn11_3_1){.backlink aria-label=" This link returns to its first in-text citation. " role="doc-backlink"} Alternate meaning mapping]{#ch11.xhtml_table-fn11_3_1}

    Note: This scheme does not negate the need to learn L2-specific meaning and usage (for 2--4) nor the possibility of L1 and L2 mutual activation/shared lexicosemantic space.

</aside>
:::

::: {.section aria-labelledby="sec11_3_4"}
### Potential Effects of Increased Semantic Processing During Incidental Vocabulary Learning {#ch11.xhtml_sec11_3_4}

As shown previously, the basic prediction of the TOPRA model is that increased semantic processing has potentially inhibitory effects on intentional learning of new L2 word forms, which is often the critical component of new L2 vocabulary learning. Does this extend to incidental vocabulary learning when L2 learners' primary goal is to complete a learning task such as reading comprehension rather than to learn new words? If so, then we should expect to observe cases []{#ch11.xhtml_p157 .pagebreak aria-label=" page 157. " role="doc-pagebreak"}in which increased semantic processing during reading yields negative effects on the learning of new L2 words.

The involvement load hypothesis proposed by [Laufer and Hulstijn (2001)](#ch11.xhtml_ref11_36){#ch11.xhtml_R_ref11_36 .biblioref role="doc-biblioref"} is another theoretical framework for incidental L2 vocabulary learning (see also [Yanagisawa & Webb, 2022](#ch11.xhtml_ref11_45){#ch11.xhtml_R_ref11_45 .biblioref role="doc-biblioref"}, for a modified version); it specifies three motivational and cognitive factors that affect the learning of new L2 vocabulary: need, search, and evaluation. The motivational factor *need* suggests that when L2 learners experience a greater need to process the new words in question, better learning of the words occurs. *Need* has two levels: moderate and strong. Moderate need is induced when learners' external agents (e.g., pedagogical tasks) require them to process the target words, whereas *strong need* is induced when learners' internal agents (e.g., desire to complete tasks) necessitate using the target words. The cognitive factor *search* refers to looking for the appropriate meaning of an unknown L2 word or seeking the form that can express the concept in question. Another cognitive factor, *evaluation*, refers to the selection of an appropriate meaning or word form (from among several possible candidates) to best fit the context. Evaluation also has two levels: moderate and strong. Evaluation is moderate if learners select an appropriate meaning from among several possible candidates when the activity is receptive (e.g., reading and listening) whereas it is strong if they use the target word with the surrounding context in a productive activity (e.g., writing and speaking).

Evaluation is the theoretically interesting factor which may be incompatible with TOPRA. The involvement load hypothesis suggests that paying attention to the context of the target word promotes vocabulary learning whereas the TOPRA model predicts that it has a potentially inhibitory effect. Future research is needed to investigate when and in what conditions these two frameworks do or do not share the prediction of L2 vocabulary learning outcome.[^23]
:::
::::::::

::::: {.section aria-labelledby="sec11_4"}
## Current Contributions and Research {#ch11.xhtml_sec11_4}

Vocabulary learning is commonly divided into two main types: intentional and incidental. Various definitions have been proposed for each of these ([Gass, 1999](#ch11.xhtml_ref11_17){#ch11.xhtml_R_ref11_17 .biblioref role="doc-biblioref"}; [Hatch & Brown, 1995](#ch11.xhtml_ref11_19){#ch11.xhtml_R_ref11_19 .biblioref role="doc-biblioref"}; Hulstijn, [2001](#ch11.xhtml_ref11_22){#ch11.xhtml_R_ref11_22 .biblioref role="doc-biblioref"}, [2003](#ch11.xhtml_ref11_23){#ch11.xhtml_R_ref11_23 .biblioref role="doc-biblioref"}). This chapter will follow definitions provided by Hulstijn ([2001](#ch11.xhtml_ref11_22){.biblioref role="doc-biblioref"}, [2003](#ch11.xhtml_ref11_23){.biblioref role="doc-biblioref"}), who argued that the differentiation between intentional and incidental vocabulary learning is based on whether the participants are informed that they will be tested afterward *before* the learning phase begins. Thus, learning that occurs under a condition where the participants are informed about the upcoming test is considered to be intentional learning. However, if they are not informed (and also not aware of the purpose of the activity) before the learning phase, the learning is considered to be incidental learning. The following subsections review studies that tested the TOPRA model in the context of intentional and incidental learning.

::: {.section aria-labelledby="sec11_4_1"}
### Intentional L2 Vocabulary Learning {#ch11.xhtml_sec11_4_1}

A number of studies have tested one central TOPRA model prediction: semantic processing of new L2 words can inhibit the learning of new L2 word forms when overall processing demands are sufficiently high, as is described in situation 3 in [Table 11.1](#ch11.xhtml_table11_1). For example, [Barcroft (2004)](#ch11.xhtml_ref11_5){.biblioref role="doc-biblioref"} asked L2 Spanish learners to remember picture--L2 word pairs. Half of the words were learned by viewing them on a screen without sentence-writing (control) whereas the other half were learned by writing them in a sentence in Spanish (semantic processing). Participants were exposed to target words for 24 seconds (six seconds and four repetitions) in the no-sentence-writing condition but 48 seconds (48 seconds and one repetition) in the sentence-writing condition (Experiment 1), or 24 seconds (24 seconds and one repetition) for the two conditions []{#ch11.xhtml_p158 .pagebreak aria-label=" page 158. " role="doc-pagebreak"}(Experiment 2). Picture-to-L2 cued recall was administered immediately after, two days after, and one week after training (Experiment 1), or immediately after and two days after training (Experiment 2). Results demonstrated that words learned in the sentence-writing condition were recalled less than those in the no-sentence-writing condition.

Partially replicating [Barcroft's (2004)](#ch11.xhtml_ref11_5){.biblioref role="doc-biblioref"} Experiment 1, [Wong and Pyun (2012)](#ch11.xhtml_ref11_43){.biblioref role="doc-biblioref"} compared the effect of a sentence-writing condition using new L2 words and a no-sentence writing condition. Participants were L2 French or L2 Korean learners. Results replicated those reported in [Barcroft's (2004)](#ch11.xhtml_ref11_5){.biblioref role="doc-biblioref"} Experiment 1. [Wong and Pyun (2012)](#ch11.xhtml_ref11_43){.biblioref role="doc-biblioref"} further demonstrated that the difference between the results obtained with and without sentence-writing conditions was greater among L2 Korean learners; they thus argued that L1--L2 orthographic distance affected the degree of semantic processing's negative effects on the learning of new L2 word forms.

[Barcroft (2003)](#ch11.xhtml_ref11_4){.biblioref role="doc-biblioref"} asked L2 Spanish learners to learn L2 picture--word pairs. Half of these were learned in the semantic processing condition where learners were asked to answer questions about the target word objects (e.g., "When was the last time (if ever) that you used this object?," "In what ways can this object be used?," "Where can you buy this object?," and "What other objects do you associate with this object?"). The other half was learned without answering questions (control). Posttests were L2 free recall, L1 free recall, and two picture-to-L2 cued recalls. The results indicated no difference between learning conditions in the two types of free recall, but participants in the semantic processing condition did not perform as well on both types of cued recall tasks.

Some studies have tested the effects of semantic as well as structural tasks in a single study. [Barcroft (2002)](#ch11.xhtml_ref11_3){.biblioref role="doc-biblioref"} asked American learners of L2 Spanish to learn Spanish words and their corresponding pictures. While they learned the L2 word--picture pairs, (a) learners in the semantic processing condition were asked to simultaneously perform a pleasantness rating task in which they indicated the pleasantness of each word meaning on a Likert-like scale (from very unpleasant to very pleasant); (b) learners in the structural processing condition performed a letter-counting task where they indicated the number of letters in each word; and (c) learners in the control condition did not perform any additional vocabulary processing tasks. After the learning phase, they performed the following tasks: L2 free recall, L1 free recall, L1-to-L2 cued recall, and L2-to-L1 cued recall. Results indicated that the semantic processing condition outperformed the structural processing condition on L1 free recall, whereas the structural processing condition outperformed the semantic processing condition on L2 free recall. This double dissociation result supported TOPRA's prediction, suggesting that word forms and meanings are dissociable in L2 vocabulary learning.

Expanding on [Barcroft (2002)](#ch11.xhtml_ref11_3){.biblioref role="doc-biblioref"}, [Kida et al. (2022)](#ch11.xhtml_ref11_34){.biblioref role="doc-biblioref"} used both semantic and structural processing tasks. Participants were Japanese learners of L2 English who learned L2 target words and their L1 translations while viewing them for eight seconds each on a PC monitor. Two English words were then shown on this monitor. Participants in the semantic processing condition were asked to decide which of the two words was similar to the target word in terms of meaning, whereas those in the structural processing condition were asked to determine which word was similar to the target word form (spelling). They attempted to learn the target words and their translations and performed semantic/structural processing tasks four times. After the learning trial, recall tasks (L2 free recall, L1 free recall, L1-to-L2 cued recall, and L2-to-L1 cued recall) were administered. Results indicated that participants in the semantic processing condition recalled more L1 translation words than those in the structural processing condition on L1 free recall, but the opposite tendency was observed in the L2 free recall. Again, this study demonstrated double dissociation results.

The studies discussed above examined the effects of semantic or structural orienting tasks on L2 vocabulary learning, where participants learned new L2 word forms and mapped them onto []{#ch11.xhtml_p159 .pagebreak aria-label=" page 159. " role="doc-pagebreak"}already known meanings (e.g., familiar pictures, L1 translations, and so on). However, as shown in [Table 11.1](#ch11.xhtml_table11_1), L2 vocabulary learning is not limited to this type of learning. For example, even if one can assume that the L2 word form and the meaning are known, the mapping between them may be novel (i.e., situation 7 in [Table 11.1](#ch11.xhtml_table11_1)). [Kida and Barcroft (2018)](#ch11.xhtml_ref11_33){.biblioref role="doc-biblioref"} examined this type of learning; they used high-frequency English homonyms (e.g., *foot*) whose primary meanings were assumed to be already known to Japanese learners of English (e.g., foot as in *hand and foot*); however, the secondary meanings were not considered "already known" (e.g., foot as in *to foot the bill*). Learners who tried to learn these secondary meanings did not need to learn L2 word forms or meanings. Rather, what was novel here was with the *mapping* between word forms and meanings. If form--meaning mapping is dissociable from word form and meaning, the TOPRA model predicts that both semantic processing and structural processing could negatively influence new mapping when overall processing demands are sufficiently high. The lower scores obtained for both the semantic and structural conitions based on both L1-to-L2 and L2-to-L1 cued recall (both examined knowledge of form--meaning mapping) were consistent with this prediction.
:::

::: {.section aria-labelledby="sec11_4_2"}
### Incidental L2 Vocabulary Learning {#ch11.xhtml_sec11_4_2}

Thus far, most studies testing the TOPRA model have involved intentional vocabulary learning research. One possible theoretical question is whether the TOPRA model's predictions are applicable when vocabulary learning occurs through L2 reading in incidental learning. During L2 reading, readers primarily focus on comprehending the text (including any unfamiliar words). Therefore, their processing resources are primarily allocated for the semantic processing of these words. In this situation, does semantic processing have the same effect as it does when vocabulary learning is intentional? [Barcroft (2009)](#ch11.xhtml_ref11_6){.biblioref role="doc-biblioref"} proposed two potential hypotheses concerning this question: resource depletion and attention drawing. Consistent with the prediction of TOPRA, the resource depletion hypothesis postulates that assigning additional semantic processing tasks related to target words during reading will deplete the resources necessary for processing target word forms which consequently will negatively affect the learning of new L2 word forms. On the other hand, the attention drawing hypothesis predicts that assigning a semantic processing task will positively affect the learning of new L2 word forms because, when text comprehension is the learners' primary task, they do not have to focus on target words (i.e., they can comprehend the overall text meaning even if they do not fully understand the target word meanings). In this situation, assigning a semantic processing task to learners will lead them to pay attention to target words and would consequently help them learn L2 word forms. [Barcroft (2009)](#ch11.xhtml_ref11_6){.biblioref role="doc-biblioref"} asked Spanish learners of English to read an L2 text that contained target words and answer comprehension questions. L1 translations of the target words were provided beside each target word, so that they did not learn novel meaning as shown in [Table 11.1](#ch11.xhtml_table11_1). Learners in the semantic processing condition were further asked to perform a synonym generation task in which they provided an L1 synonym for each L1 translation; those in the control condition were not assigned any additional tasks. Results demonstrated that participants in the semantic processing condition performed worse than the control group with regard to both L1-to-L2 and L2-to-L1 cued recall. This finding was thus consistent with predictions of the TOPRA model and the resource depletion hypothesis.

[Kida (2010b)](#ch11.xhtml_ref11_31){.biblioref role="doc-biblioref"} asked Japanese learners of English to read an English passage that contained 10 low-frequency words and answer comprehension questions. Half of these low-frequent words were repeated once, while the other half were repeated three times in the passage. Participants in the semantic processing condition performed pleasantness ratings, whereas those in the structural processing condition performed a phonological encoding task where they had to guess the []{#ch11.xhtml_p160 .pagebreak aria-label=" page 160. " role="doc-pagebreak"}possible pronunciation of each target word and provide this pronunciation in their L1. Participants in the control condition were not assigned any additional target word processing tasks. Based on L1-to-L2 and L2-to-L1 cued recall tasks, results revealed that those in the structural processing condition performed better than those in the semantic processing condition with regard to the one-exposure and three-exposure words on the L1-to-L2 cued recall test; however, no clear difference was observed between the three learning conditions on the L2-to-L1 cued recall test, possibly because L1-to-L2 cued recall required them to produce L2 word forms whereas L2-to-L1 cued recall required them to only recognize L2 word forms (see also [Elgort et al., 2018](#ch11.xhtml_ref11_13){#ch11.xhtml_R_ref11_13 .biblioref role="doc-biblioref"}, for the comparison of the effects of meaning-focused and form-focused elaboration on learning new L2 vocabulary through reading). These studies demonstrated that predictions of TOPRA are applicable to incidental L2 vocabulary learning through reading and suggest that allocating processing-resources to semantic aspects of new L2 words can impede the learning of novel L2 word forms.
:::
:::::

:::::: {.section aria-labelledby="sec11_5"}
## Main Research Methods {#ch11.xhtml_sec11_5}

::: {.section aria-labelledby="sec11_5_1"}
### Pretests {#ch11.xhtml_sec11_5_1}

In order to demonstrate that participants lack previous knowledge of target words, researchers may use nonwords (pseudowords) or unknown real words as target words. Because nonwords do not exist in participants' lexicon, researchers do not have to conduct pretests for nonwords. However, when researchers use real words, they must use pretests to endure that participants do not already know the words. Pretests may be of various types, ranging from asking participants to indicate whether they think they know the words (i.e., yes/no format) to asking them to produce target word meaning or forms. Pretests may have limitations since they can expose participants to target word *before* the experiment, which could affect the results. It is particularly difficult to give a pretest in incidental learning research because if researchers conduct a pretest and then give participants the incidental learning task (e.g., reading task) which contain the target words just tested, participants could easily guess the purpose of the study. Therefore, when using a pretest in incidental learning research, researchers need to ensure that participants will not view the task as a vocabulary pretest. For example, because [Barcroft (2009)](#ch11.xhtml_ref11_6){.biblioref role="doc-biblioref"} gave participants a set of activities using target words that were not related to the main reading, participants were not likely to know that the purpose of the activities was to assess prior target word knowledge. Another way of checking participants' prior knowledge of target words is to administer a questionnaire or interview to them *after* the experiment. In this way, researchers can avoid exposing participants to target words before the experiment. However, they need to be careful about the credibility of learners' introspective memory.
:::

::: {.section aria-labelledby="sec11_5_2"}
### Experimental Designs {#ch11.xhtml_sec11_5_2}

Intentional vocabulary learning studies on the TOPRA model have often followed a Latin square design because this type of design allows participants to experience each experimental condition and receive an equal amount of target word exposure (e.g., Barcroft, [2002](#ch11.xhtml_ref11_3){.biblioref role="doc-biblioref"}, [2003](#ch11.xhtml_ref11_4){.biblioref role="doc-biblioref"}; [Kida et al., 2022](#ch11.xhtml_ref11_34){.biblioref role="doc-biblioref"}). For example, suppose one wants to examine the effects of both semantic and structural processing conditions and compare these effects with those in the control condition using 24 target words. By using a Latin square design, all participants can experience the three learning conditions (semantic processing, structural processing, and control); furthermore, across the three list conditions, all target words can be learned in each learning condition. When possible, the word presentation order in each cell can be randomized for each participant.

[]{#ch11.xhtml_p161 .pagebreak aria-label=" page 161. " role="doc-pagebreak"}The Latin square design is not usually used in incidental vocabulary learning research because participants' focus is on completing the learning task (e.g., reading comprehension). In this situation, participants are typically randomly assigned into experimental conditions (e.g., semantic elaboration, structural elaboration, control), and the data analyses are carried out in a between-subject design.
:::

::: {.section aria-labelledby="sec11_5_3"}
### Lexical Decision {#ch11.xhtml_sec11_5_3}

So far, studies testing TOPRA have used four types of posttests as shown in [Table 11.3](#ch11.xhtml_table11_3): L1 free recall, L2 free recall, L1-to-L2 cued recall, and L2-to-L1 cued recall. These types of posttests are used to measure the participants' *memory* of target lexical information. On the other hand, some studies used a lexical decision task as a posttest to measure their *processing* within the memory system ([Kida, 2010a](#ch11.xhtml_ref11_30){.biblioref role="doc-biblioref"}, [Kida et al., 2022](#ch11.xhtml_ref11_34){.biblioref role="doc-biblioref"}). [Kida (2010a)](#ch11.xhtml_ref11_30){.biblioref role="doc-biblioref"}, for example, used the lexical decision task as a posttest in an incidental vocabulary learning study and found that structural processing of target words during reading sped up post-reading lexical decision of these words compared to semantic processing of these words. This shows the flexibility of the TOPRA model's predictions and may suggest that different types of processing affect the processing and learning of lexical information.
:::
::::::

::: {.section aria-labelledby="sec11_6"}
## Recommendations for Practice {#ch11.xhtml_sec11_6}

One practical implication predicted by TOPRA is that we should consider whether the processing type is appropriate for the learning goal. Studies have shown that when L2 learners need to learn L2 word forms in a typical new L2 word learning situation (i.e., when L2 learners need to learn new L2 word forms but do not have to learn new meanings as in situation 3 in [Table 11.1](#ch11.xhtml_table11_1)), instructors should not direct learners to allocate too much of their processing resources to the semantic aspect of words. Instead, structural processing should be used to promote word form learning. However, when novel meaning learning is required (e.g., situation 1 or 4 in [Table 11.1](#ch11.xhtml_table11_1)), semantic processing should be used to help learners to learn meaning. Therefore, instructors and curriculum developers must consider whether the processing activity matches the learning goal.

TOPRA-based studies have used various types of orienting tasks. [Table 11.4](#ch11.xhtml_table11_4){#ch11.xhtml_R_table11_4} summarizes the task types that could be used in L2 classrooms and their learning outcomes. As shown in the []{#ch11.xhtml_p162 .pagebreak aria-label=" page 162. " role="doc-pagebreak"}table, the basic assumption of these tasks is that, when it comes to the most typical L2 vocabulary situation where learners need to learn new L2 word forms and map them onto the known meanings (situation 3 in [Table 11.1](#ch11.xhtml_table11_1)), semantic processing can have a negative effect on learning, whereas structural processing has a positive effect.

+------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------+----------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------+
| *Task or Learning Condition*                                                                                                                                                                                     | *What to Expect*                                                                                   |

| 1.  Writing target words in original sentences ([Barcroft, 2004](#ch11.xhtml_ref11_5){.biblioref role="doc-biblioref"})                                                                                          | Less vocabulary learning compared to simple paired-association learning                            |
+------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------+----------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------+
| 1.  Answering questions about the meaning of target words ([Barcroft, 2003](#ch11.xhtml_ref11_4){.biblioref role="doc-biblioref"})                                                                               | Less vocabulary learning compared to learning performed without answering these questions          |
+------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------+----------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------+
| 1.  Generating L1 synonyms for target words while reading a text (*with* and *without* explicit instructions to learn the target words) ([Barcroft, 2009](#ch11.xhtml_ref11_6){.biblioref role="doc-biblioref"}) | Less vocabulary learning compared to learning performed with no synonym generation                 |
+------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------+----------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------+
| 1.  Selecting L2 synonyms/neighborhoods when doing paired-association L2 vocabulary learning ([Kida et al., 2022](#ch11.xhtml_ref11_34){.biblioref role="doc-biblioref"})                                        | Less/more vocabulary learning compared to learning performed with neighborhoods/synonyms selection |
+------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------+----------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------+

: [[Table ]{.label}[11.4 ]{.ordinal}Examples of Types of Tasks and Expected L2 Vocabulary Learning Outcomes](#ch11.xhtml_R_table11_4){.backlink aria-label=" This link returns to its first in-text citation. " role="doc-backlink"} {#table11_4}
:::

::::: {.section aria-labelledby="sec11_7"}
## Future Directions {#ch11.xhtml_sec11_7}

This chapter has described the nature and predictions of the TOPRA model with its historical background, summarized what we have known from previous studies, and outlined methodological key issues that researchers need to consider. Below we discuss some directions for future research.

::: {.section aria-labelledby="sec11_7_1"}
### Multiple Aspects of L2 Vocabulary Acquisition {#ch11.xhtml_sec11_7_1}

Existing studies have mainly examined the effects of semantic and structural processing on the learning of L2 word meaning, form, and form--meaning mapping. Future research should include other components of vocabulary learning, such as the acquisition of automaticity, multiword expressions, sociolinguistic register of words, and so on. How do semantic and structural processing (or any other kind of processing) for new L2 vocabulary affect these underinvestigated aspects of L2 vocabulary learning?
:::

::: {.section aria-labelledby="sec11_7_2"}
### Quality and Quantity of L2 Lex-IP {#ch11.xhtml_sec11_7_2}

Another possible future direction is to examine the TOPRA model in relation to other vocabulary learning frameworks. Based on a broader perspective, TOPRA was conceptualized within an information processing framework ([McLaughlin et al., 1983](#ch11.xhtml_ref11_38){#ch11.xhtml_R_ref11_38 .biblioref role="doc-biblioref"}). This model assumes that "humans are limited-capacity information processors, both in terms of what they can attend to at a given point in time and in terms of what they can handle on the basis of knowledge and expectations" ([McLauglin et al., 1983](#ch11.xhtml_ref11_38){.biblioref role="doc-biblioref"}, p. 137). Most L2 vocabulary learning studies thus far have been conducted within this framework. The quality (type) and quantity (amount) of information processing form two crucial factors in L2 vocabulary learning within the information processing framework ([Hulstijn, 2001](#ch11.xhtml_ref11_22){.biblioref role="doc-biblioref"}). *Quality of processing* refers to how L2 learners process (novel) L2 words (e.g., semantic/structural processing) whereas *quantity of processing* refers to how many times L2 learners process them (e.g., rehearsal and repeated exposures). Based on an IP perspective, both processing quality and processing quantity utilize L2 input as a source for information processing. Furthermore, lex-IP refers to "the processes by which learners allocate processing resources when they are exposed to a new word or lexical phrase as input" ([Barcroft, 2015](#ch11.xhtml_ref11_7){.biblioref role="doc-biblioref"}, p. 2). In this sense, the TOPRA model, when applied to L2 vocabulary, can be considered as an L2 vocabulary learning model that highlights processing quality (rather than the quantity of different types of processing) within an IP framework.

Finally, processing quantity is the other factor for successful L2 vocabulary learning. As [Greene (1992)](#ch11.xhtml_ref11_18){#ch11.xhtml_R_ref11_18 .biblioref role="doc-biblioref"} clearly indicated, "All other things being equal, our memory for information will depend on the number of times that we have encountered or studied it" (p. 132). The question is whether processing quality interacts with processing quantity. If it does, what is the nature of this interaction? Does the effect of processing quality increase or decrease as processing quantity increases? Do the effects of processing quantity differ depending on the processing quality? These questions can be explored in future research.
:::
:::::

::: {.endnotes role="doc-endnotes"}
## []{#ch11.xhtml_p163 .pagebreak aria-label=" page 163. " role="doc-pagebreak"}Notes {#ch11.xhtml_h2}
:::

::: {.section .reference aria-labelledby="r11_1"}
## Further Reading {#ch11.xhtml_r11_1}

- [Barcroft, J. (2015). `<cite>`{=html}Lexical input processing and vocabulary learning`</cite>`{=html}. John Benjamins.]{#ch11.xhtml_ref11_1}
- [Kida, S. (2019). ::: {.section .part role="doc-part"}
# []{#pt4.xhtml_p167 .pagebreak aria-label=" page 167. " role="doc-pagebreak"}[Part IV ]{.chnum .ordinal}[Mechanisms of Input Processing and the Acquisition of Morphosyntax[]{#pt4.xhtml_p168 .pagebreak aria-label=" page 168 is blank. " role="doc-pagebreak"}]{.chtitle} {#pt4.xhtml_h1}
