[]{#ch12.xhtml}

::: {}
# []{#ch12.xhtml_p169 .pagebreak aria-label=" page 169. " role="doc-pagebreak"}[12 ]{.chnum .ordinal}[Attention to Form and Meaning ]{.chtitle}[VanPatten (1990) and Beyond]{.chsubtitle .subtitle role="doc-subtitle"} {#ch12.xhtml_h1}

Cristina Sanz

Georgetown University

DOI: [10.4324/9781003041412-16](https://dx.doi.org/10.4324/9781003041412-16){aria-label="D.O.I. link to this document."}
:::

::::: {.section .introduction role="doc-introduction"}
## Introduction and Definitions {#ch12.xhtml_sec12_1}

While the role of attention in second language acquisition (SLA) had been present in discussions since the 1970s, it was not until the 1990s that the construct of attention was fully brought into applied linguistics from cognitive psychology (e.g., [Schmidt 1990](#ch12.xhtml_ref12_31){#ch12.xhtml_R_ref12_31 .biblioref role="doc-biblioref"}; [Tomlin & Villa, 1994](#ch12.xhtml_ref12_35){#ch12.xhtml_R_ref12_35 .biblioref role="doc-biblioref"}; [Robinson 1995](#ch12.xhtml_ref12_26){#ch12.xhtml_R_ref12_26 .biblioref role="doc-biblioref"}). It is in this context that [VanPatten (1996)](#ch12.xhtml_ref12_37){#ch12.xhtml_R_ref12_37 .biblioref role="doc-biblioref"} proposed his input processing (IP) model to explain how a limited capacity processor (drawing from [McLaughlin, 1987](#ch12.xhtml_ref12_20){#ch12.xhtml_R_ref12_20 .biblioref role="doc-biblioref"}) transforms input into intake, a process that involves making form--meaning connections (FMCs). One of the strengths of the IP model is that it combines internal factors, such as cognitive resources as they relate to attention, with linguistic factors that are present in the input, such as saliency (e.g., where a form appears in a sentence) and redundancy (e.g., whether a lexical form conveys the same meaning as a grammatical form). The basis of VanPatten's proposals is tied to an empirical study, that of [VanPatten (1990)](#ch12.xhtml_ref12_36){#ch12.xhtml_R_ref12_36 .biblioref role="doc-biblioref"}, which focused on the competition for resources between conscious attention to meaning and form. The study concluded that learners' limited processing capacities are divided in ways that determine what is extracted from the input for additional processing and ultimately becomes part of interlanguage. This helps in explaining the connection between acquisition orders and attention during input processing (IP); i.e., what is acquired and, importantly, how it is acquired (VanPatten, [1996](#ch12.xhtml_ref12_37){.biblioref role="doc-biblioref"}, [2004](#ch12.xhtml_ref12_38){#ch12.xhtml_R_ref12_38 .biblioref role="doc-biblioref"}).

As the first experimental study to investigate the role of conscious attention to form and meaning during IP, the relevance of [VanPatten (1990)](#ch12.xhtml_ref12_36){.biblioref role="doc-biblioref"} for SLA today justifies its position among the ten most cited studies in *Studies in Second Language Acquisition*'s history. Its findings have also been amongst the most widely replicated in a number of US and international labs. Early partial replications ([Barcroft, 2001](#ch12.xhtml_ref12_5){#ch12.xhtml_R_ref12_5 .biblioref role="doc-biblioref"}; [Greenslade, Bouden & Sanz, 1999](#ch12.xhtml_ref12_9){#ch12.xhtml_R_ref12_9 .biblioref role="doc-biblioref"}; [Wong, 2001](#ch12.xhtml_ref12_40){#ch12.xhtml_R_ref12_40 .biblioref role="doc-biblioref"}) extended VanPatten's original results to other languages and to the written modality. In contrast, a newer strand of studies ([Leow et al., 2008](#ch12.xhtml_ref12_18){#ch12.xhtml_R_ref12_18 .biblioref role="doc-biblioref"}; [Morgan-Short et al., 2012](#ch12.xhtml_ref12_21){#ch12.xhtml_R_ref12_21 .biblioref role="doc-biblioref"}; [Morgan-Short et al., 2018](#ch12.xhtml_ref12_22){#ch12.xhtml_R_ref12_22 .biblioref role="doc-biblioref"}) concluded that low proficiency learners can consciously process any form without detrimental effects on comprehension, regardless of how salient or meaning-bearing the form may be. A recent 'replication of replications' ([Sanz & McCormick, 2021](#ch12.xhtml_ref12_30){#ch12.xhtml_R_ref12_30 .biblioref role="doc-biblioref"}) showed that the differences in results across strands are due to differences in modality and proficiency, as well as the implementation in the new strand of less reliable assessment tasks and a text too complex to allow for 'focus on form' effects to surface. Therefore, there remains evidence to support the competition between []{#ch12.xhtml_p170 .pagebreak aria-label=" page 170. " role="doc-pagebreak"}form and meaning within the constraints imposed by a limited processor, the concept at the basis of the IP model. This has been confirmed in a recent eye-tracking study ([Son et al., 2021](#ch12.xhtml_ref12_33){#ch12.xhtml_R_ref12_33 .biblioref role="doc-biblioref"}).

::: {.section aria-labelledby="sec12_1_1"}
### Definitions {#ch12.xhtml_sec12_1_1}

Form, meaning, and form--meaning connections are a critical part of the first step in the three-step process of language development. *Form* refers to the surface manifestations of underlying representations---lexemes, inflections, and functors. *Meaning* refers to sociolinguistic, pragmatic, and concrete and abstract referential meaning. In semiotics, form--meaning constructions can vary in size from minimal meaningful units, such as morphemes and words, to larger linguistic units such as sentences ([Robinson & Ellis, 2008](#ch12.xhtml_ref12_27){#ch12.xhtml_R_ref12_27 .biblioref role="doc-biblioref"}) and even more holistic types of meanings at the level of discourse and rhetoric. There are also visual, graphic, and auditory modes of meaning-making ([Kress, 2009](#ch12.xhtml_ref12_16){#ch12.xhtml_R_ref12_16 .biblioref role="doc-biblioref"}), such as intonation, gestures, and orthographic and typographic representations in the case of written language. Although most previously documented IP research has focused on morphosyntax, [Barcroft (2015)](#ch12.xhtml_ref12_6){#ch12.xhtml_R_ref12_6 .biblioref role="doc-biblioref"} expands theory and research on IP to the lexical domain (Lex-IP) and notes that IP should spread beyond these domains to phon-IP, soc-IP, and prag-IP. We refer the reader to [Barcroft (2021)](#ch12.xhtml_ref12_7){#ch12.xhtml_R_ref12_7 .biblioref role="doc-biblioref"}, where these ideas are fully developed with suggestions for further research.

*Form--meaning connections* (FMCs) are made when forms are cognitively registered and associated with their corresponding meanings. These relationships present themselves in three ways: 1) one form to one meaning correspondence; 2) one form corresponding to multiple meanings; 3) multiple forms encoding the same meaning ([VanPatten, 2004](#ch12.xhtml_ref12_38){.biblioref role="doc-biblioref"}, pp. 1--3). According to VanPatten, FMCs can be placed on a continuum from weak to robust, partial to complete, or non-target-like to target-like (p. 6). FMCs are made in *working memory* (WM), a limited space ([Baddeley, 1990](#ch12.xhtml_ref12_4){#ch12.xhtml_R_ref12_4 .biblioref role="doc-biblioref"}, cf. [VanPatten 2004](#ch12.xhtml_ref12_38){.biblioref role="doc-biblioref"}) where incoming linguistic information is processed and temporarily stored. Albeit without making any claims about levels of awareness, VanPatten's proposals go beyond Schmidt's *noticing* ([1990](#ch12.xhtml_ref12_36){.biblioref role="doc-biblioref"}), which is restricted to the registration of a form and does not entail connection to a meaning; consequently, FMCs impose heavy provisions on attentional resources. Factors that facilitate FMCs are input characteristics, including saliency and frequency, and learner factors, such as cognitive capacity, L1, and proficiency.
:::

::: {.section aria-labelledby="sec12_1_2"}
### Form, Function, and the Learner as a Limited Processor {#ch12.xhtml_sec12_1_2}

With the IP model, [VanPatten (1996)](#ch12.xhtml_ref12_37){.biblioref role="doc-biblioref"} sought to answer three fundamental questions: What form--meaning connections do learners make, when do they make them, and why do they make some but not others. The model follows a linear modular design that starts with input and ends with output \[Input \> Intake \> System \> Output\]. The first set of processes is responsible for the transformation of input into intake. The second set is responsible for restructuring and intake accommodation into the developing mental representation of the L2. The third set of processes allows learners to access their developing system to produce output. The model is predicated on a limited capacity processor, meaning that working memory capacity determines how much of the input is processed. One important prediction is that as proficiency increases, processes are automatized and the pressure for attentional resources decreases. Therefore, cognitive space remains available for form--meaning connections, allowing more intake to be extracted from the input.

Like Bates and McWhinney's competition model (CM; [1981](#ch12.xhtml_ref12_8){#ch12.xhtml_R_ref12_8 .biblioref role="doc-biblioref"}), which it often parallels, VanPatten's IP model is based on a functionalist view of SLA. In both the CM and the IP model, meaning derives from the competition among linguistic cues during processing, including but not limited to stress and intonation, animacy, position---beginning or end of a []{#ch12.xhtml_p171 .pagebreak aria-label=" page 171. " role="doc-pagebreak"}structure---word order, and inflection; these cues compete during real-time processing to yield a particular meaning. Cues have different weights in different languages; in this view, language learning is synonymous with weight realignment to match those of the L2. Weights are set and strengthened as the learner processes language to extract meaning. The interpretation with the highest probability given the available cues and their relative weights prevails. If a sentence is successfully processed, the relevant cues are strengthened; if it results in a processing error, the weight of the cues is adjusted. Most IP principles rely on the functionalist idea of cue validity, which is proportional to its weight and is related to perceptual saliency, functional usefulness, and communicative value. Similar to cues, IP's principles depict competition between elements. The lexical preference principle states that lexical items are high validity cues; The preference for non-redundancy principle states that redundant elements have low validity; The meaning-before-non-meaning principle proposes a correlation between degree of meaning of a form and its validity; The sentence location principle assigns higher validity to elements in initial or final position. (See also Leeser, this volume, for an in-depth discussion of the principles of IP.) These ideas---form--meaning connections, processing strategies, the notions of semantic weight and redundancy---are inspired in functionalist work discussed in the 1980s and 1990s.

Also central to the IP model and SLA since the 1990s is the view of the learner as a limited capacity processor. In order to explain what is learned and how it is learned, functionalists often frame these questions in terms of processing demands, with WM emerging as (one of) the stand-in(s) for human processing limitations. Some features in L2 grammar require more processing resources than others---because they do not have a feature in the L1 to map onto, because of the distance between morpheme and referent, low communicative value, or due to salience, for example. These features are therefore more difficult to acquire than others because novice learners' cognitive resources are tasked first and foremost with deciphering meaning, the most fundamental of the processes in the hierarchy. Once comprehension becomes more automatized, recruiting fewer cognitive resources, VanPatten's IP model predicts that learners' resources move towards attending to more meaningful grammatical features (e.g., subject--verb agreement) before less meaningful features (e.g., noun--adjective gender agreement). As these processes also become automatic, cognitive resources are freed to attend to higher-order processes (see [Sanz & McCormick, 2021](#ch12.xhtml_ref12_30){.biblioref role="doc-biblioref"}, for a recent overview).

WM, the construct where incoming linguistic information is processed and temporarily stored, is also a key variable in explaining differential rates of success across learners. The research on WM has mostly focused on learning morphology and word order, either in the laboratory with novice learners of artificial grammars or with beginner and intermediate L2 learners in cross-sectional designs. However, examples of longitudinal studies (e.g., [Serafini & Sanz, 2016](#ch12.xhtml_ref12_32){#ch12.xhtml_R_ref12_32 .biblioref role="doc-biblioref"}) that include advanced learners in their samples (e.g., [Grey et al., 2015](#ch12.xhtml_ref12_10){#ch12.xhtml_R_ref12_10 .biblioref role="doc-biblioref"}) and that manipulate learning conditions ([Issa, 2019](#ch12.xhtml_ref12_12){#ch12.xhtml_R_ref12_12 .biblioref role="doc-biblioref"}) have proven to be most informative (see [McCormick & Sanz, 2022](#ch12.xhtml_ref12_19){#ch12.xhtml_R_ref12_19 .biblioref role="doc-biblioref"}, for a recent overview of the research on WM and L2 grammar development in adults). Contrary to what has been proposed for children learning their L1 ([Newport 1990](#ch12.xhtml_ref12_23){#ch12.xhtml_R_ref12_23 .biblioref role="doc-biblioref"}), most but not all research on WM and L2 learning supports the idea that greater WM resources are beneficial to adult L2 learning ([Indrarathne & Kormos, 2018](#ch12.xhtml_ref12_11){#ch12.xhtml_R_ref12_11 .biblioref role="doc-biblioref"}). To understand these mixed findings, researchers must consider how studies differ in the tasks posed, as WM's role reveals itself empirically only when the task pushes the learners to their cognitive limits; otherwise, differences in performance between high- and low-WMC individuals will be similar or too small to be observable. However, when these learners are challenged with a task that (1) requires storage and processing and (2) is outside the threshold of automaticity, differences in WMC amplify the differences in L2 learners' performance to reveal WM's importance ([McCormick & Sanz, 2022](#ch12.xhtml_ref12_19){.biblioref role="doc-biblioref"}). [Serafini and Sanz (2016)](#ch12.xhtml_ref12_32){.biblioref role="doc-biblioref"} show that WM differences correlate to outcomes across ten []{#ch12.xhtml_p172 .pagebreak aria-label=" page 172. " role="doc-pagebreak"}morphosyntactic structures among beginner and intermediate, but not advanced, L2 Spanish learners. The findings obtained from cross-sectional, longitudinal studies like this are valuable to the field because they can pinpoint not only what correlates with WM, but when and where WM is most involved in L2 learning.
:::
:::::

::: {.section aria-labelledby="sec12_2"}
## Historical Perspectives {#ch12.xhtml_sec12_2}

Examining how proficiency moderates the interaction between attention-related internal factors and linguistic factors such as saliency and redundancy is fundamental in explaining which forms are acquired before others; this question is at the basis of VanPatten's IP model and motivates [VanPatten's (1990)](#ch12.xhtml_ref12_36){.biblioref role="doc-biblioref"} study. VanPatten designed an empirical investigation on the posited competition for resources between conscious attention to meaning and conscious attention to form. The study aimed to show how this competition determines what is extracted from the input for further processing as intake and is ultimately incorporated into the learner's grammar. Specifically, VanPatten aimed at providing experimental data to substantiate that attention to grammatical form impedes comprehension, and that proficiency moderates the competition between form and meaning.

VanPatten collected data from a total of 202 participants at three proficiency levels (first-semester, fourth-semester, and third-year conversation classes). Participants listened to an audio passage about inflation in Latin America and were then instructed to complete a free recall task, writing down as much information as they could remember from the recording. To activate prior knowledge and facilitate comprehension, participants were told in advance that the recording was about inflation. The three-minute passage included brief non-native-like pauses at natural boundaries to allow for processing and was read by a near-native speaker of Spanish. Four groups were formed: The control group listened to the text and later completed the free recall task, but the three experimental groups completed an additional task that required attention to lexical or grammatical morphemes while listening to the audio passage. Attention to grammatical or lexical forms was operationalized by asking participants to put a mark on a blank page each time they heard their target item *inflación* (lexical item), *la* ("the", an unbound morpheme), and *-n* (a bound morpheme indicating a third-person plural conjugated verb). To be included in the sample, participants had to make eight or more marks out of the 11 or 12 occurrences of the target form. Meaning was operationalized as ideas recalled.

VanPatten's study generally supported the hypotheses. The first two hypotheses, which proposed that attention to grammatico-morphological forms would negatively impact comprehension, and that attention to the keyword *inflación* (the topic of the text) would not, were both supported outright by the results, which yielded a main effect for the task. Proficiency was not found to moderate the relationship between form and meaning, as higher and lower proficiency learners revealed similar patterns when attending to form and meaning in the study.

About 10 years later, [VanPatten's (1990)](#ch12.xhtml_ref12_36){.biblioref role="doc-biblioref"} study saw several conceptual replications ([Barcroft, 2001](#ch12.xhtml_ref12_5){.biblioref role="doc-biblioref"}; [Greenslade et al., 1999](#ch12.xhtml_ref12_9){.biblioref role="doc-biblioref"}; [Wong, 2001](#ch12.xhtml_ref12_40){.biblioref role="doc-biblioref"}). The first conceptual replications used the original materials but extended the questions to the written mode ([Barcroft, 2001](#ch12.xhtml_ref12_5){.biblioref role="doc-biblioref"}; [Greenslade et al., 1999](#ch12.xhtml_ref12_9){.biblioref role="doc-biblioref"}) or to another language, English, and compared both the written and aural modes ([Wong, 2001](#ch12.xhtml_ref12_40){.biblioref role="doc-biblioref"}). To elucidate the mixed findings within the written mode from [Greenslade et al. (1999)](#ch12.xhtml_ref12_9){.biblioref role="doc-biblioref"} and [Wong's (2001)](#ch12.xhtml_ref12_40){.biblioref role="doc-biblioref"} written study, [Barcroft (2001)](#ch12.xhtml_ref12_5){.biblioref role="doc-biblioref"} then asked how timed versus untimed reading may play a role in attention to form and meaning.

Almost 20 years after VanPatten's original study, a new wave of replications began with [Leow et al. (2008)](#ch12.xhtml_ref12_18){.biblioref role="doc-biblioref"}, continued with [Morgan-Short et al. (2012)](#ch12.xhtml_ref12_21){.biblioref role="doc-biblioref"}, and culminated in an international multisite replication study ([Morgan-Short et al., 2018](#ch12.xhtml_ref12_22){.biblioref role="doc-biblioref"}). [Leow et al. (2008)](#ch12.xhtml_ref12_18){.biblioref role="doc-biblioref"} first identified what []{#ch12.xhtml_p173 .pagebreak aria-label=" page 173. " role="doc-pagebreak"}were considered limitations in VanPatten's original design, i.e., limitations dealing with saliency and distribution of the forms in the text, as well as the use of recall as a measure of comprehension. Based on these claims, they changed and added forms, replaced the original text, eliminated the recall task, and introduced a 10-point multiple-choice test. What remained from the original study was VanPatten's basic research question addressing the competition for conscious attention between form and meaning. The second round of studies are therefore not direct or partial replications, but rather an extreme example of conceptual replication that introduced significant changes to both the operationalization of the dependent variable, i.e., meaning, and independent variable, i.e., form.

Except for Barcroft's study, both strands of replications abandoned proficiency as a variable in [VanPatten's (1990)](#ch12.xhtml_ref12_36){.biblioref role="doc-biblioref"} original design to pursue, in greater depth, the question of how attention to lexical and grammatico-morphological forms differentially affect semantic processing. The following section examines these studies in more detail.
:::

::::::: {.section aria-labelledby="sec12_3"}
## Critical Issues and Topics {#ch12.xhtml_sec12_3}

[VanPatten (1990)](#ch12.xhtml_ref12_36){.biblioref role="doc-biblioref"} and its replications have investigated four issues related to attention to form and meaning, namely: 1. Does modality moderate the relationship between attention to form and meaning?; 2. Does type of form moderate the competition between attention to form and meaning?; 3. Does text dfficulty moderate the relationship between attention to form and meaning?; 4. Does proficiency level moderate the relationship between attention to form and meaning? Each is considered separately in this section.

::: {.section aria-labelledby="sec12_3_1"}
### Does Modality Moderate the Relationship Between Attention to Form and Meaning? {#ch12.xhtml_sec12_3_1}

[Greenslade et al.'s (1999)](#ch12.xhtml_ref12_9){.biblioref role="doc-biblioref"} study in the written modality demonstrated that attention to the key lexical item did not affect comprehension, whereas attention to grammatico-morphological forms did. Furthermore, differences between morphemes were also identified, as attention to *la* resulted in worse comprehension than attention to the verbal inflection *-n*. The authors propose that this may reflect [VanPatten's (1996)](#ch12.xhtml_ref12_37){.biblioref role="doc-biblioref"} preference for nonredundancy principle, which states that processing *-n*, a plural morpheme attached to the verb, assists the learner in extracting meaning. In contrast, *la* has negligible semantic content because the information it encodes (feminine gender of the noun) is redundant and, therefore, does not contribute to the comprehension of the passage. [Wong (2001)](#ch12.xhtml_ref12_40){.biblioref role="doc-biblioref"} replicated the two prior studies (written and aural modes) with native French speakers learning English and found the same pattern as the previous studies in the aural mode: attention to the article, in this case *the*, negatively affected comprehension, while attention to the lexical item, *inflation*, did not. However, in the written mode, Wong did not find any significant differences across conditions (p = 0.0552), a result that should be carefully interpreted given cell size (n = 12, 12 and 10). To investigate the written mode further, [Barcroft (2001)](#ch12.xhtml_ref12_5){.biblioref role="doc-biblioref"} created two different reading conditions: in the unconstrained condition, participants had the complete text, while in the constrained condition the text was presented sentence-by-sentence on a screen, to avoid backtracking. The study did not yield any interactions between task and reading condition, and main effects were limited to task, whereby the control group outperformed the group attending to *la*. The decision to include first and second semester learners of Spanish may account for overall floor effects in comprehension scores. As Barcroft concludes, at this level of proficiency, readers did not benefit from the opportunity to backtrack to extract more meaning from the text, whether they were pushed to focus on []{#ch12.xhtml_p174 .pagebreak aria-label=" page 174. " role="doc-pagebreak"}meaning or on form. This result was later confirmed by [Morgan-Short et al. (2018)](#ch12.xhtml_ref12_22){.biblioref role="doc-biblioref"}, a multisite replication that compared modality, specifically aural vs. reading using the rapid serial visual presentation paradigm (see also discussion in Leow & Li, this volume). Overall, evidence accumulated suggests that modality is not a key moderator when text presentation in the written condition is manipulated to match time constraints that are inherent to the aural mode, especially when proficiency (Barcroft) or comprehension scores (Morgan-Short et al.) are low.
:::

::: {.section aria-labelledby="sec12_3_2"}
### Does Type of Form Moderate the Competition Between Attention to Form and Meaning? {#ch12.xhtml_sec12_3_2}

The strand of replications that starts with [Leow et al. (2008)](#ch12.xhtml_ref12_18){.biblioref role="doc-biblioref"}, changed the target forms from the original forms in VanPatten's study: they introduced two new forms, the lexical item *sol*, which replaced *inflación*, because it is monosyllabic, like *la* and *lo*; and the clitic, *lo*. The unbound morpheme *la* and the bound morpheme -*n* were kept. Crucially, the contribution that '*sol'* (sun) makes to the overall meaning in the newly introduced text about Aztec mythology and culture is reduced when compared to the contribution *inflación* makes in the original text on inflation. In addition, while *inflación, sol, la, -n* have a one-to-one relationship with meaning, the referents---the meanings, in the form--meaning connection---for the clitic *lo* ("it") introduced by Leow et al. change throughout the text. Importantly, in these studies, the increased complexity of the new forms (*sol, lo*), and their relationship with meaning was compounded by the lower proficiency level of the participants. Taken together, and contrary to VanPatten's results, these studies found that attending to lexical or grammatical morphemes did not affect overall text comprehension when processing in the aural mode and for constrained reading. This was the state of affairs until recently, when eye-tracking evidence from [Son et al. (2021)](#ch12.xhtml_ref12_33){.biblioref role="doc-biblioref"} showing that focusing attention on morphology, specifically on the bound morpheme *-n* and the unbound morpheme *la*, hinders the learner's ability to extract meaning from the text. This study is discussed in more detail in the next section. In conclusion, as [Barcroft (2001, p. 117)](#ch12.xhtml_ref12_5){.biblioref role="doc-biblioref"} states, the best way to approach the study of the competition between form and meaning is conceptualizing it as a matter of degree and move beyond the morphosyntactic vs lexical forms contrasts to consider forms that differ in communicative value. In VanPatten's text, *Inflación* has a higher communicative value than other lexemes in the same text or than *sol* has in the *Aztecs* text; likewise, the plural form *-n* attached to the verb is higher in communicative value than the article *la*. Directing attention to forms of increasingly lower communicative value will increasingly interfere with semantic processing.
:::

::: {.section aria-labelledby="sec12_3_3"}
### Does Text Difficulty Moderate the Relationship Between Attention to Form and Meaning? {#ch12.xhtml_sec12_3_3}

The new text in [Leow et al. (2008)](#ch12.xhtml_ref12_18){.biblioref role="doc-biblioref"}---*Aztecas*---differs from VanPatten's---*Inflación*---in topic and length. After numerous manipulations from the original ([Rangel Montemayos, 1999](#ch12.xhtml_ref12_25){#ch12.xhtml_R_ref12_25 .biblioref role="doc-biblioref"}), the resulting adapted text is 1/3 longer than VanPatten's; it is also unnatural and more complex both syntactically and lexically than *Inflación*. Also, because traditional college students in the United States are likely to be significantly more familiar with the concept of inflation than with the Aztecs, background knowledge likely played a facilitative role in the extraction of meaning in the original [VanPatten (1990)](#ch12.xhtml_ref12_36){.biblioref role="doc-biblioref"} study. Since a multiple-choice test replaced the free recall task in these studies, one is left to wonder what the recall protocols would have looked like. However, scores in Leow et al. show the mean for comprehension at 4.8 out of a total possible score of 10 (max: 5.2, SD= 1.6). Even the control group charged with reading for comprehension with no []{#ch12.xhtml_p175 .pagebreak aria-label=" page 175. " role="doc-pagebreak"}other burden scored below 50%; it is also very telling that a large number of participants failed to meet the 60% circling threshold. The low comprehension scores across groups and the issue of attrition and its distribution across conditions in Leow et al's study call into question the use of the Aztec text. Arguably, choosing a more difficult text, compounded by the inclusion of participants at a lower proficiency level (first year vs. VanPatten's second year) made it too difficult for any attention to form to negatively affect the extraction of meaning. This conclusion is confirmed by results reported in [Morgan-Short et al. (2018)](#ch12.xhtml_ref12_22){.biblioref role="doc-biblioref"}.
:::

::: {.section aria-labelledby="sec12_3_4"}
### Does Proficiency Level Moderate the Relationship Between Attention to Form and Meaning? {#ch12.xhtml_sec12_3_4}

This last issue addresses the second research question in VanPatten's initial study, i.e., proficiency level. [Morgan-Short et al. (2018)](#ch12.xhtml_ref12_22){.biblioref role="doc-biblioref"} is the only replication study to address the matter, which had already been noted by [Barcroft (2001)](#ch12.xhtml_ref12_5){.biblioref role="doc-biblioref"}. A total of 631 Spanish L2 learners across seven sites in three countries participated. In total, the study reports on the results from two partial replications of [Leow et al. (2008)](#ch12.xhtml_ref12_18){.biblioref role="doc-biblioref"}: One that changed modality from written to aural (as in VanPatten's original study), and a second that changed presentation of the written text from untimed paper-and-pencil to constrained computer-delivered (like Barcroft's), which prevented backtracking and matched the text's rate of presentation in the aural version. Overall, their results replicated those in [Leow et al. (2008)](#ch12.xhtml_ref12_18){.biblioref role="doc-biblioref"} and [Morgan-Short et al. (2012)](#ch12.xhtml_ref12_21){.biblioref role="doc-biblioref"} namely that attending to lexical or grammatical morphemes does not affect overall text comprehension when processing in the aural mode and for constrained reading. However, the authors could not generalize the overall results to learners at higher levels of proficiency, given a reliable significant contrast between the control and the bound morpheme *-n* condition in the groups that showed higher proficiency. Therefore, while VanPatten's original study failed to fully identify proficiency as moderator of the relationship between form and meaning, as higher and lower proficiency learners revealed similar patterns, [Barcroft (2001)](#ch12.xhtml_ref12_5){.biblioref role="doc-biblioref"} and [Morgan-Short et al. (2018)](#ch12.xhtml_ref12_22){.biblioref role="doc-biblioref"} did. [Szudarski and Mikolajczak (2022)](#ch12.xhtml_ref12_34){#ch12.xhtml_R_ref12_34 .biblioref role="doc-biblioref"} confirmed these results.
:::
:::::::

::: {.section aria-labelledby="sec12_4"}
## Current Contributions and Research {#ch12.xhtml_sec12_4}

Two different teams recently approached the issues identified in the previous section in different ways; [Sanz and McCormick (2021)](#ch12.xhtml_ref12_30){.biblioref role="doc-biblioref"} published a 'replication of replications' to test the limits of conceptual replications in this strand of research, and [Son et al. (2021)](#ch12.xhtml_ref12_33){.biblioref role="doc-biblioref"} conducted an eye-tracking study---the first in the series of replications to introduce a non-obtrusive, online measure of attention. [Sanz and McCormick (2021)](#ch12.xhtml_ref12_30){.biblioref role="doc-biblioref"} investigated the consequences of changing the operationalization of meaning comprehension, the dependent variable, when investigating the role of simultaneous attention to form and meaning during input processing in an L2. In their study, intact classes were assigned the aural or the written mode and individual participants were assigned either the control condition, attention to lexical form condition, or attention to free grammatico-morphemic form condition. Text and assessment tasks were all within-group variables; presentation was randomized. In their first published report, they focused on data from the control condition, as they wanted to make within-subject comparisons of texts and assessment tasks. Thirty-six fourth-semester (n~a~= 13, n= 23) and 11 sixth-semester participants were included in the multiple-choice analyses, and 34 fourth-semester (n~aural~ = 13, n~written~ = 21) and 11 sixth-semester participants were included in the free recall analyses. The researchers included a perception questionnaire to gauge participants' sense of difficulty. Respondents were asked to select whether one passage was more difficult than the other and to select any and all []{#ch12.xhtml_p176 .pagebreak aria-label=" page 176. " role="doc-pagebreak"}reasons that explained difficulty. Six reasons were provided: text length, sentence length, prior knowledge, sentence complexity, and vocabulary complexity. After this question, participants reported whether the free recall, multiple-choice, or neither comprehension measure was more difficult. Finally, they ranked each of the six individual tasks (reading/listening, free recall, or multiple-choice) in order of their perceived difficulty.

Their quantitative results showed overwhelming evidence that the *Inflación* text in VanPatten's study was significantly easier to comprehend than the *Aztecas* text. These results were true for both the written and the aural modes and for intermediate and advanced learners in the study, and were confirmed by qualitative data, as the Aztecs text was also perceived to be more difficult to comprehend by 42 of the 46 participants who completed the perception questionnaire. They attributed difficulty to more complex vocabulary and to a lack of prior knowledge on the topic of the passage, but also to sentence structure and text length. Results also showed that the original assessment, a free recall task, was more fine-grained and could capture differences across proficiency levels and modality, which the less reliable multiple-choice task could not; also, those differences followed the expected pattern, i.e., more proficient students showed greater comprehension, and the written texts proved easier to understand than the aural texts. In contrast, the multiple-choice task could capture only differences between the passages, as well as a counterintuitive result: that the more difficult text was easier to comprehend when presented aurally, possibly because the more difficult text presented in the more difficult mode pushed participants to guess, further jeopardizing the reliability of the measurement. Sanz and McCormick concluded that the long and winding path that the original VanPatten study and its replications followed does not lead to 'mixed results', as the studies are not comparable. On the contrary, differences were to be expected. By entirely changing the meaning variable in the meaning vs. form competition of the original study, these conceptual replications made the extraction of meaning significantly more difficult with these effects compounded by the inclusion of lower-proficiency participants and by the requirement to verbalize while reading. Finally, the 10-point multiple choice task allowed for random guessing, a problem evidenced by the low reliability of Leow et al.'s multiple-choice test as reported in [Morgan-Short et al. (2018)](#ch12.xhtml_ref12_22){.biblioref role="doc-biblioref"}, [Szudarski and Mikolajczak (2022)](#ch12.xhtml_ref12_34){.biblioref role="doc-biblioref"}, and [Son et al. (2021)](#ch12.xhtml_ref12_33){.biblioref role="doc-biblioref"}.

[Son et al. (2021)](#ch12.xhtml_ref12_33){.biblioref role="doc-biblioref"} (n=75) followed Leow et al.'s design: written mode, same text, same forms, and same assessment tasks, but their study differed from it in significant ways. Participants were third-semester students of Spanish, rather than second semester, as their pilot made clear that comprehension scores for the latter were too low to expect any form--meaning competition to be observed. The study also controlled for proficiency across experimental groups, provided a practice task, and instructed participants in the experimental groups to keep their eyes on the assigned form for two seconds and to click on the response pad. In contrast with previous replications, and specifically Leow et al.'s study, attrition was low. Only six participants failed to meet the 60% cut off. The researchers used an Eyelink 1000 to track eye movements while participants read the passages on a computer monitor; this allowed the authors to contribute to the debate on L2 simultaneous attention to form and meaning by providing concurrent evidence of attention while avoiding reactivity associated with think-alouds ([Sanz et al., 2009](#ch12.xhtml_ref12_29){#ch12.xhtml_R_ref12_29 .biblioref role="doc-biblioref"}). Two types of behavior from the control and the experimental groups were compared: their scores on the comprehension test, as well as their eye movements throughout the entire text and on the target forms; i.e., the study compared both off-line, and on-line data, each reflecting product and processes, respectively.

The study yielded clear results. First, global reading processes were not affected by the instruction to check and look at the different forms, but the product of reading was; i.e., comprehension, as shown by the significant difference in comprehension scores between the control []{#ch12.xhtml_p177 .pagebreak aria-label=" page 177. " role="doc-pagebreak"}and two of the experimental groups. Specifically, negative effects on comprehension were large for participants who attended to purely grammatical bound (*-n*) and unbound morphemes (*la*). Finally, while proficiency did not relate to reading behavior, slower, more careful readers were better at extracting meaning from the text regardless of their attentional condition. In conclusion, results from the most recent replication, and the first to include an unobtrusive online measure of attention, run counter to those from the second strand of replications that started with Leow et al. The authors conclude that the requirement to focus on form results in "larger deficits in comprehension than have been assumed in recent years" (p. 25).

To conclude this section, the goal of VanPatten's original 1990 study was to shed light on the competition between attention to form and attention to meaning within a limited processor. The evidence the study produced is at the basis of several principles included in VanPatten's IP model: the lexical preference principle; the preference for non-redundancy principle; the meaning-before-non-meaning principle. [Son et al. (2021)](#ch12.xhtml_ref12_33){.biblioref role="doc-biblioref"}, the last in a series of replications of [VanPatten (1990)](#ch12.xhtml_ref12_36){.biblioref role="doc-biblioref"}, shows that when learners interact with the input, they go for meaning first, and only when basic processes have been automatized can learners allocate attentional resources to process form; this is especially true for less salient, less meaningful forms. More research is needed before we can conclude that the competition between form and meaning is more obvious in the aural mode or during constrained reading, when participants cannot reread a sentence and timing adds more pressure.
:::

::: {.section aria-labelledby="sec12_5"}
## Main Research Methods {#ch12.xhtml_sec12_5}

One of the takeaways of this chapter is the importance of implementing sound research methods in general, and in particular, in replication research that investigates the competition between form and meaning during online IP. Reading the previous sections on [VanPatten's (1990)](#ch12.xhtml_ref12_36){.biblioref role="doc-biblioref"} study and the multiple replications that have followed, one may have the impression that researchers have lost the forest for the trees, and that we place too much attention on the details involved in research methodology to the detriment of the 'big ideas' in VanPatten's IP model. In empirical research, however, big ideas are only as good as the methods to test them.

Online measures did not transfer to SLA from cognitive psychology until 12 years after VanPatten's publication of the IP model and 15 years after the first publication on PI ([VanPatten & Cadierno, 1993](#ch12.xhtml_ref12_39){#ch12.xhtml_R_ref12_39 .biblioref role="doc-biblioref"}). As noted in [Keating (2021)](#ch12.xhtml_ref12_15){#ch12.xhtml_R_ref12_15 .biblioref role="doc-biblioref"}, for decades, evidence in support of the IP model and PI, its pedagogical development, was characterized for being off-line, even though VanPatten's interest was the moment-to-moment making of form--meaning connections during online processing. Verbalizations had been, until recently, the only online measure included in the design of IP studies. Cost-free and easy to code, simultaneously thinking aloud while completing verbal tasks such as reading, is an example of dual task. As such, verbalizations have been shown to affect the very processes, in our case the competition between form and meaning, that they are supposed to inform us about (e.g. [Sanz et al., 2009](#ch12.xhtml_ref12_29){.biblioref role="doc-biblioref"}). For example, [Morgan-Short et al. (2012)](#ch12.xhtml_ref12_21){.biblioref role="doc-biblioref"} reported a statistically significant association between the requirement to verbalize and participant attrition; it is perhaps not surprising that of the replications discussed here, those that include verbalizations in their design are precisely those that fail to report any significant contrasts between conditions.

[VanPatten (1990)](#ch12.xhtml_ref12_36){.biblioref role="doc-biblioref"} and its subsequent replications relied on techniques to operationalize form and meaning that seem rather crude today. To keep track of the forms that participants 'noticed', researchers asked them to write a check mark, mark the text, or press a key whenever they noticed the form---a lexical item or a bound or unbound morpheme. A number of reports have appeared since [Leeser et al.'s (2021)](#ch12.xhtml_ref12_1){#ch12.xhtml_R_ref12_1 .biblioref role="doc-biblioref"} call for the implementation of online measures in PI and IP []{#ch12.xhtml_p178 .pagebreak aria-label=" page 178. " role="doc-pagebreak"}research. For example, in a series of eye-tracking studies ([Issa, 2019](#ch12.xhtml_ref12_12){.biblioref role="doc-biblioref"}; [Issa & Morgan-Short, 2019](#ch12.xhtml_ref12_13){#ch12.xhtml_R_ref12_13 .biblioref role="doc-biblioref"}; [Issa et al., 2015](#ch12.xhtml_ref12_14){#ch12.xhtml_R_ref12_14 .biblioref role="doc-biblioref"}), the authors investigate the effects of an explicit grammar component (or structured input (SI), in PI parlance) on the processing of pre-verbal direct object clitics. As indicated by higher fixation rates and longer total gaze time on the pronoun, SI had the effect of drawing the learner's attention to the form. However, the researchers warn against concluding that the increase in attention brought about by SI leads to higher learning gains. They also concluded that individual differences mediate the effects of SI, as higher working memory learners need to allocate less attention than low working memory individuals to reach the same linguistic outcome. Just as Issa et al.'s work contributes to our understanding of the effects of PI, [Son et al. (2021)](#ch12.xhtml_ref12_33){.biblioref role="doc-biblioref"} evidences the important contributions that eye-tracking data can make to the study of simultaneous attention to form and text meaning. For example, their results validate the offline measure of attention first introduced by VanPatten and implemented in all its replications, to circle, click, or underline the target form that seemed too crude. Eye movement behavior shows that, as intended, the experimental groups paid more conscious attention to their assigned target form than the control group.
:::

::: {.section aria-labelledby="sec12_6"}
## Recommendations for Research Practice {#ch12.xhtml_sec12_6}

A number of recommendations can be drawn from studies on FMCs that address good research practices. First, rather than criticizing ourselves for what has been called 'the replication crisis' in the social sciences, and given the importance of the integrity and reproducibility of research, we should encourage replication that is clearly defined according to what variables have been modified *to ensure comparability with the original study* ([Porte & McManus, 2019](#ch12.xhtml_ref12_24){#ch12.xhtml_R_ref12_24 .biblioref role="doc-biblioref"}). Results from the most recent replication of [VanPatten (1990)](#ch12.xhtml_ref12_36){.biblioref role="doc-biblioref"} by [Szudarski and Mikolajczak (2022)](#ch12.xhtml_ref12_34){.biblioref role="doc-biblioref"} further adds to the argument.

Second, IP and PI research, like other SLA research, would benefit from a mixed methods design and data triangulation, as in [Sanz and McCormick's (2021)](#ch12.xhtml_ref12_30){.biblioref role="doc-biblioref"} replication of replications, which combine participants' perceptions of text difficulty with actual comprehension data (see also Marijuan, this volume). Third, and as discussed in the previous section, of the different online measures reviewed in [Keating (2021)](#ch12.xhtml_ref12_15){.biblioref role="doc-biblioref"}, eye-tracking is particularly advantageous due to its unobstrusiveness compared to verbalizations and its high temporal resolution. While recall provides a picture of the product of comprehension, the data generated by the eye-tracker can track the true course of the activity it measures, namely, reading for comprehension while paying attention to form. Importantly, while the connection between eye-tracking data and acquisition has been questioned, the connection between eye movement and attention, the key variable in VanPatten's study, is better understood. Finally, we should start designing IP studies that are longitudinal, rather than only just cross-sectional, that include learners beyond second semester and that incorporate individual differences in their design, especially cognitive variables given the importance they carry in the IP model (see also discussion in Ruiz & Rebuschat, this volume).
:::

::: {.section aria-labelledby="sec12_7"}
## Recommendations for Teaching Practice {#ch12.xhtml_sec12_7}

Many of the questions posed by VanPatten underscore important issues in instructed SLA. Can form--meaning connections be taught? Can we teach learners to process grammatical form when their tendency is to process input for meaning due to limited processing resources? Can instruction change L2 learners' processing strategies? PI, a psycholinguistically motivated pedagogical []{#ch12.xhtml_p179 .pagebreak aria-label=" page 179. " role="doc-pagebreak"}intervention, was designed to address these questions. For example, as recommended in the guidelines for carrying out PI, target forms should be presented one at a time to assist learners in making form--meaning connections and avoid taxing learners' limited processing resources ([Lee & VanPatten, 2003](#ch12.xhtml_ref12_17){#ch12.xhtml_R_ref12_17 .biblioref role="doc-biblioref"}). For an in-depth discussion on how PI teaches L2 learners better strategies to make form--meaning connections, see Wong, this volume.

Issues of saliency, communicative value, and transfer, among others, coalesce to make these forms more difficult or less difficult to learn; this knowledge will raise the teachers' awareness of the challenge faced by learners. Not all grammar forms are the same: Spanish *ser/estar*, preterite/imperfect contrasts, the clitic system, and word order are examples of late acquired structures. These forms need special attention and extra feedback that can be provided by software packages for language learning with lessons that can be assigned for homework. Technology provides feedback to students that is individualized, immediate, and focused, which is arguably the best kind of feedback ([Sanz, 2004](#ch12.xhtml_ref12_28){#ch12.xhtml_R_ref12_28 .biblioref role="doc-biblioref"}). Individualized feedback addresses the student's needs, and when received in the privacy of the student's home, may lessen the likelihood of potentially raising the student's affective filter, while immediate feedback makes good use of the cognitive window of opportunity: it is not affected by working memory, as the learner's original answer is fresh in their mind and can be compared to the feedback.
:::

::: {.section aria-labelledby="sec12_8"}
## Future Directions {#ch12.xhtml_sec12_8}

Given the potential role of WM and executive control (EC) as moderators in the process of making form--meaning connections, and ultimately in the benefits from PI, a productive venue for this strand of research is to continue testing the IP model including measures of cognitive capacity such as WM and EC. This would, for example, address VanPatten's availability of resources principle ([VanPatten, 1996](#ch12.xhtml_ref12_37){.biblioref role="doc-biblioref"}), according to which, for learners to process either redundant meaningful grammatical forms or non-meaningful forms, the processing of the meaning of the sentence must not drain available processing resources. This procedure will also act as a comparison across replications, as it is possible that some of the differences in results between studies within the same strand ([Barcroft, 2001](#ch12.xhtml_ref12_5){.biblioref role="doc-biblioref"}; [Greenslade et al., 1999](#ch12.xhtml_ref12_9){.biblioref role="doc-biblioref"}; [VanPatten, 1990](#ch12.xhtml_ref12_36){.biblioref role="doc-biblioref"}; [Wong, 2001](#ch12.xhtml_ref12_40){.biblioref role="doc-biblioref"}) are due to differences in cognitive capacities between samples. The explanatory power would increase significantly too. For example, [Serafini and Sanz (2016)](#ch12.xhtml_ref12_32){.biblioref role="doc-biblioref"} found that during a semester of study, cognitive variables are a predictor of gains on Spanish morphological structures at lower proficiencies, but not higher proficiencies. Additionally, neuroimaging studies reveal higher engagement of cognitive control during low proficiency processing that is not obtained for higher proficiencies ([McCormick & Sanz, 2022](#ch12.xhtml_ref12_19){.biblioref role="doc-biblioref"}). Moving forward, it is important to consider how specific tasks and conditions (in both linguistic and WM measures) call upon specific executive control processes to better elucidate the contributions of specific control mechanisms to L2 processing and proficiency development ([Zalbidea & Sanz, 2020](#ch12.xhtml_ref12_41){#ch12.xhtml_R_ref12_41 .biblioref role="doc-biblioref"}).
:::
