[]{#ch13.xhtml}

::: {}
# []{#ch13.xhtml_p182 .pagebreak aria-label=" page 182. " role="doc-pagebreak"}[13 ]{.chnum .ordinal}[Lexical Preference in Input Processing]{.chtitle} {#ch13.xhtml_h1}

Justin P. White

Florida Atlantic University

Wynne Wong

The Ohio State University

DOI: [10.4324/9781003041412-17](https://dx.doi.org/10.4324/9781003041412-17){aria-label="D.O.I. link to this document."}
:::

::: {.section .introduction role="doc-introduction"}
## Introduction and Definitions {#ch13.xhtml_sec13_1}

A central aim of VanPatten's input processing (IP) theory (VanPatten, [1996](#ch13.xhtml_ref13_45){#ch13.xhtml_R_ref13_45 .biblioref role="doc-biblioref"}, [2020](#ch13.xhtml_ref13_48){#ch13.xhtml_R_ref13_48 .biblioref role="doc-biblioref"}) is to account for what learners attend to during language processing and to explain why. The theory makes predictions about the strategies that second language (L2) learners tend to use when processing input. These strategies are articulated as a set of IP principles (see Leeser, this volume). The lexical preference principle (LPP) is one of the principles of IP. This principle is based on the notion that because learners have a limited capacity to process information and cannot process all features of input at once, they tend to process input for meaning before form. While meaning and form are not mutually exclusive, [VanPatten (1996)](#ch13.xhtml_ref13_45){.biblioref role="doc-biblioref"} postulates that there may be contexts where form and meaning compete for processing resources: "when all else is equal, form and meaning compete for detection---with meaning generally winning out" (p. 288).

*Form* in VanPatten's theory refers to grammatical form, i.e., surface features of language such as verb inflections and functional items such as prepositions, articles, and pronouns ([VanPatten, 1996](#ch13.xhtml_ref13_45){.biblioref role="doc-biblioref"}). *Meaning* refers to real-world referential meaning. Given limited processing resources and the push to process input for meaning, the theory predicts that items in input that will help facilitate comprehension are the items that tend to get processed first. This implies that content words are the first features to get processed because these elements tend to carry the most meaning. This is reflected in VanPatten's primacy of content words principle (VanPatten, [2004](#ch13.xhtml_ref13_46){#ch13.xhtml_R_ref13_46 .biblioref role="doc-biblioref"}, [2020](#ch13.xhtml_ref13_48){.biblioref role="doc-biblioref"}).

As discussed in Leeser (this volume), the preference for processing content words before anything else has implications for the processing of grammatical markers that encode the same semantic information as content words such as verbal inflections for person/number, tense, etc. For example, tense morphology can co-occur with temporal adverbs. In "John walked to the store last week," past tense marker -ed and the content words "last week" encode the same semantic information but given learners' reliance on content words to get meaning, learners will rely on "last week" rather than -ed to interpret the tense of the sentence. This tendency to process lexical items before grammatical form is reflected in the LPP: "Learners process lexical items for meaning before grammatical forms when both encode the same semantic information" (VanPatten, [1996](#ch13.xhtml_ref13_45){.biblioref role="doc-biblioref"}, [2004](#ch13.xhtml_ref13_46){.biblioref role="doc-biblioref"}, [2020](#ch13.xhtml_ref13_48){.biblioref role="doc-biblioref"}).

[]{#ch13.xhtml_p183 .pagebreak aria-label=" page 183. " role="doc-pagebreak"}What happens if learners do not yet know what the lexical items mean? It is possible that learners will not process the redundant grammatical markers (e.g., -ed) until they have "processed and incorporated lexical forms such as 'yesterday' and 'last night'" ([VanPatten, 2020](#ch13.xhtml_ref13_48){.biblioref role="doc-biblioref"}, p. 108). This led VanPatten to include an alternative to the original LPP in later versions of the theory. The revised lexical preference principle states "If grammatical forms express a meaning that can also be encoded lexically (i.e., the grammatical marker is redundant), then learners will not initially process those grammatical forms until they have lexical forms to which they can match them" ([VanPatten, 2020](#ch13.xhtml_ref13_48){.biblioref role="doc-biblioref"}).

Therefore, as a consequence of their preference for processing lexical items (assuming they have these lexical items in mental representation), L2 learners may direct their processing resources away from some linguistic features which may be disadvantageous for building their implicit linguistic system.

This chapter discusses theory and research related to the LPP. We provide a historical overview of the LPP and discuss critical issues related to this principle including how the LPP impacts IP, forms that are impacted by the LPP, and the mechanisms that underlie L2 learners' choice to rely on lexical or grammatical cues during IP. We review LPP-related research and highlight commonly implemented research methods. We also address implications for practice and conclude with directions for future research.
:::

:::: {.section aria-labelledby="sec13_2"}
## Historical Perspectives {#ch13.xhtml_sec13_2}

The origins of the LPP are grounded in works from cognitive psychology and first language (L1) acquisition recognizing that learners selectively focus their attention during their processing of incoming linguistic data (e.g., [McLaughlin et al., 1983](#ch13.xhtml_ref13_31){#ch13.xhtml_R_ref13_31 .biblioref role="doc-biblioref"}; [Miesel, 1987](#ch13.xhtml_ref13_32){#ch13.xhtml_R_ref13_32 .biblioref role="doc-biblioref"}; [Schneider & Shiffrin, 1977](#ch13.xhtml_ref13_37){#ch13.xhtml_R_ref13_37 .biblioref role="doc-biblioref"}; [Slobin, 1985](#ch13.xhtml_ref13_39){#ch13.xhtml_R_ref13_39 .biblioref role="doc-biblioref"}). In L1 acquisition, [Slobin (1985)](#ch13.xhtml_ref13_39){.biblioref role="doc-biblioref"} states that "the only linguistic material that can figure in language-making are stretches of speech that attract the child's attention to a sufficient degree to be noticed and held in memory" (p. 1164). [McLaughlin et al. (1983)](#ch13.xhtml_ref13_31){.biblioref role="doc-biblioref"} posit, on the basis that humans have limited-capacity information processors, that learners can attend to input in either focal or peripheral ways; 'peripheral' referring to that information deemed not important or not relevant to the task and 'focal' being that information deemed important or relevant to the task. The task, effectively, refers to learners attending to the linguistic input in an effort to comprehend its meaning. That is after all, one of the purposes of communication---to comprehend a message in an incoming input stream during information exchange (i.e., interaction). As part of this process of decoding focal and peripheral elements, [Schapira (1978)](#ch13.xhtml_ref13_38){#ch13.xhtml_R_ref13_38 .biblioref role="doc-biblioref"} suggests that what may be 'stripped' (not attended to for the purpose of processing meaning) during processing can be entire words or morphologically bound items such as verb affixes. In short, language learners attend to the elements included in input that are necessary to comprehend meaning, and all others may not be initially processed given that they are not necessary for meaning comprehension and due to a limited capacity for processing. The results of VanPatten's ([1983](#ch13.xhtml_ref13_40){#ch13.xhtml_R_ref13_40 .biblioref role="doc-biblioref"}, [1984](#ch13.xhtml_ref13_41){#ch13.xhtml_R_ref13_41 .biblioref role="doc-biblioref"}) interview-based studies on adult learners of Spanish confirmed that their subjects focused on attending to the overall message while being interviewed based on observations that they ignored the forms used in the interview questions themselves as well as during recast type feedback. Effectively, learners' limited capacity processors affect their ability to simultaneously process both form and meaning, oftentimes to the detriment of linguistic form processing. [VanPatten (1984)](#ch13.xhtml_ref13_41){.biblioref role="doc-biblioref"} questioned how learners acquire structure during IP if their focus is indeed on meaning and therefore posited that an underlying learning strategy may be involved allowing this to take place.

[]{#ch13.xhtml_p184 .pagebreak aria-label=" page 184. " role="doc-pagebreak"}Building upon this idea of focal and peripheral attention, [VanPatten (1985)](#ch13.xhtml_ref13_42){#ch13.xhtml_R_ref13_42 .biblioref role="doc-biblioref"} noted that many meaning-bearing forms may also be expressed by lexical items (or phrases) found in the input, which lead to the creation of the construct of 'communicative value'. Communicative value refers to the contribution that a particular form makes to the overall sentence (or utterance) meaning and is based on both the absence or presence of inherent semantic value and redundancy. These two features function in a dichotomous relationship, creating four possibilities of +/- inherent semantic value and +/- redundancy. Some forms of verbal inflections (but not all, in all languages) are + inherent semantic value and are also at times accompanied by lexical markers such as temporal adverbs. This, in turn, creates a + inherent semantic value and + redundancy environment thereby reducing the communicative value of the inflection itself. In the following sentence in Spanish, "Victor estudiará en la biblioteca mañana" (Victor will study in the library tomorrow), the temporal adverb "mañana" (tomorrow) encodes the futureness of the sentence and therefore the verbal inflection in the verb form "estudiará" is considered redundant. In the same example in Spanish, but without the adverb "mañana" (tomorrow), the sentence, "Victor estudiará en la biblioteca" (Victor will study in the library), is now considered + inherent semantic meaning and - redundancy given that it is only the verbal inflection that encodes futureness. The end result for IP is that the more communicative value a form has, and when the input is devoid of redundancy, the more likely it will be attended to for meaning owing to the fact that the form itself is essential for accurate comprehension. These observations lead to the development of a series of models capturing what guides learners during IP (VanPatten, [1984](#ch13.xhtml_ref13_41){.biblioref role="doc-biblioref"}, [1985](#ch13.xhtml_ref13_42){.biblioref role="doc-biblioref"}, [1990](#ch13.xhtml_ref13_43){#ch13.xhtml_R_ref13_43 .biblioref role="doc-biblioref"}, [1995](#ch13.xhtml_ref13_44){#ch13.xhtml_R_ref13_44 .biblioref role="doc-biblioref"}) until the earliest, most-complete version first appearing in [VanPatten (1996)](#ch13.xhtml_ref13_45){.biblioref role="doc-biblioref"}. This initial version of the model in 1996 consisted of a set of processing principles that characterize the push to get meaning ([VanPatten, 1996](#ch13.xhtml_ref13_45){.biblioref role="doc-biblioref"}, p. 14):

1.  P1. Learners process input for meaning before they process it for form.

    1.  P1(a) Learners process content words in the input before anything else.
    2.  P1(b) Learners prefer processing lexical items to grammatical items (e.g., morphology) for the same semantic information.
    3.  P1(c) Learners prefer processing more meaningful morphology before less or nonmeaningful morphology.

2.  P2. For learners to process form that is not meaningful, they must be able to process information or communicative content at no (or little) cost to attention.

The principles related to the tendency to process input for meaning before form have undergone modifications since the first iteration of the model in 1996. In 2004, VanPatten reorganized the principles into one main principle underscoring the primacy of meaning with six subprinciples (for descriptions of the principles, see [VanPatten, 2004](#ch13.xhtml_ref13_46){.biblioref role="doc-biblioref"}, p. 14):

1.  Principle 1. The primacy of meaning principle.
    1.  Principle 1a. The primacy of content words principle.
    2.  Principle 1b. The lexical preference principle.
    3.  Principle 1c. The preference for nonredundancy principle
    4.  Principle 1d. The meaning-before-nonmeaning principle
    5.  Principle 1e. The availability of resources principle
    6.  Principle 1f. The sentence location principle.

[]{#ch13.xhtml_p185 .pagebreak aria-label=" page 185. " role="doc-pagebreak"}In the most recent 2020 version, the principles related to the primacy of meaning are articulated as follows:

- Primacy of content words principle
- Lexical preference principle
- Revised lexical preference principle
- Preference for nonredundancy principle
- Meaning before nonmeaning principle

::: {.section aria-labelledby="sec13_2_1"}
### Empirical Support for LPP {#ch13.xhtml_sec13_2_1}

[Lee et al. (1997)](#ch13.xhtml_ref13_30){#ch13.xhtml_R_ref13_30 .biblioref role="doc-biblioref"} demonstrated learners' preference for lexical over morphological cues by exposing them to aural passages with sentences containing either the Spanish past tense lexical markers paired with verbal inflections or sentences solely containing the verb final morpheme itself. Their results showed that learners reconstructed more past tense temporal references when their exposure included lexical markers than those whose exposure was limited to morphological markers. These results were replicated in the written mode by [Lee (1999)](#ch13.xhtml_ref13_29){#ch13.xhtml_R_ref13_29 .biblioref role="doc-biblioref"}. [Musumeci (1989)](#ch13.xhtml_ref13_33){#ch13.xhtml_R_ref13_33 .biblioref role="doc-biblioref"} found that the presence or absence of temporal adverbs was a significant predictor of correct tense assignment in processing L2 French and L2 Italian sentences, while [Glass (1994)](#ch13.xhtml_ref13_21){#ch13.xhtml_R_ref13_21 .biblioref role="doc-biblioref"} found similar results with discourse level input. [Bardovi-Harlig (1992)](#ch13.xhtml_ref13_4){#ch13.xhtml_R_ref13_4 .biblioref role="doc-biblioref"} found that beginning English as a second language (ESL) learners preferred to express temporal reference using adverbs rather than verbal morphology but as they progressed in their mastery of verbal inflections, they relied less on adverbs.

Rossamondo (2007) exposed learners to passages containing either the Spanish future tense accompanied by lexical temporal markers, or passages with only the verbal morphological cues to rely on for tense encoding. Results revealed that learners who were exposed to passages without lexical markers and who were thereby forced to rely on morphological markers, performed worse than those whose passages contained lexical temporal cues as measured by a post-passage multiple choice reading comprehension task regardless of the reading protocol (i.e., aloud or silent).

These studies show that when provided the option of either attending to the temporal adverbs or the morphological markers to encode tense, L2 learners prefer to rely on lexical temporal items, regardless of the modality and level of input. Furthermore, the pretests in many processing instruction (PI) studies also serve as evidence for the LPP in that they suggest that the presence of lexical cues impedes participants from processing the target grammatical forms (e.g., [Benati, 2001](#ch13.xhtml_ref13_6){#ch13.xhtml_R_ref13_6 .biblioref role="doc-biblioref"}; [Wong, 2004](#ch13.xhtml_ref13_50){#ch13.xhtml_R_ref13_50 .biblioref role="doc-biblioref"}). More recently, psycholinguistic studies using eye-tracking and self-paced reading (SPR) provide additional support for the LPP (e.g., [Cameron, 2011](#ch13.xhtml_ref13_10){#ch13.xhtml_R_ref13_10 .biblioref role="doc-biblioref"}; [Dracos & Henry, 2018](#ch13.xhtml_ref13_15){#ch13.xhtml_R_ref13_15 .biblioref role="doc-biblioref"}; [Ellis & Sagarra, 2010b](#ch13.xhtml_ref13_18){#ch13.xhtml_R_ref13_18 .biblioref role="doc-biblioref"}; [VanPatten & Keating, 2007](#ch13.xhtml_ref13_49){#ch13.xhtml_R_ref13_49 .biblioref role="doc-biblioref"}). These studies demonstrate that beginning and intermediate L2 learners rely more heavily on lexical rather than grammatical cues to make temporal reference decisions.
:::
::::

:::::::::: {.section aria-labelledby="sec13_3"}
## Critical Issues and Topics {#ch13.xhtml_sec13_3}

::: {.section aria-labelledby="sec13_3_1"}
### How Does the LPP Impact IP? {#ch13.xhtml_sec13_3_1}

Given that only processed input has the potential to impact language development, the LPP has consequences for SLA. If learners filter out grammatical forms in favor of lexical processing, language development could consequently be delayed.

[]{#ch13.xhtml_p186 .pagebreak aria-label=" page 186. " role="doc-pagebreak"}Besides the drive to process input for meaning, other factors also give lexical cues an advantage. Lexical cues tend to be more salient than grammatical forms. They have longer character length and are easier to segment in speech stream while morphological cues tend to be shorter and appear in less salient medial positions (see VanPatten's sentence location principle, Leeser, this volume). Grammatical forms also tend to be more homophonous than lexical items making them more ambiguous and less reliable cues. As posited by Bates and MacWhinney's competition model ([1981](#ch13.xhtml_ref13_5){#ch13.xhtml_R_ref13_5 .biblioref role="doc-biblioref"}), cue strength and cue reliability impact which cues ultimately win out during IP.
:::

::::::: {.section aria-labelledby="sec13_3_2"}
### What Forms Are Impacted by the LPP? {#ch13.xhtml_sec13_3_2}

The following are examples of some forms that are affected by the LPP.

::: {.section aria-labelledby="sec13_3_2_1"}
#### Tense Morphology {#ch13.xhtml_sec13_3_2_1}

In the Spanish sentence, *Ayer hablé con Juan* (Yesterday, I spoke with Juan), the lexical temporal marker *ayer* (yesterday) encodes the past tense aspect of the sentence and thus obviates learners' need to make the form--meaning connection between the verbal inflection and the tense of the sentence. When learners rely on the LPP, they will process *ayer* rather than the verbal inflection for tense.
:::

::: {.section aria-labelledby="sec13_3_2_2"}
#### Nominal Plural Markers {#ch13.xhtml_sec13_3_2_2}

Some languages have nominal plural markers to express "more than one" such as -s in English (e.g., books) or the plural inflection marker -i on nouns in Italian (e.g., *gatti* \[cats\]). When these plural inflection markers are accompanied by lexical forms that express the same meaning such as "**several** books," or "**cinque** \[five\] gatti," the LPP would predict that learners will process "several" and "cinque" for plurality rather than the grammatical markers.
:::

::: {.section aria-labelledby="sec13_3_2_3"}
#### Subjunctive Forms {#ch13.xhtml_sec13_3_2_3}

Subjunctive verbs encode meanings such as doubt, volition, or opinion. However, subjunctive verbs are also accompanied by main clauses that express the same information which render subjunctive forms redundant. In the French sentence, "*Paul doute que Marie fasse du yoga*," (Paul doubts that Marie does yoga), the LPP predicts that as soon as learners process "Paul doute que," they will already know the sentence is expressing doubt so there is no need to process "fasse" to interpret that the sentence is expressing doubt. This same processing issue for the subjunctive affects other languages such as Spanish and Italian.
:::

::: {.section aria-labelledby="sec13_3_2_4"}
#### Verbal Morphology {#ch13.xhtml_sec13_3_2_4}

Verbal morphology also encodes person (1^st^, 2^nd^, 3^rd^) and number (singular/plural). In the case of third-person singular -s in English, the -s signals that the subject of the verb is third person singular. However, since English is not a null-subject language, a third person singular noun or pronoun always accompanies the -s marker making this form redundant: "Anna goes to the store often." ESL learners thus will derive the meaning of third-person singular from the noun or pronoun (Anna) and consequently may skip over the -s marker during IP.
:::
:::::::

::: {.section aria-labelledby="sec13_3_3"}
### []{#ch13.xhtml_p187 .pagebreak aria-label=" page 187. " role="doc-pagebreak"}LPP: Universal Strategy or L1 Influence? {#ch13.xhtml_sec13_3_3}

What is it that drives learners to attend to lexical items for meaning and to not rely on grammatical form during processing? Questions have been raised as to whether the lexical preference processing strategy might be a universal strategy or if it is influenced by L1 transfer. [VanPatten and Keating (2007)](#ch13.xhtml_ref13_49){.biblioref role="doc-biblioref"} used an eye-tracking protocol with L1 English learners of L2 Spanish at varying levels (beginning, intermediate, and advanced) and native L1 Spanish speakers. Participants were exposed to congruent sentences in which the temporal adverb matched the verb tense (e.g., "Now I am talking to John") or incongruent sentences in which the temporal adverb did not match (e.g., "\*Yesterday, I am talking to John"). They found that beginning-level and intermediate-level L1 English Spanish learners "regressed" to the adverbs (they looked at them again), thereby demonstrating reliance on the lexical items. However, these results differed from those of the advanced-level L2 learners and Spanish native speakers who exhibited similar behavior; both groups "regressed" to the verb forms for sentence resolution. In this case, the advanced-level L2 learners performed similarly to native speakers thereby demonstrating their adoption of 'native-like' processing. In the second part of the study, they tracked L1 Spanish speakers of L2 English (beginning-level) during exposure to English congruent and incongruent sentences. In this case, they found that these L1 Spanish speakers did not rely on the verbal inflections for tense during English sentence processing (as they did during exposure to Spanish sentences), but instead relied on temporal adverbs much like the beginning and intermediate-level L2 learners of Spanish. Both studies conclude that beginning-level and intermediate-level L2 learners (but not advanced learners), regardless of whether their L1 was Spanish or English, followed the predictions of the LPP and relied on lexical markers instead of verbal inflections for meaning which according to [VanPatten (2020)](#ch13.xhtml_ref13_48){.biblioref role="doc-biblioref"} suggests "this strategy is universal and not dependent on L1 experience" (p. 109).

Ellis and Sagarra's ([2010a](#ch13.xhtml_ref13_17){#ch13.xhtml_R_ref13_17 .biblioref role="doc-biblioref"}, [2011](#ch13.xhtml_ref13_19){#ch13.xhtml_R_ref13_19 .biblioref role="doc-biblioref"}) research on learned attention and blocking from an associative learning perspective offers insight into L2 learners' tendency to rely on lexical cues for temporality. Associative learning theory ([Kamin, 1969](#ch13.xhtml_ref13_26){#ch13.xhtml_R_ref13_26 .biblioref role="doc-biblioref"}; [Kruschke & Blair, 2000](#ch13.xhtml_ref13_27){#ch13.xhtml_R_ref13_27 .biblioref role="doc-biblioref"}) posits that once a cue has been learned to be a reliable predictor of some outcome, learners will have difficulty learning subsequent cues because this initial learning or *learned attention* will *block* subsequent attempts to learn new cues. As discussed in [Ellis and Sagarra (2011)](#ch13.xhtml_ref13_19){.biblioref role="doc-biblioref"}, learned attention from L1 cues impedes the learning of other cues that may also be relevant: "L1-derived knowledge that there are reliable lexical cues to temporal reference (words like *gestern, hier, ayer*, yesterday) might block the acquisition of verb tense morphology from analysis of utterances such as yesterday I walked" (p. 591). They point out that this blocking is not limited to tense. It has been documented that naturalistic L2 learners tend to omit plural -s endings on nouns when preceded by lexical quantifiers ([Pica, 1983](#ch13.xhtml_ref13_34){#ch13.xhtml_R_ref13_34 .biblioref role="doc-biblioref"}). [Jiang (2004)](#ch13.xhtml_ref13_25){#ch13.xhtml_R_ref13_25 .biblioref role="doc-biblioref"} found that late Chinese learners of English tend to focus on the plurality of the subject rather than on the verb inflection.

In [Ellis and Sagarra (2010a)](#ch13.xhtml_ref13_17){.biblioref role="doc-biblioref"}, native English speakers learned a small number of Latin (a morphologically rich language) phrases with English translations in one of three conditions: adverb pretraining, verb pretraining, or no pretraining. Adverb pretraining consisted of learning two adverbs: *hodie* "today" and *heri* "yesterday." The verb pretraining group learned two verbs: *cogito* "I think" and *cogitavi* "I thought." The control group had no pretraining. Results revealed that participants who were trained with adverbs relied heavily on adverbs to make their judgments while those trained with verbs relied on verbal inflections. The untrained control group relied on both cues almost equally.

[Ellis and Sagarra (2011)](#ch13.xhtml_ref13_19){.biblioref role="doc-biblioref"} replicated their 2010 study with a more complex verbal paradigm. Instead of just one verb form (first person) for each tense, the study used three tenses and three []{#ch13.xhtml_p188 .pagebreak aria-label=" page 188. " role="doc-pagebreak"}persons (I, you, he). In the second experiment of the study, they compared speakers of a morphologically rich language (Spanish, Russian), a language that is morphologically poor (English) and a language that does not mark tense by verbal inflections (Chinese). While the 2010 study demonstrated a pretraining bias for one cue over the other, with a more complex verbal paradigm, the 2011 study revealed that all three groups relied more heavily on adverbs including the verb pretraining and the control group. When different L1s were compared, results showed that in general, when both a verbal cue and an adverb were present, speakers, regardless of their L1, were more influenced by the adverb than they were by the verbal inflections. However, speakers of Chinese relied on the adverbs more than L1 speakers of English, Spanish, and Russian, who were more sensitive to inflectional cues.

Studies by Ellis and Sagarra support associative learning theory in that they have evidence to demonstrate that early learned cues (from the pretraining phase of [Ellis and Sagarra, 2010a](#ch13.xhtml_ref13_17){.biblioref role="doc-biblioref"}) can block the learning of subsequent cues (i.e., those pretrained with adverbs prefer adverbs while those trained with verbs prefer verbal inflections). However, as complexity increases ([Ellis & Sagarra, 2011](#ch13.xhtml_ref13_19){.biblioref role="doc-biblioref"}) and processing resources are taxed (which tends to be the case with beginning learners), regardless of previously learned cues, learners will go for the more salient and reliable cue, i.e., lexical items. This is not incompatible with VanPatten's position that there is a strong drive for learners to process input for meaning during IP. Cues that are higher in communicative value tend to be more salient. In order for learners to process forms that are not meaningful or redundant, "they must be able to process information or communicative content at no or little cost to attentional resources" [VanPatten, 1996](#ch13.xhtml_ref13_45){.biblioref role="doc-biblioref"}, p. 421). Thus, reliance on lexical cues appears to be a default universal strategy when processing resources are taxed. However, reliance on the LPP is also modulated by the L1 since the degree to which learners will rely on lexical cues is impacted by how morphologically rich or poor their L1 is.
:::
::::::::::

::::: {.section aria-labelledby="sec13_4"}
## Current Contributions and Research {#ch13.xhtml_sec13_4}

One line of research related to the LPP is the strand that examines L2 learners' cue preference, lexical or grammatical, during IP. Another investigates what can override or impact reliance on the LPP.

::: {.section aria-labelledby="sec13_4_1"}
### Cue Preference {#ch13.xhtml_sec13_4_1}

In their research on blocking under an associative learning framework, [Ellis and Sagarra (2010b)](#ch13.xhtml_ref13_18){.biblioref role="doc-biblioref"} used eye-tracking to study how L1 English learners of Spanish processed adverb--verb and verb--adverb sentences that were either congruent or incongruent in a classroom setting. Participants were beginning and intermediate English learners of Spanish, English monolinguals, and Spanish monolinguals. They read sentences that consisted of four types: (1) past adverb--past verb, (2) \*past adverb--present verb, (3) past verb--past adverb, and (4) \*present verb--past adverb. Overall, results revealed that Spanish monolinguals whose L1 is morphologically rich tended to rely on morphological cues to resolve incongruent sentences while English monolinguals (morphologically poor) and late English-Spanish learners relied on adverbs. Ellis and Sagarra conclude these findings demonstrate long-term learned attention effects: "L1 experience that lexical cues are simpler and more reliable in their expression than morphological ones blocks the acquisition of morphological cues in a language that more extensively uses these than lexical cues" (p. 99). However, intermediate learners regressed less to adverbs than beginners which suggests that more experience with a morphologically rich L2 leads to higher levels of inflectional cue use.

[]{#ch13.xhtml_p189 .pagebreak aria-label=" page 189. " role="doc-pagebreak"}[Sagarra and Ellis (2013)](#ch13.xhtml_ref13_501){#ch13.xhtml_R_ref13_501 .biblioref role="doc-biblioref"} investigated whether [Ellis and Sagarra's (2010b)](#ch13.xhtml_ref13_18){.biblioref role="doc-biblioref"} English-Spanish learners' reliance on adverbs was due to a default or an L1-based strategy. To address this question, they compared intermediate and advanced English (morphologically impoverished) and Romanian (morphologically rich) learners of Spanish and English, Romanian, and Spanish monolinguals. Participants read sentences similar to the four types in [Ellis and Sagarra (2010b)](#ch13.xhtml_ref13_18){.biblioref role="doc-biblioref"} (sentences were translated into English and Romanian for the English and Romanian monolinguals) and selected the picture that best matched the sentence they read while their eye movements were tracked. Eye-tracking results showed that while these intermediate and advanced learners were all sensitive to tense incongruencies, the Romanian learners of L2 Spanish looked longer at verbs in incongruent adverb--verb sentences than intermediate and advanced English learners of L2 Spanish. The advanced Romanian learners also regressed longer to verbs with verb--adverb incongruencies than advanced English learners. These results thus support L1 transfer effects at the intermediate and advanced levels.

[Sagarra's (2021)](#ch13.xhtml_ref13_36){#ch13.xhtml_R_ref13_36 .biblioref role="doc-biblioref"} eye-tracking study investigated lexical/grammatical cue preference with subject--verb number agreement. Intermediate and advanced English and Romanian learners of L2 Spanish and Spanish monolinguals read sentences with adjacent subject--verb number congruencies and incongruencies while their eye movements were tracked. To assess whether working memory (WM) impacts cue preference, participants were also given a WM test. Results revealed that while all groups were sensitive to subject--verb number violations, how groups processed the sentences was influenced by their L1, i.e., the less morphologically rich the L1 (i.e., English), the greater the reliance on subjects. Proficiency level was also a factor because the more advanced the learners were, the more they relied on verbs. Finally, WM was also a factor. Sagarra found that higher memory spans led to more sensitivity to violations.

Taken together, this line of research suggests that cue preference (lexical or grammatical) is modulated by learners' L1, their proficiency level, and WM constraints. Given the universal drive to get meaning, in L2 contexts where processing resources tend to be more taxing (especially for beginners), learners will rely on lexical items when available because these are more reliable cues to help them get meaning.
:::

::: {.section aria-labelledby="sec13_4_2"}
### Overriding the LPP {#ch13.xhtml_sec13_4_2}

Another strand of research examines whether reliance on the LPP can be overridden or destabilized. [Cintrón-Valentín and Ellis (2016)](#ch13.xhtml_ref13_12){#ch13.xhtml_R_ref13_12 .biblioref role="doc-biblioref"}, for example, explored whether the "learned attentional biases" reported in the studies by Ellis and Sagarra (and others) could be overcome by instructional interventions to "retune attention" (p. 201). L1 English speakers learned Latin sentences similar to those in [Ellis and Sagarra (2011)](#ch13.xhtml_ref13_19){.biblioref role="doc-biblioref"} but under three form-focused conditions: (1) explicit instruction (EI), (2) bolding/coloring verbal inflectional cues via textual enhancement (TE), and (3) verb pretraining (as used in [Ellis and Sagarra, 2011](#ch13.xhtml_ref13_19){.biblioref role="doc-biblioref"}). Results showed that EI and TE were successful in retuning attention. The control condition was more influenced by adverbs than by verbs (replicating [Ellis & Sagarra, 2011](#ch13.xhtml_ref13_19){.biblioref role="doc-biblioref"}) while the three form-focused groups were more influenced by verbs.

Given that classroom input may contain overuses of lexical cues to L2 learners (e.g., [Dracos, 2010](#ch13.xhtml_ref13_14){#ch13.xhtml_R_ref13_14 .biblioref role="doc-biblioref"}; [Goodall, 2010](#ch13.xhtml_ref13_22){#ch13.xhtml_R_ref13_22 .biblioref role="doc-biblioref"}), [LaBrozzi (2012)](#ch13.xhtml_ref13_28){#ch13.xhtml_R_ref13_28 .biblioref role="doc-biblioref"} examined whether exposure to naturalistic input in a study abroad (SA) experience might override learners' preference for lexical cues. The study compared L1 English speaking intermediate learners of Spanish who had recently returned from a 16-week SA experience with a comparable group of learners without SA. Participants read sentences where verbs and adverbs were either congruent or incongruent and answered comprehension questions based on the content (not grammaticality) of sentences. While the []{#ch13.xhtml_p190 .pagebreak aria-label=" page 190. " role="doc-pagebreak"}participants without SA continued to rely on lexical cues in adverb-verb incongruent sentences, the SA participants started to rely on morphological cues along with lexical cues to assign temporal reference suggesting that they were in a transitional stage. LaBrozzi concludes that exposing learners to naturalistic input in a study abroad (SA) context can "start to alter their L2 processing strategies to mirror those of native speakers of the target language and come to rely on morphological cues more and lexical ones less" (p. 239).

[VanPatten's (1996)](#ch13.xhtml_ref13_45){.biblioref role="doc-biblioref"} processing instruction (PI) has overall been found to be an effective intervention to override learners' preference for lexical items over grammatical forms. PI aims to alter learners' default processing strategies to maximize the creation of intake during IP. In the case of the LPP, PI uses structured input (SI) activities to push learners to rely on form rather than lexical cues for meaning (see Wong, this volume). Target structures investigated under the LPP under the PI framework include temporal reference (e.g., [Angelovska & Roehm, 2020](#ch13.xhtml_ref13_3){#ch13.xhtml_R_ref13_3 .biblioref role="doc-biblioref"}; [Benati, 2001](#ch13.xhtml_ref13_6){.biblioref role="doc-biblioref"}; [Benati & Angelovska, 2015](#ch13.xhtml_ref13_7){#ch13.xhtml_R_ref13_7 .biblioref role="doc-biblioref"}; [Benati & Lee, 2010](#ch13.xhtml_ref13_9){#ch13.xhtml_R_ref13_9 .biblioref role="doc-biblioref"}; [Chan, 2018](#ch13.xhtml_ref13_11){#ch13.xhtml_R_ref13_11 .biblioref role="doc-biblioref"}), French negation and articles ([Wong, 2004](#ch13.xhtml_ref13_50){.biblioref role="doc-biblioref"}), subjunctive (e.g., [Fernández, 2008](#ch13.xhtml_ref13_20){#ch13.xhtml_R_ref13_20 .biblioref role="doc-biblioref"} \[Spanish\]), Russian directional/locational expressions ([Comer & deBenedette, 2011](#ch13.xhtml_ref13_13){#ch13.xhtml_R_ref13_13 .biblioref role="doc-biblioref"}), and person--number verb agreement (Dracos & Henry, [2018](#ch13.xhtml_ref13_15){.biblioref role="doc-biblioref"}, [2021](#ch13.xhtml_ref13_16){#ch13.xhtml_R_ref13_16 .biblioref role="doc-biblioref"}). The majority of studies conducted to date have compared SI with output-oriented interventions or they have isolated the variable of EI. Additionally, two studies ([Benati & Lee, 2007](#ch13.xhtml_ref13_8){#ch13.xhtml_R_ref13_8 .biblioref role="doc-biblioref"}) have further enhanced target forms in SI by typographically or aurally modifying them for potential increased salience (e.g., visual or perceptual) and one study ([Fernández, 2008](#ch13.xhtml_ref13_20){.biblioref role="doc-biblioref"}) tracked learner behavior during input exposure via "trials-to-criterion", response time, and accuracy after having reached criterion. More recently, [Dracos and Henry (2021)](#ch13.xhtml_ref13_16){.biblioref role="doc-biblioref"} used self-paced reading to observe L2 Spanish learners' processing of verbal morphology in real time and to investigate whether working memory (WM) modulates the effects of training with PI. Offline results showed that PI led to more accurate interpretations of both person-number and adverb--tense agreement and that higher WM did not lead to higher gains. However, SPR results revealed that PI did not increase sensitivity to either subject--verb or adverb--verb tense agreements. Among those who received PI, this online measure also showed WM enhanced sensitivity to violations under certain conditions (see [Dracos and Henry, 2021](#ch13.xhtml_ref13_16){.biblioref role="doc-biblioref"}, for discussion). Thus, while offline measures consistently show that PI can override reliance on the LPP, its effect on online processing is less clear.
:::
:::::

::: {.section aria-labelledby="sec13_5"}
## Main Research Methods {#ch13.xhtml_sec13_5}

In research under an associative learning framework examining learners' tendency to rely on lexical or grammatical cues during IP, L2 learners and native speakers of morphologically rich and impoverished languages are typically given sentences containing both lexical and grammatical cues that are congruent and incongruent to process for temporality (e.g., [Ellis & Sagarra, 2010a](#ch13.xhtml_ref13_17){.biblioref role="doc-biblioref"}) or person--number agreement (e.g., [Sagarra, 2021](#ch13.xhtml_ref13_36){.biblioref role="doc-biblioref"}) to examine which is their preferred cue. Some of these studies have also looked at whether some kind of training (e.g., verb pretraining, grammar instruction, TE) could destabilize their preferred L1 cues ([Cintrón-Valentín & Ellis, 2016](#ch13.xhtml_ref13_12){.biblioref role="doc-biblioref"}). This line of research has used both behavioral (e.g., cue reliance tasks \[[Dracos & Henry, 2018](#ch13.xhtml_ref13_15){.biblioref role="doc-biblioref"}\]) and online instruments (e.g., self-paced reading, eye-tracking).

Research that focuses on whether PI can help L2 learners process (redundant) grammatical forms have typically involved offline pre-treatment tests, treatment sessions, posttests immediately after treatment, and at times delayed posttests (two to four weeks after treatment) that measure interpretation (e.g., picture-selection \[[Comer & DeBenedette, 2011](#ch13.xhtml_ref13_13){.biblioref role="doc-biblioref"}) and production performance. More []{#ch13.xhtml_p191 .pagebreak aria-label=" page 191. " role="doc-pagebreak"}recently, self-paced reading has been used to observe learners' IP in real time (e.g., [Dracos & Henry, 2021](#ch13.xhtml_ref13_16){.biblioref role="doc-biblioref"}). (See also Ruiz & Rebuschat, this volume, for a review of PI research methods.)

Research using these different methods supports that L2 learners rely on the LPP when processing input that contains redundant grammatical forms. In terms of whether PI can override learners' reliance on this strategy, offline methods measuring accuracy consistently show that this intervention is successful. However, strategy change is not always demonstrated with online measures such as SPR as evidenced in [Dracos and Henry (2021)](#ch13.xhtml_ref13_16){.biblioref role="doc-biblioref"}. Research that uses both behavioral and online measures would provide more robust empirical evidence to help understand the processing strategies that learners use and whether certain interventions are effective in helping them process input better (see Marijuan, this volume).
:::

::: {.section aria-labelledby="sec13_6"}
## Recommendations for Practice {#ch13.xhtml_sec13_6}

As established throughout this chapter, learners' default processing strategy is to rely on lexical items (when available) for meaning, especially when comprehension is more effortful. Given this, if the goal of instruction is to acquire grammatical form, instructors need to ensure that input is comprehensible so that learners will be able to allocate attention to form. If processing input for meaning is too taxing due to unknown vocabulary, learners may have more difficulty processing grammatical cues.

Any instructional intervention that is expected to facilitate successful acquisition must take learners' processing mechanisms into account by pushing them to make form--meaning connections with the linguistic features themselves. We already discussed that textual enhancement (TE) can be effective in reallocating learners' attention from lexical to morphological cues ([Cintrón-Valentín & Ellis, 2016](#ch13.xhtml_ref13_12){.biblioref role="doc-biblioref"}). Given that research also shows that intensive exposure to morphological cues in a study abroad context can be beneficial in overriding learners' preference for lexical cues (e.g., [LaBrozzi, 2012](#ch13.xhtml_ref13_28){.biblioref role="doc-biblioref"}), instructors could use input flood (IF) along with TE to give learners extra doses of enhanced input in the classroom to help them attend to form.

SI activities under the PI framework have consistently been shown to be effective in overriding reliance on the LPP on accuracy measures. Input in SI is structured to ensure that learners must rely on grammatical and not lexical cues to interpret the meaning that the forms encode. Below is an example of a referential SI activity.

Indicate whether each action about Simone occurred last month or if it is something she currently does regularly. Was Simone more or less active last month than usual?

  --------------------- ------------ ----------------
  Simone                Last month   Does regularly
  1\. watches TV.       ◻            ◻
  2\. jogged 6 miles.   ◻            ◻
  3\. played tennis.    ◻            ◻
  4\. takes naps.       ◻            ◻
  --------------------- ------------ ----------------

Notice that learners cannot rely on adverbs to complete the task. They must process verbal morphology to determine the temporal reference of each sentence. The question asking whether Simone was more or less active last month ensures that learners must also focus on meaning.

Below are sample items for SI activities for verbal morphology and plural -s respectively. In each activity, learners must rely on grammatical rather than on lexical cues to arrive at correct answers.

<aside aria-label="box13_1" class="box ruled">

[]{#ch13.xhtml_p192 .pagebreak aria-label=" page 192. " role="doc-pagebreak"}Indicate whether each view expressed belongs to John or to John's classmates. Is John more conservative or more liberal than his classmates?

  --------- ------------------- ------------------------------------
  John...   His classmates...   
  \_\_\_    \_\_\_              want to legalize marijuana.
  \_\_\_    \_\_\_              thinks abortion should be illegal.
  \_\_\_    \_\_\_              support same-sex marriages.
  \_\_\_    \_\_\_              despises women's rights.
  --------- ------------------- ------------------------------------

Pay attention to each noun to determine how many of each item Samira is taking with her on her trip. Is Samira traveling to a warm or to a cold climate?

1.  ...sweaters

    - \_\_ one
    - \_\_ five

2.  ...shirt

    - \_\_ three
    - \_\_ one

3.  ...belt

    - \_\_ one
    - \_\_ three

4.  ...coats

    - \_\_ one
    - \_\_ four

</aside>

See Wong (this volume) for an activity on the subjunctive and for more detailed information on creating SI.

For instructors who wish to integrate plurilingual instruction into their teaching, they could explore with students how different L1s in the class encode temporal aspect. In realizing that some languages encode tense in more than one way, students may be more likely to consider other cues besides lexical ones to interpret and express tense. For example, Chinese learners whose L1 does not encode tense morphologically may have a tendency to skip over verbal inflections during L2 IP. However, as a result of engaging in crosslinguistic comparisons, they may become more inclined to consider inflections when assigning tense. Furthermore, this practice validates the different L1s in the classroom and students' plurilingual identities.
:::

:::::::: {.section aria-labelledby="sec13_7"}
## Future Directions {#ch13.xhtml_sec13_7}

::: {.section aria-labelledby="sec13_7_1"}
### Innovation in Instrumentation {#ch13.xhtml_sec13_7_1}

Given the longstanding track record of LPP research comparing pre-treatment measures with post-treatment measures, and given that IP, in general, is interested in 'the moment-by-moment computation of sentence structure' ([VanPatten, 2015](#ch13.xhtml_ref13_47){#ch13.xhtml_R_ref13_47 .biblioref role="doc-biblioref"}, p. 92), future research measuring processing []{#ch13.xhtml_p193 .pagebreak aria-label=" page 193. " role="doc-pagebreak"}using online tasks and tools is needed. Future studies should embark on this endeavor by returning to previous studies, resurrecting their materials, and conducting further investigations by incorporating more modern instrumentation such as eye-tracking devices, SPR measures (e.g., [Dracos & Henry, 2021](#ch13.xhtml_ref13_16){.biblioref role="doc-biblioref"}), electroencephalography/event-related potentials (EEG/ERPs), and neuroimaging along with tasks targeting online processing behavior. Additionally, the scope of L2s, target linguistic features, and L1s can be expanded. Some of the insights that these online measures can offer are determining at which point(s) in language development do we begin to see changes in L2 learners' processing preferences? Do they suddenly abandon their lexical preference processing strategy, or does it slowly begin to disappear? What sorts of exposure and training can influence learners' cue preference, and is there a distinct turning point at which they adopt native-like processing tendencies? And, does it reappear at any time, or are the effects durative over time? Are these effects similar across linguistic features and L1s, or are there differences? In one eye-tracking study, [VanPatten and Keating (2007)](#ch13.xhtml_ref13_49){.biblioref role="doc-biblioref"} found that native Spanish speakers relied on verb forms (regressed) to resolve adverb/verb incongruencies but L2 learners did not. With that in mind, what sorts of similarities/differences would we find across the range of heritage learners (HL) levels, L2 learners, and native speakers? What sorts of cue preference would we see with this speaker population? [Potowski et al. (2009)](#ch13.xhtml_ref13_35){#ch13.xhtml_R_ref13_35 .biblioref role="doc-biblioref"} found positive effects for PI on L2 and HL learners using offline measures targeting the Spanish subjunctive. The use of online tasks would allow us to determine their more precise effects on processing during treatment exposure. Is HL learners' lexical preference strategy affected sooner or differently than that of L2 learners? Would we find differences if their heritage language were English and their dominant language were Spanish given that they differ in morphological richness? Or, are the effects similar?
:::

::: {.section aria-labelledby="sec13_7_2"}
### Longer Term Effects of PI {#ch13.xhtml_sec13_7_2}

Research conducted under the PI framework has been promising in that the findings consistently show that the LPP can be destabilized by having learners engage in SI. However, because the majority of these studies are short term studies, we have little data on the durability of such effects. Future research needs to implement delayed posttests beyond two-four weeks to investigate long terms effects of PI on overriding the LPP.
:::

::: {.section aria-labelledby="sec13_7_3"}
### Contextualized Instruction {#ch13.xhtml_sec13_7_3}

Previous research has primarily included treatment materials and assessment measures in formats void of additional non-verbal linguistic cues. Given that many natural interactions using language include additional simultaneous cues (i.e., hand gestures, facial expressions, varied intonation), future research can include materials that mimic natural spontaneous language use and exposure. Language processing involves decoding linguistic data along with using non-verbal linguistic data in order to infer meaning and therefore it stands to reason that these additional cues might contribute to enhancing language processing. One early study, [Musumeci (1989)](#ch13.xhtml_ref13_33){.biblioref role="doc-biblioref"}, included non-verbal cues (hand gestures signaling past tense by motioning backwards or forwards for future tense) during instruction paired with temporal adverbs and verbs marked morphologically in French, Spanish, and Italian. Although they found no significant differences in their results by adding extralinguistic cues, further research might unveil additional findings in other languages and target forms or with different paralinguistic or extralinguistic cues. More recently, [Henry (2022)](#ch13.xhtml_ref13_23){#ch13.xhtml_R_ref13_23 .biblioref role="doc-biblioref"} and [Henry et al. (2017)](#ch13.xhtml_ref13_24){#ch13.xhtml_R_ref13_24 .biblioref role="doc-biblioref"} found that prosodic cues can impact syntactic processing on structures affected by the first-noun principle. Future research should examine whether prosody also impacts the processing of structures affected by the LPP.

[]{#ch13.xhtml_p194 .pagebreak aria-label=" page 194. " role="doc-pagebreak"}Relatedly, the issue of modality type needs to be further explored (see also Leow & Li, this volume). [Ellis and Sagarra (2011)](#ch13.xhtml_ref13_19){.biblioref role="doc-biblioref"} found that when input became more complex and processing resources become more taxed, there was an even greater tendency for L2 learners to rely on the LPP. Since processing input in the aural mode can be more challenging than processing input in the written mode, learners may rely more heavily on the LPP in the aural than in the written mode. This remains to be empirically investigated.

[Angelovska and Roehm (2020)](#ch13.xhtml_ref13_3){.biblioref role="doc-biblioref"} found a slight advantage for written input immediately after exposure. Would this effect be mediated when pairing aural input with paralinguistic or extralinguistic cues? And, if so, are form--meaning connections enhanced by such additional cues? To further contribute to the body of research, future research can include cues such as facial expressions, gestures, and other forms of corporeal language paired with aural linguistic input (with paralinguistic modifications) in order to explore the possible effects of their inclusion.
:::

::: {.section aria-labelledby="sec13_7_4"}
### Learner Variables {#ch13.xhtml_sec13_7_4}

Future research needs to not only continue to explore the instructional effects of input modality type with other structures related to the LPP in isolation as we have seen in [Angelovska and Roehm (2020)](#ch13.xhtml_ref13_3){.biblioref role="doc-biblioref"}, but also look at the interaction between individual learner differences and input modality types. What may be the most insight-producing aspect of investigating the effects of individual differences is how these characteristics respond with different modality types and combinations thereof. By pairing input modality types with individual differences, we might find answers to questions such as: How do learners representing a range of WM capacities and proficiency levels respond to written input, aural input, and combinations of both? Does WM or proficiency level function differently as indicators regarding which modality of input might best serve these learners?
:::

::: {.section aria-labelledby="sec13_7_5"}
### Revised Lexical Preference Principle {#ch13.xhtml_sec13_7_5}

The revised LPP states "If grammatical forms express a meaning that can also be encoded lexically (i.e., the grammatical marker is redundant), then learners will not initially process those grammatical forms until they have lexical forms to which they can match them" ([VanPatten, 2020](#ch13.xhtml_ref13_48){.biblioref role="doc-biblioref"}). Research to date has not empirically investigated what happens when learners do not yet have lexical forms to match redundant grammatical markers. If learners cannot comprehend the lexical cue, will the grammatical cue remain unprocessed as the revised LPP predicts? Or could they rely on other cues such as prosody (e.g., [Henry, 2022](#ch13.xhtml_ref13_23){.biblioref role="doc-biblioref"}), gestures, or context?

Along the same vein, if learners are taught the meaning of grammatical cues, could they subsequently use this knowledge to learn the meaning of lexical cues? In other words, can learned grammatical cues potentially facilitate lexical learning? For example, once ESL learners learn the past perfect tense (via explicit instruction or some other means), could they use this knowledge to learn the word *hitherto* in processing sentences such as the following?: "John had *hitherto* been afraid of flying."
:::
::::::::

::: {.section .conclusion role="doc-conclusion"}
## Summary/Conclusion {#ch13.xhtml_sec13_8}

This chapter has explored theory and research related to the LPP. The research reviewed supports that while reliance on the LPP appears to be universal in L2 IP, other factors such as L1 influence, and proficiency level modulate L2 learners' tendency to rely on lexical over []{#ch13.xhtml_p195 .pagebreak aria-label=" page 195. " role="doc-pagebreak"}grammatical cues. We hope this chapter has helped elucidate issues that affect learners' reliance on the LPP and the impact that this processing principle has on SLA to help move research forward to advance theory building and classroom application.
:::

::: {.section .reference aria-labelledby="r13_1"}
## Further Reading {#ch13.xhtml_r13_1}

- [Lee, J., & Benati, A. (2007). `<cite>`{=html}Second language processing: An analysis of theory, problems and possible solutions`</cite>`{=html}. Continuum.]{#ch13.xhtml_ref13_1}
- [VanPatten, B. (2020). Input processing in adult L2 acquisition. In B. VanPatten, G. Keating & S. Wulff (Eds.). `<cite>`{=html}Theories in second language acquisition`</cite>`{=html} (pp. 105--127). Routledge.]{#ch13.xhtml_ref13_2}
:::
