[]{#ch14.xhtml}

::: {}
# []{#ch14.xhtml_p198 .pagebreak aria-label=" page 198. " role="doc-pagebreak"}[14 ]{.chnum .ordinal}[The First-Noun Principle]{.chtitle} {#ch14.xhtml_h1}

Russell Simonsen

Miami University of Ohio

Bill VanPatten

Independent Scholar

DOI: [10.4324/9781003041412-18](https://dx.doi.org/10.4324/9781003041412-18){aria-label="D.O.I. link to this document."}
:::

::: {.section .introduction role="doc-introduction"}
## Introduction and Definitions {#ch14.xhtml_sec14_1}

We begin this chapter by offering our working definition of processing as it relates to input. Processing is the unconscious, moment-by-moment computation of sentence structure during comprehension along with the mapping of meaning onto form. As such, processing is implicated in both acquisition and the outcome of acquisition.

On the one hand, learners build mental representation of language through a combination of processing and internal linguistic and cognitive mechanisms that organize language data in particular ways (e.g., [Sharwood Smith & Truscott, 2014](#ch14.xhtml_ref14_35){#ch14.xhtml_R_ref14_35 .biblioref role="doc-biblioref"}; VanPatten, [1996](#ch14.xhtml_ref14_44){#ch14.xhtml_R_ref14_44 .biblioref role="doc-biblioref"}, [2020](#ch14.xhtml_ref14_50){#ch14.xhtml_R_ref14_50 .biblioref role="doc-biblioref"}). That is, processing input is part of how language is built in the minds/brains of learners. On the other hand, as mental representation grows, it is linked to processing incoming sentences much the same way that native speakers of a language have an interface with their representations when comprehending sentences, as in the case of ambiguity resolution (e.g., [Dussias, 2003](#ch14.xhtml_ref14_14){#ch14.xhtml_R_ref14_14 .biblioref role="doc-biblioref"}; [Gorrell, 1995](#ch14.xhtml_ref14_18){#ch14.xhtml_R_ref14_18 .biblioref role="doc-biblioref"}; [Keating et al., 2011](#ch14.xhtml_ref14_24){#ch14.xhtml_R_ref14_24 .biblioref role="doc-biblioref"}). In this case, the representation is already acquired, and the learner deploys it in particular ways to comprehend a sentence ([VanPatten, 2014](#ch14.xhtml_ref14_45){#ch14.xhtml_R_ref14_45 .biblioref role="doc-biblioref"}).

In this chapter, we focus on processing as it relates to acquisition: that is, processing as a mechanism that delivers data to the internal mechanisms that subsequently organize and construct a linguistic system over time. Thus, from here on in we will use the term *input processing* (IP) to mean the role of processing in the creation and evolution of a linguistic system---and we will pay particular attention to the earlier stages of acquisition. As the chapter title suggests, we will zero in on one particularly well-researched aspect of IP, the first-noun principle (FNP). We offer a few cautionary notes, however.

Our first cautionary note is that a focus on the FNP is not meant to be a singular answer to the question of how acquisition happens. Instead, we see the FNP as one of a number of principles, heuristics, or strategies that work together to shape the data that is delivered to the mechanisms responsible for organizing language in the mind of the learner. It is certainly only one aspect of IP.

Our second cautionary note is that our education and bias are toward generative perspectives on the nature of language. We believe that in order to understand acquisition, one must have a working theory of the object of acquisition: in this case, language. A good deal of research on input and on IP has lacked solid grounding on the relationship between processing and what, []{#ch14.xhtml_p199 .pagebreak aria-label=" page 199. " role="doc-pagebreak"}ultimately, is acquired (for some discussion, see [VanPatten, 2015b](#ch14.xhtml_ref14_47){#ch14.xhtml_R_ref14_47 .biblioref role="doc-biblioref"}). Therefore, we dovetail with [Leeser (2021)](#ch14.xhtml_ref14_30){#ch14.xhtml_R_ref14_30 .biblioref role="doc-biblioref"}, in our perspective on language and will relate the FNP to issues of how sentence structure is computed by learners and the consequences of that computation for mental representation within the generative tradition.
:::

::::: {.section aria-labelledby="sec14_2"}
## Historical Perspectives {#ch14.xhtml_sec14_2}

::: {.section aria-labelledby="sec14_2_1"}
### What Is the FNP? {#ch14.xhtml_sec14_2_1}

Following [VanPatten (2020)](#ch14.xhtml_ref14_50){.biblioref role="doc-biblioref"}, the FNP is stated as follows: "Learners tend to process the first noun or pronoun they encounter in a sentence as the subject" (p. 112). In this latest iteration, VanPatten has dropped the word "agent" from the FNP (i.e., "the subject/agent"), a point to which we will return later in this section.

The origins of the FNP are found in early child first language (L1) acquisition (e.g., [Bever, 1970](#ch14.xhtml_ref14_10){#ch14.xhtml_R_ref14_10 .biblioref role="doc-biblioref"}; [Slobin, 1966](#ch14.xhtml_ref14_37){#ch14.xhtml_R_ref14_37 .biblioref role="doc-biblioref"}), in which children were shown to misinterpret passives. When presented with sentences such as "The lion was chased by the tiger" and given stuffed animals to act out the event, children before the age of eight years tended to have the lion chasing the tiger. As such, children equated "subject" with "agent" resulting in erroneous interpretation of sentences. Subsequent work in second language (L2) acquisition demonstrated a similar phenomenon. [Nam (1975)](#ch14.xhtml_ref14_32){#ch14.xhtml_R_ref14_32 .biblioref role="doc-biblioref"} and [Ervin-Tripp (1974)](#ch14.xhtml_ref14_17){#ch14.xhtml_R_ref14_17 .biblioref role="doc-biblioref"} demonstrated that older children and adults misinterpreted passives much like child L1 learners, regardless of whether their L1 had passives or not and whether passives in their L1 were structurally similar to English passives or not (e.g., French, Korean). [Uludag and VanPatten (2012)](#ch14.xhtml_ref14_40){#ch14.xhtml_R_ref14_40 .biblioref role="doc-biblioref"} later studied Turkish L1 learners of English L2 regarding the processing of passive structures in English and demonstrated the same interpretation/processing problems.

Motivated by the work on passive structures, [VanPatten (1984)](#ch14.xhtml_ref14_42){#ch14.xhtml_R_ref14_42 .biblioref role="doc-biblioref"} examined the interpretation of OVS sequences in Spanish L2 by English L1 speakers early on in acquisition. In his research, O was operationalized both as a fronted case-marked object (e.g., *A María la ve Juan* lit: [acc-]{.sc}Mary her sees John "John sees Mary") and a simple clitic object pronoun (e.g., *La ve Juan* lit: her sees John "John sees her"). Learners overwhelmingly interpreted both fronted objects and clitic object pronouns as the subject of the sentence. This tendency to incorrectly process preverbal objects in sentences with postverbal subjects has been replicated over and over, especially in research on processing instruction (PI) (see later section). In research on French, for example, it has been shown how the FNP causes misinterpretation of causative structures such as *Phillipe fait preparer le dîner à Jean* (lit: Phillip makes to prepare the dinner to John "Phillip makes John prepare dinner"), in which case, learners interpret the sentence to mean something like "Phillip makes dinner for John" (e.g., [VanPatten & Wong, 2004](#ch14.xhtml_ref14_56){#ch14.xhtml_R_ref14_56 .biblioref role="doc-biblioref"}; [Wong & Ito, 2018](#ch14.xhtml_ref14_58){#ch14.xhtml_R_ref14_58 .biblioref role="doc-biblioref"}). As [VanPatten (1996 and elsewhere)](#ch14.xhtml_ref14_44){.biblioref role="doc-biblioref"} has argued, something like the FNP delivers erroneous data to the internal mechanisms and can delay acquisition of particular morpho-syntactic properties of a given language.
:::

::: {.section aria-labelledby="sec14_2_2"}
### Corollaries {#ch14.xhtml_sec14_2_2}

Often overlooked in the research on the FNP are several corollaries as formulated in VanPatten ([1996](#ch14.xhtml_ref14_44){.biblioref role="doc-biblioref"}, [2020](#ch14.xhtml_ref14_50){.biblioref role="doc-biblioref"}). They are:

- The event probability principle. Learners may rely on event probabilities, where possible, instead of the FNP to interpret sentences.
- The lexical semantics principle. Learners may rely on lexical semantics, where possible, instead of the FNP to interpret sentences.
- []{#ch14.xhtml_p200 .pagebreak aria-label=" page 200. " role="doc-pagebreak"}The contextual constraint principle. Learners may rely less on the FNP if preceding context constrains the possible interpretation of a clause or sentence.

The event probability principle accounts for some observed data in both L1 and L2 processing during early and intermediate stages of acquisition in which a learner interprets a sentence using real-world experience. For example, for the event of scolding, it is more likely for a parent to scold a child rather than the other way around. Thus, a passive sentence such as "The child was scolded by the mother" is more likely to be interpreted correctly than a situation in which both entities are equally capable of doing something (e.g., "The child was kissed by the mother").

The lexical semantics principle accounts for other observed data in L1 and L2 processing in which learners interpret sentences based on which entity is actually capable of doing something. In the case of a verb like "kick", the event requires an entity with legs and the capability of kicking (e.g., a horse, a cow, a person) ruling out entities that can't kick (e.g., a snail, a hammer, a rock). When given passive sentences such as "The fence was kicked by the horse", learners tend to converge on the correct interpretation based on their knowledge of the requirements of the theta roles (or thematic elements) required in an event like "kick".

Finally, the contextual constraint principle accounts for research that has shown that L2 learners will not rely on the FNP if prior context contradicts the meaning normally resulting from the FNP (e.g., [VanPatten & Houston, 1998](#ch14.xhtml_ref14_53){#ch14.xhtml_R_ref14_53 .biblioref role="doc-biblioref"}). For example, in the sentence *Juan está en el hospital porque lo atacó María con un cuchillo* (lit: John is in the hospital because him attacked Mary with a knife "John is in the hospital because Mary attacked him with a knife") the FNP would typically lead the learner to interpret the embedded clause as "he attacked Mary". However, learners may abandon the FNP because the context preceding the clause doesn't fit with that interpretation (i.e., If John attacked Mary with a knife, why isn't she in the hospital?).

What these corollaries suggest is that the FNP is the default structural processing heuristic that guides learner interpretation when there is nothing non-structural (as described in the corollaries) to attenuate it. In short, the FNP is always the starting point for determining who did what to whom unless or until other non-syntactic information intercedes.
:::
:::::

::: {.section aria-labelledby="sec14_3"}
## Critical Issues and Topics {#ch14.xhtml_sec14_3}

The most critical issues related to the FNP are its effects on acquisition. The FNP may affect a variety of aspects of language; however, such effects depend on the individual language itself. Because the present authors have worked principally with Spanish, we will illustrate here what some of the outcomes of the FNP are for learners of Spanish.

The FNP may cause a delay in the projection of new functional features in a language. It has been posited that a language like Spanish, for example, has a functional projection that serves as a landing site for a clitic object pronoun as it raises out of the VP (e.g., [Uriagareka, 1995](#ch14.xhtml_ref14_41){#ch14.xhtml_R_ref14_41 .biblioref role="doc-biblioref"}). However, input data are variable in languages. Learners of Spanish are exposed to sentences with and without overt subjects such that the following orders are all possible: SVOpro, SOproV, OproV, OproVS. The former two sentence orders reinforce the FNP and allow for the misanalysis of the latter two sentence orders. In this case, Opro is not correctly analyzed and the development of a new functional phrase in the learners' developing syntax is delayed.

In addition, the 3rd-person clitic object pronouns in Spanish (*lo/s, la/s*) have been analyzed to have D (Determiner) features, which is not the case in a language like English. Subjects, on the other hand, do not have D features. If learners make use of the FNP and incorrectly analyze 3rd-person clitic object pronouns (e.g., *lo/la* in Spanish), then this crucial feature of these []{#ch14.xhtml_p201 .pagebreak aria-label=" page 201. " role="doc-pagebreak"}particular pronouns is missed, causing further delay in the behavior of these elements in the learner's syntax.

And, of course, there may be delayed acquisition of other pronouns such as reflexives (e.g., *Se levanta* lit: *pro* gets up "He/She gets up.) which are misanalyzed as versions of subject pronouns, dative pronouns and grammaticalized OproVS word orders (e.g., *Le gusta Juan a Roberto* lit: to him John is pleasing to Robert "Robert likes John"), passives, and other structures. In addition, person-number morphology on verbs may be delayed as learners rely on what they perceive to be subjects, and the processor "skips" inflectional morphology until some point when parsing fails (i.e., morphology becomes crucial to determine who did what to whom). In short, depending on the language and the structures involved in that language as they intersect or not with the FNP, this particular processing heuristic can have consequences throughout a learner's developing grammar.
:::

::::: {.section aria-labelledby="sec14_4"}
## Current Contributions and Research {#ch14.xhtml_sec14_4}

As previewed earlier, the FNP has been documented repeatedly over the past several decades. Because extensive summaries of early evidence for the FNP can be found elsewhere (e.g., [Malovhr & Lee, 2013](#ch14.xhtml_ref14_31){#ch14.xhtml_R_ref14_31 .biblioref role="doc-biblioref"}), this section will focus on studies from the past 15 years. We will focus on studies that directly research the FNP and will show in a later section how the FNP continues to show up in the research on processing instruction (PI).

::: {.section aria-labelledby="sec14_4_1"}
### Evidence for the FNP {#ch14.xhtml_sec14_4_1}

We begin this section by examining a tendency in natural languages. [VanPatten (1996, and elsewhere)](#ch14.xhtml_ref14_44){.biblioref role="doc-biblioref"} has argued that there is something about the human mind and the shape of languages that wants subjects (and agents) to precede objects (and patients/themes) in word order. Surveys of world languages have found that word orders in which objects precede subjects are quite rare. [Dryer (2013)](#ch14.xhtml_ref14_13){#ch14.xhtml_R_ref14_13 .biblioref role="doc-biblioref"}, for example, found that of the 1377 surveyed languages, SVO, SOV, and VSO constituted 83% of the basic word orders. VOS, OVS and OSV combined constituted only 3.1% of confirmed word orders. The residual 13.7% of languages seemed to not have a basic word order, but even among these O-first orders were rare. Dryer's results were largely corroborated by [Hammarström (2016)](#ch14.xhtml_ref14_19){#ch14.xhtml_R_ref14_19 .biblioref role="doc-biblioref"} with a survey of 5252 languages.

In short, there is a predisposition for something like the FNP in individual languages' canonical word orders. It is our contention that this predisposition underlies how learners begin to process sentences in a new language, regardless of their L1. (See, also, our comments in "Future Directions.")

Turning our attention to empirical research with L2 learners, [Tight (2012a)](#ch14.xhtml_ref14_38){#ch14.xhtml_R_ref14_38 .biblioref role="doc-biblioref"} tested the influence of the FNP with an aural sentence interpretation task that included three different word orders: SVO, VSO, and VOS. In his study, L2 learners heard a sentence and were asked to identify who was doing the action in the sentence (i.e., the subject) within eight seconds. He found that for first-, third-, and fifth-semester learners of Spanish, SVO sentences were the easiest to comprehend, which is expected based on the fact that it follows the canonical NVN pattern that served as the foundation for the FNP ([Bever, 1970](#ch14.xhtml_ref14_10){.biblioref role="doc-biblioref"}; VanPatten, [1984](#ch14.xhtml_ref14_42){.biblioref role="doc-biblioref"}, [1996](#ch14.xhtml_ref14_44){.biblioref role="doc-biblioref"}). The results were somewhat more mixed for the VSO and VOS sequences. First-year learners showed influence of the FNP in these word orders because they correctly interpreted VSO more consistently than VOS. This extra difficulty with VOS order would be predicted given that the object precedes the subject. Third and fifth-semester learners did not perform significantly differently between VSO and VOS, however. They only seemed to distinguish canonical word order (SVO) from non-canonical []{#ch14.xhtml_p202 .pagebreak aria-label=" page 202. " role="doc-pagebreak"}word orders (VSO/VOS). The overall results suggest that the FNP is a driving force in canonical NVN structures but shows a more variable influence in non-canonical structures (e.g., VNN as VSO or VOS) as acquisition progresses. Therefore, it is possible that the frequency of specific structures plays a role in modulating the effects of the FNP. We remind the reader that the FNP is posited as a starting point for processing. We would expect to see higher reliance on it in the early stages, and this is precisely what Tight showed as evidenced by the first-year learners' higher accuracy in VSO conditions in comparison to VOS. What would be unexpected, and to our knowledge is unattested, is if L2 Spanish learners were to perform significantly better with VOS in contrast to VSO in the earliest stages.

Sentence interpretation tasks that require participants to indicate *who is doing what to whom* have been the most common tool when investigating the FNP. Usually, participants read or hear a sentence, and then provide an offline response (although sometimes under time-pressure). However, there are ways to investigate the FNP with online methods. For example, in [Simonsen (2020)](#ch14.xhtml_ref14_36){#ch14.xhtml_R_ref14_36 .biblioref role="doc-biblioref"}, beginner, intermediate, and advanced L2 Spanish learners (L1 English) completed a self-paced reading task that consisted of sentences with different word orders (SVO vs. OVS) and verb types (action verbs vs. psych\[ological\] verbs). (For online processing methods, see Jegerski, this volume.) As a control, a group of native Spanish speakers also completed the task in the Simonsen study. The goal was to examine how word order and verb type interact in online processing in L2 Spanish at different proficiency levels, given that in Spanish there is a strong connection between SVO word order and action verbs (*Juana le canta a Ramona* lit: Juana to her sings to Ramona "Juana sings to Ramona"), and OVS word order and psych verbs (*A Juana le gusta Ramona* lit: To Juana to her is pleasing Ramona "Juana likes Ramona"). The reading-time results at critical points in the sentence revealed that beginner and intermediate learners were not sensitive to the interaction between these variables. Instead, their reading times were affected almost exclusively by word order in a way that would be predicted by the FNP: sentences that began with a subject (i.e., SVO) were read faster at the critical region of the sentence in comparison to sentences that began with an object (i.e., OVS). Assuming that relative reading times are indicative of processing difficulty, these lower-level L2 learners appeared to find OVS word order more difficult to process, which can be explained by a reliance on the FNP. In the advanced L2 learner and native speaker groups, on the other hand, not all SVO sentences were read faster. In fact, the non-canonical pairing of SVO word order with a psych verb (e.g., *Juana le gusta a Ramona* "Ramona likes Juana") caused the greatest reading delay. Overall the results reveal that the FNP is a predominant force among beginner and intermediate Spanish learners, but that it can be attenuated with advanced proficiency.

So far, we have examined NVN structures. But what about NNV or VNN structures? Recent evidence from [Erdocia et al. (2009)](#ch14.xhtml_ref14_15){#ch14.xhtml_R_ref14_15 .biblioref role="doc-biblioref"} and [Erdocia et al. (2014)](#ch14.xhtml_ref14_16){#ch14.xhtml_R_ref14_16 .biblioref role="doc-biblioref"} demonstrates that the FNP does in fact operate independently of the position of the verb. These studies included behavioral and electrophysiological analyses of word order processing in L1 and L2 Basque (L1 Spanish). Basque is a head-final language with a canonical SOV word order, but it also allows for a non-canonical OSV word order. [Erdocia et al. (2009)](#ch14.xhtml_ref14_15){.biblioref role="doc-biblioref"} showed that L1 Basque speakers, although familiar with OSV word order, process the canonical SOV word order faster and more easily. These researchers also included ambiguous sentences that could be interpreted as either SVO or OSV due to the ambiguous sequence *-ak*, which can indicate either a singular subject or a plural object (e.g., *Emakume-ak gizon-ak ikusi ditu* "The woman saw the men" *or* "The men saw the women). The authors discovered that when reading such sentences, L1 Basque speakers "apply a default strategy and process them as canonical SOV sentences, ignoring the OSV possibility" ([Erdocia et al., 2014](#ch14.xhtml_ref14_16){.biblioref role="doc-biblioref"}, p. 820). The behavioral results from the L2 Basque group similarly revealed faster reading times in SOV as compared to OSV sentences, and a default preference for an SOV []{#ch14.xhtml_p203 .pagebreak aria-label=" page 203. " role="doc-pagebreak"}interpretation in sentences that were ambiguous between SOV and OSV. In sum, while both L1 and L2 Basque speakers are capable of interpreting objects before subjects, their default assumption is that the subject will appear before the object; when this is not the case, they experience a processing delay. The FNP, as currently constructed, would predict these findings.[^24]
:::

::: {.section aria-labelledby="sec14_4_2"}
### Evidence Against the FNP? {#ch14.xhtml_sec14_4_2}

In addition to the FNP, [VanPatten (2020)](#ch14.xhtml_ref14_50){.biblioref role="doc-biblioref"} has offered the L1 transfer principle as an alternative, which he claimed had weak support at best. The L1 transfer principle holds that "L2 learners begin acquisition with L1 parsing procedures" (p. 122). [Isabelli (2008)](#ch14.xhtml_ref14_22){#ch14.xhtml_R_ref14_22 .biblioref role="doc-biblioref"} attempted to tease apart the effects of the first-noun principle from those that might result from L1 transfer. She compared how accurately L1 English and L1 Italian speakers could identify the grammatical subject in different Spanish word orders in an aural comprehension task. The word orders included SVO (*El chico escucha a la chica* "The boy listens to the girl"), OVS (*La escucha el chico* "The boy listens to her"), and OOVS (*A la chica la escucha el chico* "The boy listens to the girl"). In the OVS and OOVS conditions, all of the objects were either pronouns (in OVS conditions) or an object of a preposition followed by a pronoun (in OOVS conditions). If the L1 transfer principle were operational, Isabelli assumed that L1 English speakers "would have difficulty with Spanish OVS structures since this structure is not allowed in English, and therefore the English parser will have problems" (p. 469). On the other hand, she assumed that "the Italian parser has mechanisms for computing OVS structure" (p. 469), and predicted that L1 Italian speakers would not have difficulty with OVS sentences in their L2, Spanish. The results revealed that L1 Italian speakers were much better than L1 English speakers at identifying the correct subject of sentences in OVS and OOVS conditions. Isabelli interpreted this as evidence that L2 speakers' argument interpretation in non-SVO sentences is influenced by their L1 parsing abilities in non-SVO sentences, which supports the L1 transfer principle.

However, there are other interpretations of her results. First, we note that the Italian L1 speakers mean score was 86% on the OVS sentences with a standard deviation of 19. This indicates a very large variance in learner performance, suggesting that either a portion of the participants were relying on the FNP to a significant extent or that many of the participants were relying on the FNP to some degree. In addition, [VanPatten (2015b)](#ch14.xhtml_ref14_47){.biblioref role="doc-biblioref"} points out that it is not clear what was being transferred in [Isabelli (2008)](#ch14.xhtml_ref14_22){.biblioref role="doc-biblioref"} because L1 Italian speakers could have been relying on both syntactic computation and case cues based on L1 lexical knowledge. The preverbal Spanish object pronouns used in the study, *lo* "him" and *la* "her", are identical in Italian, and they have inherent accusative case (e.g., *Lo vede María* "María sees him"). This makes them incompatible with a subject interpretation, regardless of their syntactic position. Consequently, upon seeing a sentence-initial object pronoun *lo* or *la*, both Italian and Spanish speakers know that these arguments cannot be the subject due to their case. And, in a context with only two referents, once an argument is determined to be the accusative object, the other argument is likely to be the subject by process of elimination. English, however, does not have a pronoun system that overlaps with the one in Spanish that would provide them with equal access to case information. Therefore, the difference in subject identification accuracy between the L1 Italian and L1 English group cannot be explained entirely by a general parsing strategy based on syntax alone. What VanPatten claimed learners had transferred in the Isabelli study was not a syntactic processing procedure, but instead lexical items and their morphological information. It could be that Italians also begin acquisition with the FNP but that shared Spanish--Italian morphological markings on lexical items attenuate its effects. The unequal access to case information between the two groups of learners in Isabelli's study is important to consider in light of []{#ch14.xhtml_p204 .pagebreak aria-label=" page 204. " role="doc-pagebreak"}[Schlesewsky and Bornkessel's (2004)](#ch14.xhtml_ref14_33){#ch14.xhtml_R_ref14_33 .biblioref role="doc-biblioref"} conclusion that there are two separate sentence processing routes: one that is case-induced, and another that is driven by syntax. The L1 Italian speakers may have taken a case-induced processing route, while L1 English speakers relied mostly on syntax and linear order, leading to different results. It is possible, then, that if speakers' L1 provides them with the knowledge to interpret unambiguous internal morphological marking in their L2 due to a crosslinguistic overlap (e.g., *lo* "it/him" only has accusative case in Spanish and Italian), they may be able to override the effects of the FNP by taking a case-driven as opposed to syntax-driven processing route. However, if internal case properties do not overlap between nouns/pronouns in an L1 and L2, a syntax-driven route such as the FNP would be expected to predominate.

Another study that claims to find support for L1 transfer is [Seibert Hanson and Carlson (2014)](#ch14.xhtml_ref14_34){#ch14.xhtml_R_ref14_34 .biblioref role="doc-biblioref"}. The researchers tested English L1 and Romanian L1 learners of Spanish L2 using a picture matching task similar to those used in previous research. The main variables were sentence type (SVO/OVS) and proficiency. Their results show that the Romanians have an advantage over English speakers in processing OVS sentences more accurately. They attribute this finding to Romanian being typologically similar to Spanish in its use of preverbal object clitics, thus allowing for L1 transfer of processing. However, we note several interesting findings that are not fully explained in the study. The first is that the mean difference between the two groups was 10% in OVS sentences (i.e., Romanian L1 learners' accuracy was 68% and English L1 learners' accuracy was 58%; meanwhile, the Spanish L1 group mean was 90%). In short, both L2 groups seem to rely on the FNP to some extent and neither reached a level that could be considered approximating how the Spanish L1 group performed. In addition, the Romanian speakers were more accurate on SVO sentences, a curious and unexplained finding given English's heavy reliance on SVO as a canonical structure. One would expect that the English speakers would have performed better than or at least the same as the Romanian speakers on SVO sentences if L1 transfer was underlying performance. More revealing is what the authors report on the effects of proficiency. At the lower levels, Romanian speakers did not seem to have an advantage over English speakers in processing OVS sentences. We take this finding to support the basic premise that the FNP is *the starting point* for acquisition regardless of L1. It may be that, in the case of the Seibert Hanson and Carlson study, the effects of the L1 only emerged as learners got more exposure to the L2.

[Tight (2012b)](#ch14.xhtml_ref14_39){#ch14.xhtml_R_ref14_39 .biblioref role="doc-biblioref"} also sought to test the universality of the FNP and ultimately concluded that it may not be relevant in all sentential contexts. Tight examined how L2 Spanish learners (L1 English) interpreted aural sentences with only one argument, such as the VS sentence *Escuchan bien los niños* "The children listen well." In this case, the one and only noun is *los niños* "the children," so it is also technically the first noun in the sentence. According to a strict reading of the FNP, L2 learners would be expected to interpret this first noun as the subject. To assess their interpretation, Tight required participants to select the most appropriate English translation of the sentences they heard. For example, for the sentence above, they had to circle either "The children listen well" or "They listen well to the children." Tight found that, contrary to expectations from the FNP, first-semester, third-semester, and fifth-semester learners incorrectly identified post-verbal subjects (e.g., *los niños*) as objects in 52% to 75% of VS sentences. He concluded that instead of using a first-noun parsing strategy, L2 learners used a verb-centric one: nouns that appeared before the verb were determined to be the subject, but nouns that appeared after the verb were determined to be the object.

[VanPatten (2015c)](#ch14.xhtml_ref14_48){#ch14.xhtml_R_ref14_48 .biblioref role="doc-biblioref"} offers an alternative explanation, pointing out that the first noun in a sentence is not always overtly expressed in Spanish. It is possible that L2 learners posit preverbal null subjects in sentences like *Escuchan bien los niños*, which draws them toward an []{#ch14.xhtml_p205 .pagebreak aria-label=" page 205. " role="doc-pagebreak"}interpretation in which some unknown group of people (i.e., "They") is listening to the children. In this interpretation, the first noun would still be the subject---it would simply be a null one: pro *escuchan bien a los niños* "They listen well to the children."[^25] If L2 learners were inclined toward this interpretation in Tight's study, they might have been encouraged to select the translation of sentences that aligned well with a null subject interpretation. The reader will recall that "They listen well to the children" was one of the two English translations that Tight provided for the Spanish sentence in question. L2 learners' ability to use null subjects when processing sentences is an important consideration given that it has been documented in previous research (e.g., [Jegerski, 2012](#ch14.xhtml_ref14_23){#ch14.xhtml_R_ref14_23 .biblioref role="doc-biblioref"}). Modifications to Tight's methodology might reveal more clearly how L2 learners parse sentences. For example, instead of providing participants with written translations that could influence their interpretations, a researcher could require participants to listen to Spanish sentences and rephrase them out loud in English, as in [Lee and Malovhr (2009)](#ch14.xhtml_ref14_28){#ch14.xhtml_R_ref14_28 .biblioref role="doc-biblioref"}. If participants still interpret *Escuchan bien los niños* as "They listen well to the children," then it would be fairly clear that they are positing a null subject because there is no overt "they" in the Spanish sentence. We remind the reader here that the actual wording of the FNP includes both nouns and pronouns: *Learners tend to process the first noun or pronoun they encounter in a sentence as the subject*. By all accounts, a null subject is considered a pronoun, hence the use of the construct *pro* by linguists when referring to it.

A final point regarding Tight's verb centric parsing strategy is that it is not applicable when two nouns are clustered before or after the verb (i.e., NNV/VNN). In the previous section, we reviewed research by [Erdocia et al. (2009)](#ch14.xhtml_ref14_15){.biblioref role="doc-biblioref"} and [Erdocia et al. (2014)](#ch14.xhtml_ref14_16){.biblioref role="doc-biblioref"}. They showed in their research on Spanish L1 learners of Basque L2 that in NNV structures, the FNP was present in the processing strategies of their participants. What is needed to bolster the claim that the FNP is present regardless of sentence type is the processing of VNN sentences (although one early study shows that the FNP is operative in VNN structures; see [Gass, 1988](#ch14.xhtml_ref14_501){#ch14.xhtml_R_ref14_501 .biblioref role="doc-biblioref"}).
:::
:::::

::::: {.section aria-labelledby="sec14_5"}
## Recommendations for Practice {#ch14.xhtml_sec14_5}

In the field of instructed SLA, the FNP has inspired a good deal of work within what is called *processing instruction* (PI), beginning with [VanPatten and Cadierno (1993)](#ch14.xhtml_ref14_52){#ch14.xhtml_R_ref14_52 .biblioref role="doc-biblioref"}. In this section, we will briefly outline the nature of PI and then summarize the research in it related to the FNP.

::: {.section aria-labelledby="sec14_5_1"}
### The Nature of PI {#ch14.xhtml_sec14_5_1}

Processing Instruction (PI) was first described in [VanPatten and Cadierno (1993)](#ch14.xhtml_ref14_52){.biblioref role="doc-biblioref"} and [VanPatten (1993)](#ch14.xhtml_ref14_43){#ch14.xhtml_R_ref14_43 .biblioref role="doc-biblioref"}, and subsequently elaborated and refined in a series of publications (e.g., VanPatten, [1996](#ch14.xhtml_ref14_44){.biblioref role="doc-biblioref"}, [2015b](#ch14.xhtml_ref14_47){.biblioref role="doc-biblioref"}, and [Wong, 2004](#ch14.xhtml_ref14_57){#ch14.xhtml_R_ref14_57 .biblioref role="doc-biblioref"}). It is a pedagogical intervention aimed at destabilizing processing strategies that may inhibit the development of language (e.g., [VanPatten, 2015a](#ch14.xhtml_ref14_46){#ch14.xhtml_R_ref14_46 .biblioref role="doc-biblioref"} and [2015b](#ch14.xhtml_ref14_47){.biblioref role="doc-biblioref"}). It is not a method or an approach and, as an intervention, can be used in a communicative or acquisition-oriented curriculum as needed. (See, also, Wong, this volume.)

PI has traditionally included three elements: (1) description of a target structure; (2) information about an incorrect or less than optimal strategy (principle) that affects the processing of the target structure; (3) structured input activities aimed at destabilizing the processing strategy as it relates to the target structure, thus encouraging better intake for acquisition. Research has demonstrated that neither (1) nor (2) is necessary for destabilization to occur, as long as the structured input activities are appropriately crafted to incur such destabilization through either parsing failure or forced mapping of meaning onto form (e.g., [Henry et al., 2009](#ch14.xhtml_ref14_20){#ch14.xhtml_R_ref14_20 .biblioref role="doc-biblioref"}; [VanPatten et al., 2013](#ch14.xhtml_ref14_51){#ch14.xhtml_R_ref14_51 .biblioref role="doc-biblioref"}; [VanPatten & Oikkenon, 1996](#ch14.xhtml_ref14_54){#ch14.xhtml_R_ref14_54 .biblioref role="doc-biblioref"}).

[]{#ch14.xhtml_p206 .pagebreak aria-label=" page 206. " role="doc-pagebreak"}Contrary to some claims, PI is not predicated on so-called "noticing" and is not meant to teach rules in the traditional sense (see [VanPatten, 2015b](#ch14.xhtml_ref14_47){.biblioref role="doc-biblioref"}, for elaboration). As [VanPatten (2019)](#ch14.xhtml_ref14_49){#ch14.xhtml_R_ref14_49 .biblioref role="doc-biblioref"} and [VanPatten and Rothman (2014)](#ch14.xhtml_ref14_55){#ch14.xhtml_R_ref14_55 .biblioref role="doc-biblioref"} have argued, the concept of "rules" is antiquated, given the widely accepted premise that the mental representation of language is not a set of stored rules but instead an abstract and complex system in which principles and features interact that give the illusion of rules. To be sure, not all theorists take a generative approach as we do, but for such theorists the concept of "rules" in the traditional sense is also questionable---and in the end, no theorist would say that rules found in language textbooks are psychologically or cognitively real. For this reason, PI is a unique intervention because its focus is not on learning rules but on destabilizing processing strategies. Indeed, the gold standard for measurement in PI research has been sentence interpretation tests and more recently, online processing (as measured by self-paced reading and eye-tracking, for example), and not the knowledge of explicitly learned grammar or its application.

Because PI is focused on input processing, the locus of research is the intersection of a morpho-syntactic structure with a processing strategy or principle. That is, participants in PI research do not learn rules but how to process a structure, so to speak. As one example, in the foundational study of [VanPatten and Cadierno (1993)](#ch14.xhtml_ref14_52){.biblioref role="doc-biblioref"}, the target structures were OVS and SOV word orders with clitic object pronouns as in *Lo ve María* lit: him-[acc]{.sc} sees Mary and *María lo ve* lit: Mary him-[acc]{.sc} sees, both meaning "Mary sees him." VanPatten and Cadierno did not set about to teach learners on the proper use of clitic object pronouns (e.g., form, placement) but instead to disrupt the FNP which leads learners to misinterpret (process incorrectly) the sample sentence as "He sees Mary." If any learning of language is going on, it is happening unconsciously in the learners' minds as they build up representation for such things as a new feature projection, flexible word order, and other features related to the more abstract components of language (e.g., that 3^rd^ person direct object clitics in Spanish carry a D feature). In this way, PI helps to deliver better intake to the internal mechanisms responsible for building mental representation over time.
:::

::: {.section aria-labelledby="sec14_5_2"}
### PI and the FNP {#ch14.xhtml_sec14_5_2}

Although PI has been researched examining the intersection of a number of structures with processing principles, in this section we list a number of studies on PI in which the FNP is central. Our goal is not to critically analyze such research nor to be exhaustive in our listing, but instead to sample the range and quantity of pedagogically-oriented research motivated by the FNP.

- Spanish
  - Word order and clitic direct object pronouns (e.g., [Malovhr & Lee, 2013](#ch14.xhtml_ref14_31){.biblioref role="doc-biblioref"}; [VanPatten & Cadierno, 1993](#ch14.xhtml_ref14_52){.biblioref role="doc-biblioref"}; [VanPatten et al., 2013](#ch14.xhtml_ref14_51){.biblioref role="doc-biblioref"}; [VanPatten & Oikkenon, 1996](#ch14.xhtml_ref14_54){.biblioref role="doc-biblioref"}).
  - Word order and clitic dative pronouns (e.g., [Lee & Malovhr, 2009](#ch14.xhtml_ref14_28){.biblioref role="doc-biblioref"}).
  - True passive (e.g., Lee, [2015](#ch14.xhtml_ref14_25){#ch14.xhtml_R_ref14_25 .biblioref role="doc-biblioref"}, [2017](#ch14.xhtml_ref14_26){#ch14.xhtml_R_ref14_26 .biblioref role="doc-biblioref"}; [Lee & Doherty, 2019](#ch14.xhtml_ref14_27){#ch14.xhtml_R_ref14_27 .biblioref role="doc-biblioref"}; [Lee et al., 2020](#ch14.xhtml_ref14_29){#ch14.xhtml_R_ref14_29 .biblioref role="doc-biblioref"}).
- French
  - Causative with faire "make; do" (e.g., [Benati, Lee, & Laval, 2009](#ch14.xhtml_ref14_8){#ch14.xhtml_R_ref14_8 .biblioref role="doc-biblioref"}; [VanPatten et al., 2013](#ch14.xhtml_ref14_51){.biblioref role="doc-biblioref"}; [VanPatten & Wong, 2004](#ch14.xhtml_ref14_56){.biblioref role="doc-biblioref"}; [Wong & Ito, 2018](#ch14.xhtml_ref14_58){.biblioref role="doc-biblioref"}).
- English
  - Causative (e.g., [Benati, 2020](#ch14.xhtml_ref14_6){#ch14.xhtml_R_ref14_6 .biblioref role="doc-biblioref"}; [Benati & Batziou, 2019](#ch14.xhtml_ref14_9){#ch14.xhtml_R_ref14_9 .biblioref role="doc-biblioref"}).
  - Passive (e.g., [Benati, 2013](#ch14.xhtml_ref14_4){#ch14.xhtml_R_ref14_4 .biblioref role="doc-biblioref"}; [Uludag & VanPatten, 2012](#ch14.xhtml_ref14_40){.biblioref role="doc-biblioref"}).
- []{#ch14.xhtml_p207 .pagebreak aria-label=" page 207. " role="doc-pagebreak"}German
  - Case marking and word order (e.g., [Henry et al., 2009](#ch14.xhtml_ref14_20){.biblioref role="doc-biblioref"}; [Henry et al., 2017](#ch14.xhtml_ref14_21){#ch14.xhtml_R_ref14_21 .biblioref role="doc-biblioref"}; [VanPatten et al., 2013](#ch14.xhtml_ref14_51){.biblioref role="doc-biblioref"}).
- Russian
  - Case marking and word order (e.g., [Comer & deBenedette, 2010](#ch14.xhtml_ref14_12){#ch14.xhtml_R_ref14_12 .biblioref role="doc-biblioref"}; [VanPatten et al., 2013](#ch14.xhtml_ref14_51){.biblioref role="doc-biblioref"}).
- Japanese
  - Passive (e.g., [Benati, 2015](#ch14.xhtml_ref14_5){#ch14.xhtml_R_ref14_5 .biblioref role="doc-biblioref"}; [Benati et al., 2010](#ch14.xhtml_ref14_7){#ch14.xhtml_R_ref14_7 .biblioref role="doc-biblioref"}).
:::
:::::

::: {.section aria-labelledby="sec14_6"}
## Summary {#ch14.xhtml_sec14_6}

As the previous section suggests, there is a wealth of research in PI motivated by the FNP in different languages with different structures. This research has validated the FNP (i.e., learners in the experiments demonstrate FNP effects *before* treatment) and has demonstrated the degree to which the FNP is resistant to explicit information and teaching and instead more amenable to treatments in which parsing failure and forced mapping of meaning to form during comprehension drive the treatment. That is, the effects of the FNP are better mitigated by careful structuring of input as opposed to any explicit teaching and practice with a target structure.
:::

::: {.section aria-labelledby="sec14_7"}
## Future Directions {#ch14.xhtml_sec14_7}

Research on the FNP and its effects on acquisition will most likely continue, and we see three avenues in which continued contributions can be made. We outline them here in no order of importance.

The first area of future research focuses on the universality of the FNP. Although we have argued that the FNP is a default, universal principle guiding all learners' *initial* processing of input strings, there remain skeptics who suggest that the research is too focused on English learners with a strong SVO language learning another language. We have shown this is not the case, but of course the *sine qua non* of universality would be to see what speakers of an SVO language do with a language that has canonical OVS order or vice versa: speakers of an OVS language processing SVO sentences. However, OVS languages are exceedingly rare. Of the languages with OVS attested as the basic word order, the population of speakers is rather small (e.g, Hixkaryana, a language of the Brazilian Amazon, with some 500 speakers). What is more, there is debate in the literature as to whether OVS is actually the canonical order for these languages. Thus, we do not see this line of research as a viable option.

Instead, we see that continued research on the universality/default nature of the FNP lies in the processing of L1/L2 congruent structures as in the work by [Simonsen (2020)](#ch14.xhtml_ref14_36){.biblioref role="doc-biblioref"}, [Ervin-Tripp (1974)](#ch14.xhtml_ref14_17){.biblioref role="doc-biblioref"}, and others. As Simonsen demonstrated, English speakers are able to correctly interpret the grammatical function of dative objects during sentence processing, regardless of whether they appear before or after the verb. For example, in the sentences "To Sam, Taylor seems like a nice person" and "Taylor seems like a nice person to Sam," English speakers can identify Taylor as the subject and Sam as the object with similar accuracy. These same participants struggled with correctly identifying the grammatical function of preverbal dative objects in their L2 Spanish, however. And in the case of Ervin-Tripp, English speakers did not process word-for-word equivalents of passives in French as passives but as actives (see also the PI research on []{#ch14.xhtml_p208 .pagebreak aria-label=" page 208. " role="doc-pagebreak"}passives). If learners do not make use of the ability to process a structure in their L1 that is congruent with one in the L2 and instead rely on the FNP in processing that L2 structure, then the case for a default universal principle of processing is strengthened.

A second area of future research involves the continued application of online methods to researching the FNP. Earlier, we described the research in [Simonsen (2020)](#ch14.xhtml_ref14_36){.biblioref role="doc-biblioref"} and in some of the PI research (e.g., [Henry, Jackson, & DiMidio, 2017](#ch14.xhtml_ref14_21){.biblioref role="doc-biblioref"}; [Wong & Ito, 2018](#ch14.xhtml_ref14_58){.biblioref role="doc-biblioref"}) in which self-paced reading and eye-tracking, for example, are used to uncover the presence of the FNP during real-time processing. We believe this research can be expanded and can offer insights into the extent to which the FNP is operative in L2 sentence processing and how, developmentally, it dissipates over time. Does the FNP slowly disappear? Is there an abrupt change at a given point in time? Is there a stage at which the FNP overlaps with a new processing heuristic? When is it that something like case marking begins to interact with the FNP (i.e., causing attenuation of the processing heuristic)? Is it possible or likely that learners may abandon the FNP in processing one kind of sentence but not another (e.g., in Spanish, true passives and OVS and OV word orders involving clitic objects)?

As part of VanPatten's model for IP, the FNP has yielded considerable research and has received direct and indirect empirical support in the study of L2 acquisition. However, as the science progresses, the FNP and VanPatten's IP model as defined in this chapter may eventually be subsumed under a larger framework that accounts for how processing interacts and develops alongside other important aspects of L2 acquisition.
:::

::: {.endnotes role="doc-endnotes"}
## Notes {#ch14.xhtml_h2}
