[]{#ch15.xhtml}

::: {}
# []{#ch15.xhtml_p212 .pagebreak aria-label=" page 212. " role="doc-pagebreak"}[15 ]{.chnum .ordinal}[Roles of Interaction and Output in Input Processing]{.chtitle} {#ch15.xhtml_h1}

Janire Zalbidea and Paul D. Toth

Temple University

DOI: [10.4324/9781003041412-19](https://dx.doi.org/10.4324/9781003041412-19){aria-label="D.O.I. link to this document."}
:::

::: {.section .introduction role="doc-introduction"}
## Introduction and Definitions {#ch15.xhtml_sec15_1}

This chapter provides an overview of how second language (L2) interaction and output production can impact L2 input processing in support of linguistic development. After defining these constructs, we present a historical outline of theoretical advancements and a discussion of prominent research lines. In keeping with the focus of this volume, and for space limitations, we center primarily on cognitive and psycholinguistic approaches to second language acquistion (SLA); however, we also briefly highlight major tenets and insights provided by theoretical approaches focusing on the sociocultural dimensions of L2 learning.

According to VanPatten, *input processing* (IP) is "the initial process by which learners connect grammatical forms with their meanings" ([2004a](#ch15.xhtml_ref15_85){#ch15.xhtml_R_ref15_85 .biblioref role="doc-biblioref"}, p. 5). More broadly, this term is also used to refer to the various processes that L2 learners engage in when deriving *intake*, defined as the linguistic information that enters working memory during input comprehension to potentially impact the developing system. *Interaction* pertains to the processes involved when L2 learners converse orally or in writing with other interlocutors in a communicative setting. Lastly, *output* is the L2 language produced by learners, and it is assumed to reflect their underlying L2 knowledge ([Leow, 2015](#ch15.xhtml_ref15_43){#ch15.xhtml_R_ref15_43 .biblioref role="doc-biblioref"}). More specifically, output has been claimed to encompass speaking, writing, collaborative dialogue, private speech, verbalizing, and verbalized metalinguistic reflection, also known as 'languaging' ([Swain, 2000](#ch15.xhtml_ref15_72){#ch15.xhtml_R_ref15_72 .biblioref role="doc-biblioref"}).
:::

::: {.section aria-labelledby="sec15_2"}
## Historical Perspectives {#ch15.xhtml_sec15_2}

In the late 1970s and early 1980s, the dominant conceptual view in SLA revolved around the role of input, as interaction and output remained of little theoretical interest in explaining SLA. Krashen's input hypothesis ([1980](#ch15.xhtml_ref15_36){#ch15.xhtml_R_ref15_36 .biblioref role="doc-biblioref"}) posited that L2 development occurred implicitly from learners' understanding of comprehensible input, namely, input that was marginally above their current L2 knowledge. According to [Krashen (1982)](#ch15.xhtml_ref15_37){#ch15.xhtml_R_ref15_37 .biblioref role="doc-biblioref"}, understanding L2 input entailed that learners were "focused on the meaning and not the form of the message" (p. 21). With input conceptualized as critical in L2 development, efforts centered on examining how comprehensible input could be implemented, such as by providing contextual cues or simplifying complex grammar. []{#ch15.xhtml_p213 .pagebreak aria-label=" page 213. " role="doc-pagebreak"}At this time, L2 output---including any that occurred during interaction---continued to be viewed as an external manifestation of what had been learned.

[Krashen's (1982)](#ch15.xhtml_ref15_37){.biblioref role="doc-biblioref"} account came under criticism as researchers observed that input comprehension did not always result in successful learning. As the potential of communicative interaction for supporting L2 learning gained recognition, [Long (1981)](#ch15.xhtml_ref15_47){#ch15.xhtml_R_ref15_47 .biblioref role="doc-biblioref"} postulated the interaction hypothesis, which emphasized conversational interaction as a primary site for SLA. Here, he argued that L2 learning results from the meaning negotiation that takes place when speakers sort out communication breakdowns. Such breakdowns trigger interactional adjustments---through communicative strategies, such as clarification requests or confirmation checks---as interlocutors work together to achieve mutual understanding. The early interaction hypothesis thus placed emphasis on the potential of interaction as grounds for providing rich access to comprehensible input, with output viewed as a tool for driving meaning negotiation.

In the later 1980s, researchers began to challenge the premise that comprehensible input was the only catalyst of SLA. Against this background, [Swain (1985)](#ch15.xhtml_ref15_70){#ch15.xhtml_R_ref15_70 .biblioref role="doc-biblioref"} formulated the first version of the output hypothesis. While input continued to be viewed as necessary for SLA, as it does to this day, Swain ([1995](#ch15.xhtml_ref15_71){#ch15.xhtml_R_ref15_71 .biblioref role="doc-biblioref"}, [2005](#ch15.xhtml_ref15_73){#ch15.xhtml_R_ref15_73 .biblioref role="doc-biblioref"}) argued that output could serve various facilitative functions. Output was claimed to improve learners' control over their linguistic knowledge and allow them to test hypotheses about the L2 while promoting metalinguistic reflection. Notably, output was also claimed to encourage conscious attention to relevant L2 forms in the input. The assumption was that when learners produce the L2, they are likely to become aware of linguistic problems, such as lack of L2 knowledge to express a given meaning, and then seek solutions in the input (e.g., a model utterance featuring that meaning).

Both the interaction hypothesis and the output hypothesis were refined in the late 1990s and early 2000s, following a vast body of research. The expansions were also motivated by the greater theoretical importance ascribed to the role of attention in SLA, particularly through [Schmidt's (1990)](#ch15.xhtml_ref15_66){#ch15.xhtml_R_ref15_66 .biblioref role="doc-biblioref"} noticing hypothesis, which stated that, to learn a formal L2 feature, learners must first consciously notice it in the input. In the updated interaction hypothesis, [Long (1996)](#ch15.xhtml_ref15_48){#ch15.xhtml_R_ref15_48 .biblioref role="doc-biblioref"} noted that comprehensible input could not be a sufficient condition for L2 learning and highlighted the need for learners to become consciously aware of discrepancies between their linguistic knowledge and the L2 (i.e., noticing the 'gap'). Incorporating [Swain's (1985)](#ch15.xhtml_ref15_70){.biblioref role="doc-biblioref"} claims, output and feedback were thus proposed to play an important role in facilitating L2 learning processes through interaction. Beyond creating opportunities for meaning negotiation, interactional feedback would also prompt learners to modify their own output, which could aid L2 learning. Additionally, growing research evidence led [Swain (2005)](#ch15.xhtml_ref15_73){.biblioref role="doc-biblioref"} to connect the consciousness-raising function of output with the notion of depth of processing ([Craik & Lockhart, 1972](#ch15.xhtml_ref15_14){#ch15.xhtml_R_ref15_14 .biblioref role="doc-biblioref"}). To this effect, the influence of output is now proposed to go beyond mere noticing, in [Schmidt's (1990)](#ch15.xhtml_ref15_66){.biblioref role="doc-biblioref"} terms, to promote more integrative processing of L2 forms (e.g., [Izumi, 2003](#ch15.xhtml_ref15_28){#ch15.xhtml_R_ref15_28 .biblioref role="doc-biblioref"}; [Swain, 2005](#ch15.xhtml_ref15_73){.biblioref role="doc-biblioref"}). We discuss this point further in the following section.

Other theoretical proposals premised on the role of attention also began to recognize the L2 processing benefits of interaction and output. For instance, VanPatten---whose input processing model (e.g., [VanPatten, 2004a](#ch15.xhtml_ref15_85){.biblioref role="doc-biblioref"}) seeks to explain how learners process L2 data and make form--meaning connections---has interpreted Swain's claims as suggesting that "output pushes learners to be better processors of input" ([VanPatten, 2004b](#ch15.xhtml_ref15_86){#ch15.xhtml_R_ref15_86 .biblioref role="doc-biblioref"}, p. 42). VanPatten acknowledges that producing the L2 while having access to input may lead to certain changes in learners' grammar, but cautions that research is needed to fully understand how that can happen. In [Gass's (2018)](#ch15.xhtml_ref15_22){#ch15.xhtml_R_ref15_22 .biblioref role="doc-biblioref"} SLA model, interaction and output are seen as beneficial in developing new knowledge, as conversational feedback serves as an important ground on which learners test hypotheses about the L2. Recently, [Leow (2015)](#ch15.xhtml_ref15_43){.biblioref role="doc-biblioref"} has conceptualized output as both a product representing learning []{#ch15.xhtml_p214 .pagebreak aria-label=" page 214. " role="doc-pagebreak"}outcomes as well as a process contributing to SLA by promoting attention to and further processing of L2 input. According to Leow, learners may modify their L2 knowledge based on the depth of processing and the awareness level that results from their engagement with input, both of which can be benefitted through output and interactional feedback.

Social perspectives with roots in educational and anthropological linguistics have also emerged since the 1990s as alternative accounts of the role of meaningful interaction in L2 development. Within these paradigms, all L2 development is said to originate in social interaction, led by learners' subjective understanding of discursive contexts and speaker intentions, and their joint activity to accomplish personal goals. Accordingly, L2 production is not only viewed as facilitating linguistic development, but as embodying it. Sociocultural research inspired by Vygotskyan theory has also suggested that interactional feedback is most effective when it responds in quantity and quality to real-time evidence of learners' discursive needs rather than being provided in a controlled, uniform manner (e.g., [Davin, 2016](#ch15.xhtml_ref15_15){#ch15.xhtml_R_ref15_15 .biblioref role="doc-biblioref"}; [Lantolf & Poehner, 2013](#ch15.xhtml_ref15_40){#ch15.xhtml_R_ref15_40 .biblioref role="doc-biblioref"}). Similarly, within a conversation analysis approach, recent research has documented how, through individual agency and contextual cues, L2 discourse participants orient their attention and participation toward emergent, jointly identified linguistic 'learnables' (i.e., shared pedagogical foci) in interaction (see, e.g., [Jakonen, 2018](#ch15.xhtml_ref15_32){#ch15.xhtml_R_ref15_32 .biblioref role="doc-biblioref"}).
:::

::: {.section aria-labelledby="sec15_3"}
## Critical Issues and Topics {#ch15.xhtml_sec15_3}

Understanding the cognitive mechanisms underlying the connection between interaction/output and L2 IP has been a longstanding critical issue in cognitively oriented SLA research. Attention, which [Robinson (2003)](#ch15.xhtml_ref15_62){#ch15.xhtml_R_ref15_62 .biblioref role="doc-biblioref"} defines as "the process that encodes language input, keeps it active in working and short-term memory, and retrieves it from long-term memory" (p. 631), has been recognized as a key mechanism in SLA. In this regard, interaction is viewed as an attention-enhancing tool, with L2 input providing the grounds on which learners' L2 hypotheses are tested (e.g., [Gass, 2018](#ch15.xhtml_ref15_22){.biblioref role="doc-biblioref"}; [Long, 1996](#ch15.xhtml_ref15_48){.biblioref role="doc-biblioref"}). Regarding output, [Izumi (2003)](#ch15.xhtml_ref15_28){.biblioref role="doc-biblioref"} explains that, when learners produce (or attempt to produce) the L2, they engage processes such as lexical access, grammatical encoding, and monitoring, through which they can become aware of their L2 knowledge limitations. This is hypothesized to increase learners' focused attention to and depth of processing of L2 features that may have otherwise been superficially processed during input comprehension (see also [Swain, 2005](#ch15.xhtml_ref15_73){.biblioref role="doc-biblioref"}).

Thus, output combined with input---including in interactional contexts---is posited to maximize opportunities for creating new form--meaning mappings because it can push learners to analyze the semantic and conceptual features of L2 forms in the input, beyond just increasing the likelihood of noticing and rehearsing them in short-term memory. From this perspective, subprocesses such as strengthening or restructuring of form--meaning connections (see [VanPatten, 2004b](#ch15.xhtml_ref15_86){.biblioref role="doc-biblioref"}) can also be promoted by interactional feedback and output alongside input. Despite such theoretical refinements, it is worth noting that little empirical work has sought to provide evidence for the superior mechanisms of L2 IP generated by output and interaction, since most studies to date have focused on [Schmidt's (1990)](#ch15.xhtml_ref15_66){.biblioref role="doc-biblioref"} construct of noticing (see 'Current Contributions' section).

Another critical issue concerns the conditions under which the facilitative effects of output/interaction may unfold and the methodological challenges in implementing such conditions empirically. The psycholinguistic rationale presented earlier demonstrates that, to derive the benefits of interaction/output discussed previously, learners must produce meaning in the L2 using their own resources, namely, they must be actively involved in formulating their message into linguistic material in the language-processing system (e.g., [Izumi, 2003](#ch15.xhtml_ref15_28){.biblioref role="doc-biblioref"}; [Swain, 2005](#ch15.xhtml_ref15_73){.biblioref role="doc-biblioref"}). []{#ch15.xhtml_p215 .pagebreak aria-label=" page 215. " role="doc-pagebreak"}Indeed, [Izumi and Izumi (2004)](#ch15.xhtml_ref15_30){#ch15.xhtml_R_ref15_30 .biblioref role="doc-biblioref"} show that when L2 production is instead operationalized as verbatim repetition without active involvement, IP is not facilitated as hypothesized. Thus, researchers should carefully consider the extent and range of production mechanisms triggered by their experimental designs. We revisit this issue in the 'Recommendations for Practice' section.
:::

:::::: {.section aria-labelledby="sec15_4"}
## Current Contributions {#ch15.xhtml_sec15_4}

::: {.section aria-labelledby="sec15_4_1"}
### Interaction: Processes, Outcomes, and Moderating Conditions {#ch15.xhtml_sec15_4_1}

By now, substantive research has considered the benefits of L2 interaction on L2 IP, particularly from the perspective of feedback (see [Loewen & Sato, 2018](#ch15.xhtml_ref15_46){#ch15.xhtml_R_ref15_46 .biblioref role="doc-biblioref"}; [Nassaji, 2016](#ch15.xhtml_ref15_58){#ch15.xhtml_R_ref15_58 .biblioref role="doc-biblioref"}). Numerous studies have assessed the effectiveness of different types of interactional feedback, often comparing more implicit types of feedback---primarily in the form of recasts---to more explicit types of feedback, such as metalinguistic clues and reformulations (e.g., [Ellis et al., 2006](#ch15.xhtml_ref15_18){#ch15.xhtml_R_ref15_18 .biblioref role="doc-biblioref"}; [Lyster & Izquierdo, 2009](#ch15.xhtml_ref15_49){#ch15.xhtml_R_ref15_49 .biblioref role="doc-biblioref"}; [Yilmaz, 2012](#ch15.xhtml_ref15_89){#ch15.xhtml_R_ref15_89 .biblioref role="doc-biblioref"}). These studies have adopted a range of experimental and quasi-experimental design approaches in both laboratory and classroom contexts. Since the efficacy of corrective feedback is assumed to be largely contingent on learners' awareness of its intent and their conscious attention to it, many studies have examined the extent to which learners notice feedback (e.g., [Kim & Han, 2007](#ch15.xhtml_ref15_34){#ch15.xhtml_R_ref15_34 .biblioref role="doc-biblioref"}; [Mackey et al., 2000](#ch15.xhtml_ref15_50){#ch15.xhtml_R_ref15_50 .biblioref role="doc-biblioref"}), as well as which features of feedback enhance its recognition and further processing (e.g., [Bao et al., 2011](#ch15.xhtml_ref15_8){#ch15.xhtml_R_ref15_8 .biblioref role="doc-biblioref"}; [Carpenter et al., 2006](#ch15.xhtml_ref15_13){#ch15.xhtml_R_ref15_13 .biblioref role="doc-biblioref"}). For instance, [Bao et al. (2011)](#ch15.xhtml_ref15_8){.biblioref role="doc-biblioref"} found that learners were more likely to notice teachers' recasts when provided with a rising as opposed to a falling intonation. Accumulated evidence also suggests that feedback efficacy depends on the characteristics of the linguistic target, as lower-salience forms may be less amenable to enhanced processing during interaction (e.g., [Sato & Loewen, 2018](#ch15.xhtml_ref15_65){#ch15.xhtml_R_ref15_65 .biblioref role="doc-biblioref"}; [Yilmaz, 2012](#ch15.xhtml_ref15_89){.biblioref role="doc-biblioref"}).

Another major line of research has centered on the effects of mode of interaction. Several studies have examined whether learners' noticing and modified output behaviors differ in face-to-face and synchronous computer-mediated communication modes, such as text chats (e.g., [Gurzynski-Weiss & Baralt, 2015](#ch15.xhtml_ref15_23){#ch15.xhtml_R_ref15_23 .biblioref role="doc-biblioref"}; [Lai & Zhao, 2006](#ch15.xhtml_ref15_39){#ch15.xhtml_R_ref15_39 .biblioref role="doc-biblioref"}; [Yuksel & Inan, 2014](#ch15.xhtml_ref15_92){#ch15.xhtml_R_ref15_92 .biblioref role="doc-biblioref"}; [Ziegler, 2017](#ch15.xhtml_ref15_95){#ch15.xhtml_R_ref15_95 .biblioref role="doc-biblioref"}). While it is assumed that text-chat environments can increase the affordances of interaction for promoting noticing and deeper processing of L2 input, given the increased durability and visibility of written language, among other reasons, findings to date remain inconclusive. For instance, [Lai and Zhao (2006)](#ch15.xhtml_ref15_39){.biblioref role="doc-biblioref"} provided initial evidence suggesting that written text-chat conditions can lead to greater noticing, but this was the case for learners' noticing of their own output and not the input received through feedback. Expanding on these insights, [Yuksel and Inan (2014)](#ch15.xhtml_ref15_92){.biblioref role="doc-biblioref"} showed that learners notice greater instances of meaning negotiation in the text-chat environment relative to the face-to-face environment. However, work by [Gurzynski-Weiss and Baralt (2015)](#ch15.xhtml_ref15_23){.biblioref role="doc-biblioref"} and [Ziegler (2017)](#ch15.xhtml_ref15_95){.biblioref role="doc-biblioref"} has reported no significant differences in the degree of learners' noticing across modes.

A rapidly growing area of research has also begun to focus on the effects of timing conditions on the efficacy of corrective feedback. These studies, conducted both in laboratory contexts via text-chat ([Arroyo & Yilmaz, 2018](#ch15.xhtml_ref15_7){#ch15.xhtml_R_ref15_7 .biblioref role="doc-biblioref"}) and classroom face-to-face contexts ([Fu & Li, 2022](#ch15.xhtml_ref15_20){#ch15.xhtml_R_ref15_20 .biblioref role="doc-biblioref"}; [Li et al., 2016](#ch15.xhtml_ref15_45){#ch15.xhtml_R_ref15_45 .biblioref role="doc-biblioref"}, [2019](#ch15.xhtml_ref15_44){#ch15.xhtml_R_ref15_44 .biblioref role="doc-biblioref"}), have compared the differential benefits of immediate and delayed interactional feedback. Findings appear to point to greater advantages for immediate feedback in both contexts ([Arroyo & Yilmaz, 2018](#ch15.xhtml_ref15_7){.biblioref role="doc-biblioref"}; [Fu & Li, 2022](#ch15.xhtml_ref15_20){.biblioref role="doc-biblioref"}; [Li et al., 2016](#ch15.xhtml_ref15_45){.biblioref role="doc-biblioref"}), suggesting that this timing entails less taxing and more favorable conditions for noticing, output-feedback comparisons, and further processing of L2 forms. Nonetheless, more research considering data on learners' cognitive processes during feedback provision is needed to better understand how feedback timing []{#ch15.xhtml_p216 .pagebreak aria-label=" page 216. " role="doc-pagebreak"}conditions and other moderating factors can constrain L2 IP, as most studies to date have either focused on L2 noticing only or have made inferences about L2 processing indirectly based only on product-level measures.
:::

::: {.section aria-labelledby="sec15_4_2"}
### Output: Processes, Outcomes, and Moderating Conditions {#ch15.xhtml_sec15_4_2}

When it comes to the role of output in enhancing L2 IP (Swain, [1985](#ch15.xhtml_ref15_70){.biblioref role="doc-biblioref"}, [1995](#ch15.xhtml_ref15_71){.biblioref role="doc-biblioref"}, [2005](#ch15.xhtml_ref15_73){.biblioref role="doc-biblioref"}), the amount of empirical work to date is modest relative to research on interactional feedback. In their seminal study, [Swain and Lapkin (1995)](#ch15.xhtml_ref15_74){#ch15.xhtml_R_ref15_74 .biblioref role="doc-biblioref"} examined the cognitive processes that L2 learners engaged in during a writing task using think-aloud protocols. Introspective comments revealed that learners became aware of gaps in their L2 knowledge during the sole act of writing and that, upon encountering a linguistic problem, they engaged in mental processes that benefit L2 development, such as testing hypotheses about the L2.

A major line of research has sought to expand Swain and Lapkin's work by documenting what learners notice when they produce the L2 and later have access to relevant input (e.g., [Hanaoka, 2007](#ch15.xhtml_ref15_25){#ch15.xhtml_R_ref15_25 .biblioref role="doc-biblioref"}; [Hanaoka & Izumi, 2012](#ch15.xhtml_ref15_26){#ch15.xhtml_R_ref15_26 .biblioref role="doc-biblioref"}; [Toth, 2006](#ch15.xhtml_ref15_78){#ch15.xhtml_R_ref15_78 .biblioref role="doc-biblioref"}). Overall, these studies have provided broad support for [Swain and Lapkin's (1995)](#ch15.xhtml_ref15_74){.biblioref role="doc-biblioref"} analysis, helping refine some of their initial proposals. For instance, [Toth's (2006)](#ch15.xhtml_ref15_78){.biblioref role="doc-biblioref"} classroom study revealed how timely teacher-provided "procedural assistance" in using the target form while L2 learners participated in whole-class speaking tasks led to stronger learning outcomes than those associated with input-only versions of the same tasks with a focus on receptive decision-making. Meanwhile, in the domain of L2 writing, [Hanaoka and Izumi (2012)](#ch15.xhtml_ref15_26){.biblioref role="doc-biblioref"} found that L2 learners consciously noticed linguistic problems while engaged in L2 writing; importantly, however, some of the challenges they encountered were not manifested in the written text they produced. This finding led the authors to propose that covert output, in addition to externally verbalized output, can lead to L2 processing benefits. Results also indicated that learners paid attention to relevant input in the feedback texts as they attempted to resolve linguistic problems, although not all types of feedback were found to be equally effective in this regard.

Another line of work has focused on empirically testing the noticing/triggering function of output as proposed by Swain ([1995](#ch15.xhtml_ref15_71){.biblioref role="doc-biblioref"}, [2005](#ch15.xhtml_ref15_73){.biblioref role="doc-biblioref"}). Initial studies by [Izumi et al. (1999)](#ch15.xhtml_ref15_31){#ch15.xhtml_R_ref15_31 .biblioref role="doc-biblioref"} and [Izumi and Bigelow (2000)](#ch15.xhtml_ref15_29){#ch15.xhtml_R_ref15_29 .biblioref role="doc-biblioref"} have been followed by a series of conceptual replications and extensions, mostly focused on written production, investigating the extent to which engaging in L2 output heightens L2 IP ([Izumi, 2002](#ch15.xhtml_ref15_27){#ch15.xhtml_R_ref15_27 .biblioref role="doc-biblioref"}; [Izumi & Izumi, 2004](#ch15.xhtml_ref15_30){.biblioref role="doc-biblioref"}; [Leeser, 2008](#ch15.xhtml_ref15_42){#ch15.xhtml_R_ref15_42 .biblioref role="doc-biblioref"}; [Russell, 2014](#ch15.xhtml_ref15_63){#ch15.xhtml_R_ref15_63 .biblioref role="doc-biblioref"}; [Song & Suh, 2008](#ch15.xhtml_ref15_69){#ch15.xhtml_R_ref15_69 .biblioref role="doc-biblioref"}; [Uggen, 2012](#ch15.xhtml_ref15_83){#ch15.xhtml_R_ref15_83 .biblioref role="doc-biblioref"}; [Zalbidea, 2021](#ch15.xhtml_ref15_93){#ch15.xhtml_R_ref15_93 .biblioref role="doc-biblioref"}). Early studies that focused on noticing failed to find consistent evidence in support of this function of output ([Izumi et al., 1999](#ch15.xhtml_ref15_31){.biblioref role="doc-biblioref"}; [Izumi & Bigelow, 2000](#ch15.xhtml_ref15_29){.biblioref role="doc-biblioref"}; [Izumi & Izumi, 2004](#ch15.xhtml_ref15_30){.biblioref role="doc-biblioref"}; [Leeser, 2008](#ch15.xhtml_ref15_42){.biblioref role="doc-biblioref"}). However, increased experimental control and the inclusion of introspective measures of L2 cognition such as stimulated recall has led to a different picture, with more robust output effects reported across studies in terms of both enhanced L2 processing and learning outcomes (e.g., [Russell, 2014](#ch15.xhtml_ref15_63){.biblioref role="doc-biblioref"}; [Uggen, 2012](#ch15.xhtml_ref15_83){.biblioref role="doc-biblioref"}; [Zalbidea, 2021](#ch15.xhtml_ref15_93){.biblioref role="doc-biblioref"}). For instance, combining process- and product-level measures, [Zalbidea (2021)](#ch15.xhtml_ref15_93){.biblioref role="doc-biblioref"} found that output led to substantial benefits in L2 IP, including the triggering of semantic associations and hypotheses about the referential meaning of the L2 target. These benefits then extended to L2 achievement in the form of greater receptive and productive accuracy within both the oral and written modalities, with the strongest effects found for higher-salience morphosyntactic forms.

Lastly, an important strand of research has compared processing instruction (see [VanPatten, 2004a](#ch15.xhtml_ref15_85){.biblioref role="doc-biblioref"}) to meaning-based output instruction, a type of communicative form-focused practice in which learners are required to employ their own linguistic resources to express meaning (e.g., [Benati, 2005](#ch15.xhtml_ref15_9){#ch15.xhtml_R_ref15_9 .biblioref role="doc-biblioref"}; [Keating & Farley, 2008](#ch15.xhtml_ref15_33){#ch15.xhtml_R_ref15_33 .biblioref role="doc-biblioref"}; [Morgan-Short & Bowden, 2006](#ch15.xhtml_ref15_57){#ch15.xhtml_R_ref15_57 .biblioref role="doc-biblioref"}). While studies []{#ch15.xhtml_p217 .pagebreak aria-label=" page 217. " role="doc-pagebreak"}vary widely in how meaning-based output instruction is operationalized, most findings indicate that this type of instruction can be as beneficial as processing instruction (e.g., [Farley, 2004](#ch15.xhtml_ref15_19){#ch15.xhtml_R_ref15_19 .biblioref role="doc-biblioref"}; [Keating & Farley, 2008](#ch15.xhtml_ref15_33){.biblioref role="doc-biblioref"}; [Morgan-Short & Bowden, 2006](#ch15.xhtml_ref15_57){.biblioref role="doc-biblioref"}), although some evidence suggests processing instruction to be more effective ([VanPatten et al., 2009](#ch15.xhtml_ref15_87){#ch15.xhtml_R_ref15_87 .biblioref role="doc-biblioref"}). Of note, [VanPatten et al. (2009)](#ch15.xhtml_ref15_87){.biblioref role="doc-biblioref"} have argued that the advantages of meaning-based output instruction are only noticeable when learners produce output but are also given the opportunity to process input featuring the target form.
:::

::: {.section aria-labelledby="sec15_4_3"}
### Individual Differences Underlying Processes and Outcomes {#ch15.xhtml_sec15_4_3}

Research has long sought to understand how individual difference variables modulate the connections between interaction, output, and IP. Previous studies have predominantly focused on identifying the cognitive factors that underlie noticing and further L2 processing behaviors, although research has also considered affective and psychosocial factors, such as motivation. Working memory has garnered most attention in this research, given its centrality in the processes of attention and noticing and its role in facilitating what [Skehan (1998)](#ch15.xhtml_ref15_67){#ch15.xhtml_R_ref15_67 .biblioref role="doc-biblioref"} describes as "consciousness-enhanced processing" of L2 input (p. 57). Most studies have focused on interactional feedback (e.g., [Kim et al., 2015](#ch15.xhtml_ref15_35){#ch15.xhtml_R_ref15_35 .biblioref role="doc-biblioref"}; [Mackey et al., 2002](#ch15.xhtml_ref15_52){#ch15.xhtml_R_ref15_52 .biblioref role="doc-biblioref"}; [Yilmaz, 2013](#ch15.xhtml_ref15_90){#ch15.xhtml_R_ref15_90 .biblioref role="doc-biblioref"}; [Révész, 2012](#ch15.xhtml_ref15_60){#ch15.xhtml_R_ref15_60 .biblioref role="doc-biblioref"}; [Trofimovich et al., 2007](#ch15.xhtml_ref15_82){#ch15.xhtml_R_ref15_82 .biblioref role="doc-biblioref"}), although some research has also considered output (e.g., [Mackey et al., 2010](#ch15.xhtml_ref15_51){#ch15.xhtml_R_ref15_51 .biblioref role="doc-biblioref"}; [Zalbidea & Sanz, 2020](#ch15.xhtml_ref15_94){#ch15.xhtml_R_ref15_94 .biblioref role="doc-biblioref"}). Accumulated evidence suggests that functions like attention, memory, and executive control are differentially involved based on the characteristics of the target form and the L2 context.

Regarding learners' noticing of corrective feedback (particularly in the form of recasts), research has reported positive roles for executive working memory (e.g., [Kim et al., 2015](#ch15.xhtml_ref15_35){.biblioref role="doc-biblioref"}; [Mackey et al., 2002](#ch15.xhtml_ref15_52){.biblioref role="doc-biblioref"}), phonological working memory (e.g., [Mackey et al., 2002](#ch15.xhtml_ref15_52){.biblioref role="doc-biblioref"}), and language analytic ability (e.g., [Ahn, 2012](#ch15.xhtml_ref15_6){#ch15.xhtml_R_ref15_6 .biblioref role="doc-biblioref"}) in the rate with which learners pay conscious attention to oral recasts as well as in the morphosyntactic gains derived from interactive tasks. Although [Trofimovich et al. (2007)](#ch15.xhtml_ref15_82){.biblioref role="doc-biblioref"} did not find any memory effects in terms of noticing, phonological working memory, attention control, and language analytic ability were found to be positively related to L2 production accuracy. A later study by [Révész (2012)](#ch15.xhtml_ref15_60){.biblioref role="doc-biblioref"} further showed that the link between memory functions and L2 development following oral recasts varies based on the L2 outcome measures considered. While cognitive factors have garnered much interest from an L2 interaction standpoint, fewer investigations have considered their involvement from an output perspective. In a notable exception, research by [Mackey et al. (2010)](#ch15.xhtml_ref15_51){.biblioref role="doc-biblioref"} revealed that executive working memory positively relates to production of modified output following feedback.

Another important set of studies has examined how cognitive factors may differentially relate to the efficacy of various interactional feedback conditions (e.g., [Lai et al., 2008](#ch15.xhtml_ref15_38){#ch15.xhtml_R_ref15_38 .biblioref role="doc-biblioref"}; [Li et al., 2019](#ch15.xhtml_ref15_44){.biblioref role="doc-biblioref"}; [Yilmaz, 2013](#ch15.xhtml_ref15_90){.biblioref role="doc-biblioref"}; [Yilmaz & Granena, 2019](#ch15.xhtml_ref15_91){#ch15.xhtml_R_ref15_91 .biblioref role="doc-biblioref"}). This research has largely followed an aptitude-treatment interaction approach by investigating interplays between cognitive factors and feedback types. For instance, [Lai et al. (2008)](#ch15.xhtml_ref15_38){.biblioref role="doc-biblioref"} found that phonological working memory was positively linked to L2 learners' rate of noticing of recasts during text-chat communication; however, this effect was only apparent for non-contingent recasts, which were spaced further apart from learners' output. In a recent study that considered several underresearched cognitive factors, [Yilmaz and Granena (2019)](#ch15.xhtml_ref15_91){.biblioref role="doc-biblioref"} reported that implicit sequence learning ability predicted L2 learning in an implicit feedback (recast) condition, whereas phonological working memory did so in an explicit feedback (rejection and reformulation) condition. They also found that additional cognitive skills, such as processing speed, related to learners' feedback awareness in the implicit feedback condition.
:::
::::::

::: {.section aria-labelledby="sec15_5"}
## []{#ch15.xhtml_p218 .pagebreak aria-label=" page 218. " role="doc-pagebreak"}Main Research Methods {#ch15.xhtml_sec15_5}

A wide array of methods and data elicitation procedures have been employed to draw inferences about the roles of interaction and output in L2 IP. Available measures can be distinguished at least based on the timing of administration (online vs. offline) and the type of data gathered (quantitative vs. qualitative, product-level vs. process-level).

Researchers have employed different strategies to tap into learners' IP behaviors as they engaged in interaction and output, including measures of noticing such as self-report, immediate repetition or report, and others such as the underlining rate while reading (e.g., [Izumi et al., 1999](#ch15.xhtml_ref15_31){.biblioref role="doc-biblioref"}; [Izumi & Bigelow, 2000](#ch15.xhtml_ref15_29){.biblioref role="doc-biblioref"}). More recent work has highlighted the value of employing introspective measures of attention where learners comment on the cognitive processes that they engage in while processing L2 data, either in an online format, such as a think-aloud protocol, or an offline, retrospective format, such as stimulated recall (e.g., [Kim et al., 2015](#ch15.xhtml_ref15_35){.biblioref role="doc-biblioref"}; [Uggen, 2012](#ch15.xhtml_ref15_83){.biblioref role="doc-biblioref"}; [Zalbidea, 2021](#ch15.xhtml_ref15_93){.biblioref role="doc-biblioref"}). Unlike the previously mentioned quantitative measures, which indicate only whether noticing has occurred, introspective measures have provided researchers with richer explanatory information regarding the nature and depth of learners' L2 processing. Such measures have the potential to bring more robust insights into how learners' mental representations change as they interact with the L2, including the timing at which different subprocesses such as strengthening and restructuring are engaged. Following technological advancements, interactional studies have also increasingly relied on online quantitative measures of attention, such as the eye-tracking technique (e.g., [McDonough et al., 2015](#ch15.xhtml_ref15_53){#ch15.xhtml_R_ref15_53 .biblioref role="doc-biblioref"}; [Smith, 2012](#ch15.xhtml_ref15_68){#ch15.xhtml_R_ref15_68 .biblioref role="doc-biblioref"}). This approach is allowing researchers to establish links between interaction, output, and attentional allocation to relevant L2 input through various eye gaze measures.

While researchers have made efforts to incorporate independent measures tapping into L2 IP behaviors, inferences about the L2 processing effects of interaction and output have also been made indirectly, by considering their impact on learning outcomes in the absence of process-level measures (e.g., [Arroyo & Yilmaz, 2018](#ch15.xhtml_ref15_7){.biblioref role="doc-biblioref"}; [Fu & Li, 2022](#ch15.xhtml_ref15_20){.biblioref role="doc-biblioref"}; [Révész, 2012](#ch15.xhtml_ref15_60){.biblioref role="doc-biblioref"}). This research has usually adopted quantitative experimental or quasi-experimental designs, administering various pre- and post-test measures of L2 knowledge and ability with the goal of assessing the effectiveness of different interaction and output features in laboratory and classroom settings. It is worth noting that the main goal of these studies was not to directly address L2 IP, but to examine behavioral L2 outcomes from different instructional interventions.

Although the tools and measures discussed here have provided valuable insights, they are not without shortcomings (for a critique, see [Nassaji, 2020](#ch15.xhtml_ref15_59){#ch15.xhtml_R_ref15_59 .biblioref role="doc-biblioref"}). The robustness and reliability of inferences made from data gathered through these various measures will depend largely on their methodological limitations---including those pertaining to veridicality, reactivity, and other validity issues---and the extent to which researchers establish data triangulation through a combination of offline/online, product-level/process-level, and quantitative/qualitative data (see Marijuan, this volume).
:::

::: {.section aria-labelledby="sec15_6"}
## Recommendations for Practice {#ch15.xhtml_sec15_6}

Because much of the research on L2 interaction and output aims to identify cognitive mechanisms that can be broadly generalized, recommendations for specific classroom practices can be difficult to discern. Effective instruction necessarily requires a nuanced understanding of how general research-based principles can be applied within the social norms and dynamics of any local context ([Toth et al., 2020](#ch15.xhtml_ref15_81){#ch15.xhtml_R_ref15_81 .biblioref role="doc-biblioref"}). Still, a number of pedagogical principles can be identified from the present discussion.

[]{#ch15.xhtml_p219 .pagebreak aria-label=" page 219. " role="doc-pagebreak"}First, as research on L2 output and interaction has shifted from concerns over the extent to which they foster L2 development to questions about how they do so, a clear consensus has emerged that effective instruction entails ongoing learner engagement in meaningful L2 use across interpretive, presentational, and interpersonal modes, rather than the mechanical manipulation of structures that often still characterizes many L2 classrooms (e.g., [Wong & VanPatten, 2003](#ch15.xhtml_ref15_88){#ch15.xhtml_R_ref15_88 .biblioref role="doc-biblioref"}). As [Toth et al. (2020)](#ch15.xhtml_ref15_81){.biblioref role="doc-biblioref"} argue, where L2 communication is concerned, "we learn by doing," (p. 77) and competent L2 interactions will not readily emerge from such practices. Instead, optimal instruction strategically provides explicit focus-on-form opportunities as a tool to mediate communicative exchanges in a manner consistent with proposals for task-based language teaching (R. [Ellis, 2019](#ch15.xhtml_ref15_17){#ch15.xhtml_R_ref15_17 .biblioref role="doc-biblioref"}; [Samuda & Bygate, 2008](#ch15.xhtml_ref15_64){#ch15.xhtml_R_ref15_64 .biblioref role="doc-biblioref"}).

Within this framework, [Krashen's (1982)](#ch15.xhtml_ref15_37){.biblioref role="doc-biblioref"} vision of instructors as mere providers of comprehensible input has evolved into one where teacher input not only offers linguistic data but also leads learners to attend to interrelationships between L2 form, meaning and use ([Larsen-Freeman, 2003](#ch15.xhtml_ref15_41){#ch15.xhtml_R_ref15_41 .biblioref role="doc-biblioref"}). The current consensus suggests that explicit knowledge about such relationships benefits L2 development by strategically directing learners' attention to key elements of a successful performance, and by lending episodic coherence to noticing and utterance formulation (N.C. [Ellis, 2015](#ch15.xhtml_ref15_16){#ch15.xhtml_R_ref15_16 .biblioref role="doc-biblioref"}; [Roehr, 2008](#ch15.xhtml_ref15_61){#ch15.xhtml_R_ref15_61 .biblioref role="doc-biblioref"}). The co-occurrence of these conscious processes with the processes of mapping L2 features onto intended meanings in turn yields the enhanced depth of processing that is believed to further robust L2 development (N.C. [Ellis, 2015](#ch15.xhtml_ref15_16){.biblioref role="doc-biblioref"}; [Lantolf & Poehner, 2013](#ch15.xhtml_ref15_40){.biblioref role="doc-biblioref"}; [Leow, 2015](#ch15.xhtml_ref15_43){.biblioref role="doc-biblioref"}). Consequently, research on the timing and nature of optimally provided focus-on-form opportunities, whether through corrective feedback or well-crafted grammatical explanations, holds important implications for L2 pedagogy ([Toth et al., 2020](#ch15.xhtml_ref15_81){.biblioref role="doc-biblioref"}). Recent work also suggests that explicit metacognitive training in how learners should engage with input sources and negotiate interaction may help ensure that they maximize their instructional benefits ([Fujii et al., 2016](#ch15.xhtml_ref15_21){#ch15.xhtml_R_ref15_21 .biblioref role="doc-biblioref"}; [Moranski & Henery, 2017](#ch15.xhtml_ref15_54){#ch15.xhtml_R_ref15_54 .biblioref role="doc-biblioref"}). This would mean clearly identifying both the communication objectives of a task as well as any instructional goals about the extent to which learners should attend to accurate use of particular structures. It would also mean modeling discursive strategies to be used when learners encounter difficulties with task performance, including appropriate ways to engage in L2 peer interactions.

Current research on L2 interaction and output also has implications for task design and sequencing within lessons and larger instructional units. Because producing novel utterances requires the integration of discursive planning with the retrieval, formulation, and articulation of morphosyntactic structures ([Izumi, 2003](#ch15.xhtml_ref15_28){.biblioref role="doc-biblioref"}; [Swain, 2005](#ch15.xhtml_ref15_73){.biblioref role="doc-biblioref"}), production tasks arguably make greater demands on working memory than processing equivalent utterances for comprehension alone. It therefore makes pedagogical sense to introduce new linguistic features in lesson units that transition from attending to novel elements in interpretive tasks to guided and independent work in interactive tasks, within a sequence that [Toth and Davin (2016)](#ch15.xhtml_ref15_80){#ch15.xhtml_R_ref15_80 .biblioref role="doc-biblioref"} have dubbed "modeling, guidance, and handover" (p. 153).

Nonetheless, recent studies focusing on learner individual differences suggest that the utility of broad generalizations about how output and interaction should be implemented is limited. Given the social and cognitive diversity among individuals in even small classroom gatherings, effective teaching requires ongoing, real-time assessments of learners' engagement with language, and a readiness to provide tailored, just-in-time feedback and assistance according to emerging learner needs ([Davin, 2016](#ch15.xhtml_ref15_15){.biblioref role="doc-biblioref"}; [van Compernolle, 2015](#ch15.xhtml_ref15_84){#ch15.xhtml_R_ref15_84 .biblioref role="doc-biblioref"}). Although larger-scale studies may find that a given feedback type under certain conditions generally yields stronger results, implementation with individuals in local contexts will require real-time adjustments as teachers assess and diagnose the difficulties that their learners experience ([Burnett, 2011](#ch15.xhtml_ref15_12){#ch15.xhtml_R_ref15_12 .biblioref role="doc-biblioref"}; [Lantolf & Poehner, 2013](#ch15.xhtml_ref15_40){.biblioref role="doc-biblioref"}; [Toth, 2006](#ch15.xhtml_ref15_78){.biblioref role="doc-biblioref"}). Relatedly, while research has amply focused on the linguistic and []{#ch15.xhtml_p220 .pagebreak aria-label=" page 220. " role="doc-pagebreak"}perceptual cues that modulate L2 IP, many other teacher- and material-related factors can strongly shape how learners understand the purpose of classroom interactions, including whether the teacher intends for them to focus on form and/or meaning ([Hall, 2004](#ch15.xhtml_ref15_24){#ch15.xhtml_R_ref15_24 .biblioref role="doc-biblioref"}; Toth, [2004](#ch15.xhtml_ref15_77){#ch15.xhtml_R_ref15_77 .biblioref role="doc-biblioref"}, [2011](#ch15.xhtml_ref15_79){#ch15.xhtml_R_ref15_79 .biblioref role="doc-biblioref"}).
:::

::: {.section aria-labelledby="sec15_7"}
## Future Directions {#ch15.xhtml_sec15_7}

While extensive research has investigated the roles of interaction and output in L2 development, there is a need for more studies to explicitly consider their effects on L2 IP beyond mere noticing, as previously mentioned. Going forward, mixed methods designs that systematically and purposefully combine quantitative and qualitative product- and process-level data, including, for example, behavioral accuracy and reaction time measures on the one hand, and introspective comments and eye gaze patterns on the other, promise to be most informative and reliable for making robust inferences about L2 IP.

Additionally, much of what we know comes from studies conducted in classroom and laboratory contexts, where researchers can implement carefully structured tasks. Recent work has begun to examine how L2 interaction and output may play out in more naturalistic L2 environments. For instance, [Bryfonski and Sanz (2018)](#ch15.xhtml_ref15_11){#ch15.xhtml_R_ref15_11 .biblioref role="doc-biblioref"} investigated how L2 learners studying abroad in Spain engaged with interactional feedback during informal chats with native speakers. They found that unstructured peer conversations provided rich input and modified output opportunities, with feedback exchanges decreasing over the course of the stay abroad. Importantly, L2 learners were relatively accurate on remembering the targeted words and phrases months after the sojourn, indicating enhanced noticing of peer feedback in immersive, input-rich L2 contexts.

Research is also increasingly focusing on heritage language learners (e.g., [Bowles et al., 2014](#ch15.xhtml_ref15_10){#ch15.xhtml_R_ref15_10 .biblioref role="doc-biblioref"}; [Torres & Cung, 2019](#ch15.xhtml_ref15_76){#ch15.xhtml_R_ref15_76 .biblioref role="doc-biblioref"}), a population that remains underrepresented in instructional studies, despite their increasing presence in classrooms. Heritage learners grow up exposed to a minority language at home and generally arrive at the classroom with a certain degree of linguistic knowledge, which is usually implicit and automatized. Recent efforts have investigated how different types and interaction modes promote opportunities for heritage language development. For example, [Torres and Cung (2019)](#ch15.xhtml_ref15_76){.biblioref role="doc-biblioref"} examined interactions of advanced heritage learners paired with either a heritage or an L2 learner during collaborative writing in face-to-face and text-chat environments. Results revealed that heritage-L2 pairs produced a greater amount of self-repair episodes relative to the heritage-heritage pairs and that heritage learners initiated discussions about linguistic problems more frequently in the face-to-face mode.

In recent years, the field has also experienced a general thrust of methodological innovation while recognizing the importance of increasing the external validity and replicability of SLA findings. Multisite research designs are seen as particularly valuable for this purpose (e.g., [Moranski & Zalbidea, 2022](#ch15.xhtml_ref15_55){#ch15.xhtml_R_ref15_55 .biblioref role="doc-biblioref"}; [Moranski & Ziegler, 2020](#ch15.xhtml_ref15_56){#ch15.xhtml_R_ref15_56 .biblioref role="doc-biblioref"}). For instance, [Moranski and Zalbidea (2022)](#ch15.xhtml_ref15_55){.biblioref role="doc-biblioref"} employed a multisite design to examine the effectiveness of different types of explicit instruction across eight intact classrooms in three US high schools. Notably, more than half of the variance in L2 accuracy was found to be attributable to within-classroom variation (i.e., groupings of students within classrooms and schools). Thus, regardless of the efficacy of each pedagogical intervention, learners who were in the same class or school shared many similarities in the way they engaged with L2 input and tasks, illustrating the central importance of contextual/local factors. Multi-site designs can provide an effective roadmap for understanding differential interaction and output effects across settings and learner groups. To this end, we also echo the need to broaden the scope of L2 IP research to include nonacademic L2 learner samples (e.g., [Tarone & Bigelow, 2005](#ch15.xhtml_ref15_75){#ch15.xhtml_R_ref15_75 .biblioref role="doc-biblioref"}).
:::

::: {.section .reference aria-labelledby="r15_1"}
## []{#ch15.xhtml_p221 .pagebreak aria-label=" page 221. " role="doc-pagebreak"}Further Reading {#ch15.xhtml_r15_1}

- [Adams, R. J., & Oliver, R. (2019). `<cite>`{=html}Teaching through peer interaction`</cite>`{=html}. Routledge.]{#ch15.xhtml_ref15_1}

  This book provides a research-based, pedagogical rationale for implementing peer interaction in L2 classrooms, with example tasks and excerpts from classroom transcripts to illustrate its proposals. Its target audience is L2 teaching practitioners.

- [Loewen, S. (2020). `<cite>`{=html}Introduction to instructed second language acquisition`</cite>`{=html} (2nd ed.). Routledge.]{#ch15.xhtml_ref15_2}

  This book provides an overview of theoretical and empirical L2 research from a cognitive-interactionist perspective. It is targeted at beginning scholars and researchers in SLA.

- [Mackey, A. (2012). `<cite>`{=html}Input, interaction, and corrective feedback in L2 learning`</cite>`{=html}. Oxford University Press.]{#ch15.xhtml_ref15_3}

  This book offers an advanced exploration of the theoretical constructs and empirical findings that underlie the interaction hypothesis. It is intended for experienced scholars in SLA.

- [Sato, M., & Ballinger, S. (Eds.). (2016). `<cite>`{=html}Peer interaction and second language learning: Pedagogical potential and research agenda`</cite>`{=html}. John Benjamins.]{#ch15.xhtml_ref15_4}

  This edited volume consists of an engaging collection of empirical studies on peer interaction from a variety of theoretical perspectives. It is intended for experienced scholars in SLA.

- [van Compernolle, R. A. (2015). `<cite>`{=html}Interaction and second language development: A Vygotskyan perspective`</cite>`{=html}. John Benjamins.]{#ch15.xhtml_ref15_5}

  This book offers an advanced but accessible exploration of the theoretical constructs and empirical findings that underlie a sociocultural perspective on L2 interaction. It is intended for experienced scholars in SLA.
:::

::: {.section .reference .bibliography role="doc-bibliography"}

Social and cognitive factors in making teacher-led classroom discourse relevant for second language development. `<cite>`{=html}Modern Language Journal`</cite>`{=html}, *95*, 1--25.]{#ch15.xhtml_ref15_79}
- [[Toth, P. D., & Davin, K. J.](#ch15.xhtml_R_ref15_80){.backlink aria-label=" This link returns to its first in-text citation. " role="doc-backlink"} (2016). The sociocognitive imperative of L2 pedagogy. `<cite>`{=html}Modern Language Journal`</cite>`{=html}, *100*, 148--168.]{#ch15.xhtml_ref15_80}
