[]{#pt5.xhtml}

::: {.section .part role="doc-part"}
# []{#pt5.xhtml_p225 .pagebreak aria-label=" page 225. " role="doc-pagebreak"}[Part V ]{.chnum .ordinal}[Mechanisms of Input Processing and the Acquisition of Phonology, Lexis, and Pragmatics[]{#pt5.xhtml_p226 .pagebreak aria-label=" page 226 is blank. " role="doc-pagebreak"}]{.chtitle} {#pt5.xhtml_h1}
:::

[]{#ch16.xhtml}

::: {}
# []{#ch16.xhtml_p227 .pagebreak aria-label=" page 227. " role="doc-pagebreak"}[16 ]{.chnum .ordinal}[Input Processing and the Acquisition of Phonology]{.chtitle} {#ch16.xhtml_h1}

Annie Tremblay

The University of Texas at El Paso

DOI: [10.4324/9781003041412-21](https://dx.doi.org/10.4324/9781003041412-21){aria-label="D.O.I. link to this document."}
:::

::: {.section .introduction role="doc-introduction"}
## Introduction and Definitions {#ch16.xhtml_sec16_1}

Input processing (IP) begins with extracting the relevant acoustic information from the speech signal, mapping it onto the corresponding *phonetic categories*[^26] (i.e., units that reflects listeners' perceptual grouping of acoustically different sounds that behave in a functionally identical way) (e.g., [McMurray & Jongman, 2011](#ch16.xhtml_ref16_73){#ch16.xhtml_R_ref16_73 .biblioref role="doc-biblioref"}; [Pisoni & Luce, 1987](#ch16.xhtml_ref16_79){#ch16.xhtml_R_ref16_79 .biblioref role="doc-biblioref"}),[^27] and activating the *lexical representations* (i.e., representations of words) that contain these phonetic categories.[^28] This task is seemingly effortless in the first (i.e., native) language (L1) but far more difficult in a second/additional language (L2), in part because L2 listeners' sound-mapping routines are optimized for the L1 (e.g., [Francis & Nusbaum, 2002](#ch16.xhtml_ref16_37){#ch16.xhtml_R_ref16_37 .biblioref role="doc-biblioref"}; [Ingvalson et al., 2012](#ch16.xhtml_ref16_48){#ch16.xhtml_R_ref16_48 .biblioref role="doc-biblioref"}; [Iverson et al., 2003](#ch16.xhtml_ref16_53){#ch16.xhtml_R_ref16_53 .biblioref role="doc-biblioref"}; [Kim et al., 2018](#ch16.xhtml_ref16_57){#ch16.xhtml_R_ref16_57 .biblioref role="doc-biblioref"}; [Schertz et al., 2015](#ch16.xhtml_ref16_80){#ch16.xhtml_R_ref16_80 .biblioref role="doc-biblioref"}). For spoken word recognition to be accurate and efficient in the L2 (*IP for language use*), non-native listeners must develop new L2 phonetic categories and/or restructure their existing L1 phonetic categories (*IP for perceptual learning*).[^29] Crucially, non-native listeners must process the input before they can develop these representations. How does this IP lead to perceptual learning?

This chapter provides an overview of past and current research on non-native listeners' perceptual learning of L2 sounds and its implications for IP, focusing on the mapping of acoustic information in the speech signal onto L2 phonetic categories by non-native listeners who have not yet attained an advanced level of proficiency in the L2 and thus whose IP can lead to perceptual learning. IP here is conceptualized in a narrow sense---*the mapping of acoustic information onto L2 phonetic categories*---because this is the task that non-native listeners are initially faced with when learning the sound system of a new language; assumptions about higher-level phonological processes can only be made after we can establish how L2 learners break down the speech signal into individual sounds.[^30] The research reviewed in this chapter does not take an IP perspective per se (in part due to the lack of research on L2 perceptual learning conducted from an IP perspective), but such a perspective is adopted when doing so is pertinent to highlight the type of IP that can lead to perceptual learning in non-native listeners. In [Barcroft (2015)](#ch16.xhtml_ref16_4){#ch16.xhtml_R_ref16_4 .biblioref role="doc-biblioref"}'s multi-level IP model (p. 16), this perceptual learning can be viewed as an essential component of *phon-IP*, the phonological IP level of the model. Hence, this chapter seeks to link research on L2 []{#ch16.xhtml_p228 .pagebreak aria-label=" page 228. " role="doc-pagebreak"}perceptual learning to this IP with the eventual goal of further developing phonological aspects of IP as a theory of L2 acquisition.
:::

::: {.section aria-labelledby="sec16_2"}
## Historical Perspectives {#ch16.xhtml_sec16_2}

Early speech perception research focused on how non-native listeners hear L2 phonetic categories in relation to L1 categories. This research demonstrated that when two non-native sounds are realized similarly to an existing L1 phonetic category, listeners perceive these two sounds as instances of the same L1 category and have difficulty learning them as distinct phonetic categories (e.g., [Goto, 1971](#ch16.xhtml_ref16_40){#ch16.xhtml_R_ref16_40 .biblioref role="doc-biblioref"}; [MacKain et al., 1981](#ch16.xhtml_ref16_67){#ch16.xhtml_R_ref16_67 .biblioref role="doc-biblioref"}; [Miyawaki et al., 1975](#ch16.xhtml_ref16_74){#ch16.xhtml_R_ref16_74 .biblioref role="doc-biblioref"}; [Strange & Dittman, 1984](#ch16.xhtml_ref16_86){#ch16.xhtml_R_ref16_86 .biblioref role="doc-biblioref"}; [Werker & Logan, 1985](#ch16.xhtml_ref16_98){#ch16.xhtml_R_ref16_98 .biblioref role="doc-biblioref"}). This phenomenon has been referred to by current L2 speech perception and learning theories as *perceptual assimilation* ([Best, 1995](#ch16.xhtml_ref16_8){#ch16.xhtml_R_ref16_8 .biblioref role="doc-biblioref"}; [Best & Tyler, 2007](#ch16.xhtml_ref16_9){#ch16.xhtml_R_ref16_9 .biblioref role="doc-biblioref"}), *perceptual magnet effect* ([Kuhl, 1991](#ch16.xhtml_ref16_59){#ch16.xhtml_R_ref16_59 .biblioref role="doc-biblioref"}; [Kuhl & Iverson, 1995](#ch16.xhtml_ref16_60){#ch16.xhtml_R_ref16_60 .biblioref role="doc-biblioref"}), or *equivalence classification* (Flege, [1987](#ch16.xhtml_ref16_29){#ch16.xhtml_R_ref16_29 .biblioref role="doc-biblioref"}, [1995](#ch16.xhtml_ref16_31){#ch16.xhtml_R_ref16_31 .biblioref role="doc-biblioref"}). The best studied sound pair that was found to cause such difficulties is English /l/-/ɹ/ for native Japanese listeners (e.g., [Flege et al., 1996](#ch16.xhtml_ref16_36){#ch16.xhtml_R_ref16_36 .biblioref role="doc-biblioref"}; [Gilette, 1980](#ch16.xhtml_ref16_39){#ch16.xhtml_R_ref16_39 .biblioref role="doc-biblioref"}; [Goto, 1971](#ch16.xhtml_ref16_40){.biblioref role="doc-biblioref"}; [MacKain et al., 1981](#ch16.xhtml_ref16_67){.biblioref role="doc-biblioref"}; [Mann, 1986](#ch16.xhtml_ref16_70){#ch16.xhtml_R_ref16_70 .biblioref role="doc-biblioref"}; [Miyawaki et al., 1975](#ch16.xhtml_ref16_74){.biblioref role="doc-biblioref"}; [Sheldon & Strange, 1982](#ch16.xhtml_ref16_81){#ch16.xhtml_R_ref16_81 .biblioref role="doc-biblioref"}; [Shimizu & Dantsuji, 1983](#ch16.xhtml_ref16_82){#ch16.xhtml_R_ref16_82 .biblioref role="doc-biblioref"}; [Strange & Dittman, 1984](#ch16.xhtml_ref16_86){.biblioref role="doc-biblioref"}; [Yamada, 1995](#ch16.xhtml_ref16_101){#ch16.xhtml_R_ref16_101 .biblioref role="doc-biblioref"}). English /l/ and/ɹ/ overlap in second-formant (F2) and third-formant (F3) frequencies with Japanese /ɾ/ ([Lotto et al., 2004](#ch16.xhtml_ref16_66){#ch16.xhtml_R_ref16_66 .biblioref role="doc-biblioref"}); as a result, Japanese listeners assimilate English /l/-/ɹ/ to Japanese /ɾ/, as evidenced by the use of /ɾ/ in Japanese loanwords containing English/l/-/ɹ/ ([Vance, 1987](#ch16.xhtml_ref16_90){#ch16.xhtml_R_ref16_90 .biblioref role="doc-biblioref"}).

Early studies revealed that although native Japanese speakers can learn to produce the /l/-/ɹ/ distinction with some articulatory training, they have difficulty hearing this contrast (e.g., [Goto, 1971](#ch16.xhtml_ref16_40){.biblioref role="doc-biblioref"}; [Sheldon & Strange, 1982](#ch16.xhtml_ref16_81){.biblioref role="doc-biblioref"}), especially in syllable onset ([C]{.underline}V, C[C]{.underline}V) positions (e.g., [Gilette, 1980](#ch16.xhtml_ref16_39){.biblioref role="doc-biblioref"}; [Sheldon & Strange, 1982](#ch16.xhtml_ref16_81){.biblioref role="doc-biblioref"}), with Japanese listeners showing a less categorical (i.e., more gradient) perception of the contrast compared to native English listeners and having difficulty distinguishing the two sounds (e.g., [MacKain et al., 1981](#ch16.xhtml_ref16_67){.biblioref role="doc-biblioref"}; [Miyawaki et al., 1975](#ch16.xhtml_ref16_74){.biblioref role="doc-biblioref"}; [Shimizu & Dantsuji, 1983](#ch16.xhtml_ref16_82){.biblioref role="doc-biblioref"}; [Strange & Dittman, 1984](#ch16.xhtml_ref16_86){.biblioref role="doc-biblioref"}). This perceptual difficulty, which is greater for inexperienced L2 learners than for experienced ones (e.g., [MacKain et al., 1981](#ch16.xhtml_ref16_67){.biblioref role="doc-biblioref"}; [Miyawaki et al., 1975](#ch16.xhtml_ref16_74){.biblioref role="doc-biblioref"}; [Shimizu & Dantsuji, 1983](#ch16.xhtml_ref16_82){.biblioref role="doc-biblioref"}), was proposed to stem from Japanese listeners' underreliance on F3 (uninformative for identifying the Japanese approximant /ɾ/) and overreliance on F2 as cues to the English /l/-/ɹ/ contrast (e.g., [MacKain et al., 1981](#ch16.xhtml_ref16_67){.biblioref role="doc-biblioref"}; [Miyawaki et al., 1975](#ch16.xhtml_ref16_74){.biblioref role="doc-biblioref"}), when F3 is the primary cue to the English /l/-/ɹ/ contrast and F2 only a secondary cue (e.g., [O'Connor et al., 1957](#ch16.xhtml_ref16_76){#ch16.xhtml_R_ref16_76 .biblioref role="doc-biblioref"}).

The perception of L2 sounds as L1 sounds has since been reported for many speech sounds in a variety of L1--L2 pairings and for L2 learners at different proficiencies (e.g., [Flege, 1993](#ch16.xhtml_ref16_30){#ch16.xhtml_R_ref16_30 .biblioref role="doc-biblioref"}; [Flege et al., 1997](#ch16.xhtml_ref16_34){#ch16.xhtml_R_ref16_34 .biblioref role="doc-biblioref"}; [Flege & MacKay, 2004](#ch16.xhtml_ref16_33){#ch16.xhtml_R_ref16_33 .biblioref role="doc-biblioref"}; [Jia et al., 2006](#ch16.xhtml_ref16_54){#ch16.xhtml_R_ref16_54 .biblioref role="doc-biblioref"}; [MacKay, Meader et al., 2001](#ch16.xhtml_ref16_69){#ch16.xhtml_R_ref16_69 .biblioref role="doc-biblioref"}; [So & Best, 2010](#ch16.xhtml_ref16_83){#ch16.xhtml_R_ref16_83 .biblioref role="doc-biblioref"}; [Strange et al., 1998](#ch16.xhtml_ref16_85){#ch16.xhtml_R_ref16_85 .biblioref role="doc-biblioref"}). Although L2 speech perception and/or learning theories differ in their explanation of these difficulties, they converge in attributing them to correspondences between L2 and L1 categories: L2 sounds that are realized similarly to L1 sounds from an acoustic and/or articulatory perspective are perceived as L1 sounds; as a result, these L2 sounds are less likely to be interpreted as intended in the input and their target-like perceptual learning is much more difficult (e.g., [Best, 1995](#ch16.xhtml_ref16_8){.biblioref role="doc-biblioref"}; [Best & Tyler, 2007](#ch16.xhtml_ref16_9){.biblioref role="doc-biblioref"}; Flege, [1987](#ch16.xhtml_ref16_29){.biblioref role="doc-biblioref"}, [1995](#ch16.xhtml_ref16_31){.biblioref role="doc-biblioref"}; see also [Kuhl & Iverson, 1995](#ch16.xhtml_ref16_60){.biblioref role="doc-biblioref"}). In other words, non-native listeners map the L2 sounds they hear onto L1 categories when processing the input, with this assimilation making it more difficult for them to learn new L2 perceptual categories.

Importantly, early research also examined whether the mapping between acoustic information and phonetic categories in the L2 could be enhanced by auditory training, leading to perceptual learning and thus more target-like L2 phonetic categories. In IP terms, this perceptual []{#ch16.xhtml_p229 .pagebreak aria-label=" page 229. " role="doc-pagebreak"}learning can be viewed as *intake*, with non-native listeners' creating new representations or restructuring existing ones following exposure to the input ([Barcroft, 2015](#ch16.xhtml_ref16_4){.biblioref role="doc-biblioref"}). In this kind of training, listeners hear minimal-pair (non) words that they have to discriminate (i.e., say whether they are the same or different) or identify (i.e., choose the word they heard from a set of options), and receive explicit feedback on the accuracy of their response. [Strange and Dittman (1984)](#ch16.xhtml_ref16_86){.biblioref role="doc-biblioref"} found that Japanese L2 learners of English can be trained to improve their discrimination and identification of *lock-rock* if the stimuli in the training phase are synthetic (i.e., computer-generated) and if the pre-test and post-test also use synthetic stimuli, with learning generalizing from *lock-rock* in the training to *lake-rake* in the testing. This learning, however, did not generalize to stimuli produced by human talkers.

In their seminal work, [Logan et al. (1991)](#ch16.xhtml_ref16_65){#ch16.xhtml_R_ref16_65 .biblioref role="doc-biblioref"} reported that the key to such generalization is training listeners with stimuli produced by multiple talkers, also referred to as *high variability phonetic training* (HVPT) ([Bradlow et al., 1999](#ch16.xhtml_ref16_11){#ch16.xhtml_R_ref16_11 .biblioref role="doc-biblioref"}; [Bradlow et al., 1997](#ch16.xhtml_ref16_12){#ch16.xhtml_R_ref16_12 .biblioref role="doc-biblioref"}; [Lively et al.,1993](#ch16.xhtml_ref16_63){#ch16.xhtml_R_ref16_63 .biblioref role="doc-biblioref"}; [Lively et al., 1994](#ch16.xhtml_ref16_64){#ch16.xhtml_R_ref16_64 .biblioref role="doc-biblioref"}; [Logan et al., 1991](#ch16.xhtml_ref16_65){.biblioref role="doc-biblioref"}; [Pisoni et al., 1994](#ch16.xhtml_ref16_78){#ch16.xhtml_R_ref16_78 .biblioref role="doc-biblioref"}). Such training makes generalization possible, because listeners learn to attend to the acoustic parameter(s) that is (are) relatively invariant for the perception of a given speech contrast. Speech sounds are conveyed by a large number of acoustic parameters and the absolute realization of these parameters varies from talker to talker, but the *relative weight* of a critical cue to a given contrast is similar among different talkers of a given language (leaving dialectal variation aside). To perceive a contrast, listeners must track the distribution of these cues in relation to the context in which they occur \[a process proposed to be underlain by statistical learning mechanisms (e.g., [Clayards et al., 2008](#ch16.xhtml_ref16_24){#ch16.xhtml_R_ref16_24 .biblioref role="doc-biblioref"}; [Idemaru & Holt, 2011](#ch16.xhtml_ref16_45){#ch16.xhtml_R_ref16_45 .biblioref role="doc-biblioref"})\], weight these acoustic parameters accordingly, and attend to those dimensions that signal the contrast.[^31] [Logan et al. (1991)](#ch16.xhtml_ref16_65){.biblioref role="doc-biblioref"} effectively demonstrated that HVPT can enhance Japanese listeners' perception of the English /l/-/ɹ/ contrast: Hearing multiple English talkers allowed Japanese listeners to give greater weight to F3 relative to other cues to the contrast (F2, duration, etc.), resulting in perceptual intake. HVPT has since been shown to improve L2 learners' discrimination and/or identification of a variety of sounds in a wide range of L1--L2 pairings and for L2 learners at different proficiencies (e.g., [Huensch & Tremblay, 2015](#ch16.xhtml_ref16_44){#ch16.xhtml_R_ref16_44 .biblioref role="doc-biblioref"}; [Iverson & Evans, 2009](#ch16.xhtml_ref16_51){#ch16.xhtml_R_ref16_51 .biblioref role="doc-biblioref"}; Y. [Wang et al., 1999](#ch16.xhtml_ref16_95){#ch16.xhtml_R_ref16_95 .biblioref role="doc-biblioref"}).

As such, HVPT is deemed one of the best methods to improve L2 learners' mapping of acoustic cues onto phonetic categories in the L2, resulting in more perceptual learning and thus greater intake than other methods (for a more detailed review, see [Barriuso & Hayes-Harb, 2018](#ch16.xhtml_ref16_6){#ch16.xhtml_R_ref16_6 .biblioref role="doc-biblioref"}; but for conflicting results, see [Wade et al., 2007](#ch16.xhtml_ref16_93){#ch16.xhtml_R_ref16_93 .biblioref role="doc-biblioref"} \[among others\]). From an IP perspective, this means that L2 learners should be exposed to acoustically variable input in order to attend to the relevant dimensions of the input and develop more robust L2 phonetic categories. Interestingly, IP research has shown that acoustically variable input also enhances L2 lexical learning ([Barcroft & Sommers, 2005](#ch16.xhtml_ref16_5){#ch16.xhtml_R_ref16_5 .biblioref role="doc-biblioref"}) and L2 syntactic learning ([Ito & Wong, 2022](#ch16.xhtml_ref16_50){#ch16.xhtml_R_ref16_50 .biblioref role="doc-biblioref"}). This benefit is attributed to the better distributed ([Barcroft & Sommers, 2005](#ch16.xhtml_ref16_5){.biblioref role="doc-biblioref"}) and more abstract ([Ito & Wong, 2022](#ch16.xhtml_ref16_50){.biblioref role="doc-biblioref"}) representation that results from hearing multiple exemplars of the same word or structure, in line with research findings on the development of L2 phonetic categories.
:::

::: {.section aria-labelledby="sec16_3"}
## Critical Issues and Topics {#ch16.xhtml_sec16_3}

One of the most important issues in research on the perceptual learning of L2 sounds is the influence of the *native language* (e.g., [Best, 1995](#ch16.xhtml_ref16_8){.biblioref role="doc-biblioref"}; [Best & Tyler, 2007](#ch16.xhtml_ref16_9){.biblioref role="doc-biblioref"}; Flege, [1987](#ch16.xhtml_ref16_29){.biblioref role="doc-biblioref"}, [1995](#ch16.xhtml_ref16_31){.biblioref role="doc-biblioref"}; [Kuhl, 1991](#ch16.xhtml_ref16_59){.biblioref role="doc-biblioref"}; [Kuhl & Iverson, 1995](#ch16.xhtml_ref16_60){.biblioref role="doc-biblioref"}). [Iverson et al. (2003)](#ch16.xhtml_ref16_53){.biblioref role="doc-biblioref"} provides a nice illustration of how L1 effects can manifest themselves. The authors investigated how native English listeners, Japanese L2 learners of English, and German L2 learners of English perceive the English /l/-/ɹ/ contrast, []{#ch16.xhtml_p230 .pagebreak aria-label=" page 230. " role="doc-pagebreak"}testing the hypothesis that early experience with the L1 interferes with the formation of higher-level linguistic representations in the L2. Like English, German has a lateral-rhotic contrast, but unlike English, its rhotic can be realized as an alveolar trill (/r/), a uvular trill (/ʀ/), or a uvular approximant (/ʁ/), among other variations (e.g., [Wiese, 2003](#ch16.xhtml_ref16_100){#ch16.xhtml_R_ref16_100 .biblioref role="doc-biblioref"}). Importantly, because F3 is an important cue for distinguishing rhotics from laterals across a variety of languages ([Lindau, 1985](#ch16.xhtml_ref16_62){#ch16.xhtml_R_ref16_62 .biblioref role="doc-biblioref"}), German L2 learners of English should be more sensitive to F3 compared to Japanese L2 learners of English, even if German does not have a retroflex rhotic (/ɹ/). The results of a discrimination task confirmed this prediction, with German listeners discriminating English /l/-/ɹ/ stimuli along the F3 dimension. Japanese listeners, by contrast, showed more sensitivity to the unreliable (secondary) F2 dimension and little sensitivity to F3. From these results, [Iverson et al. (2003)](#ch16.xhtml_ref16_53){.biblioref role="doc-biblioref"} concluded that perceptual processes altered by early exposure to the L1 can interfere with the formation of L2 phonetic categories (for similar findings with other language pairings, see Charles B. [Chang, 2018](#ch16.xhtml_ref16_19){#ch16.xhtml_R_ref16_19 .biblioref role="doc-biblioref"}; Charles B. [Chang & Kwon, 2020](#ch16.xhtml_ref16_20){#ch16.xhtml_R_ref16_20 .biblioref role="doc-biblioref"}; [Flege, 1993](#ch16.xhtml_ref16_30){.biblioref role="doc-biblioref"}; [Flege et al., 1997](#ch16.xhtml_ref16_34){.biblioref role="doc-biblioref"}; [Gilette, 1980](#ch16.xhtml_ref16_39){.biblioref role="doc-biblioref"}; [Iverson & Evans, 2009](#ch16.xhtml_ref16_51){.biblioref role="doc-biblioref"}; [So & Best, 2010](#ch16.xhtml_ref16_83){.biblioref role="doc-biblioref"}).

Perceptual interference from the L1 raises a few important questions for IP research in the L2: (i) How does the age of onset of exposure to the L2 modulate the degree of perceptual interference from the L1? (ii) How does experience with the L2 influence the degree of perceptual interference from the L1? And (iii) does the use of the L1 increase the degree of perceptual interference from the L1 in the L2?

With respect to (i), *age of acquisition*---typically operationalized as age of arrival in the country where the target language is spoken---has been shown to influence the degree of L1 perceptual interference in the L2: Listeners who are exposed to the L2 at an earlier age (i.e., *early* learners) tend to experience less perceptual interference from the L1 than those who are exposed to the L2 at a later age (i.e., *late* learners) (e.g., [Flege et al., 1999](#ch16.xhtml_ref16_35){#ch16.xhtml_R_ref16_35 .biblioref role="doc-biblioref"}; [MacKay, Flege et al., 2001](#ch16.xhtml_ref16_68){#ch16.xhtml_R_ref16_68 .biblioref role="doc-biblioref"}; [MacKay, Meador et al., 2001](#ch16.xhtml_ref16_69){.biblioref role="doc-biblioref"}; [Yamada, 1995](#ch16.xhtml_ref16_101){.biblioref role="doc-biblioref"}). However, in some studies, age of acquisition is inversely related to experience with the L2, operationalized as the amount of time that L2 learners have spent in the country where the target language is spoken (e.g., [Flege, 1993](#ch16.xhtml_ref16_30){.biblioref role="doc-biblioref"}; [Yamada, 1995](#ch16.xhtml_ref16_101){.biblioref role="doc-biblioref"}). As a result, it is sometimes unclear which of these two variables is the underlying predictor of L2 learners' perceptual performance. In studies that compare early and late L2 learners with at least 10 years of exposure to the L2, a common finding is that late L2 learners *can* show a comparable amount of perceptual interference from the L1 as early learners *if* they do not use their L1 on a regular basis (e.g., [MacKay, Flege et al., 2001](#ch16.xhtml_ref16_68){.biblioref role="doc-biblioref"}; [MacKay, Meador et al., 2001](#ch16.xhtml_ref16_69){.biblioref role="doc-biblioref"}). From an IP perspective, this means that late L2 learners can overcome age-of-acquisition effects if they do not regularly process input (or produce output) in the L1.

The degree of L1 perceptual interference that L2 learners experience has also been shown to decrease with increasing *experience* with the target language (ii): The more L2 learners have opportunities to process input (and produce output) in the L2, the weaker the perceptual interference from the L1 (e.g., [Flege, 1993](#ch16.xhtml_ref16_30){.biblioref role="doc-biblioref"}; [Flege et al., 1997](#ch16.xhtml_ref16_34){.biblioref role="doc-biblioref"}; [Flege & Liu, 2001](#ch16.xhtml_ref16_32){#ch16.xhtml_R_ref16_32 .biblioref role="doc-biblioref"}; [Flege et al., 1996](#ch16.xhtml_ref16_36){.biblioref role="doc-biblioref"}; [Ingvalson et al., 2011](#ch16.xhtml_ref16_49){#ch16.xhtml_R_ref16_49 .biblioref role="doc-biblioref"}; [Jia et al., 2006](#ch16.xhtml_ref16_54){.biblioref role="doc-biblioref"}; [Kim et al., 2018](#ch16.xhtml_ref16_57){.biblioref role="doc-biblioref"}). For instance, in their study on the perception of English /l/-/ɹ/, [Ingvalson et al. (2011)](#ch16.xhtml_ref16_49){.biblioref role="doc-biblioref"} reported that Japanese listeners who had resided in the United States for a longer period of time were better at perceiving the English /l/-/ɹ/ contrast in natural speech than those who had resided in the United States for a shorter amount of time, suggesting that perceptual learning takes place over time; however, L2 learners' ability to use F3 as the primary cue to this contrast did not change as a function of their experience with English. From an IP perspective, these results suggest that more intense exposure to phonetically variable input (via the HVPT) may be needed to further enhance L2 learners' performance.

The degree of L1 perceptual interference has additionally been found to be mitigated by how much adult L2 learners continue to *use* their L1 (iii): L2 learners who more often use their L1 []{#ch16.xhtml_p231 .pagebreak aria-label=" page 231. " role="doc-pagebreak"}have been reported to display a higher degree of L1 perceptual interference than L2 learners who less often use their L1, a finding that is true of both early and late L2 learners (e.g., [Flege & MacKay, 2004](#ch16.xhtml_ref16_33){.biblioref role="doc-biblioref"}; [MacKay, Flege et al., 2001](#ch16.xhtml_ref16_68){.biblioref role="doc-biblioref"}; [MacKay, Meador et al., 2001](#ch16.xhtml_ref16_69){.biblioref role="doc-biblioref"}). Again, from an IP perspective, this suggests that the regular processing of input (and production of output) in the L1 can interfere with L2 perceptual learning.

Notably, L1 effects on the perception of speech sounds in the L2 can also result in *advantageous* transfer: An increasingly growing body of research has shown that non-native listeners can, in some circumstances, show greater sensitivity to L2 sound contrasts than native listeners do (e.g., [Bohn & Best, 2012](#ch16.xhtml_ref16_10){#ch16.xhtml_R_ref16_10 .biblioref role="doc-biblioref"}; C. B. [Chang & Mishler, 2012](#ch16.xhtml_ref16_21){#ch16.xhtml_R_ref16_21 .biblioref role="doc-biblioref"}; [Choi, 2022](#ch16.xhtml_ref16_22){#ch16.xhtml_R_ref16_22 .biblioref role="doc-biblioref"}; [Choi et al., 2019](#ch16.xhtml_ref16_23){#ch16.xhtml_R_ref16_23 .biblioref role="doc-biblioref"}; [Wiener & Goss, 2019](#ch16.xhtml_ref16_99){#ch16.xhtml_R_ref16_99 .biblioref role="doc-biblioref"}). This can happen when a cue to an L2 contrast is more important in the L1 than in the L2. For example, [Bohn and Best (2012)](#ch16.xhtml_ref16_10){.biblioref role="doc-biblioref"} reported that Danish and German L2 learners of English showed better discrimination of the English /w/-/j/ contrast than native English listeners do despite not having /w/ in their L1. The authors attributed these findings to the importance of the acoustic correlates of lip rounding for distinguishing high front vowels in Danish and German but not in English, thus leading to greater sensitivity in the perception of a consonantal contrast that is distinguished in part by the same cue. From an IP perspective, this means that L2 perceptual learning may not be needed when cues that are useful for distinguishing sounds in the L2 are also used in the L1.
:::

::: {.section aria-labelledby="sec16_4"}
## Current Contributions and Research {#ch16.xhtml_sec16_4}

Recent research on L2 learners' perceptual learning has focused on how listeners weight acoustic cues to non-native sound contrasts (e.g., [Chandrasekaran et al., 2010](#ch16.xhtml_ref16_18){#ch16.xhtml_R_ref16_18 .biblioref role="doc-biblioref"}; Charles B. [Chang, 2018](#ch16.xhtml_ref16_19){.biblioref role="doc-biblioref"}; [Francis & Nusbaum, 2002](#ch16.xhtml_ref16_37){.biblioref role="doc-biblioref"}; [Ingvalson et al., 2011](#ch16.xhtml_ref16_49){.biblioref role="doc-biblioref"}; [Iverson et al., 2003](#ch16.xhtml_ref16_53){.biblioref role="doc-biblioref"}; [Kim et al., 2018](#ch16.xhtml_ref16_57){.biblioref role="doc-biblioref"}; [Zhang & Francis, 2010](#ch16.xhtml_ref16_102){#ch16.xhtml_R_ref16_102 .biblioref role="doc-biblioref"}), and whether exposing L2 learners to phonetically variable stimuli where these cues have been systematically manipulated can enhance their intake (e.g., [Hu et al., 2016](#ch16.xhtml_ref16_43){#ch16.xhtml_R_ref16_43 .biblioref role="doc-biblioref"}; [Ingvalson et al., 2012](#ch16.xhtml_ref16_48){.biblioref role="doc-biblioref"}; [Iverson et al., 2005](#ch16.xhtml_ref16_52){#ch16.xhtml_R_ref16_52 .biblioref role="doc-biblioref"}). Charles B. [Chang (2018)](#ch16.xhtml_ref16_19){.biblioref role="doc-biblioref"} proposed that L1 effects on L2 cue weightings come from the transfer of processing strategies that direct listeners' attention to cues that have a *high functional load* (i.e., cues that reliably signal multiple contrasts) in the L1 (see also [Francis & Nusbaum, 2002](#ch16.xhtml_ref16_37){.biblioref role="doc-biblioref"}). Perceptual learning from the input thus involves assigning a greater weight (and thus attending more) to cues that have a higher functional load in the L2 than in the L1.

As indicated earlier, the primary acoustic cue to English /l/-/ɹ/ is F3 (/ɹ/ has a lower F3 than /l/) (e.g., [O'Connor et al., 1957](#ch16.xhtml_ref16_76){.biblioref role="doc-biblioref"}). Importantly, this contrast is also (less reliably) signaled by F2 (lower in /ɹ/, higher in /l/), closure duration (shorter in /ɹ/, longer in /l/), and transition duration (longer in /ɹ/, shorter in /l/), with the two categories showing a great deal of overlap in the distribution of these secondary cues (e.g., [Iverson et al., 2005](#ch16.xhtml_ref16_52){.biblioref role="doc-biblioref"}; [Underbakke et al., 1988](#ch16.xhtml_ref16_89){#ch16.xhtml_R_ref16_89 .biblioref role="doc-biblioref"}). [Iverson et al. (2003)](#ch16.xhtml_ref16_53){.biblioref role="doc-biblioref"} found that Japanese listeners rely on F2 to discriminate English /l/-/ɹ/, and [Iverson et al. (2005)](#ch16.xhtml_ref16_52){.biblioref role="doc-biblioref"} report that, prior to perceptual training, Japanese listeners have a strong bias to label long closures and long transitions as English /ɹ/, suggesting that Japanese listeners assign a strong weight these three secondary cues. One question that arises from these findings is whether perceptual training in which acoustic cues are systematically manipulated to increase reliance on F3 and decrease reliance on F2, closure duration, and transition duration can enhance Japanese listeners' perception of English /l/-/ɹ/ above and beyond HVPT with nonmanipulated speech stimuli.

In [Iverson et al. (2005)](#ch16.xhtml_ref16_52){.biblioref role="doc-biblioref"}, Japanese L2 learners of English were exposed to stimuli produced by multiple talkers in one of five conditions: (i) non-manipulated cues; (ii) cues exaggerated to extreme bimodal values; (iii) cues whose strength was gradually reduced from (ii) to (i) over several training sessions; and (iv) random bimodal distribution of secondary cues (F2, closure []{#ch16.xhtml_p232 .pagebreak aria-label=" page 232. " role="doc-pagebreak"}duration, transition duration) such that they no longer signaled the contrast. The results showed that (i)--(iv) were equally successful in enhancing Japanese listeners' identification of English /l/ and /ɹ/, suggesting that the phonetic variability in nonmanipulated speech stimuli may be sufficient to trigger perceptual learning and intake. These results, however, contrast with those of [Hu et al. (2016)](#ch16.xhtml_ref16_43){.biblioref role="doc-biblioref"}, who found that equalizing the duration of English vowels (a secondary cue to English vowel contrasts) in perceptual training stimuli helped reduce Mandarin listeners' reliance on duration as a cue to vowel contrasts. Cue manipulation in synthetic stimuli, however, has not been found to lead to robust L2 perceptual learning (e.g., [Ingvalson et al., 2012](#ch16.xhtml_ref16_48){.biblioref role="doc-biblioref"}). More research is needed to investigate how systematic manipulations of cues in the input modulate L2 perceptual learning.

Current research on L2 learners' speech perception and processing has also sought to explain individual variability among L2 learners in their ability to perceive non-native sound contrasts. Non-native listeners have been found to show a great deal of variability in their weighting of acoustic cues to non-native sound contrasts (e.g., Charles B. [Chang & Kwon, 2020](#ch16.xhtml_ref16_20){.biblioref role="doc-biblioref"}; [Díaz et al., 2012](#ch16.xhtml_ref16_26){#ch16.xhtml_R_ref16_26 .biblioref role="doc-biblioref"}; [Hattori & Iverson, 2009](#ch16.xhtml_ref16_42){#ch16.xhtml_R_ref16_42 .biblioref role="doc-biblioref"}; [Idemaru et al., 2012](#ch16.xhtml_ref16_46){#ch16.xhtml_R_ref16_46 .biblioref role="doc-biblioref"}; [Schertz et al., 2015](#ch16.xhtml_ref16_80){.biblioref role="doc-biblioref"}), but it is unclear what cognitive factors relate to this individual variability (e.g., [Chandrasekaran et al., 2010](#ch16.xhtml_ref16_18){.biblioref role="doc-biblioref"}; [Ingvalson et al., 2013](#ch16.xhtml_ref16_47){#ch16.xhtml_R_ref16_47 .biblioref role="doc-biblioref"}; [Perrachione et al., 2011](#ch16.xhtml_ref16_77){#ch16.xhtml_R_ref16_77 .biblioref role="doc-biblioref"}; note, however, that all of these studies focused on the perception of lexical tones). [Perrachione et al. (2011)](#ch16.xhtml_ref16_77){.biblioref role="doc-biblioref"}, for example, report that English listeners who are more sensitive to pitch movement in a pitch contour perception test that uses non-speech stimuli benefit more (i.e., show greater intake) from HVPT targeting the learning of lexical tones than listeners who show less sensitivity. This suggests that the perception of pitch movement in a non-linguistic task can translate into the ability to extract and learn lexical-tone categories when listening to a variety of talkers. What is less clear from this research, however, is whether pitch contour perception in a non-linguistic task would also predict the learning of segmental (i.e., consonant, vowel) information, raising questions about the nature of the cognitive abilities that underlie L2 perceptual learning.
:::

::: {.section aria-labelledby="sec16_5"}
## Main Research Methods {#ch16.xhtml_sec16_5}

The most commonly used experiments in the aforementioned L2 perceptual learning research are forced-choice identification and discrimination tasks with naturally produced nonmanipulated or resynthesized[^32] speech stimuli, either as stand-alone tasks or as pre- and post-tests in perceptual training paradigms (for a detailed review, see [Beddor & Gottfried, 1995](#ch16.xhtml_ref16_7){#ch16.xhtml_R_ref16_7 .biblioref role="doc-biblioref"}).

In identification tasks, listeners hear an auditory word or nonword and identify it from a given set of options. Discrimination tasks can take a few different forms. In AX tasks, listeners hear Stimuli A and X and must decide whether they are the same or different; in ABX and AXB tasks, listeners hear three stimuli and must decide whether Stimulus X is the same as Stimulus A or Stimulus B; in oddball discrimination tasks, listeners hear three stimuli and must decide which stimulus is different from the other two. In discrimination tasks, the more phonetically variable the stimuli (e.g., recordings of two words produced by two talkers are more phonetically variable than recordings of two words produced by the same talker) and the longer the interstimulus interval, the more likely the researcher is to tap into listeners' phonological representation of the contrast, requiring listeners to abstract away from acoustic details and hold the different categories heard in short-term memory (e.g., [Werker & Logan, 1985](#ch16.xhtml_ref16_98){.biblioref role="doc-biblioref"}). The results of identification and discrimination tasks are more likely to converge if the latter taps into L2 learners' phonological representations.

Many other types of experiments have been used in research on L2 learners' use of sound information in IP (not discussed herein due to space limitations), including sequence recall []{#ch16.xhtml_p233 .pagebreak aria-label=" page 233. " role="doc-pagebreak"}tasks, lexical decision tasks with or without priming, and visual-world eye-tracking tasks (e.g., [Broersma, 2012](#ch16.xhtml_ref16_13){#ch16.xhtml_R_ref16_13 .biblioref role="doc-biblioref"}; Broersma & Cutler, [2008](#ch16.xhtml_ref16_14){#ch16.xhtml_R_ref16_14 .biblioref role="doc-biblioref"}, [2011](#ch16.xhtml_ref16_15){#ch16.xhtml_R_ref16_15 .biblioref role="doc-biblioref"}; [Connell et al., 2018](#ch16.xhtml_ref16_25){#ch16.xhtml_R_ref16_25 .biblioref role="doc-biblioref"}; [Dupoux et al., 2008](#ch16.xhtml_ref16_28){#ch16.xhtml_R_ref16_28 .biblioref role="doc-biblioref"}; [Tremblay et al., 2018](#ch16.xhtml_ref16_88){#ch16.xhtml_R_ref16_88 .biblioref role="doc-biblioref"}; [Weber & Cutler, 2004](#ch16.xhtml_ref16_96){#ch16.xhtml_R_ref16_96 .biblioref role="doc-biblioref"}). Details on these paradigms can also be found in [Grosjean and Frauenfelder (1996)](#ch16.xhtml_ref16_41){#ch16.xhtml_R_ref16_41 .biblioref role="doc-biblioref"}.
:::

::: {.section aria-labelledby="sec16_6"}
## Recommendations for Practice {#ch16.xhtml_sec16_6}

The previously discussed research highlights HVPT as a key method for enhancing L2 learners' mapping of acoustic information onto phonetic categories that can subsequently be used for processing input in the L2. HVPT uses forced-choice identification tasks and discrimination tasks with naturally produced nonmanipulated or resynthesized speech stimuli and provides listeners with explicit feedback on the accuracy of their responses. Listeners are typically trained to perceive a contrast with 1 or 2 talkers per training session over the course of 6--12 training sessions (depending on the sound contrast and L1--L2 pairing), with each session lasting approximately 30 minutes to avoid fatigue. Unfortunately, this type of training has not been used much in more applied classroom research settings ([Barriuso & Hayes-Harb, 2018](#ch16.xhtml_ref16_6){.biblioref role="doc-biblioref"}). A few notable exceptions are [Wang and Munro (2004)](#ch16.xhtml_ref16_94){#ch16.xhtml_R_ref16_94 .biblioref role="doc-biblioref"} and [Thomson (2011)](#ch16.xhtml_ref16_87){#ch16.xhtml_R_ref16_87 .biblioref role="doc-biblioref"}, who report beneficial effects of HVPT on the perceptual learning of English vowels in native speakers of Mandarin and Cantonese. They frame HVPT as computer-assisted training for use in the classroom, thereby bridging the gap between theoretical and applied research (see also [Ito & Wong, 2022](#ch16.xhtml_ref16_50){.biblioref role="doc-biblioref"}, who report benefits of acoustically variable input on L2 syntactic learning with classroom learners).

Other training paradigms that use phonetically variable stimuli but do not necessarily draw listeners' attention to the trained contrast have also been found to enhance L2 perceptual learning: A few studies have demonstrated that listeners can learn complex phonetic categories incidentally by playing a videogame where they implicitly learn to associate the phonetic categories with characters (e.g., [Lim & Holt, 2011](#ch16.xhtml_ref16_61){#ch16.xhtml_R_ref16_61 .biblioref role="doc-biblioref"}; [Wade & Holt, 2005](#ch16.xhtml_ref16_92){#ch16.xhtml_R_ref16_92 .biblioref role="doc-biblioref"}) or by using the phonetic categories to predict the location of an object on the screen (e.g., [Gabay et al., 2015](#ch16.xhtml_ref16_38){#ch16.xhtml_R_ref16_38 .biblioref role="doc-biblioref"}). In these paradigms, listeners do not receive explicit feedback on the associations they create; instead, feedback takes the form of reinforcements (e.g., point gains or losses) that participants receive when playing the game. Holt and colleagues have attributed the success of this paradigm to listeners binding the acoustically different forms of the same phonetic category via the temporal associations they create between the visual and motor dimensions. From an IP perspective, these findings suggest that it is not necessary for non-native listeners to attend explicitly to the form of the speech sounds they hear for perceptual learning from the input to take place; attending to meaning (here, the characters or the location of the objects in the games) is sufficient. These incidental learning paradigms are extremely promising for enhancing listeners' learning of L2 phonetic categories that map onto a single L1 category (see also [Chandrasekaran et al., 2014](#ch16.xhtml_ref16_17){#ch16.xhtml_R_ref16_17 .biblioref role="doc-biblioref"}), and they mesh well with the principles of IP ([VanPatten, 2002](#ch16.xhtml_ref16_91){#ch16.xhtml_R_ref16_91 .biblioref role="doc-biblioref"}), which emphasizes a focus on meaning. They could also be incorporated in the classroom as a form of computer-assisted training, complementing and enhancing the benefits of processing instruction (see Wong, this volume).
:::

::: {.section aria-labelledby="sec16_7"}
## Future Directions {#ch16.xhtml_sec16_7}

The present review points to several directions for future research on L2 learners perceptual learning from an IP perspective: (i) providing a refined assessment of how L2 learners' mapping of acoustic information onto L2 phonetic categories is influenced by their acoustic cue weightings and how these weightings change with increasing opportunities to process input in the L2, thus substantiating one of the most basic aspects of phonological intake in IP; (ii) investigating []{#ch16.xhtml_p234 .pagebreak aria-label=" page 234. " role="doc-pagebreak"}whether and how systematic manipulations of acoustic cues in perceptual training stimuli can modulate L2 learners' phonological intake; (iii) reaching a more precise understanding of what cognitive factors relate to individual variability in L2 perceptual learning; (iv) determining how attention to form and attention to meaning modulate L2 perceptual learning and IP, including whether there are trade-offs between the two; ultimately (v) developing a model of phonological input processing that would explain how the learning of phonetic categories unfolds over time and how this learning interacts with higher-level phonological processes in the L2 (not discussed here due to space limitations); and (vi) incorporating computer-assisted perceptual training modules that follow the tenets of HVPT in processing instruction.
:::

::: {.section .conclusion role="doc-conclusion"}
## Conclusion {#ch16.xhtml_sec16_8}

This chapter has provided an overview of past and current research on non-native listeners' perceptual learning of L2 sounds and its implications for IP, with the eventual goal of further developing phonological aspects of IP as a theory of L2 acquisition. Much of this research showed that non-native listeners transfer their weighting of acoustic cues to sound contrasts from the L1 to the L2, with age of acquisition and use of the L1 modulating these effects (the earlier and the least the L1 is used, the weaker the L1 transfer). This research also revealed that perceptual training with acoustically variable input (varying talkers, varying phonetic contexts) bolsters perceptual learning, suggesting that phonological intake from this input is much more likely in the presence of variability, enabling non-native listeners to develop robust L2 phonetic categories. Importantly, attention to meaning appears sufficient for L2 perceptual learning to take place, in line with the principles of IP. With this groundwork and with future research directions established, it should be possible to develop a more fully fledged theory of phonological input processing---possibly within the *phon-IP* level of [Barcroft (2015)](#ch16.xhtml_ref16_4){.biblioref role="doc-biblioref"}'s multi-level IP model (p. 16)---that explains how the learning of phonetic categories unfolds over time and how this learning interacts with higher-level phonological processes in the L2.
:::

::: {.endnotes role="doc-endnotes"}
## Notes {#ch16.xhtml_h2}
:::
