::: {}
# []{#ch17.xhtml_p240 .pagebreak aria-label=" page 240. " role="doc-pagebreak"}[17 ]{.chnum .ordinal}[Input Processing and Incidental Vocabulary Acquisition]{.chtitle} {#ch17.xhtml_h1}

Susanne Rott

University of Illinois Chicago

DOI: [10.4324/9781003041412-22](https://dx.doi.org/10.4324/9781003041412-22){aria-label="D.O.I. link to this document."}
:::

:::::: {.section .introduction role="doc-introduction"}
## Introduction and Definitions {#ch17.xhtml_sec17_1}

Second language (L2) learners need to build a large vocabulary to reach advanced levels of language proficiency. Most researchers agree that deliberate learning and incidental acquisition complement each other. The relative effectiveness of both approaches for lexical development depends on how words are processed in meaning-bearing input and which word aspects learners attend to when they encounter unfamiliar words (e.g., [Barcroft, 2015](#ch17.xhtml_ref17_9){#ch17.xhtml_R_ref17_9 .biblioref role="doc-biblioref"}). Because a large amount of incidental vocabulary acquisition happens during reading, viewing, and listening to texts, the processing and acquisition of unfamiliar words is intricately related to text comprehension, whether audio, video, or written. Research on lexical input processing (lex-IP) therefore seeks to determine how factors such as degree of attention to words in context leads to varying degrees of success in incidental word acquisition ([Barcroft, 2015](#ch17.xhtml_ref17_9){.biblioref role="doc-biblioref"}).

This chapter first defines some essential concepts, such as incidental acquisition, lex-IP, and what we mean by vocabulary. The critical issues section then further explores cognitive processes involved in incidental acquisition. In order for incidental acquisition to take place, learners need to be able to infer word meaning, establish initial form--meaning connections, and process words for long-term retention. This section also outlines how attention to different word aspects (such as form or meaning) affects the efficient learning of a novel word form or its meaning, as detailed in the type of processing -- resource allocation (TOPRA) model (e.g., [Barcroft, 2015](#ch17.xhtml_ref17_9){.biblioref role="doc-biblioref"}). In the subsequent sections on current research contributions and research methodology, the chapter describes the use of important classroom-based and lab-specific research tools, namely, think-aloud protocols and eye-tracking procedures. A section on future directions for research includes issues such as extending studies to the processing and acquisition of multi-word units, assignment of multiple word meanings to one word form, and the need to consider a variety of input modalities, such as listening and viewing. Finally, the chapter concludes with several suggestions for language instruction.

::: {.section aria-labelledby="sec17_1_1"}
### Vocabulary {#ch17.xhtml_sec17_1_1}

*Vocabulary* can be defined as individual words of any word class, such as *cat*, *run*, and *sweet*, and multiword phrases, such as *litter box* or *copy cat*. When language learners encounter novel []{#ch17.xhtml_p241 .pagebreak aria-label=" page 241. " role="doc-pagebreak"}word forms for the first time, they need to encode orthographic and phonological aspects and assign meaning to them. Importantly though, few word forms carry only one meaning or have an exact translation equivalent between their L1 and L2 versions. In fact, many words are polysemous and are part of conventionalized multi-word expressions. That is, the referential meaning of a word form depends on the (thematic) context. For example, while there is a one-to-one correspondence between the body part *head* and its German equivalent *Kopf*, in the context of a university a department *head* is translated with an entirely different word form in German, namely, *Leiter/Leiterin/Leiter\*innen*. In the context of newspapers, the English compound noun *headlines* has only a partial literal equivalent in German (*Schlagzeilen*). *Head* is instantiated with the meaning of "to hit an opponent" (*Schlag*) while *line* has a literal German equivalent (*Zeile*). For the English metaphorical expression *heading out* there is no metaphorical equivalent in German. Conversely, Germans use *head* in the metaphorical expression *etwas auf den Kopf stellen* (*stand something on its head*) to express that something is *turned upside down*. These examples demonstrate that the form--meaning mapping process is different for English speakers learning German than for German speakers learning English. L2 learners of English need to assign multiple meanings to the form *head* while learners of German need to learn different forms.
:::

::: {.section aria-labelledby="sec17_1_2"}
### Incidental Acquisition and Intentional Learning {#ch17.xhtml_sec17_1_2}

*Incidental vocabulary acquisition* refers to a learning context in which L2 learners pick up words as they engage in a meaning-focused activity that was not designed for the purpose of word learning (e.g., [Gass, 1999](#ch17.xhtml_ref17_19){#ch17.xhtml_R_ref17_19 .biblioref role="doc-biblioref"}; [Hulstijn, 2001](#ch17.xhtml_ref17_22){#ch17.xhtml_R_ref17_22 .biblioref role="doc-biblioref"}). Reading, of course, is a paradigmatic example of such an activity inasmuch as the reader's main objective is to comprehend the message of a text, not to learn vocabulary. Incidental acquisition is generally contrasted with *intentional vocabulary learning*, during which learners direct their attention to word form and meaning aspects with the goal of encoding, retaining, and retrieving the word at a later time. The intention to learn words corresponds to a variety of learning behaviors and the use of learning tasks. These behaviors and tasks vary, for example, in the amount of time spent memorizing the word, the frequency of retrieval, and the type of production task used, and ultimately lead to different learning outcomes.

The rationale that a large part of one's second language (L2) vocabulary is acquired incidentally has its logical appeal. Acknowledging that advanced L2 learners need thousands of words to participate in oral and written discourse, many researchers agree that relying on intentional learning with flashcards or dictionary look-ups seems a tedious and unrealistic strategy (e.g., [Krashen, 1989](#ch17.xhtml_ref17_25){#ch17.xhtml_R_ref17_25 .biblioref role="doc-biblioref"}). In addition, focused attentional learning of vocabulary in isolation does not do justice to the multifaceted nature of word knowledge needed for communicative language use (see [Nation's \[2001\]](#ch17.xhtml_ref17_29){#ch17.xhtml_R_ref17_29 .biblioref role="doc-biblioref"} outline of 18 word knowledge aspects regarding word form, meaning, and use). Naturally, aspects such as word associations (e.g., synonyms, antonyms, hyponyms), a word's grammatical aspects (inflectional or derivational forms) or its collocational uses (e.g., *run a marathon*, *run a meeting*) are acquired incrementally through multiple meaning-bearing contextual encounters after a word form has been mapped to a core meaning. Consequently, L2 research adopted from L1 acquisition studies the *incidental learning hypothesis*, which states that "incidental learning from context during free reading is the major mode of vocabulary acquisition during the school years" ([Nagy et al., 1985](#ch17.xhtml_ref17_30){#ch17.xhtml_R_ref17_30 .biblioref role="doc-biblioref"}, p. 234). Currently, there is a wealth of freely accessible audio-visual content, which begs that the incidental learning hypothesis also be extended from reading to viewing and listening.
:::

::: {.section aria-labelledby="sec17_1_3"}
### []{#ch17.xhtml_p242 .pagebreak aria-label=" page 242. " role="doc-pagebreak"}Lexical Input Processing {#ch17.xhtml_sec17_1_3}

Learning words during reading, viewing, or listening requires a set of sub-processes outlined in [Barcroft's (2015)](#ch17.xhtml_ref17_9){.biblioref role="doc-biblioref"} *lexical input processing* (*lex-IP*) model. When L2 learners encounter unfamiliar words in the input passage, they must "minimally (a) encode the form of the new word in memory, (b) activate an appropriate semantic representation for the word, and (c) map the form of the word onto the appropriate semantic representation of the word" (p. 3). An additional key process in an incidental learning context is the process of inferring the meaning of the new word form (e.g., [Ellis, 1994](#ch17.xhtml_ref17_17){#ch17.xhtml_R_ref17_17 .biblioref role="doc-biblioref"}). While inferring and (successfully) assigning a correct meaning to an unfamiliar L2 word form aids aural and written text comprehension, L2 readers and listeners might not allocate sufficient cognitive processing resources to precisely those sub-processes of word learning that result in an ability to retrieve the (complete) word form or access the meaning from long-term memory after several weeks. Rather, processing the new word form for the first time most likely results only in partial word form encoding, a weak connection with its meaning, and only a vague meaning assignment. Therefore, processing the new word form multiple times in a variety of input contexts is needed to fill in and strengthen the form--meaning connection ([VanPatten et al., 2004](#ch17.xhtml_ref17_41){#ch17.xhtml_R_ref17_41 .biblioref role="doc-biblioref"}).
:::
::::::

::: {.section aria-labelledby="sec17_2"}
## Historical Perspectives {#ch17.xhtml_sec17_2}

Lexical input processing (lex-IP) as a research area was formalized by [Barcroft (2015)](#ch17.xhtml_ref17_9){.biblioref role="doc-biblioref"} for incidental and intentional word learning. Lexical input processing "focuses on how learners allocate their limited cognitive processing resources" ([2015](#ch17.xhtml_ref17_9){.biblioref role="doc-biblioref"}, p. 43) as they attend to novel word forms or phrases in meaning-bearing input. In essence, *lex-IP* has initiated a research agenda seeking to identify cognitive word processing phenomena and their relationship to acquisition. The lex-IP model considers and integrates aspects of theories of human memory in psychology, such as levels of processing (LOP) ([Craik & Lockhart, 1972](#ch17.xhtml_ref17_14){#ch17.xhtml_R_ref17_14 .biblioref role="doc-biblioref"}) and transfer--appropriate processing (TAP) ([Morris et al., 1977](#ch17.xhtml_ref17_28){#ch17.xhtml_R_ref17_28 .biblioref role="doc-biblioref"}). Even though the large corpus of incidental word acquisition research from the 1980s and 1990s was not conducted using a lex-IP framework, findings can be interpreted from this research perspective. For example, several studies on lex-IP were conducted prior to Barcrofts' formal introduction of the lex-IP perspective. These studies (e.g., Rott, [2000](#ch17.xhtml_ref17_37){#ch17.xhtml_R_ref17_37 .biblioref role="doc-biblioref"}, [2005](#ch17.xhtml_ref17_38){#ch17.xhtml_R_ref17_38 .biblioref role="doc-biblioref"}) were inspired by [VanPatten's (1996)](#ch17.xhtml_ref17_40){#ch17.xhtml_R_ref17_40 .biblioref role="doc-biblioref"} theory of input processing (IP) and its aim to understand the process of establishing form--meaning connections.

In his groundbreaking study, [Barcroft (2002)](#ch17.xhtml_ref17_4){#ch17.xhtml_R_ref17_4 .biblioref role="doc-biblioref"} challenged Craik and Lockhart's proposal that "deeper" semantic processing should lead to better memory for novel words than "shallower" form-oriented phonological and orthographic verbal processing. Morris et al.'s transfer--appropriate processing theory proposed that memory performance for previously learned items (e.g., L1 words) is determined by the relationship between how information is initially encoded and how it is later retrieved. Consistent with this general notion, [Barcroft (2002)](#ch17.xhtml_ref17_4){.biblioref role="doc-biblioref"} established the type of processing -- resource allocation (TOPRA) model, which predicts that "when processing demands are sufficiently high, increased semantic processing can increase learning for the semantic properties of words while decreasing learning for their formal properties" ([Barcroft, 2015](#ch17.xhtml_ref17_9){.biblioref role="doc-biblioref"}, p. 64) and vice versa. A large number of intentional vocabulary learning studies have repeatedly confirmed the central premises of the TOPRA model. These studies have shown how tasks that direct L2 learners' processing resources to semantic word elaboration lead to decreases in processing form-oriented or structural word properties. As a result, semantic word elaboration tied to tasks such as pleasantness ratings or a sentence-writing task, decrease resources available for the encoding of a novel word from encountered in input during intentional L2 []{#ch17.xhtml_p243 .pagebreak aria-label=" page 243. " role="doc-pagebreak"}vocabulary learning (e.g., Barcroft, [2003](#ch17.xhtml_ref17_5){#ch17.xhtml_R_ref17_5 .biblioref role="doc-biblioref"}, [2006](#ch17.xhtml_ref17_6){#ch17.xhtml_R_ref17_6 .biblioref role="doc-biblioref"}; [Wong & Pyun, 2012](#ch17.xhtml_ref17_44){#ch17.xhtml_R_ref17_44 .biblioref role="doc-biblioref"}). Two more recent investigations on the TOPRA model have further confirmed similar patterns for incidental word acquisition through reading ([Barcroft, 2009](#ch17.xhtml_ref17_7){#ch17.xhtml_R_ref17_7 .biblioref role="doc-biblioref"}; [Kida, 2022](#ch17.xhtml_ref17_24){#ch17.xhtml_R_ref17_24 .biblioref role="doc-biblioref"}).

Another independent line of research that seeks to better understand the relationship between cognitive word processing and learning has been using eye-tracking technology (e.g., [Elgort, Brysbaert et al., 2018](#ch17.xhtml_ref17_15){#ch17.xhtml_R_ref17_15 .biblioref role="doc-biblioref"}; [Godfroid et al., 2018](#ch17.xhtml_ref17_20){#ch17.xhtml_R_ref17_20 .biblioref role="doc-biblioref"}; [Wang & Pellicer-Sánchez, 2022](#ch17.xhtml_ref17_42){#ch17.xhtml_R_ref17_42 .biblioref role="doc-biblioref"}). Their findings show that the various word-processing behaviors result in higher scores on word form or word meaning measures.
:::

::::::::: {.section aria-labelledby="sec17_3"}
## Critical Issues and Topics {#ch17.xhtml_sec17_3}

::: {.section aria-labelledby="sec17_3_1"}
### What Counts as Incidental Vocabulary Acquisition? {#ch17.xhtml_sec17_3_1}

In much of the research literature, incidental vocabulary acquisition has been described as a "by-product," a description that is misleading if taken to imply that no strategic or cognitive effort is involved in incidental acquisition. In fact, any kind of meaning-oriented task naturally requires the comprehension of words and, therefore, might direct the L2 learners' attention to the words they do not know in a text. That is, incidental vocabulary acquisition involves some form of noticing---attending to the unfamiliar word---and some degree of intentionality to infer the meaning of the unfamiliar word instead of ignoring it (e.g., [Godfroid et al., 2018](#ch17.xhtml_ref17_20){.biblioref role="doc-biblioref"}). In order to establish an incidental learning context, researchers need to ensure that participants are not aware that word learning is investigated or that a vocabulary knowledge test follows the reading comprehension task. If learners know that they will be tested on word knowledge after the meaning-focused task, then vocabulary learning is considered intentional ([Hulstijn, 2001](#ch17.xhtml_ref17_22){.biblioref role="doc-biblioref"}). Recognizing that there is oftentimes some level of intentionality involved in incidental word acquisition, [Barcroft (2015)](#ch17.xhtml_ref17_9){.biblioref role="doc-biblioref"} suggested that "incidentally-oriented" (p. 44) word acquisition describes the learning process more accurately than the term "incidental" alone.

[Barcroft's (2015)](#ch17.xhtml_ref17_9){.biblioref role="doc-biblioref"} clarification of the term incidental is particularly useful in the context of recent studies that have used semi-incidental research designs directing L2 readers' attention to unfamiliar words in the reading passage or video and listening clip. These studies address the seemingly unpredictable and low learning rate of incidental acquisition during free reading or viewing. Following Schmidt's *noticing hypothesis* ([2001](#ch17.xhtml_ref17_39){#ch17.xhtml_R_ref17_39 .biblioref role="doc-biblioref"}), researchers assume that "people learn about the things they attend to and do not learn much about the things they do not attend to" (p. 30). Such semi-incidental studies have used pre-modified input by increasing the formal salience of words by, for example, bolding them ([Choi, 2017](#ch17.xhtml_ref17_13){#ch17.xhtml_R_ref17_13 .biblioref role="doc-biblioref"}), or enhancing the semantic relevance of words by increasing the target word frequency and providing glosses in a text (e.g., [Hulstijn et al., 1996](#ch17.xhtml_ref17_23){#ch17.xhtml_R_ref17_23 .biblioref role="doc-biblioref"}). Likewise, multimodal input enhancements have yielded positive effects on incidental acquisition, for example, through imagery while watching a documentary (e.g., [Rodgers, 2018](#ch17.xhtml_ref17_35){#ch17.xhtml_R_ref17_35 .biblioref role="doc-biblioref"}), subtitling of video clips (e.g., Peters et al., 2016), and combining audio and visual text in a reading-while-listening condition (e.g., [Webb & Chang, 2015](#ch17.xhtml_ref17_43){#ch17.xhtml_R_ref17_43 .biblioref role="doc-biblioref"}). Since "attention is not a dichotomous entity" ([Gass, 1999](#ch17.xhtml_ref17_19){.biblioref role="doc-biblioref"}), input tasks need to be placed on a continuum from purely incidental (e.g., unenhanced reading or viewing) to purely intentional (e.g., studying with flashcards) ([Barcroft, 2015](#ch17.xhtml_ref17_9){.biblioref role="doc-biblioref"}). While this line of semi-incidental research has shown positive effects on word learning on post-tests, the majority of these investigations do not investigate or control how acquired words were processed in the input.
:::

::: {.section aria-labelledby="sec17_3_2"}
### []{#ch17.xhtml_p244 .pagebreak aria-label=" page 244. " role="doc-pagebreak"}How Do Learners Infer Word Meaning? {#ch17.xhtml_sec17_3_2}

The process of inferring the meaning of an unknown word from textual or aural input is the first step to the form--meaning mapping process. Case studies have shown that L2 learners use different reading strategies to compensate for their limited linguistic, cultural, content, and background knowledge when trying to comprehend L2 texts. Accordingly, L2 readers take varying approaches to dealing with unfamiliar words they encounter in texts (e.g., Perry, 1992). The ability to successfully infer the correct meaning depends on a multitude of L2 reader-based and input-based factors that vary in each instance. In a longitudinal reading-based study, Perry (1992), for example, discovered that participants noted words as unfamiliar only during the third encounter. Perry concluded that the contexts of the first two encounters with the word were sufficiently comprehensible without assigning the novel L2 word form an exact meaning. That is, in a natural reading situation multiple encounters with unfamiliar words might be needed to trigger attention to the word forms and initiate a search for an explicit meaning. Besides skipping to infer word meanings, L2 readers often fail to infer the correct meaning. [Bensoussan and Laufer (1984)](#ch17.xhtml_ref17_10){#ch17.xhtml_R_ref17_10 .biblioref role="doc-biblioref"} summarized numerous potential problems students encounter when trying to make sense of words from the surrounding context. Students' strategies range from wild incorrect guesses, to confusions about synophones and synographs (implication/application), mistranslations of morphological troublemakers (*outline / out of line*), use of false cognates, to the retrieval of a wrong choice of a polyseme. Moreover, [Liu and Nation (1985)](#ch17.xhtml_ref17_27){#ch17.xhtml_R_ref17_27 .biblioref role="doc-biblioref"} have pointed out that at least 95% of the words in a text must be familiar for inferencing to be successful. That is, prior vocabulary knowledge affects incidental word acquisition (e.g., [Pulido, 2003](#ch17.xhtml_ref17_33){#ch17.xhtml_R_ref17_33 .biblioref role="doc-biblioref"}).
:::

::: {.section aria-labelledby="sec17_3_3"}
### How Do Learners Make Initial Form--Meaning Connections? {#ch17.xhtml_sec17_3_3}

For adult language learners, acquiring a new word most likely means learning a new L2 word form for a concept with which they are familiar and which overlaps with the semantic properties in their L1. For example, the core meaning of the English word *to ride* and its German translation *reiten* overlap in the thematic context of *riding a horse* (*ein Pferd reiten*). Consequently, for English speakers learning German, the form--meaning mapping process requires the activation of the existing semantic representation and the encoding of the German word form *reiten*. Word form encoding includes the orthographical and phonological word aspects. However, as outlined above, the successful inferencing process that results in word acquisition involves the readers in searching for meaning clues in the text passage. Logically, readers probably pay little attention to the word form itself. In fact, L2 readers might not have sufficient processing resources left to attend to and encode the novel word form. Likewise, L2 listeners might not be able to isolate the phonological form of an unfamiliar word and spend cognitive processing resources on encoding the new form as they process the audio input for content. The findings from one study on incidental vocabulary acquisition from viewing a documentary alludes to this idea. In a semi-incidental word learning study, [Wang and Pellicer-Sánchez (2022)](#ch17.xhtml_ref17_42){.biblioref role="doc-biblioref"} provided learners with bilingual subtitles. They found that L2 viewers spent considerably more time with L1 subtitles than L2 captions because the goal of the task was to comprehend the documentary. As a result, learners directed less attention to the new L2 word forms.
:::

::: {.section aria-labelledby="sec17_3_4"}
### How Does Processing Textual and Aural Input Affect Lexical Processing? {#ch17.xhtml_sec17_3_4}

To date, research directed at or pertinent to lex-IP in incidental contexts has mostly been based on the processing of textual input. In order to emulate a natural reading situation, L2 readers read paragraph- or ideally book-length texts. Consequently, the task goal---namely, reading for []{#ch17.xhtml_p245 .pagebreak aria-label=" page 245. " role="doc-pagebreak"}meaning---engages learners in the sub-processes of text comprehension. These sub-processes include the rapid decoding of words, automatic accessing word meanings, application of background and topical knowledge, integration of clause-level meanings, and the recognition of discourse-level relationships ([Grabe, 2009](#ch17.xhtml_ref17_21){#ch17.xhtml_R_ref17_21 .biblioref role="doc-biblioref"}). Acknowledging that language learners typically watch more videos than they read books ([Peters & Webb, 2018](#ch17.xhtml_ref17_32){#ch17.xhtml_R_ref17_32 .biblioref role="doc-biblioref"}), the value of aural input sources, such as listening to songs ([Pavia et al., 2019](#ch17.xhtml_ref17_31){#ch17.xhtml_R_ref17_31 .biblioref role="doc-biblioref"}) and watching film clips or TV series (e.g., [Rodgers & Webb, 2020](#ch17.xhtml_ref17_36){#ch17.xhtml_R_ref17_36 .biblioref role="doc-biblioref"}), have been investigated more recently. Processing aural input poses additional challenges. Beyond the integration of aural-based and learner-based information, understanding the message of aural input requires the efficient segmentation of the speech stream in addition to the rapid retrieval and integration of words in a coherent mental representation of the content.
:::

::: {.section aria-labelledby="sec17_3_5"}
### What Are the Roles of Different Types of Processing? {#ch17.xhtml_sec17_3_5}

Based on the assumption that learners have a limited cognitive capacity to process information, [Barcroft (2015)](#ch17.xhtml_ref17_9){.biblioref role="doc-biblioref"} established the type of processing -- resource allocation (TOPRA) model. In presenting this model, Barcroft outlines how the processing of form, the processing of meaning, and the process of form--meaning mapping operate independently. If processing demands for one of the three processes increases, the availability of resources for the other two processes decreases. That is, if learners allocate their processing resources to semantic word aspects, then semantic learning increases, thereby, when overall processing demands are sufficiently high, reducing the availability of processing resources needed to encode and learn the new word form. For adult learners in particular, who are most likely familiar with the semantic concept of the new L2 word from their L1, semantic elaboration is commonly not needed to establish an initial form--meaning connection, at least not in the same way it is needed during L1 acquisition. Semantic processing is needed to refine L2 word knowledge, however. Besides collocational phrases, learners need to notice differences in grammatical word aspects between two languages. For example, unlike in English the German word form of *to complain about something* requires a reflexive pronoun as in ***sich** über etwas beschweren* (sich is the reflexive pronoun). In turn, directing attentional resources to the new L2 word form fosters the encoding of word form. The TOPRA model has been repeatedly confirmed in intentional word learning studies in which the requirement to engage in semantically oriented processing through a pleasantness rating task hindered the processing and learning of word forms (e.g., [Barcroft, 2002](#ch17.xhtml_ref17_4){.biblioref role="doc-biblioref"}). A recent study by [Kida (2022)](#ch17.xhtml_ref17_24){.biblioref role="doc-biblioref"} confirmed the TOPRA model for semi-incidental word learning during reading. Readers who were prompted to attend to phonological word aspects (form elaboration) performed better on a word form recall task as compared to readers who had engaged in rating the pleasantness of the word form (semantic elaboration). This line of investigation has important implications for the design of instructional materials. In fact, since the initial goal is to encode the form of a novel word, form-focused attention-drawing tasks will foster incidental acquisition.
:::

::: {.section aria-labelledby="sec17_3_6"}
### What Promotes Long-term Retention of Incidentally Learned Vocabulary? {#ch17.xhtml_sec17_3_6}

Addressing the low rate of long-term word retention from reading, [Hulstijn (2001)](#ch17.xhtml_ref17_22){.biblioref role="doc-biblioref"} suggested that not only the quantity but also the quality of word processing affects retention. Retention is enhanced by attending to "a word's morphological, orthographic, prosodic, semantic and pragmatic features and to intraword and interword relations" (p. 285). The notion that elaborative processing fosters word retention has been further advanced in the *involvement load hypothesis* (ILH; [Laufer & Hulstijn, 2001](#ch17.xhtml_ref17_26){#ch17.xhtml_R_ref17_26 .biblioref role="doc-biblioref"}). The ILH claims that "the more attention that is paid to the []{#ch17.xhtml_p246 .pagebreak aria-label=" page 246. " role="doc-pagebreak"}formal and semantic aspects of words and the richer the associations that are made with existing knowledge \[...\], the higher are the chances that the new information will be retained" (p. 1). Consequently, the ILH attempts to account for the quantity and quality of learners' attention to unfamiliar words and predicts the resulting input processing behaviors. These behaviors include one motivational and two cognitive (information processing) dimensions that affect word retention: namely, need, search, and evaluation. *Need* is the motivational factor relating to whether learners perceive the need to know a word's meaning to complete the task. *Search* is a cognitive dimension that is either present or absent. Search is the attempt by the learner to infer word meaning from the text or to consult with a resource (e.g., dictionary or teacher). Finally, the cognitive process of *evaluation* entails comparing an unfamiliar word to familiar words. In essence, the ILH suggests that the more time L2 learners spend with a new word and the more word aspects they attend to, the higher the involvement load and the chance to establish a robust form--meaning connection.

The results of studies that attempted to validate the effect of task-induced involvement have been mixed. Additionally, studies conducted under the framework of the ILH oftentimes have not controlled for (held constant) the time spent with each target word nor the specific type of processing (meaning or form) learners engage in during tasks with different involvement loads. In fact, research conducted on the TOPRA model has repeatedly confirmed that initial word learning requires the encoding of the new word form and that semantic processing tasks can interfere with this initial step of word learning in both intentional (e.g., [Barcroft 2003](#ch17.xhtml_ref17_5){.biblioref role="doc-biblioref"}) and incidentally oriented ([Barcroft, 2009](#ch17.xhtml_ref17_7){.biblioref role="doc-biblioref"}; [Kida, 2022](#ch17.xhtml_ref17_24){.biblioref role="doc-biblioref"}) contexts of learning. Naturally, if the new L2 word form is not at least partially encoded, then a link to a (basic) L1 meaning and further elaborative processing for long-term retention cannot be guaranteed. A reinterpretation of ILH tasks from a TOPRA perspective might prove useful for gaining further insights into long-term word retention. More recently, [Yanagisawa and Webb (2021)](#ch17.xhtml_ref17_45){#ch17.xhtml_R_ref17_45 .biblioref role="doc-biblioref"} have created a newer predictive model of the ILH based on a meta-analysis of existing investigations. Their model awaits confirmation through empirical investigations.
:::
:::::::::

:::::::::: {.section aria-labelledby="sec17_4"}
## Current Contributions and Research {#ch17.xhtml_sec17_4}

::: {.section aria-labelledby="sec17_4_1"}
### Input Modification to Influence Processing of Word Form and Meaning {#ch17.xhtml_sec17_4_1}

To date, only a few studies have investigated incidental vocabulary acquisition from a lex-IP perspective. Two of these studies have focused on predictions of the TOPRA model. In the first, [Barcroft (2009)](#ch17.xhtml_ref17_7){.biblioref role="doc-biblioref"} asked Spanish-speaking learners of English to read a 491 word-long passage containing 10 target words (TWs). To ensure that learners were able to link the new form to a meaning, the L1 translation was provided in parentheses next to the TW. In a classroom setting the students read texts either a) for meaning (incidental), b) for meaning and learning the TW (intentional), c) for meaning and generating a synonym for the TW (incidental + semantic), or d) for meaning, learning the translated words, and then generating a synonym (intentional + semantic). The findings confirmed the applicability of the TOPRA model to incidental and intentional word learning by demonstrating that when readers were asked to generate synonyms of the TWs, they gained significantly less word knowledge on the immediate L1-to-L2 and L2-to-L1 tests.

In a second study on TOPRA and incidental L2 vocabulary learning, [Kida (2022)](#ch17.xhtml_ref17_24){.biblioref role="doc-biblioref"} manipulated the frequency of the TW encounters (1 time vs. 3 times) as well as processing type. L2 readers were either prompted to engage in structural elaboration by guessing and writing down the pronunciation of the TW in a different script or in semantic elaboration by rating the []{#ch17.xhtml_p247 .pagebreak aria-label=" page 247. " role="doc-pagebreak"}pleasantness of the TW. Japanese learners of English read a 802 word-long text with 10 TWs in one of six conditions: semantic elaboration + 1 or 3 counters of the TW, structural elaboration + 1 or 3 counters of the TW, or no elaboration + 1 or 3 counters of the TW. The study did not confirm the applicability of the TOPRA model in that no significant negative effect for the semantic elaboration condition was observed. In fact, not every meaning-focused task might interfere with the learning of word form equally, and the learning context, incidental or intentional, might play an important role in the nature of the effects of any given semantically oriented task. And yet the study was in line with TOPRA predictions by revealing that when learners encountered the TWs 3 times or with a structural elaboration prompt, they were significantly better at recalling the TW forms.

Outside the framework of the TOPRA model, two complementary strands of research have developed over the years that have also sought to identify the cognitive processes involved in successful incidental word acquisition through reading and viewing. Early studies (Rott, [2000](#ch17.xhtml_ref17_37){.biblioref role="doc-biblioref"}, [2005](#ch17.xhtml_ref17_38){.biblioref role="doc-biblioref"}) used think-aloud protocols to identify word processing strategies, namely, those that lead to correct word meaning inferences, the incremental integration of word form and semantic knowledge across encounters, and subsequently to word knowledge retention. More recent studies (e.g., [Elgort, Brysbaert et al., 2018](#ch17.xhtml_ref17_15){.biblioref role="doc-biblioref"}; [Feng & Webb, 2020](#ch17.xhtml_ref17_18){#ch17.xhtml_R_ref17_18 .biblioref role="doc-biblioref"}; [Godfroid et al., 2018](#ch17.xhtml_ref17_20){.biblioref role="doc-biblioref"}; [Wang & Pellicer-Sánchez, 2022](#ch17.xhtml_ref17_42){.biblioref role="doc-biblioref"}) have used eye-tracking technology to determine (1) the effect of word repetition on the speed of word form processing and (2) the effect of time spent processing individual target words on incidental vocabulary acquisition.
:::

::: {.section aria-labelledby="sec17_4_2"}
### Input Modification to Foster Word Inferencing and Long-term Retention {#ch17.xhtml_sec17_4_2}

Two studies (Rott, [2000](#ch17.xhtml_ref17_37){.biblioref role="doc-biblioref"}, [2005](#ch17.xhtml_ref17_38){.biblioref role="doc-biblioref"}) used think-aloud procedures to identify intermediate readers' of German word processing strategies that led to word retention. Both studies were semi-incidental. In the first study ([Rott, 2000](#ch17.xhtml_ref17_37){.biblioref role="doc-biblioref"}), the TW occurred seven times in the passage and was part of a different compound noun in each instance. The eight learners' text and TW processing profiles revealed that noticing the TW and correctly assigning the meaning during the first encounter did not necessarily lead to word knowledge transfer in later encounters. While the majority of learners were able to produce an English translation of the TW immediately after reading the text passage, only one learner was able to recall the meaning (L2 to L1 translation task) two weeks later. That is, noticing the word without any structural elaboration led to a weak form--meaning connection and inability to recall the meaning two weeks later. The successful reader's processing profile confirmed predictions of the TOPRA model. This reader demonstrated elaborative word form processing by verbalizing the TW in each encounter repeatedly. Additionally, this participant gathered semantic information about the TW while reading the passage. While meaning assignments during the first three encounters were vague, this participant assigned the correct meaning during the fifth encounter and was able to retrieve it during the two subsequent encounters.

The second study assessed a similar semantic information gathering to compare the impact of word processing in two semi-incidental conditions with textual enhancements: multiple-choice (MCG) and single-translation (STG) glosses. In this study, the readers' attention was directed to the TW during the first encounter through either a MCG or STG to establish a form--meaning connection. Rott was interested in observing how learners would process the three additional TW encounters in the text and which processing behavior would strengthen the form--meaning connection measured four weeks later. The quantity of strategic interaction with the TW was determined by the number of processing strategies and type of structural and semantic resources used during each encounter. The learner profiles revealed that the type of []{#ch17.xhtml_p248 .pagebreak aria-label=" page 248. " role="doc-pagebreak"}gloss (multiple-choice or single-translation) induced different processing behavior during subsequent encounters with the TW. To be specific, multiple-choice glosses triggered more consistent word form processing and search to reassess the initial meaning assignment. In contrast, readers whose form--meaning mapping was enhanced with a single translation gloss either simply transferred the TW from the gloss or skipped the TW by not verbalizing it.
:::

::: {.section aria-labelledby="sec17_4_3"}
### Input Modification to Determine Length of Word Form Processing {#ch17.xhtml_sec17_4_3}

[Godfroid et al. (2018)](#ch17.xhtml_ref17_20){.biblioref role="doc-biblioref"} explored whether repeated word exposures and the amount of processing time contribute to incidental word acquisition independently. The researchers used eye-fixation measures to elicit how unfamiliar words are processed in text-length input. Eye movements showed that the acquisition of orthographic word form was aided by frequent word encounters. A sharp decrease in processing time within the first four encounters and a gradual decrease after eleven exposures indicated a partial and incremental growth towards fluent processing. Word form recognition reached about 82% for the most frequently occurring words (23, 18, and 12 instances). In turn, the ability to recognize and recall word meanings was not only enhanced by repeated exposures but also by the amount of time readers spent processing words (summed total reading time). Meaning recognition of the most frequently occurring words reached about 74% and meaning recall 47%. Less frequent words resulted in 30% of receptive word form and meaning knowledge gain, and 13% productive word meaning gain. The study did not assess long-term word knowledge retention.

Exploring aural IP, [Wang and Pellicer-Sánchez (2022)](#ch17.xhtml_ref17_42){.biblioref role="doc-biblioref"} conducted a semi-incidental study to determine the impact of processing bilingual and monolingual subtitles while watching a documentary. Not surprisingly, longer TW reading times predicted gains in word form recognition when learners were exposed to L2 captions and bilingual captions. Moreover, when learners were exposed to L2 captions, word processing time was only a predictor for meaning recognition, not for meaning recall. The limited word knowledge gain seems to indicate that learners spent the time trying to infer the meaning of words. Nevertheless, processing time was a predictor for meaning recall for the bilingual subtitle group. Since the TW meaning was provided through the L1 subtitle, these learners were able to spend processing resources on establishing a form--meaning connection that allowed meaning retrieval.

[Elgort, Candry et al. (2018)](#ch17.xhtml_ref17_16){#ch17.xhtml_R_ref17_16 .biblioref role="doc-biblioref"} expanded this line of research by explicitly investigating the development of word form and word meaning gain through textual IP. Moreover, this study explored L2 readers' ability to abstract word knowledge from the context of the input passage and to access the established from-meaning link in a novel context. Based on eye fixation, gaze, and regression-in measures, the researchers determined that the L2 learners developed orthographic familiarity within five to ten encounters. Yet, these L2 readers were less successful with inferring the correct word meanings. On the meaning generation posttest they were only able to produce the meaning of 34% of the TWs. Even though the go past time measure decreased over the 40 encounters, learners, when encountering the TWs in a new neutral context, were not able to transfer the faster processing ability they had gained reading the input text. Again, no long-term word knowledge retention was assessed.
:::

:::::: {.section aria-labelledby="sec17_4_4"}
### Synthesis and Analysis of Different Areas of Research {#ch17.xhtml_sec17_4_4}

Findings from the three strands of research outlined above complement each other. Overall, the studies confirm that incidental word acquisition while processing textual or aural input is a complex and cognitively demanding process.

::: {.section aria-labelledby="sec17_4_4_1"}
#### []{#ch17.xhtml_p249 .pagebreak aria-label=" page 249. " role="doc-pagebreak"}Attention to Unfamiliar Words {#ch17.xhtml_sec17_4_4_1}

Eye-movement data reveal that readers and viewers attend to unfamiliar words. In fact, longer reading times and regressions to TWs indicate attempts to assign meaning to the novel word forms. However, eye movements also suggest that L2 readers spend more time with new words in some contexts and not in others. That is, "not all exposures affect reading and learning of new vocabulary the same way" (p. 575, [Godfroid et al., 2018](#ch17.xhtml_ref17_20){.biblioref role="doc-biblioref"}). Qualitative think-aloud profiles further reveal that readers' attention to the TWs vary qualitatively and depend on both the comprehension of the local context and an awareness of the TW reoccurrence in the text. Word processing behavior appears to be learner specific and tied to overall reading strategies.
:::

::: {.section aria-labelledby="sec17_4_4_2"}
#### Word Form Acquisition {#ch17.xhtml_sec17_4_4_2}

The eye-movement studies show that fluent orthographic word processing is acquired independently from form--meaning mapping. The studies report that after a steep initial decrease from the first through fourth exposure to a new word, reading times plateau and then decrease steadily after 11 exposures. In the context of viewing the availability of L2 captions significantly benefits the acquisition of word form as compared to viewing with L1 subtitles or no subtitles. That is, hearing new word forms only in the input is not sufficient to encode the orthographic word form. Moreover, eye-tracking records suggest that the availability of the L1 subtitle competes with the attending to the word form presented in the L2 caption and leaves few attentional resources to encode the new word form. Semantic elaboration studies confirm that the processing of word meaning indeed reduces processing resources for word form learning.
:::

::: {.section aria-labelledby="sec17_4_4_3"}
#### Form--Meaning Mapping {#ch17.xhtml_sec17_4_4_3}

Eye-movement studies have further confirmed that incidental word acquisition is a slow and incremental form--meaning mapping process. Generally, studies have found that L2 readers' attention to the L2 word form predicted the recognition and recall of word meaning in an offline posttest. Think-aloud studies further corroborate that a higher total reading time can be interpreted as elaborative processing leading to a robust form--meaning connection. An elaborate search for meaning coupled with a subsequent meaning confirmation process establishes robust from-meaning connections lasting at least four weeks. Similarly, eye fixation data explain that when learners have L1 subtitles and L2 captions available, they spend more time reading those L1 subtitles needed to assign a meaning to the TW form. Since learners do not have to search for meaning their form--meaning link is less complex. In turn, when viewers have only L2 captions available they spend their eye fixation time on inferring meaning. This search process leads to more elaborative processing and subsequently in establishing a more complex form--meaning connection. Yet, robustness should be assessed with L2 learners' ability to transfer fluent word retrieval to new and semantically neutral sentences. Qualitative learner profiles suggest that form--meaning assignments might be tentative and successful retrieval dependent on the word's context. Even the same text might present readers with seemingly neutral contexts. As a consequence, the lack of retrieval of word meaning when processing the novel word form might interfere with the strengthening of the form--meaning connection.
:::
::::::
::::::::::

::: {.section aria-labelledby="sec17_5"}
## Main Research Methods {#ch17.xhtml_sec17_5}

The two lex-IP studies that investigated the applicability of TOPRA to incidental acquisition during reading ([Barcroft, 2009](#ch17.xhtml_ref17_7){.biblioref role="doc-biblioref"}; [Kida, 2022](#ch17.xhtml_ref17_24){.biblioref role="doc-biblioref"}) were conducted in classrooms and used modified []{#ch17.xhtml_p250 .pagebreak aria-label=" page 250. " role="doc-pagebreak"}instructional materials. Embedded pretests used in studies of this nature, such as in the 2009 study, can help to confirm that novel words of interest are truly novel. The elaboration tasks, namely synonym generation and pleasantness ratings for semantic elaboration and phonological recording for structural elaboration, were clearly specified. Consequently, other variables such as time learners spent with the words were controlled. By providing the L1 translation for the target words, the researchers were able to isolate the processing of structural and semantic word aspects.

Think-aloud protocols (i.e., learners' verbalization of their thoughts as they complete a learning tasks) as used in Rott's studies (2002; [2005](#ch17.xhtml_ref17_38){.biblioref role="doc-biblioref"}) do not provide direct access to cognitive mechanisms, but this type of concurrent data offers indirect insight into participants' mental activities and their attention to processing word form and word meaning (see Marijuan, this volume). Finally, eye-tracking provides a non-invasive way to study L2 readers' processing of unfamiliar words and their attempts to derive meaning as they read (e.g., [Elgort, Brysbaert et al., 2018](#ch17.xhtml_ref17_15){.biblioref role="doc-biblioref"}; [Godfroid et al., 2018](#ch17.xhtml_ref17_20){.biblioref role="doc-biblioref"}; [Wang & Pellicer-Sánchez, 2022](#ch17.xhtml_ref17_42){.biblioref role="doc-biblioref"}) (see Jegerski, this volume, for more details on eye-tracking as a psycholinguistic research tool).
:::

::: {.section aria-labelledby="sec17_6"}
## Recommendations for Practice {#ch17.xhtml_sec17_6}

Most vocabulary researchers agree that both incidental and intentional word learning need to be seen as complementary learning practices in order to reach advanced language levels. [Barcroft (2012)](#ch17.xhtml_ref17_8){#ch17.xhtml_R_ref17_8 .biblioref role="doc-biblioref"} outlined how a complementary approach to intentional and incidental learning could be implemented in an instructional learning setting using input-based incremental vocabulary instruction (IBI), an approach that details how to integrate multiple sources of input and careful treatment of tasks and content in a curriculum. He also provides examples of detailed lesson plans.

While it is essential that instructors implement an effective vocabulary program that integrates intentional and incidental word learning, most incidental acquisition probably happens when learners become more autonomous and work outside the classroom with a variety of input sources. Given that the studies outlined above demonstrate that multiple encounters with a novel word are essential for the development of fluent orthographic processing and opportunities to infer and confirm word meaning, narrow reading of a specific topic provides opportunities for incidental acquisition ([Chang, 2019](#ch17.xhtml_ref17_12){#ch17.xhtml_R_ref17_12 .biblioref role="doc-biblioref"}). Since language learners seem to prefer audio-visual input texts over written texts ([Peters & Webb, 2018](#ch17.xhtml_ref17_32){.biblioref role="doc-biblioref"}) the motivational factors outweigh potential challenges when processing texts aurally. Providing learners with video sources in the target culture that allow turning on captioning for videos ([Wang & Pellicer-Sánchez, 2022](#ch17.xhtml_ref17_42){.biblioref role="doc-biblioref"}) or songs ([Pavia et al., 2019](#ch17.xhtml_ref17_31){.biblioref role="doc-biblioref"}) will foster initial form--meaning mappings. The motivational potential for learners to become autonomous viewers and listeners might result in similar word learning gains, as predicted in the incidental learning hypothesis ([Nagy et al., 1985](#ch17.xhtml_ref17_30){.biblioref role="doc-biblioref"}) for L1 vocabulary acquisition.
:::

::: {.section aria-labelledby="sec17_7"}
## Future Directions {#ch17.xhtml_sec17_7}

While the incidental learning hypothesis ([Nagy et al., 1985](#ch17.xhtml_ref17_30){.biblioref role="doc-biblioref"}) has spurred many L2 investigations, research on lex-IP for incidental acquisition is still in its infancy. There are three major areas that should be addressed in future investigations when looking at reading, viewing, and listening as sources of input for incidental word acquisition.

1.  *Acquisition measures*: The goal of lexical acquisition is to build a large and complex lexicon. Therefore, acquisition processes need to be linked to long-term word retention. In particular, if research seeks to establish which eye fixations or elaborative processing behaviors are most efficient to build a large lexicon, delayed posttests are crucial. Moreover, additional word []{#ch17.xhtml_p251 .pagebreak aria-label=" page 251. " role="doc-pagebreak"}knowledge measures are needed to provide a more fine-grained understanding of the incremental word acquisition process during input processing. Especially for semi-incidental acquisition studies, which direct learners' attention to specific word aspects, an understanding of how learners allocate their processing resources is important. While productive and receptive levels of form and meaning knowledge should be considered core measures, the development of grammatical and semantic aspects, as well as collocational use, are trajectories that are vitally important to understand. Such findings will help confirm or adjust the TOPRA model ([Barcroft, 2015](#ch17.xhtml_ref17_9){.biblioref role="doc-biblioref"}) regarding its degree of applicability to incidental acquisition.
2.  *Depth of word knowledge*: Current research has focused mainly on the learning of novel word forms. Yet encountering words in their natural aural or textual context provides a wealth of information fostering the refinement of word knowledge. For example, [Bogaards (2001)](#ch17.xhtml_ref17_11){#ch17.xhtml_R_ref17_11 .biblioref role="doc-biblioref"} found in an explicit learning context that the assignment of a new meaning to an already familiar form is easier to learn than learning a novel word form. Does this finding hold in an incidental acquisition context where learners are exposed to text-length input? Another interesting research avenue to pursue would be the possibility of incidental acquisition of collocations during input processing. Of particular interest is the possible acquisition of collocations in morphosyntactially more complex languages other than English. For example, in some languages, unlike English, multiple word components of collocations can appear in different parts of the sentence. The expression *to brush one's teeth* is used in a consecutive string in English (*I **brush my teeth** every morning after breakfast*). L2 learners of German might not necessarily process and store the words as a collocation (or do so as readily) if they encounter them in different parts of the sentence, as in *Ich **putze mir** jeden Morgen nach dem Frühstück **die Zähne*** (literal translation: I brush myself every morning after breakfast the teeth).
3.  *The relationship between input comprehension and incidental acquisition*: To date, most studies assessing lexical processing in text-length input sources report that study participants answered comprehension questions. Yet, these studies do not report the relationship between text comprehension, processing input, and incidental acquisition. Naturally, it seems logical that better text comprehension is related to incidental acquisition ([Pulido, 2004](#ch17.xhtml_ref17_34){#ch17.xhtml_R_ref17_34 .biblioref role="doc-biblioref"}), and semi-incidental studies have shown that lexical input enhancements affect the overall reading process. For example, [Rott (2005)](#ch17.xhtml_ref17_38){.biblioref role="doc-biblioref"} demonstrated that the use of multiple-choice glosses caused L2 readers to focus on the comprehension of details throughout the text. Likewise, [Choi (2017)](#ch17.xhtml_ref17_13){.biblioref role="doc-biblioref"} found that bolding collocations in a text passage directed readers' attentional resources to the comprehension of ideas containing the bolded collocations. As a result, readers paid less attention to ideas the were not bolded.
:::

::: {.section .reference aria-labelledby="r17_1"}
