[]{#ch18.xhtml}

::: {}
# []{#ch18.xhtml_p254 .pagebreak aria-label=" page 254. " role="doc-pagebreak"}[18 ]{.chnum .ordinal}[Input Processing and Intentional Vocabulary Acquisition]{.chtitle} {#ch18.xhtml_h1}

Akifumi Yanagisawa

University of Tsukuba

DOI: [10.4324/9781003041412-23](https://dx.doi.org/10.4324/9781003041412-23){aria-label="D.O.I. link to this document."}
:::

::: {.section .introduction role="doc-introduction"}
## Introduction and Definitions {#ch18.xhtml_sec18_1}

Vocabulary learning may fall into two categories: incidental and intentional. *Incidental vocabulary learning* occurs where vocabulary is learned as a byproduct of other activities such as reading, listening, writing, and speaking (see [Webb, 2020](#ch18.xhtml_ref18_56){#ch18.xhtml_R_ref18_56 .biblioref role="doc-biblioref"} for a review). In research contexts, incidental learning tends to be operationalized as learners not being aware that they will be tested on vocabulary knowledge after learning tasks ([Hulstijn, 2001](#ch18.xhtml_ref18_21){#ch18.xhtml_R_ref18_21 .biblioref role="doc-biblioref"}). In intentional vocabulary learning, learners engage in tasks where they deliberately attempt to learn new words (e.g., flashcards or word lists) and are aware that they will be tested on vocabulary knowledge after the tasks. Research on lexical input processing (lex-IP) examines how cognitive factors such as degree of attention to different aspects of words (e.g., form, meaning) may lead to varying degrees of word learning ([Barcroft, 2015](#ch18.xhtml_ref18_11){#ch18.xhtml_R_ref18_11 .biblioref role="doc-biblioref"}). This chapter focuses on lex-IP research in intentional vocabulary learning, complementing another chapter in this volume that focuses on lex-IP in incidental vocabulary learning (see Rott, this volume).

This chapter begins by looking at how lex-IP has been defined and investigated in research. After discussing two theories from memory research in cognitive psychology (i.e., transfer appropriate processing and level of processing), we turn our attention to two related frameworks that have been applied to intentional vocabulary learning, *type of processing -- resource allocation* (TOPRA) model and *technique feature analysis* (TFA), and related research findings. We review research on input processing (IP) in intentional vocabulary and offer pedagogical implications. The chapter concludes with suggestions for future research.

*Input* is defined as "samples of a language to which a learner is exposed" ([Barcroft, 2015](#ch18.xhtml_ref18_11){.biblioref role="doc-biblioref"}, p. 1). Research on IP has focused primarily on processing at the sentence/morphosyntactic level. In the context of vocabulary learning, input refers to various aspects of lexical information, such as individual words, word parts, multiword units, units of meaning, and mappings between lexical forms and their meanings ([Barcroft, 2015](#ch18.xhtml_ref18_11){.biblioref role="doc-biblioref"}). IP is primarily concerned with how learners attend to and process input to make form--meaning connections as they attempt to comprehend input (e.g., [VanPatten 1996](#ch18.xhtml_ref18_50){#ch18.xhtml_R_ref18_50 .biblioref role="doc-biblioref"}). The part of input that one processes and is ultimately made available to the developing language system is called *intake* ([VanPatten, 1996](#ch18.xhtml_ref18_50){.biblioref role="doc-biblioref"}, p. 7). It is widely accepted that processing language input sufficiently and successfully leads to language development (e.g., [Barcroft, 2015](#ch18.xhtml_ref18_11){.biblioref role="doc-biblioref"}; [Krashen, 1982](#ch18.xhtml_ref18_25){#ch18.xhtml_R_ref18_25 .biblioref role="doc-biblioref"}; [VanPatten, 1996](#ch18.xhtml_ref18_50){.biblioref role="doc-biblioref"}).

[]{#ch18.xhtml_p255 .pagebreak aria-label=" page 255. " role="doc-pagebreak"}With respect to vocabulary learning, [Barcroft (2015)](#ch18.xhtml_ref18_11){.biblioref role="doc-biblioref"} defines *lex-IP* as "the manner in which learners process words and lexical phrases as input, or samples of the target language presented in communicative contexts" (p. 14) and "the processes by which learners allocate processing resources when they are exposed to a new word or lexical phrase as input" (p. 2). Lex-IP can be affected by the manner in which vocabulary appears in the input (input-based effects), the type of tasks in which learners engage when exposed to vocabulary in the input (task-based effects), or both. The types of tasks in which learners engage can vary with regard to the type and amount of elaboration they entail. In the context of *intentional* vocabulary learning, elaborate processing may refer to processing a word with a specific intention; for example, repeating a word or phrase to oneself ([Ellis & Beaton, 1993](#ch18.xhtml_ref18_18){#ch18.xhtml_R_ref18_18 .biblioref role="doc-biblioref"}; [Wong & Barcroft, 2020](#ch18.xhtml_ref18_60){#ch18.xhtml_R_ref18_60 .biblioref role="doc-biblioref"}) or writing down a word following a teacher's instruction to do so ([Barcroft, 2006](#ch18.xhtml_ref18_7){#ch18.xhtml_R_ref18_7 .biblioref role="doc-biblioref"}; [Webb & Piasecki, 2018](#ch18.xhtml_ref18_58){#ch18.xhtml_R_ref18_58 .biblioref role="doc-biblioref"}).

Elaboration during IP tends to be perceived positively as a factor that leads to more effective learning of certain aspects of vocabulary. Hulstijn argued, for example, that "the more a learner pays attention to a word's morphonological, orthographic, prosodic, semantic and pragmatic features and to intraword and interword relations, the more likely is it that the new lexical information will be retained" ([Hulstijn, 2001](#ch18.xhtml_ref18_21){.biblioref role="doc-biblioref"}, p. 285; see also [Laufer & Hulstijn, 2001](#ch18.xhtml_ref18_27){#ch18.xhtml_R_ref18_27 .biblioref role="doc-biblioref"}). However, many studies on lex-IP have demonstrated that certain types of elaborative processing, such as writing down L2 target words or thinking of synonyms for the words' meanings, can hinder word form learning compared to simply trying to remember word forms and mapping them to their meanings (e.g., [Barcroft, 2006](#ch18.xhtml_ref18_7){.biblioref role="doc-biblioref"}).
:::

::: {.section aria-labelledby="sec18_2"}
## Historical Perspectives {#ch18.xhtml_sec18_2}

Whereas IP had focused primarily on sentence-level processing and syntactic acquisition, other research over the past 25 years has focused on IP with regard to lexical units such as words and phrases (see [Barcroft, 2015](#ch18.xhtml_ref18_11){.biblioref role="doc-biblioref"}). Barcroft's (2000) seminal study on lex-IP, which examined the effects of increased semantic processing by writing target words in original sentences while intentionally learning unknown L2 words, was motivated by the level of processing framework (LOP; [Craik & Lockhart, 1972](#ch18.xhtml_ref18_16){#ch18.xhtml_R_ref18_16 .biblioref role="doc-biblioref"}), which claims positive effects for semantic processing with regard to memory for items in general. It was initially hypothesized that a semantically oriented condition (e.g., sentence writing) would outperform a control condition in which participants were simply told to study the words. However, Barcroft's (2000) results showed a negative effect for increased semantic processing on word form learning, the control condition without sentence writing led to higher scores on a cued recall test (produce target word forms based on picture cues). Consistent findings have also been reported in L1 research indicating non-facilitative effects of other semantically oriented strategies (e.g., create a mental image related to the meaning of a word) on intentional vocabulary learning (e.g., [Pressley et al., 1982](#ch18.xhtml_ref18_38){#ch18.xhtml_R_ref18_38 .biblioref role="doc-biblioref"}). Based on these findings, Barcroft focused increasingly on the role of limited cognitive resources for processing different types of lexical information in learning vocabulary, which led him to propose the type of processing -- resource allocation (TOPRA) model. The basic idea of the TOPRA model is that because one's overall cognitive resources for IP are limited, increased processing for one subcomponent of vocabulary learning (i.e., form, meaning, or mapping) can decrease resources available to process other components, potentially hindering specific aspects of vocabulary learning, such as word form learning.

The TOPRA model motivated many subsequent studies examining specific aspects of lex-IP by testing different predictions of the TOPRA model with various tasks, such as asking questions about word meaning ([Barcroft, 2003](#ch18.xhtml_ref18_5){#ch18.xhtml_R_ref18_5 .biblioref role="doc-biblioref"}), sentence writing ([Barcroft, 2004](#ch18.xhtml_ref18_6){#ch18.xhtml_R_ref18_6 .biblioref role="doc-biblioref"}; []{#ch18.xhtml_p256 .pagebreak aria-label=" page 256. " role="doc-pagebreak"}[Wong & Pyun, 2012](#ch18.xhtml_ref18_61){#ch18.xhtml_R_ref18_61 .biblioref role="doc-biblioref"}), synonym generation during reading ([Barcroft, 2009](#ch18.xhtml_ref18_9){#ch18.xhtml_R_ref18_9 .biblioref role="doc-biblioref"}), word learning with sentences in which the amount of semantic information is increased ([Barcroft, 2019](#ch18.xhtml_ref18_12){#ch18.xhtml_R_ref18_12 .biblioref role="doc-biblioref"}), and rating a word's pleasantness versus counting letters in each word ([Barcroft, 2002](#ch18.xhtml_ref18_4){#ch18.xhtml_R_ref18_4 .biblioref role="doc-biblioref"}). These studies generally demonstrated a negative effect of semantically elaborative tasks on word form learning when compared to simply trying to process new words as input, supporting predictions of TOPRA. Subsequent studies have also investigated the learning of novel secondary meanings of homonyms, demonstrating how increases in semantically oriented processing and form-oriented processing can decrease processing for and learning of the mapping component of vocabulary learning ([Kida & Barcroft, 2018](#ch18.xhtml_ref18_24){#ch18.xhtml_R_ref18_24 .biblioref role="doc-biblioref"}).

Another line of lex-IP research has examined how different tasks influence how learners process lexical input. Tasks include word-copying ([Barcroft, 2006](#ch18.xhtml_ref18_7){.biblioref role="doc-biblioref"}) and choral repetition ([Wong & Barcroft, 2020](#ch18.xhtml_ref18_60){.biblioref role="doc-biblioref"}). Other studies examined the effect of retrieval ([Barcroft, 2007](#ch18.xhtml_ref18_8){#ch18.xhtml_R_ref18_8 .biblioref role="doc-biblioref"}; [Kang et al., 2013](#ch18.xhtml_ref18_23){#ch18.xhtml_R_ref18_23 .biblioref role="doc-biblioref"}; [Terai et al., 2021](#ch18.xhtml_ref18_45){#ch18.xhtml_R_ref18_45 .biblioref role="doc-biblioref"}) and found that learning new words while providing opportunities to retrieve target words (such as when cued by pictures of referents) led to larger vocabulary gains than simply trying to remember words or orally repeating words while viewing corresponding illustrations. Several studies also investigated the effect of input variability. [Barcroft and Sommers (2005)](#ch18.xhtml_ref18_13){#ch18.xhtml_R_ref18_13 .biblioref role="doc-biblioref"} compared an acoustically varied input condition (e.g., a word was pronounced by multiple talkers or in multiple speaking styles) to a control condition (e.g., a word is pronounced by a single talker or in a single speaking style) and found that the former condition led to greater vocabulary learning. [Sommers and Barcroft (2013)](#ch18.xhtml_ref18_41){#ch18.xhtml_R_ref18_41 .biblioref role="doc-biblioref"} also examined the effects of referent token variability (seeing multiple referents as opposed to a single referent token when learning new L2 word forms) and found that such a visual/meaning-oriented variety of variability produced negative effects on L2 word form learning. These combined findings provide evidence for the TOPRA model by demonstrating that a manipulation of word form (acoustic variability) positively affected L2 word form learning whereas a manipulation on the semantic front (referent token variability) negatively affected word form learning.

Findings and implications of lex-IP research have been summarized in *Thirty observations about lexical input processing and vocabulary learning* ([Barcroft, 2015](#ch18.xhtml_ref18_11){.biblioref role="doc-biblioref"}, pp. 166--171) whereby lex-IP is treated as one of many levels of IP (in addition to syntactic IP, pragmatic IP, and so forth), and vocabulary learning is comprised of the learning of three main subcomponents: form, meaning, and mapping. Observations include the following: (a) increased lex-IP for one subcomponent can increase processing/learning for that subcomponent and can decrease processing/learning for other subcomponents, (b) output without access to meaning (e.g., word copying) can detract from lex-IP and vocabulary learning whereas output with access to meaning (e.g., retrieval practice) increases vocabulary learning, and (c) some types of input enhancement (e.g., acoustic variability) can positively affect the quality of lexical representations.
:::

::::::: {.section aria-labelledby="sec18_3"}
## Critical Issues and Topics {#ch18.xhtml_sec18_3}

This section first discusses theories related to intentional vocabulary learning from a lex-IP perspective, i.e., LOP and *transfer-appropriate processing* (TAP) ([Morris et al., 1977](#ch18.xhtml_ref18_30){#ch18.xhtml_R_ref18_30 .biblioref role="doc-biblioref"}), which concern how one processes information and the effects of processing on memory (see [Surprenant & Neath, 2009](#ch18.xhtml_ref18_42){#ch18.xhtml_R_ref18_42 .biblioref role="doc-biblioref"} for a comprehensive review). The discussion then moves to the applicability of LOP and TAP in L2 contexts, to the TOPRA model and TFA, which make predictions about how learners allocate their limited processing resources when exposed to novel vocabulary in the input, leading to different amounts of learning of different aspects of vocabulary knowledge.

::: {.section aria-labelledby="sec18_3_1"}
### []{#ch18.xhtml_p257 .pagebreak aria-label=" page 257. " role="doc-pagebreak"}What Frameworks for Research on Human Memory May Be Applicable to L2 Vocabulary Learning? {#ch18.xhtml_sec18_3_1}

*Levels of processing* (LOP, or *depth of processing*) seeks to explain how the manner in which one processes information influences retention ([Craik & Lockhart, 1972](#ch18.xhtml_ref18_16){.biblioref role="doc-biblioref"}; [Craik & Tulving, 1975](#ch18.xhtml_ref18_17){#ch18.xhtml_R_ref18_17 .biblioref role="doc-biblioref"}). [Craik and Lockhart (1972)](#ch18.xhtml_ref18_16){.biblioref role="doc-biblioref"} argued that deeper (or greater) processing that involves "a greater degree of semantic or cognitive analysis" ([Craik & Lockhart, 1972](#ch18.xhtml_ref18_16){.biblioref role="doc-biblioref"}, p. 675) leads to better memory performance than shallower processing. Craik and Lockhart demonstrated that when participants engaged in semantic processing tasks (e.g., answering a question about an already known first language (L1) word such as "Is the word a type of fish?" or "Would the word fit the sentence: He met a\_\_\_\_\_in the street?"), their recall of the word was higher than when they engaged in structural processing tasks (e.g., answering a question such as "Is the word in capital letters") or in phonemic processing tasks (e.g., "Does the word rhyme with WEIGHT?"). Importantly, [Craik and Lockhart (1972)](#ch18.xhtml_ref18_16){.biblioref role="doc-biblioref"} dealt with native speakers of English, not language learners. While there is some evidence showing that LOP can be applied to L2 learners when they already know both the form and meaning of a word ([Bird, 2012](#ch18.xhtml_ref18_14){#ch18.xhtml_R_ref18_14 .biblioref role="doc-biblioref"}), LOP may not be of immediate use for L2 vocabulary learning when students need to learn novel forms of unknown words (see [Pressley et al., 1982](#ch18.xhtml_ref18_38){.biblioref role="doc-biblioref"} for evidence showing that different semantic-based strategies did not enhance the learning of unknown L1 words). A large number of studies have demonstrated that semantically oriented processing does not necessarily lead to better L2 word form learning and it can even hinder it (e.g., Barcroft, [2002](#ch18.xhtml_ref18_4){.biblioref role="doc-biblioref"}, [2003](#ch18.xhtml_ref18_5){.biblioref role="doc-biblioref"}, [2004](#ch18.xhtml_ref18_6){.biblioref role="doc-biblioref"}; [Sommers & Barcroft, 2013](#ch18.xhtml_ref18_41){.biblioref role="doc-biblioref"}).

As an alternative framework to LOP, [Morris et al., (1977)](#ch18.xhtml_ref18_30){.biblioref role="doc-biblioref"} proposed *transfer-appropriate processing* (TAP). TAP claims that memory performance is determined not only by how it is processed but also by how it is measured. [Morris et al. (1977)](#ch18.xhtml_ref18_30){.biblioref role="doc-biblioref"} demonstrated that a structural processing task resulted in higher memory recall than a semantic processing task when memory performance was measured with a task involving structural processing. TAP predicts that memory performance is greatest when participants are tested in the same way at study and at testing (e.g., [Anderson, 2000](#ch18.xhtml_ref18_3){#ch18.xhtml_R_ref18_3 .biblioref role="doc-biblioref"}, p. 284; but see also [Nairne, 2002](#ch18.xhtml_ref18_31){#ch18.xhtml_R_ref18_31 .biblioref role="doc-biblioref"}). TAP has been used to explain L2 research findings and suggested as a useful framework to apply to L2 pedagogy (e.g., [Lightbown, 2008](#ch18.xhtml_ref18_28){#ch18.xhtml_R_ref18_28 .biblioref role="doc-biblioref"}). TAP also provides support for the general idea that "the more time you spend doing something, the better you are likely to be at doing it" ([Nation & Newton, 2009](#ch18.xhtml_ref18_35){#ch18.xhtml_R_ref18_35 .biblioref role="doc-biblioref"}, p. 2).
:::

::::: {.section aria-labelledby="sec18_3_2"}
### What Available Predictive Models Are Pertinent to Lex-IP and Intentional Vocabulary Learning? {#ch18.xhtml_sec18_3_2}

Two models that have been applied to intentional vocabulary learning are the type of processing -- resource allocation (TOPRA) model and technique feature analysis (TFA). [Barcroft's (2002)](#ch18.xhtml_ref18_4){.biblioref role="doc-biblioref"} TOPRA model was primarily based on discussions and findings from lex-IP research. [Nation and Webb's (2011)](#ch18.xhtml_ref18_36){#ch18.xhtml_R_ref18_36 .biblioref role="doc-biblioref"} TFA has some aspects in common lex-IP, even though it is not directly related to lex-IP research.

::: {.section aria-labelledby="sec18_3_2_1"}
#### Type of Processing -- Resource Allocation Model {#ch18.xhtml_sec18_3_2_1}

The TOPRA model claims that "different amounts of specific types of processing predict similar corresponding amounts of the same types of learning" ([Barcroft, 2003](#ch18.xhtml_ref18_5){.biblioref role="doc-biblioref"}, p. 548), as illustrated in [Figure 18.1](#ch18.xhtml_fig18_1){#ch18.xhtml_R_fig18_1}. As demonstrated by the thicker outer lines in the figure, a learner's cognitive resources for processing are limited. Therefore, when a learner allocates their resources to a specific type of processing, the cognitive resources that are available for other types of []{#ch18.xhtml_p258 .pagebreak aria-label=" page 258. " role="doc-pagebreak"}processing decrease. Consequently, learning other aspects of word knowledge can be hindered. For example, TOPRA predicts that "when processing demands are sufficiently high, increased semantic processing can increase learning for the semantic properties of words while decreasing learning for their formal properties" ([Barcroft, 2015](#ch18.xhtml_ref18_11){.biblioref role="doc-biblioref"}, p. 64) and vice versa.

![[[Figure]{.label} [18.1 ]{.ordinal}The relationship between processing types and lexical learning in the TOPRA model](#ch18.xhtml_R_fig18_1){.backlink aria-label=" This link returns to its first in-text citation. " role="doc-backlink"} [([Barcroft, 2003](#ch18.xhtml_ref18_5){.biblioref role="doc-biblioref"}, p. 549).]{.source .credit role="doc-credit"}](images/fig18_1_B.jpg){#fig18_1 alt="A diagram illustrating three types of processing in vocabulary learning with thick fixed lines on each outer side to illustrate that processing resources are fixed. The thinner inner vertical lines can move to the left or right depending on the type of processing that a learner is engaged in at a point in time."}

Increased semantic and form processing during intentional vocabulary learning may frequently be observed in language classrooms. For example, it is not uncommon to find students repeatedly writing down new words to memorize them. Such a strategy may increase processing of word form but decrease semantic processing, potentially hindering the learning of semantic aspects of the word. Answering questions about the meaning of a word while learning the word may increase semantic processing but deplete cognitive resources for form processing.
:::

::: {.section aria-labelledby="sec18_3_2_2"}
#### Technique Feature Analysis {#ch18.xhtml_sec18_3_2_2}

In the context of incidental L2 vocabulary learning, [Laufer and Hulstijn (2001)](#ch18.xhtml_ref18_27){.biblioref role="doc-biblioref"} proposed the involvement load hypothesis (ILH), while [Yanagisawa and Webb (2022)](#ch18.xhtml_ref18_63){#ch18.xhtml_R_ref18_63 .biblioref role="doc-biblioref"} proposed the involvement load hypothesis plus (ILH+) to serve as frameworks to explain how different components of a task (i.e., motivational and cognitive factors) influence vocabulary learning. To provide a broader framework that includes more factors that have been found to facilitate vocabulary learning covering both incidental and intentional vocabulary learning, [Nation and Webb (2011)](#ch18.xhtml_ref18_36){.biblioref role="doc-biblioref"} created technique feature analysis (TFA). Although TFA was developed separately from IP research, it aims to explain how different types of IP and learning conditions contribute to lexical learning (as is one aim of lex-IP research, see, e.g., [Barcroft, 2015](#ch18.xhtml_ref18_11){.biblioref role="doc-biblioref"}).

TFA predicts the efficacy of vocabulary learning techniques based on the idea that learning conditions including a larger number of facilitative factors lead to greater gains compared to activities that include fewer factors. TFA is comprised of 18 criteria grouped into five psychological conditions that have been found to facilitate vocabulary learning: motivation, noticing, retrieval, generation, and retention. Each of the 18 criteria is written as questions for which there is a yes or no answer (see [Table 18.1](#ch18.xhtml_table18_1){#ch18.xhtml_R_table18_1}; see also [Nation & Webb, 2011](#ch18.xhtml_ref18_36){.biblioref role="doc-biblioref"}, [Chapter 1](#ch1.xhtml) for detailed explanation of each criterion). If the answer is yes, 1 point is awarded for that criterion. If the answer is no, a score of 0 is awarded. TFA postulates that an activity that meets more criteria leads to greater vocabulary learning compared to an activity that meets fewer criteria. [Table 18.1](#ch18.xhtml_table18_1) shows how TFA can be used to score three activities: word cards, writing using target words, and using the keyword technique (adapted from [Nation & Webb, 2011](#ch18.xhtml_ref18_36){.biblioref role="doc-biblioref"}). Word cards []{#ch18.xhtml_p259 .pagebreak aria-label=" page 259. " role="doc-pagebreak"}scored the highest (11 points), while the writing task and keyword technique scored 8 points. Therefore, vocabulary learning is predicted to be higher when learning with word cards.

  Criteria                                                           Word cards (recalling L1 from L2)   Writing with target words   Keyword technique
  ------------------------------------------------------------------ ----------------------------------- --------------------------- -------------------
  **Motivation**                                                                                                                     
  Is there a clear vocabulary learning goal?                         1                                   1                           1
  Does the activity motivate learning?                               1                                   0                           1
  Do the learners select the words?                                  1                                   0                           1
  **Noticing**                                                                                                                       
  Does the activity focus attention on the target words?             1                                   1                           1
  Does the activity raise awareness of new vocabulary learning?      1                                   1                           1
  Does the activity involve negotiation?                             0                                   0                           0
  **Retrieval**                                                                                                                      
  Does the activity involve retrieval of the word?                   1                                   0                           0
  Is it productive retrieval?                                        0                                   0                           0
  Is it recall?                                                      1                                   0                           0
  Are there multiple retrievals of each word?                        1                                   0                           0
  Is there spacing between retrievals?                               1                                   0                           0
  **Generation**                                                                                                                     
  Does the activity involve generative use?                          0                                   1                           0
  Is it productive?                                                  0                                   1                           0
  Is there a marked change that involves the use of other words?     0                                   1                           0
  **Retention**                                                                                                                      
  Does the activity ensure successful linking of form and meaning?   1                                   1                           1
  Does the activity involve instantiation?                           0                                   0                           0
  Does the activity involve imaging?                                 0                                   0                           1
  Does the activity avoid interference?                              1                                   1                           1

  : [[Table ]{.label}[18.1 ]{.ordinal}Criteria of Technique Feature Analysis and Examples of Scoring](#ch18.xhtml_R_table18_1){.backlink aria-label=" This link returns to its first in-text citation. " role="doc-backlink"} {#table18_1}

<aside aria-label="Notes for Table 18.1" class="tfoot">

Adapted from [Nation & Webb, 2011](#ch18.xhtml_ref18_36){.biblioref role="doc-biblioref"}, p. 318.

</aside>

Some criteria of TFA are closely connected to research findings that are pertinent to and have been interpreted from a lex-IP perspective. For example, the *retrieval* component of TFA asks whether a learner engages in a specific type of cognitive activity, one that has been viewed to strengthen memory pathways during lex-IP. The criteria *Are there multiple retrievals of each word?* and *Is there spacing between retrievals?* relate to learning conditions in which learners were provided with opportunities to recall (or recognize) the information related to target words. Such conditions may direct learners' processing resources towards both form and meaning, which facilitates word form learning and form--meaning connections ([Barcroft, 2007](#ch18.xhtml_ref18_8){.biblioref role="doc-biblioref"}, see also 2015, p. 167 for findings of lex-IP research demonstrating that output with access to meaning []{#ch18.xhtml_p260 .pagebreak aria-label=" page 260. " role="doc-pagebreak"}\[retrieval\] facilitates learning whereas output without access to meaning \[no retrieval\] hinders learning). Other criteria are also conceptually related to whether lexical input is processed, such as *Does the activity ensure successful linking of form and meaning?* and *Is there a clear vocabulary learning goal?* Such criteria may influence how learners process lexical input, leading to different amounts of vocabulary learning.
:::
:::::
:::::::

:::::: {.section aria-labelledby="sec18_4"}
## Current Contributions and Research {#ch18.xhtml_sec18_4}

::: {.section aria-labelledby="sec18_4_1"}
### Does Research Support the TOPRA Model? {#ch18.xhtml_sec18_4_1}

A large body of research has examined TOPRA as it relates to intentional vocabulary learning (Barcroft, [2003](#ch18.xhtml_ref18_5){.biblioref role="doc-biblioref"}, [2004](#ch18.xhtml_ref18_6){.biblioref role="doc-biblioref"}, [2006](#ch18.xhtml_ref18_7){.biblioref role="doc-biblioref"}, [2009](#ch18.xhtml_ref18_9){.biblioref role="doc-biblioref"}, [2019](#ch18.xhtml_ref18_12){.biblioref role="doc-biblioref"}; [Kida & Barcroft, 2018](#ch18.xhtml_ref18_24){.biblioref role="doc-biblioref"}; [Wong & Barcroft, 2020](#ch18.xhtml_ref18_60){.biblioref role="doc-biblioref"}; [Wong & Pyun, 2012](#ch18.xhtml_ref18_61){.biblioref role="doc-biblioref"}; see [Barcroft, 2015](#ch18.xhtml_ref18_11){.biblioref role="doc-biblioref"} for a review). In a seminal study, [Barcroft (2002)](#ch18.xhtml_ref18_4){.biblioref role="doc-biblioref"} compared the effects of semantic and structural elaboration and a condition that did not require either type of processing on word--picture based intentional L2 vocabulary learning. Using a within-participants design, English-speaking participants tried to learn unknown L2 Spanish words in one of three conditions while viewing word picture pairs on a screen: (a) no elaboration (participants were just told to do their best to learn word--picture pairs), (b) structural elaboration (count the number of letters in the L2 word), and (c) semantic elaboration (rate each word based on its perceived pleasantness). After the learning phase, participants took three tests: (a) L2 free recall (write all the Spanish words they learned), (b) L1 free recall (write corresponding English translations to target words), and (c) picture cued L2 recall (write corresponding target word for each picture). Results supported predictions of TOPRA in that L2 free recall scores were higher for the letter-counting condition than for the pleasantness-rating condition whereas L1 free recall scores were higher for the pleasantness-rating condition than for the letter-counting condition. Additionally, scores on the picture cued L2 recall were higher for structural elaboration (word counting) than for semantic elaboration (pleasantness-rating). These findings support TOPRA by demonstrating that the memory for semantic and structural aspects of target words were facilitated by semantic elaboration and structural elaboration, respectively. Lastly, on all three measurements, the no elaboration condition outperformed the two types of elaboration conditions, which in large part may have been due substantially to distractive aspects of completing tasks on paper, as was pointed out by the researcher, but which also speak to the benefits of not getting in the learner's way during lexical IP.

The TOPRA model has also been investigated by using tasks that are more commonly used in classroom contexts. [Barcroft (2004)](#ch18.xhtml_ref18_6){.biblioref role="doc-biblioref"} examined the effects of increased semantic processing by comparing sentence writing (semantic elaboration) to no sentence writing. English-speaking learners of L2 Spanish engaged in two conditions to learn different sets of 12 unknown Spanish words. In the sentence-writing condition, participants viewed word--picture pairs and were given 24 seconds to write each target word in an original sentence. In the no-sentence-writing condition, participants were simply asked to try to learn the target words while looking at each word--picture pair for 24 seconds each. Results from a picture cued recall task (participants wrote a target word for each picture they saw) which was sensitive to word form learning were significantly lower for sentence writing than for no sentence writing. These results support the prediction of TOPRA, i.e., increased semantic processing/elaboration (sentence writing in this case) can decrease word form learning due to learners' overall limited processing capacity during lex-IP.

[Wong and Pyun (2012)](#ch18.xhtml_ref18_61){.biblioref role="doc-biblioref"} replicated [Barcroft's (2004)](#ch18.xhtml_ref18_6){.biblioref role="doc-biblioref"} study with two different populations: English-speaking learners of L2 French and L2 Korean. Results of both populations []{#ch18.xhtml_p261 .pagebreak aria-label=" page 261. " role="doc-pagebreak"}demonstrated negative effects for sentence writing supporting the predictions of TOPRA. Moreover, the negative effect of sentence writing (the semantically elaborative task) was more pronounced for Korean. From the TOPRA perspective, the L2 Korean word forms, being more orthographically distant from English than French is from English, may have been more sensitive to the negative effect of increased semantic processing. Such a negative effect has also been found with other tasks involving semantic elaboration: answering questions related to target words ([Barcroft, 2003](#ch18.xhtml_ref18_5){.biblioref role="doc-biblioref"}), generating synonyms for target words in L1 intentional ([Pressley et al., 1982](#ch18.xhtml_ref18_38){.biblioref role="doc-biblioref"}) and intentional and incidental L2 ([Barcroft, 2009](#ch18.xhtml_ref18_9){.biblioref role="doc-biblioref"}) contexts, and learning words when multiple contexts were provided for each word ([Barcroft, 2019](#ch18.xhtml_ref18_12){.biblioref role="doc-biblioref"}; see also, [Pressley et al., 1982](#ch18.xhtml_ref18_38){.biblioref role="doc-biblioref"} for L1 vocabulary learning).
:::

::: {.section aria-labelledby="sec18_4_2"}
### Does Research Support TFA? {#ch18.xhtml_sec18_4_2}

Studies that have examined vocabulary learning tasks from a TFA perspective include [Marcos Miguel and Cubas-Mora (2017)](#ch18.xhtml_ref18_29){#ch18.xhtml_R_ref18_29 .biblioref role="doc-biblioref"}, [Zou et al. (2018)](#ch18.xhtml_ref18_65){#ch18.xhtml_R_ref18_65 .biblioref role="doc-biblioref"}, and [Zou and Xie (2018)](#ch18.xhtml_ref18_64){#ch18.xhtml_R_ref18_64 .biblioref role="doc-biblioref"}. [Zou et al. (2018)](#ch18.xhtml_ref18_65){.biblioref role="doc-biblioref"} compared the effectiveness of three types of tasks (i.e., reading comprehension, fill-in-the-blanks, and sentence writing) and two types of annotations for target words (i.e., pictorial annotations and textual annotations). Results supported the prediction of TFA; reading comprehension with pictorial annotations and fill-in-the-blanks with textual annotations, both of which meet seven criteria of TFA, led to a similar degree of vocabulary learning. Similarly, fill-in-the-blanks with pictorial annotations and sentence writing with textual annotations, both of which meet eight criteria of TFA, led to a similar degree of learning gain. Also, as TFA predicts, the latter two activities were more effective than the former two activities. [Zou et al. (2018)](#ch18.xhtml_ref18_65){.biblioref role="doc-biblioref"} concluded that TFA is a reliable framework for evaluating and predicting task effectiveness.

TFA can also guide language teachers to select and design effective word-focused activities ([Nation & Webb, 2011](#ch18.xhtml_ref18_36){.biblioref role="doc-biblioref"}; [Webb, 2012](#ch18.xhtml_ref18_55){#ch18.xhtml_R_ref18_55 .biblioref role="doc-biblioref"}; [Webb & Nation, 2017](#ch18.xhtml_ref18_57){#ch18.xhtml_R_ref18_57 .biblioref role="doc-biblioref"}). [Zou and Xie (2018)](#ch18.xhtml_ref18_64){.biblioref role="doc-biblioref"} developed a personalized e-learning system that recommends tasks to L2 English learners based on TFA. Three groups of participants learned 40 target words in one of the three conditions: (a) personalized system based on a full list of TFA, (b) personalized condition based on a reduced list of TFA (focusing on noticing, retrieval, and generation), and (c) non-personalized condition where participants selected their own tasks. Results showed that the personalized system with a full list of TFA led to the greatest learning gains, followed by the personalized system with the reduced list of TFA, and the non-personalized condition, respectively. These results demonstrate that TFA may also work as a guideline for learning. [Marcos Miguel and Cubas-Mora (2017)](#ch18.xhtml_ref18_29){.biblioref role="doc-biblioref"} asked nine instructors to use TFA to evaluate vocabulary-focused activities in a college-level beginner's Spanish textbook. Participants successfully identified the different facilitative characteristics involved in the activities based on the criteria of TFA, showing that TFA may serve as a useful tool to evaluate vocabulary learning activities in the classroom.

Despite a growing body of studies on TFA showing its usefulness for intentional vocabulary learning, the number of studies evaluating the predictive ability of TFA remains limited. Future studies should explore how accurately TFA predicts vocabulary learning and the relative impact of the different criteria of TFA. Additionally, there is some overlap between TFA and [Barcroft's (2015)](#ch18.xhtml_ref18_11){.biblioref role="doc-biblioref"} *thirty observations* about lex-IP. Both frameworks discuss the inhibitory effect of semantically related words and the facilitative effect of retrieval and spaced learning. In terms of theory building, it would be interesting to consider how TFA and the thirty lex-IP related observations complement each other. For example, how do the different criteria of TFA impact different components of vocabulary learning, i.e., form, meaning, and form--meaning connections?
:::

::: {.section aria-labelledby="sec18_4_3"}
### []{#ch18.xhtml_p262 .pagebreak aria-label=" page 262. " role="doc-pagebreak"}Other Research on Lex-IP: Writing, Choral Repetition, and Acoustically Varied Input {#ch18.xhtml_sec18_4_3}

Although not directly related to TOPRA or TFA, there are several other areas of research pertinent to lex-IP writing activities, oral repetition, and varied input. To begin, research has examined the effect of word-copying, where students try to memorize words by repeatedly writing them down. [Thomas and Dieter (1987)](#ch18.xhtml_ref18_46){#ch18.xhtml_R_ref18_46 .biblioref role="doc-biblioref"} found an advantage for word-copying over rote-learning at the level of form recall, while no clear differences were found between gains at the levels of meaning recall and recognition. In contrast, [Barcroft (2006)](#ch18.xhtml_ref18_7){.biblioref role="doc-biblioref"} found that learning words associated with pictures while writing the words led to smaller gains than simply viewing the word--picture pairs, showing a negative effect for word-copying. Interestingly, [Webb and Piasecki (2018)](#ch18.xhtml_ref18_58){.biblioref role="doc-biblioref"} revealed that the effectiveness of word-copying is influenced by time-on-task. They compared three conditions for the learning of word--picture pairs: (a) limited time to write words, (b) unlimited time to write, and (c) timed memorization of word--picture pairs without writing. The findings suggested that writing led to greater learning gains when it was not timed and students took as much time as they needed, whereas little difference was found between writing and memorization conditions when time-on-task was held constant.

Studies investigating sentence writing also point to a potential confounding effect of time-on-task. [Barcroft (2004)](#ch18.xhtml_ref18_6){.biblioref role="doc-biblioref"} and [Wong and Pyun (2012)](#ch18.xhtml_ref18_61){.biblioref role="doc-biblioref"} found that sentence writing, where participants were told to study word--picture pairs while writing an original sentence using each word yielded smaller learning gains for meaning recall compared to simply trying to study word--picture pairs given the same amount of time. [Webb (2005)](#ch18.xhtml_ref18_53){#ch18.xhtml_R_ref18_53 .biblioref role="doc-biblioref"} compared sentence writing to list learning with example sentences. Results showed that when the same amount of time was provided, list learning led to more learning than sentence writing. However, when there was no time limit, sentence writing took longer and led to larger learning gains than list learning. These results suggest that time-on-task may play an important role in the effectiveness of writing activities (see e.g., [Folse, 2006](#ch18.xhtml_ref18_19){#ch18.xhtml_R_ref18_19 .biblioref role="doc-biblioref"}).

While the results of the two studies cannot be compared directly because of differences in methodology (e.g., different target words, meaning representations \[picture vs. L1 words\], L1 backgrounds, learning contexts), research indicates that sentence writing does not improve and can reduce vocabulary learning when compared to conditions in which participants were allowed to study words (without sentence writing) when time-on-task is held constant. Findings also suggest that in order to see benefits of sentence writing, students need to be provided with unlimited time (see [Laufer & Hulstijn, 2001](#ch18.xhtml_ref18_27){.biblioref role="doc-biblioref"} for a similar discussion; see also [Yanagisawa & Webb, 2021](#ch18.xhtml_ref18_62){#ch18.xhtml_R_ref18_62 .biblioref role="doc-biblioref"} for the relationship between involvement loads and time-on-task). This discussion may also highlight the importance of evaluating "effectiveness" of learning from multiple perspectives, including words learned per minute ([Krashen, 1989](#ch18.xhtml_ref18_26){#ch18.xhtml_R_ref18_26 .biblioref role="doc-biblioref"}; [Webb et al., 2020](#ch18.xhtml_ref18_59){#ch18.xhtml_R_ref18_59 .biblioref role="doc-biblioref"}) in addition to raw learning gains.

Oral repetition can be viewed as a spoken version of word-copying. [Wong and Barcroft's (2020)](#ch18.xhtml_ref18_60){.biblioref role="doc-biblioref"} study with L2 French and L2 Spanish learners on the effects of choral repetition on vocabulary learning compared a condition in which participants attempted to learn L2 words while orally repeating them to a condition that did not involve choral repetition. Results indicated no clear differences across conditions and indicated no clear advantage of choral repetition. [Kang et al. (2013)](#ch18.xhtml_ref18_23){.biblioref role="doc-biblioref"} compared oral repetition to retrieval practice and found that retrieval practice yielded greater vocabulary learning. These findings indicate that retrieval effectively strengthens form--meaning mapping whereas oral repetition does not.

Several studies have examined the effect of acoustically varied input, e.g., listening to words pronounced by multiple talkers as opposed to a single talker, on intentional vocabulary []{#ch18.xhtml_p263 .pagebreak aria-label=" page 263. " role="doc-pagebreak"}learning. For instance, [Barcroft and Sommers (2005)](#ch18.xhtml_ref18_13){.biblioref role="doc-biblioref"} demonstrated that presenting target L2 words paired with pictures in acoustically varied conditions yielded significantly greater learning than no-variability conditions. Their follow-up study revealed that the positive effect of acoustic variability may be attributed to the enhanced quality of form representations (i.e., spoken aspect of words) as a result of varied input rather than increased cognitive demands induced by variability ([Sommers & Barcroft, 2011](#ch18.xhtml_ref18_40){#ch18.xhtml_R_ref18_40 .biblioref role="doc-biblioref"}). Meanwhile, [Sommers and Barcroft (2013)](#ch18.xhtml_ref18_41){.biblioref role="doc-biblioref"} examined input variability in the form of referent tokens (i.e., visual presentations of words). They compared learning words with one corresponding picture (no variation) to learning words with several corresponding pictures (referent token varied). In contrast to the positive effect of form-oriented acoustic variability, a negative effect of semantically oriented referent token variability was revealed, which provides support for TOPRA by demonstrating that increased semantic processing (in this case tied to the multicomponential visual processing of properties of referents) can decrease word form learning whereas form-based variability, as demonstrated in other studies, can increase word form learning.
:::
::::::

:::::: {.section aria-labelledby="sec18_5"}
## Recommendations for Practice {#ch18.xhtml_sec18_5}

Lex-IP research has pedagogical implications for language instructors, learners, and material developers. This section focuses on three main implications informed by findings of lex-IP research (see also [Barcroft, 2013](#ch18.xhtml_ref18_10){#ch18.xhtml_R_ref18_10 .biblioref role="doc-biblioref"} for a comprehensive framework for input-based incremental vocabulary instruction based on lex-IP research for the L2 classroom).

::: {.section aria-labelledby="sec18_5_1"}
### Be Cautious When Asking Students to Use Semantic Elaboration When Time is Limited {#ch18.xhtml_sec18_5_1}

As discussed previously, research demonstrates that instructing students to use semantically elaborative processes (e.g., synonym generation, sentence writing, pleasantness rating) when study time is limited can decrease vocabulary learning (Barcroft, [2003](#ch18.xhtml_ref18_5){.biblioref role="doc-biblioref"}, [2004](#ch18.xhtml_ref18_6){.biblioref role="doc-biblioref"}, [2009](#ch18.xhtml_ref18_9){.biblioref role="doc-biblioref"}; [Kida & Barcroft, 2018](#ch18.xhtml_ref18_24){.biblioref role="doc-biblioref"}; [Wong & Pyun, 2012](#ch18.xhtml_ref18_61){.biblioref role="doc-biblioref"}). Additionally, [Webb (2005)](#ch18.xhtml_ref18_53){.biblioref role="doc-biblioref"} found that a semantically elaborated task (i.e., sentence writing) took longer and led to larger learning gains than reading sentences when students could engage in learning at their own pace whereas reading sentences led to greater learning when the same amount of time was provided. These findings provide three implications: (a) providing students with ample time for learning is crucial for effective deliberate vocabulary learning; (b) when each student cannot be provided with ample learning time, asking them to engage in an elaborated task such as writing and generating synonyms is best avoided; and (c) when time is limited, it may be more useful for students to engage in rote learning such as list learning or retrieval practice with flashcards. However, since only a few studies have directly examined the effect of variations on time-on-task ([Webb, 2005](#ch18.xhtml_ref18_53){.biblioref role="doc-biblioref"}; [Webb et al., 2020](#ch18.xhtml_ref18_59){.biblioref role="doc-biblioref"}; [Webb & Piasecki, 2018](#ch18.xhtml_ref18_58){.biblioref role="doc-biblioref"}), these suggestions should be considered tentative and require confirmation or revision with future research.
:::

::: {.section aria-labelledby="sec18_5_2"}
### Provide Students with a Number of Retrieval Opportunities {#ch18.xhtml_sec18_5_2}

Many researchers have highlighted the importance of repeated opportunities for retrieval in vocabulary learning (e.g., [Barcroft, 2007](#ch18.xhtml_ref18_8){.biblioref role="doc-biblioref"}; [Schmitt, 2008](#ch18.xhtml_ref18_39){#ch18.xhtml_R_ref18_39 .biblioref role="doc-biblioref"}; [Webb & Nation, 2017](#ch18.xhtml_ref18_57){.biblioref role="doc-biblioref"}). One easy way to implement retrieval practice in intentional vocabulary learning is to use flashcards and word lists. The effectiveness of flashcards has been well documented both with paper-based cards []{#ch18.xhtml_p264 .pagebreak aria-label=" page 264. " role="doc-pagebreak"}(e.g., [Waring, 1997a](#ch18.xhtml_ref18_51){#ch18.xhtml_R_ref18_51 .biblioref role="doc-biblioref"}) and with computer software (e.g., [Nakata, 2016](#ch18.xhtml_ref18_32){#ch18.xhtml_R_ref18_32 .biblioref role="doc-biblioref"}). A meta-analysis by [Webb et al. (2020)](#ch18.xhtml_ref18_59){.biblioref role="doc-biblioref"} also revealed that learning gains were higher on average for flashcards and word lists compared to writing or fill-in-the-blank activities. As demonstrated by [Webb (2009)](#ch18.xhtml_ref18_54){#ch18.xhtml_R_ref18_54 .biblioref role="doc-biblioref"}, students can use a word list to remember new words while retrieving either L2 target words or their corresponding L1 translations by covering the corresponding cues with their hand. These findings point to how L2 students could learn vocabulary moderately well by using simple strategies that provide retrieval opportunities.
:::

::: {.section aria-labelledby="sec18_5_3"}
### Combine Intentional Learning with Incidental Learning {#ch18.xhtml_sec18_5_3}

While intentional learning tends to lead to greater immediate gains in vocabulary knowledge than incidental learning (e.g., [Barcroft, 2009](#ch18.xhtml_ref18_9){.biblioref role="doc-biblioref"}), intentional learning alone has three main fundamental weaknesses. The first weakness is related to practicality. [Webb et al.'s (2020)](#ch18.xhtml_ref18_59){.biblioref role="doc-biblioref"} meta-analysis demonstrated that learning gains from intentional learning activities quickly decayed when measured by delayed posttests. Given the high demand for students' vocabulary acquisition ([Schmitt, 2008](#ch18.xhtml_ref18_39){.biblioref role="doc-biblioref"}), it may be impossible to learn new words and maintain all of the learned words only by intentional learning. Because learned words can be forgotten without additional contact, students need to keep encountering and using the learned words in incidental learning contexts such as reading, listening, viewing, writing, and speaking ([Uchihara et al., 2019](#ch18.xhtml_ref18_49){#ch18.xhtml_R_ref18_49 .biblioref role="doc-biblioref"}). Additionally, intentional learning often focuses only on the form--meaning mapping of target words by associating words with their corresponding L1 translations or L2 meanings. To fluently use learned words in communicative contexts, as suggested in [Barcroft's (2013)](#ch18.xhtml_ref18_10){.biblioref role="doc-biblioref"} input-based incremental (IBI) vocabulary instruction principles, it is important to promote both intentional and incidental vocabulary learning, and to provide students with ample meaning-bearing comprehensible input, with which students can foster development of their lexical competence (e.g., collocational use, fluency, nuance, and range of usage).
:::
::::::

::: {.section aria-labelledby="sec18_6"}
## Future Directions {#ch18.xhtml_sec18_6}

Lex-IP research should be expanded in three areas. The first is to deepen our understanding of how one's cognitive resources are used during learning. Given that lex-IP research focuses on how L2 students allocate their cognitive resources for processing different aspects of vocabulary knowledge, it would be useful to quantify students' cognitive resources. As mentioned in [Barcroft (2015)](#ch18.xhtml_ref18_11){.biblioref role="doc-biblioref"}, electrophysiological measures and neuroimaging might reveal which type of processing students are engaging in during learning and how they use limited cognitive resources (see e.g., [Suzuki et al., 2022](#ch18.xhtml_ref18_44){#ch18.xhtml_R_ref18_44 .biblioref role="doc-biblioref"} for functional magnetic resonance imaging (fMRI) research exploring implicit and explicit knowledge). Similarly, elaborating on the TOPRA model by hypothesizing when certain students' cognitive resources are depleted would also be useful. One example could be related to time-on-task. When students are provided with a relatively short time to conduct a certain task while deliberately studying new words, they may easily run short on their cognitive resources, resulting in insufficient resources for adequate processing of form, meaning, and mapping. Conversely, when students engage in the same task (or different tasks) repeatedly, they might be able to allocate resources adequately. For instance, with students learning new L1 words, [Pressley and Levin (1981, Exp. 2)](#ch18.xhtml_ref18_37){#ch18.xhtml_R_ref18_37 .biblioref role="doc-biblioref"} found that learning the form of a word beforehand enhanced the effectiveness of semantic learning through the keyword method. Similar situations may often be observed in L2 learning contexts (e.g., learning word form first with word copying, then trying to connect it to its meaning with flashcards or by encountering it in reading), as []{#ch18.xhtml_p265 .pagebreak aria-label=" page 265. " role="doc-pagebreak"}vocabulary learning occurs incrementally. In these situations, where students repeatedly engage in tasks to deal with the same new words, they might be able to cope with limited cognitive resources differently. Creating sub-hypotheses might help identify when learners' cognitive resources are taxed and when they are not, which hopefully leads to further development of the TOPRA model by suggesting how students can strategize the use of their limited cognitive resources.

A second area of research that requires further attention is to investigate how to increase the effectiveness of learning strategies such as retrieval practice. Research has indicated that gains from retrieval practice can be enhanced by spaced learning and interleaving (e.g., [Nakata, 2020](#ch18.xhtml_ref18_33){#ch18.xhtml_R_ref18_33 .biblioref role="doc-biblioref"}). *Desirable difficulty* claims that learning outcomes are optimized when the difficulty in learning is at the *desirable* level and that the effects of spaced learning and interleaving can be explained as these conditions regulate the difficulty in learning (e.g., [Bjork & Bjork, 2011](#ch18.xhtml_ref18_15){#ch18.xhtml_R_ref18_15 .biblioref role="doc-biblioref"}). [Suzuki et al., (2019)](#ch18.xhtml_ref18_43){#ch18.xhtml_R_ref18_43 .biblioref role="doc-biblioref"} call for the consideration of *desirable difficulty* as one framework to explain L2 practice. Despite its usefulness as a framework, it is likely to be challenging to determine when difficulty is at a *desirable* level. [Suzuki et al. (2019)](#ch18.xhtml_ref18_43){.biblioref role="doc-biblioref"} discussed several ways to operationalize this difficulty in research. It may be useful for future studies to explore (a) which type of difficulty relates to the effect of IP on learning and (b) how one can tell the difficulty is at a "desirable" level to maximize learning outcomes.

A third area concerns semantic clustering. Many studies have revealed a negative effect of semantic clustering on intentional vocabulary learning. For example, [Tinkham's (1993)](#ch18.xhtml_ref18_47){#ch18.xhtml_R_ref18_47 .biblioref role="doc-biblioref"} seminal study found that learning a set of semantically related words needed more repetition than a set of unrelated words, suggesting that semantic clustering results in increased difficulty (e.g., see also [Tinkham, 1997](#ch18.xhtml_ref18_48){#ch18.xhtml_R_ref18_48 .biblioref role="doc-biblioref"}; [Waring, 1997b](#ch18.xhtml_ref18_52){#ch18.xhtml_R_ref18_52 .biblioref role="doc-biblioref"}). [Barcroft (2015)](#ch18.xhtml_ref18_11){.biblioref role="doc-biblioref"} points to an interesting potential interpretation from the TOPRA mode that when target words are presented in a semantic set, it might overload the learner's ultimately limited capacity for semantically oriented processing. Additionally, and interestingly, several studies found facilitative and null effects of semantic clustering (e.g., [Hoshino, 2010](#ch18.xhtml_ref18_20){#ch18.xhtml_R_ref18_20 .biblioref role="doc-biblioref"}; [Nakata & Suzuki, 2018](#ch18.xhtml_ref18_34){#ch18.xhtml_R_ref18_34 .biblioref role="doc-biblioref"}). [Ishii (2015)](#ch18.xhtml_ref18_22){#ch18.xhtml_R_ref18_22 .biblioref role="doc-biblioref"} also found that when similarity in the shapes of word references (e.g., round or rectangular) was controlled, the negative effect of semantic clustering was not observed. Semantic clustering might lead to better learning when the difficulty in learning is at a "desirable level" ([Nakata & Suzuki, 2018](#ch18.xhtml_ref18_34){.biblioref role="doc-biblioref"}) or when ample learning time is provided ([Ishii, 2015](#ch18.xhtml_ref18_22){.biblioref role="doc-biblioref"}). Future studies attempting to identify the mechanism responsible for semantic clustering negatively (or positively) influencing vocabulary learning may provide insights on how semantic clustering relates to lex-IP in light of learners' limited processing capacity.
:::

::: {.section .reference aria-labelledby="r18_1"}
## Further Reading {#ch18.xhtml_r18_1}

- [Barcroft, J. (2015). `<cite>`{=html}Lexical input processing and vocabulary learning`</cite>`{=html}. John Benjamins.]{#ch18.xhtml_ref18_1}

  This book reviews theories and studies related to lex-IP and discusses their pedagogical implications. It provides comprehensive reviews and examinations of the studies related to the TOPRA model. Pedagogical guidance in relation to exposure frequency, avoiding presenting target words in semantic sets, and learning with enhanced input such as speaker variability in spoken input is provided.

- [Webb, S. A. (Ed.). (2020). `<cite>`{=html}The Routledge handbook of vocabulary studies`</cite>`{=html}. Routledge.]{#ch18.xhtml_ref18_2}

  This handbook includes several chapters pertinent to lex-IP as related to vocabulary learning. Batia Laufer's chapter (Ch. 23) discusses the TOPRA model as well as the ILH and TFA from the perspective of analyzing the effectiveness of word-focused activities on vocabulary learning. Seth Lindstromberg's (Ch. 16) and Judit Kormos's (Ch. 14) chapters discuss several frameworks related to intentional vocabulary learning and vocabulary acquisition in general. Elke Peters (Ch. 9) and Frank Boers (Ch. 10) review various factors influencing the learning of single-word items and multiword items.
:::
