[https://doi.org/10.1007/978-981-13-0008-0_1](https://doi.org/10.1007/978-981-13-0008-0_1){aria-label="D.O.I. link to Technology in Education. Innovative Solutions and Practices"}]{#ch18.xhtml_ref18_65}
:::

[]{#ch19.xhtml}

::: {}
# []{#ch19.xhtml_p269 .pagebreak aria-label=" page 269. " role="doc-pagebreak"}[19 ]{.chnum .ordinal}[Input Processing and the Acquisition of Pragmatics]{.chtitle} {#ch19.xhtml_h1}

Friederike Fichtner

California State University, Chico

DOI: [10.4324/9781003041412-24](https://dx.doi.org/10.4324/9781003041412-24){aria-label="D.O.I. link to this document."}
:::

:::::: {.section .introduction role="doc-introduction"}
## Introduction and Definitions {#ch19.xhtml_sec19_1}

The acquisition of pragmatics in a second language (L2) involves learning sociocultural conventions and norms of language use in different contexts---such as what to say (or not to say) in a given situation or how to infer and convey intensions in contextually appropriate ways. To develop pragmatic competence, learners need to attend to co-occurrences of form, function, and context, which are not only complex but also variable. But what mechanisms are involved in input processing (IP) when it comes to the comprehension and acquisition of pragmatics? Surprisingly, psycholinguistic approaches to acquiring pragmatics have remained relatively scarce in second language acquisition (SLA) research ([Kasper & Rose, 1999](#ch19.xhtml_ref19_32){#ch19.xhtml_R_ref19_32 .biblioref role="doc-biblioref"}; [Holtgraves et al., 2019](#ch19.xhtml_ref19_30){#ch19.xhtml_R_ref19_30 .biblioref role="doc-biblioref"}), and it is only recently that experimental research on L2 pragmatic development has started to emerge. Despite the increasing number of processing-oriented studies in L2 pragmatics, no study to date has investigated IP for the acquisition of pragmatics. While most research on IP has focused on the acquisition of morphosyntax and, more recently, vocabulary, IP happens at all levels of linguistic analysis, including pragmatics.

This chapter proposes an IP perspective on acquiring pragmatics as a new direction in both L2 pragmatics and IP research. It seeks to stimulate crosstalk between these two areas of research by outlining questions and possible theoretical and methodological avenues for a new type of IP research focused on acquiring pragmatics. The chapter first addresses theoretical underpinnings and key constructs before turning to how input, IP, and intake have been defined in L2 pragmatics since the field's inception. The critical issues section discusses questions such as how central constructs of IP theory can be defined for research on IP for pragmatics. After highlighting current contributions related to pragmatic IP, the chapter concludes with directions for future research.

::: {.section aria-labelledby="sec19_1_1"}
### Pragmatics {#ch19.xhtml_sec19_1_1}

*Pragmatics* refers to "the study of speaker and hearer meaning created in their joint actions that include both linguistic and non-linguistic signals in the context of socioculturally organized activities" ([LoCastro, 2003](#ch19.xhtml_ref19_41){#ch19.xhtml_R_ref19_41 .biblioref role="doc-biblioref"}, p. 15). In other words, pragmatics investigates the various linguistic and non-linguistic ways in which meaning is communicated in interaction, as well as the []{#ch19.xhtml_p270 .pagebreak aria-label=" page 270. " role="doc-pagebreak"}sociocultural factors that influence how people convey and interpret meaning. Pragmatics can further be subdivided into *pragmalinguistics*, which deals with linguistic forms and their communicative functions, and *sociopragmatics*, which concerns the cultural and social norms that govern language use within a given speech community ([Thomas, 1983](#ch19.xhtml_ref19_64){#ch19.xhtml_R_ref19_64 .biblioref role="doc-biblioref"}). Moreover, while the fields of pragmatics and semantics both have in common the study of linguistic meaning, semantics focuses on truth-conditional, explicit, and context-variant sentence-level meaning while pragmatics investigates the non-truth-conditional, indirect, and non-literal meaning conveyed by a given speaker's utterances ([Ariel, 2010](#ch19.xhtml_ref19_6){#ch19.xhtml_R_ref19_6 .biblioref role="doc-biblioref"}).
:::

::: {.section aria-labelledby="sec19_1_2"}
### L2 Pragmatics and Pragmatic Competence {#ch19.xhtml_sec19_1_2}

Whereas *interlanguage pragmatics* investigates L2 learners' "comprehension, production and acquisition of linguistic action in L2" ([Kasper, 1998](#ch19.xhtml_ref19_31){#ch19.xhtml_R_ref19_31 .biblioref role="doc-biblioref"}, p. 184), its subfield *L2 pragmatics* (or *acquisitional pragmatics*) examines specifically "the development of the L2 pragmatic system" ([Bardovi-Harlig, 2013](#ch19.xhtml_ref19_12){#ch19.xhtml_R_ref19_12 .biblioref role="doc-biblioref"}, p. 69). Current L2 pragmatics research views *pragmatic competence* as a multidimensional construct that encompasses: (1) (pragma)linguistic knowledge (linguistic resources necessary for achieving certain communicative functions) and sociopragmatic knowledge (social norms that underlie use and interpretation of utterances and their intended communicative functions, and contextual factors that determine the appropriateness of the use of a given form); (2) interactional abilities to use this pragmalinguistic/sociopragmatic knowledge according to changing discursive contexts; and (3) agency---as speakers ultimately decide for themselves whether or how to implement their knowledge in a given community ([Taguchi, 2019](#ch19.xhtml_ref19_56){#ch19.xhtml_R_ref19_56 .biblioref role="doc-biblioref"}).
:::

::: {.section aria-labelledby="sec19_1_3"}
### Input, Intake, and IP {#ch19.xhtml_sec19_1_3}

The most elaborate account of IP in SLA is VanPatten's ([1996](#ch19.xhtml_ref19_68){#ch19.xhtml_R_ref19_68 .biblioref role="doc-biblioref"}, [2020](#ch19.xhtml_ref19_69){#ch19.xhtml_R_ref19_69 .biblioref role="doc-biblioref"}) IP model, which conceptualizes L2 acquisition as a "by-product of learners' actively attempting to comprehend input" ([VanPatten, 2020](#ch19.xhtml_ref19_69){.biblioref role="doc-biblioref"}, p. 120). While comprehension does not guarantee acquisition, it is during online comprehension that learners make form--meaning connections. In VanPatten's model, *input* refers to samples of a language to which learners are exposed, whereas *input processing* concerns how learners derive *intake*, which is the subset of the input that becomes available and provides information or data to the learners' developing linguistic system ([VanPatten, 1996](#ch19.xhtml_ref19_68){.biblioref role="doc-biblioref"}, p. 10). As intake is the type of data the developing system needs to restructure itself, it may subsequently be incorporated---partially or completely---into the developing linguistic system (a process called *accommodation*). IP is thus concerned with the initial data gathering (i.e. making initial form--meaning connections) as the first stage in a series of internal processes that lead to acquisition. As such, IP differs from other language processing research as it is focused on IP as it relates to learning (as opposed to language processing research focused on the nature of existing representations or knowledge). The fundamental questions of IP concern (a) under what conditions learners make initial form--meaning connections; (b) why learners make some but not other form--meaning connections; and (c) what internal mechanisms and psycholinguistic (unconscious) strategies learners use during real-time comprehension and how these affect acquisition ([VanPatten, 2020](#ch19.xhtml_ref19_69){.biblioref role="doc-biblioref"}, p. 106; see also Leeser, this volume).

While the bulk of IP research has focused on sentence-level IP for the acquisition of morphosyntax, more recently, studies have begun to investigate IP for other linguistic subsystems. Research on *lexical input processing* (lex-IP), for instance, examines "how learners allocate their limited attentional and cognitive processing resources when they are exposed to a new word or lexical phrase" ([Barcroft, 2015](#ch19.xhtml_ref19_8){#ch19.xhtml_R_ref19_8 .biblioref role="doc-biblioref"}, p. 1). Yet, the multilevel input processing framework ([]{#ch19.xhtml_p271 .pagebreak aria-label=" page 271. " role="doc-pagebreak"}[Barcroft, 2015](#ch19.xhtml_ref19_8){.biblioref role="doc-biblioref"}) is the only IP framework to date that mentions "*pragmatic input* (prag-INPUT) and *pragmatic input processing* (prag-IP) at the pragmatic level" (p. 14) as integrated with other information processing systems at different levels of linguistic analysis---however, without further defining the constructs of prag-INPUT and prag-IP. What might IP for acquiring pragmatics entail, and how have input and intake been defined thus far in L2 pragmatics research?
:::
::::::

::: {.section aria-labelledby="sec19_2"}
## Historical Perspectives {#ch19.xhtml_sec19_2}

Although researchers in L2 pragmatics have underscored the importance of understanding cognitive and psycholinguistic processes involved in acquiring pragmatics since the field's beginnings in the 1980s ([Faerch & Kasper, 1984](#ch19.xhtml_ref19_21){#ch19.xhtml_R_ref19_21 .biblioref role="doc-biblioref"}), processing issues have received little attention due to the sociolinguistic rather than psycholinguistic orientation of the field ([Kasper & Rose, 1999](#ch19.xhtml_ref19_32){.biblioref role="doc-biblioref"}).

[Schmidt's (1995)](#ch19.xhtml_ref19_49){#ch19.xhtml_R_ref19_49 .biblioref role="doc-biblioref"} *noticing hypothesis* represents the most widely used theoretical proposal regarding the role of input in L2 pragmatic acquisition to date. It assumes that "what learners notice in input is what becomes intake for learning" ([Schmidt, 1995](#ch19.xhtml_ref19_49){.biblioref role="doc-biblioref"}, p. 20) and proposes that for input to become *intake*, it has to be noticed or detected under awareness. *Noticing* is defined as a lower level of awareness, i.e. consciously registering specific instances of language, whereas *understanding* refers to a higher level of awareness and entails generalizations across these instances. Applied to pragmatics, [Schmidt (1995, p. 30)](#ch19.xhtml_ref19_49){.biblioref role="doc-biblioref"} delineates *noticing* and *understanding* as follows:

> In pragmatics, awareness that on a particular occasion someone says to their interlocutor something like *I'm terribly sorry to bother you, but if you have time could you look at this problem?* is a matter of noticing. Relating the various forms to their strategic development in the service of politeness and recognizing their co-occurrence with elements of context such as social distance, power, level of imposition and so on, are all matters of understanding.

Because the noticing hypothesis does not go beyond this initial step of awareness of the co-occurrence of form, communicative function, and context to explain the cognitive mechanisms involved in linking meaning with form during comprehension (see [VanPatten, 2020](#ch19.xhtml_ref19_69){.biblioref role="doc-biblioref"}; [Truscott & Sharwood Smith, 2011](#ch19.xhtml_ref19_65){#ch19.xhtml_R_ref19_65 .biblioref role="doc-biblioref"}), it is distinctly different from the notion of *input processing* in IP research. In other words, what remains unaccounted for by the noticing hypothesis is the key question in IP research, namely: What comes after noticing?
:::

::::::::: {.section aria-labelledby="sec19_3"}
## Critical Issues and Topics {#ch19.xhtml_sec19_3}

In L2 pragmatics, there is growing interest in addressing IP-related questions. To do so systematically, however, the field first needs to stimulate discussions about how basic constructs of IP theory can be defined with regard to IP for acquiring pragmatics. While it will take a collective effort within the field to further address the nature of these constructs, the following sections take some first steps toward defining input, IP, and intake for research on pragmatic IP.

::: {.section aria-labelledby="sec19_3_1"}
### What Counts as Input for Pragmatic IP? {#ch19.xhtml_sec19_3_1}

*Input* in current IP research refers exclusively to linguistic input (samples of the target language to which a learner is exposed), and studies on syntactic and lexical IP have predominantly focused on linguistic input at the word or sentence level as their primary interest is how learners []{#ch19.xhtml_p272 .pagebreak aria-label=" page 272. " role="doc-pagebreak"}process the *semantic* (literal, not context-dependent) *meaning* encoded in the linguistic forms in the input. But what counts as input when it comes to pragmatic IP? In particular, what do learners process as input when it comes to *pragmatic* (context-dependent, non-literal, implied) *meaning*, i.e., when meaning is not encoded in the linguistic forms, but rather conveyed by them, requiring the hearer to infer what the speaker actually intends to say? Because pragmatic comprehension involves both linguistic decoding as well as drawing inferences about the implied meaning of an utterance from context ([Sperber & Wilson, 1995](#ch19.xhtml_ref19_52){#ch19.xhtml_R_ref19_52 .biblioref role="doc-biblioref"}), *input* for pragmatic IP extends beyond linguistic input and beyond the sentence level. Research on pragmatic comprehension (e.g., [Zufferey, 2014](#ch19.xhtml_ref19_70){#ch19.xhtml_R_ref19_70 .biblioref role="doc-biblioref"}) indicates that L1 and L2 speakers alike use several linguistic and non-linguistic contextual cues as evidence when assigning meaning to ambiguous messages (e.g., nonliteral language). Context encompasses the immediately preceding discourse, the physical environment, paralinguistic cues (e.g., facial expressions, gestures, eye contact, gasps and sighs, intonation, etc.), and the hearer's world knowledge, but also the assumptions the hearer uses to infer the intended meaning of an utterance, including "expectations about the future, scientific hypotheses or religious beliefs, anecdotal memories, general cultural assumptions, and beliefs about the mental state of the speaker" ([Sperber & Wilson, 1995](#ch19.xhtml_ref19_52){.biblioref role="doc-biblioref"}, p. 15). Recent neurophysiological evidence from event-related potential (ERP) studies confirms the parallel processing and online integration of various types of contextual information during L2 pragmatic comprehension, including voice-based assumptions about the speaker ([Foucart, Garcia et al., 2015](#ch19.xhtml_ref19_25){#ch19.xhtml_R_ref19_25 .biblioref role="doc-biblioref"}), world knowledge ([Martin et al., 2016](#ch19.xhtml_ref19_42){#ch19.xhtml_R_ref19_42 .biblioref role="doc-biblioref"}), and one's own moral values ([Foucart, Moreno et al., 2015](#ch19.xhtml_ref19_26){#ch19.xhtml_R_ref19_26 .biblioref role="doc-biblioref"}).
:::

::: {.section aria-labelledby="sec19_3_2"}
### What Is Pragmatic Input Processing? {#ch19.xhtml_sec19_3_2}

Drawing on current IP theory (e.g., [VanPatten, 2020](#ch19.xhtml_ref19_69){.biblioref role="doc-biblioref"}), we can define *pragmatic input processing* as the process of deriving intake from input for the acquisition of pragmatics. More specifically, pragmatic IP is concerned with how L2 learners make *initial* connections between pragmalinguistic forms and the communicative functions and contexts of use associated with these forms during attempts to comprehend messages. As such, pragmatic IP is interested in the internal mechanisms acting on input and how learners allocate their limited attentional and processing resources when they process pragmatic language use as input.

Given this definition, what aspects of acquiring pragmatics can pragmatic IP account for? Pragmatic IP is concerned with linguistic encoding that results from the initial data collection as part of acquiring pragmatics. Thus, it may not explain the acquisition of all pragmatic phenomena, as pragmatic meaning is not always encoded in the linguistic forms in the input. Importantly, acquiring an L2 pragmatic system involves not only psycholinguistic processes but also depends on several universal cognitive abilities that begin to develop early on as we acquire pragmatic competence in L1. These abilities include inferencing via logical reasoning; using one's own assumptions to draw conclusions about what is meant; *theory of mind abilities* (i.e., the ability to reason about other people's mental states, e.g., their beliefs and desires); and the ability to integrate contextual information from various input sources ([Zufferey, 2014](#ch19.xhtml_ref19_70){.biblioref role="doc-biblioref"}; [Zufferey et al., 2019](#ch19.xhtml_ref19_71){#ch19.xhtml_R_ref19_71 .biblioref role="doc-biblioref"}).

How can we further specify the components of form--meaning mapping during pragmatic IP? According to [Schmidt (1993)](#ch19.xhtml_ref19_48){#ch19.xhtml_R_ref19_48 .biblioref role="doc-biblioref"}, learners must attend to the co-occurrence of form, communicative function, and context in the input and develop *form*--*communicative function--context connections* or *mappings*. However, little is known about the nature of form--meaning mapping and its processing components when it comes to IP for learning pragmatics. For instance, while 'communicative function' and 'context' can be clearly separated from a descriptive pragmatics perspective, from a psycholinguistic and neuropragmatic perspective the independence of the 'function' and 'context' processing components has not been firmly established empirically, and []{#ch19.xhtml_p273 .pagebreak aria-label=" page 273. " role="doc-pagebreak"}in fact, findings from ERP studies (e.g., [Van Berkum et al., 2008](#ch19.xhtml_ref19_67){#ch19.xhtml_R_ref19_67 .biblioref role="doc-biblioref"}) suggest that context processing cannot be separated from meaning processing at the level of the brain, as listeners rapidly relate the social context to the message. More research is needed to improve our understanding of whether communicative function and context represent independent processing components during pragmatic IP.

*Pragmalinguistic form* refers to the linguistic resources needed to achieve communicative functions in various contexts. This includes lexical form, i.e., words, lexical phrases and formulaic expressions (e.g., address forms), but also (morpho)syntactic form (e.g., modal verbs used to make polite requests) and phonological form (e.g., intonation used to convey irony). Acquiring pragmatics may involve "expanding formal linguistic resources by learning new forms of expression" ([Bialystok, 1993](#ch19.xhtml_ref19_15){#ch19.xhtml_R_ref19_15 .biblioref role="doc-biblioref"}, p. 53), and in this case, pragmatic IP involves linking novel pragmalinguistic forms with function and/or context of use. But pragmatic IP may also entail, for instance, mapping a new meaning and pragmatic use onto an existing mental representation of a lexical item.

Given the complexity of pragmatics and its interface with syntax and lexis, the study of pragmatic IP may be approached from the perspective of *multilevel input processing*, which refers to how learners allocate processing resources among different levels of IP and process input both within and across these different levels ([Barcroft, 2021](#ch19.xhtml_ref19_9){#ch19.xhtml_R_ref19_9 .biblioref role="doc-biblioref"}, p. 202). While all IP research applies information-processing theory ([Miller, 1956](#ch19.xhtml_ref19_44){#ch19.xhtml_R_ref19_44 .biblioref role="doc-biblioref"}) to language acquisition to investigate how L2 learners process linguistic input, [Barcroft (2021)](#ch19.xhtml_ref19_9){.biblioref role="doc-biblioref"} proposes further specifying the general stages of IP during language acquisition to distinguish between different levels of IP (morpho-syntactic IP, lexical IP, phonological IP, etc.). With regard to IP for acquiring pragmatics, these stages can be represented as follows:

> prag-INPUT \> prag-INPUT PROCESSING \> prag-INTAKE \> DEVELOPING
>
> PRAGMATIC SYSTEM \> prag-OUTPUT PROCESSING \> prag-OUTPUT

As this model illustrates, pragmatic IP concerns only the first set of processes involved in acquiring an L2 pragmatic system.
:::

::: {.section aria-labelledby="sec19_3_3"}
### What Is the Nature of Pragmatic Intake and the Developing L2 Pragmatic System? {#ch19.xhtml_sec19_3_3}

*Intake* and *developing system* have been defined differently with regard to different types of IP. In morpho-syntactic IP research, *intake* refers to the subset of information in the input that concerns the nature of the *developing system*, i.e., the mental representations that develop based on examples of syntactic procedures in the input. In lex-IP research, *developing lexical system* concerns "the developing lexicosemantic system in the target language in which degree of knowledge of individual word forms and the semantic networks associated with those word forms gradually develops and is refined over time," whereas *lexical intake* refers to "the multiple bits and pieces of word form and the multiple bits and pieces of semantic information associated with word forms that are attended to in the input and made available to the lexicosemantic system" ([Barcroft, 2015](#ch19.xhtml_ref19_8){.biblioref role="doc-biblioref"}, p. 23).

In turn, *pragmatic intake* may be described as the bits and pieces of pragmalinguistic form and bits and pieces of pragmatic information, such as communicative function and context of use associated with these forms, that learners attend to in the input and from which initial form--meaning connections have been made. Moreover, pragmatic intake may be partially or completely incorporated into the developing L2 pragmatic system, which leads to "acquiring new []{#ch19.xhtml_p274 .pagebreak aria-label=" page 274. " role="doc-pagebreak"}representations or to the restructuring of existing ones" ([Kasper & Schmidt, 1996](#ch19.xhtml_ref19_33){#ch19.xhtml_R_ref19_33 .biblioref role="doc-biblioref"}, p. 157). Drawing on Andersen ([1984](#ch19.xhtml_ref19_4){#ch19.xhtml_R_ref19_4 .biblioref role="doc-biblioref"}, [1990](#ch19.xhtml_ref19_5){#ch19.xhtml_R_ref19_5 .biblioref role="doc-biblioref"}), [Bardovi-Harlig (2013, p. 78)](#ch19.xhtml_ref19_12){.biblioref role="doc-biblioref"} suggests that in the early stages of L2 acquisition, learners may start with "one form for one function" before expanding to "multiple forms for one function (or multiple functions for one form)"---thus gradually developing multifunctionality over time:

> A learner who has one functional reply to *thank you*, such as *you're welcome*, may not be in the market for another, such as *no problem*. Adding *no problem* to the repertoire does not just involve the addition of another expression, but rather remapping. *You're welcome* may start as the only reply to *thank you*. If *no problem* is added, it may be mapped to the function of deflecting the thanks, while *you're welcome* is remapped to accepting thanks.

While *developing L2 pragmatic system* as such has not been explicitly defined, two cognitive approaches in L2 pragmatics address the nature of pragmatic knowledge. [Faerch and Kasper (1984)](#ch19.xhtml_ref19_21){.biblioref role="doc-biblioref"} describe *pragmatic knowledge* as rules and procedures and thus as *declarative* (non-automatized, conscious) and *procedural* (automatized, unconscious) in nature. *Declarative pragmatic knowledge* encompasses linguistic knowledge "about the rules and items of one or more languages at the phonological, morpho-syntactic and lexical levels" (p. 215), knowledge about community-specific speech acts, their sequential and interactional features and contexts of use as well as knowledge about community-specific norms, values, behavioral patterns, and world knowledge. *Procedural pragmatic knowledge* is used for goal-formation and context-analysis, verbal planning, and monitoring execution during production. [Bialystok's (1993)](#ch19.xhtml_ref19_15){.biblioref role="doc-biblioref"} two-dimensional model, on the other hand, explains the development of already available pragmatic knowledge in terms of analysis of existing representations and control of processing. However, neither proposal has been tested experimentally nor explains the peculiarities of the developing pragmatic system from an IP perspective.

Yet, different types of IP have recently been shown to yield different types of knowledge and memory. Based on [Ullman's (2004)](#ch19.xhtml_ref19_66){#ch19.xhtml_R_ref19_66 .biblioref role="doc-biblioref"} declarative/procedural model and the neuroimaging evidence supporting it, syntactic knowledge resides in procedural (implicit) memory in the frontal, basal ganglia, parietal, and cerebellum, whereas lexical knowledge resides in the neural networks that underlie declarative (explicit) memory in the temporal lobe (see also Grey, this volume). Given that different levels of IP (i.e., lexical and morpho-syntactic IP) are subserved by processing and storage in different brain areas and yield qualitatively different and independent types of knowledge, what types of memory and learning might pragmatic IP correspond to? (See also Sharwood Smith & Truscott, this volume.)
:::

::: {.section aria-labelledby="sec19_3_4"}
### Why Do Learners Acquire Some Types of Indirect Meaning Before Others? {#ch19.xhtml_sec19_3_4}

Since IP research is also interested in identifying non-optimal processing strategies learners naturally employ during IP, research on pragmatic IP can be informed by empirical findings and theoretical accounts that address learners' pragmatic comprehension difficulties. Research indicates that learners' pragmatic acquisition is not constant over different types of indirect meaning ([Taguchi, 2008](#ch19.xhtml_ref19_55){#ch19.xhtml_R_ref19_55 .biblioref role="doc-biblioref"}). Explanations of this phenomenon draw on relevance theory ([Sperber & Wilson, 1995](#ch19.xhtml_ref19_52){.biblioref role="doc-biblioref"}), which posits that during utterance comprehension, human perceptual mechanisms pick out the most relevant stimuli from the input, i.e., those with the greatest contextual effect (the most relevant interpretation) and the smallest processing effort. Several factors influence learners' processing effort during comprehension of indirect meaning, including L2 proficiency, the syntactic complexity and conventionality of the utterance, and the complexity of []{#ch19.xhtml_p275 .pagebreak aria-label=" page 275. " role="doc-pagebreak"}inferencing required (e.g., amount and accessibility of contextual cues). When an utterance is linguistically complex and requires online integration of a large amount of contextual information, the processing load increases, whereas when a form or discourse pattern is frequently used to convey an indirect speech act, the inference does not have to be calculated online as it is "compressed by precedent" ([Zufferey, 2014](#ch19.xhtml_ref19_70){.biblioref role="doc-biblioref"}, p. 53), resulting in a lower processing load. When conventionality is not shared, L2 learners need to rely on bottom-up processing of the linguistic code and top-down-processing of the contextual cues to derive indirect meaning, leading to a higher processing load. The facilitation effect of conventionality results from positive L1 pragmatic transfer and is limited to pragmatic universals such as indirect refusals ([Taguchi & Yamaguchi, 2019](#ch19.xhtml_ref19_57){#ch19.xhtml_R_ref19_57 .biblioref role="doc-biblioref"}). L2 learners have inferencing difficulties when it comes to differences in culture-specific conventions, e.g., pope questions and irony ([Bouton, 1988](#ch19.xhtml_ref19_16){#ch19.xhtml_R_ref19_16 .biblioref role="doc-biblioref"}).

How might research on pragmatic IP contribute to our understanding of how learners process different types of nonconventional indirect meaning? While the comprehension of nonconventional indirect meaning may not lead to robust form--meaning connections, learners do acquire the expression of irony, for instance, in the target language eventually, even if slowly ([Bouton, 1994](#ch19.xhtml_ref19_17){#ch19.xhtml_R_ref19_17 .biblioref role="doc-biblioref"}). What might pragmatic intake look like when meaning is not or only partially encoded in the linguistic forms? For instance, are suprasegmental features of a given nonconventional pragmalinguistic form (e.g., intonation associated with irony in given language) converted into intake?
:::

::: {.section aria-labelledby="sec19_3_5"}
### Can Processing Instruction Be Used to Teach Pragmatics? {#ch19.xhtml_sec19_3_5}

Several studies on effects of input-based practice on L2 pragmatic development have aimed to apply processing instruction (PI) to teaching pragmatics ([Takimoto, 2009](#ch19.xhtml_ref19_63){#ch19.xhtml_R_ref19_63 .biblioref role="doc-biblioref"}; [S. Li, 2012](#ch19.xhtml_ref19_39){#ch19.xhtml_R_ref19_39 .biblioref role="doc-biblioref"}). However, the activities utilized do not represent structured input (SI) tasks for PI. PI is a pedagogical intervention whose principles are grounded in VanPatten's ([1996](#ch19.xhtml_ref19_68){.biblioref role="doc-biblioref"}, [2020](#ch19.xhtml_ref19_69){.biblioref role="doc-biblioref"}) IP theory and empirical findings regarding the processing strategies learners naturally apply during morphosyntactic (semantic) IP at the sentence level (e.g., the *first-noun principle* and the *lexical preference principle*). PI first identifies the processing strategy that hinders learners from processing a certain morphosyntactic feature in the input, and the goal of SI is to force learners to abandon this strategy so that they process the feature. However, [Takimoto (2009)](#ch19.xhtml_ref19_63){.biblioref role="doc-biblioref"} and [S. Li (2012)](#ch19.xhtml_ref19_39){.biblioref role="doc-biblioref"} do not address what non-optimal pragmatic processing strategy the tasks in their studies would address during pragmatic comprehension and why PI would be effective for teaching pragmatics using discourse-level input (see also discussion in Wong, this volume). A more promising direction for future research would be to identify principles for processing-oriented pragmatic instruction based on pragmatic IP.
:::

::: {.section aria-labelledby="sec19_3_6"}
### To What Extent Can the Levels-of-Processing Framework Explain Pragmatic Learning? {#ch19.xhtml_sec19_3_6}

Additionally, some researchers ([Takimoto, 2007](#ch19.xhtml_ref19_62){#ch19.xhtml_R_ref19_62 .biblioref role="doc-biblioref"}; [Li & Taguchi, 2014](#ch19.xhtml_ref19_40){#ch19.xhtml_R_ref19_40 .biblioref role="doc-biblioref"}) explain the effectiveness of certain input-based tasks in light of the levels-of-processing (LOP) framework ([Craik & Lockhart, 1972](#ch19.xhtml_ref19_18){#ch19.xhtml_R_ref19_18 .biblioref role="doc-biblioref"}) for research on human memory. [Takimoto (2007)](#ch19.xhtml_ref19_62){.biblioref role="doc-biblioref"}, for instance, proposes that the problem-solving activities in the study required learners to "discuss the metapragmatic features of target structures, thereby reinforcing pragmalinguistic--sociopragmatic connections and allowing for processing at a deeper level" (p. 21). However, using the LOP framework post-hoc to explain effects of inductive and deductive approaches to L2 pragmatics instruction is problematic given that Craik and Lockhart's LOP claim refers to effects of semantically focused []{#ch19.xhtml_p276 .pagebreak aria-label=" page 276. " role="doc-pagebreak"}tasks on the processing of semantic meaning at the word level rather than on pragmatic meaning at the discourse level. It should also be noted that empirical support for the claim that semantically focused tasks lead to 'deeper processing' and better memory traces fell short early on (e.g., [Pressley et al., 1982](#ch19.xhtml_ref19_46){#ch19.xhtml_R_ref19_46 .biblioref role="doc-biblioref"}). Moreover, [Barcroft (2002)](#ch19.xhtml_ref19_7){#ch19.xhtml_R_ref19_7 .biblioref role="doc-biblioref"} and [Kida and Barcroft (2018)](#ch19.xhtml_ref19_35){#ch19.xhtml_R_ref19_35 .biblioref role="doc-biblioref"} provided evidence that the formal, semantic, and mapping components of word learning are largely dissociable, demonstrating that semantically oriented tasks increase processing and learning of the meaning aspect of word knowledge but at the same time decrease the amount of processing resources available for learning the other aspects (see Kida, this volume).
:::
:::::::::

::::::: {.section aria-labelledby="sec19_4"}
## Current Contributions and Research {#ch19.xhtml_sec19_4}

While no studies to date have been conducted under an IP framework to investigate experimentally how learners link form with meaning during real-time pragmatic comprehension, this section reviews findings that contribute in some way to our current understanding of pragmatic IP.

::::: {.section aria-labelledby="sec19_4_1"}
### Processing-Oriented Studies on L2 Pragmatic Comprehension {#ch19.xhtml_sec19_4_1}

::: {.section aria-labelledby="sec19_4_1_1"}
#### Indirect Meaning {#ch19.xhtml_sec19_4_1_1}

The topic that L2 pragmatics research has examined most from a processing perspective---employing methods and theories from psycholinguistics rather than descriptive pragmatics---is the comprehension of *indirect meaning*. Meaning is conveyed indirectly when the literal interpretation of an utterance does not fully specify the speaker's intended meaning, such as in the case of irony, metaphors, and indirect requests. For instance, Paula may utter *It's hot in here* to indirectly ask John to open the window. Because the connection between her utterance and her informative intention is not encoded in the sentence, John is expected to recognize Paula's intention within the situational context (i.e., they are in a room, and the window is out of Paula's reach) to understand the meaning she aims to convey. According to [Grice (1975)](#ch19.xhtml_ref19_28){#ch19.xhtml_R_ref19_28 .biblioref role="doc-biblioref"}, all types of non-literal or indirect language use trigger c*onversational implicatures*, i.e., inferences that the hearer draws about the intended meaning of the utterance based on linguistic knowledge, contextual clues, and the assumption of relevance. *Conventional* or *generalized implicatures* can be understood independent of context: hearers draw inferences from an utterance based on the conventional meaning encoded in linguistic forms, e.g., formulaic expressions or common discourse patterns associated with some indirect speech acts (e.g., *Would you mind...?* for indirect requests). By contrast, *nonconventional* or *particularized implicatures* can only be understood by considering contextual information in the input (e.g., the preceding discourse).

Research suggests that during conventional implicature comprehension, the conventionality encoded in utterances reduces L2 learners' processing demands. For instance, [Takahashi and Roitblat (1994)](#ch19.xhtml_ref19_61){#ch19.xhtml_R_ref19_61 .biblioref role="doc-biblioref"} examined how advanced L2 English learners process conventional indirect requests, which are typically performed by questioning the hearer's ability (e.g., *Could you give me a ride?*) or willingness (e.g., *Would you give me a ride?*) to carry out the requested act. Participants read stories that induced either a literal interpretation or a conventional request interpretation of a priming sentence (e.g., *Can you open the door?*). Results indicated shorter response times for processing conventional non-literal meaning, suggesting that the conventional interpretation bypasses the literal interpretation.

Subsequent research has corroborated the finding that learners encounter greater processing difficulties with nonconventional over conventional forms, suggesting that different types of indirect meaning involve different comprehension processes. For instance, using a multiple-choice []{#ch19.xhtml_p277 .pagebreak aria-label=" page 277. " role="doc-pagebreak"}listening test and introspective verbal reports, Taguchi ([2005](#ch19.xhtml_ref19_54){#ch19.xhtml_R_ref19_54 .biblioref role="doc-biblioref"}, [2008](#ch19.xhtml_ref19_55){.biblioref role="doc-biblioref"}) examined how Japanese L2 learners of English comprehend conventional implicatures (indirect requests and indirect refusals that follow common discourse patterns) and nonconventional implicatures (indirect opinions that involve idiosyncratic forms). Results demonstrated that learners processed conventionalized indirect refusals faster and more accurately than indirect opinions. Similarly, [Taguchi et al. (2013)](#ch19.xhtml_ref19_58){#ch19.xhtml_R_ref19_58 .biblioref role="doc-biblioref"} investigated the effects of L2 proficiency and learning context on the comprehension of indirect meaning in L2 Chinese. Results partially replicated previous findings ([Taguchi, 2008](#ch19.xhtml_ref19_55){.biblioref role="doc-biblioref"}) regarding the effect of implicature type on accuracy, suggesting that both conventionality and L2 proficiency facilitate implicature comprehension. Additionally, [Taguchi et al. (2016)](#ch19.xhtml_ref19_59){#ch19.xhtml_R_ref19_59 .biblioref role="doc-biblioref"} used a multimedia listening test to examine whether L2 Spanish learners' comprehension varied across three types of implicatures: indirect refusals, indirect opinions, and irony. Results showed a significant effect of item type on accuracy and response time, and nonconventional indirect meaning (irony) was the most difficult to comprehend.

Moreover, research ([Lee, 2010](#ch19.xhtml_ref19_37){#ch19.xhtml_R_ref19_37 .biblioref role="doc-biblioref"}; [Taguchi, 2002](#ch19.xhtml_ref19_53){#ch19.xhtml_R_ref19_53 .biblioref role="doc-biblioref"}) suggests that L2 learners use multiple linguistic and non-linguistic contextual cues in the auditory and visual input to infer implied meaning. Using introspective verbal reports, [Taguchi (2002)](#ch19.xhtml_ref19_53){.biblioref role="doc-biblioref"} found that Japanese L2 English learners use six cognitive inferencing strategies: identifying paralinguistic cues to draw conclusions about the speaker's emotional state and attitude toward the message; use of knowledge about turn-taking and background knowledge; key word inferencing; logical reasoning; and recognizing the speaker's intention.
:::

::: {.section aria-labelledby="sec19_4_1_2"}
#### Formulaic Expressions and Speech Act Recognition {#ch19.xhtml_sec19_4_1_2}

A large body of research has demonstrated positive effects of frequency on L2 learners' acquisition of conventional (or formulaic) expressions (see [Siyanova-Chanturia & Van Lancker Sidtis, 2019](#ch19.xhtml_ref19_51){#ch19.xhtml_R_ref19_51 .biblioref role="doc-biblioref"}, for a review). While formulaic expressions are commonly associated with acquiring pragmatics because of their prominent use in some speech acts, SLA research documenting the conventionality of such expressions suggests that "pragmatics is not as formulaic as some believe" ([Bardovi-Harlig, 2019](#ch19.xhtml_ref19_13){#ch19.xhtml_R_ref19_13 .biblioref role="doc-biblioref"}, p. 50) due to variation among native speakers (NSs) (e.g., [Bardovi-Harlig, 2009](#ch19.xhtml_ref19_11){#ch19.xhtml_R_ref19_11 .biblioref role="doc-biblioref"}; [Fichtner, 2020](#ch19.xhtml_ref19_23){#ch19.xhtml_R_ref19_23 .biblioref role="doc-biblioref"}). What is more, research suggests that to acquire the sociopragmatic use of conventional expressions, learners need to recognize the speech act and situational context in which the conventional expression is used.

However, little is known about L2 learners' speech act recognition during online comprehension. [Holtgraves (2007)](#ch19.xhtml_ref19_29){#ch19.xhtml_R_ref19_29 .biblioref role="doc-biblioref"} examined whether NSs and L2 English learners automatically recognize implicit speech acts (speech acts realized without a performative verb, e.g., *to remind*). Participants performed a lexical decision task immediately after reading conversational utterances that were either implicit speech acts or control utterances. Results indicated that online (automatic) speech act activation occurred in L1 but not in L2. However, a significant positive correlation between degree of speech act activation and years spent using English suggested that recognition develops over time.
:::
:::::

::: {.section aria-labelledby="sec19_4_2"}
### Effects of Input Enhancement on Learning Pragmatics {#ch19.xhtml_sec19_4_2}

Pedagogical intervention studies that operationalized implicit instruction as simple exposure to input (e.g., [Koike & Pearson, 2005](#ch19.xhtml_ref19_36){#ch19.xhtml_R_ref19_36 .biblioref role="doc-biblioref"}; [Fordyce, 2014](#ch19.xhtml_ref19_24){#ch19.xhtml_R_ref19_24 .biblioref role="doc-biblioref"}; [Takahashi, 2001](#ch19.xhtml_ref19_60){#ch19.xhtml_R_ref19_60 .biblioref role="doc-biblioref"}) suggest that L2 learners are unlikely to attend to the target pragmatic features in the input unless pedagogical interventions, such as input enhancement, are used. *Input enhancement* (IE) refers to any manipulations of the input that cause target forms to stand out so that learners attend to them ([]{#ch19.xhtml_p278 .pagebreak aria-label=" page 278. " role="doc-pagebreak"}[Sharwood Smith, 1993](#ch19.xhtml_ref19_50){#ch19.xhtml_R_ref19_50 .biblioref role="doc-biblioref"}). Whereas written input involves *textual enhancement* (manipulations of typographical cues, e.g., bolding, underlining), spoken IE may involve varying indexical features of the speech signal (e.g., speaker, amplitude). Audiovisual input can be enhanced by adding captions in the target language. Several pedagogical intervention studies have investigated effects of IE on L2 pragmatic learning. While findings are inconclusive, these studies suggest that IE can positively affect pragmatic learning, at least under certain circumstances.

In a study on L2 English learners' acquisition of suggestions ([Martínez-Flor, 2006](#ch19.xhtml_ref19_43){#ch19.xhtml_R_ref19_43 .biblioref role="doc-biblioref"}), both explicit instruction (metapragmatic information on suggestions) and implicit treatments (textual enhancement and recasts) yielded significant improvements in learners' comprehension and production of accurate and pragmatically appropriate suggestions. Yet, in several other studies (e.g., [Alcón-Soler, 2007](#ch19.xhtml_ref19_3){#ch19.xhtml_R_ref19_3 .biblioref role="doc-biblioref"}; [Fukuya & Martínez-Flor, 2008](#ch19.xhtml_ref19_27){#ch19.xhtml_R_ref19_27 .biblioref role="doc-biblioref"}; Q. [Li, 2012](#ch19.xhtml_ref19_38){#ch19.xhtml_R_ref19_38 .biblioref role="doc-biblioref"}; [Nguyen et al., 2012](#ch19.xhtml_ref19_45){#ch19.xhtml_R_ref19_45 .biblioref role="doc-biblioref"}; [Rafieyan, 2016](#ch19.xhtml_ref19_47){#ch19.xhtml_R_ref19_47 .biblioref role="doc-biblioref"}), implicit treatments involving IE fell short of (non-enhanced) explicit treatments in terms of learning gains. For instance, in [Alcón-Soler's (2007)](#ch19.xhtml_ref19_3){.biblioref role="doc-biblioref"} study of L2 English learners' awareness of requests, both instructional groups viewed clips and completed awareness-raising tasks based on transcripts of the clips, but the implicit group received transcripts with textually enhanced request forms and social factors, whereas the explicit group received metapragmatic information. While both treatments increased learners' recognition of requests, the explicit group outperformed the implicit group in production.

Additionally, some research ([Félix-Brasdefer, 2008](#ch19.xhtml_ref19_22){#ch19.xhtml_R_ref19_22 .biblioref role="doc-biblioref"}; [Fordyce, 2014](#ch19.xhtml_ref19_24){.biblioref role="doc-biblioref"}) suggests that learners benefit from combining metapragmatic explanations and IE. [Félix-Brasdefer's (2008)](#ch19.xhtml_ref19_22){.biblioref role="doc-biblioref"} study on L2 Spanish learners' acquisition of refusals, participants observed NS refusals in different scenarios and watched a presentation in which different structural components of these refusals were textually enhanced and their sociopragmatic use was explained. The treatment group outperformed the control at posttest and retained most target forms on the delayed posttest.
:::
:::::::

::::::::::: {.section aria-labelledby="sec19_5"}
## Future Directions {#ch19.xhtml_sec19_5}

While these studies approach important questions related to pragmatic IP, these questions can only be answered systematically once the field finds a way to further define and explore key constructs of IP theory with regard to pragmatic IP. Besides these unresolved issues, why do the findings of the L2 pragmatics studies reviewed above not count as evidence for pragmatic IP? From an IP perspective, a limitation of the existing processing-oriented research on L2 learners' comprehension of implicatures is that it cannot speak to how learners come to acquire (or not acquire) these types of indirect meaning during real-time comprehension. Likewise, a limitation of existing IE studies in L2 pragmatics is that they do not employ psycholinguistic, experimental study designs to examine how learners interact with input and make form--meaning connections during comprehension, and observed effects of specific input modulations cannot be clearly isolated as conditions combine multiple input- and/or output-based tasks. Likewise, a limitation of existing IE studies in L2 pragmatics is that they do not employ psycholinguistic experimental designs to examine how learners interact with input and make form--meaning connections during comprehension. Additionally, the effects of input manipulations in these studies cannot be clearly discerned from one another because the conditions compared included multiple input-based tasks, output-based tasks, or a combination of both. Therefore, the essential task of future research on pragmatic IP is to combine the focus on the effects of certain learning phase treatments with the use of psycholinguistic, highly controlled study designs to examine how learners link meaning with form during real-time comprehension.

::::: {.section aria-labelledby="sec19_5_1"}
### []{#ch19.xhtml_p279 .pagebreak aria-label=" page 279. " role="doc-pagebreak"}What are Features of Research on Pragmatic IP? {#ch19.xhtml_sec19_5_1}

::: {.section aria-labelledby="sec19_5_1_1"}
#### Theoretical Framework {#ch19.xhtml_sec19_5_1_1}

Studies on pragmatic IP need to be theoretically situated within an IP framework (e.g., [VanPatten, 2020](#ch19.xhtml_ref19_69){.biblioref role="doc-biblioref"}; [Barcroft, 2015](#ch19.xhtml_ref19_8){.biblioref role="doc-biblioref"}) and thus grounded in information-processing theory ([Miller, 1956](#ch19.xhtml_ref19_44){.biblioref role="doc-biblioref"}). Moreover, the complexity of pragmatic processing underlines [Barcroft's (2021)](#ch19.xhtml_ref19_9){.biblioref role="doc-biblioref"} call for a multilevel IP perspective to investigate how learners allocate their limited processing resources across multiple different levels of linguistic analysis (lexical, morphosyntactic, pragmatic, etc.) during IP. Future research could utilize this framework to investigate, for instance, how IP for learning grammar and lexis interfaces with IP for learning pragmatics, not to determine whether syntactic or pragmatic knowledge develops first but to understand "how the two systems interact, and how one supports the other" ([Bardovi-Harlig, 2013](#ch19.xhtml_ref19_12){.biblioref role="doc-biblioref"}, p. 78--79).
:::

::: {.section aria-labelledby="sec19_5_1_2"}
#### Study Design {#ch19.xhtml_sec19_5_1_2}

Studies on pragmatic IP should be designed to examine how learners interact with input in real time and under what conditions they make initial form--meaning connections during comprehension. According to [VanPatten (2020, p. 117)](#ch19.xhtml_ref19_69){.biblioref role="doc-biblioref"}, methods for studying *noticing* are not appropriate for IP research because they measure knowledge/metalinguistic awareness (e.g., acceptability judgment tasks, introspective research such as think-alouds during reading comprehension, mark-up tasks). Typical designs in IP research include sentence interpretation tasks, self-paced reading, trials-to-criterion, and eye-tracking, whereas studies in lex-IP typically employ free or cued recall tasks with controlled learning-phase treatments and retrieval times.

To investigate how learners interact with input during pragmatic IP, studies can employ online measures, such as eye-tracking (see Jegerski, this volume). Eye-tracking is a particularly promising technique for pragmatic IP research as it provides "fine-grained information about moment-by-moment sentence comprehension" ([Keating & Jegerski, 2015](#ch19.xhtml_ref19_34){#ch19.xhtml_R_ref19_34 .biblioref role="doc-biblioref"}, p. 2) by tracking the location and duration of learners' eye gazes as they hear or read utterances and interact with visual stimuli. Pragmatic IP studies can also use pupillometry to measure changes in pupil dilation, which indicate processing effort.

Finally, studies comparing effects of different learning-phase treatments should ensure that these treatments differ in only one aspect so that observed effects can be clearly isolated. Additionally, these studies should assess learning receptively (e.g., through sentence interpretations tasks), although production tasks (e.g., cued recall) can be added to further assess learning of pragmalinguistic forms.
:::
:::::

::::::: {.section aria-labelledby="sec19_5_2"}
### What Questions Can Research on Pragmatic IP Investigate? {#ch19.xhtml_sec19_5_2}

::: {.section aria-labelledby="sec19_5_2_1"}
#### How and Why Does Input Enhancement Impact Learning During Pragmatic IP? {#ch19.xhtml_sec19_5_2_1}

While the studies reviewed above suggest that IE can effectively draw learners' attention to pragmalinguistic forms in the input, their offline measures do not reveal how learners interact with enhanced input during online comprehension. What might IE affect during pragmatic IP to improve learning? Do the attention-drawing properties of IE increase the time a learner spends focusing on a target pragmalinguistic form, thus allowing more time for pragmatic IP, or do certain types of IE affect the quality of pragmatic IP and the mental representations it yields? For instance, studies on lex-IP (e.g., [Barcroft & Sommers, 2005](#ch19.xhtml_ref19_10){#ch19.xhtml_R_ref19_10 .biblioref role="doc-biblioref"}) indicate that presenting lexical []{#ch19.xhtml_p280 .pagebreak aria-label=" page 280. " role="doc-pagebreak"}items in more acoustically varied conditions (e.g., six speakers versus one) yield more robust or distributed mental representations of these items.

Recently, [Barón and Luz Celaya (2022)](#ch19.xhtml_ref19_14){#ch19.xhtml_R_ref19_14 .biblioref role="doc-biblioref"} found that exposing L2 learners to captioned audiovisual input in incidental learning conditions yielded better learning of pragmatic expressions and their use in certain speech acts than did non-captioned audiovisual input. However, the productive assessment tasks in the study (role-plays) do not speak to how and why this type of IE positively impacted pragmatic learning during comprehension. A pragmatic IP study could use eye-tracking to investigate how learners interact with captioned and non-captioned audiovisual material during real-time comprehension of the target expressions. In a pre/posttest experimental design, the study could assess learning in the two different conditions using visual world eye-tracking with a passive listening task (see [Dussias et al., 2014](#ch19.xhtml_ref19_19){#ch19.xhtml_R_ref19_19 .biblioref role="doc-biblioref"}), such as a dichotomous scene selection task to measure eye movements and accuracy in picture selection as participants process the target expressions via auditory (spoken) input. Moreover, the study could repeat the same experiment while adding explicit metapragmatic information to examine its impact on the effectiveness of IE.
:::

::: {.section aria-labelledby="sec19_5_2_2"}
#### How do Learners' Limited Processing Resources Impact Pragmatic IP? {#ch19.xhtml_sec19_5_2_2}

Pragmatic IP research also needs to investigate learners' *processing resource allocation*, i.e. "how learners distribute their limited cognitive processing capacities toward encoding and retaining different types of data provided in the input" ([Barcroft, 2015](#ch19.xhtml_ref19_8){.biblioref role="doc-biblioref"}, p. 58). A central finding in lex-IP research concerns the importance of specifying the *type of processing* when it comes to predicting learning outcomes of tasks. Specifically, [Barcroft's (2015)](#ch19.xhtml_ref19_8){.biblioref role="doc-biblioref"} *type of processing -- resource allocation* (TOPRA) model posits that tasks invoking one type of processing (i.e., word form processing, word meaning processing, or processing for form--meaning mapping) "necessarily exhausts processing resources that now become unavailable for other types of processing" (p. 64). Assuming that the communicative functions and contexts of use associated with certain pragmalinguistic lexical forms (e.g., conventional expressions used in certain speech acts) are part of the semantic representation of these lexical items, studies on pragmatic IP could test whether predictions of the TOPRA model apply when it comes to encoding pragmalinguistic form. For instance, do tasks that invoke processing communicative function (i.e., meaning) decrease cognitive resources available for processing form-related properties of a target pragmalinguistic expression?
:::

::: {.section aria-labelledby="sec19_5_2_3"}
#### Effects of Frequency of Exposure or L2 Proficiency? {#ch19.xhtml_sec19_5_2_3}

Several factors affect pragmatic competence development, including L2 proficiency and length of residency ([Taguchi, 2008](#ch19.xhtml_ref19_55){.biblioref role="doc-biblioref"}). Pragmatic IP research can help to decouple effects of L2 proficiency from effects of frequency of exposure, such as when it comes to learning certain types of indirect speech acts. For example, the finding that more advanced learners have less difficulty with conventionality in indirectness comprehension has been interpreted as an effect of L2 proficiency ([Taguchi et al., 2013](#ch19.xhtml_ref19_58){.biblioref role="doc-biblioref"}). An IP-focused study could examine whether this result is indeed an effect of the learners' L2 proficiency or rather an effect of the frequency of exposure.
:::

::: {.section aria-labelledby="sec19_5_2_4"}
#### What Types of Memory Does Pragmatic IP Yield? {#ch19.xhtml_sec19_5_2_4}

Future studies should also investigate the nature of pragmatic knowledge to shed more light on what types of memory and learning pragmatic IP might yield. Neuroimaging research on L1 speech act processing has provided evidence revealing the independence of pragmatic processing and semantic processing at the level of the brain. For example, using functional magnetic []{#ch19.xhtml_p281 .pagebreak aria-label=" page 281. " role="doc-pagebreak"}resonance imaging (fMRI), [Egorova et al. (2016)](#ch19.xhtml_ref19_20){#ch19.xhtml_R_ref19_20 .biblioref role="doc-biblioref"} found that processing the same utterance as different speech acts (naming and requesting) involves the activation of different areas of the brain, suggesting different neural substrates for different types of speech acts. Whereas requests activated bilateral premotor, left inferior frontal and temporo-parietal cortical areas associated with action-related and social interactive knowledge, naming activated the left angular gyrus, an area relevant for linking information about word forms and reference objects. Similar research could be conducted for L2 pragmatic processing to advance our understanding of what types of memory correspond to pragmatic IP.
:::
:::::::
:::::::::::

::: {.section .conclusion role="doc-conclusion"}
## Conclusion {#ch19.xhtml_sec19_6}

This chapter attests to the growing interest in investigating IP-related issues in L2 pragmatics and outlines possible theoretical and methodological avenues for new IP research focused on acquiring pragmatics. Once the field stimulates more crosstalk between research on IP and L2 pragmatics to further address the constructs of pragmatic IP, it will be exciting to see what studies in this new area can contribute to our understanding of L2 pragmatic acquisition.
:::

::: {.section .reference aria-labelledby="r19_1"}

- [Jegerski, J., & VanPatten, B. (2014). `<cite>`{=html}Research methods in second language psycholinguistics`</cite>`{=html}. Routledge.]{#ch19.xhtml_ref19_1}

:::
