::: {.section .part role="doc-part"}
# []{#pt6.xhtml_p285 .pagebreak aria-label=" page 285. " role="doc-pagebreak"}[Part VI ]{.chnum .ordinal}[Input Processing and Second Language Instruction[]{#pt6.xhtml_p286 .pagebreak aria-label=" page 286 is blank. " role="doc-pagebreak"}]{.chtitle} {#pt6.xhtml_h1}
:::

[]{#ch20.xhtml}

::: {}
# []{#ch20.xhtml_p287 .pagebreak aria-label=" page 287. " role="doc-pagebreak"}[20 ]{.chnum .ordinal}[Thirty Years of Processing Instruction and Structured Input]{.chtitle} {#ch20.xhtml_h1}

Wynne Wong

The Ohio State University

DOI: [10.4324/9781003041412-26](https://dx.doi.org/10.4324/9781003041412-26){aria-label="D.O.I. link to this document."}
:::

::::::: {.section .introduction role="doc-introduction"}
## Introduction and Definitions {#ch20.xhtml_sec20_1}

Processing instruction (PI) is a pedagogical intervention that is tied to VanPatten's theory of input processing (IP) ([VanPatten, 1996](#ch20.xhtml_ref20_50){#ch20.xhtml_R_ref20_50 .biblioref role="doc-biblioref"}) and is one of the most researched types of form-focused instruction. This chapter reviews nearly 30 years of PI research while highlighting issues for future empirical inquiry and pedagogical practice. After defining PI and structured input (SI), i.e., the activities that make up PI, the chapter offers a historical perspective of PI by discussing the foundational study that launched this research agenda before tracing the contributions of nearly three decades of research. Issues discussed include why PI is effective, whether SI is synonymous with task-essentialness, and whether SI can be created for other linguistic subsystems. After discussing recommendations for practice (e.g., integrating PI into task-based instruction and differentiated learning), the chapter concludes with directions for future research which include investigating the acquisition of other linguistic subsystems from an IP perspective, the need to examine a greater variety of populations and languages, the need to measure strategy change in real-time, and a proposal to investigate PI through plurilingual lenses.

::: {.section aria-labelledby="sec20_1_1"}
### The Theory {#ch20.xhtml_sec20_1_1}

The theory of IP focuses on the process of second language acquisition (SLA) that concerns how second language (L2) learners derive intake from input, i.e., how they make initial form-meaning connections and how they parse sentences during real-time comprehension. The theory expounds a set of principles that describe what constraints or non-optimal strategies guide learners as they attempt to process input. For example, the lexical preference principle (LLP), says learners tend to rely on lexical items (e.g., adverbs of time) rather than grammatical form (e.g., verbal morphology) when both encode the same meaning ([VanPatten, 2015](#ch20.xhtml_ref20_51){#ch20.xhtml_R_ref20_51 .biblioref role="doc-biblioref"}). The revised LLP states that if learners do not yet know the meaning of the lexical items, the grammatical forms that convey the same meaning as those lexical items may remain unprocessed ([VanPatten, 2020](#ch20.xhtml_ref20_53){#ch20.xhtml_R_ref20_53 .biblioref role="doc-biblioref"}; see also White & Wong, this volume). The first-noun principle (FNP) describes the tendency for L2 learners to assume that the first noun in a sentence/utterance is the subject, which would lead to incorrect interpretations of object/patient-first sentences (e.g., passives in English) (see Simonsen & VanPatten, this volume). According to the sentence location principle, []{#ch20.xhtml_p288 .pagebreak aria-label=" page 288. " role="doc-pagebreak"}linguistic elements in initial position are most salient followed by those in final position while those in medial position are the least salient, which may make them less likely to get processed. (See Leeser, this volume, for a discussion of the principles of IP.)

These principles suggest that learners tend to use non-optimal processing strategies to filter out parts of input that they need to build a mental representation of the L2.
:::

::::: {.section aria-labelledby="sec20_1_2"}
### The Intervention {#ch20.xhtml_sec20_1_2}

Processing instruction (PI), the pedagogical response to the negative effects that IP biases could have on acquisition, was created to address the processing challenges that the theory predicts L2 learners face. The goal of PI is to change or destabilize the non-optimal input processing strategies for processing grammatical form.

PI uses structured input (SI) to train learners to overcome different processing problems. If the processing problem is that learners skip over a target grammatical form because they privilege lexical items to derive the same meaning (LLP), the SI activities will train them to process the form instead for this information. If the issue is that they tend to process the first noun in an utterance as the subject/agent, SI will help them realize that the first noun is not always the subject/agent.

The processing problem behind the French subjunctive form may be explained by the LLP. The subjunctive verb form encodes doubt, volition, opinion or emotion but so do the main clauses of utterances that contain these verbs. According to the theory of IP, learners have difficulty processing the subjunctive form *connaisse* in (1) because they derive the meaning that the subjunctive encodes, i.e., doubt, from the main clause *Je doute que* instead.

``` {#ch20.xhtml_preformat20_1}
   
(1) Marc doute que Marie connaisse mon professeur. 
    Mark doubts that Marie knows-[subjunctive] my professor. 
```

Below is a referential SI activity for the French subjunctive. The activity structures input in such a way to ensure that learners process what they tend to miss, i.e., the verb forms, in order to derive the meaning of doubt (subjunctive form) or certainty (indicative form). Learners cannot use their default strategy of relying on lexical items (i.e., the main clauses) to get the information. The verb form, which is always in the least salient medial position, is in initial position to increase the likelihood that learners will process it. To ensure that learners are processing input for meaning, they are required to answer a question that makes them formulate a conclusion based on the meaning of their responses.

::: {.section aria-labelledby="sec20_1_2_1"}
#### Referential SI {#ch20.xhtml_sec20_1_2_1}

A talk-show host is making statements about a celebrity. For each statement, pay attention to the verb forms to determine if she is expressing certainty or doubt, and pay attention to the meaning to determine if she likely has a favorable or unfavorable impression of the celebrity.

1.  ...soit toujours gentil avec les fans. (...is-\[subjunctive\] nice with fans.)

    a.  Je doute qu'il (I doubt that he)
    b.  Je suis certain qu'il (I am certain that he)

2.  ...est souvent en retard pour les entrevues. (... is-\[indicative\] often late for interviews.)
    a.  Il est évident qu'il (It is evident that he)
    b.  Il n'est pas certain qu'il (It isn't certain that he)

3.  []{#ch20.xhtml_p289 .pagebreak aria-label=" page 289. " role="doc-pagebreak"}...ait beaucoup d'amis. (...has-\[subjunctive\] lots of friends.)
    a.  Il est certain qu'il (It is certain that he)
    b.  Il n'est pas certain qu'il (It is not certain that he)

Does the talk-show host likely have a favorable or unfavorable opinion of the celebrity?

Referential SI has one correct response for each item and learners are given feedback as to whether their response is right or wrong. PI has a second type of activity called affective activities.
:::

::: {.section aria-labelledby="sec20_1_2_2"}
#### Affective SI {#ch20.xhtml_sec20_1_2_2}

Below are some statements about Lady Gaga. Check the statements that you agree with.

``` {#ch20.xhtml_preformat20_2}
Il n’est pas certain qu’elle… (It isn’t certain that she…)
       ___ ait un chien. (has-[subjunctive] a dog.)
       ___ aille aux Oscars chaque année. (goes-[subjunctive] to the Oscars every year.)

Il est certain qu’elle… (It is certain that she…)
       ___ va aux galas souvent. (goes-[indicative] to galas often.)
       ___ est une bonne danseuse. (is-[indicative] a good dancer.)
```

Affective activities do not have right/wrong answers; they instead require learners to express an opinion, belief, or some other affective response as they are engaged in processing information about the real world. Research shows that it is the referential SI activities that have an impact on altering processing strategies ([Marsden, 2006](#ch20.xhtml_ref20_42){#ch20.xhtml_R_ref20_42 .biblioref role="doc-biblioref"}). Affective activities provide additional input to reinforce the work done by referential activities.

Not all input activities are SI. SI activities are unique in that they are structured to destabilize the non-optimal processing strategies L2 learners tend to use as identified by the theory of IP. For an input activity to be SI, it must minimally have the following characteristics:

- Created with the goal of changing/destabilizing specific non-optimal processing strategies (as articulated by the principles of IP);
- Input is structured to privilege the processing of target forms, i.e., input is structured so that learners must process the target form/sentence structure to derive the meaning that the form/sentence structure encodes;
- Ensures learners cannot rely on anything else in the sentence/utterance (e.g., lexical items, background knowledge) to derive the meaning that the target form/sentence structure encodes.
- The activity requires learners to attend to meaning by asking them to formulate a response based on processing the meaning of sentences/utterances.

*[(See [Wong & Simard \[2015\]](#ch20.xhtml_ref20_62){#ch20.xhtml_R_ref20_62 .biblioref role="doc-biblioref"} for guidelines to create SI activities.)]{.credit role="doc-credit"}*

In sum, IP and PI are related but they are not the same. IP is the theory. PI is the pedagogical application of the theory. SI are unique input activities used in PI that target specific processing difficulties articulated in the theory of IP.
:::
:::::
:::::::

::: {.section aria-labelledby="sec20_2"}
## Historical Perspectives {#ch20.xhtml_sec20_2}

PI as a research agenda was officially launched with [VanPatten and Cadierno (1993)](#ch20.xhtml_ref20_55){#ch20.xhtml_R_ref20_55 .biblioref role="doc-biblioref"} who called for a shift from a focus on knowledge/product to a focus on understanding the processes that []{#ch20.xhtml_p290 .pagebreak aria-label=" page 290. " role="doc-pagebreak"}underly SLA ([VanPatten, 2015](#ch20.xhtml_ref20_51){.biblioref role="doc-biblioref"}, p. 91), specifically, how learners process input. If instruction could change the non-optimal strategies learners use to process input, then instruction could have an impact on learners' mental representation by altering the quantity as well as the quality of what gets processed in the input.

VanPatten and Cadierno compared PI to what they called traditional instruction (TI), a type of instruction that was prevalent at the time that involved giving learners explicit information (EI) followed by output practice in the form of mechanical, meaningful, and communicative drills. The target structure was Spanish object pronouns, a structure affected by the FNP. In addition to SI activities, PI also included EI about the target structure. Because the goal of PI was to alter non-optimal processing strategies, this EI also alerted learners to not assume that the first noun is always the subject/agent. Results revealed that PI was better than TI on a sentence-level interpretation task (TI was no better than the control group), and that PI was as good as TI on a sentence-level production task (results were maintained on delayed posttests one week and one month later). These results seemed counter intuitive. How was it possible that PI participants who never engaged in output practice during training were able to produce the target structure as well as those TI participants who received extensive training on production practice? These results make sense when one considers what PI sets out to do. Unlike interventions that focus on rule learning and output practice, SI activities in PI are designed to change how learners process input so that intake data is created. The PI group performed well on the production task because participants' engagement with SI delivered intake to the developing linguistic system which consequently allowed them to access this data for production when they were required to. TI activities on the other hand, did not enhance IP and therefore did not provide any intake to the developing system so these participants could only perform well on a task that they practiced during training.

[VanPatten and Cadierno's (1993)](#ch20.xhtml_ref20_55){.biblioref role="doc-biblioref"} study provided empirical evidence to support that instruction that focuses on learners' IP strategies can create intake to affect mental representation. This ground-breaking study subsequently sparked a robust research agenda that has now spanned three decades of empirical investigation.
:::

:::::: {.section aria-labelledby="sec20_3"}
## Current Contributions and Research {#ch20.xhtml_sec20_3}

::: {.section aria-labelledby="sec20_3_1"}
### First Two Decades: 1993--2013 {#ch20.xhtml_sec20_3_1}

Research from the first two decades include the following findings:

- *PI is effective because its focus is on changing the way learners process input*. These studies compare PI to other interventions including more traditional teaching of grammar involving rule explanation followed by drill practice (e.g., [Benati, 2001](#ch20.xhtml_ref20_5){#ch20.xhtml_R_ref20_5 .biblioref role="doc-biblioref"}; [Comer & deBenedette, 2011](#ch20.xhtml_ref20_14){#ch20.xhtml_R_ref20_14 .biblioref role="doc-biblioref"}; [VanPatten & Cadierno, 1993](#ch20.xhtml_ref20_55){.biblioref role="doc-biblioref"}; [VanPatten & Wong, 2004](#ch20.xhtml_ref20_58){#ch20.xhtml_R_ref20_58 .biblioref role="doc-biblioref"}), meaning-based output instruction (e.g., [Benati, 2005](#ch20.xhtml_ref20_7){#ch20.xhtml_R_ref20_7 .biblioref role="doc-biblioref"}; [Farley, 2001](#ch20.xhtml_ref20_19){#ch20.xhtml_R_ref20_19 .biblioref role="doc-biblioref"}), dictogloss (e.g., [Uludag & VanPatten, 2012](#ch20.xhtml_ref20_49){#ch20.xhtml_R_ref20_49 .biblioref role="doc-biblioref"}), and other input-based instruction (e.g., [Marsden, 2006](#ch20.xhtml_ref20_42){.biblioref role="doc-biblioref"}). In these studies, PI always outperforms other interventions on processing/interpretation tasks because PI is designed to enhance input processing. On other tasks, PI is either better or as good as other pedagogical techniques.
- *The causative variable is SI, not EI, (explicit corrective) feedback or enhancement*. It is not necessary to provide learners with EI (e.g., [Benati, 2004](#ch20.xhtml_ref20_6){#ch20.xhtml_R_ref20_6 .biblioref role="doc-biblioref"}; [VanPatten & Oikkenon, 1996](#ch20.xhtml_ref20_57){#ch20.xhtml_R_ref20_57 .biblioref role="doc-biblioref"}; [Wong, 2004](#ch20.xhtml_ref20_60){#ch20.xhtml_R_ref20_60 .biblioref role="doc-biblioref"}) or feedback (e.g., [Sanz & Morgan-Short, 2004](#ch20.xhtml_ref20_44){#ch20.xhtml_R_ref20_44 .biblioref role="doc-biblioref"}) because participants who received SI alone did as well as those who also received EI and/or feedback. Using trials-to-criterion, other studies found that EI helped participants process certain structures sooner, []{#ch20.xhtml_p291 .pagebreak aria-label=" page 291. " role="doc-pagebreak"}but that EI was ultimately not necessary since the SI-only groups eventually caught up (e.g., [Fernández, 2008](#ch20.xhtml_ref20_20){#ch20.xhtml_R_ref20_20 .biblioref role="doc-biblioref"}; [VanPatten et al., 2013](#ch20.xhtml_ref20_54){#ch20.xhtml_R_ref20_54 .biblioref role="doc-biblioref"}). In a series of studies, [Lee and Benati (2007a)](#ch20.xhtml_ref20_30){#ch20.xhtml_R_ref20_30 .biblioref role="doc-biblioref"} examined whether the effects of SI would be greater if the target forms were visually or orally enhanced but found no effect for enhancement. Because SI is successful in helping learners process target forms correctly, adding EI or enhancement has little to no impact.
- *Effective for younger and older learners*. The bulk of PI research has focused on adults, but a few studies show that PI is effective for adolescents (e.g., [Benati, 2005](#ch20.xhtml_ref20_7){.biblioref role="doc-biblioref"}; [Marsden, 2006](#ch20.xhtml_ref20_42){.biblioref role="doc-biblioref"}; [VanPatten & Oikkenon, 1996](#ch20.xhtml_ref20_57){.biblioref role="doc-biblioref"}), middle school ([Benati et al., 2008](#ch20.xhtml_ref20_10){#ch20.xhtml_R_ref20_10 .biblioref role="doc-biblioref"}), and primary school age children ([Benati & Lee, 2010](#ch20.xhtml_ref20_9){#ch20.xhtml_R_ref20_9 .biblioref role="doc-biblioref"}), and learners over age 60 (e.g., [Cox & Sanz, 2015](#ch20.xhtml_ref20_16){#ch20.xhtml_R_ref20_16 .biblioref role="doc-biblioref"}).
- *Effective with a variety of languages*. Benefits of PI have been seen with L2s such as English ([Benati, 2005](#ch20.xhtml_ref20_7){.biblioref role="doc-biblioref"}), French ([VanPatten & Wong, 2004](#ch20.xhtml_ref20_58){.biblioref role="doc-biblioref"}), German ([Culman et al., 2009](#ch20.xhtml_ref20_17){#ch20.xhtml_R_ref20_17 .biblioref role="doc-biblioref"}), Italian ([Benati, 2001](#ch20.xhtml_ref20_5){.biblioref role="doc-biblioref"}), Japanese ([Lee & Benati, 2007c](#ch20.xhtml_ref20_32){#ch20.xhtml_R_ref20_32 .biblioref role="doc-biblioref"}), Russian ([Comer & deBenedette, 2011](#ch20.xhtml_ref20_14){.biblioref role="doc-biblioref"}), Spanish ([VanPatten & Cadierno, 1993](#ch20.xhtml_ref20_55){.biblioref role="doc-biblioref"}), Chinese ([Glimois, 2019](#ch20.xhtml_ref20_21){#ch20.xhtml_R_ref20_21 .biblioref role="doc-biblioref"}) and Latin ([Cox & Sanz, 2015](#ch20.xhtml_ref20_16){.biblioref role="doc-biblioref"}). Besides English, PI has been investigated with L1 speakers of Chinese ([Benati & Lee, 2010](#ch20.xhtml_ref20_9){.biblioref role="doc-biblioref"}), German ([Angelovska & Benati, 2013](#ch20.xhtml_ref20_3){#ch20.xhtml_R_ref20_3 .biblioref role="doc-biblioref"}), Greek ([Benati, 2005](#ch20.xhtml_ref20_7){.biblioref role="doc-biblioref"}), Italian ([Lee & Benati, 2007c](#ch20.xhtml_ref20_32){.biblioref role="doc-biblioref"}), Korean ([Benati et al., 2008](#ch20.xhtml_ref20_10){.biblioref role="doc-biblioref"}), Polish ([Lee & McNulty, 2013](#ch20.xhtml_ref20_35){#ch20.xhtml_R_ref20_35 .biblioref role="doc-biblioref"}), and Turkish ([Uludag & VanPatten, 2012](#ch20.xhtml_ref20_49){.biblioref role="doc-biblioref"}).
- *Not modulated by individual differences such as aptitude or working memory*. [Lee and Benati (2013)](#ch20.xhtml_ref20_33){#ch20.xhtml_R_ref20_33 .biblioref role="doc-biblioref"} and [VanPatten et al., (2013)](#ch20.xhtml_ref20_54){.biblioref role="doc-biblioref"} show that these variables do not appear to play a role on accuracy measures that investigate the impact of PI. However, PI research focusing on individual differences in general is sparse.
- *Effective for both classroom and online environments*. Studies by [Lee and Benati (2007b)](#ch20.xhtml_ref20_31){#ch20.xhtml_R_ref20_31 .biblioref role="doc-biblioref"} directly compared classroom and computer-delivered PI and found that both environments were equally effective.
- *PI results in transfer-of-training effects*. If PI trains learners to no longer rely on the ineffective strategies that they tend to use in favor of more optimal L2 processing strategies, then those trained with PI should be able to transfer these more effective strategies to process new target forms. A handful of PI studies show that learners who have been trained with PI for a particular structure make gains on sentence-level tasks when they process novel structures for which they have not been trained which supports that PI leads to transfer-of-training effects (see volume by [Benati & Lee, 2008](#ch20.xhtml_ref20_8){#ch20.xhtml_R_ref20_8 .biblioref role="doc-biblioref"}).
- *PI has long-term effects*. In [VanPatten and Fernández (2004)](#ch20.xhtml_ref20_56){#ch20.xhtml_R_ref20_56 .biblioref role="doc-biblioref"}, the effects of PI were evident on a delayed posttest nine months later. However, this is the only study that uses such a substantial interval between initial and delayed posttests. More studies with delayed posttest beyond a few weeks are needed to adequately investigate long-term effects.
- *Effects of PI on a variety of tasks*. While standard tasks in PI studies have been sentence-level interpretation tasks, studies have also used discourse-level tasks (see volume by [Benati & Lee, 2010](#ch20.xhtml_ref20_9){.biblioref role="doc-biblioref"}) such as oral video narration ([Sanz & Morgan-Short, 2004](#ch20.xhtml_ref20_44){.biblioref role="doc-biblioref"}), and guided compositions ([Cheng, 2002](#ch20.xhtml_ref20_13){#ch20.xhtml_R_ref20_13 .biblioref role="doc-biblioref"}).
:::

:::: {.section aria-labelledby="sec20_3_2"}
### Third Decade: 2014 to present {#ch20.xhtml_sec20_3_2}

The third decade looked at questions such as the number of SI items necessary for PI to have primary and secondary effects (e.g., [White, 2015](#ch20.xhtml_ref20_59){#ch20.xhtml_R_ref20_59 .biblioref role="doc-biblioref"}), and how prosodic cues might impact the effectiveness of PI ([Henry et al., 2017](#ch20.xhtml_ref20_23){#ch20.xhtml_R_ref20_23 .biblioref role="doc-biblioref"}). Work by [Cox (2017)](#ch20.xhtml_ref20_15){#ch20.xhtml_R_ref20_15 .biblioref role="doc-biblioref"} and [Glimois (2019)](#ch20.xhtml_ref20_21){.biblioref role="doc-biblioref"} examined if PI might impact bilinguals and monolinguals differently. While Cox found an advantage for bilinguals in the learning of Latin morphosyntax, there was no significance difference between bilinguals and monolinguals in Glimois' work with L2 Chinese classifiers. Glimois speculates that the SI activities []{#ch20.xhtml_p292 .pagebreak aria-label=" page 292. " role="doc-pagebreak"}might have leveled out any differences between the two groups, i.e., the SI might have helped the monolinguals compensate for any disadvantages they have compared to the bilinguals.

::: {.section aria-labelledby="sec20_3_2_1"}
#### Online Measures {#ch20.xhtml_sec20_3_2_1}

Research turned to online measures to observe how PI impacts L2 learners' processing of input during real time.

In a study on L2 German accusative case markers, [Henry (2015)](#ch20.xhtml_ref20_22){#ch20.xhtml_R_ref20_22 .biblioref role="doc-biblioref"} investigated changes in learners' real-time processing strategies with a self-paced reading (SPR) task and found that participants did not process experimental sentences in a native-like way before training, but those who received PI and those who received PI along with prosodic cues directed more of their attentional resources toward case markers following training. Also using SPR, [Lee et al. (2020)](#ch20.xhtml_ref20_36){#ch20.xhtml_R_ref20_36 .biblioref role="doc-biblioref"} found that participants trained with PI made gains in accuracy and processing speed as they processed L2 Spanish verb forms when compared to a control group. Using the same materials as [Lee et al. (2020)](#ch20.xhtml_ref20_36){.biblioref role="doc-biblioref"}, [Malvorh et al.'s (2020)](#ch20.xhtml_ref20_41){#ch20.xhtml_R_ref20_41 .biblioref role="doc-biblioref"} SPR study compared PI to a type of guided-induction computer-delivered instruction and found that while the guided-induction condition had higher accuracy scores on the immediate posttest, the PI group had more durable results on the delayed posttest. Because the guided-induction group also had to spend 50% more time on task than the PI group, the researchers concluded that PI was also more efficient.

Using eye-tracking, [Issa et al. (2015)](#ch20.xhtml_ref20_25){#ch20.xhtml_R_ref20_25 .biblioref role="doc-biblioref"} found that both textual enhancement and SI were successful in drawing learners' attention to target Spanish object pronouns (measured in terms of fixation duration and skipping rate), but in qualitatively different ways. Specifically, textual enhancement led to "a more superficial type of processing" while SI led to "a deeper level of processing" (p. 137).

[Wong and Ito's (2018)](#ch20.xhtml_ref20_61){#ch20.xhtml_R_ref20_61 .biblioref role="doc-biblioref"} PI eye-tracking study focused on the French causative using the visual world paradigm with a dichotomous picture-selection task. Eye movement data revealed that participants relied on the FNP (see also Simonsen & VanPatten, this volume) before training. After training, the PI group displayed more looks to the correct picture (showing a change in eye movement behavior) while the TI group did not improve. However, the PI group did not improve as much as anticipated and the point at which they started to look at the correct picture occurred later than expected which led Wong and Ito to conclude that the FNP remains resilient. Eye movement data showed that adding EI did not enhance results.

[Ito and Wong's (2019)](#ch20.xhtml_ref20_26){#ch20.xhtml_R_ref20_26 .biblioref role="doc-biblioref"} eye-tracking study on voice familiarity revealed that hearing the same voice through training and testing may generally facilitate the processing of target structures (i.e., the French causative) but the effect appears to depend on which part of speech conveys more salient grammatical cues. Additionally, they found that PI in the aural and written modes are equally effective.

Targeting Spanish accusative case pronouns (also affected by the FNP), [Issa and Morgan-Short's (2019)](#ch20.xhtml_ref20_24){#ch20.xhtml_R_ref20_24 .biblioref role="doc-biblioref"} eye-tracking results revealed that participants who engaged in SI activities allocated more visual attention to target forms. Additionally, changes in eye movement patterns predicted delayed posttest accuracy scores for those trained with SI.

[Lee and Doherty (2019)](#ch20.xhtml_ref20_34){#ch20.xhtml_R_ref20_34 .biblioref role="doc-biblioref"} investigated native and nonnative speakers' processing of Spanish active and passive sentences. Eye-tracking data revealed that processing passive sentences, a structure affected by the FNP, comes at a cost to not just nonnative speakers but also to native speakers. After training with PI, the nonnative speakers' processing behaviors became more native-like but did not reach the same level as that of native speakers.

Eye movement behavior in [Wong et al. (2021)](#ch20.xhtml_ref20_63){#ch20.xhtml_R_ref20_63 .biblioref role="doc-biblioref"} showed PI but not TI had a positive effect on the processing of the primary French causative structure, but neither treatment had any effect on the []{#ch20.xhtml_p293 .pagebreak aria-label=" page 293. " role="doc-pagebreak"}untrained passive construction. They speculated that PI training on the primary causative structure might have trained participants to rely on the necessary cues to process causative sentences accurately, but the training might not have been effective enough to decrease reliance on the FNP. Using the same target structure and experimental design, [Wong et al. (2021)](#ch20.xhtml_ref20_63){.biblioref role="doc-biblioref"} found no differences in accuracy across three sizes of PI training. However, eye-tracking data revealed that exposing learners to PI using multi-voice input (MVI) (four voices) was better than single-voice input, but the advantage of MVI disappeared when the number of training items increased from 48 to 96, suggesting that more intensive PI training is not necessarily better (at least with MVI). PI training with as few as 24 items was sufficient to reduce looks to the incorrect picture.

[Dracos and Henry (2021)](#ch20.xhtml_ref20_18){#ch20.xhtml_R_ref20_18 .biblioref role="doc-biblioref"} used SPR to observe L2 Spanish learners' processing of verbal morphology and to examine whether working memory (WM) modulates the effects of PI. They found that PI led to more accurate interpretations of person--number and adverb--tense agreement, but higher WM did not result in higher accuracy gains. The SPR results, however, revealed that PI did not increase sensitivity to either subject--verb or adverb--verb tense agreements. Furthermore, SPR results revealed that among participants who received PI, higher WM enhanced sensitivity to violations under certain conditions (i.e., future tense). Thus, while the accuracy measure showed that PI can override reliance on lexical items, PI's effect on online processing was not as clear. The online measure also suggests that PI along with higher WM can increase learners' sensitivity to some adverb--tense violations.
:::
::::
::::::

::: {.section aria-labelledby="sec20_4"}
## Main Research Methods {#ch20.xhtml_sec20_4}

Experimental designs in PI research have primarily used pre and post training designs (offline \[e.g., [VanPatten & Cadierno, 1993](#ch20.xhtml_ref20_55){.biblioref role="doc-biblioref"}\] and online \[e.g., [Lee & Doherty, 2019](#ch20.xhtml_ref20_34){.biblioref role="doc-biblioref"}, [Wong & Ito, 2018](#ch20.xhtml_ref20_61){.biblioref role="doc-biblioref"}\]) and trials-to-criterion (e.g., [Fernández, 2008](#ch20.xhtml_ref20_20){.biblioref role="doc-biblioref"}). Because PI is about the processing of morpho-phonological units and sentences rather than the internalization of rules, assessment measures in PI studies should not use tasks that test the knowledge of rules (e.g., grammaticality judgment tasks, certain fill-in-the-blanks, etc.). Because PI is about enhancing IP, the standard assessment measure of the effects of PI is an interpretation task ([VanPatten, 2015](#ch20.xhtml_ref20_51){.biblioref role="doc-biblioref"}). Formats have ranged from paper-and-pencil to computerized formats to online formats (e.g., eye-tracking, SPR). Some studies have included a sentence-level production task to assess whether participants could access processed input for production when required to. However, [VanPatten (2015)](#ch20.xhtml_ref20_51){.biblioref role="doc-biblioref"} points out that production tasks are not necessary since PI is not about the development of communicative skill.

An anonymous reviewer noted that PI studies tend to use 60% accuracy on pretests as the cutoff score and questions whether this could lead to confounding effects of prior knowledge. On the issue of prior knowledge, VanPatten points out that PI research should ideally look at *ab initio* learners because IP is about making initial form--meaning connections (see interview with VanPatten, this volume, for more on this topic). Finally, while the bulk of PI research has used RMANOVAs, more recent studies (e.g., [Ito & Wong, 2019](#ch20.xhtml_ref20_26){.biblioref role="doc-biblioref"}, [2022](#ch20.xhtml_ref20_27){#ch20.xhtml_R_ref20_27 .biblioref role="doc-biblioref"}) are using mixed effects models which may better account for random effects.
:::

::::::: {.section aria-labelledby="sec20_5"}
## Critical Issues and Topics {#ch20.xhtml_sec20_5}

::: {.section aria-labelledby="sec20_5_1"}
### Why Does PI Work? {#ch20.xhtml_sec20_5_1}

Unprocessed input cannot affect acquisition. PI targets the processes that create intake for acquisition and is informed by a theory that is devoted to understanding how learners derive []{#ch20.xhtml_p294 .pagebreak aria-label=" page 294. " role="doc-pagebreak"}intake from input. An intervention that is based on an understanding of why learners process certain features of input before (or not) others can teach learners better strategies to maximize the creation of intake. This is what PI does.

PI does not equate linguistic knowledge with "rule knowledge" and language development with "rule use" ([Leeser et al., 2021](#ch20.xhtml_ref20_38){#ch20.xhtml_R_ref20_38 .biblioref role="doc-biblioref"}, p. 11). Instead of rule internalization, PI views acquisition as the creation of a complex, abstract, implicit linguistic system: "Learners do not acquire rules from the input. Instead, learners process surface morpho-phonological units (e.g., lexical form, morphological form) and internalize these units along with underlying features or specifications" ([VanPatten & Rothman, 2014](#ch20.xhtml_ref20_501){#ch20.xhtml_R_ref20_501 .biblioref role="doc-biblioref"}, p. 25). PI is also not the same as noticing nor is its goal to develop communicative skill (see discussion in [VanPatten, 2017](#ch20.xhtml_ref20_52){#ch20.xhtml_R_ref20_52 .biblioref role="doc-biblioref"}). PI is effective because rather than practicing rules, PI teaches learners to process forms in input that they might otherwise not process or not process correctly.
:::

::: {.section aria-labelledby="sec20_5_2"}
### Interfaces with other Theories {#ch20.xhtml_sec20_5_2}

The theory that underlies PI interfaces well with broader frameworks that link processing with acquisition. As also discussed by [Leeser (2021)](#ch20.xhtml_ref20_38){.biblioref role="doc-biblioref"}, "in both Autonomous Induction Theory and the MOGUL project, new representations and their links among phonology, syntax, and semantics are established by processing input---connecting form, function, and meaning during comprehension" (pp. 308--309). This is precisely what PI attempts to do. In the modular cognition framework (formerly known as MOGUL), [Sharwood Smith and Truscott (2014)](#ch20.xhtml_ref20_46){#ch20.xhtml_R_ref20_46 .biblioref role="doc-biblioref"} propose that when an internal processor encounters novel input for which no representation yet exists, the processor will attempt to create a representation by activating an item or items that already exist(s) in its memory store, but "it does so, purely for the purpose of processing the current input" and will have a very low resting level (p. 94). This resting activation level can only be raised "if similar input is received on a subsequent occasion and the new chain is again activated" (p. 95). Leeser explains that working through referential SI activities generates "intake data for the potential creation of new representations and links in the language module" (p. 310). The opportunity to process additional input of the target form through referential and affective SI activities "can potentially raise the resting activation levels of newly linked structures" to facilitate acquisition (p. 310).

In Carroll's ([2001](#ch20.xhtml_ref20_11){#ch20.xhtml_R_ref20_11 .biblioref role="doc-biblioref"}, [2007](#ch20.xhtml_ref20_12){#ch20.xhtml_R_ref20_12 .biblioref role="doc-biblioref"}) autonomous induction theory, acquisition happens due to a series of parsing failures. When a learner's internal parser parses input in a way that does not match up to the real-world information that the input conveys, this creates opportunities for the parser to make adjustments. If an L2 English learner parses the utterance "Tom is kissed by Mary" as "Tom is kissing Mary" because the parser assigns the role of subject to the first noun (Tom), but then sees it is Mary who is kissing Tom, the learner's parser has the opportunity to notice the failure and make adjustments. If the correct adjustment is made, acquisition will proceed to a new stage of development. However, such opportunities to notice failure are not always available, and the parser is consequently denied the needed opportunities to respond to processing failures and to make necessary adjustments. SI in PI provide learners' parsers with the needed opportunities to notice failure. First, input in SI is structured to facilitate correct parsing. Should learners parse an item incorrectly, the immediate feedback that they receive that their response was incorrect creates an opportunity to notice 'failure' and to allow the parser to make necessary adjustments to further acquisition.

Drawing from [Sharwood Smith and Truscott (2014)](#ch20.xhtml_ref20_46){.biblioref role="doc-biblioref"}, [Leeser (2021)](#ch20.xhtml_ref20_38){.biblioref role="doc-biblioref"} says the EI in PI may help learners make better sense of the input they encounter but is clear that SI does not entail []{#ch20.xhtml_p295 .pagebreak aria-label=" page 295. " role="doc-pagebreak"}practicing the EI to internalize a rule. The EI in PI is "at the service of processing (structured) input" (p. 310). [Carroll (2007)](#ch20.xhtml_ref20_12){.biblioref role="doc-biblioref"} points out, "explicit instruction and practice will not lead to changes in the learner's mental representation unless the instruction itself causes changes in processing primary linguistic data... (as in VanPatten's processing instruction---e.g., VanPatten, 2004)" (cited in [Leeser, 2021](#ch20.xhtml_ref20_37){#ch20.xhtml_R_ref20_37 .biblioref role="doc-biblioref"}, p. 311). In short, only input that is processed can lead to the creation of new representations to further language development.
:::

::: {.section aria-labelledby="sec20_5_3"}
### Is SI the Same as Task-Essentialness? {#ch20.xhtml_sec20_5_3}

Since first used by [Sanz and Morgan-Short (2004)](#ch20.xhtml_ref20_44){.biblioref role="doc-biblioref"} in a PI context, sometimes "task-essentialness" is used synonymously with "structured input." Coined by [Loschky and Bley-Vroman (1993)](#ch20.xhtml_ref20_40){#ch20.xhtml_R_ref20_40 .biblioref role="doc-biblioref"}, a task has "task-essentialness" if "the task cannot be completed without the grammar point" and if "the grammar point itself is the 'essence' of what is to be attended to" (p. 139). Given that a trait of (referential) SI is that these activities require learners to rely on the target structure to interpret the meaning or function that the structure encodes, we could say SI has the characteristic "task-essentialness." However, SI is not just about requiring learners to attend to a target structure to complete a task or activity. SI also requires that input be 'structured' to change processing behavior. A task can be "task-essential" and not be SI (e.g., puzzle tasks in [Rosa & Leow \[2004\]](#ch20.xhtml_ref20_43){#ch20.xhtml_R_ref20_43 .biblioref role="doc-biblioref"} and "task-essential noticing practice" in [Kasprowicz & Marsen \[2018\]](#ch20.xhtml_ref20_29){#ch20.xhtml_R_ref20_29 .biblioref role="doc-biblioref"}). Thus, reducing SI to task-essentialness risks oversimplifying what SI was designed to do.
:::

::: {.section aria-labelledby="sec20_5_4"}
### Can We Create SI for Other Linguistic Subsystems? {#ch20.xhtml_sec20_5_4}

PI is issued from a theory that is largely about the processing of morphosyntax and sentence-level input. The purpose of SI is to alter the strategies identified in the theory of IP that hinder learners from processing input correctly so that they come to use more optimal strategies. Can SI activities be created for other linguistic subsystems? If linguistic subsystem X has a way of accounting for how X-input becomes X-intake and can identify processing issues that hinder learners from processing X-input, it is conceivable that SI can be developed to help learners process X-input. However, we would first need to define what constitutes input and intake for linguistic subsystem X, and how input becomes intake for this linguistic domain. With the exception of research on lexical-IP (see [Barcroft, 2015](#ch20.xhtml_ref20_4){#ch20.xhtml_R_ref20_4 .biblioref role="doc-biblioref"}), research on other linguistic subsystems that take an IP approach is scarce. Nevertheless, we see publications on SI for pragmatics ([Li, 2012](#ch20.xhtml_ref20_39){#ch20.xhtml_R_ref20_39 .biblioref role="doc-biblioref"}; [Takimoto, 2009](#ch20.xhtml_ref20_47){#ch20.xhtml_R_ref20_47 .biblioref role="doc-biblioref"}) and even for the teaching of literature ([Katz, 2002](#ch20.xhtml_ref20_28){#ch20.xhtml_R_ref20_28 .biblioref role="doc-biblioref"}). An examination of these "SI" activities reveal that they are input activities, but that is where the resemblance to SI stops. As discussed in Fichtner (this volume), current studies on SI for pragmatics (e.g., [Li, 2012](#ch20.xhtml_ref20_39){.biblioref role="doc-biblioref"}) do not define what is input and intake for pragmatics and they do not target any specific IP strategies. As for the teaching of literature, what IP strategy would an "SI" activity for the teaching of literary works target? This does not mean that these activities are not sound activities for what these scholars want to accomplish, but they are not SI. When the nature of SI is oversimplified (e.g., SI=input or SI = task-essentialness), the purpose of SI gets muddled and PI risks evolving into practices that are removed from its original goal.

Before we can consider developing SI for other linguistic subsystems, we need to understand the nature of input and intake in other linguistic domains. We would need to know the processing challenges that learners face and the strategies that they use that hinder them from processing input efficiently in other linguistic subsystems.
:::
:::::::

:::::: {.section aria-labelledby="sec20_6"}
## []{#ch20.xhtml_p296 .pagebreak aria-label=" page 296. " role="doc-pagebreak"}Recommendations for Practice {#ch20.xhtml_sec20_6}

Because PI is not a method or approach, this intervention may be used in any context where better processing of L2 input is a desired outcome. While EI was originally a component of PI, studies show that it is the SI that creates intake for acquisition. The decision to include EI or not should be based on specific learning contexts.

::: {.section aria-labelledby="sec20_6_1"}
### Task-Based Instruction {#ch20.xhtml_sec20_6_1}

SI supports task-based instruction and other communicative approaches. While the goal of SI is not to develop communicative skill, when students are provided with copious opportunities to receive input and practice processing input correctly, we increase the likelihood that they will get language into their developing system which in turn will allow them to perform tasks more successfully. In a task-based curriculum, linguistic areas that students are weaker in can be identified and SI can subsequently be used either as homework or as brief in-class activities to help students process certain structures better, so they are better equipped to engage in various types of tasks.
:::

::: {.section aria-labelledby="sec20_6_2"}
### Differentiated Learning {#ch20.xhtml_sec20_6_2}

Because SI can target structures that individual students have difficulty processing, PI can be used as a tool in differentiated learning contexts and with learners who may need help catching up to their peers. After identifying the areas these students need help with, SI could be used to teach these learners better processing strategies to help them process these linguistic forms successfully. The context of SI activities may be tailored to the interests of different types of learners as appropriate.
:::

::: {.section aria-labelledby="sec20_6_3"}
### PI and Plurilingual Instruction {#ch20.xhtml_sec20_6_3}

In plurilingual instruction contexts where learners are encouraged to tap into their full linguistic repertoire, instructors may want to draw on the different languages that learners know should they decide to use EI. Typically, during the EI phase of PI, instructors give learners an explanation of the target structure in either the L2 or in the L1 (of the majority of learners), and alert learners to the non-optimal strategies that may hinder them from processing the target form. In plurilingual instruction, where we want to validate and tap into all languages that learners know, rather than delivering EI in the majority language only, instructors can attempt to provide EI in multiple languages to the extent that it is feasible so that all learners in the class have more equal access to the EI. While explaining the structure, instructors could ask learners if the target structure works the same way in other languages that they know. Along the same lines, when alerting learners to the non-optimal strategies associated with processing this structure, learners could be encouraged to reflect on whether there are also less-than-optimal strategies associated with processing this structure in other languages. This plurilingual inspired approach to delivering EI in PI has the potential to not just enhance learners' processing of the target structure; this practice validates all learners' languages in the classroom and contributes to promoting diversity and linguistic equity in language teaching.
:::
::::::

::::::: {.section aria-labelledby="sec20_7"}
## Future Directions and Conclusion {#ch20.xhtml_sec20_7}

::: {.section aria-labelledby="sec20_7_1"}
### PI Through Plurilingual Lenses {#ch20.xhtml_sec20_7_1}

Could the plurilingual inspired approach to delivering EI in PI described above be more effective than the more typical ways of giving EI that are exemplified in current PI research? Many []{#ch20.xhtml_p297 .pagebreak aria-label=" page 297. " role="doc-pagebreak"}PI studies (e.g., [VanPatten & Oikkenon, 1996](#ch20.xhtml_ref20_57){.biblioref role="doc-biblioref"}) have demonstrated that EI does not play a critical role in PI. Might EI have a greater impact if it were given through this more plurilingual manner? How might encouraging learners to draw on their full linguistic repertoire impact IP more generally and how PI is implemented in the classroom?
:::

::: {.section aria-labelledby="sec20_7_2"}
### Other Languages, Populations, and Purposes {#ch20.xhtml_sec20_7_2}

Existing PI research has largely focused on young adults learning languages in academic settings who share the same L1 (see also discussion in Ruiz & Rebuschat, this volume, about potential sampling bias). In an increasing multilingual world with learners from different educational and socioeconomic backgrounds, future research on PI should focus on the evolving needs of different learners. Does PI impact educated and less educated (or not educated) learners in the same way? Do literacy skills impact the type of processing strategies learners use?

Research also needs to investigate L2s that have different orthographies (i.e., Arabic, Chinese kanji) because different orthographies may have different implications for IP which would directly affect what PI would look like. Does the sentence location principle apply to a language like Arabic where script is read from right to left? What about non-spoken languages like ASL or braille? Can PI be used to help learners of these languages process input better?
:::

::: {.section aria-labelledby="sec20_7_3"}
### Measuring Strategy Change {#ch20.xhtml_sec20_7_3}

Because PI is about destabilizing/changing processing strategies, PI research must be able to demonstrate strategy change as a result of PI/SI. The fact that PI groups perform well on production tasks even without any output practice during treatment has been taken as evidence that PI changed learners' processing strategies and created intake for the developing system to allow them to access it for production (e.g., [VanPatten & Cadierno, 1993](#ch20.xhtml_ref20_55){.biblioref role="doc-biblioref"}). The observation that learners trained with one target structure make gains when they process new structures for which they have not been trained (i.e., transfer-of-training effects) supports that learners trained with PI approach the processing of novel input with different strategies (e.g., [Benati & Lee, 2008](#ch20.xhtml_ref20_8){.biblioref role="doc-biblioref"}). Finally, changes in eye-movement patterns (e.g., [Wong & Ito, 2018](#ch20.xhtml_ref20_61){.biblioref role="doc-biblioref"}) demonstrate processing behavior changes in real-time.

Because the effectiveness of PI lies in its ability to alter processing strategies, future research must continue to demonstrate strategy change in real-time. If strategy destabilization/change is not demonstrated, then research needs to investigate what is responsible for the improved performance of learners who receive PI. [Wong et al. (2021)](#ch20.xhtml_ref20_63){.biblioref role="doc-biblioref"} postulated that if PI training on a particular structure alters learners' processing strategies "such that they rely less on the \[default strategy\] for interpreting L2 input regardless of structural variation," (p. 289) we may expect them to also rely less on that strategy for processing a new structure. However, if PI reduces learners' reliance on the default strategy "via increasing their sensitivities to the cues specific to the target structure \[...\], then the effects of PI may be limited to the structure that the learners are trained on" (p. 289). Future research needs to examine more closely whether learners trained with PI process input more accurately because PI has changed overall processing strategies or whether PI has trained them to rely on the cues necessary for processing target structures more accurately (p. 290).
:::

::: {.section aria-labelledby="sec20_7_4"}
### Other Linguistic Subsystems {#ch20.xhtml_sec20_7_4}

We have seen attempts to create SI activities for other linguistic domains such as pragmatics. Before we can research the effectiveness of PI/SI on other linguistic domains, we need research on []{#ch20.xhtml_p298 .pagebreak aria-label=" page 298. " role="doc-pagebreak"}the acquisition of different subsystems of language (e.g., lexis, pragmatics, phonology) from an IP perspective. What is the nature of input/intake in other linguistic subsystems? Barcroft defines *lexical intake* as "the multiple bits and pieces of word form and the multiple bits and pieces of semantic information associated with word forms that are attended to in the input and made available to the developing lexicosemantic system" ([Barcroft, 2015](#ch20.xhtml_ref20_4){.biblioref role="doc-biblioref"}, p. 23). What about other linguistic subsystems? For example, what is the nature of input and intake in pragmatics and phonology? Discussions by Fichtner and Tremblay in this volume provide springboards to encourage researchers to investigate the acquisition of pragmatics and phonology from an IP perspective. Research of this nature will not only deepen our understanding of IP across linguistic subsystems on a theoretical level; it may lead to the development of sound pedagogical practice that is informed by what we know about how learners process input for different linguistic domains.

Within the domain of syntax and morphosyntax, researchers are also beginning to explore the use of PI outside L2 contexts. Drawing on the theory of IP, [Tye-Murray (2024)](#ch20.xhtml_ref20_48){#ch20.xhtml_R_ref20_48 .biblioref role="doc-biblioref"} and colleagues propose SI activities for children and adults with hearing loss as a form of auditory training. Along the same lines, [Santamaria et al. (2013)](#ch20.xhtml_ref20_45){#ch20.xhtml_R_ref20_45 .biblioref role="doc-biblioref"} examined if SI activities could improve syntactic processing in people with aphasia. While results were mixed with a small subject pool, their findings inspire future work on how PI could be used to inform interventions outside SLA.

In their analysis of 108 studies that deal with IP and/or PI, Ruiz and Rebuschat (this volume) offer additional recommendations for future research. These suggestions include using more longitudinal designs to measure the effects of PI over time, the inclusion of qualitative methods, and the investigation of more individual difference variables to better understand how internal (e.g., individual differences) and external factors (e.g., PI) interact to account for language development. Readers are invited to consult this chapter.
:::
:::::::

::: {.section .conclusion role="doc-conclusion"}
## Conclusion {#ch20.xhtml_sec20_8}

As this volume attests, the scope of IP/PI research is broadening as we close in on three decades of empirical inquiry. For example, researchers have begun to explore the role that IP plays across different theoretical perspectives, linguistic subsystems, and domains outside SLA. Such new areas of IP research may in turn have an impact on what PI will look like and its implementation in classrooms in the future. We look forward to what the next decade of PI research will bring.
:::

::: {.section .reference aria-labelledby="r20_1"}

:::
