::: {}
# []{#ch21.xhtml_p302 .pagebreak aria-label=" page 302. " role="doc-pagebreak"}[21 ]{.chnum .ordinal}[Explicit Information, Input Processing, and Second Language Acquisition]{.chtitle} {#ch21.xhtml_h1}

Nick Henry

University of Texas

DOI: [10.4324/9781003041412-27](https://dx.doi.org/10.4324/9781003041412-27){aria-label="D.O.I. link to this document."}
:::

::: {.section .introduction role="doc-introduction"}
## Introduction {#ch21.xhtml_sec21_1}

Within the context of input processing (IP), research on the role of explicit information (EI) in second language (L2) instruction explores how EI influences learners' processing strategies and pushes them to link form and meaning. Research in this area has historically centered on whether EI is a necessary component of instructional interventions and whether task-essential practice is sufficient to change learners' processing strategies. In the past decade research has shifted focus towards factors that mediate EI's effects, including differences between linguistic forms and individual differences. More recently, research has explored whether EI affects online processes. Despite these shifts, methodological advances have also enabled researchers to revisit research questions using ever-more sensitive measures. Many of the research questions that drove research programs early on are thus still being explored. The present chapter first contextualizes this research within SLA and traces the evolution of IP research on the role of EI in L2 instruction. The chapter then turns to current developments in the field, pedagogical applications, and future directions.
:::

::: {.section aria-labelledby="sec21_2"}
## Historical Perspectives {#ch21.xhtml_sec21_2}

The roots of research on EI in the context of L2 IP and instruction are found in early theoretical approaches to SLA, such as Krashen's acquisition-learning distinction ([Krashen, 1982](#ch21.xhtml_ref21_34){#ch21.xhtml_R_ref21_34 .biblioref role="doc-biblioref"}), which distinguishes between consciously controlled explicit knowledge about language ('learned' knowledge) and implicit, abstract knowledge ('acquired' knowledge). While the field now widely recognizes the centrality of implicit knowledge and the limits of explicit information (i.e., grammar instruction) in acquisition (R. [Ellis, 1989](#ch21.xhtml_ref21_16){#ch21.xhtml_R_ref21_16 .biblioref role="doc-biblioref"}), numerous theoretical and pedagogical positions have attempted to explain how---or indeed whether---explicit and implicit information interface with each other (see [VanPatten et al., 2020](#ch21.xhtml_ref21_62){#ch21.xhtml_R_ref21_62 .biblioref role="doc-biblioref"}). Critically, numerous theoretical frameworks suggest that explicit knowledge could play a significant role in the development of implicit knowledge. Important to the present discussion are approaches suggesting that EI could increase awareness of and attention to forms and their meanings; this awareness could change how learners process input and increase intake.

One of the most prominent of these approaches is the *noticing hypothesis* (see [Schmidt, 1990](#ch21.xhtml_ref21_54){#ch21.xhtml_R_ref21_54 .biblioref role="doc-biblioref"}), which posits that conscious awareness (noticing) is necessary to convert input to intake. The []{#ch21.xhtml_p303 .pagebreak aria-label=" page 303. " role="doc-pagebreak"}strong version of this hypothesis has now been supplanted by weaker formulations that deemphasize the role of noticing in developing implicit knowledge (see [Truscott, 1998](#ch21.xhtml_ref21_56){#ch21.xhtml_R_ref21_56 .biblioref role="doc-biblioref"} for a critical review). Nevertheless, researchers broadly recognize that conscious attention to forms could benefit learners by shaping how they attend to the input they process (i.e., how they create intake). It is possible that metalinguistic explanations could raise consciousness of linguistic forms, which would in turn increase comprehension and aid the development of implicit knowledge ([VanPatten, 1993](#ch21.xhtml_ref21_57){#ch21.xhtml_R_ref21_57 .biblioref role="doc-biblioref"}). Thus, while it is no longer assumed that conscious noticing is requisite for acquisition, it could aid acquisition indirectly and may be especially beneficial for complex forms (see [DeKeyser & Prieto Botana, 2015](#ch21.xhtml_ref21_12){#ch21.xhtml_R_ref21_12 .biblioref role="doc-biblioref"}).

Similarly, researchers have long acknowledged that a form's salience could impact learners' ability to process forms and derive intake ([Carroll, 2006](#ch21.xhtml_ref21_8){#ch21.xhtml_R_ref21_8 .biblioref role="doc-biblioref"}). While *salience* is notoriously difficult to define, and there is no current consensus definition within SLA, current conceptualizations of salience include three broad types: perceptual salience, psychological salience, and constructed salience ([Gass et al., 2017](#ch21.xhtml_ref21_21){#ch21.xhtml_R_ref21_21 .biblioref role="doc-biblioref"}; [Sagarra & Ellis, 2013](#ch21.xhtml_ref21_50){#ch21.xhtml_R_ref21_50 .biblioref role="doc-biblioref"}). Perceptual salience refers to the ability to perceive forms as distinct from their surroundings and therefore relates primarily to phonological factors such as length in phones, syllabicity, stress, sonority, and intonation (see [Goldschneider & DeKeyser, 2001](#ch21.xhtml_ref21_22){#ch21.xhtml_R_ref21_22 .biblioref role="doc-biblioref"}). Psychological salience includes attentional bias (i.e., processing strategies or learned attention), and the effects of experience (e.g., surprisal and frequency effects). Constructed salience refers to whether a form has been made perceptually or psychologically salient through instruction (e.g., input enhancement). Just as EI could raise consciousness of a particular form, it is possible that EI "constructs" salience and makes otherwise non-salient forms more perceptible, more prominent, and more likely to be processed.[^33]

In instructed SLA, questions about EI's role in IP emerged primarily within the context of three distinct but overlapping pedagogical applications, each with their own theoretical underpinnings and research traditions: input enhancement ([Sharwood Smith, 1993](#ch21.xhtml_ref21_55){#ch21.xhtml_R_ref21_55 .biblioref role="doc-biblioref"}), processing instruction (PI) and structured input (SI; see Wong, this volume), and "task-essential" training (TET) or practice ([Loschkey & Bley-Vroman, 1993](#ch21.xhtml_ref21_39){#ch21.xhtml_R_ref21_39 .biblioref role="doc-biblioref"}). While these approaches and traditions have often occupied separate spaces in the literature, they all aim to push learners to allocate attention to a target form and/or change how they process input. Consequently, these approaches must be considered together. However, it is important to consider how these approaches intersect and diverge, especially for PI and TET, which can sometimes be quite similar, but may also be very different in scope and purpose.

As Wong (this volume) notes, PI[^34] and TET share the essential feature that learners must use a target form in order to complete a task, and this form is the focus of the learners' attention. Thus, PI and TET often employ forced-choice or picture-selection tasks that cannot be completed without attention to the target form. However, the theoretical and practical specification for PI is more well-defined than for TET. PI explicitly draws from VanPatten's input processing (IP) model (see Leeser, this volume) and its framework contains specific guidelines for trainings (see [Wong, 2004b](#ch21.xhtml_ref21_66){#ch21.xhtml_R_ref21_66 .biblioref role="doc-biblioref"}), including the provision that instruction aim to change processing strategies detailed in the IP model. TET, on the other hand encompasses a wider range of instructional methods that are influenced by various theoretical approaches and deviate from each other in aims and construction. Despite this, many contemporary uses of TET (e.g., [Dracos & Henry, 2018](#ch21.xhtml_ref21_13){#ch21.xhtml_R_ref21_13 .biblioref role="doc-biblioref"}; [Filgueras-Gomez, 2016](#ch21.xhtml_ref21_20){#ch21.xhtml_R_ref21_20 .biblioref role="doc-biblioref"}) adopt core guidelines of PI, such as structuring input to change processing behavior and designing tasks such that learners' answers show whether or not they connected the target form to its linguistic function or meaning. Moreover, the theoretical framing and core issues under investigation have often intersected and complemented each other. Thus, research on PI and TET often inform each other, but it is necessary to evaluate the characteristics of trainings independently.
:::

:::::: {.section aria-labelledby="sec21_3"}
## []{#ch21.xhtml_p304 .pagebreak aria-label=" page 304. " role="doc-pagebreak"}Critical Issues and Topics {#ch21.xhtml_sec21_3}

::: {.section aria-labelledby="sec21_3_1"}
### Is EI a Necessary Component of Training? {#ch21.xhtml_sec21_3_1}

The first systematic investigations of EI in the IP literature arose out of studies on PI and tested whether EI or practice was the causative variable during training. The earliest studies on PI (e.g., [VanPatten & Cadierno, 1993](#ch21.xhtml_ref21_58){#ch21.xhtml_R_ref21_58 .biblioref role="doc-biblioref"}) had compared the effects of PI with traditional instruction (TI), which, unlike PI, focuses on production (including mechanical production) of the target form. These studies found that the PI groups outperformed the TI groups on comprehension measures and equaled their gains on production (see Leeser, this volume for an overview). However, the PI and TI groups received different EI: while both groups received information about the form, the PI group also received information about processing strategies. Thus, it was unclear whether the differences between groups stemmed from the training or the EI.

[VanPatten and Oikkenon (1996)](#ch21.xhtml_ref21_60){#ch21.xhtml_R_ref21_60 .biblioref role="doc-biblioref"} investigated the effects of EI by partially replicating [VanPatten and Cadierno's (1993)](#ch21.xhtml_ref21_58){.biblioref role="doc-biblioref"} study on PI and Spanish clitic pronouns to isolate the effects of EI and SI from PI. They compared three groups. The SI group received only the SI activities, in which they had to use the pronouns to interpret SVO and OVS sentences and select a picture. A second group received only EI about the target form. The PI group received both SI and EI. They found that both the PI and SI-only groups scored significantly higher than the EI-only group on a sentence-level comprehension task with no differences between the PI and SI groups. On a fill-in-the-blank production task, all three groups improved from pretest to posttest. Further, on the posttest, the PI group scored higher than the EI only group, but not the SI-only group; the SI-only group was statistically similar to both the PI and EI-only groups. As the SI-only group was statistically equivalent to the PI group in both interpretation and production, VanPatten and Oikkenon concluded that EI plays a nonsignificant role in PI, and SI activities are the causative variable in PI.

Several studies have explored whether [VanPatten and Oikkenon's (1996)](#ch21.xhtml_ref21_60){.biblioref role="doc-biblioref"} findings generalize to case in German and Russian and to causative *faire* in French (e.g., [Henry et al., 2017](#ch21.xhtml_ref21_27){#ch21.xhtml_R_ref21_27 .biblioref role="doc-biblioref"}; [VanPatten et al., 2013](#ch21.xhtml_ref21_61){#ch21.xhtml_R_ref21_61 .biblioref role="doc-biblioref"}). Others have investigated whether EI is the causative variable in PI when the target form had a different processing problem (lexical preference) (Benati, [2004a](#ch21.xhtml_ref21_6){#ch21.xhtml_R_ref21_6 .biblioref role="doc-biblioref"}, [2004b](#ch21.xhtml_ref21_7){#ch21.xhtml_R_ref21_7 .biblioref role="doc-biblioref"}; [Farley, 2004](#ch21.xhtml_ref21_17){#ch21.xhtml_R_ref21_17 .biblioref role="doc-biblioref"}; [Wong, 2004a](#ch21.xhtml_ref21_65){#ch21.xhtml_R_ref21_65 .biblioref role="doc-biblioref"}), was more complex ([Farley, 2004](#ch21.xhtml_ref21_17){.biblioref role="doc-biblioref"}) or had less communicative value ([Benati, 2004a](#ch21.xhtml_ref21_6){.biblioref role="doc-biblioref"}; [Wong, 2004a](#ch21.xhtml_ref21_65){.biblioref role="doc-biblioref"}). [Sanz and Morgan-Short (2004)](#ch21.xhtml_ref21_53){#ch21.xhtml_R_ref21_53 .biblioref role="doc-biblioref"} investigated whether the effects of EI varied when encountered as feedback rather than as a pre-training presentation. These studies all demonstrated that SI training resulted in greater comprehension and production[^35] of the target forms. Thus, SI alone is sufficient to bring about significant gains in performance.

Parallel investigations on TET focused not only on the effects of EI presented before training, but also on the explicitness of feedback presented during the training. [Rosa (1999)](#ch21.xhtml_ref21_49){#ch21.xhtml_R_ref21_49 .biblioref role="doc-biblioref"}, for example, trained participants on Spanish past-tense, contrary-to-fact conditional sentences using a task-essential puzzle task. In this task, participants read a prompt sentence and matched other sentences to them using the verb form. Rosa tested whether the outcomes of training varied according to the presence of EI and the availability of metalinguistic information in feedback. Rosa found that groups that received either EI or metalinguistic feedback outperformed the group that completed the training without either. However, as [Sanz and Morgan-Short (2004)](#ch21.xhtml_ref21_53){.biblioref role="doc-biblioref"} point out, the group who did not receive EI or metalinguistic feedback also outperformed an EI only control; thus, there is reason to believe that neither EI, nor metalinguistic feedback are necessary. Subsequent studies using TET more similar to SI have also shown that training is sufficient to lead to changes in comprehension and production (e.g., [Dracos & Henry, 2018](#ch21.xhtml_ref21_13){.biblioref role="doc-biblioref"}; [Filgueras-Gomez, 2016](#ch21.xhtml_ref21_20){.biblioref role="doc-biblioref"}).

[]{#ch21.xhtml_p305 .pagebreak aria-label=" page 305. " role="doc-pagebreak"}In short, research has shown relatively consistently that EI is not necessary to cause improvement from pretest to posttest. However, multiple studies in both the PI and TET literature also show that EI may lead to an additional performance boost. For example, [Farley (2004)](#ch21.xhtml_ref21_17){.biblioref role="doc-biblioref"} and Russell (2012) both found an advantage for EI with a more complex form, the Spanish subjunctive. Studies on the German accusative case have found that EI leads to greater advantages when it is easy to utilize during training ([Henry et al., 2017](#ch21.xhtml_ref21_27){.biblioref role="doc-biblioref"}; [VanPatten et al., 2013](#ch21.xhtml_ref21_61){.biblioref role="doc-biblioref"}).[^36] In the TET literature, [Dracos & Henry (2018)](#ch21.xhtml_ref21_13){.biblioref role="doc-biblioref"} found an advantage for metalinguistic feedback over simple corrective feedback (i.e., "Correct"/"Incorrect") (see also [Lado et al., 2013](#ch21.xhtml_ref21_35){#ch21.xhtml_R_ref21_35 .biblioref role="doc-biblioref"}; c.f. [Filgueras-Gomez, 2016](#ch21.xhtml_ref21_20){.biblioref role="doc-biblioref"}). In two studies, [Prieto Botana (2013)](#ch21.xhtml_ref21_47){#ch21.xhtml_R_ref21_47 .biblioref role="doc-biblioref"} and [Prieto Botana & DeKeyser (2019)](#ch21.xhtml_ref21_48){#ch21.xhtml_R_ref21_48 .biblioref role="doc-biblioref"} found that EI led to greater gains when instruction was not task-essential. When training was task-essential, EI was expendable in all but one task, and for only one of the forms tested (production of Spanish object pronouns).

Thus, EI does lend some advantages to learners, though advantages are not always large, durable, or consistent. Even so, small and inconsistent advantages could be important to learners who may not have much exposure to the target forms.
:::

::: {.section aria-labelledby="sec21_3_2"}
### Does EI Help Learners Make Form-Connections Faster? {#ch21.xhtml_sec21_3_2}

Reflecting a trend in SLA research, research on EI in PI and TET has generally moved away from research designs that rely solely on pre-test and post-test measures. This shift was precipitated in part by [Farley's (2004)](#ch21.xhtml_ref21_17){.biblioref role="doc-biblioref"} and [Fernández's (2008)](#ch21.xhtml_ref21_18){#ch21.xhtml_R_ref21_18 .biblioref role="doc-biblioref"} observation that advantages from EI may not be apparent after training, but EI may speed up correct IP. Along with this observation was a new scoring measure that is now standard within PI research: *trials-to-criterion* (TTC). TTC refers to the number of items attempted before a participant begins to process sentences correctly during an SI training and thus provides a window into how learners perform the training itself irrespective of its eventual outcome (see [Fernández, 2021](#ch21.xhtml_ref21_19){#ch21.xhtml_R_ref21_19 .biblioref role="doc-biblioref"}; [Henry, 2023](#ch21.xhtml_ref21_1001){#ch21.xhtml_R_ref21_1001 .biblioref role="doc-biblioref"}). Following [Fernández (2008)](#ch21.xhtml_ref21_18){.biblioref role="doc-biblioref"}, then, research began to focus on whether EI increases the speed with which learners make initial form--meaning connections and begin to process forms correctly.

[Fernández (2008)](#ch21.xhtml_ref21_18){.biblioref role="doc-biblioref"} targets two different forms in Spanish, (1) OVS sentences with clitic pronouns and (2) the third person present subjunctive in expressions of doubt. The results indicated that, while EI did not provide an advantage for OVS sentences, participants who received EI reached criterion faster (i.e., had fewer TTC) when learning the subjunctive. Thus, EI appeared beneficial for one form (the subjunctive), but not for the other (OVS sentences). Fernández therefore concluded that EI can increase attention to some forms and promote the development of form--meaning connections. She further speculated that EI may have limited effects depending on the nature of the task or the processing problem associated with the target form. Two studies on OVS sentences and the accusative case in German, [Culman et al. (2009)](#ch21.xhtml_ref21_11){#ch21.xhtml_R_ref21_11 .biblioref role="doc-biblioref"} and [Henry et al. (2009)](#ch21.xhtml_ref21_26){#ch21.xhtml_R_ref21_26 .biblioref role="doc-biblioref"}, subsequently found that the provision of EI did lead learners to process OVS sentences correctly sooner. These studies lent further evidence that EI helps learners identify form--meaning connections sooner during SI practice, though they also cast doubt on Fernández's speculation that EI has differential effects based on the processing problem. Further research ([Henry et al., 2017](#ch21.xhtml_ref21_27){.biblioref role="doc-biblioref"}; [VanPatten et al., 2013](#ch21.xhtml_ref21_61){.biblioref role="doc-biblioref"}) has corroborated these results[^37] and suggested that EI may be particularly useful when the information is "portable", that is, easy to hold in working memory and utilize during the task.
:::

::: {.section aria-labelledby="sec21_3_3"}
### Does EI Lead to Qualitatively Different Types of Knowledge? {#ch21.xhtml_sec21_3_3}

As discussed previously, an ongoing discussion in SLA research concerns the degree to which acquisition is driven by implicit or explicit learning, whether different types of learning results []{#ch21.xhtml_p306 .pagebreak aria-label=" page 306. " role="doc-pagebreak"}in different types of knowledge, and whether explicit knowledge aids the acquisition of implicit knowledge ([VanPatten et al., 2020](#ch21.xhtml_ref21_62){.biblioref role="doc-biblioref"}). The central question for research on EI in instructional contexts is whether training with and without EI leads to qualitatively different kinds of learning or to qualitatively different representations.

One series of studies by Morgan-Short and colleagues ([Morgan-Short, Finger et al., 2012](#ch21.xhtml_ref21_44){#ch21.xhtml_R_ref21_44 .biblioref role="doc-biblioref"}; [Morgan-Short et al., 2010](#ch21.xhtml_ref21_43){#ch21.xhtml_R_ref21_43 .biblioref role="doc-biblioref"}; [Morgan-Short, Steinhauer et al., 2012](#ch21.xhtml_ref21_45){#ch21.xhtml_R_ref21_45 .biblioref role="doc-biblioref"}) explored whether implicit and explicit training conditions elicit ERP components consistent with reliance on declarative memory systems (i.e., the N400) or native-like control of linguistic information in procedural memory (i.e., a biphasic P600 and anterior negativity) (see [Morgan-Short & Tanner, 2014](#ch21.xhtml_ref21_42){#ch21.xhtml_R_ref21_42 .biblioref role="doc-biblioref"} for a review of the ERP method in L2 research). In these studies, learners were trained on an artificial language using a computer game and were tested on their sensitivity to gender-agreement (noun--article and noun--adjective) and word order violations. The explicitness of training was operationalized through EI delivered during training. Across these studies, results were generally consistent in showing that implicit and explicit training led to similar learning gains; however, the two trainings also led to qualitatively different types of processing: the implicit groups reliably showed native-like ERP components when trained to high proficiency, while the EI groups did not. These results suggest that learners trained with EI rely more heavily on explicit (declarative) knowledge and could take longer to develop native-like processing patterns.[^38]
:::
::::::

::::: {.section aria-labelledby="sec21_4"}
## Current Contributions and Research {#ch21.xhtml_sec21_4}

One major development in research on EI in input processing and instructional contexts is the gradual adoption of methodologies that provide more detail about processing behaviors. While it is tempting to view this development as purely methodological, the change is reflective of a gradual shift away from questions that focus on learning outcomes to those that focus on how EI affects attention and how individual differences interact with EI and properties of the target form. Both of these lines of inquiry have provided important theoretical contributions to our understanding of EI's role in input processing, but research in these areas is still emerging, and relatively few studies exist within each.

::: {.section aria-labelledby="sec21_4_1"}
### Does EI Affect Online Processing and Attentional Allocation? {#ch21.xhtml_sec21_4_1}

Over the past three decades, research in psycholinguistics has gradually adopted measures of language processing that allow researchers to measure which types of information learners rely on and how processing unfolds in real-time. These include both interpretative tasks, such as "cue tasks" and online tasks such as self-paced reading (SPR), eye-tracking, and event-related potentials (ERP). SPR and eye-tracking provide detailed information about the time-course of processing, while ERP also includes information about the language systems used to process linguistic forms (see Jegerski, this volume; [VanPatten & Jegerski, 2014](#ch21.xhtml_ref21_59){#ch21.xhtml_R_ref21_59 .biblioref role="doc-biblioref"}). These methods have become critical to understanding how training affects processing because data from such tasks (1) are less likely to be influenced by explicit monitoring processes, (2) provide a measure of automatized language use, and (3) provide more direct evidence of attentional allocation (see [Henry, 2022](#ch21.xhtml_ref21_24){#ch21.xhtml_R_ref21_24 .biblioref role="doc-biblioref"}; [Issa et al., 2015](#ch21.xhtml_ref21_31){#ch21.xhtml_R_ref21_31 .biblioref role="doc-biblioref"}; [Lee & Doherty, 2018](#ch21.xhtml_ref21_37){#ch21.xhtml_R_ref21_37 .biblioref role="doc-biblioref"}). Thus, studies using these measures are important to understanding whether EI affects language processing, the creation of intake, and the development of implicit knowledge.

While there are still relatively few studies that take such an approach, the literature has expanded rapidly in the past few years. A recent study by [Dracos and Henry (2018)](#ch21.xhtml_ref21_13){.biblioref role="doc-biblioref"}, for example, used an interpretative measure, a so-called "cue-task", which is popular in psycholinguistic []{#ch21.xhtml_p307 .pagebreak aria-label=" page 307. " role="doc-pagebreak"}studies such as those on learned attention (N. C. [Ellis & Sagarra, 2010](#ch21.xhtml_ref21_15){#ch21.xhtml_R_ref21_15 .biblioref role="doc-biblioref"}). In this task, participants process sentences in which different types of linguistic cues present conflicting information, (e.g., \*"**Next month** I visit**ed** the schools"). They then answer a simple question intended to reveal which cue they relied on for interpretation (e.g., When did the action occur?). Dracos and Henry used this task to investigate how metalinguistic feedback (i.e., EI) and simple corrective feedback ("Correct"/"Incorrect") affected learners' attention to verb endings in Spanish, which carry both subject and tense information. Participants were trained using a forced-choice task inspired by SI. Although they found that training increased the rate at which participants attended to verbal information, and that metalinguistic feedback led to descriptive advantages over simple corrective feedback, there were no significant differences between these two groups.

Another study by [Wong and Ito (2018)](#ch21.xhtml_ref21_67){#ch21.xhtml_R_ref21_67 .biblioref role="doc-biblioref"} tested whether EI affected the outcomes of PI and traditional instruction (TI) on the acquisition of OVS word order and the *faire* causative in French. In this study, Wong and Ito used an eye-tracking task, in which participants listened to sentences and selected a matching picture. The task was administered before and after training in order to determine whether training influenced the participants' ability to process cues to sentence structure incrementally (i.e., as the sentence unfolded). The results showed that the PI group was more accurate than the TI group. The PI group was also faster to overcome their subject-first preference and adopt the accurate object-first interpretation of sentences. Further, although EI resulted in higher accuracy regardless of training, EI only influenced eye-movements for the TI group. This suggests that SI activities are sufficient to change processing behaviors, but that EI could be beneficial in TI, which lacks task-essential practice. Finally, [Indrarathne & Kormos (2017)](#ch21.xhtml_ref21_28){#ch21.xhtml_R_ref21_28 .biblioref role="doc-biblioref"} used eye-tracking to test the effects of EI in conjunction with textual input enhancement on acquisition of the "causative had" construction. In contrast to Wong and Ito's study, however, eye-tracking was used not to investigate incremental processing before and after training, but to track learner attention on the target forms as they were read. They found that, overall, the group that received EI had the largest learning gains and the largest correlation between learning gains and eye-tracking measures. Thus, they concluded that EI efficiently directs learner attention to targeted constructions and in doing so increases learning gains.
:::

::: {.section aria-labelledby="sec21_4_2"}
### Do Individual Differences Modulate the Effects of EI? {#ch21.xhtml_sec21_4_2}

To date, research has not yet fully considered how the effects of EI are modulated by learner factors such as age, aptitude, or working memory ([DeKeyser & Prieto Botana, 2015](#ch21.xhtml_ref21_12){.biblioref role="doc-biblioref"}). A handful of studies have explored these issues, though researchers have not yet converged on any single factor of importance.

Following calls to diversify the populations tested in SLA and PI research ([Cox, 2019](#ch21.xhtml_ref21_9){#ch21.xhtml_R_ref21_9 .biblioref role="doc-biblioref"}; [Lee & Benati, 2013](#ch21.xhtml_ref21_36){#ch21.xhtml_R_ref21_36 .biblioref role="doc-biblioref"}), several studies have considered the interaction of age and EI in training. For example, [Lenet et al. (2011)](#ch21.xhtml_ref21_38){#ch21.xhtml_R_ref21_38 .biblioref role="doc-biblioref"}, tested the effects of TET among L2 learners of Latin between 18--21 and 60--80 years old. Learners were assigned to two groups, a less explicit group that received simple corrective feedback, and an explicit group that received metalinguistic feedback. Contrary to previous studies, which tend to show either no effect or an advantage for EI, Lenet et al. found that the older group of learners were disadvantaged when EI was included. In a similar study, [Cox and Sanz (2015)](#ch21.xhtml_ref21_10){#ch21.xhtml_R_ref21_10 .biblioref role="doc-biblioref"} tested age effects but used pre-training EI rather than feedback as Lenet et al. had done. While they found that older learners were disadvantaged directly after the presentation of EI, practice leveled out these differences. Thus, Cox and Sanz concluded that practice is more important to development than EI and that EI may be more effective for older learners if it is not entangled with practice, giving them time to process the information.

[]{#ch21.xhtml_p308 .pagebreak aria-label=" page 308. " role="doc-pagebreak"}The most comprehensive set of studies on EI and language aptitude was conducted by VanPatten and colleagues ([VanPatten et al., 2013](#ch21.xhtml_ref21_61){.biblioref role="doc-biblioref"}). In a series of experiments, they investigated whether the outcomes of PI varied according to the presence of EI and learners' grammatical sensitivity, a subcomponent of language aptitude which involves a learner's ability to recognize grammatical functions and relationships between words. Trainings focused on forms in four languages---Spanish, German, French, and Russian---which were related through their processing problem, the first-noun principle. For Spanish clitic pronouns and Russian case markers, VanPatten et al. found that the provision of EI did not significantly influence the outcome of training, nor did it lead to benefits during training (as measured by trials-to-criterion). For German case markers and the French *faire* causative, they found no advantages for EI after training, but EI did lead learners to process sentences sooner during training. Most importantly to the present discussion, performance was not correlated with grammatical sensitivity on any of the posttest measures. Moreover, grammatical sensitivity only correlated with performance during training in one group: German learners who received EI. VanPatten et al. treated this result as anomalous given that other findings rendered their possible explanations untenable, but this result needs to be explored by future research. According to VanPatten et al., this pattern of results signifies that PI does not rely on the same type of learning measured by the construct of grammatical sensitivity, namely rule internalization; rather, it is focused on processing form and meaning together, which is not related to traditional aptitude measures.

Another line of individual-difference research investigates the effects of working memory (WM), which involves the temporary control and regulation of task-relevant information while performing cognitive tasks ([Williams, 2012](#ch21.xhtml_ref21_64){#ch21.xhtml_R_ref21_64 .biblioref role="doc-biblioref"}). Because many approaches to SLA---including models of IP (see Leeser, this volume)---assume that learners have limited cognitive resources with which to process input, WM research has long been included in studies on L2 learning (e.g., [Harrington & Sawyer, 1992](#ch21.xhtml_ref21_23){#ch21.xhtml_R_ref21_23 .biblioref role="doc-biblioref"}; [Miyake & Friedman, 1998](#ch21.xhtml_ref21_41){#ch21.xhtml_R_ref21_41 .biblioref role="doc-biblioref"}) and processing (e.g., [Juffs, 2004](#ch21.xhtml_ref21_32){#ch21.xhtml_R_ref21_32 .biblioref role="doc-biblioref"}; [Juffs & Harrington, 2011](#ch21.xhtml_ref21_33){#ch21.xhtml_R_ref21_33 .biblioref role="doc-biblioref"}). Studies on the relationship between WM and input processing in the instructional context are still relatively limited but have grown over the last few years ([Issa, 2019](#ch21.xhtml_ref21_30){#ch21.xhtml_R_ref21_30 .biblioref role="doc-biblioref"}; [Santamaria & Sunderman, 2015](#ch21.xhtml_ref21_51){#ch21.xhtml_R_ref21_51 .biblioref role="doc-biblioref"}; [Villegas & Morgan-Short, 2019](#ch21.xhtml_ref21_63){#ch21.xhtml_R_ref21_63 .biblioref role="doc-biblioref"}). Only a select few of these have included both WM and EI as variables in the research design, even though previous research has argued that WM could mediate the use of EI during training ([Fernández, 2008](#ch21.xhtml_ref21_18){.biblioref role="doc-biblioref"}; [Henry et al., 2009](#ch21.xhtml_ref21_26){.biblioref role="doc-biblioref"}). One study on textual input enhancement, [Indrarathne and Kormos (2018)](#ch21.xhtml_ref21_29){#ch21.xhtml_R_ref21_29 .biblioref role="doc-biblioref"}, focused on how WM mediated learning outcomes and attention to English *causative had* constructions. They found that higher WM was most closely associated with production and attention to the form when training included EI. In another study, [Dracos & Henry (2021)](#ch21.xhtml_ref21_14){#ch21.xhtml_R_ref21_14 .biblioref role="doc-biblioref"} explored the role of WM and the provision of EI (metalinguistic feedback) in TET focused on Spanish subject and tense morphology (although EI was not the focus of their study). Results suggested that WM is generally associated with morphological learning and online processing; however, WM played no role in mediating the effects of training regardless of whether EI was provided in feedback.
:::
:::::

::: {.section aria-labelledby="sec21_5"}
## Main Research Methods {#ch21.xhtml_sec21_5}

Research in this area has primarily used pretest--posttest designs to measure the effects of training with and without EI. Historically, these designs used traditional pencil-and-paper tests with comprehension and production tasks. The scope of these tasks can vary considerably between studies; although comprehension tasks tend to focus on sentence-level interpretation ([VanPatten & Oikkenon, 1996](#ch21.xhtml_ref21_60){.biblioref role="doc-biblioref"}; [Filgueras-Gomez, 2016](#ch21.xhtml_ref21_20){.biblioref role="doc-biblioref"}), production tasks range from single-word production in sentence context ([Dracos & Henry, 2018](#ch21.xhtml_ref21_13){.biblioref role="doc-biblioref"}; [Prieto Botana & DeKeyser, 2019](#ch21.xhtml_ref21_48){.biblioref role="doc-biblioref"}), to connected []{#ch21.xhtml_p309 .pagebreak aria-label=" page 309. " role="doc-pagebreak"}sentence-level production ([Henry et al., 2017](#ch21.xhtml_ref21_27){.biblioref role="doc-biblioref"}), to discourse-level narration tasks ([Sanz, 2004](#ch21.xhtml_ref21_52){#ch21.xhtml_R_ref21_52 .biblioref role="doc-biblioref"}). Less often, studies include grammatical judgement tasks ([Lado et al., 2013](#ch21.xhtml_ref21_35){.biblioref role="doc-biblioref"}; [Lenet et al., 2011](#ch21.xhtml_ref21_38){.biblioref role="doc-biblioref"}; [Indrarathne & Kormos, 2017](#ch21.xhtml_ref21_28){.biblioref role="doc-biblioref"}). Researchers also commonly use computerized versions of these tests and computer-mediated trainings ([Sanz & Morgan-Short, 2004](#ch21.xhtml_ref21_53){.biblioref role="doc-biblioref"}), allowing them to gather data on training (e.g., trials-to-criterion; [Fernández, 2008](#ch21.xhtml_ref21_18){.biblioref role="doc-biblioref"}) and reaction times. As discussed previously, studies have recently adopted psycholinguistic measures, such as SPR, eye-tracking, and ERP. Regardless of the assessment method, researchers must be careful to guard against explicit monitoring, especially because this may bias results in favor of groups that receive EI.
:::

::: {.section aria-labelledby="sec21_6"}
## Recommendations for Practice {#ch21.xhtml_sec21_6}

Instructional training that emphasizes comprehension and accurate processing of L2 input---whether PI, TET, or input enhancement---can be used in a wide variety of instructional contexts and with many different types of learners. As Wong (this volume) discusses, "the decision to include EI \[in Processing Instruction\] or not should be based on specific learning contexts" (p. 296). Teachers therefore need to consider numerous factors, including the age, proficiency, and learning history of their learners, the mode of instruction, whether instruction is self-guided (as in flipped classrooms), and the ultimate goals of instruction (e.g., "is learning the 'rule' an important outcome of training?").

To these contextual considerations, the implementation of EI may depend on the form targeted by training. While research is mostly clear that EI is not a necessary component of training ([Sanz & Morgan-Short, 2004](#ch21.xhtml_ref21_53){.biblioref role="doc-biblioref"}; [VanPatten & Oikkenon, 1996](#ch21.xhtml_ref21_60){.biblioref role="doc-biblioref"}), some forms seem particularly difficult to process correctly without EI (e.g., German accusative case; Henry et al., [2009](#ch21.xhtml_ref21_26){.biblioref role="doc-biblioref"}, [2017](#ch21.xhtml_ref21_27){.biblioref role="doc-biblioref"}). Learners may also receive an extra boost from EI, particularly when forms are complex (e.g., for the Spanish subjunctive; see [Farley 2004](#ch21.xhtml_ref21_17){.biblioref role="doc-biblioref"}; [Fernández, 2008](#ch21.xhtml_ref21_18){.biblioref role="doc-biblioref"}). On the other hand, learners may have difficulty using EI if it is too complex to be used during practice ([Fernández, 2008](#ch21.xhtml_ref21_18){.biblioref role="doc-biblioref"}; [Henry et al., 2009](#ch21.xhtml_ref21_26){.biblioref role="doc-biblioref"}). Thus, EI should ideally focus only on those forms and functions directly related to the task.

Decisions about the delivery of EI rest on numerous factors that practitioners must balance with respect to the learning context and the goals of instruction. For example, practitioners may choose to spend less time on EI if the activity will be completed with instructor guidance rather than as homework. Similarly, practitioners might consider how to sequence EI and whether to present it prior to training, or as metalinguistic feedback. As a third option, teachers might consider withholding EI at the beginning of training but presenting it in the middle of training, which could balance potential benefits of more implicit instruction (see Morgan-Short et. al., 2004) against benefits of EI and better account for students' diverse needs. As an added activity, students could even take an active role in devising the rule after a prescribed amount of time.

These sorts of practical considerations will necessarily interact with learner factors. For instance, older adults may need more time to process EI ([Cox & Sanz, 2015](#ch21.xhtml_ref21_10){.biblioref role="doc-biblioref"}). Thus, EI might be more effective if presented before practice or in a self-guided training that provides the opportunity to review the EI. While some individual differences, such as language aptitude or working memory do not seem to meaningfully affect the outcomes of training with or without EI ([Indrarathne & Kormos, 2018](#ch21.xhtml_ref21_29){.biblioref role="doc-biblioref"}; [VanPatten et al., 2013](#ch21.xhtml_ref21_61){.biblioref role="doc-biblioref"}), research on individual differences is still developing, and new research will no doubt shed new light on which differences are most important to consider. Finally, one should not overlook learner affect as an important consideration, as EI may help some learners avoid frustration that could discourage active attention to the task.
:::

::: {.section aria-labelledby="sec21_7"}
## []{#ch21.xhtml_p310 .pagebreak aria-label=" page 310. " role="doc-pagebreak"}Future Directions {#ch21.xhtml_sec21_7}

The future of research on EI will likely be shaped by current trends for some time, focusing on (1) EI's role in promoting attention to and processing of different forms, and (2) how individual differences mediate the effects of EI. There are two primary reasons for this.

First, there are still relatively few studies that use psycholinguistic methods (i.e., self-paced reading, eye-tracking, and ERP) to investigate how EI influences attention and processing. More research on this topic is therefore needed, especially as previous research has found differences in outcomes depending on which forms are targeted, how instruction is implemented, and how EI is operationalized. In this respect, future research will need to utilize online methods while controlling other methodological variables (e.g., type or length of instruction) so that the differences between target language, forms, and instructional materials are clearer. Multi-site replication research such as that in [Morgan-Short et al. (2018)](#ch21.xhtml_ref21_46){#ch21.xhtml_R_ref21_46 .biblioref role="doc-biblioref"}, for example, would help move such an agenda forward constructively.

Secondly, research on individual differences and other mediating factors will necessarily split the research literature into several smaller slices. As [DeKeyser and Prieto Botana (2015)](#ch21.xhtml_ref21_12){.biblioref role="doc-biblioref"} point out, research in PI especially has traditionally been remarkably consistent in that it has tested a relatively small variety of structures, closely followed the research design of seminal studies (e.g., [Fernández, 2008](#ch21.xhtml_ref21_18){.biblioref role="doc-biblioref"}; [VanPatten & Oikkenon, 1996](#ch21.xhtml_ref21_60){.biblioref role="doc-biblioref"}), and tested young adult learners (college aged students) from WEIRD (western, educated, industrialized, rich, and democratic) populations. Current trends have seen this uniformity break down, partly because research has not concentrated on a single individual difference. This bifurcation will present the field with challenges, especially because research on individual differences can be methodologically difficult, and there can be little consensus even after decades of research. Yet, these new strains of research will ultimately help researchers clarify outstanding issues in the field, for example, which internal and external factors make EI "portable" enough to be used during training.

Aside from research on attention, online processing, and individual differences, research will need to address several open lines of inquiry. One issue is that EI research has typically regarded *the provision of EI* as the variable under investigation, not *the understanding of EI*. That is, research to date has typically not tested whether participants understood the EI that they were given and has assumed that the provided EI was sufficient for understanding the target forms ([DeKeyser & Prieto Botana, 2015](#ch21.xhtml_ref21_12){.biblioref role="doc-biblioref"}). While this is perhaps the more ecologically valid approach for research on classroom methods, it is less informative for debates about the interface between explicit and implicit information because researchers have not controlled for incomplete learning of EI (c.f. [Marsden & Chen, 2011](#ch21.xhtml_ref21_40){#ch21.xhtml_R_ref21_40 .biblioref role="doc-biblioref"}; [Prieto Botana, 2013](#ch21.xhtml_ref21_47){.biblioref role="doc-biblioref"}). Related to this issue is whether the benefits of EI (when they occur) are an effect of explicit knowledge or are related to more general psychological principles, for example, the effects of salience. To illustrate, consider a study by [Henry et al. (2017)](#ch21.xhtml_ref21_27){.biblioref role="doc-biblioref"}. Henry et al. found an advantage for training with EI, but the provision of prosodic cues washed out the effects of EI on the posttests (note that EI still lent an advantage during training). This raises questions as to whether or not the primary advantage from EI is in the explicit knowledge about the language or in the increased salience of the form. Similarly, researchers might ask how EI about processing strategies relates to inhibitory processes such as cue blocking ([Henry, 2021](#ch21.xhtml_ref21_25){#ch21.xhtml_R_ref21_25 .biblioref role="doc-biblioref"}).

Taken together, these lines of inquiry reflect the need to further understand the circumstances under which EI is effective and to relate these to more general principles of learning and acquisition. As noted above, this work is already underway in many respects, but it will likely take many years of dedicated work to fully explore these topics.
:::

::: {.section .conclusion role="doc-conclusion"}
## []{#ch21.xhtml_p311 .pagebreak aria-label=" page 311. " role="doc-pagebreak"}Conclusions {#ch21.xhtml_sec21_8}

As a whole, research on L2 instruction and the role of EI in input processing has had a long tradition and contributed much to both SLA theory and practice. First and foremost, this research has demonstrated the advantages and limitations of EI for L2 acquisition. While researchers continue to investigate factors that mediate its effects, it is clear that instruction without EI can be quite effective and lead learners to develop implicit knowledge. Even so, the literature clearly demonstrates that EI can help learners process forms in the input sooner. Research will continue to unravel the underlying causes for these findings, such as individual differences, incomplete understanding of EI, "portability", and other, yet unknown factors. These issues are not only important to researchers, but to practitioners who are concerned with optimized implementation of pedagogical methods. This research therefore represents a case in which sound theoretical investigations can be conducted in an ecologically valid manner, providing opportunities for researchers and practitioners to learn from each other.
:::

::: {.endnotes role="doc-endnotes"}
## Notes {#ch21.xhtml_h2}
