[]{#ch22.xhtml}

::: {}
# []{#ch22.xhtml_p315 .pagebreak aria-label=" page 315. " role="doc-pagebreak"}[22 ]{.chnum .ordinal}[Comprehensible Input in Language Instruction ]{.chtitle}[Approaches and Techniques]{.chsubtitle .subtitle role="doc-subtitle"} {#ch22.xhtml_h1}

Philippa Bell and Caroline Payant

Université du Québec à Montréal

DOI: [10.4324/9781003041412-28](https://dx.doi.org/10.4324/9781003041412-28){aria-label="D.O.I. link to this document."}
:::

::: {.section .introduction role="doc-introduction"}
## Introduction and Definitions {#ch22.xhtml_sec22_1}

This chapter discusses the role and importance of comprehensible input (CI) in additional language (AL) instructional approaches and techniques. We begin by distinguishing CI and input. Input[^39] is any language a person hears or reads in any context (instructed and natural) regardless of its length (words, sentences, paragraphs, etc.) or whether the input is understood. However, not all input is equal. Complex language from sources such as academic articles would not be useful input in terms of its accessibility for a beginner learner. The notion of CI is key here -- input whose overall meaning (message) is comprehended by the learner ([Krashen, 1982](#ch22.xhtml_ref22_36){#ch22.xhtml_R_ref22_36 .biblioref role="doc-biblioref"}) and that is being processed by the learner in a communicative context with a communicative purpose ([VanPatten et al., 2020](#ch22.xhtml_ref22_72){#ch22.xhtml_R_ref22_72 .biblioref role="doc-biblioref"}). In other words, the kind of input that is necessary for language acquisition minimally has two essential characteristics: (1) it is comprehensible, and (2) it has a communicative purpose. For example, a teacher may ask learners in a classroom to take their textbooks out by saying: "Okay, it's time to get to work. Please take out your textbook and have a pencil and eraser ready." This speech has a communicative purpose as the teacher wants students to get organized to start working. Upon hearing this statement, students should be organizing their textbook, pencil, and eraser. However, if students stare blankly at the instructor and do not react, then the input was likely not comprehensible even though it has a communicative purpose. If the instructor said, "Rachid has a textbook, pencil, and an eraser" and the students understood the statement, the input is comprehensible but there is no communicative purpose (unless the teacher's goal was to indicate that the other students had not taken out their materials), so while "comprehensible," this is not the kind of input that is optimal for acquisition. [VanPatten (2010)](#ch22.xhtml_ref22_70){#ch22.xhtml_R_ref22_70 .biblioref role="doc-biblioref"} underscores that input for learning "is not a sample of language to illustrate how language works" (p. 4). We use the term CI to refer to input that is comprehensible (in the sense that learners can understand its overall meaning) and has a communicative purpose (i.e., there is a communicative reason for the learners to attend to the input; they are not simply learning language). CI (or input for acquisition) can be aural, written, gestural, or tactile ([Barcroft & Wong, 2013](#ch22.xhtml_ref22_7){#ch22.xhtml_R_ref22_7 .biblioref role="doc-biblioref"}).
:::

::: {.section aria-labelledby="sec22_2"}
## []{#ch22.xhtml_p316 .pagebreak aria-label=" page 316. " role="doc-pagebreak"}Historical Perspectives {#ch22.xhtml_sec22_2}

Prior to the 1960s, while AL instructional approaches included input (i.e., the target language was used part or most of the time), most of this input could not be classified as CI as used in this chapter. For example, methods based on structural linguistics and behaviorist principles of learning (e.g., the Audio-Lingual Method, *méthode SGAV -- structure-globale audio-visuel*) used structured oral dialogues that did not need to be processed for meaning and had no communicative intent -- learners memorized the dialogues to induce syntactic patterns ([Germain, 1993](#ch22.xhtml_ref22_23){#ch22.xhtml_R_ref22_23 .biblioref role="doc-biblioref"}; [Larsen-Freeman & Anderson, 2011](#ch22.xhtml_ref22_39){#ch22.xhtml_R_ref22_39 .biblioref role="doc-biblioref"}).

During the 1960s and 1970s, often identified as the onset of contemporary second language acquisition (SLA) ([Gass et al., 1998](#ch22.xhtml_ref22_21){#ch22.xhtml_R_ref22_21 .biblioref role="doc-biblioref"}), ideas flourished with regards to the role of input for language acquisition ([Corder, 1967](#ch22.xhtml_ref22_12){#ch22.xhtml_R_ref22_12 .biblioref role="doc-biblioref"}; [Hatch, 1972](#ch22.xhtml_ref22_28){#ch22.xhtml_R_ref22_28 .biblioref role="doc-biblioref"}). The importance of input is frequently attributed to the seminal work of Krashen in the input hypothesis (part of the monitor model) ([1982](#ch22.xhtml_ref22_36){.biblioref role="doc-biblioref"}) which states that acquisition occurs "by understanding language that contains structure a bit beyond our current level of competence (*i +* 1)...with the help of context or extra-linguistic information" (p. 21, [1982](#ch22.xhtml_ref22_36){.biblioref role="doc-biblioref"}). Because Krashen was theorizing during a time when classroom input tended to be used to highlight grammatical structures, he emphasized the importance of not offering an *i +* 1 grammatical syllabus; *i +* 1 exists naturally when language is used for meaning (communicative intent) and is understood by learners for its overall message. Thus, CI is language to which learners are exposed that they can understand with "understand" to mean that the learner is focused on the communicative content, not the formal properties of the message ([Krashen, 1982](#ch22.xhtml_ref22_36){.biblioref role="doc-biblioref"}).

Based on [Dulay and Burt's (1977)](#ch22.xhtml_ref22_14){#ch22.xhtml_R_ref22_14 .biblioref role="doc-biblioref"} proposition of an affective filter ([Krashen & Terrell, 1983](#ch22.xhtml_ref22_38){#ch22.xhtml_R_ref22_38 .biblioref role="doc-biblioref"}), Krashen also hypothesized that affective factors, such as motivation and anxiety, play a role in acquisition. Thus, students with positive affect will seek out more input and thus will have access to more CI. In turn, due to their "lower affective filter" ([Krashen & Terrell, 1983](#ch22.xhtml_ref22_38){.biblioref role="doc-biblioref"}, p. 38), they will also be able to attend to this input for acquisition whereas learners with higher anxiety will do so to a lesser degree. This metaphor nicely demonstrates the individual nature of acquisition.

The impact of Krashen's work on the field of SLA is unquestionable. Despite criticisms concerning the testability of certain claims, it is largely due to Krashen that input became an important construct in SLA research and language teaching.
:::

:::::::::::::::: {.section aria-labelledby="sec22_3"}
## Critical Issues and Topics {#ch22.xhtml_sec22_3}

::: {.section aria-labelledby="sec22_3_1"}
### Why Is CI Critical and How Do Learners Extract Data from Input? {#ch22.xhtml_sec22_3_1}

Regardless of the theoretical framework in which researchers work, there is no dispute that CI is critical for SLA ([Gass, 1997](#ch22.xhtml_ref22_20){#ch22.xhtml_R_ref22_20 .biblioref role="doc-biblioref"}) because input starts the process of acquisition. Each time a learner is exposed to CI, they are exposed to a variety of language features that can potentially be processed to become part of their linguistic system. When the teacher says, "please take out your crayons," learners are provided with key linguistic information concerning, for example, the imperative mood, adverb syntax rules (for requests), possessive determiner placement and formation with regards to singular and plural nouns. As this is oral input, they are also provided with phonetic, phonemic, and phonological cues that could be processed (e.g., after long vowels, /s/ is pronounced /z/). For any of these features to be processed for acquisition, they must be attended to. The quantity and type of attention needed to process input has been a matter of debate starting with [Schmidt's (1990)](#ch22.xhtml_ref22_60){#ch22.xhtml_R_ref22_60 .biblioref role="doc-biblioref"} noticing hypothesis.

Today, it is generally accepted that linguistic features that receive no attention are unlikely to become intake, namely information that is useable for language development. The conversion of []{#ch22.xhtml_p317 .pagebreak aria-label=" page 317. " role="doc-pagebreak"}available input to intake occurs when the learner makes a connection between a form at the surface level of the input and its meaning ([VanPatten, 1996](#ch22.xhtml_ref22_69){#ch22.xhtml_R_ref22_69 .biblioref role="doc-biblioref"}). Importantly, it is insufficient to merely attend to a form in the input. As underscored by VanPatten, noticing a form is not synonymous with processing a form. Learners may notice that there is an -ed at the end of the verb played but not understand the meaning that this form encodes. If, however, learners make the connection that -ed encodes the past, then the form is processed, a form--meaning connection is made, and intake is delivered to the developing system. Therefore, processing CI may be viewed as a gateway to SLA as it makes further processing possible to impact language development.

We turn now to a discussion of the role CI plays in different language teaching methods. This is divided into two sections. Firstly, meaning-oriented instructional approaches are presented. Here, approach refers to the fact that these are general teaching principles or guidelines (which may evolve or be adapted) that are grounded in SLA theory and research ([Long, 2015](#ch22.xhtml_ref22_46){#ch22.xhtml_R_ref22_46 .biblioref role="doc-biblioref"}). Secondly, teaching techniques centralizing CI for AL learning are discussed. Here, technique is used to refer to a specific instructional practice that could be used in a classroom and could form part of a broader teaching methodology. These techniques may or may not be driven by SLA research and theory.
:::

:::::: {.section aria-labelledby="sec22_3_2"}
### What Is the Role of CI in Meaning-Oriented Instructional Approaches? {#ch22.xhtml_sec22_3_2}

Today, it is widely accepted that teaching approaches should centralize language use to convey meaning rather than attending to language mainly for its form ([Ellis et al., 2019](#ch22.xhtml_ref22_16){#ch22.xhtml_R_ref22_16 .biblioref role="doc-biblioref"}; [Loewen & Sato, 2017](#ch22.xhtml_ref22_44){#ch22.xhtml_R_ref22_44 .biblioref role="doc-biblioref"}). In what follows, we discuss the role of CI in three well-known meaning-focused teaching approaches: communicative language teaching (CLT), task-based language teaching (TBLT) and content-based instruction (CBI).

::: {.section aria-labelledby="sec22_3_2_1"}
#### Communicative Language Teaching {#ch22.xhtml_sec22_3_2_1}

CLT is a teaching approach that revolutionized how we think about AL teaching and learning. Despite previous teaching methods, such as the direct method or the audiolingual method, purporting to develop oral communication ([Germain, 1993](#ch22.xhtml_ref22_23){.biblioref role="doc-biblioref"}), in practice, they centralized the development of linguistic competence, understood at the time as a student's ability to manipulate and understand language structure. In the 1970s, the concept of communication shifted as researchers focused on broader concepts than linguistic structure -- language functions ([Wilkins, 1976](#ch22.xhtml_ref22_80){#ch22.xhtml_R_ref22_80 .biblioref role="doc-biblioref"}), the social aspect of language ([Halliday, 1973](#ch22.xhtml_ref22_24){#ch22.xhtml_R_ref22_24 .biblioref role="doc-biblioref"}), and the distinction between knowing about language and being able to use it ([Widdowson, 1978](#ch22.xhtml_ref22_79){#ch22.xhtml_R_ref22_79 .biblioref role="doc-biblioref"}). This led to the introduction of the term communicative competence ([Hymes, 1971](#ch22.xhtml_ref22_33){#ch22.xhtml_R_ref22_33 .biblioref role="doc-biblioref"}), which included a focus on the social aspect of language -- "know(ing) when and how to say what to whom" ([Larsen-Freeman & Anderson, 2011](#ch22.xhtml_ref22_39){.biblioref role="doc-biblioref"}, p. 115). CLT thus shifted the goal of language teaching from the development of linguistic knowledge using structurally based formal approaches to the development of language use in context.

Defining CLT is complex as it can be, and has been, interpreted in a multitude of ways. Indeed, in 1993, Krashen and Terrell wrote about communicative approacheS and made reference to, for example, the silent way, Asher's total physical response, community language learning, and Lozanov's suggestopedia whilst introducing their communicative approach, the natural approach. Furthermore, research has demonstrated that how teachers understand and apply CLT varies widely ([Mason & Payant, 2019](#ch22.xhtml_ref22_51){#ch22.xhtml_R_ref22_51 .biblioref role="doc-biblioref"}).

Overall, in a communicative classroom, the aim is to engage learners, so they become users of the target language through processing CI and producing language in teacher--learner and learner--learner interactions. Input should be authentic and available in communicative contexts []{#ch22.xhtml_p318 .pagebreak aria-label=" page 318. " role="doc-pagebreak"}because this should permit exposure to CI that can be processed for acquisition (i.e., the creation of intake from CI). Today, this idea of authenticity is sometimes questioned because it has been interpreted by some to mean that the input must come from sources produced for first language (L1) speakers ([Waters, 2009](#ch22.xhtml_ref22_76){#ch22.xhtml_R_ref22_76 .biblioref role="doc-biblioref"}). Others point out that exclusively using input intended for L1 speakers may have the negative effect of providing students with texts that can be beyond their proficiency level, and this in turn would restrict the amount of CI they can process ([Badger & MacDonald, 2010](#ch22.xhtml_ref22_5){#ch22.xhtml_R_ref22_5 .biblioref role="doc-biblioref"}; [Waters, 2009](#ch22.xhtml_ref22_76){.biblioref role="doc-biblioref"}). The question of whether instructors should simplify/modify authentic input or not for language learning purposes is not new (e.g., [Horst et al., 1998](#ch22.xhtml_ref22_31){#ch22.xhtml_R_ref22_31 .biblioref role="doc-biblioref"}; [Long, 1983](#ch22.xhtml_ref22_45){#ch22.xhtml_R_ref22_45 .biblioref role="doc-biblioref"}). Regardless of the type of input L2 instructors choose to use (authentic or tailored to the level of language learners), activities are considered communicative if learners are placed in a situation in which information needs to be exchanged/shared. In other words, learners are given opportunities to interpret, express, and negotiate meaning in a purposeful way.
:::

::: {.section aria-labelledby="sec22_3_2_2"}
#### Task-Based Language Teaching {#ch22.xhtml_sec22_3_2_2}

An important approach in North America today is task-based language teaching (TBLT), which is usually considered to be an offshoot of CLT ([Ellis, 2017](#ch22.xhtml_ref22_15){#ch22.xhtml_R_ref22_15 .biblioref role="doc-biblioref"}) and overlaps with the action-oriented approach (*approche actionnelle*) of the Common European Framework (see [Payant & Michaud, 2020](#ch22.xhtml_ref22_54){#ch22.xhtml_R_ref22_54 .biblioref role="doc-biblioref"}). TBLT focuses on AL development through programs or syllabi that are structured around tasks and that are developed based on a thorough needs analysis to identify the language needs of the learners and the tasks that can meet these needs ([Long, 2015](#ch22.xhtml_ref22_46){.biblioref role="doc-biblioref"}). A task, thus, is a language learning activity that is message-oriented (i.e., focused on meaning) with a non-language related goal with its contents reflecting real-life, appropriate language use based on the individual context ([Samuda & Bygate, 2008](#ch22.xhtml_ref22_59){#ch22.xhtml_R_ref22_59 .biblioref role="doc-biblioref"}). TBLT is situated within cognitive-interactionist and sociocultural theories ([Kim, 2017](#ch22.xhtml_ref22_35){#ch22.xhtml_R_ref22_35 .biblioref role="doc-biblioref"}) and is rooted in the notion that learner--learner interactions can help make input become more comprehensible.

TBLT research investigates task design and task implementation variables on interactional features (LREs, negotiation of meaning) and on linguistic performance (conceptualized using a framework of complexity, accuracy, and fluency) ([Housen et al., 2012](#ch22.xhtml_ref22_32){#ch22.xhtml_R_ref22_32 .biblioref role="doc-biblioref"}). Input, in terms of task design, considers the "written, visual, or aural information that learners performing a task work on to achieve the goal of the task" ([Robinson, 2011](#ch22.xhtml_ref22_58){#ch22.xhtml_R_ref22_58 .biblioref role="doc-biblioref"}, p. 7). When tasks are manipulated to investigate the impact of different variables on interactional opportunities and language performance, this can have an effect on the type of input presented and the type of output expected. For example, tasks may be one-way or two-way, or include descriptive rather than argumentative texts. Manipulating different variables has advanced our understanding of what type of input to include (or exclude) and the importance of using CI for more than overall message meaning.

In general, TBLT views tasks as a vehicle for the provision of meaningful input that will create the necessary conditions for learners to engage in meaningful interactions, produce language and develop their interlanguage through the creation of form--meaning connections. Much research focuses on analyzing student output in terms of their language performance during and after tasks, which is understood in terms of complexity, accuracy, and fluency ([Housen et al., 2012](#ch22.xhtml_ref22_32){.biblioref role="doc-biblioref"}). Recently, TBLT researchers have turned their attention to the importance of comprehension-based tasks to provide learners with the opportunity to process CI for acquisition through the development of form--meaning connections ([Beaulieu et al., 2020](#ch22.xhtml_ref22_8){#ch22.xhtml_R_ref22_8 .biblioref role="doc-biblioref"}; [Erlam & Ellis, 2019](#ch22.xhtml_ref22_18){#ch22.xhtml_R_ref22_18 .biblioref role="doc-biblioref"}; [Shintani, 2018](#ch22.xhtml_ref22_63){#ch22.xhtml_R_ref22_63 .biblioref role="doc-biblioref"}). This research has focused on children and adult learning of specific linguistic features (lexical and grammatical), but only at early stages of language acquisition.
:::

::: {.section aria-labelledby="sec22_3_2_3"}
#### []{#ch22.xhtml_p319 .pagebreak aria-label=" page 319. " role="doc-pagebreak"}Content-Based Instruction {#ch22.xhtml_sec22_3_2_3}

Content-based instruction (CBI) is a type of meaning-oriented instruction that provides learners with CI as they are taught the AL through subject matter content ([Lyster, 2017](#ch22.xhtml_ref22_48){#ch22.xhtml_R_ref22_48 .biblioref role="doc-biblioref"}). For example, students learn history in the AL. CBI programs, often attributed to French immersion in Canada, influenced the development of Krashen's monitor model as he witnessed the relatively rapid acquisition that occurred in these classrooms in which classroom input was meaning-focused and not form-focused ([Krashen, 1984](#ch22.xhtml_ref22_37){#ch22.xhtml_R_ref22_37 .biblioref role="doc-biblioref"}). CBI programs are premised on the importance of CI because during the teaching of subject-matter, learners process the CI for meaning, which drives acquisition. Furthermore, Krashen's position on CI and acquisition are shared by many educators in content-based programs (e.g., [Peter et al., 2008](#ch22.xhtml_ref22_56){#ch22.xhtml_R_ref22_56 .biblioref role="doc-biblioref"}).

Research into CBI programs in school settings has demonstrated their efficiency for acquisition when compared to regular AL classrooms. These input-rich environments provide learners with continued opportunities to process CI to create intake for language development. However, learners continue to have non-standard interlanguage forms (i.e., form--meaning connections are either not developed or are developed incorrectly) ([Lyster, 2017](#ch22.xhtml_ref22_48){.biblioref role="doc-biblioref"}). This has been attributed, in part, to the fact that the CI that learners are exposed to in these programs is "functionally-restricted input" ([Swain, 1988](#ch22.xhtml_ref22_65){#ch22.xhtml_R_ref22_65 .biblioref role="doc-biblioref"}, p. 74). Indeed, verb forms in French immersion classroom input, for example, are heavily skewed towards present tense and imperative forms ([Harley et al., 1987](#ch22.xhtml_ref22_27){#ch22.xhtml_R_ref22_27 .biblioref role="doc-biblioref"}). This restricted input may be a reality in instructed AL acquisition ([Collins et al., 2009](#ch22.xhtml_ref22_11){#ch22.xhtml_R_ref22_11 .biblioref role="doc-biblioref"}) and it demonstrates the importance of selecting CI that represents a wide range of language features so students can develop varied form--meaning connections.

Research has thus investigated the different types of form-focused instruction in CBI programs to help learners attend to and process linguistic features that may be difficult to process or are limited in the input and in interactional opportunities. [Doughty and Varela (1998)](#ch22.xhtml_ref22_13){#ch22.xhtml_R_ref22_13 .biblioref role="doc-biblioref"}, for example, conducted action research in two intact middle school science classes (n = 34). Using recasts, they successfully helped the experimental group become more accurate when using past time reference orally. Today, to ensure the continued development of both language and content, a proactive approach has been suggested through recommended teaching practices (e.g., noticing, awareness, guided practice, and autonomous practice) ([Lyster, 2017](#ch22.xhtml_ref22_48){.biblioref role="doc-biblioref"}). Thus, in CBI classrooms, input may need to be adapted in certain ways (see the section "Enhancing CI to Facilitate Learning") to facilitate input processing (IP) and language development. Again, research in CBI, as in TBLT, is advancing our understanding of CI and how to work with this input beyond message meaning.
:::
::::::

:::::: {.section aria-labelledby="sec22_3_3"}
### What Is the Role of Comprehensible Input in Meaning-Oriented (Communicative) Pedagogical Techniques? {#ch22.xhtml_sec22_3_3}

This section discusses communicative pedagogical techniques premised on the role of CI for acquisition.

::: {.section aria-labelledby="sec22_3_3_1"}
#### Total Physical Response {#ch22.xhtml_sec22_3_3_1}

[Asher (1965)](#ch22.xhtml_ref22_4){#ch22.xhtml_R_ref22_4 .biblioref role="doc-biblioref"} proposed total physical response (TPR) based on observations that listening precedes speaking in L1 learners, and this listening is facilitated by gestures (e.g., grabbing or pointing). TPR is an input-based technique in which learners demonstrate their understanding of CI through actions. As learners become more comfortable, they can also produce language. Today, while TPR may still be integrated into communicative classrooms, it is considered an instructional technique rather than an approach due to its limited applicability for the diversity of language use ([Larsen-Freeman & Anderson, 2011](#ch22.xhtml_ref22_39){.biblioref role="doc-biblioref"}).
:::

::: {.section aria-labelledby="sec22_3_3_2"}
#### []{#ch22.xhtml_p320 .pagebreak aria-label=" page 320. " role="doc-pagebreak"}Teaching Proficiency through Reading and Storytelling {#ch22.xhtml_sec22_3_3_2}

While Teaching Proficiency through Reading and Storytelling (TPRS) (formerly know as Total Physical Response Storytelling) tends to be promoted as an approach, we categorize it as a technique because it requires a specific set of procedures to be followed. TPRS was founded by Blaine Ray based on Krashen's theories related to CI and Asher's TPR (TPRS [Books, 2021](#ch22.xhtml_ref22_67){#ch22.xhtml_R_ref22_67 .biblioref role="doc-biblioref"}). Teaching and the provision of CI occurs largely through storytelling using appropriate topics for the students. A TPRS class includes teacher storytelling with the inclusion of personalized details to help learners engage with the content. Students retell these stories in writing, or they write new stories based on templates. This can be done as a class, in groups, or individually. Students also read stories created in class by teachers or their peers or from other sources (e.g., authentic texts such as newspaper articles or "children's books and short novels written for TPRS^®^ classrooms" \[What Does a TPRS^®^ class look like?, third paragraph\]). Finally, students may also watch videos to increase storytelling time, and thus access to CI.

TPRS is especially popular with American high school educators ([Foster, 2011](#ch22.xhtml_ref22_19){#ch22.xhtml_R_ref22_19 .biblioref role="doc-biblioref"}), and has produced many materials that can be accessed primarily through paid internet sites. In a book published by the founder of TPRS (Blaine Ray Workshops & Command Performance Language Institute), Lichtman ([2015](#ch22.xhtml_ref22_41){#ch22.xhtml_R_ref22_41 .biblioref role="doc-biblioref"}, [2019](#ch22.xhtml_ref22_42){#ch22.xhtml_R_ref22_42 .biblioref role="doc-biblioref"}) reports that research on TPRS is quickly growing while reviewing 30 studies that include TPRS as part of the research design. Based on this research synthesis, Lichtman states that overall, "TPRS equals or outperforms traditional language teaching" (e.g., grammar-based syllabi) (p. 318) and is especially effective in the areas of vocabulary learning, speaking, and reading. However, while the onus of TPRS on CI is positive, a potential lack of a communicative intent, its structured nature that requires training and a lack of access to free materials makes this intervention more restrictive than some others ([VanPatten, 2020](#ch22.xhtml_ref22_71){#ch22.xhtml_R_ref22_71 .biblioref role="doc-biblioref"}).
:::

::: {.section aria-labelledby="sec22_3_3_3"}
#### Extensive Reading {#ch22.xhtml_sec22_3_3_3}

Extensive reading involves reading texts that require discourse-level understanding to provide meaning-focused CI ([Mason & Krashen, 1997](#ch22.xhtml_ref22_50){#ch22.xhtml_R_ref22_50 .biblioref role="doc-biblioref"}; [Waring & McLean, 2015](#ch22.xhtml_ref22_75){#ch22.xhtml_R_ref22_75 .biblioref role="doc-biblioref"}). Extensive reading is frequently realized through using graded readers (specially designed books using language that match various levels of proficiency). Overall, graded readers are considered an excellent means of providing learners with opportunities to receive more CI than usual. This has been of particular interest in L2 contexts to increase opportunities for exposure to CI. Research into graded readers has shown they are a useful means for developing vocabulary ([Elturki & Harmon, 2020](#ch22.xhtml_ref22_17){#ch22.xhtml_R_ref22_17 .biblioref role="doc-biblioref"}), can increase access to input, and enhance motivation for reading ([Jez et al., 2014](#ch22.xhtml_ref22_34){#ch22.xhtml_R_ref22_34 .biblioref role="doc-biblioref"}).

Thus far, we have focused on meaning-based approaches and techniques in which CI is provided to support language development. However, learners may not process all language features within CI (e.g., [Trahey & White, 1993](#ch22.xhtml_ref22_68){#ch22.xhtml_R_ref22_68 .biblioref role="doc-biblioref"}; [VanPatten, 1996](#ch22.xhtml_ref22_69){.biblioref role="doc-biblioref"}). In the following section, we discuss some reasons for this, and we present three form-focused instructional techniques whose goal is to increase the likelihood that form--meaning connections for specific language features develop.
:::
::::::

::: {.section aria-labelledby="sec22_3_4"}
### When May CI Not Lead to Language Development? {#ch22.xhtml_sec22_3_4}

Even when learners receive copious amounts of CI, they may not process all the necessary features of input to create intake to further language development. According to VanPatten's IP theory ([1996](#ch22.xhtml_ref22_69){.biblioref role="doc-biblioref"}), because learners have limited processing resources and so cannot process all features of input simultaneously, they tend to process input for meaning (in order to understand messages) before form. Therefore, learners may struggle with processing certain grammatical []{#ch22.xhtml_p321 .pagebreak aria-label=" page 321. " role="doc-pagebreak"}features of input, especially less salient grammatical forms that are not important for understanding overall meaning. (See Leeser, this volume, for a discussion of the principles of IP.)

While there is no debate that positive evidence in the form of CI is critical for language development, research has also demonstrated that negative evidence may be needed to help learners become aware of what is not possible in the AL. Without negative evidence, learners may make the incorrect assumption that what is possible in the L1 is also possible in the L2 when this may not be the case. For example, certain adverb placement positions that are possible in English are not possible in French and vice versa ([Trahey & White, 1993](#ch22.xhtml_ref22_68){.biblioref role="doc-biblioref"}; [White, 1991](#ch22.xhtml_ref22_78){#ch22.xhtml_R_ref22_78 .biblioref role="doc-biblioref"}). Furthermore, because implicit unconscious rules about L1 grammatical structure may influence students' intuitions about grammatical and ungrammatical structures in the AL ([Ammar et al., 2010](#ch22.xhtml_ref22_3){#ch22.xhtml_R_ref22_3 .biblioref role="doc-biblioref"}), raising learner awareness about differences and similarities across languages can be effective for AL learning ([McManus & Marsden, 2019](#ch22.xhtml_ref22_49){#ch22.xhtml_R_ref22_49 .biblioref role="doc-biblioref"}; [White & Horst, 2012](#ch22.xhtml_ref22_77){#ch22.xhtml_R_ref22_77 .biblioref role="doc-biblioref"}).

Other factors such as frequency and perception may also affect whether features of input are processed. In terms of frequency, research into teacher talk in different instructed contexts (e.g., CBI) has shown that certain language forms tend to be infrequent in input ([Collins et al., 2009](#ch22.xhtml_ref22_11){.biblioref role="doc-biblioref"}; [Lyster, 2017](#ch22.xhtml_ref22_48){.biblioref role="doc-biblioref"}). Thus, in instructed settings, efforts must be made to ensure that infrequent forms are present in CI and that learners' attention is drawn to these forms. Research on phoneme detection has also demonstrated that learners may not perceive certain phonemes in the input and, as such, mere exposure to CI may be insufficient for learners to notice and process certain phonemes for potential intake. For example, research on the perception of the past --ed morpheme in experimental and naturalistic conditions has demonstrated that the perception difficulty of this morpheme varies based on its phonological realization (/t/, /d/ or /әd/), the following sound, and whether there are competing lexical cues ([Bell et al., 2015](#ch22.xhtml_ref22_9){#ch22.xhtml_R_ref22_9 .biblioref role="doc-biblioref"}; [Strachan & Trofimovich, 2017](#ch22.xhtml_ref22_64){#ch22.xhtml_R_ref22_64 .biblioref role="doc-biblioref"}). The results demonstrate that while this perception difficulty was found for both L1 and AL speakers, the L1 speakers outperformed the AL speakers. Furthermore, as AL proficiency improved, so did perception. Initial perception practice may thus help learners better benefit from CI.
:::

::::: {.section aria-labelledby="sec22_3_5"}
### Enhancing CI to Facilitate Learning {#ch22.xhtml_sec22_3_5}

Different form-focused instructional techniques exist to help draw learner attention (explicitly or implicitly) to non-salient forms in the input. Much of this research has been conducted to understand the development of grammatical and/or lexical knowledge.

::: {.section aria-labelledby="sec22_3_5_1"}
#### Textual Enhancement and Input Flood {#ch22.xhtml_sec22_3_5_1}

To increase the likelihood that learners attend to non-salient features in CI, these features can be enhanced through underlining, highlighting, bolding, intonation, etc. The idea of enhancing input with textual enhancement (TE) was advanced by [Sharwood-Smith (1981)](#ch22.xhtml_ref22_62){#ch22.xhtml_R_ref22_62 .biblioref role="doc-biblioref"} to draw learners' attention to forms in input that they otherwise may not notice due to their low saliency. TE research has had mixed results ([Han et al., 2008](#ch22.xhtml_ref22_25){#ch22.xhtml_R_ref22_25 .biblioref role="doc-biblioref"}; [Lee & Huang, 2008](#ch22.xhtml_ref22_40){#ch22.xhtml_R_ref22_40 .biblioref role="doc-biblioref"}), due to the variety of target language forms used and to methodological inconsistencies across studies. Overall, positive effects for TE tend to be at the level of noticing only which has led some to suggest that this type of enhancement may be more effective if it is accompanied by more explicit techniques such as feedback ([Winke, 2013](#ch22.xhtml_ref22_81){#ch22.xhtml_R_ref22_81 .biblioref role="doc-biblioref"}).

Another pedagogical technique that may help enhance the saliency of input is input flood (IF), which consists of increasing the number of times a learner will be exposed to a form in the input. Given that there is a positive relationship between frequency and learning in AL acquisition ([Collins & Ellis, 2009](#ch22.xhtml_ref22_10){#ch22.xhtml_R_ref22_10 .biblioref role="doc-biblioref"}), theoretically, IF could enhance the possibility that learners notice a form. []{#ch22.xhtml_p322 .pagebreak aria-label=" page 322. " role="doc-pagebreak"}Overall, research on this pedagogical intervention suggests that while IF alone tends not to lead to significant pedagogical gains, this technique might be more effective when used with other types of form-focused instruction ([Lee & Huang, 2008](#ch22.xhtml_ref22_40){.biblioref role="doc-biblioref"}; [Hernández & Rodríguez-González, 2013](#ch22.xhtml_ref22_30){#ch22.xhtml_R_ref22_30 .biblioref role="doc-biblioref"}).
:::

::: {.section aria-labelledby="sec22_3_5_2"}
#### Processing Instruction {#ch22.xhtml_sec22_3_5_2}

Processing instruction (PI) is a technique that aims to help learners create form--meaning connections by having them engage in structured input (SI) activities. SI activities are informed by the non-optimal strategies that learners use to process input as predicted by VanPatten's IP theory ([1996](#ch22.xhtml_ref22_69){.biblioref role="doc-biblioref"}). The input in SI is structured so that learners must process the target grammatical form correctly to interpret the meaning that the form encodes (see Wong, this volume). For example, the lexical preference principle states that learners will process a lexical item in input before the grammatical form if they both encode the same meaning. Thus, in the sentence "Yesterday, Martha cooked dinner" learners will process "yesterday" instead of the morphosyntactic marker -*ed* at the end of "cooked' for past tense because "yesterday" is a more salient lexical item. In order to help learners process the -*ed*, the input in SI needs to be structured so that learners can only rely on the presence or absence of -*ed* to interpret tense. In addition to structuring the input to focus learners' attention on form, SI activities must be carefully constructed to include a coherent context and to have a communicative purpose (See Wong, and White & Wong, this volume, for examples of SI).
:::
:::::
::::::::::::::::

::::: {.section aria-labelledby="sec22_4"}
## Current Contributions and Research {#ch22.xhtml_sec22_4}

Considering the centrality of CI for SLA, it is surprising how little research directly addresses its role in AL learning. Looking back on Krashen's monitor theory 40 years later and the criticisms that arose when Krashen articulated his input hypothesis, [Lichtman and VanPatten (2021)](#ch22.xhtml_ref22_43){#ch22.xhtml_R_ref22_43 .biblioref role="doc-biblioref"} point out that the term "comprehensible input" has fallen out of favor in contemporary SLA research although it continues to be widely used among language instructors.

> Because of the rejection of the input hypothesis, many researchers did not focus on "comprehensible input" as fundamental to acquisition. That is, this particular construct fell out of favor. More often than not, just "input" has been used, with the intention that it is somehow comprehensible or that somehow learners make sense of the communicative intent of what they are exposed to.
>
> *[(p. 297)]{.credit role="doc-credit"}*

Research into CI perhaps has its roots in French immersion classrooms in Canada ([Genesee, 1987](#ch22.xhtml_ref22_22){#ch22.xhtml_R_ref22_22 .biblioref role="doc-biblioref"}; [Harley, 1998](#ch22.xhtml_ref22_26){#ch22.xhtml_R_ref22_26 .biblioref role="doc-biblioref"}). In these French immersion CBI classrooms, learners are exposed to ample amounts of CI through being taught subject-matter in their L2. The French immersion context in Canada has permitted researchers to study the benefits and challenges to AL learning in classrooms where CI is at the core of instruction. Overall, research shows that these programs excel in improving learners' L2 comprehension and fluency skills, but learners continue to produce inaccurate language. In immersion classrooms today, while the focus remains on the provision of CI, there are now also discussions concerning the role that other languages could play in immersion classrooms. For example, [Ballinger et al. (2017)](#ch22.xhtml_ref22_6){#ch22.xhtml_R_ref22_6 .biblioref role="doc-biblioref"} discusses the importance of not allowing increased use of majority languages except for very specific purposes, such as the use of crosslinguistic pedagogy for teaching certain grammar points. Others such as [Lyster (2016)](#ch22.xhtml_ref22_47){#ch22.xhtml_R_ref22_47 .biblioref role="doc-biblioref"} propose that using formal instruction to temporarily draw learners' attention to grammatical []{#ch22.xhtml_p323 .pagebreak aria-label=" page 323. " role="doc-pagebreak"}forms in input within broader communicative activities that contain CI could be beneficial in immersion contexts.

Different research initiatives outside of French immersion have also been undertaken. In the next section, we summarize two studies that investigate the effectiveness of input-based instructional techniques which exemplify some current research trends on the role of CI in meaning-based instruction.

::: {.section aria-labelledby="sec22_4_1"}
### [Beaulieu et al. (2020)](#ch22.xhtml_ref22_8){.biblioref role="doc-biblioref"} {#ch22.xhtml_sec22_4_1}

This study on CI focuses on a group of under-represented learners -- adult immigrants with low L1 literacy in AL French classrooms in Canada. Its objective was to examine the feasibility and usefulness of input-based tasks focusing on targeted vocabulary, plural markers, and prepositions with this profile of learners, who could not necessarily benefit from the support of written CI that is usually present in adult L2 classrooms ([Tarone, 2010](#ch22.xhtml_ref22_66){#ch22.xhtml_R_ref22_66 .biblioref role="doc-biblioref"}). The authors developed four tasks designed to provide CI orally without requiring production. Importantly, similar to PI, the target structures had to be processed for task completion. Task 1 was a "Listen-and-Find" task that focused on singular nouns. Task 2 focused on the same items either in their singular or plural form. Task 3 was a "Listen-and-Do" task where participants had to place items in the correct room in a house. Task 4 was similar, but the items had to be placed in a specific location within a room (prepositions). The teacher's and students' positive reactions towards the task, based on an analysis of students' interactions and teacher comments, support the use of input-based tasks for low-literate learners. Student engagement was high and less proficient learners were able to participate in and complete tasks. The analysis of the classroom interactions demonstrated that during the input-based tasks, learners also produced the forms regularly either in the form of self-directed speech or during learner--learner interaction. This research demonstrates that low-literacy learners can benefit from CI to support subsequent classroom interactions and task performance.
:::

::: {.section aria-labelledby="sec22_4_2"}
### [Walenta (2018)](#ch22.xhtml_ref22_74){#ch22.xhtml_R_ref22_74 .biblioref role="doc-biblioref"} {#ch22.xhtml_sec22_4_2}

Research into PI and SI has largely focused on typical L2 classrooms for adults where the primary goal is to help learners process target forms (see Wong, this volume). Walenta, however, investigated the utility of PI and SI in CBI classrooms, where there is a constant balancing act between the teaching of language and content. Previous CI research in immersion contexts has focused on what students learn and do not learn from being exposed to copious amounts of CI. Walenta's study examined whether CI in the form of SI can be used in a CBI context to help students learn subject matter content and process challenging target structures at the same time. SI activities were constructed around the topic of architecture (the informational content that students had to learn). The target structure was the *what* as a nominal relative and *which* as a sentential relative with adult Polish learners of English. While the control group (*n* = 21) received their regular instruction including reading passages and comprehension tasks that included the target structures to the same degree as the experimental group, the experimental group (*n* = 20) was exposed to SI activities on two occasions for 20-minute sessions. These activities were designed to push learners to make accurate form--meaning connections rather than generalizing from their L1, Polish. Using a quasi-experimental, pre-post-delayed test design, the experimental group performed better than the control group on both timed-production and controlled production tests. Walenta's study supports the use of SI activities in CBI classrooms to help learners learn subject matter content and process challenging grammatical structures at the same time.
:::
:::::

::: {.section aria-labelledby="sec22_5"}
## []{#ch22.xhtml_p324 .pagebreak aria-label=" page 324. " role="doc-pagebreak"}Recommendations for Practice {#ch22.xhtml_sec22_5}

In classroom practice, it is essential to evaluate whether CI is truly comprehensible insofar as it meets the two conditions of general understanding and communicative purpose. Discussions pertaining to making adjustments to input to make it more understandable are not new. [Hatch (1983)](#ch22.xhtml_ref22_29){#ch22.xhtml_R_ref22_29 .biblioref role="doc-biblioref"}, for example, presented different ways to promote comprehension through interactional adjustments focusing on speech rate, vocabulary, syntax, and discourse. Today, there are varied means in which (written) texts can be analyzed to determine their lexical and syntactic complexity, the frequency of the words used in the text, and overall readability scores (e.g., see LexTutuor, Linguistic Analysis Tools, Flesch-Kincaid readability tests). Research has shown that learners need to be able to understand (reading or listening) more than 90% of the words in a text to be able to comprehend about 75% of the text ([Van Zeeland & Schmitt, 2012](#ch22.xhtml_ref22_73){#ch22.xhtml_R_ref22_73 .biblioref role="doc-biblioref"}). Furthermore, knowledge of approximately 6000--7000 word families for spoken discourse and 8000--9000 for written discourse are targets for competent language users ([Nation, 2006](#ch22.xhtml_ref22_52){#ch22.xhtml_R_ref22_52 .biblioref role="doc-biblioref"}). These figures can help guide teachers when deciding what may be comprehensible for their learners. Clearly, these figures need to be considered within individual contexts since language background, familiarity of genre, text familiarity, etc. can all play a role ([Schmitt et al., 2017](#ch22.xhtml_ref22_61){#ch22.xhtml_R_ref22_61 .biblioref role="doc-biblioref"}).

For input to be CI, there also needs to be a communicative purpose. In other words, learners need to interact with input for meaning-focused reasons rather than simply to learn language. In TBLT, for example, tasks should have a meaningful target outcome ([Samuda & Bygate, 2008](#ch22.xhtml_ref22_59){.biblioref role="doc-biblioref"}) to ensure a communicative purpose. This is a tricky, slippery notion. Often to ensure a communicative purpose, texts destined for native-speakers have been employed based on the premise that native-speakers are users, not learners, of a language ([Badger & MacDonald, 2010](#ch22.xhtml_ref22_5){.biblioref role="doc-biblioref"}). However, at the same time, this can lead to comprehensibility issues. Furthermore, whether language has or does not have a communicative purpose can sometimes be subjective. For example, as [VanPatten (2020)](#ch22.xhtml_ref22_72){.biblioref role="doc-biblioref"} mentions in relation to TPRS,

> What is not clear in TPRS is whether it has a communicative purpose: What is the outcome of a TPRS lesson other than to learn a language? Do learners know something about themselves and the world around them that they didn't know before? Arguably, telling a story is the outcome, but how this outcome fits within the psychosocial or cognitive-informational purpose of communication remains to be seen.
>
> *[(p. 95)]{.credit role="doc-credit"}*

Students' perceptions of communication may play a role. A student who loves stories may perceive the kind of storytelling in TPRS as having communicative purpose whilst another student may only see the language learning goal of such storytelling. Furthermore, the instructor's delivery of the CI (whether it be in the form of storytelling or something else) may also impact whether learners will view a "communicative" event as purposeful or not. For example, asking a student "When is your birthday?" would have a communicative purpose if the instructor genuinely wanted to know the students' birthdays for some purpose (e.g., so the class can celebrate everyone's birthdays throughout the term), or this question could solely have a language learning goal (without any real communicative purpose) if the instructor just wanted to assess whether students could say dates correctly. Despite the prickliness of this concept, teachers can ask themselves whether task completion is successful if students produce accurate language only or whether "they have to produce a pragmatically credible response" ([Samuda & Bygate, 2008](#ch22.xhtml_ref22_59){.biblioref role="doc-biblioref"}, p. 14).
:::

::: {.section .conclusion role="doc-conclusion"}
## []{#ch22.xhtml_p325 .pagebreak aria-label=" page 325. " role="doc-pagebreak"}Future Directions and Conclusion {#ch22.xhtml_sec22_6}

CI is central in meaning-focused approaches to AL instruction. Regardless of the theoretical background or the recommended teaching approach, providing learners with CI is pivotal for driving AL learning. Research into teaching approaches or pedagogical techniques should continue to explore the benefits of drawing on CI to create meaning whilst creating form--meaning connections. Avenues in which this research could turn are multiple. For example, the reality of today's multimodal input and output texts, texts that can include images, writing, speech, emoticons, etc., is a fertile ground for CI research. Over the past 15 years, research has attempted to understand the effects of multimodal input on language processing and the learning of specific linguistic phenomena to further understanding of language learning and improve pedagogical practices ([Perez, 2020](#ch22.xhtml_ref22_55){#ch22.xhtml_R_ref22_55 .biblioref role="doc-biblioref"}). Research has already demonstrated, for example, that words accompanied by their images whilst watching a documentary resulted in more learning than hearing the words without a supporting image ([Peters, 2019](#ch22.xhtml_ref22_57){#ch22.xhtml_R_ref22_57 .biblioref role="doc-biblioref"}). Currently, research into multimodal input is largely cognitive in nature and has focused largely on multimodal input through television outside of classroom settings ([Perez, 2020](#ch22.xhtml_ref22_55){.biblioref role="doc-biblioref"}). Further research into multimodal input within classroom settings is warranted as many students receive much of their AL education inside the classroom. Including affective factors such as motivation or flow within tasks in this research is also warranted to understand whether they modify the relationship between different types of multimodal input and learning.

It is important to note that research on CI (as well as much of instructed SLA research) remains skewed towards educated, adult participants. As the heterogeneity of learners and their needs expand, it is vital that CI research is conducted with other populations. It is currently unknown, for example, whether the comprehensibility of input is affected by learner literacy levels. Do students with lower metalinguistic abilities due to lower educational and literacy levels benefit from CI in the same way? Are they able to notice and process elements in CI sufficiently for these elements to be integrated into their developing linguistic system?

Finally, the plurilingual turn in L2 research merits attention when considering the role that CI plays in instructed SLA. Traditionally, meaning-focused classrooms have aimed to provide as much CI in the target language as possible. However, current research on plurilingual instruction demonstrates that there are benefits to encouraging learners to tap into their full linguistic repertoires for both language learning and for socio-affective reasons ([Payant & Bell, 2022](#ch22.xhtml_ref22_53){#ch22.xhtml_R_ref22_53 .biblioref role="doc-biblioref"}). Can instructors embrace plurilingual instruction in their classrooms without compromising opportunities for students to receive the CI they need for language learning? Further research is thus warranted to understand whether the quantity or quality of CI is affected positively or negatively in classrooms adopting a plurilingual approach.
:::

::: {.endnotes role="doc-endnotes"}
## Note {#ch22.xhtml_h2}
:::
