[]{#ch23.xhtml}

::: {}
# []{#ch23.xhtml_p329 .pagebreak aria-label=" page 329. " role="doc-pagebreak"}[23 ]{.chnum .ordinal}[Approaches to Vocabulary Instruction from an Input Processing Perspective]{.chtitle} {#ch23.xhtml_h1}

Brent Wolter

Idaho State University

DOI: [10.4324/9781003041412-29](https://dx.doi.org/10.4324/9781003041412-29){aria-label="D.O.I. link to this document."}
:::

::: {.section .introduction role="doc-introduction"}
## Introduction and Definitions {#ch23.xhtml_sec23_1}

There are few researchers, teachers, or learners in the modern world who would argue against the necessity of input in language learning. Nonetheless, early theories of input processing (IP) had little to say about the role of input in second language (L2) vocabulary learning. In this chapter, I will begin by elaborating on the historical role of vocabulary in IP before presenting some of the critical issues and topics that have emerged. Next, I will consider what the research literature to date has revealed regarding vocabulary learning in relation to IP, with a specific focus on the role of input and output in vocabulary learning. I will then move on to consider some of the pedagogical implications emerging from this body of research before discussing what future research questions will help us further develop our understanding of IP and how to use it for more effective teaching and learning of L2 vocabulary.

To begin, it would be useful to start with definitions for some of the key concepts that will be discussed in the chapter. *Vocabulary* refers both to single words and multiword units mapped to single meanings. *Lex-IP* refers to how learners allocate limited processing resources when they are exposed to novel vocabulary, regardless of the extent to which the context of learning is intentional or incidental. *Incidental vocabulary acquisition* describes situations in which vocabulary is acquired despite not being the primary focus of study, for example through reading or watching a movie for enjoyment. *Intentional vocabulary acquisition* refers to situations in which there is an explicit focus on learning new vocabulary.
:::

::: {.section aria-labelledby="sec23_2"}
## Historical Perspectives {#ch23.xhtml_sec23_2}

Although vocabulary has played an important role in models of IP, it has typically been considered primarily with respect to the role it plays in the acquisition of (morpho)syntax ([Barcroft, 2015](#ch23.xhtml_ref23_12){#ch23.xhtml_R_ref23_12 .biblioref role="doc-biblioref"}). [VanPatten's (1996)](#ch23.xhtml_ref23_42){#ch23.xhtml_R_ref23_42 .biblioref role="doc-biblioref"} well-known IP model, for example, asserts that learners attend first and foremost to the meaning of content words when processing a sentence before relying on grammatical aspects such as morphological markers and word order. Nonetheless, this attentional allocation is considered with respect to its influence on parsing a sentence for (morpho)syntactical information so that the verb's argument structure can be decoded, and the nouns' thematic roles can be ascertained. Indeed, it would not be an exaggeration to suggest that there []{#ch23.xhtml_p330 .pagebreak aria-label=" page 330. " role="doc-pagebreak"}is often an implicit assumption in most, if not all, early IP models (e.g., [Gass, 2013](#ch23.xhtml_ref23_21){#ch23.xhtml_R_ref23_21 .biblioref role="doc-biblioref"}; [VanPatten, 1996](#ch23.xhtml_ref23_42){.biblioref role="doc-biblioref"}), that all the words in the input are already known. This tendency to de-emphasize the process of lexical acquisition is not surprising given the strong link between IP models and, traditionally, their reliance on assumptions of language acquisition devices such as Universal Grammar, which have historically adopted a simplified view of vocabulary in respect to the complexity of what it means to learn and know a word and the role vocabulary plays in influencing what have historically been described as grammatical structures (see Dekydtspotter, and Morales-Front & Barcroft, this volume, for more on these issues).

Over the past few decades, however, L2 vocabulary acquisition has come into its own as an important and active area in L2 acquisition research, and with this shift we have seen the development of IP accounts for lexical acquisition. The most comprehensive theoretical explanation for lex-IP to date is described in [Barcroft's (2015)](#ch23.xhtml_ref23_12){.biblioref role="doc-biblioref"} book *Lexical Input Processing and Vocabulary Learning*, which was preceded by his more pedagogically-oriented book entitled *Input-Based Incremental Vocabulary Instruction* ([Barcroft, 2012](#ch23.xhtml_ref23_11){#ch23.xhtml_R_ref23_11 .biblioref role="doc-biblioref"}). In the latter of these two books, [Barcroft (2012, p. 17)](#ch23.xhtml_ref23_11){.biblioref role="doc-biblioref"} outlines ten principles for effective vocabulary instruction under the umbrella of input-based instruction (IBI) vocabulary instruction. They are as follows:

1.  Develop and implement a vocabulary acquisition plan.
2.  Present new words frequently and repeatedly in the input.
3.  Promote both intentional and incidental vocabulary learning.
4.  Use meaning-bearing comprehensible input when presenting new words.
5.  Present new words in an enhanced manner.
6.  Limit forced output without access to meaning during the initial stages.
7.  Limit forced semantic elaboration during the initial stages.
8.  Promote learning L2-specific word meanings and usage over time.
9.  Progress from less demanding to more demanding activities over time.
10. Apply research findings with direct implications for vocabulary instruction.

Many of these principles are in line with other approaches and thinking in L2 vocabulary research and instruction. For example, there is a large body of research that emphasizes the importance of repetition and frequent exposure when learning new words, as well as research suggesting that textual enhancements of new vocabulary (e.g., [underlining]{.underline} or **bolding**) promotes acquisition. Current thinking in L2 vocabulary acquisition aligns with the idea that vocabulary knowledge is complex, multifaceted, and built up slowly and incrementally ( e.g., [Nation, 2013](#ch23.xhtml_ref23_33){#ch23.xhtml_R_ref23_33 .biblioref role="doc-biblioref"}; [Schmitt & Schmitt, 2020](#ch23.xhtml_ref23_38){#ch23.xhtml_R_ref23_38 .biblioref role="doc-biblioref"}).

What primarily differentiates the IBI approach from other approaches to vocabulary instruction is a warning about activities involving semantic elaboration, such as using new words in sentences during the initial stages of L2 vocabulary learning. While some approaches to vocabulary learning suggest that semantically elaborative activities such as writing new words in sentences are effective during the early stages, Barcroft asserts that this is the case only in a restricted sense, an issue tied to allocation of limited processing resources given the dissociability of different types of processing and learning, which I will return to in the next section. Additionally, as with all IP approaches to instruction, the IBI approach strongly advocates for the use of vocabulary learning incorporated in meaningful, communicative-based activities. However, unlike some IP approaches to grammar instruction (e.g., [Krashen, 1999](#ch23.xhtml_ref23_29){#ch23.xhtml_R_ref23_29 .biblioref role="doc-biblioref"}), IBI does not devalue the importance of direct instruction in light of the fact that vocabulary knowledge is declarative in nature rather than procedural (see [Barcroft, 2015](#ch23.xhtml_ref23_12){.biblioref role="doc-biblioref"}, p. 25). Finally, because IBI views input as an []{#ch23.xhtml_p331 .pagebreak aria-label=" page 331. " role="doc-pagebreak"}essential source of information, it is wary of approaches that use negative evidence (i.e., examples of what is incorrect in the L2) to teach learners about L2 vocabulary.
:::

:::::: {.section aria-labelledby="sec23_3"}
## Critical Issues and Topics {#ch23.xhtml_sec23_3}

::: {.section aria-labelledby="sec23_3_1"}
### What Is the Impact of Semantic Elaboration? {#ch23.xhtml_sec23_3_1}

The degree of utility of semantic elaboration and forced output without access to meaning during the initial stages of vocabulary learning are probably the most contentious aspects of Barcroft's IBI principles. Some explanations of L2 vocabulary acquisition favor the use of such activities, such as [Hulstijn and Laufer's (2001)](#ch23.xhtml_ref23_23){#ch23.xhtml_R_ref23_23 .biblioref role="doc-biblioref"} involvement load hypothesis (ILH), which draws upon theoretical assumptions from [Craik and Lockhart's (1972)](#ch23.xhtml_ref23_16){#ch23.xhtml_R_ref23_16 .biblioref role="doc-biblioref"} levels of processing (LOP) model of human memory. Briefly stated, LOP contends that concepts, information, words, etc. that are learned at a "deeper" level are more likely to be retained in memory than things learned at a more "shallow" level. Applying this specifically to L2 vocabulary acquisition, the ILH assumes that words learned incidentally through semantically elaborative tasks such as sentence writing are more likely to be retained than words learned incidentally in a more superficial way, such as by reading alone or while performing a task with a lower involvement load than sentence writing with the underlying assumption being that a higher involvement load results in better learning ([Hulstijn & Laufer, 2001](#ch23.xhtml_ref23_23){.biblioref role="doc-biblioref"}).

In a study on the ILH, [Hulstijn & Laufer (2001)](#ch23.xhtml_ref23_23){.biblioref role="doc-biblioref"} demonstrated that retention of target vocabulary was highest for participants who engaged in a writing task incorporating the new vocabulary, followed by participants who engaged in a reading task coupled with a fill-in-the blank task, followed by participants who engaged in a read-only task with marginal glosses. Time-on-task was different in each of these conditions, however, with writing participants taking longer than the fill-in-the-blank participants who, in turn, took longer than the read-only participants. Furthermore, other studies (Barcroft, [2002](#ch23.xhtml_ref23_5){#ch23.xhtml_R_ref23_5 .biblioref role="doc-biblioref"}, [2003](#ch23.xhtml_ref23_6){#ch23.xhtml_R_ref23_6 .biblioref role="doc-biblioref"}, [2004](#ch23.xhtml_ref23_7){#ch23.xhtml_R_ref23_7 .biblioref role="doc-biblioref"}; [Wong & Pyun, 2012](#ch23.xhtml_ref23_47){#ch23.xhtml_R_ref23_47 .biblioref role="doc-biblioref"}) have demonstrated strong negative effects for sentence writing and negative effects for other semantically oriented tasks such as pleasantness ratings on intentional L2 vocabulary learning (note that the ILH was designed for incidental vocabulary learning), which are consistent with Barcroft's type of processing -- resource allocation (TOPRA) model of vocabulary acquisition. (See Kida, this volume, for a discussion of the TOPRA model.)

The TOPRA model is consistent with assumptions from models of working memory (e.g., [Baddeley & Hitch, 1974](#ch23.xhtml_ref23_4){#ch23.xhtml_R_ref23_4 .biblioref role="doc-biblioref"}), which suggest that humans are limited in their ability to hold information in short-term memory while simultaneously performing calculations or manipulations on this information, and as the need for one task increases the capacity to perform the other necessarily decreases (e.g., [Baddeley & Hitch, 1974](#ch23.xhtml_ref23_4){.biblioref role="doc-biblioref"}, p. 76). One key difference between working memory models and TOPRA concerns levels of specificity; while working memory models focus on memory and cognitive processes in general, TOPRA has been applied most often to the tension that potentially can arise when allocating resources between two or more dissociable types of processing, such as between processing for word form or word meaning and the mapping component of vocabulary learning (see [Kida & Barcroft, 2018](#ch23.xhtml_ref23_28){#ch23.xhtml_R_ref23_28 .biblioref role="doc-biblioref"}).
:::

::: {.section aria-labelledby="sec23_3_2"}
### How Do We Present Vocabulary as Input? {#ch23.xhtml_sec23_3_2}

Other issues key issues in vocabulary instruction include how vocabulary is presented to learners and whether to avoid negative evidence. With respect to the first of these two issues, the IBI approach advocates presenting vocabulary in meaningful and thematically based contexts. []{#ch23.xhtml_p332 .pagebreak aria-label=" page 332. " role="doc-pagebreak"}Thematically based groupings of target vocabulary are thought to be superior to semantically based groupings under the assumption that semantically based groupings (in which the learner is presented with a number of words from the same semantic set) can lead to interference and negatively affect vocabulary learning (see, e.g., [Erten & Tekin, 2008](#ch23.xhtml_ref23_18){#ch23.xhtml_R_ref23_18 .biblioref role="doc-biblioref"}). Once a theme and the target vocabulary have been selected, the teacher should proceed to teach vocabulary in a systematic way that focuses on different aspects of vocabulary knowledge over time so as not to overburden the learners' limited cognitive resources (see [Barcroft, 2012](#ch23.xhtml_ref23_11){.biblioref role="doc-biblioref"}, [Chapter 4](#ch4.xhtml) for examples).
:::

::: {.section aria-labelledby="sec23_3_3"}
### Should We Avoid Negative Evidence? {#ch23.xhtml_sec23_3_3}

The IBI approach also contends that teachers should avoid presenting examples of incorrect vocabulary usage in an attempt to refine understanding of target vocabulary. One such example can be seen in activities based on [Lewis' (2002)](#ch23.xhtml_ref23_31){#ch23.xhtml_R_ref23_31 .biblioref role="doc-biblioref"} lexical approach (see [Lewis, 2002](#ch23.xhtml_ref23_31){.biblioref role="doc-biblioref"}, p. 261). In this particular activity, learners are provided with a list of verbs and two possible noun collocates with the objective of selecting the one possible collocate (e.g., *make a deal* or *\*make a meeting*). The IBI approach would view this type of activity as potentially detrimental because the incorrect collocation *\*make a meeting* could still serve as unintentional (and potentially harmful) input that may be processed, encoded, and retained by the learner.
:::
::::::

::::::: {.section aria-labelledby="sec23_4"}
## Current Contributions and Research {#ch23.xhtml_sec23_4}

As noted above, [Barcroft (2012)](#ch23.xhtml_ref23_11){.biblioref role="doc-biblioref"} provides a comprehensive approach to teaching vocabulary using the IBI approach. Because this approach is holistic, it draws on research from a number of areas, not all of which are directly and exclusively tied to lex-IP. For this reason, I will limit the discussion in this section specifically to issues related to input and output since these are both relevant for any IP-based theory. However, fuller accounts of vocabulary teaching and learning can be found in [Nation (2013)](#ch23.xhtml_ref23_33){.biblioref role="doc-biblioref"} and [Schmitt and Schmitt (2020)](#ch23.xhtml_ref23_38){.biblioref role="doc-biblioref"}.

::: {.section aria-labelledby="sec23_4_1"}
### The Role of Input in Incidental Vocabulary Acquisition {#ch23.xhtml_sec23_4_1}

Although all vocabulary researchers would agree that L2 vocabulary acquisition can occur incidentally through input alone, there is less agreement about whether or not incidental acquisition represents an efficient way to learn a sufficiently large vocabulary. This is an issue that has attracted a good deal of research attention over the past 20 years or so, and although most studies have shown positive gains in vocabulary knowledge from incidental reading, these gains have generally been modest even after multiple exposures to a particular lexical item (e.g., [Horst et al., 1998](#ch23.xhtml_ref23_22){#ch23.xhtml_R_ref23_22 .biblioref role="doc-biblioref"}; [Waring & Takaki 2003](#ch23.xhtml_ref23_44){#ch23.xhtml_R_ref23_44 .biblioref role="doc-biblioref"}). In an effort to understand the collective results of the numerous studies in this area, particularly the effects of repetition, [Uchihara et al. (2019)](#ch23.xhtml_ref23_41){#ch23.xhtml_R_ref23_41 .biblioref role="doc-biblioref"} performed a meta-analysis using 26 studies investigating the influence of frequency of encounters on incidental vocabulary learning. Overall, they reported a positive relationship between frequency of exposure and vocabulary retention with a medium effect size (*r* = .34). The authors also suggest that this figure might have been lower than expected given the "great theoretical value and substantial attention that many researchers have given to the role of frequency in vocabulary acquisition over the past four decades" (p. 583).

From a lex-IP perspective, we can understand the modest level of vocabulary gains in these studies by considering allocation of cognitive resources and the effects that it can have on one's ability to attend to vocabulary in input. In the studies analyzed by Uchihara et al. the main aim []{#ch23.xhtml_p333 .pagebreak aria-label=" page 333. " role="doc-pagebreak"}of the participants was text comprehension, which, though seemingly effortless in most L1 situations, is an exceedingly complex process in L2 contexts that requires convergence of a number of skills and inferences to be done successfully. From a language processing standpoint alone, there are many factors competing for the reader's attention, including orthographic/phonological qualities, syntactic structures (and the parsing this requires), and morphological factors to name only a few (see also Rott, this volume). If we assume, as most models of memory do, that our capacity to simultaneously store and process information is limited, then it is not surprising that vocabulary acquisition may be low, particularly if unknown words do not act as a significant barrier to overall comprehension.
:::

::: {.section aria-labelledby="sec23_4_2"}
### Pedagogical Implications for Incidental Vocabulary Acquisition {#ch23.xhtml_sec23_4_2}

From a pedagogical perspective, one question that arises here is how to make reading activities like these lead to greater gains in vocabulary knowledge, especially when vocabulary is not the explicit focus of such activities. In essence, the problem is one of lexical input not becoming *lexical intake* (the subset of lexical input that becomes available to the developing lexicosemantic system). As numerous studies on IP have demonstrated, input is much more likely to become intake when the input is more salient to the learner. Similarly, if we assume that input is processed first and foremost for meaning, then it follows that learners will use the most effective and efficient strategies they have to complete this task. In some cases, this will be simply to skip over an unknown word if its contribution to the interpretation of the overall utterance is not necessary for a reasonably accurate interpretation, a strategy employed by all language users. However, it also deprives learners of taking full advantage of a valuable opportunity for learning.

A natural way to increase the likelihood of attending to key lexical items in a text is through glossing target lexical items (with marginal glosses either in the L1 or the L2, hyperlinks to definitions, pictures, videos, etc.), often accompanied by textual enhancement of target words through practices such as **bolding** or [underlining]{.underline}. Research in this area parallels research into incidental acquisition through input in that although studies have typically shown gains in vocabulary knowledge (e.g., [Hulstijn et al., 1996](#ch23.xhtml_ref23_24){#ch23.xhtml_R_ref23_24 .biblioref role="doc-biblioref"}; [Jung, 2016](#ch23.xhtml_ref23_26){#ch23.xhtml_R_ref23_26 .biblioref role="doc-biblioref"}), these gains have often been modest. [Jung (2016)](#ch23.xhtml_ref23_26){.biblioref role="doc-biblioref"}, for example, measured form and meaning recall of 10 pseudo-words that had been glossed in two texts versus 10 pseudo-words that had not been glossed in the same texts. The results showed significantly higher form and meaning recall on an immediate posttest for glossed items, but this difference was not significant on a delayed posttest. Similar results were reported by [Hulstijn et al. (1996)](#ch23.xhtml_ref23_24){.biblioref role="doc-biblioref"} who demonstrated significant gains for words presented in a marginal gloss over control words presented with no gloss, even though, again, overall percentages of gains were modest.

To gain a holistic understanding of the findings in this area, another meta-analysis on glossing studies was conducted by [Yanagisawa et al. (2020)](#ch23.xhtml_ref23_48){#ch23.xhtml_R_ref23_48 .biblioref role="doc-biblioref"}. As with the meta-analysis on repetition in vocabulary learning, the results indicated that glossing increases gains in vocabulary. More specifically, on average learners who read texts with glosses learned 45.3% of unknown words on immediate posttests and 33.4% on delayed posttests, significantly higher percentages than for learners who had no exposure to glosses both on immediate posttests (26.6%) and delayed posttests (19.8%). Additionally, the results showed an advantage for multiple-choice glosses for which a reader had to select the correct meaning from a list of possibilities over other types of glossing (e.g., marginal glosses, hyperlinks, etc.). Furthermore, Yanagisawa et al. also found a benefit for L1 glosses over L2 glosses, though they point out that L1 glosses may not be feasible when the learners in a class have different L1s.
:::

::: {.section aria-labelledby="sec23_4_3"}
### []{#ch23.xhtml_p334 .pagebreak aria-label=" page 334. " role="doc-pagebreak"}Quality and Variety of Input {#ch23.xhtml_sec23_4_3}

An additional input-related factor that should be considered is quality of lexical input. Research to date has mostly considered quality of input in respect to initial acquisition of form and meaning, and some patterns have emerged. As noted previously, input that groups new vocabulary thematically is more likely to be successful than words grouped along semantical-similarity lines, owing to the fact that the close overlap between meanings in semantic groups can result in confusion for learners. Additionally, there is evidence indicating that varying the presentation of new words in spoken input leads to better initial learning than less varied presentation. This was shown in two studies in particular: [Barcroft and Sommers (2005)](#ch23.xhtml_ref23_13){#ch23.xhtml_R_ref23_13 .biblioref role="doc-biblioref"} and [Sommers and Barcroft (2007)](#ch23.xhtml_ref23_39){#ch23.xhtml_R_ref23_39 .biblioref role="doc-biblioref"}. In the first, Barcroft and Sommers found that presenting new vocabulary while increasing talker variability and voice-type variability resulted in better learning when factors like number of exposures were held constant. In the second, similar benefits were also reported with respect to speaking-rate variability.
:::

::: {.section aria-labelledby="sec23_4_4"}
### The Role of Output {#ch23.xhtml_sec23_4_4}

Although IP models for syntax often tend to advise against using output in the early stages of learning (e.g., [VanPatten, 2002](#ch23.xhtml_ref23_43){#ch23.xhtml_R_ref23_43 .biblioref role="doc-biblioref"}), this view is less commonly held among researchers focusing on L2 vocabulary learning. However, research findings to date have shown that for early output to be effective, some important conditions need to be met. First, output without access to meaning (e.g., through choral repetition or writing down forms) seems to be ineffective or, under certain conditions, counterproductive ([Barcroft, 2006](#ch23.xhtml_ref23_8){#ch23.xhtml_R_ref23_8 .biblioref role="doc-biblioref"}; [Wong & Barcroft, 2020](#ch23.xhtml_ref23_46){#ch23.xhtml_R_ref23_46 .biblioref role="doc-biblioref"}). [Wong and Barcroft (2020)](#ch23.xhtml_ref23_46){.biblioref role="doc-biblioref"} found no significant improvements in immediate learning, shorter-term retention (measured after two days), or longer-term retention (measured after one week) for learners who used choral repetition versus those who did not. Similarly, [Barcroft (2006)](#ch23.xhtml_ref23_8){.biblioref role="doc-biblioref"} observed a significant negative effect for learning and retention of new L2 word forms when participants were required to write down the words during the learning process.

Other studies have demonstrated a negative effect for requiring participants to use new words in a sentence-writing task versus those who had no such requirement. [Barcroft (2004)](#ch23.xhtml_ref23_7){.biblioref role="doc-biblioref"}, for example, reported significantly greater immediate and delayed retention for new words in a no-writing versus a sentence-writing condition. In a partial replication study, [Wong and Pyun (2012)](#ch23.xhtml_ref23_47){.biblioref role="doc-biblioref"} also found that the negative effects of writing target words in novel sentences were more pronounced when the L2 in question was less formally similar to the L1 of the participants. Finally, [Kida (2020)](#ch23.xhtml_ref23_27){#ch23.xhtml_R_ref23_27 .biblioref role="doc-biblioref"} found negative effects for semantic processing (in this case, assigning a pleasantness rating to newly learned words) compared to structural processing (guessing the pronunciation of newly learned words and attempting to write them in L1 script).

Secondly, it appears that learners need to produce output *with* access to meaning, which involves retrieval of target word forms on the part of the learners, for early output to be beneficial. Numerous studies have demonstrated that opportunities for output of this nature can increase L2 vocabulary learning, beginning with a seminal study in this area by [Royer (1973)](#ch23.xhtml_ref23_36){#ch23.xhtml_R_ref23_36 .biblioref role="doc-biblioref"} and continuing with others, such as those by [Joe (1995)](#ch23.xhtml_ref23_25){#ch23.xhtml_R_ref23_25 .biblioref role="doc-biblioref"}, [McNamara and Healy (1995)](#ch23.xhtml_ref23_32){#ch23.xhtml_R_ref23_32 .biblioref role="doc-biblioref"}, and [Barcroft (2007)](#ch23.xhtml_ref23_9){#ch23.xhtml_R_ref23_9 .biblioref role="doc-biblioref"}. [Joe (1995)](#ch23.xhtml_ref23_25){.biblioref role="doc-biblioref"}, for instance, observed that word generation (i.e., using a word productively in a novel context without time restrictions) led to better retention and deeper learning of meaning than either being asked to attend to or retrieve newly learned words. Findings such as this are consistent with the proposal that the phenomenon known as the *generation effect* (how retrieving items on one's own can increase memory and learning) is applicable to L2 vocabulary learning.

[]{#ch23.xhtml_p335 .pagebreak aria-label=" page 335. " role="doc-pagebreak"}Additionally, not all studies have indicated negative or null effects for word-level output without access to meaning at the initial stages of learning as long as adequate time-on-task is provided. [Webb and Piasecki (2018)](#ch23.xhtml_ref23_45){#ch23.xhtml_R_ref23_45 .biblioref role="doc-biblioref"} found that when a short time limit of six seconds per word was implemented, learners who were asked to write down new words presented in word--picture pairings performed slightly worse than learners who only attended to the word--picture pairings, as is consistent with the negative effect of word writing observed by [Barcroft (2006)](#ch23.xhtml_ref23_8){.biblioref role="doc-biblioref"}. However, when learners were given an unlimited amount of time to write and remember the pairings, they performed significantly better than in the other two conditions, both with respect to immediate and long-term retention of word forms, and regardless of whether knowledge was measured receptively or productively.
:::
:::::::

::: {.section aria-labelledby="sec23_5"}
## Main Research Methods {#ch23.xhtml_sec23_5}

In considering the main research methods used in lex-IP research, it is useful to make an initial distinction between incidental acquisition studies and intentional acquisition studies. Incidental acquisition studies focus on whether or not acquisition occurs even when vocabulary learning is not the focal point of the task. Studies on incidental acquisition (e.g., [Pulido, 2004](#ch23.xhtml_ref23_35){#ch23.xhtml_R_ref23_35 .biblioref role="doc-biblioref"}) typically require learners to read a text which incorporates a number of unknown words. A range of variables can be manipulated depending on the interests of the researcher and the goals of the study, such as frequency of encounters for the new words, richness of contexts, the effectiveness of textual enhancements, the use of glosses, dictionary use, etc. In some cases, these studies will use low frequency words which the learners are unlikely to know while other studies will use pseudo words attached to a specific meaning that are in line with the orthographic and phonotactics rules of the language being tested but are not actually words.

In intentional acquisition studies, the focus is on overt vocabulary learning. In these studies (e.g., Barcroft, [2004](#ch23.xhtml_ref23_7){.biblioref role="doc-biblioref"}, [2006](#ch23.xhtml_ref23_8){.biblioref role="doc-biblioref"}), researchers typically present new vocabulary (defined and operationalized in the same ways as in incidental studies) in various conditions and attempt to determine which conditions leads to the greatest vocabulary gains. Again, a number of variables can be manipulated, such as number of presentations, whether words are presented with pictures indicating the meaning or L1 translations, tasks involved in the learning process, time allotted for the learning to take place, etc. In studies such as these, it is considered good practice to use some form of counterbalancing to offset the potential influence of individual words. *Counterbalancing* can take many forms, but the basic idea behind it is that all target words should be presented in all conditions. For example, one group or participants could be presented with all the words in one list of vocabulary items (e.g., List A) in an experimental condition and a second group of vocabulary items (List B) in a control condition to see which condition promotes better acquisition. A second group of participants would then use List B items for the experimental condition and List A items for the control condition, effectively "cancelling out" the potential influence of the individual words on the two lists.

One thing that both incidental and intentional lines of research have in common is the practice of measuring gains in two main aspects of vocabulary knowledge, form and meaning, at two different levels of understanding, recognition and recall. Naturally, these variables can be operationalized in a number of different ways. *Form recognition*, for example, can be tested by giving learners a target word presented in a list of similarly spelled distractor items and asking them to identify the correctly spelled word. *Meaning recall*, on the other hand, can be tested by asking learners to provide L1 or L2 translations of the target words, or by presenting learners with pictures indicating the meaning of the target words and asking them to supply the correct L2 word.

[]{#ch23.xhtml_p336 .pagebreak aria-label=" page 336. " role="doc-pagebreak"}In addition to these basic practices, newer technologies and methodologies have also made it possible to investigate vocabulary acquisition in ways that have historically not been possible. These include things like event related potentials (ERPs) which provide an understanding of cognitive processing at a neurological level via an electroencephalogram (EEG) device (e.g., [Yum et al., 2014](#ch23.xhtml_ref23_49){#ch23.xhtml_R_ref23_49 .biblioref role="doc-biblioref"}). Another technological advance that has already begun to show its potential for L2 vocabulary research is eye-tracking (e.g., [Pellicer-Sánchez, 2016](#ch23.xhtml_ref23_34){#ch23.xhtml_R_ref23_34 .biblioref role="doc-biblioref"}). Modern eye-trackers can determine where a person is looking on a computer screen when presented with visual stimuli, such as texts with new vocabulary items, with incredibly high precision.
:::

:::::: {.section aria-labelledby="sec23_6"}
## Recommendations for Practice {#ch23.xhtml_sec23_6}

To assist teachers in setting up a systematic approach to vocabulary teaching and learning in a classroom setting that is in line with an lex-IP approach, [Fichtner and Barcroft (2019)](#ch23.xhtml_ref23_19){#ch23.xhtml_R_ref23_19 .biblioref role="doc-biblioref"} proposed a useful framework for evaluating approaches to vocabulary instruction based on five criteria (p. 84) regarding the extent to which the approach:

1.  consists of an overall approach versus an isolated instructional technique;
2.  is specific about how to treat vocabulary on an ongoing basis;
3.  is grounded in theory;
4.  is based on research findings;
5.  is integrated with in meaning-oriented instruction.

Any worthwhile approach to vocabulary teaching should attend to the first four of these criteria, and the fifth criterion is clearly essential if one wishes to implement an approach that is consistent with other principles of IP theories.

Some additional conclusions emerging from the body of research reviewed in the previous sections should also be kept in mind when designing language courses that are in line with a lex-IP approach. One is that vocabulary learning is a slow, piecemeal process that requires multiple learning encounters that sometimes results in a low rate of return (with respect to lexical items taught/encountered and lexical items retained). However, it can be improved considerably by planning and implementing practices that have been shown to be effective. The second is that teachers and learners should utilize IP-informed approaches as a means of developing vocabulary knowledge beyond the initial stages of learning. The third is simply that teachers should avoid overwhelming students when learning new vocabulary by carefully considering the best ways to present vocabulary in a systematic way. I will consider the pedagogical implications of these conclusions in the rest of this section.

::: {.section aria-labelledby="sec23_6_1"}
### Vocabulary Learning as a Long-term, Carefully Planned Process {#ch23.xhtml_sec23_6_1}

The first step in setting up a teaching plan for vocabulary should involve identifying what vocabulary is most important for the learners (see [Barcroft, 2012](#ch23.xhtml_ref23_11){.biblioref role="doc-biblioref"}, p. 17, principle 1). This will obviously vary considerably based on the proficiency of students, students' needs (e.g., learners preparing for graduate studies in an English-speaking environment versus learners preparing to work as a tour guide, etc.). Regardless of the teaching/learning situation, however, teachers have a responsibility to identify the subsets of words that are essential for learners to succeed in their goals for the language. Although it is never possible to fully anticipate all the needs of one's learners, there are several resources available that can guide the identification of words and the selection of texts and other materials. A good place to start is by checking one of the *[]{#ch23.xhtml_p337 .pagebreak aria-label=" page 337. " role="doc-pagebreak"}frequency-based lists*, which are available not only for single words through online corpora, but also for multiword items (e.g., [Garnier & Schmitt, 2015](#ch23.xhtml_ref23_20){#ch23.xhtml_R_ref23_20 .biblioref role="doc-biblioref"}). There are also word lists targeted at specific learners, such as the Academic Word List ([Coxhead, 2000](#ch23.xhtml_ref23_15){#ch23.xhtml_R_ref23_15 .biblioref role="doc-biblioref"}). Similarly, when using authentic texts to promote vocabulary growth, a teacher can check the frequency profile of a text using online analysis tools, such as Tom Cobb's lextutor ([https://www.lextutor.ca/](https://www.lextutor.ca)). These tools can provide the percentages of words in different frequency bands, allowing instructors to determine if the text is at the right level for students.

Once target vocabulary has been identified, a teacher should endeavor to create a systematic procedure for how the vocabulary will be presented. This should incorporate a balance of implicit and explicit presentation with regular and repeated exposures. Though learners should, naturally, not be dissuaded from acquiring vocabulary incidentally, a more effective approach, and one that is very much in line with IBI principles, is to couple input for incidental vocabulary learning with input for intentional vocabulary learning. [Sonbul and Schmitt (2010)](#ch23.xhtml_ref23_40){#ch23.xhtml_R_ref23_40 .biblioref role="doc-biblioref"}, for example, studied the effectiveness of incidental vocabulary learning alone and incidental learning coupled with explicit instruction on form recall, meaning recognition, and meaning recall. The results showed significantly higher gains for the incidental learning + explicit instruction condition in all areas, leading the authors to conclude that "direct teaching of vocabulary during reading is definitely worth the effort" (p. 259). Even when explicit teaching is not incorporated and the immediate focus is not vocabulary, teachers should still approach language learning activities as opportunities for vocabulary growth through input. In reading comprehension activities, for example, a teacher using the IBI approach would make sure new words and *frontier words* (i.e., those words which are only vaguely known) are presented in sufficiently rich contexts, repeated regularly, and made more salient (e.g., through glosses and enhancement). Such steps will maximize the likelihood for incidental acquisition.

One potential challenge that arises here is the need to teach vocabulary in a systematic way (i.e., from a word frequency list) in a thematically grouped, input-rich manner that is in line with the principles of IBI. In some cases, such as with academic vocabulary taken from the Academic Word List, the groupings will be fairly straightforward since this list is designed to include vocabulary that is essential for learners in any academic field (see [Barcroft, 2012](#ch23.xhtml_ref23_11){.biblioref role="doc-biblioref"}, pp. 54--56). In situations where the target vocabulary items are selected on the basis of general frequency, however, the thematic connections will be less obvious. In such situations, it may be better for teachers to start instead with a topic that is useful and relevant to their learners, then selectively evaluate materials (such as a text) for their lexical usefulness. In some cases, this might involve replacing more obscure words with more commonly used synonyms, but such changes must be done in a judicious and principled way.
:::

::: {.section aria-labelledby="sec23_6_2"}
### All Sorts of Input for Long-term Vocabulary Development {#ch23.xhtml_sec23_6_2}

A second recommendation is related to vocabulary development after initial learning of novel words. Like many approaches to vocabulary instruction, the IBI approach advocates promoting continued development of vocabulary knowledge well after the initial stages of learning form and meaning. Exposure to different kinds of input supports this type of learning because it allows learners to encounter known words in a variety of contexts, which will often lead to a revision and refinement of their understanding of the semantic value of an L2 word. In some situations, extensive high-quality input will provide an opportunity for a learner to adjust the concept they associate with the L2 word, particularly in cases where the concept associated with an L1 translation for the L2 word only partially overlaps with that of the L2. Similarly (and perhaps relatedly given the connection between semantics and collocations), regular exposures []{#ch23.xhtml_p338 .pagebreak aria-label=" page 338. " role="doc-pagebreak"}help learners get a better sense of the range of collocations for a particular word. This knowledge becomes increasingly important for learners as they move from receptive use of target words to productive use. Additionally, exposure to input can also help learners develop better knowledge of derivational forms a word, the register of a word, etc. In fact, if one considers all that goes into "knowing" a word as presented by [Nation (2013, p. 49)](#ch23.xhtml_ref23_33){.biblioref role="doc-biblioref"}, it is easy to see how opportunities to receive input can benefit learners in refining their understanding of almost all aspects of vocabulary knowledge.

A question that arises here is *What sorts of input activities and approaches would be most beneficial for learners to develop such knowledge?* Although more research is needed before we can provide any definitive answers to these questions, one approach that is in line with the principles of IBI and that seems particularly promising is data-driven learning. Data-driven learning involves use of authentic corpora, and it often takes the form of presenting learners with a list of concordance lines focusing on particular target words or lexico-grammatical patterns. As noted by Boulton and Cobb ([2017](#ch23.xhtml_ref23_14){#ch23.xhtml_R_ref23_14 .biblioref role="doc-biblioref"}, pp. 350--351), the principles behind data-driven learning are well founded in current theoretical beliefs related to linguistic theory, learning theory, psycholinguistic theory, and SLA research findings. Equally important, as also revealed through Boulton and Cobb's extensive meta-analysis of 64 data-driven studies, data-driven learning has been shown to be highly effective; Boulton and Cobb's results indicated that most of the data-driven learning studies they evaluated demonstrated medium to very large effect sizes in respect to gains in vocabulary and lexico-grammatical knowledge.
:::

::: {.section aria-labelledby="sec23_6_3"}
### Not Overwhelming the Learner {#ch23.xhtml_sec23_6_3}

Finally, with respect to the third point, it is important to avoid overwhelming learners by pacing instruction carefully when teaching new vocabulary. Given the sheer volume of words that must be learned in order to function at even an advanced beginner level in an L2, teachers (and learners) may feel compelled to cover a great number of words in a short amount of time. While this is understandable, one of the most fundamental lessons that can be learned from the IP research to date is that teachers should take care not to overburden learners with too much learning-related activity in too short a time, as articulated in Barcroft's ten principles for effective IBI vocabulary teaching (2012, p. 17). Furthermore, teachers should not overburden learners with extensive and encompassing definitions for words that might have multiple meaning senses. Along these same lines, it is important to remember that even if initial gains in word knowledge are modest, they are still gains nonetheless. As was observed in several studies described above (e.g., Barcroft, [2004](#ch23.xhtml_ref23_7){.biblioref role="doc-biblioref"}, [2006](#ch23.xhtml_ref23_8){.biblioref role="doc-biblioref"}; [Waring & Takaki, 2003](#ch23.xhtml_ref23_44){.biblioref role="doc-biblioref"}; [Webb & Piasecki, 2018](#ch23.xhtml_ref23_45){.biblioref role="doc-biblioref"}), form recall and meaning recall can occur distinctly. However, research also suggests that form recall is often an important first step in developing a fuller understanding to a word, and as such, it makes sense to devote class time to overtly focusing on forms, even though the ultimate aim is to improve knowledge of form and meaning. It also makes sense to encourage students to view such gains as the first step to full learning to avoid feelings of discouragement.
:::
::::::

::: {.section aria-labelledby="sec23_7"}
## Future Directions {#ch23.xhtml_sec23_7}

A potentially fruitful approach for lex-IP research, at least as it relates to initial acquisition of forms and meanings, might be to try to merge incidental and intentional studies under a broader umbrella driven by questions related to return on investment in vocabulary learning (see [Barcroft, 2009](#ch23.xhtml_ref23_10){#ch23.xhtml_R_ref23_10 .biblioref role="doc-biblioref"}). In other words, if we continue to assume that acquiring a suitable large []{#ch23.xhtml_p339 .pagebreak aria-label=" page 339. " role="doc-pagebreak"}vocabulary as quickly as possible is a reasonable initial goal, then what sorts of tasks, activities, etc. are going to provide the greatest amount of vocabulary gains in the least amount of time? To accomplish this goal, the IBI approach advocates merging input-based incidental learning with intentional learning, but we still need a more precise understanding of the optimal balance of incidental versus intentional learning, including what combination of incidental and intentional learning tasks is most effective.

Another issue that warrants further investigation is the effects of input on words from different word classes. Though some studies have looked at the differential effects of word class on incidental vocabulary acquisition (e.g., [Kweon & Kim, 2008](#ch23.xhtml_ref23_30){#ch23.xhtml_R_ref23_30 .biblioref role="doc-biblioref"}), to the best of my knowledge, there have been no studies that have considered word class specifically from an IP theoretical framework. Additionally, some of the main underpinning assumptions for IP of syntax can also be applied to vocabulary if we take into account differences in word class. One such assumption is the idea that learners attend to linguistic information in accordance to the relative communicative value of the information. [VanPatten (2002, p. 759)](#ch23.xhtml_ref23_43){.biblioref role="doc-biblioref"} defines communicative value as "the meaning that a form contributes to overall sentence meaning." In syntactic processing, communicative value can be affected by a number of features, including the semantic value of part of an utterance and/or whether or not there is redundancy in meaning inherent in a particular utterance. If, for example, a beginning level learner is presented with the sentence *Yesterday, I played tennis*, then they might fail to attend to the grammatical morpheme signaling past tense given that the past nature of the event has already been established through the temporal adverb (and is therefore redundant).

In applying this to vocabulary, it could be argued that nouns and verbs typically carry more communicative value than adjectives and adverbs since adjectives serve largely to embellish nouns and adverbs often modify verbs or moderate the intensity of an adjective. Take the following sentence as an example:

> *Bighorn sheep reside primarily in extremely rugged mountains*.

A learner who is unfamiliar with the adverbs *primarily* and *extremely* and/or the adjective *rugged* would no doubt not fully understand the meaning of the sentence, but they would still be able to successfully grasp the general meaning (i.e., *Bighorn sheep reside in mountains*). If, however, the meaning of *sheep* or *reside* is unknown, then it is likely that the learner is going to struggle to grasp this general meaning. If we assume that learners tend to lend minimal attention to words that have less communicative value, then we might expect them to engage in a strategy of skipping adverbs and adjectives more than nouns and verbs. Although this hypothesis may seem self-evident, it is something that should be further investigated in vocabulary learning, particularly in incidental vocabulary acquisition.

Finally, more work needs to be done on the role of lex-IP in vocabulary development beyond the initial acquisition of forms and basic meanings. Though research is sparse, what has been done to date suggests that learners at all levels of proficiency struggle to acquire more complex knowledge of L2 words, even after a considerable number of exposures in immersive environments (e.g., [Crossley et al., 2010](#ch23.xhtml_ref23_17){#ch23.xhtml_R_ref23_17 .biblioref role="doc-biblioref"}; [Schmitt, 1998](#ch23.xhtml_ref23_37){#ch23.xhtml_R_ref23_37 .biblioref role="doc-biblioref"}). This finding suggests that input alone may not be sufficient, but it would still seem to be necessary since input seems particularly well-suited in revealing subtler aspects of word knowledge like extended metaphorical senses, semantic prosody, register, etc., (as well as more obvious aspects, such as collocations). Therefore, if we assume that input plays an important role in vocabulary development beyond the initial stages of learning, a number of important research questions are raised. For example, what conditions prompt learners to successfully identify gaps in their understanding of a particular lexical item? []{#ch23.xhtml_p340 .pagebreak aria-label=" page 340. " role="doc-pagebreak"}Additionally, once gaps have been identified, what types of input, activities, or both will help them to modify their knowledge to bring it more in line with that of an L1 speaker? Addressing questions such as these, as well as others relevant to vocabulary development, will help us to gain a fuller understanding of lexical development as a dynamic and ongoing process.
:::

::: {.section .reference aria-labelledby="r23_1"}

::: {.section .part role="doc-part"}
# []{#pt7.xhtml_p343 .pagebreak aria-label=" page 343. " role="doc-pagebreak"}[Part VII ]{.chnum .ordinal}[Methodological Issues in Research on Input Processing[]{#pt7.xhtml_p344 .pagebreak aria-label=" page 344 is blank. " role="doc-pagebreak"}]{.chtitle} {#pt7.xhtml_h1}
:::

[]{#ch24.xhtml}

::: {}
# []{#ch24.xhtml_p345 .pagebreak aria-label=" page 345. " role="doc-pagebreak"}[24 ]{.chnum .ordinal}[A Systematic Methodological Review of Offline Input Processing Research[^40]]{.chtitle} {#ch24.xhtml_h1}

Simón Ruiz

University of Tübingen

Patrick Rebuschat

Lancaster University

DOI: [10.4324/9781003041412-31](https://dx.doi.org/10.4324/9781003041412-31){aria-label="D.O.I. link to this document."}
:::

::: {.section .introduction role="doc-introduction"}
## Introduction {#ch24.xhtml_sec24_1}

Research on input processing (IP) investigates how second language (L2) learners process the language they encounter in oral or written input, seeking to understand the psycholinguistic principles that guide L2 comprehension and their impact on subsequent acquisition processes. Much of this research is applied in nature (i.e., more focused on second language classrooms) and entails the application of insights from VanPatten's ([1996](#ch24.xhtml_ref24_52){#ch24.xhtml_R_ref24_52 .biblioref role="doc-biblioref"}, [2004](#ch24.xhtml_ref24_53){#ch24.xhtml_R_ref24_53 .biblioref role="doc-biblioref"}, [2020](#ch24.xhtml_ref24_56){#ch24.xhtml_R_ref24_56 .biblioref role="doc-biblioref"}) IP model for the development of more effective pedagogical interventions (processing instruction \[PI\]). In this chapter, we focus on the methodology of, and methodological issues surrounding IP research by analyzing a corpus of 108 empirical studies that relied on offline measures of learning (see Jegerski, this volume, for online measures, and Marijuan, this volume on measures at the offline/online interface). In particular, we limit our review to research that examines sentence-level IP. These studies either tested the IP model or evaluated the effectiveness of PI. After briefly providing some background on IP research (historical perspectives, critical issues and topics, and current research), we herein present a systematic methodological review ([Chong & Plonsky, 2023](#ch24.xhtml_ref24_12){#ch24.xhtml_R_ref24_12 .biblioref role="doc-biblioref"}) of the general characteristics of this type of research (from research emphasis to preferred publication venues) and then describe key methodological features of IP studies in depth (participants, design features, treatment and outcome measures, statistics). We conclude with methodological recommendations and suggestions for future research on IP and PI. We hope that readers of this chapter will come away with a more thorough understanding of (methodological) strengths and weaknesses of existing research, which should enable them to further improve the methodology of future studies using offline measures.
:::

::: {.section aria-labelledby="sec24_2"}
## []{#ch24.xhtml_p346 .pagebreak aria-label=" page 346. " role="doc-pagebreak"}Historical Perspectives {#ch24.xhtml_sec24_2}

VanPatten's ([1996](#ch24.xhtml_ref24_52){.biblioref role="doc-biblioref"}, [2004](#ch24.xhtml_ref24_53){.biblioref role="doc-biblioref"}, [2020](#ch24.xhtml_ref24_56){.biblioref role="doc-biblioref"}) IP model explains how L2 learners parse language in input in order to establish form--meaning connections. The model provides a conceptual framework within which applied linguists and language educators can investigate the fundamental cognitive processes underlying L2 acquisition and apply these insights to the development of pedagogical interventions. In terms of methodology, studies on IP (e.g., [VanPatten, 1996](#ch24.xhtml_ref24_52){.biblioref role="doc-biblioref"}) require adult L2 learners to complete a series of language comprehension tasks, either in classroom or laboratory settings. The studies usually follow a one-shot design, using offline and/or online tasks to measure language processing.

At the heart of the IP model are a series of psycholinguistic principles that explain how L2 learners encode or filter incoming linguistic information when tasked with comprehension ([VanPatten, 2020](#ch24.xhtml_ref24_56){.biblioref role="doc-biblioref"}). The first set of principles is predicated upon the idea that because L2 have limited processing resources and are driven to get meaning during comprehension, they process linguistic input for meaning before they process it for form. That is, by default, learners seek primarily to understand what is being said rather than how it is being said. VanPatten called this the "primacy of meaning" in the 2004 version of the IP model ([VanPatten, 2004](#ch24.xhtml_ref24_53){.biblioref role="doc-biblioref"}). In the current version ([VanPatten, 2020](#ch24.xhtml_ref24_56){.biblioref role="doc-biblioref"}), the principles associated with the primacy of meaning include: (i) the primacy of content words principle; (ii) the lexical preference principle; (iii) the preference for nonredundancy principle; and (iv) the meaning-before-nonmeaning principle. The second main principle is the first-noun principle (FNP), which states that learners tend to process the first noun (or pronoun) in a given sentence as the subject or agent of that sentence. The principle thus focuses on basic sentence interpretation, in other words "who did what to whom." Related principles are (i) the lexical semantics principle, (ii) the event probabilities principle, and (iii) the contextual constraint principle. Finally, the sentence location principle states that learners tend to process items in initial position (the most salient position) before items in final and medial position. These principles illuminate the process of L2 comprehension, the essential first step in learning a new language, and they explain why sometimes language learners' processing biases will lead them astray, hampering their efforts of learning a new language (see Leeser, this volume, for a discussion of the principles of IP).

The IP model informed the development of a pedagogical intervention called processing instruction (PI) (VanPatten and Cadierno, [1993a](#ch24.xhtml_ref24_57){#ch24.xhtml_R_ref24_57 .biblioref role="doc-biblioref"}, [1993b](#ch24.xhtml_ref24_58){#ch24.xhtml_R_ref24_58 .biblioref role="doc-biblioref"}; VanPatten, [1996](#ch24.xhtml_ref24_52){.biblioref role="doc-biblioref"}, [2004](#ch24.xhtml_ref24_53){.biblioref role="doc-biblioref"}). PI seeks to alter learners' default IP strategies by providing them with (i) explicit information, including processing strategy training, and (ii) structured input (SI) activities that require them to understand and (correctly) process language forms during comprehension in order to complete the activity (task-essentialness; [Loschky & Bley-Vroman, 1993](#ch24.xhtml_ref24_33){#ch24.xhtml_R_ref24_33 .biblioref role="doc-biblioref"}; [Wong, 2004](#ch24.xhtml_ref24_61){#ch24.xhtml_R_ref24_61 .biblioref role="doc-biblioref"}). In short, PI aims to ensure that learners process the L2 input they receive accurately and efficiently, i.e., in such a way that the L2 acquisition process is facilitated. In terms of methodology, studies on PI (e.g., [VanPatten & Cadierno, 1993a](#ch24.xhtml_ref24_57){.biblioref role="doc-biblioref"}) usually follow a pretest--posttest design, using similar comprehension tasks to the ones used in IP research for testing participants before and after the pedagogical treatment. A key difference is that here a pedagogical intervention is administered.

Research on IP and PI has increased steadily in the past three decades. [Figure 24.1](#ch24.xhtml_fig24_1){#ch24.xhtml_R_fig24_1} illustrates the timeline of publications in applied linguistics and second language research that focus on the two strands of research. As can be observed, there has been an ever growing stream of empirical studies since the mid-1980s, with a more pronounced increase since the early 2000s. This increase appears to be driven by empirical studies on the effectiveness of PI.

![[[Figure]{.label} [24.1 ]{.ordinal}Cumulative number of publications reviewed herein that focus on IP and PI since Lee (1987). The focus is on 108 empirical studies that investigated the acquisition and teaching of L2 morphosyntax. The peaks in the years 2004, 2013 and 2015 of the PI timeline are partially explained by the publication, in those years, of edited collections on the topic. See Appendix 2 online for references.](#ch24.xhtml_R_fig24_1){.backlink aria-label=" This link returns to its first in-text citation. " role="doc-backlink"}](images/fig24_1_B.jpg){#fig24_1 alt="A graph that shows the cumulative number of publications reviewed that focus on I P and P I since 1987."}
:::

::: {.section aria-labelledby="sec24_3"}
## []{#ch24.xhtml_p347 .pagebreak aria-label=" page 347. " role="doc-pagebreak"}Critical Issues and Topics {#ch24.xhtml_sec24_3}

Research conducted within the framework of VanPatten's ([1996](#ch24.xhtml_ref24_52){.biblioref role="doc-biblioref"}, [2004](#ch24.xhtml_ref24_53){.biblioref role="doc-biblioref"}) IP model seeks to understand how learners process L2 input and to use these insights to optimize language instruction (via PI). For this reason, most empirical research in this area falls into two strands of inquiry, namely (i) studies that test the IP model (e.g., the different processing principles) and (ii) studies that evaluate the effectiveness of PI in L2 teaching. The majority of this research has focused on the acquisition, processing and teaching of morphosyntax ([Part IV](#pt4.xhtml) of this handbook), and this will also be the emphasis in this chapter. For more information on IP at the lexical, phonological, and pragmatic levels, we refer the reader to Part V of the handbook.

Many research topics have been addressed within the context of IP and PI (for recent reviews, see e.g., Benati, [2019](#ch24.xhtml_ref24_7){#ch24.xhtml_R_ref24_7 .biblioref role="doc-biblioref"}, [2020](#ch24.xhtml_ref24_8){#ch24.xhtml_R_ref24_8 .biblioref role="doc-biblioref"}; [Leeser, 2021](#ch24.xhtml_ref24_31){#ch24.xhtml_R_ref24_31 .biblioref role="doc-biblioref"}). These topics include the investigation of simultaneous attention to form and meaning (e.g., [VanPatten, 1996](#ch24.xhtml_ref24_52){.biblioref role="doc-biblioref"} and its replications); the role of acoustic salience of grammatical forms in IP (e.g., [Barcroft & VanPatten, 1997](#ch24.xhtml_ref24_6){#ch24.xhtml_R_ref24_6 .biblioref role="doc-biblioref"}); the comparison of PI to other types of interventions (e.g., output-based instruction) (e.g., VanPatten & Cadierno, [1993a](#ch24.xhtml_ref24_57){.biblioref role="doc-biblioref"}, [1993b](#ch24.xhtml_ref24_58){.biblioref role="doc-biblioref"}); the investigation of different types of PI (e.g., with or without explicit information, as in VanPatten & Cadierno, [1993a](#ch24.xhtml_ref24_57){.biblioref role="doc-biblioref"}, [1993b](#ch24.xhtml_ref24_58){.biblioref role="doc-biblioref"}; [VanPatten & Oikkenon, 1996](#ch24.xhtml_ref24_59){#ch24.xhtml_R_ref24_59 .biblioref role="doc-biblioref"}); the long-term effects of PI (e.g., [Cadierno, 1995](#ch24.xhtml_ref24_11){#ch24.xhtml_R_ref24_11 .biblioref role="doc-biblioref"}; [Villegas & Morgan-Short, 2019](#ch24.xhtml_ref24_60){#ch24.xhtml_R_ref24_60 .biblioref role="doc-biblioref"}); the primary and secondary []{#ch24.xhtml_p348 .pagebreak aria-label=" page 348. " role="doc-pagebreak"}transfer-of-training effects of PI (e.g., [Benati & Lee, 2008](#ch24.xhtml_ref24_10){#ch24.xhtml_R_ref24_10 .biblioref role="doc-biblioref"}; [Lee, 2019](#ch24.xhtml_ref24_28){#ch24.xhtml_R_ref24_28 .biblioref role="doc-biblioref"}); PI and discourse effects (e.g., [Sanz, 2004](#ch24.xhtml_ref24_46){#ch24.xhtml_R_ref24_46 .biblioref role="doc-biblioref"}; [Benati & Batziou, 2019](#ch24.xhtml_ref24_9){#ch24.xhtml_R_ref24_9 .biblioref role="doc-biblioref"}); and the role of individual differences on PI (e.g., [Lee & Benati, 2013](#ch24.xhtml_ref24_29){#ch24.xhtml_R_ref24_29 .biblioref role="doc-biblioref"}). Naturally, many of these important issues await further investigation, as do several themes that received relatively little attention in the past. The contributions in Parts I and VI of the handbook will provide a helpful summary of what has been researched and what research is still lacking, and our recommendations below will hopefully contribute to further methodological refinement of future studies that address these topics.
:::

::::::::::::::::::::::: {.section aria-labelledby="sec24_4"}
## Systematic Literature Review from 1987 to 2020 {#ch24.xhtml_sec24_4}

To analyze the methodology of empirical studies on IP or PI, we first conducted a comprehensive literature search to identify empirical studies that focus on IP or PI. Specifically, we sought to identify studies that satisfied the following criteria.

Firstly, the study had to empirically test VanPatten's ([1996](#ch24.xhtml_ref24_52){.biblioref role="doc-biblioref"}, [2004](#ch24.xhtml_ref24_53){.biblioref role="doc-biblioref"}) model of IP (e.g., the processing principles) or evaluate the effectiveness of PI. In the latter case, the study had to meet two additional subcriteria, namely (i) the selection of the target feature had to be guided by a processing principle and (ii) SI activities were designed to modify learners' processing strategies ([Wong, 2004](#ch24.xhtml_ref24_61){.biblioref role="doc-biblioref"}; see [Shintani, 2014](#ch24.xhtml_ref24_48){#ch24.xhtml_R_ref24_48 .biblioref role="doc-biblioref"}, for similar criteria). Secondly, the study had to be published in a peer-reviewed journal, an edited volume, conference proceedings, or in a monograph. Master's theses and doctoral dissertations were excluded. The publication cut-off date for inclusion was July 2020. Thirdly, the study had to use offline measures of learning, i.e., reading or listening tasks that measured what was learned *after* input exposure (research employing both offline and offline measures is discussed in [Chapter 26](#ch26.xhtml); online measures are discussed in [Chapter 25](#ch25.xhtml) of the handbook). Fourthly, the study had to focus on the acquisition, processing or teaching of L2 morphosyntax. Finally, the study had to be published in English. We are aware that our criteria potentially exclude articles that might have been of interest, e.g., studies published in Spanish, a language that has played a prominent role in second language acquisition (SLA) research, or studies that focus on the lexical, phonological, and pragmatic levels. However, the inclusion of papers in languages other than English or that investigated other aspects of language would have greatly exceeded the scope of the present chapter. We encourage other researchers to address this shortcoming and to provide a complementary perspective to our synthesis.

Our literature search and the application of the criteria above resulted in a corpus of 108 empirical studies; the reference list can be found in the online supplementary materials (Appendix 1). After consulting previous methodological reviews (e.g., [Plonsky, 2013](#ch24.xhtml_ref24_40){#ch24.xhtml_R_ref24_40 .biblioref role="doc-biblioref"}; [Sok et al., 2019](#ch24.xhtml_ref24_50){#ch24.xhtml_R_ref24_50 .biblioref role="doc-biblioref"}), we decided to classify the studies based on six broad categories, covering a wide range of variables that are relevant to the characterization of the studies in our corpus and their methodologies.

1.  General characteristics: research focus, geographical location, educational context, settings of data collection, publication venues;
2.  Participants: sample size, age, proficiency, native languages, target languages;
3.  Research design: type of research design, type of experiments, number of groups, group size, target structure;
4.  Treatment and outcome measures: number and duration of sessions, response mode, types of tasks, awareness measures, individual difference measures, piloting of materials;
5.  Statistics: test types, effect sizes, reliability measures, power analyses.

After agreeing on the coding scheme (see Appendix 2, supplementary materials), the first author coded the studies, checking with the second author in case of doubts. The second author double []{#ch24.xhtml_p349 .pagebreak aria-label=" page 349. " role="doc-pagebreak"}checked the application of the criteria in a random sample of 10 percent of papers. To assess inter-rater agreement, Cohen's kappa was computed. There was almost perfect agreement between the two authors, kappa = 0.997.

We have created an online repository to share the list of 108 included studies, the coding criteria, and additional tables and figures from our analysis. Tables and figures that are only available in the supplementary materials online are marked by the letter S before the table number (e.g., Table S1, Appendix 3, supplementary materials). We encourage the reader to make use and to build on these materials, which are freely available at both the Open Science Framework ([https://osf.io/p9mc2/](https://osf.io)), and the IRIS database (<https://www.iris-database.org>).

::::: {.section aria-labelledby="sec24_4_1"}
### Main Findings of Systematic Literature Review {#ch24.xhtml_sec24_4_1}

In this section, we characterize the methodology employed in the 108 studies that constitute our corpus. As mentioned, our focus is on studies that employed offline measures of learning, thus complementing the emphasis on studies with both offline and online measures in [Chapter 26](#ch26.xhtml), and online measures in [Chapter 25](#ch25.xhtml).

:::: {.section aria-labelledby="sec24_4_1_1"}
#### General Characteristics {#ch24.xhtml_sec24_4_1_1}

In terms of research focus, the analysis revealed that 69% of the studies (74 out of 108) focused on the evaluation of PI as a pedagogical intervention, while the remaining studies (31%) tested the IP model. As far as the investigation of processing principles is concerned, almost all empirical studies focused either on one of the primacy of meaning principles or the FNP in isolation, with only two studies out of 108 investigating both principles within the same study. It is worth noting that the analysis presented here was based on [VanPatten's 2004](#ch24.xhtml_ref24_53){.biblioref role="doc-biblioref"} model of IP. In the current version of the model ([VanPatten, 2020](#ch24.xhtml_ref24_56){.biblioref role="doc-biblioref"}), the "primacy of meaning" principle was reorganized slightly differently (see above). The most widely studied was the lexical preference principle (71%), followed by the preference for nonredundancy principle (16%) and the sentence location principle (7%). [Table 24.1](#ch24.xhtml_table24_1){#ch24.xhtml_R_table24_1} summarizes the main principles investigated in the studies represented in our corpus. For a summary of the subprinciples that were investigated, please consult Table S1 online.

::: {.section .tscroll}

| *Principle*             | *k*         | *percent*   | *k*         | *percent*   | *k* | *percent* |
+=========================+=============+=============+=============+=============+=====+===========+
| FNP                     | 16          | 47          | 37          | 50          | 53  | 49        |
+-------------------------+-------------+-------------+-------------+-------------+-----+-----------+
| Primacy of Meaning[^41] | 18          | 53          | 35          | 47          | 53  | 49        |
+-------------------------+-------------+-------------+-------------+-------------+-----+-----------+
| Both                    |             |             | 2           | 3           | 2   | 2         |
+-------------------------+-------------+-------------+-------------+-------------+-----+-----------+
| Total                   | 34          | 100         | 74          | 100         | 108 | 100       |
+-------------------------+-------------+-------------+-------------+-------------+-----+-----------+

<aside aria-label="Notes for Table 24.1" class="tfoot">

*Note:*

1.  [[^a^](#ch24.xhtml_R_table-fn24_1_1){.backlink aria-label=" This link returns to its first in-text citation. " role="doc-backlink"} This analysis was based on the 2004 version of the model of IP ([VanPatten, 2004](#ch24.xhtml_ref24_53){.biblioref role="doc-biblioref"}). The focus here is on the two main principles. For subprinciples, please see Table S1.]{#ch24.xhtml_table-fn24_1_1}

</aside>
:::

In terms of geography, the studies were conducted across 15 different countries, with most of them taking place in the United States (64%), followed by the United Kingdom (11%). The []{#ch24.xhtml_p350 .pagebreak aria-label=" page 350. " role="doc-pagebreak"}majority of the research (80%) was conducted at universities, which is probably true for most second language research (as it is for research in cognitive psychology), a shortcoming researchers are trying to address by conducting, and also encouraging, research in other contexts (see [Andringa & Godfroid, 2020](#ch24.xhtml_ref24_5){#ch24.xhtml_R_ref24_5 .biblioref role="doc-biblioref"}). The data was mostly collected in classroom (70%) or laboratory (23%) settings. Tables S2 through S4 characterize the contexts in which the 108 empirical studies in our corpus were carried out.

As far as publication venues are concerned, most studies in our corpus were either published as journal articles (64%) or as book chapters in edited volumes or monographs (32%). The preferred journals for IP and PI research are *Hispania* and *Studies in Second Language Acquisition*, followed by *Language Learning*, the *International Review of Applied Linguistics in Language Teaching*, and the *Modern Language Journal*. Tables S5 and S6 provide more detailed information on publication venues for both research strands.
::::
:::::

::::::::::::::: {.section aria-labelledby="sec24_4_2"}
### Participants {#ch24.xhtml_sec24_4_2}

The studies in our sample had a total of 9,258 participants, and the median number of participants size was 62. This median size is almost the same (i.e., 61) as the one reported in a recent methodological review of L2 research ([Sok et al., 2019](#ch24.xhtml_ref24_50){.biblioref role="doc-biblioref"}). Our analysis further showed that 85% of studies recruited and tested adult participants, suggesting that research with child or adolescent populations is lacking. Participants in the studies were either classified as having low (46%) or intermediate levels of proficiency (35%). In terms of language background, eight first languages and ten target languages were covered across the 108 studies in our corpus, with English the most frequent L1 (73% of studies) and Spanish the most frequent L2 (61%). The predominance of this specific language constellation (L1 English, L2 Spanish) may stem from our search restriction to studies published in English, but it may also coincide with the languages studied in the seminal work of VanPatten and colleagues ([VanPatten, 2012](#ch24.xhtml_ref24_54){#ch24.xhtml_R_ref24_54 .biblioref role="doc-biblioref"}; VanPatten & Cadierno, [1993a](#ch24.xhtml_ref24_57){.biblioref role="doc-biblioref"}, [1993b](#ch24.xhtml_ref24_58){.biblioref role="doc-biblioref"}) and reflects the fact that research on IP and PI developed primarily in Spanish and Portuguese Departments in North American universities. However, we are witnessing an increasing diversification of the first and second languages studied, which over time will provide an important complementary perspective on the foundational work done on English and Spanish. [Table 24.2](#ch24.xhtml_table24_2){#ch24.xhtml_R_table24_2}, [Table 24.3](#ch24.xhtml_table24_3){#ch24.xhtml_R_table24_3}, [Table 24.4](#ch24.xhtml_table24_4){#ch24.xhtml_R_table24_4}, [Table 24.5](#ch24.xhtml_table24_5){#ch24.xhtml_R_table24_5} and [Table 24.6](#ch24.xhtml_table24_6){#ch24.xhtml_R_table24_6} provide additional information on the participants recruited for research on IP and PI.[]{#ch24.xhtml_p351 .pagebreak aria-label=" page 351. Table on this page. " role="doc-pagebreak"}
