::::: {.section aria-labelledby="sec24_4_2_1"}
#### []{#ch24.xhtml_p352 .pagebreak aria-label=" page 352. " role="doc-pagebreak"}Research Design {#ch24.xhtml_sec24_4_2_1}

[Tables 24.7](#ch24.xhtml_table24_7){#ch24.xhtml_R_table24_7} and [24.8](#ch24.xhtml_table24_8){#ch24.xhtml_R_table24_8} summarize the design characteristics of the 108 studies in the corpus. The majority of studies in the corpus were quantitative in nature, namely 94% of the IP and 99% of the PI studies. As the tables illustrate, most studies (82%) were quasi-experimental, meaning that participants were not randomly assigned to experimental and control conditions. This could be due to the fact that much of the research was conducted in classroom settings, and so entire classrooms were assigned to different conditions. In terms of research design, IP studies usually follow a one-shot, within-subject design (97%), which is characteristic of psycholinguistic studies on language processing. In this type of design, participants are tested during a single session on a variety of tasks. In contrast, all PI studies in our corpus followed pretest--posttest, between-subjects designs. That is, studies in this strand first tested participants' existing knowledge of the target structure, then assigned them to different experimental conditions and administered the treatment(s), and finally measured the effect of the intervention(s) by means of a posttest.

::: {.section .tscroll}
+--------------------+-------------------+------------------+------------------+
|                    | IP Model          | PI               | Total            |
+--------------------+------+------------+-----+------------+-----+------------+
| *Type of Research* | *k*  | *percent*  | *k* | *percent*  | *k* | *percent*  |
+====================+======+============+=====+============+=====+============+
| Quantitative       | 29   | 85         | 73  | 99         | 102 | 94         |
+--------------------+------+------------+-----+------------+-----+------------+
| Mixed              | 5    | 15         | 1   | 1          | 6   | 6          |
+--------------------+------+------------+-----+------------+-----+------------+
| Total              | 34   | 100        | 74  | 100        | 108 | 100        |
+--------------------+------+------------+-----+------------+-----+------------+

: [[Table ]{.label}[24.7 ]{.ordinal}Types of Research](#ch24.xhtml_R_table24_7){.backlink aria-label=" This link returns to its first in-text citation. " role="doc-backlink"} {#table24_7}
:::

::: {.section .tscroll}
+-----------------------+-------------------+------------------+------------------+
|                       | IP Model          | PI               | Total            |
+-----------------------+------+------------+-----+------------+-----+------------+
| *Types of Experiment* | *k*  | *percent*  | *k* | *percent*  | *k* | *percent*  |
+=======================+======+============+=====+============+=====+============+
| Quasi-experimental    | 29   | 85         | 60  | 81         | 89  | 82         |
+-----------------------+------+------------+-----+------------+-----+------------+
| Experimental          | 5    | 15         | 14  | 19         | 19  | 18         |
+-----------------------+------+------------+-----+------------+-----+------------+
| Total                 | 34   | 100        | 74  | 100        | 108 | 100        |
+-----------------------+------+------------+-----+------------+-----+------------+

: [[Table ]{.label}[24.8 ]{.ordinal}Types of Experiment](#ch24.xhtml_R_table24_8){.backlink aria-label=" This link returns to its first in-text citation. " role="doc-backlink"} {#table24_8}

<aside aria-label="Notes for Table 24.8" class="tfoot">

*Note:* Experimental = Studies used random assignment to allocate participants to the different groups. Quasi-experimental = Studies that did not employ random assignment and used intact groups or existing classes ([Sok et al., 2019](#ch24.xhtml_ref24_50){.biblioref role="doc-biblioref"}).

</aside>
:::

In terms of group number, 85% percent of studies have more than one group (Table S7). The most frequent number of groups was three (32%), followed by two (22%) and four groups (13%). Nine percent did not mention the distribution of the groups; only the final sample size. This distribution of participants seems to be fairly similar to the typical number of groups found in []{#ch24.xhtml_p353 .pagebreak aria-label=" page 353. " role="doc-pagebreak"}L2 research ([Plonsky, 2013](#ch24.xhtml_ref24_40){.biblioref role="doc-biblioref"}). Table S8 and Figure 2 display information about average sample size per group. IP studies had an average sample size per group of 28 participants, whereas in PI research the average sample size per group was 21. Overall, as can be seen in [Figure 24.2](#ch24.xhtml_fig24_2){#ch24.xhtml_R_fig24_2}, there has been little change over time in the average sample size per group in both IP and PI research.

![[[Figure]{.label} [24.2 ]{.ordinal}Average sample size per group of participants in IP and PI since Lee (1987). The focus is on empirical studies that investigated the acquisition and teaching of L2 morphosyntax.](#ch24.xhtml_R_fig24_2){.backlink aria-label=" This link returns to its first in-text citation. " role="doc-backlink"}](images/fig24_2_B.jpg){#fig24_2 alt="A graph that shows the average sample size per group of participants in I P and P I research since 1987."}

Figure S1, Tables S9 and S10 summarize the most frequently investigated (morphosyntactic) structures across the studies. In terms of target structure, our analysis indicates that, overall, the most frequently investigated language structure was the Spanish direct object pronoun, followed by the Spanish subjunctive. Looking at the two strands, we found that the Spanish direct object pronoun was also the most widely researched in PI studies. In IP research, on the other hand, the most extensively investigated target feature was the Spanish future tense.
:::::

::::::: {.section aria-labelledby="sec24_4_2_2"}
#### []{#ch24.xhtml_p354 .pagebreak aria-label=" page 354. " role="doc-pagebreak"}Treatment and Outcome Measures {#ch24.xhtml_sec24_4_2_2}

We first analyzed the frequency and duration of experimental sessions in the studies. Below we characterize two types of sessions, namely pedagogical treatment sessions (only for PI research, as IP studies do not include treatments) and test sessions (for both IP and PI research).

With regards to treatment sessions, we found that the majority of PI studies had either one instructional session (39% of studies) or two instructional sessions (49%). Seven percent of studies had three or more instructional sessions, and four percent did not report the number of instructional sessions. In terms of treatment duration, 50% of studies administered the treatment sessions over two days, 41% on one day, and the remaining 8% spread over several days. Generally, we can assume that each treatment session is administered on a different day, though the analysis shows that this is not always the case. When looking at the studies that reported treatment duration in minutes/hours (55/74), the total treatment time was about two hours (median). The prevalence of short pedagogical treatments is consistent with previous findings in L2 methodological syntheses (e.g., [Norris & Ortega, 2000](#ch24.xhtml_ref24_38){#ch24.xhtml_R_ref24_38 .biblioref role="doc-biblioref"}; [Sok et al., 2019](#ch24.xhtml_ref24_50){.biblioref role="doc-biblioref"}). Tables S11 through S17 show how pedagogical treatments were delivered in PI studies. Most studies (74%) delivered instruction to a group, and about a quarter (23%) delivered instruction individually. Only 2% of studies used both group and individual modes of instruction delivery. In terms of medium of treatment administration, the majority of studies (74%) implemented face-to-face treatments. Almost a quarter of studies (23%) delivered instruction by means of computers. Finally, only 2% of studies implemented mixed face-to-face and computer-based instruction.

With regards to the test sessions (outcome measures), we found that most IP studies consisted of a single, brief session, i.e., lasting less than one hour. In the case of PI research, 57% of studies had three test sessions (pretest, posttest, and delayed posttest), 30% of studies had two test sessions (one pretest and one posttest), and 11% of studies had four test sessions (pretest, posttest, and two delayed posttests). We have no information about the duration of these test sessions but given that the tasks are similar to the one used in IP research, we assume the session length to be similar, i.e., each test session lasting less than one hour.

Tables S18 and 10 provide a summary of outcome measures, i.e., the dependent variables used in testing phase of the studies. In terms of response mode, outcome measures were classified as aural, written or both. Again, differences between the two strands are noticeable. In IP research, the most common response mode is aural (53%), followed by written (38%). Only 9% of studies used outcome measures that require both response modes. In contrast, in PI research, the majority of studies (76%) use outcome measures that require both response modes.

In terms of task types, outcome measures required participants to provide (i) a selected response (e.g., selecting the appropriate picture after listening to an L2 sentence, cf. [VanPatten & Cadierno, 1993a](#ch24.xhtml_ref24_57){.biblioref role="doc-biblioref"}); (ii) a free constructed response (e.g., prompting participants to recall as much as they can after they have read an L2 text, cf. Lee, 2002); (iii) a constrained constructed response (e.g., asking participants to complete sentences by providing the correctly conjugated verb form, cf. Lee, 2002); or (iv) a metalinguistic judgment (e.g., judging grammaticality of L2 sentences, cf. Potowsky et al., 2017). Following [Norris and Ortega (2000)](#ch24.xhtml_ref24_38){.biblioref role="doc-biblioref"}, we coded tasks that required extended communicative use (here, free constructed responses) as measuring primarily implicit knowledge and tasks that required learning to use the L2 in discrete and focused linguistic tasks (selected response, constrained constructed response, metalinguistic judgments) as measuring primarily explicit knowledge.

Our analysis showed that, in IP research, two outcome measures are widely used, namely selected response (50%) and free constructed response (41%) ([Table 24.9](#ch24.xhtml_table24_9){#ch24.xhtml_R_table24_9}). In contrast, in PI, outcome measures with selected responses are easily the most widely used, occurring in 81% of []{#ch24.xhtml_p355 .pagebreak aria-label=" page 355. " role="doc-pagebreak"}the studies. Constrained constructed responses occur rarely, and metalinguistic judgments are practically not used in either strand (1 out of 108 studies). If we analyze these tasks in terms of the measurement of implicit and explicit knowledge (see [Isbell & Rogers, 2021](#ch24.xhtml_ref24_27){#ch24.xhtml_R_ref24_27 .biblioref role="doc-biblioref"}; [Rebuschat, 2013](#ch24.xhtml_ref24_44){#ch24.xhtml_R_ref24_44 .biblioref role="doc-biblioref"}, for overviews), we find that the majority of studies employed explicit tests (86%), and only 14% implicit tests ([Table 24.10](#ch24.xhtml_table24_10){#ch24.xhtml_R_table24_10}). This finding is congruent with that of previous methodologically oriented L2 research ([Norris & Ortega, 2000](#ch24.xhtml_ref24_38){.biblioref role="doc-biblioref"}; [Spada & Tomita, 2010](#ch24.xhtml_ref24_51){#ch24.xhtml_R_ref24_51 .biblioref role="doc-biblioref"}; [Sok et al., 2019](#ch24.xhtml_ref24_50){.biblioref role="doc-biblioref"}). Moreover, no measures of awareness were reported (e.g., verbal reports). Only one study ([Villegas & Morgan-Short, 2019](#ch24.xhtml_ref24_60){.biblioref role="doc-biblioref"}) indicated that measures of awareness were included in the design (confidence ratings and source attributions) but the results were not reported in the paper (this was indicated in a footnote).

::: {.section .tscroll}
+----------------------------------+-------------------+------------------+------------------+
|                                  | IP Model          | PI               | Total            |
+----------------------------------+------+------------+-----+------------+-----+------------+
| *Outcome Measure*                | *k*  | *percent*  | *k* | *percent*  | *k* | *percent*  |
+==================================+======+============+=====+============+=====+============+
| Selected response                | 17   | 50         | 70  | 95         | 87  | 81         |
+----------------------------------+------+------------+-----+------------+-----+------------+
| Free constructed response        | 14   | 41         | 1   | 1          | 15  | 14         |
+----------------------------------+------+------------+-----+------------+-----+------------+
| Constrained constructed response | 3    | 9          | 2   | 3          | 5   | 5          |
+----------------------------------+------+------------+-----+------------+-----+------------+
| Metalinguistic judgment          |      |            | 1   | 1          | 1   | 1          |
+----------------------------------+------+------------+-----+------------+-----+------------+
| Total                            | 34   | 100        | 74  | 100        | 108 | 100        |
+----------------------------------+------+------------+-----+------------+-----+------------+

:::

::: {.section .tscroll}
+------------------------+-------------------+------------------+------------------+
|                        | IP Model          | PI               | Total            |
+------------------------+------+------------+-----+------------+-----+------------+
| *Measure of Knowledge* | *k*  | *percent*  | *k* | *percent*  | *k* | *percent*  |
+========================+======+============+=====+============+=====+============+
| Explicit               | 20   | 59         | 73  | 99         | 93  | 86         |

| Implicit               | 14   | 41         | 1   | 1          | 15  | 14         |

: [[Table ]{.label}[24.10 ]{.ordinal}Type of Measure of Second Language Knowledge](#ch24.xhtml_R_table24_10){.backlink aria-label=" This link returns to its first in-text citation. " role="doc-backlink"} {#table24_10}
:::

We also analyzed if the studies included individual difference variables (defined herein as any measurable personal characteristic, [Granena & Yilmaz, 2018](#ch24.xhtml_ref24_22){#ch24.xhtml_R_ref24_22 .biblioref role="doc-biblioref"}) as part of the research design. We found that this was only the case for 13% of studies, with little difference between the two research strands. There are differences, however, in the selection of individual difference variables. In the case of IP, we found that only working memory capacity was explored as an individual difference variable, whereas in the case of PI, age was the most widely studied variable (42%), followed by working memory and language background (17% each). Tables S19 and 12 summarize these findings ([Table 24.11](#ch24.xhtml_table24_11){#ch24.xhtml_R_table24_11}).

::: {.section .tscroll}
+-------------------------+-------------------+------------------+------------------+
|                         | IP Model          | PI               | Total            |
+-------------------------+------+------------+-----+------------+-----+------------+
| *Individual Difference* | *n*  | *percent*  | *k* | *percent*  | *k* | *percent*  |
+=========================+======+============+=====+============+=====+============+
| Age                     |      |            | 5   | 42         | 5   | 36         |
+-------------------------+------+------------+-----+------------+-----+------------+
| Working memory          | 2    | 100        | 2   | 17         | 4   | 29         |
+-------------------------+------+------------+-----+------------+-----+------------+
| Language background     |      |            | 2   | 17         | 2   | 14         |
+-------------------------+------+------------+-----+------------+-----+------------+
| Aptitude                |      |            | 1   | 8          | 1   | 7          |
+-------------------------+------+------------+-----+------------+-----+------------+
| Gender                  |      |            | 1   | 8          | 1   | 7          |
+-------------------------+------+------------+-----+------------+-----+------------+
| Proficiency[^44]        |      |            | 1   | 8          | 1   | 7          |
+-------------------------+------+------------+-----+------------+-----+------------+
| Total                   | 2    | 100        | 12  | 100        | 14  | 100        |
+-------------------------+------+------------+-----+------------+-----+------------+

: [[Table ]{.label}[24.11 ]{.ordinal}Individual Differences](#ch24.xhtml_R_table24_11){.backlink aria-label=" This link returns to its first in-text citation. " role="doc-backlink"} {#table24_11}

<aside aria-label="Notes for Table 24.11" class="tfoot">

*Note:*

1.  [[^a^](#ch24.xhtml_R_table-fn24_11_1){.backlink aria-label=" This link returns to its first in-text citation. " role="doc-backlink"} Lee (2019) treated language experience (i.e., proficiency) as an individual difference (see also [Lee & Doherty, 2020](#ch24.xhtml_ref24_30){#ch24.xhtml_R_ref24_30 .biblioref role="doc-biblioref"}, for a similar treatment; and our definition of "individual difference variables" above).]{#ch24.xhtml_table-fn24_11_1}

</aside>
:::

Finally, concerning instrument piloting, only 8% of studies reported having piloted and/or revised their instruments ([Table 24.12](#ch24.xhtml_table24_12){#ch24.xhtml_R_table24_12}). This small percentage is similar to that reported by [Derrick (2016)](#ch24.xhtml_ref24_19){#ch24.xhtml_R_ref24_19 .biblioref role="doc-biblioref"} for L2 research.

::: {.section .tscroll}

+=======================+======+============+=====+============+=====+============+

:::
:::::::
:::::::::::::::

:::::: {.section aria-labelledby="sec24_4_3"}
### []{#ch24.xhtml_p356 .pagebreak aria-label=" page 356. " role="doc-pagebreak"}Statistics {#ch24.xhtml_sec24_4_3}

With regards to the statistics reported in the 108 studies, the majority described the results of mean-based analyses, primarily analyses of variance (ANOVAs) (74%), followed by *t-*tests (9%). There were no major differences between the two research strands ([Table 24.13](#ch24.xhtml_table24_13){#ch24.xhtml_R_table24_13}). The widespread preference for means-based analyses, and the use of ANOVA as the default analytical tool, is in line with common L2 research practices (e.g., [Norris & Ortega, 2000](#ch24.xhtml_ref24_38){.biblioref role="doc-biblioref"}; Plonsky, [2013](#ch24.xhtml_ref24_40){.biblioref role="doc-biblioref"}, [2014](#ch24.xhtml_ref24_41){#ch24.xhtml_R_ref24_41 .biblioref role="doc-biblioref"}). The use of novel analytical techniques such as mixed-effect modeling ([Cunnings, 2012](#ch24.xhtml_ref24_16){#ch24.xhtml_R_ref24_16 .biblioref role="doc-biblioref"}) and Bayesian statistical modeling ([Norouzian et al., 2018](#ch24.xhtml_ref24_37){#ch24.xhtml_R_ref24_37 .biblioref role="doc-biblioref"}) was almost nonexistent, as they were used only by two studies (3%) in the IP strand ([Morgan-Short et al., 2018](#ch24.xhtml_ref24_36){#ch24.xhtml_R_ref24_36 .biblioref role="doc-biblioref"}; [Seibert Hanson & Carlson, 2014](#ch24.xhtml_ref24_49){#ch24.xhtml_R_ref24_49 .biblioref role="doc-biblioref"}). Of which, the same study ([Morgan-Short et al., 2018](#ch24.xhtml_ref24_36){.biblioref role="doc-biblioref"}; [Potowski et al., 2009](#ch24.xhtml_ref24_43){#ch24.xhtml_R_ref24_43 .biblioref role="doc-biblioref"}) used both mixed effects modeling and Bayesian data analysis in the same paper.

::: {.section .tscroll}
+-----------------------------------+-------------------+------------------+------------------+
|                                   | IP Model          | PI               | Total            |
+-----------------------------------+------+------------+-----+------------+-----+------------+
| *Type of Analysis*                | *k*  | *percent*  | *k* | *percent*  | *k* | *percent*  |
+===================================+======+============+=====+============+=====+============+
| ANOVA                             | 26   | 54         | 71  | 86         | 97  | 74         |
+-----------------------------------+------+------------+-----+------------+-----+------------+
| *t-*test                          | 6    | 12         | 6   | 7          | 12  | 9          |
+-----------------------------------+------+------------+-----+------------+-----+------------+
| Chi-square                        | 6    | 12         |     |            | 6   | 5          |
+-----------------------------------+------+------------+-----+------------+-----+------------+
| Correlation                       | 3    | 6          | 3   | 4          | 6   | 5          |
+-----------------------------------+------+------------+-----+------------+-----+------------+
| MANOVA                            | 1    | 2          | 1   | 1          | 2   | 2          |
+-----------------------------------+------+------------+-----+------------+-----+------------+
| Mixed-effects logistic regression | 2    | 4          |     |            | 2   | 2          |
+-----------------------------------+------+------------+-----+------------+-----+------------+
| Logistic regression               | 2    | 4          |     |            | 2   | 2          |
+-----------------------------------+------+------------+-----+------------+-----+------------+
| ANCOVA                            | 1    | 2          |     |            | 1   | 1          |
+-----------------------------------+------+------------+-----+------------+-----+------------+
| Bayes factors                     | 1    | 2          |     |            | 1   | 1          |
+-----------------------------------+------+------------+-----+------------+-----+------------+
| Nonparametrics                    |      |            | 2   | 2          | 2   | 2          |
+-----------------------------------+------+------------+-----+------------+-----+------------+
| Total                             | 48   | 100        | 83  | 100        | 131 | 100        |
+-----------------------------------+------+------------+-----+------------+-----+------------+

: [[Table ]{.label}[24.13 ]{.ordinal}Types of Statistical Analyses](#ch24.xhtml_R_table24_13){.backlink aria-label=" This link returns to its first in-text citation. " role="doc-backlink"} {#table24_13}
:::

In terms of the reporting of effect sizes, which describe the relationship strength among variables, only 21% of studies reported any effect sizes (Table S20). When effect sizes were reported, eta squared was the most commonly reported effect size estimate (59%), followed by Cohen's *d* (22%) ([Table 24.14](#ch24.xhtml_table24_14){#ch24.xhtml_R_table24_14}). Eta squared is an estimate of how much of the total variance in the data is accounted for by the main effects, errors, or interactions with an ANOVA. Therefore, its high occurrence in the current sample is consistent with the highly frequent use of ANOVAs. The small percentage of effect size reporting in our corpus is consistent with previous research that has found that effect size estimates are still not widely reported in L2 research ([Norris & Ortega, 2000](#ch24.xhtml_ref24_38){.biblioref role="doc-biblioref"}; [Plonsky 2013](#ch24.xhtml_ref24_40){.biblioref role="doc-biblioref"}), although they, and confidence intervals, should be.

::: {.section .tscroll}
+----------------+-------------------+------------------+------------------+
|                | IP Model          | PI               | Total            |
+----------------+------+------------+-----+------------+-----+------------+
| *Effect Size*  | *k*  | *percent*  | *k* | *percent*  | *k* | *percent*  |
+================+======+============+=====+============+=====+============+
| eta squared    | 5    | 45         | 14  | 67         | 19  | 59         |
+----------------+------+------------+-----+------------+-----+------------+
| Cohen's *d*    | 1    | 9          | 6   | 29         | 7   | 22         |
+----------------+------+------------+-----+------------+-----+------------+
| Log odds ratio | 3    | 27         |     |            | 3   | 9          |
+----------------+------+------------+-----+------------+-----+------------+
| R2             | 2    | 18         |     |            | 2   | 6          |
+----------------+------+------------+-----+------------+-----+------------+
| Hedges *g*     |      |            | 1   | 5          | 1   | 3          |
+----------------+------+------------+-----+------------+-----+------------+
| Total          | 11   | 100        | 21  | 100        | 32  | 100        |
+----------------+------+------------+-----+------------+-----+------------+

: [[Table ]{.label}[24.14 ]{.ordinal}Effect Sizes](#ch24.xhtml_R_table24_14){.backlink aria-label=" This link returns to its first in-text citation. " role="doc-backlink"} {#table24_14}
:::

With regards to reliability (both interrater and instrument reliability), only 11% of studies reported any estimate of reliability for their dependent measures (Table S21). This is again similar to what has been previously described in L2 research ([Norris & Ortega, 2000](#ch24.xhtml_ref24_38){.biblioref role="doc-biblioref"} \[16%\]; []{#ch24.xhtml_p357 .pagebreak aria-label=" page 357. " role="doc-pagebreak"}[Derrick, 2016](#ch24.xhtml_ref24_19){.biblioref role="doc-biblioref"} \[28%\]), and is an additional trend that needs to change within L2 research. We observed that IP studies were more likely to report interrater reliability than instrument reliability, and the opposite pattern in PI studies, but the sample is too small to warrant any substantial conclusions ([Table 24.15](#ch24.xhtml_table24_15){#ch24.xhtml_R_table24_15}) other than an increase in reliability reporting is needed, as we will outline below.

::: {.section .tscroll}
+------------------------+-------------------+------------------+------------------+
|                        | IP Model          | PI               | Total            |
+------------------------+------+------------+-----+------------+-----+------------+
| *Type of Reliability*  | *k*  | *percent*  | *k* | *percent*  | *k* | *percent*  |
+========================+======+============+=====+============+=====+============+
| Interrater reliability | 5    | 71         | 1   | 20         | 6   | 50         |
+------------------------+------+------------+-----+------------+-----+------------+
| Instrument reliability | 2    | 29         | 3   | 60         | 5   | 42         |
+------------------------+------+------------+-----+------------+-----+------------+
| Both                   |      |            | 1   | 20         | 1   | 8          |
+------------------------+------+------------+-----+------------+-----+------------+
| Total                  | 7    | 100        | 5   | 100        | 12  | 100        |
+------------------------+------+------------+-----+------------+-----+------------+

: [[Table ]{.label}[24.15 ]{.ordinal}Type of Reliability](#ch24.xhtml_R_table24_15){.backlink aria-label=" This link returns to its first in-text citation. " role="doc-backlink"} {#table24_15}
:::

Finally, power analysis was reported only in 2% of the entire sample ([Sanz & Morgan-Short 2004](#ch24.xhtml_ref24_47){#ch24.xhtml_R_ref24_47 .biblioref role="doc-biblioref"}; [Cox & Sanz 2015](#ch24.xhtml_ref24_15){#ch24.xhtml_R_ref24_15 .biblioref role="doc-biblioref"}), which is almost the same percentage previously found for L2 research (1%; [Plonsky, 2013](#ch24.xhtml_ref24_40){.biblioref role="doc-biblioref"}) (Table S22).
::::::
:::::::::::::::::::::::

::::::::::: {.section aria-labelledby="sec24_5"}
## []{#ch24.xhtml_p358 .pagebreak aria-label=" page 358. " role="doc-pagebreak"}Recommendations for Practice {#ch24.xhtml_sec24_5}

Considering the findings reported in the current synthesis, we would like to offer the following methodological suggestions for future research on IP and PI.

::: {.section aria-labelledby="sec24_5_1"}
### Diversification of Sample {#ch24.xhtml_sec24_5_1}

The existing research has focused on a restricted population sample (young adults, pursuing a university degree in the United States) (sampling bias, [Andringa & Godfroid, 2020](#ch24.xhtml_ref24_5){.biblioref role="doc-biblioref"}). To further increase the generalizability of findings, we encourage researchers to test different age groups (e.g., children, adolescents, and older adults), to prioritize different language constellations (beyond L1 English and L2 Spanish), and to investigate different settings (e.g., online learning). Moving research to remote testing via the web, for instance, could be a viable alternative (see [Ruiz et al., 2019](#ch24.xhtml_ref24_45){#ch24.xhtml_R_ref24_45 .biblioref role="doc-biblioref"}). This would enable researchers to reach more representative population samples.
:::

::: {.section aria-labelledby="sec24_5_2"}
### Sample Size and Power Analysis {#ch24.xhtml_sec24_5_2}

In the same vein, we recommend increasing sample sizes, which in IP research is, according to our analysis, a median average of 62 participants per study. This is a small sample size considering that more than a third (68%) of studies have two to three groups/conditions in their design. This amounts to about 20 participants per condition, which may result in an underpowered study, that is, having insufficient statistical power (too few participants/observations per group) to detect the effects of interest if they truly exist (Type II error) ([Cohen, 1988](#ch24.xhtml_ref24_13){#ch24.xhtml_R_ref24_13 .biblioref role="doc-biblioref"}). Furthermore, significant findings with small sample sizes may be unreliable (high sampling error), and even an overestimate ([Plonsky, 2014](#ch24.xhtml_ref24_41){.biblioref role="doc-biblioref"}). Relatedly, we recommend researchers to perform a *priori* power analysis in order to pre-determine an appropriate sample size (see Plonsky, [2014](#ch24.xhtml_ref24_41){.biblioref role="doc-biblioref"}, [2017](#ch24.xhtml_ref24_42){#ch24.xhtml_R_ref24_42 .biblioref role="doc-biblioref"}, for a similar recommendation). To this end, when planning a study, researchers need to estimate an expected effect size based on related previous research (e.g., a meta-analysis). Doing this will undoubtedly increase the likelihood that the planned study yields useful and reliable information.
:::

::: {.section aria-labelledby="sec24_5_3"}
### Experimental Design {#ch24.xhtml_sec24_5_3}

As described above, the majority of studies are quasi-experimental, i.e., participants were not randomly assigned to different experimental conditions. This can be partially explained by the fact that studies are conducted in classroom settings, but the situation is not ideal as quasi-experimental designs limit our ability to confirm a causal connection between treatments and outcomes. A solution would be the use of large-scale randomized controlled field trials (RCTs). RCTs are widely used in many fields, including educational psychology, but have been rarely used in SLA research (for important exceptions, see [Harper et al., 2021](#ch24.xhtml_ref24_24){#ch24.xhtml_R_ref24_24 .biblioref role="doc-biblioref"}; [Meurers et al., 2019](#ch24.xhtml_ref24_35){#ch24.xhtml_R_ref24_35 .biblioref role="doc-biblioref"}).
:::

::: {.section aria-labelledby="sec24_5_4"}
### Inclusion of Implicit Tests {#ch24.xhtml_sec24_5_4}

We recommend researchers use more tests of implicit (unconscious) knowledge (e.g., free production) (see Jegerski, this volume, for an overview of recent IP research using online measures, such as eye tracking and self-paced reading, which allow the measurement of implicit processes). Explicit tests have been prevalent in L2 research ([Sok et al., 2019](#ch24.xhtml_ref24_50){.biblioref role="doc-biblioref"}), and, according to our results, this situation is not different in the case of IP research (i.e., 86%). Therefore, we join other researchers (e.g., [Ellis, 2005](#ch24.xhtml_ref24_21){#ch24.xhtml_R_ref24_21 .biblioref role="doc-biblioref"}; [Norris & Ortega, 2000](#ch24.xhtml_ref24_38){.biblioref role="doc-biblioref"}; [Spada & Tomita, 2010](#ch24.xhtml_ref24_51){.biblioref role="doc-biblioref"}) in calling for the use of more implicit tests in L2 research. Implicit measures are likely to provide a more accurate []{#ch24.xhtml_p359 .pagebreak aria-label=" page 359. " role="doc-pagebreak"}account of learners' mastery of the targeted linguistic feature, i.e., use the language form in real-life communication ([Ellis, 2005](#ch24.xhtml_ref24_21){.biblioref role="doc-biblioref"}).
:::

::: {.section aria-labelledby="sec24_5_5"}
### Piloting {#ch24.xhtml_sec24_5_5}

We also recommend piloting of instruments as well as its reporting (see [Green, 2021](#ch24.xhtml_ref24_23){#ch24.xhtml_R_ref24_23 .biblioref role="doc-biblioref"}, for advice on pilot testing). Instrument piloting is a necessary step to avoid potential design problems in the actual data collection, thereby rendering it a crucial stage in the research process. As such, instrument piloting contributes to ensure study validity ([Loewen & Philp, 2012](#ch24.xhtml_ref24_32){#ch24.xhtml_R_ref24_32 .biblioref role="doc-biblioref"}). Furthermore, reporting information about the origins of instruments and their development is critical for the interpretation of the study findings ([Derrick, 2016](#ch24.xhtml_ref24_19){.biblioref role="doc-biblioref"}).
:::

::: {.section aria-labelledby="sec24_5_6"}
### Reliability {#ch24.xhtml_sec24_5_6}

Similarly, we recommend estimating and reporting instrument reliability ([Norris & Ortega, 2000](#ch24.xhtml_ref24_38){.biblioref role="doc-biblioref"}; see [McKay & Plonsky, 2021](#ch24.xhtml_ref24_34){#ch24.xhtml_R_ref24_34 .biblioref role="doc-biblioref"}, for an overview of reliability analyses in L2 research). This should be always the case, even when instruments have been previously used in other studies, as the reliability of an instrument can vary with every new administration and group of test takers ([Derrick, 2016](#ch24.xhtml_ref24_19){.biblioref role="doc-biblioref"}).
:::

::: {.section aria-labelledby="sec24_5_7"}
### Effect Sizes {#ch24.xhtml_sec24_5_7}

In addition to reporting *p-*values, we recommend estimating and reporting effect sizes ([Norris & Ortega, 2000](#ch24.xhtml_ref24_38){.biblioref role="doc-biblioref"}; [Plonsky, 2014](#ch24.xhtml_ref24_41){.biblioref role="doc-biblioref"}). Of crucial importance, when reporting effect sizes, researchers need to ensure that effect sizes (i) are explained in terms of how they directly answer the research questions that motivate their estimation (practical significance), (ii) are easily and intuitively accessible to the average reader; and (iii) are based on meaningful metrics (e.g., simple mean differences, unstandardized regression coefficients) (see [Pek & Flora, 2018](#ch24.xhtml_ref24_39){#ch24.xhtml_R_ref24_39 .biblioref role="doc-biblioref"}, for a tutorial).
:::

::: {.section aria-labelledby="sec24_5_8"}
### Moving Beyond Mean-based Analyses {#ch24.xhtml_sec24_5_8}

Finally, we recommend researchers analyze their L2 data beyond mean-based analyses (e.g., ANOVA, *t-*tests) and multiple univariate analyses (e.g., multiple *t-*tests) ([Plonsky, 2014](#ch24.xhtml_ref24_41){.biblioref role="doc-biblioref"}; [In'nami & Murakami, 2021](#ch24.xhtml_ref24_26){#ch24.xhtml_R_ref24_26 .biblioref role="doc-biblioref"}). Instead, we advocate for the use of other statistical approaches, such as regression analysis, as well as multivariate analyses ([Plonsky 2017](#ch24.xhtml_ref24_42){.biblioref role="doc-biblioref"}). In particular, we encourage researchers to consider the use of mixed-effects regression modeling ([Cunnings, 2012](#ch24.xhtml_ref24_16){.biblioref role="doc-biblioref"}). Mixed-effects modeling allows for the estimation of multiple effects (e.g., type of instructional treatment, individual differences) on the dependent variable, as well as the estimation of individual variation between stimuli and participants in the same study ([Cunnings & Finlayson, 2015](#ch24.xhtml_ref24_17){#ch24.xhtml_R_ref24_17 .biblioref role="doc-biblioref"}). In this manner, experimental condition effects and inter-individual differences can be calculated using a single analysis.
:::
:::::::::::

::: {.section aria-labelledby="sec24_6"}
## Future Directions {#ch24.xhtml_sec24_6}

To close this chapter, we offer some suggestions for future IP research from a methodological perspective. As noted earlier, further research needs to increase sample sizes and to include different learner populations. These steps will improve internal and external validity, respectively.

Future research should also compare offline and online measures. Online measures offer a series of advantages over offline tasks (e.g., they can serve as more reliable tests of implicit, or []{#ch24.xhtml_p360 .pagebreak aria-label=" page 360. " role="doc-pagebreak"}automatized knowledge, see next two chapters). A comparison between offline and online measures can yield important insights into language learning and processing, for example by providing combined evidence for whether targeted linguistic features are processed differently or not, depending on the type of processing demands put on the learner (see suggestions on interactional research below).

Considering that only 6% of studies in our sample used qualitative research methods (e.g., think-aloud protocols), we suggest incorporating mixed-methods designs in future research. This will allow for the triangulation (integration) of quantitative and qualitative data, which can provide a richer insight into L2 input processing (see [Hashemi & Babaii, 2013](#ch24.xhtml_ref24_25){#ch24.xhtml_R_ref24_25 .biblioref role="doc-biblioref"}, for a review of mixed-methods research). Moreover, adopting a mixed sampling design (i.e., collecting data from different groups) can also alleviate some of the limitations regarding using more representative samples in IP research, as stated earlier. Of course, this will depend on practicality and feasibility of this type of design for researchers.

Although almost half (49%) of the studies in the current sample used delayed posttesting, there is still a clear need for more longitudinal studies. This is important because one-shot or cross-sectional designs, which are so commonly used in the area, are unable to capture the developmental nature of language learning. More specifically, given that L2 learning is nonlinear and incremental ([In'nami & Murakami, 2021](#ch24.xhtml_ref24_26){.biblioref role="doc-biblioref"}), longitudinal data obtained through time-series designs permits the examination of individual variation and language development over long periods of time.

Further research should consider statistical interactions between learner backgrounds, instructional treatments, and linguistic structures as well ([DeKeyser, 2012](#ch24.xhtml_ref24_18){#ch24.xhtml_R_ref24_18 .biblioref role="doc-biblioref"}). Language learning is multivariate in nature and thus it is important to explore how internal (e.g., individual differences) and external (e.g., treatments) factors interact to account for language development. In the case of IP, for instance, future interactional research can explore how certain factors, such as complexity of target structure can hinder or enhance processing, i.e., processing is contingent on the type of structure ([VanPatten, 2017](#ch24.xhtml_ref24_55){#ch24.xhtml_R_ref24_55 .biblioref role="doc-biblioref"}). Regarding PI, future interactional research can further explore whether the effectiveness of this type of instruction depends on learners' individual characteristics (aptitude-treatment interactions; [Cronbach & Snow, 1977](#ch24.xhtml_ref24_14){#ch24.xhtml_R_ref24_14 .biblioref role="doc-biblioref"}; task-based motivation; see [Dörnyei, 2019](#ch24.xhtml_ref24_20){#ch24.xhtml_R_ref24_20 .biblioref role="doc-biblioref"}). With regards to individual differences and IP, our results show that research on this area is lacking and thus much needed, as only 13% of studies consider individual differences as variables in their design. Finally, we expect that the findings of the present work can contribute to producing IP research that is methodologically more robust.
:::

::: {.section .reference aria-labelledby="r24_1"}
## Further Reading {#ch24.xhtml_r24_1}

- [Plonsky, L. (2017). Quantitative research methods. In S. Loewen & M. Sato (Eds.), `<cite>`{=html}The Routledge handbook of instructed second language acquisition`</cite>`{=html} (pp. 505--521). Routledge.]{#ch24.xhtml_ref24_1}

  Plonsky provides an excellent and comprehensive overview of research in instructed second language acquisition, and although the focus is on quantitative research, this text offers a number of suggestions (e.g., data reporting) for improving study quality in general, regardless of research orientation.

- [Cunnings, I. (2012). An overview of mixed-effects statistical models for second language researchers. `<cite>`{=html}Second Language Research`</cite>`{=html}, *28*(3), 369--382. [https://doi.org/10.1177/0267658312443651](https://doi.org/10.1177/0267658312443651){aria-label="D.O.I. link to Second Language Research"}]{#ch24.xhtml_ref24_2}

  Cunnings provides a good introduction to mixed-effects modeling for L2 researchers using R.

- [Cunnings, I., & Finlayson, I. (2015). Mixed effects modelling and longitudinal data analysis. In L. Plonsky (Ed.), `<cite>`{=html}Advancing quantitative methods in second language research`</cite>`{=html} (pp. 159--181). Routledge.]{#ch24.xhtml_ref24_3}

  Cunnings and Finlayson describe how to analyze longitudinal data using mixed-effects modeling with R.

- [Pek, J., & Flora, D. B. (2018). Reporting effect sizes in original psychological research: A discussion and tutorial. `<cite>`{=html}Psychological Methods`</cite>`{=html}, *23*(2), 208--225. [https://doi.org/10.1037/met0000126](https://doi.org/10.1037/met0000126){aria-label="D.O.I. link to Psychological Methods"}]{#ch24.xhtml_ref24_4}

  This article is an excellent tutorial on different effect size measures.
:::
