[]{#ch25.xhtml}

::: {}
# []{#ch25.xhtml_p364 .pagebreak aria-label=" page 364. " role="doc-pagebreak"}[25 ]{.chnum .ordinal}[Research on Online Input Processing ]{.chtitle}[Self-Paced Reading, Eye-tracking, ERPs, and Beyond]{.chsubtitle .subtitle role="doc-subtitle"} {#ch25.xhtml_h1}

Jill Jegerski

University of Illinois at Urbana-Champaign

DOI: [10.4324/9781003041412-32](https://dx.doi.org/10.4324/9781003041412-32){aria-label="D.O.I. link to this document."}
:::

::: {.section .introduction role="doc-introduction"}
## Introduction and Definitions {#ch25.xhtml_sec25_1}

This chapter provides an overview of online methods as they pertain to research on input processing (IP). IP refers to the comprehension of the target language, either spoken or written, by language learners and is the means by which they derive intake. The processing of input has been widely discussed in second language acquisition (SLA) theory for decades and is a fundamental piece of acquisition. VanPatten's theory of IP ([1996](#ch25.xhtml_ref25_67){#ch25.xhtml_R_ref25_67 .biblioref role="doc-biblioref"}, [2015a](#ch25.xhtml_ref25_68){#ch25.xhtml_R_ref25_68 .biblioref role="doc-biblioref"}) is based on the observation that not all linguistic forms in the input are processed by learners, and only input that has been successfully processed, such that connections are made between form and meaning, can be acquired. It is therefore of great relevance to language learning and teaching to understand what is processed, what is not processed, and why, because the answers to these questions can inform the practical goal of strategically manipulating IP via instruction.

Research methods that are referred to as *online* (sometimes spelled "on-line" with a hyphen to avoid confusion with the homonymous word that refers to connection to the internet) provide a unique perspective on IP because data is recorded in real time, as the processing of input unfolds on a moment-to-moment basis. In the study of sentence processing in psycholinguistics, the distinction between *online* and *offline* measures is of fundamental importance (e.g., [Ferreira & Yang, 2019](#ch25.xhtml_ref25_18){#ch25.xhtml_R_ref25_18 .biblioref role="doc-biblioref"}, p. 6; [Sandra, 2009](#ch25.xhtml_ref25_58){#ch25.xhtml_R_ref25_58 .biblioref role="doc-biblioref"}, p. 305). An online measure is taken live, during sentence processing, without interrupting the primary task of reading or listening. Incrementality is at the heart of the distinction, as online data allow for an analysis of the processing of successive parts of a stimulus. An offline measure, on the other hand, is taken after processing is complete, with the stimulus viewed as a whole, and typically involves a secondary task like providing a response to some type of question. To illustrate, the pattern of eye movements to individual words while reading a stimulus sentence is an online measure, while a common offline measure is the response to a comprehension question that appears after the linguistic stimulus. Under the above definition, the most commonly used *online* research methods in SLA are self-paced reading, eye-tracking, and event-related potentials (ERPs). One advantage of online methods versus most offline methods is that they better reflect the demands of authentic language use in the real []{#ch25.xhtml_p365 .pagebreak aria-label=" page 365. " role="doc-pagebreak"}world, in which processing often must occur very quickly, at essentially the same rapid rate at which speech is produced or words are typed (i.e., a few hundred milliseconds per word).

In instructed SLA, the notion of *online* research methods can be taken from a broader perspective than in psycholinguistics. Given that the primary object of investigation is language learning rather than just the processing of sentences, *online* measures are those that record data during learning. For instance, a think-aloud protocol is classified as online ([Godfroid, 2020](#ch25.xhtml_ref25_20){#ch25.xhtml_R_ref25_20 .biblioref role="doc-biblioref"}; [Leow, 2015](#ch25.xhtml_ref25_41){#ch25.xhtml_R_ref25_41 .biblioref role="doc-biblioref"}; [Morgan-Short, 2020](#ch25.xhtml_ref25_47){#ch25.xhtml_R_ref25_47 .biblioref role="doc-biblioref"}) because the participant says their thoughts out loud while they are engaged in a primary, language-related task such as reading in the second language (L2). In psycholinguistics, it would be important that the think-aloud is not fully simultaneous with the primary task of reading in the same sense as with eye-tracking or the recording of ERPs because it is not possible to read and voice one's thought processes out loud at exactly the same time. It is, however, possible to quickly alternate back and forth between the two tasks, which is also not typical of traditional offline tasks.

Ultimately, the taxonomy of research methods is not as important as the fit of the chosen technique with the goals of the investigation. The present chapter will follow the narrower definition of online methods, including self-paced reading, eye-tracking, ERPs, and also neuroimaging (which is much less common than the others). Think-aloud protocols and related concurrent methods are covered in [Chapter 26](#ch26.xhtml) (Marijuan, this volume) of this handbook and offline methods are covered in [Chapter 25](#ch25.xhtml) (Ruiz & Rebuschat, this volume).
:::

::: {.section aria-labelledby="sec25_2"}
## Historical Perspectives {#ch25.xhtml_sec25_2}

Although online techniques are a relatively recent development in research on IP, the theoretical motivation for this approach is not new. VanPatten's theories ([1996](#ch25.xhtml_ref25_67){.biblioref role="doc-biblioref"}), for instance, were cited as motivation for some of the earliest studies using online methods (e.g., [Felser et al., 2003](#ch25.xhtml_ref25_16){#ch25.xhtml_R_ref25_16 .biblioref role="doc-biblioref"}; [Juffs, 1998](#ch25.xhtml_ref25_33){#ch25.xhtml_R_ref25_33 .biblioref role="doc-biblioref"}) and the current version of VanPatten's ([2015a](#ch25.xhtml_ref25_68){.biblioref role="doc-biblioref"}, p. 114) model of IP includes an explicit interest in real-time measurement: "IP is a model of *moment-by-moment* sentence processing during comprehension and how learners connect or don't connect particular forms with particular meanings *at a given moment in time* \[emphases added\]." Another example of theoretical work with a longstanding interest in real-time language processing is that of Sharwood Smith ([1986](#ch25.xhtml_ref25_60){#ch25.xhtml_R_ref25_60 .biblioref role="doc-biblioref"}; Truscott & Sharwood Smith, [2004](#ch25.xhtml_ref25_66){#ch25.xhtml_R_ref25_66 .biblioref role="doc-biblioref"}).

Within theoretical discussions of L2 processing, it is important to note that the relationship between processing and acquisition can be approached, somewhat paradoxically, from two opposing perspectives ([VanPatten & Jegerski, 2010](#ch25.xhtml_ref25_70){#ch25.xhtml_R_ref25_70 .biblioref role="doc-biblioref"}), which have been referred to as "the processing outcomes of acquisition" and "acquisition-as-processing" ([VanPatten & Rothman, 2015](#ch25.xhtml_ref25_71){#ch25.xhtml_R_ref25_71 .biblioref role="doc-biblioref"}, p. 113) in SLA and as "learning to parse" and "parsing to learn" in L1 psycholinguistics ([Fodor, 1998](#ch25.xhtml_ref25_19){#ch25.xhtml_R_ref25_19 .biblioref role="doc-biblioref"}, p. 286). In the first approach, the assumption that language processing entails some type of mental representation of language is the starting point. Thus, acquisition (of that representation) occurs before processing and processing is viewed as an outcome of acquisition that can reflect different milestones of achievement. To date, this first perspective has been assumed by the majority SLA research using online methods. Recent theoretical work of note in this area includes [Clahsen and Felser (2017)](#ch25.xhtml_ref25_11){#ch25.xhtml_R_ref25_11 .biblioref role="doc-biblioref"} and [Hopp (2018)](#ch25.xhtml_ref25_26){#ch25.xhtml_R_ref25_26 .biblioref role="doc-biblioref"}, both of which also review relevant empirical research.

In the second approach, which is the focus of this chapter, processing is how the language system obtains new data that allows it to develop, so processing precedes acquisition. Under this view, processing also reflects a variety of tendencies that can occur under different learning conditions and empirical observation of these tendencies has the potential to inform teaching methods. Although this perspective on processing has been present in SLA theory for decades, []{#ch25.xhtml_p366 .pagebreak aria-label=" page 366. " role="doc-pagebreak"}empirical research in this vein has thus far been relatively scarce. To my knowledge, the present chapter is the first review of L2 research with online methods that focuses specifically on IP, although one part of a previous book chapter by [Keating (2021)](#ch25.xhtml_ref25_35){#ch25.xhtml_R_ref25_35 .biblioref role="doc-biblioref"} had similar goals and covered a few of the same studies.
:::

::: {.section aria-labelledby="sec25_3"}
## Main Research Methods {#ch25.xhtml_sec25_3}

With the self-paced reading method, sentences or paragraphs are read one word (or phrase) at a time. Initially, a cue symbol such as a "+" appears at the same location in which the beginning of the first word of text will appear, then the participant presses a button to reveal just the first word of the stimulus, presses again to see just the next word, presses again to see the next word, and so on. The most common format for self-paced reading is known as *moving window* because the word that is visible moves across the screen from left to right as the button is pressed. Computer software records the amount of time between button presses, which represents the time taken to read each word and is typically on the order of a few hundred milliseconds. Hence, the primary data are reading times corresponding to specific words of interest. Stimuli are designed to determine whether a particular linguistic phenomenon of interest is associated with longer reading times versus a comparison condition, as in the verb in a sentence with a tense agreement error versus the verb in a different version of the same sentence without the error. In addition, researchers often include some type of question after each stimulus, which provides additional data in the form of comprehension accuracy. For more information on the self-paced reading method in SLA research, see [Jegerski (2014)](#ch25.xhtml_ref25_31){#ch25.xhtml_R_ref25_31 .biblioref role="doc-biblioref"} and [Keating and Jegerski (2015)](#ch25.xhtml_ref25_36){#ch25.xhtml_R_ref25_36 .biblioref role="doc-biblioref"}.

With eye-tracking, small cameras focused on participants' eyes are used to record where they look and for how long, so the data are similar to those from self-paced reading. One notable difference, however, is that only eye-tracking allows participants to go back and reread any part of the stimulus, which is common during normal reading. Eye-tracking thus allows for more natural reading, and also provides a more nuanced data set, with numerous different eye movement measures available to the researcher. Some commonly reported ones include: *first fixation duration*, which is the amount of time that the participant spent the first time the eye gaze was fixated on a word (or multi-word region of interest); *total fixation time* or *total dwell time*, which is the sum of all the fixations on the word; and *regressions to* and *regressions from* a word, which are whether there was a backward eye movement to a word from an area to the right of it or if there was a backward eye movement from the word to an area to the left of it, respectively. For further information on eye-tracking in SLA research, the reader is referred to [Keating (2014)](#ch25.xhtml_ref25_34){#ch25.xhtml_R_ref25_34 .biblioref role="doc-biblioref"} and [Keating and Jegerski (2015)](#ch25.xhtml_ref25_36){.biblioref role="doc-biblioref"}.

In addition to experiments with textual stimuli, eye-tracking is also employed in the *visual world paradigm*, in which the linguistic stimuli are auditory rather than written and participants' eye movements are tracked as they look at two or more images related to the auditory stimulus. The eye movement data of interest are the fixations to the different pictures on display at a given point in the auditory stimulus, under the assumption that where participants look reflects their processing. These data are usually reported as a proportion of total fixation time during a given time window, so they show if a participant group looked more at one picture versus the other(s). There should be a point of reference or comparison, so data are typically compared across different stimulus conditions. To illustrate, it might be of interest to see whether there were more fixations on a picture corresponding to the subject or the object of a stimulus sentence while the participants heard a noun phrase at the beginning of it, and to see if this varied according to whether the stimulus had OVS (object--verb--subject) or SVO (subject--verb--object) word order because this might indicate if they followed a first noun strategy while processing the stimuli. []{#ch25.xhtml_p367 .pagebreak aria-label=" page 367. " role="doc-pagebreak"}For more detailed information regarding the visual world eye-tracking paradigm in SLA research, see [Dussias et al. (2014)](#ch25.xhtml_ref25_13){#ch25.xhtml_R_ref25_13 .biblioref role="doc-biblioref"}.

The neurolinguistic or brain-based research method that is most frequently used in language-related research is the ERP (sometimes referred to as "brain waves" by nonspecialists). In this technique, an elastic cap with an array of electrodes is placed on the participant's scalp in order to record electrical activity of the brain that occurs as a means of communication between neurons. The activity recorded is synchronized with the presentation of a written or auditory linguistic stimulus. Raw electroencephalography (EEG) signals typically contain a lot of "noise", or extraneous fluctuations arising from blinking and other muscle movements, for example, so a carefully designed procedure of data filtering and averaging over many trials is required in order for meaningful patterns (the ERPs) to emerge. In language-related research, the ERP components of greatest interest are the N400 and the P600, which are, respectively, a negative-going wave that peaks at around 400 milliseconds after the presentation of a stimulus and a positive-going wave that peaks at around 600 milliseconds post-stimulus. The N400 is most often observed in conjunction with semantic processing ([Kutas & Federmeier, 2011](#ch25.xhtml_ref25_37){#ch25.xhtml_R_ref25_37 .biblioref role="doc-biblioref"}; [Kutas & Hillyard, 1980](#ch25.xhtml_ref25_38){#ch25.xhtml_R_ref25_38 .biblioref role="doc-biblioref"}) and the P600 is most often seen with syntactic processing ([Osterhout & Holcomb, 1992](#ch25.xhtml_ref25_55){#ch25.xhtml_R_ref25_55 .biblioref role="doc-biblioref"}), although these tendencies are not absolute. In both cases, the waveform of interest is identified based on comparison of an ungrammatical, anomalous, or otherwise unexpected stimulus condition with a typical stimulus condition that does not contain the anomaly. For further information regarding the ERP technique in SLA research, see [Morgan-Short and Tanner (2014)](#ch25.xhtml_ref25_48){#ch25.xhtml_R_ref25_48 .biblioref role="doc-biblioref"} and [Luk et al. (2020)](#ch25.xhtml_ref25_44){#ch25.xhtml_R_ref25_44 .biblioref role="doc-biblioref"}.

Finally, only one neuroimaging study is referenced in this chapter, but it nevertheless may be helpful to provide a basic description of the method. That study ([Morgan-Short et al., 2015](#ch25.xhtml_ref25_4){#ch25.xhtml_R_ref25_4 .biblioref role="doc-biblioref"}) was conducted using fMRI, which is similar to the MRI that is common for medical diagnostic purposes, except that fMRI measures areas of brain activation, in addition to providing images of brain structure. This is accomplished indirectly, as what is actually measured is blood oxygenation and this in turn reflects brain activity. As a measure of IP, the classification of fMRI as *online* could be questioned, as changes in blood oxygenation in response to a stimulus occur with a considerable delay, beginning after approximately 2 seconds and peaking 6 to 8 seconds later (cf., lags of only about 200--300 milliseconds for eye-tracking and ERP effects; [Salthouse & Ellis, 1980](#ch25.xhtml_ref25_57){#ch25.xhtml_R_ref25_57 .biblioref role="doc-biblioref"}). This, combined with a slow sampling rate of around 2 seconds, means the data cannot typically be broken down to reflect incremental processing of input. Nevertheless, similar to online methods, the data are recorded during the primary language-related task (such as reading a sentence) as opposed to resulting from a secondary task (such as answering a question about the sentence). For more details about fMRI, see [Newman (2014)](#ch25.xhtml_ref25_54){#ch25.xhtml_R_ref25_54 .biblioref role="doc-biblioref"}.
:::

:::::: {.section aria-labelledby="sec25_4"}
## Critical Issues and Topics {#ch25.xhtml_sec25_4}

To date, there have been three broad topics of inquiry in IP research using online methods. This section presents a brief description of each. A review of empirical research on each of these three topics is provided in the following section of this chapter.

::: {.section aria-labelledby="sec25_4_1"}
### Empirical Indicators of Learning During IP {#ch25.xhtml_sec25_4_1}

The first issue concerns the potential to examine learning gains with specific empirical measures offered by emerging investigational techniques, such as the reading times (RTs) for individual words provided by self-paced reading, the total fixation time measure for words provided by eye-tracking with text, or the P600 waveform revealed via EEG. Such data are typically recorded []{#ch25.xhtml_p368 .pagebreak aria-label=" page 368. " role="doc-pagebreak"}during an instructional treatment or training and then analyzed in conjunction with measures of learning, such as pre- to posttest gains on a test of the target form. In one example of how an online measure can be employed as an index of learning that occurs during IP, eye-tracking has revealed an association between longer total fixation time on target forms during the reading of input and improvement shown on a subsequent posttest versus a pretest ([Godfroid & Uggen, 2013](#ch25.xhtml_ref25_22){#ch25.xhtml_R_ref25_22 .biblioref role="doc-biblioref"}; [Indrarathne & Kormos, 2017](#ch25.xhtml_ref25_27){#ch25.xhtml_R_ref25_27 .biblioref role="doc-biblioref"}).
:::

::: {.section aria-labelledby="sec25_4_2"}
### Processing Strategies and Pitfalls {#ch25.xhtml_sec25_4_2}

The second issue is to identify the default tendencies and potential pitfalls that characterize learners' processing. To the extent that some processing patterns may prevent learners from processing certain linguistic forms (i.e., words and morphemes) in the input or may lead them to regularly process a form inaccurately, such processing tendencies can present obstacles to acquisition. VanPatten's theory of IP ([1996](#ch25.xhtml_ref25_67){.biblioref role="doc-biblioref"}, [2015a](#ch25.xhtml_ref25_68){.biblioref role="doc-biblioref"}) proposes a series of principles to describe the strategies that learners employ to maximize efficiency during processing, due to limited cognitive resources, and details how each one can contribute to specific difficulties in acquisition. (See Leeser, this volume, for a complete description of the IP model.) Research using online methods has begun to explore ways to test the claims of the theory and to potentially observe some of the IP principles in action, such as the first-noun principle (FNP) which says that learners tend to interpret the first noun in a sentence as the subject, even when its grammatical role is actually something different, like a direct object (see [Keating, 2021](#ch25.xhtml_ref25_35){.biblioref role="doc-biblioref"}, for a more in-depth discussion of the IP principles as they pertain to online processing). In two examples, [Henry (2015)](#ch25.xhtml_ref25_25){#ch25.xhtml_R_ref25_25 .biblioref role="doc-biblioref"} explored the FNP with self-paced reading and [Wong and Ito (2018)](#ch25.xhtml_ref25_73){#ch25.xhtml_R_ref25_73 .biblioref role="doc-biblioref"} investigated it with visual world eye-tracking.
:::

::: {.section aria-labelledby="sec25_4_3"}
### The Effects of Instruction on Processing {#ch25.xhtml_sec25_4_3}

The third issue pertains to the identification of maximally effective methods of language instruction. Of course, this is a broad question that can be approached from many angles, so it is possible to identify sub-topics that are all related to the effects of instruction. To date, the primary instructional strategies that have been investigated using online methods are textual enhancement, processing instruction (PI) (VanPatten, [1996](#ch25.xhtml_ref25_67){.biblioref role="doc-biblioref"}, [2015b](#ch25.xhtml_ref25_69){#ch25.xhtml_R_ref25_69 .biblioref role="doc-biblioref"}), and explicit and implicit instruction. Textual enhancement, a type of input enhancement, involves the modification of text to improve the visual salience of target linguistic forms in the input, for example, with bolded font or a change of font color. This line of research has most often employed eye-tracking to examine the potential for enhanced forms to be associated with an increase in eye fixation times as compared to unenhanced forms. Online research on PI has examined the potential of structured input and explicit focus on processing problems to be associated with apparent changes in how the input is processed. Finally, online research has also examined the effects of explicit and implicit instruction, comparing the two with particular attention to their potential to lead to patterns of processing that are thought to index language processing in native speakers.
:::
::::::

:::::: {.section aria-labelledby="sec25_5"}
## Current Contributions and Research {#ch25.xhtml_sec25_5}

This section presents a review of the existing body of empirical work using psycholinguistic methods to examine IP, organized in terms of the main research topics presented in the previous section. Some studies have addressed more than one of these issues, which means they will be referred to multiple times in this section.

::: {.section aria-labelledby="sec25_5_1"}
### []{#ch25.xhtml_p369 .pagebreak aria-label=" page 369. " role="doc-pagebreak"}Empirical Indicators of Learning During IP {#ch25.xhtml_sec25_5_1}

A number of empirical studies have addressed the first issue regarding the potential connection between different experimental measures of processing during exposure to input and apparent learning. For example, in the area of lexical learning, several eye-tracking studies have observed that more time spent looking at target vocabulary words in context can be associated with improved performance on subsequent vocabulary tests ([Godfroid et al., 2013](#ch25.xhtml_ref25_23){#ch25.xhtml_R_ref25_23 .biblioref role="doc-biblioref"}; [Godfroid et al., 2018](#ch25.xhtml_ref25_24){#ch25.xhtml_R_ref25_24 .biblioref role="doc-biblioref"}; [Godfroid & Schmidtke, 2013](#ch25.xhtml_ref25_21){#ch25.xhtml_R_ref25_21 .biblioref role="doc-biblioref"}; [Mohamed, 2018](#ch25.xhtml_ref25_46){#ch25.xhtml_R_ref25_46 .biblioref role="doc-biblioref"}; [Pellicer-Sánchez, 2016](#ch25.xhtml_ref25_56){#ch25.xhtml_R_ref25_56 .biblioref role="doc-biblioref"}).

Additionally, several studies on grammar learning have also revealed connections between experimental measures of processing during exposure to input and apparent learning of a target form, which, like the work on vocabulary cited in the previous paragraph, have been taken as support for the claim that attention facilitates learning (e.g., [Schmidt, 2001](#ch25.xhtml_ref25_59){#ch25.xhtml_R_ref25_59 .biblioref role="doc-biblioref"}). First, [Godfroid and Uggen's (2013)](#ch25.xhtml_ref25_22){.biblioref role="doc-biblioref"} eye-tracking study revealed a positive association between the total fixation time spent looking at irregular German verbs during an exposure treatment---a meaning-oriented picture selection task that presented the target form in two similar sentences, one with a stem change form and one without---and gains by beginning learners on a written production posttest. [Indrarathne and Kormos (2017)](#ch25.xhtml_ref25_27){.biblioref role="doc-biblioref"} also used eye-tracking and obtained similar results. In this study, pre-intermediate English learners in Sri Lanka read short stories with the target form, English causative *had*, and answered questions after each text. The researchers observed a positive association between total fixation time on the target form during exposure to input and gain scores, calculated as a combined score from a sentence reconstruction task and an aural grammaticality judgment administered before and after exposure. Similarly, [Issa and Morgan-Short (2019)](#ch25.xhtml_ref25_28){#ch25.xhtml_R_ref25_28 .biblioref role="doc-biblioref"} examined the connection between gaze duration increases for target forms due to instruction (either textual enhancement or structured input versus control conditions) and interpretation accuracy gains. They found that the instruction-related increase in gaze durations on Spanish direct object pronouns (plus the verb that followed, also included in the critical region of interest) predicted improvement in interpretation scores from pretest to posttest.

[Ellis et al. (2014)](#ch25.xhtml_ref25_15){#ch25.xhtml_R_ref25_15 .biblioref role="doc-biblioref"} and [Cintrón-Valentín and Ellis (2015)](#ch25.xhtml_ref25_10){#ch25.xhtml_R_ref25_10 .biblioref role="doc-biblioref"} obtained largely similar results also using eye-tracking with text, but with a slightly different design. In these studies, English-speaking novice learners read Latin sentences and indicated whether they referred to the past, present, or future, receiving immediate feedback on their responses during a training phase. Temporal reference was expressed with both verb tense and adverbs of time (e.g., *today*, *yesterday*) and in some sentences, the verb and adverb conflicted with regard to temporal reference (e.g., *Yesterday I think*). Reliance on the verb versus the adverb during an interpretation posttest and verb accuracy on a production posttest both positively correlated with time spent looking at the verb during training. The researchers also found similar positive correlations between eye movements during training and performance on the posttests.

In another study in this line of research, [McDonough et al. (2017)](#ch25.xhtml_ref25_45){#ch25.xhtml_R_ref25_45 .biblioref role="doc-biblioref"} employed an innovative version of the visual world paradigm, in which the input was provided via a live interlocutor rather than a computer. In this study, novice learners listened to *Esperanto* sentences with object--verb--subject (OVS) word order spoken by the interlocutor and selected the picture that corresponded to the meaning of the sentence from two pictures. The researchers observed a positive correlation between posttest accuracy and the amount of time spent looking at a picture that corresponded to the correct interpretation of a stimulus sentence during the instructional treatment.

Hence, there is evidence of a connection between learners' eye movements during exposure to input and how well they perform on tests of the target form, which is seen as evidence in support of the claim that attention facilitates learning (e.g., [Schmidt, 2001](#ch25.xhtml_ref25_59){.biblioref role="doc-biblioref"}). On the other hand, []{#ch25.xhtml_p370 .pagebreak aria-label=" page 370. " role="doc-pagebreak"}[Issa et al. (2015)](#ch25.xhtml_ref25_29){#ch25.xhtml_R_ref25_29 .biblioref role="doc-biblioref"} did not observe this connection. In this study, novice L2 learners read Spanish sentences containing direct object pronouns, a grammatical form new to them, and selected one of two pictures that illustrated the meaning of the sentence, receiving immediate written feedback regarding the accuracy of their response. The researchers did not find a correlation between two eye-tracking measures recorded during exposure to written input (total fixation time and skipping rate) and score improvements on a post-treatment written interpretation task. Hence, the connection between attention (operationalized as looking) and learning is not entirely clear.

Finally, [Batterink and Neville (2014)](#ch25.xhtml_ref25_8){#ch25.xhtml_R_ref25_8 .biblioref role="doc-biblioref"} employed ERPs to see if these brainwave indices of language processing obtained during exposure to input correlated with subsequence performance on a posttest. In this study, novice learners of an artificial mini-French language read narratives comprised of simple SVO sentences presented along with pictures illustrating their meaning and responded to comprehension questions. The brainwave pattern of interest was the N100, a negative-going waveform at approximately 100 milliseconds after stimulus onset that is an ERP indicator of selective attention (i.e., focusing on one stimulus while ignoring others). The target forms for this investigation were word order, article--noun number agreement, and subject--verb number agreement. Analyses revealed that the half of the participant group with the highest scores on a grammaticality judgement posttest showed a word class N100 effect during exposure to input sentences, with a larger N100 for open class words than for closed class words, and the remaining participants with lower scores on the posttest did not show the word class effect. The researchers took this outcome as evidence that attention to open class words may facilitate language learning. Furthermore, it seems the N100 ERP effect may be another useful measure of attention in empirical tests of the claim that attention facilitates learning (e.g., [Schmidt, 2001](#ch25.xhtml_ref25_59){.biblioref role="doc-biblioref"}).
:::

::: section
### Processing Strategies and Pitfalls {#ch25.xhtml_sec25_5_2}

At least three online studies have investigated the second issue regarding the default strategies and pitfalls that might characterize the processing of L2 input and thereby present obstacles in acquisition. For instance, two visual world eye-tracking studies ([Ito & Wong, 2019](#ch25.xhtml_ref25_30){#ch25.xhtml_R_ref25_30 .biblioref role="doc-biblioref"}; [Wong & Ito, 2018](#ch25.xhtml_ref25_73){.biblioref role="doc-biblioref"}) have explored whether learners follow VanPatten's first-noun principle---which says that the first noun in a sentence tends to be interpreted as the subject---during online processing. Although this strategy very often coincides with a correct interpretation of sentence word order, it can also impede the processing of sentences such as *Pierre fait faire du café à Marie* "Pierre makes Marie make coffee," which is commonly misinterpreted as "Pierre makes Marie coffee." The L2 English participants in these studies heard such French sentences with the causative *faire* and clicked on one of two pictures to indicate their interpretation of the sentence. The eye movement data revealed that participants looked more at the picture that erroneously reflected the first noun as the subject than at the correct picture, usually right after hearing only the first word or two of the sentence, so they did appear to follow the first-noun strategy.

Another processing tendency was observed in [Armstrong et al.'s (2018)](#ch25.xhtml_ref25_6){#ch25.xhtml_R_ref25_6 .biblioref role="doc-biblioref"} ERP study of the role of lexical and morphological number cues in the processing of subject--verb number agreement in L2 English. The L1 Chinese participants in this investigation exhibited a P600 effect in response to written stimulus sentences with ungrammatical versus grammatical verbal agreement. In addition, sensitivity to ungrammatical agreement morphology (meaning the amplitude of the P600 waveform) was reduced when the grammatical number of the subject was additionally marked with an overt plural quantifier like *many* instead of just with the plural morpheme --*s*. In other words, sentences with subjects like *Many cookies* elicited smaller P600 effects than did stimulus sentences with subjects like *The cookies*. This result differed from that of an L1 group []{#ch25.xhtml_p371 .pagebreak aria-label=" page 371. " role="doc-pagebreak"}of participants in a previous study with the same materials ([Tanner & Bulkes, 2015](#ch25.xhtml_ref25_64){#ch25.xhtml_R_ref25_64 .biblioref role="doc-biblioref"}), who had shown the opposite trend. Hence, the outcome of [Armstrong et al. (2018)](#ch25.xhtml_ref25_6){.biblioref role="doc-biblioref"} appears to lend support for [VanPatten's (2015a)](#ch25.xhtml_ref25_68){.biblioref role="doc-biblioref"} lexical preference principle, which proposes that learners are more likely to process lexical forms than grammatical forms when both communicate the same meaning. More specifically, it seems that once they had read a lexical marker of number like *many* on the subject of a sentence, they were not as sensitive to number morphology on the verb.
:::

::: section
### The Effects of Instruction on Processing {#ch25.xhtml_sec25_5_3}

Moving on to the third issue, the most widely studied instructional intervention in research on IP using online methods is probably textual enhancement ([Sharwood Smith, 1993](#ch25.xhtml_ref25_61){#ch25.xhtml_R_ref25_61 .biblioref role="doc-biblioref"}), in which the visual salience of target forms in the input is increased via typographical cues such as bolding or a change in font color. Several eye-tracking studies have found that textual enhancement can be associated with an increase in time-based eye movement measures while reading ([Choi, 2017](#ch25.xhtml_ref25_9){#ch25.xhtml_R_ref25_9 .biblioref role="doc-biblioref"}; [Cintrón-Valentín & Ellis, 2015](#ch25.xhtml_ref25_10){.biblioref role="doc-biblioref"}; [Godfroid & Uggen, 2013](#ch25.xhtml_ref25_22){.biblioref role="doc-biblioref"}; [Issa & Morgan-Short, 2019](#ch25.xhtml_ref25_28){.biblioref role="doc-biblioref"}; [Simard & Foucambert, 2013](#ch25.xhtml_ref25_62){#ch25.xhtml_R_ref25_62 .biblioref role="doc-biblioref"}; [Winke, 2013](#ch25.xhtml_ref25_72){#ch25.xhtml_R_ref25_72 .biblioref role="doc-biblioref"}) or a reduction in word skipping rates ([Issa et al., 2015](#ch25.xhtml_ref25_29){.biblioref role="doc-biblioref"}), which is taken as evidence that textual enhancement can encourage the processing of specific forms in the input. There is also evidence from one ERP study ([Tolentino, 2010](#ch25.xhtml_ref25_65){#ch25.xhtml_R_ref25_65 .biblioref role="doc-biblioref"}) that textual enhancement can affect the processing of target forms, as evidenced by a P600 effect. On the other hand, one self-paced reading study and two eye-tracking studies have failed to find any effect of textual enhancement on online processing measures ([Indrarathne & Kormos, 2017](#ch25.xhtml_ref25_27){.biblioref role="doc-biblioref"}; [Loewen & Inceoglu, 2016](#ch25.xhtml_ref25_43){#ch25.xhtml_R_ref25_43 .biblioref role="doc-biblioref"}), so online evidence is somewhat mixed.

Moreover, one important limitation to keep in mind is that, although a number of studies have shown that textual enhancement can lead learners to look more at target forms, whether or not this leads to greater learning is a separate question. To illustrate, [Winke (2013)](#ch25.xhtml_ref25_72){.biblioref role="doc-biblioref"} found that textual enhancement showed an increase in time spent looking at English passive forms, but there was no associated increase in learning gains from pre- to posttest. This outcome is consistent with the findings of [Leow and Martin (2018)](#ch25.xhtml_ref25_42){#ch25.xhtml_R_ref25_42 .biblioref role="doc-biblioref"}, who examined a body of work on textual enhancement with both online and offline measures and found that 18 of 23 studies showed no benefit of textual enhancement alone, in the absence of other instructional strategies. Leow and Martin suggested that "mere attention" ([2018](#ch25.xhtml_ref25_42){.biblioref role="doc-biblioref"}, p. 183) is not sufficient for learning, as it is potentially too superficial from a depth of processing perspective ([Leow, 2015](#ch25.xhtml_ref25_41){.biblioref role="doc-biblioref"}). Additionally, [VanPatten (2015b)](#ch25.xhtml_ref25_69){.biblioref role="doc-biblioref"} explains the apparent ineffectiveness of textual enhancement by positing a fundamental distinction between *noticing* and *processing*, in which only the latter leads to the formation of the form--meaning connections that are critical to acquisition.

Another instructional intervention that has been examined in a number of studies using online methods is processing instruction (VanPatten, [1996](#ch25.xhtml_ref25_67){.biblioref role="doc-biblioref"}, [2015b](#ch25.xhtml_ref25_69){.biblioref role="doc-biblioref"}) and its core component, structured input. These techniques incorporate VanPatten's IP principles ([1996](#ch25.xhtml_ref25_67){.biblioref role="doc-biblioref"}, [2015a](#ch25.xhtml_ref25_68){.biblioref role="doc-biblioref"}) into interpretation-based learning activities, with the goal of maximizing learners' processing of the target form. For instance, target forms might be strategically placed at the beginning of structured input sentences because sentence-initial position is thought to be more salient than the middle or end of a sentence (VanPatten, [1996](#ch25.xhtml_ref25_67){.biblioref role="doc-biblioref"}, [2015a](#ch25.xhtml_ref25_68){.biblioref role="doc-biblioref"}). Furthermore, structured input activities often require learners to interpret the target form in order to respond to questions, so processing of the target form is task-essential. Research has shown that this type of intervention can lead to apparent changes in the way learners process target forms, as measured by the time course of looks to correct interpretive pictures in visual world eye-tracking ([Wong & Ito, 2018](#ch25.xhtml_ref25_73){.biblioref role="doc-biblioref"}), first fixation duration, first pass time, and second pass time in eye-tracking with text ([Lee & Doherty, 2019](#ch25.xhtml_ref25_40){#ch25.xhtml_R_ref25_40 .biblioref role="doc-biblioref"}), total fixation duration in eye-tracking with text ([Fernández Cuenca, 2019](#ch25.xhtml_ref25_17){#ch25.xhtml_R_ref25_17 .biblioref role="doc-biblioref"}; [Issa & Morgan-Short, 2019](#ch25.xhtml_ref25_28){.biblioref role="doc-biblioref"}; []{#ch25.xhtml_p372 .pagebreak aria-label=" page 372. " role="doc-pagebreak"}[Laval & Lowe, 2020](#ch25.xhtml_ref25_39){#ch25.xhtml_R_ref25_39 .biblioref role="doc-biblioref"}), and self-paced reading times ([Henry, 2015](#ch25.xhtml_ref25_25){.biblioref role="doc-biblioref"}). In addition, [Ito and Wong (2019)](#ch25.xhtml_ref25_30){.biblioref role="doc-biblioref"} observed that the modality of input can be important, with written input appearing to have a greater potential to affect learners' input processing, perhaps because auditory input could incur a higher processing load during an instructional treatment.

Finally, online methods have also been used in investigations of implicit and explicit instruction. In principle, this line of empirical research is interested in whether instruction can affect the way in which input is processed, but because this line of research is new and the role of implicit and explicit learning in SLA is controversial (e.g., [Ellis & Wulff, 2020](#ch25.xhtml_ref25_14){#ch25.xhtml_R_ref25_14 .biblioref role="doc-biblioref"}, vs. [DeKeyser, 2020](#ch25.xhtml_ref25_12){#ch25.xhtml_R_ref25_12 .biblioref role="doc-biblioref"}), an equally important goal thus far has been to explore what types of experimental measures might be appropriate for addressing this topic. Much of the work already referenced above in this section included either implicit learning conditions (as in incidental vocabulary learning, for instance) or explicit instruction (as in the explanation of a processing problem at the beginning of a processing instruction treatment), but here the focus will be on studies that have included both.

A series of ERP studies with exposure to artificial language input have compared the effects of explicit and implicit language training conditions on language processing (see [Morgan-Short, Faretta-Stutenberg, et al., 2015](#ch25.xhtml_ref25_50){#ch25.xhtml_R_ref25_50 .biblioref role="doc-biblioref"}, for a more detailed review). Overall, this research has found that learners trained under both types of conditions can develop native-like N400 or P600 brainwave effects in response to semantic and morphosyntactic errors ([Morgan-Short et al., 2010](#ch25.xhtml_ref25_52){#ch25.xhtml_R_ref25_52 .biblioref role="doc-biblioref"}; [Morgan-Short, Steinhauer et al., 2012](#ch25.xhtml_ref25_53){#ch25.xhtml_R_ref25_53 .biblioref role="doc-biblioref"}), which can start to appear even after only very brief exposure to the target language ([Batterink & Neville, 2013](#ch25.xhtml_ref25_7){#ch25.xhtml_R_ref25_7 .biblioref role="doc-biblioref"}). Despite this broad similarity, implicit training conditions can also lead to such effects appearing sooner, after less exposure than under conditions of explicit instruction ([Morgan-Short et al., 2010](#ch25.xhtml_ref25_52){.biblioref role="doc-biblioref"}; [Morgan-Short, Steinhauer et al. 2012](#ch25.xhtml_ref25_53){.biblioref role="doc-biblioref"}). Perhaps most interestingly, only implicit training conditions have been associated with the development of an early anterior negativity (AN) effect ([Morgan-Short, Finger, et al., 2012](#ch25.xhtml_ref25_51){#ch25.xhtml_R_ref25_51 .biblioref role="doc-biblioref"}; [Morgan-Short, Steinhauer et al., 2012](#ch25.xhtml_ref25_53){.biblioref role="doc-biblioref"}), another index of morphosyntactic processing that is less often observed with L2 learners than the N400 and P600 components ([Morgan-Short, Faretta-Stutenberg, et al., 2015](#ch25.xhtml_ref25_50){.biblioref role="doc-biblioref"}). Hence, this has been interpreted by researchers as a sign of exceptionally native-like processing (although it should be noted that L1 users do not always exhibit ANs either and the interpretation of AN effects is not entirely straightforward, [Steinhauer & Drury, 2012](#ch25.xhtml_ref25_63){#ch25.xhtml_R_ref25_63 .biblioref role="doc-biblioref"}).

Neuroimaging research has yet to compare the effects of explicit and implicit training conditions, but one functional magnetic resonance imaging (fMRI) study examined changes in the processing of a mini artificial language as a result of just implicit training. [Morgan-Short, Deng, et al. (2015)](#ch25.xhtml_ref25_49){#ch25.xhtml_R_ref25_49 .biblioref role="doc-biblioref"} found that implicit training with syntax resulted in more focused activation of brain areas in the left hemisphere, which suggests the processing became more efficient and more language-focused after training. Additionally, analysis of individual results revealed that one subset of participants relied more on neural circuits typically associated with L1 grammar (such as the inferior frontal gyrus or Broca's area), even from the early stages of this seven-session study, whereas other participants relied on more generalist brain regions that are not associated specifically with linguistic processing. Thus, there can be individual differences in the processing of L2 input.

[Indrarathne and Kormos (2017)](#ch25.xhtml_ref25_27){.biblioref role="doc-biblioref"} employed eye-tracking to investigate the effects of implicit and explicit instruction. This study used four different instructional treatment conditions that all included an input flood component and varied in terms of explicitness, manipulated via pre-exposure instructions to pay attention to highlighted forms (i.e., textual enhancement) with or without a metalinguistic explanation of the target form. The two more explicit conditions showed greater total fixation durations on target forms than the less explicit conditions, which []{#ch25.xhtml_p373 .pagebreak aria-label=" page 373. " role="doc-pagebreak"}had either textual enhancement only or no focus on form. This is an interesting finding because it has been suggested that online techniques are particularly well-suited to the measurement of implicit processes (e.g., [Morgan-Short, Faretta-Stutenberg et al., 2015](#ch25.xhtml_ref25_50){.biblioref role="doc-biblioref"}). Hence, it seems most likely that online measures can reflect both explicit and implicit processing to varying degrees.
:::
::::::

::: {.section aria-labelledby="sec25_6"}
## Recommendations for Practice {#ch25.xhtml_sec25_6}

Like all research methods, each of the experimental techniques that have been the focus of this chapter has practical advantages and disadvantages. Research methods also all have practical limitations, which means that not every technique is appropriate for every research question. It is beyond the scope of this chapter to go into detail regarding the specific advantages and disadvantages of each of the different research methods discussed herein and this information is already available in guides to psycholinguistic research methods such as that of [Jegerski and VanPatten (2014)](#ch25.xhtml_ref25_32){#ch25.xhtml_R_ref25_32 .biblioref role="doc-biblioref"}. Nevertheless, there are some general trends that are worth noting. On the positive side, one advantage of many online methods is that they can be relatively covert measures, meaning that instructions and tasks do not necessarily need to make explicit reference to the goals of a study in order to obtain useable data. In terms of disadvantages, some of these experimental techniques require participants to have already developed a certain degree of reading fluency and listening skill in the target language, so they may not be appropriate for use with beginning learners. An additional disadvantage that is probably widely known is that conducting research with online methods typically requires a considerable up-front investment of both money to purchase specialized software and usually equipment as well, and time, to acquire the advanced technical knowledge required to program and run experiments and to analyze complex data. This is especially true with eye-tracking and ERPs and less so with self-paced reading. Despite the extra time and money often required to use online methods, they are not inherently better for research than offline methods, which are still well-suited to many research goals. It is therefore important to provide clear motivation for employing a particular research method in a given study to avoid the pitfall of assuming that online methods and technologically advanced techniques are always better and potentially wasting valuable time and research funding.
:::

::: {.section aria-labelledby="sec25_7"}
## Future Directions {#ch25.xhtml_sec25_7}

To date, the amount of research conducted using online methods that can inform the study of IP has been minimal. Although emerging methods such as self-paced reading, eye-tracking, and ERPs have shown a notable increase in SLA in the last 15 years and continue to gain in popularity, the majority of this work has approached processing as an outcome, as described in the "Historical Perspectives" section above. In this approach, processing is viewed as an assessment of language learning that occurred in the past and little attention is paid to the learning process itself; the focus is on the end rather than the means, so to speak ([VanPatten & Jegerski, 2010](#ch25.xhtml_ref25_70){.biblioref role="doc-biblioref"}; [VanPatten & Rothman, 2015](#ch25.xhtml_ref25_71){.biblioref role="doc-biblioref"}). Relatively few studies have taken the alternative perspective, in which language processing is seen as acquisition in action, or the means to the end ([VanPatten & Jegerski, 2010](#ch25.xhtml_ref25_70){.biblioref role="doc-biblioref"}; [VanPatten & Rothman, 2015](#ch25.xhtml_ref25_71){.biblioref role="doc-biblioref"}), so there is a clear need for more research with this learning-oriented approach. Examples have been presented in the "Current Contributions and Research" section of this chapter and include the investigation of empirical indicators of learning during IP, of processing strategies and pitfalls, and of the effects of instruction on processing. These three existing lines of inquiry make an excellent starting point for future research using online methods, as the issues guiding them are both fundamental and broad, plus additional questions will also undoubtedly arise as this area of study continues to advance over time. []{#ch25.xhtml_p374 .pagebreak aria-label=" page 374. " role="doc-pagebreak"}Some research topics with potential may be found within L1 psycholinguistics; one example is the study of misinterpretation errors during language comprehension ([Ferreira & Yang, 2019](#ch25.xhtml_ref25_18){.biblioref role="doc-biblioref"}). [Keating (2021)](#ch25.xhtml_ref25_35){.biblioref role="doc-biblioref"} outlines two additional directions that have potential for future research on the effects of PI: predictive processing and morphological decomposition.
:::
