[]{#ch26.xhtml}

::: {}
# []{#ch26.xhtml_p378 .pagebreak aria-label=" page 378. " role="doc-pagebreak"}[26 ]{.chnum .ordinal}[Think-Aloud Protocol, Trials-to-Criterion, and Triangulation Between Online and Offline Measures in IP Research]{.chtitle} {#ch26.xhtml_h1}

Silvia Marijuan

California Polytechnic State University

DOI: [10.4324/9781003041412-33](https://dx.doi.org/10.4324/9781003041412-33){aria-label="D.O.I. link to this document."}
:::

:::: {.section .introduction role="doc-introduction"}
## Introduction/Definitions {#ch26.xhtml_sec26_1}

Input processing (IP) has been defined as "a model of moment-by-moment sentence processing during comprehension and how learners connect or don't connect particular forms with particular meanings at a given moment in time" ([VanPatten, 2020](#ch26.xhtml_ref26_40){#ch26.xhtml_R_ref26_40 .biblioref role="doc-biblioref"}, p. 106). Given that online tasks are considered "to provide on-line measures of cognitive processes because they help reveal what is going on in our minds while language processing is unfolding" ([Jiang, 2013](#ch26.xhtml_ref26_18){#ch26.xhtml_R_ref26_18 .biblioref role="doc-biblioref"}, p. 5), online measures have gained terrain in IP research and, consequently, in processing instruction (PI) research, the pedagogical intervention inspired by VanPatten's model of IP. (For more about PI and structured input, see Wong, this volume.) The gradual integration of online tools originating in cognitive psychology and neuroscience (e.g., self-paced reading and eye-tracking) with offline data elicitation measures (i.e., accuracy measures) in IP and PI research aims to uncover processing pattern behaviors and underlying cognitive processes involved in L2 processing and L2 development.

Whereas online measures are process-oriented -- they tap into *how* L2 learners interpret L2 input in real time -- offline measures (such as responding to a comprehension question) are oriented towards the product of learning; they gauge *what* learners have interpreted after being exposed to the L2 stimuli ([VanPatten & Jegerski, 2010](#ch26.xhtml_ref26_44){#ch26.xhtml_R_ref26_44 .biblioref role="doc-biblioref"}). (See Ruiz & Rebuschat, this volume, for a discussion of offline measures.) [Godfroid (2019)](#ch26.xhtml_ref26_13){#ch26.xhtml_R_ref26_13 .biblioref role="doc-biblioref"} characterizes offline measures as "temporarily disconnected from the task processes under investigation"; in Godfroid's view, "any method providing accuracy data as its main output, often as part of a pretest--posttest design, can be considered offline" (p. 2). These characterizations are critical for discussing online/offline measure categorization.

The present chapter discusses the triangulation of online and offline measures in IP research while focusing on the use of two popular concurrent data elicitation procedures in IP/PI research: think-aloud protocols (TAs) and trials-to-criterion (TTC). After defining these two data elicitation procedures and situating them in a historical context, the chapter discusses where these two []{#ch26.xhtml_p379 .pagebreak aria-label=" page 379. " role="doc-pagebreak"}procedures fall within online/offline distinctions and how they differ from other online techniques such as eye-tracking and self-paced reading (SPR). Additionally, critical issues including the benefits, challenges, and limitations of these two procedures will be discussed. After reviewing research that uses these two elicitation procedures in conjunction with offline measures, recommendations for practice and future directions for IP/PI research are offered which include triangulating online/offline measures to examine under-researched learning contexts and learner populations.

::: {.section aria-labelledby="sec26_1_1"}
### Definitions {#ch26.xhtml_sec26_1_1}

Concurrent think-aloud protocols (TAs) are a form of introspective data elicitation procedure in which learners are prompted to report their thoughts (i.e., "think aloud") while engaging in a linguistic task. Scholars have classified TAs into two categories: (a) *non-metacognitive*: where participants simply verbalize their thoughts; and (b) *metacognitive*: where participants verbalize their thoughts and provide reasons or justifications for their thoughts ([Bowles, 2010](#ch26.xhtml_ref26_6){#ch26.xhtml_R_ref26_6 .biblioref role="doc-biblioref"}). Participants are instructed to think out loud either in their L1 (to avoid language ability constraints), their L2, or a combination of both, while carrying out a primary task (such as reading a passage for meaning).

Trials-to-criterion (TTC) is a behavioral measure defined as the number of responses, instructional trials, or practice opportunities required for participants to achieve a predetermined level of performance or accuracy (See [Cooper et al., 2019](#ch26.xhtml_ref26_7){#ch26.xhtml_R_ref26_7 .biblioref role="doc-biblioref"}). The nature of the trial varies based on the specific target behavior under study. Following participants' performance, researchers tally and report the number of instances (trials) needed to reach criterion. For instance, if a participant reached criterion at item 12, their TTC would be 11 (indicating the number of items that preceded the desired level of performance). As explained by [VanPatten and Price (2012)](#ch26.xhtml_ref26_45){#ch26.xhtml_R_ref26_45 .biblioref role="doc-biblioref"}, TTC works "like golf: the lower the score the better" (p. 114). TTC is often employed to compare the effectiveness of different treatments or instructional methods, or to assess learners' increasing competence in acquiring a specific concept and its related class of concepts.
:::
::::

::: {.section aria-labelledby="sec26_2"}
## Historical Perspectives {#ch26.xhtml_sec26_2}

In early IP/PI research, scholars relied strictly on accuracy measures (e.g., multiple-choice, sentence-completion tasks, sentence-picture matching) to assess whether L2 learners at different levels of proficiency efficiently made form--meaning connections when interpreting L2 data. PI studies began with [VanPatten and Cadierno's (1993)](#ch26.xhtml_ref26_43){#ch26.xhtml_R_ref26_43 .biblioref role="doc-biblioref"} seminal study on Spanish object pronouns. PI research was subsequently advanced by other scholars investigating a variety of structures and languages (see Wong, this volume, for a discussion of PI research). In the 1990s, IP research (e.g., Leow, [1997](#ch26.xhtml_ref26_20){#ch26.xhtml_R_ref26_20 .biblioref role="doc-biblioref"}, [2000](#ch26.xhtml_ref26_21){#ch26.xhtml_R_ref26_21 .biblioref role="doc-biblioref"}) started to use concurrent TAs to investigate the role of attention and (levels of) awareness during the input and intake processing stages of L2 learning ([Leow et al., 2014](#ch26.xhtml_ref26_26){#ch26.xhtml_R_ref26_26 .biblioref role="doc-biblioref"}). The first empirical studies that employed TAs to examine whether visual input enhancement promoted learners' noticing and intake of the forms in the input was [Alanen (1995)](#ch26.xhtml_ref26_4){#ch26.xhtml_R_ref26_4 .biblioref role="doc-biblioref"} and [Leow (2001)](#ch26.xhtml_ref26_22){#ch26.xhtml_R_ref26_22 .biblioref role="doc-biblioref"}.

[Fernández (2008)](#ch26.xhtml_ref26_11){#ch26.xhtml_R_ref26_11 .biblioref role="doc-biblioref"} was the first to use TTC as an alternative methodological option to observe and assess PI. The primary goal for [Fernández (2008)](#ch26.xhtml_ref26_11){.biblioref role="doc-biblioref"} (and other replication studies also using TTC that followed) was to measure whether explicit information (EI) played a role in facilitating L2 processing via structured input (SI) activities (see Wong, this volume, for information about structured input) and how many SI items (i.e., trials) it would take before L2 learners started to make correct form--meaning connections.
:::

::::::::: {.section aria-labelledby="sec26_3"}
## []{#ch26.xhtml_p380 .pagebreak aria-label=" page 380. " role="doc-pagebreak"}Critical Issues and Topics {#ch26.xhtml_sec26_3}

This section examines the issue of categorizing data elicitation methods as online (concurrent) or offline and offers a critical overview of TA and TTC procedures. At the end of the section, the benefits of triangulating online and offline measures are addressed.

::::: {.section aria-labelledby="sec26_3_1"}
### Categorizing TAs and TTC as Online Measures {#ch26.xhtml_sec26_3_1}

Selecting TAs or TTC raises a critical question: Where do these measures fit within the continuum of available online techniques? Not all concurrent measures are considered equally "online." As [Jiang (2013)](#ch26.xhtml_ref26_18){.biblioref role="doc-biblioref"} argues, the term "online" is "not always well defined or unanimously understood." Moreover, "online" should be considered a "relative term" given that "some tasks are more on-line than others" (p. 5). Categorizing measures like TAs and TTC as "online," therefore, warrants a careful examination of the processing information they yield, the analysis that can be done, and the inferences that can be drawn about learners' mental processes from the collected data. Researchers may disagree on the classification of a measure as "online" due to the constraints/limitations of that measure, which can lead to debate.

To untangle where TA and TTC fall in the realm of online techniques, it is useful to contrast them with more recent and complex online procedures that have been introduced in L2 research due to technological advances. Among the array of online techniques, eye-tracking (ET) stands out for its high temporal--spatial precision in capturing eye movement patterns as participants read or observe stimuli. Moreover, ET is non-intrusive. The core assumption underlying ET research is the strong connection between eyes and mind, often referred to as the eye-mind hypothesis ([Just & Carpenter, 1980](#ch26.xhtml_ref26_19){#ch26.xhtml_R_ref26_19 .biblioref role="doc-biblioref"}). ET produces a wide range of measures recorded in milliseconds, such as *fixations*, *gaze duration* and *regressions* (reanalysis of parts of the stimuli). These ET measures provide insights into subtle IP behaviors that would not be detected otherwise ([Marijuan & Sanz, 2017](#ch26.xhtml_ref26_28){#ch26.xhtml_R_ref26_28 .biblioref role="doc-biblioref"}; see also Jegerski, this volume).

Another online methodology closely resembling ET is self-paced reading (SPR) ([Godfroid, 2019](#ch26.xhtml_ref26_13){.biblioref role="doc-biblioref"}). SPR involves collecting reaction time (RT) data while learners read sentences that are divided into separate words (word-by-word) or segments (phrase-by-phrase) that are "masked" with dashes. In a noncumulative word-by-word SPR task (a widely used format in sentence processing research) participants press the space bar to read the first word. Then, they press the space bar again to reveal the next word in the sentence while the previous word is "re-masked" and replaced with a dash. The dependent variable in SPR is the reading time (i.e., response time/latency) between button-presses as participants advance from reading one word to the next. The assumption underlying SPR is that learners slow down their rate of button pressing as processing becomes difficult. (See Jegerski, this volume, for an overview of ET and SPR).

::: {.section aria-labelledby="sec26_3_1_1"}
#### Think-Aloud Protocols (TAs) {#ch26.xhtml_sec26_3_1_1}

While TAs have been proposed as an online procedure in L2 studies investigating the role of awareness in L2 development (e.g., Leow [2000](#ch26.xhtml_ref26_21){.biblioref role="doc-biblioref"}, [2001](#ch26.xhtml_ref26_22){.biblioref role="doc-biblioref"}), they do not offer the same fine-grained, real-time account of learners' processing as ET or SPR. First, concurrent TAs do not collect chronometric data (RT data measured in milliseconds). Second, even though TAs can be analyzed quantitively (see [Leow et al., 2014](#ch26.xhtml_ref26_26){.biblioref role="doc-biblioref"}), these concurrent verbal reports mainly yield qualitative data as "their primary outcome" ([Godfroid, 2019](#ch26.xhtml_ref26_13){.biblioref role="doc-biblioref"}, p. 3), which requires researchers to assess interrater reliability during the coding process. A shared characteristic between TAs and SPR is that both require participants to engage in a secondary task: either speaking out loud or []{#ch26.xhtml_p381 .pagebreak aria-label=" page 381. " role="doc-pagebreak"}pressing a button ([Godfroid, 2019](#ch26.xhtml_ref26_13){.biblioref role="doc-biblioref"}). L2 researchers have used TAs as a supplementary method alongside other online methods that yield quantitative data as their primary outcome, such as eye-tracking (ET).
:::

::: {.section aria-labelledby="sec26_3_1_2"}
#### Trials-to-Criterion (TTC) {#ch26.xhtml_sec26_3_1_2}

Categorizing TTC as an "online" measure poses challenges since, as will be discussed later, accuracy data tend to be the primary outcome considered in PI research that employs this procedure (e.g., [Fernández, 2008](#ch26.xhtml_ref26_11){.biblioref role="doc-biblioref"}). In contrast to ET and SPR, TTC data do not provide specific information about the areas of stimuli that learners fixated on or reanalyzed. Moreover, unlike TAs, TTC data cannot be used to address which segments of the L2 input learners noticed or become aware of. Due to its reliance on accuracy data, an argument could be made that TTC might be more suitably categorized as "offline" rather than "online," or at the very least it falls within a grey area.
:::
:::::

::::: {.section aria-labelledby="sec26_3_2"}
### Addressing Critical Issues with TAs and TTC {#ch26.xhtml_sec26_3_2}

To delve into the challenges surrounding the application of think-aloud protocols (TAs) and trials-to-criterion (TTC), it is essential to thoroughly examine the critical issues associated with each data elicitation procedure.

::: {.section aria-labelledby="sec26_3_2_1"}
#### Issues with TAs {#ch26.xhtml_sec26_3_2_1}

The primary concern regarding the validity of TA data is "reactivity," which raises the question of whether verbalizing thoughts during encoding might alter learner cognitive processes instead of offering a true reflection of their thoughts. Reactivity has been assessed in terms of latency (the time participants spend on the task while thinking aloud) and its impact on performance (accuracy). Non-metacognitive TAs are thought to accurately reflect the nature of cognitive processes and only slightly slow processing. On the other hand, metacognitive TAs where participants explain their thoughts "may be more reactive, not only slowing processing but also potentially causing changes in cognitive processing" ([Bowles, 2010](#ch26.xhtml_ref26_6){.biblioref role="doc-biblioref"}, p. 14). The degree of reactivity of concurrent TAs in L2 research remains inconclusive.

TAs have been employed in studies investigating the effects of various degrees of instructional explicitness on L2 processing, as they are considered to capture conscious cognitive processes ([Ericsson & Simon, 1993](#ch26.xhtml_ref26_10){#ch26.xhtml_R_ref26_10 .biblioref role="doc-biblioref"}) such as noticing and awareness that occur as learners engage with L2 data, especially in early stages of learning when input becomes intake (i.e., what has been focally attended to in the input) ([Leow et al., 2014](#ch26.xhtml_ref26_26){.biblioref role="doc-biblioref"}). However, operationalizing *awareness* presents challenges. As [Leow (1997)](#ch26.xhtml_ref26_20){.biblioref role="doc-biblioref"} pointed out, "there are different definitions of what constitutes awareness"; moreover, some learners may be unable to verbalize their awareness (p. 471).

In the endeavor to operationalize awareness, it is critical to consider that consciousness has been conceptualized in a range of levels, a notion that traces back to [Schmidt (1990)](#ch26.xhtml_ref26_35){#ch26.xhtml_R_ref26_35 .biblioref role="doc-biblioref"}. For [Schmidt (1990)](#ch26.xhtml_ref26_35){.biblioref role="doc-biblioref"}, *noticing*, which refers to subjectively experienced stimuli available for verbal report, serves as the necessary and sufficient condition for the conversion from input to intake, and it is situated at the lower level. Meanwhile, *understanding* which implies the "recognition of a general principle, rule or pattern" ([Schmidt, 1990](#ch26.xhtml_ref26_35){.biblioref role="doc-biblioref"}, p. 29) is situated at the higher or meta-level. In empirical studies, *noticing* has been treated as one level of awareness, which has been broadly defined as "some form of subjective awareness of new targeted forms in L2 data" ([Leow, 1997](#ch26.xhtml_ref26_20){.biblioref role="doc-biblioref"}, p. 474).

[]{#ch26.xhtml_p382 .pagebreak aria-label=" page 382. " role="doc-pagebreak"}TA protocols have been used to measure noticing according to different descriptors: making a verbal/written correction of the target form and/or commenting on the target item ([Leow, 1997](#ch26.xhtml_ref26_20){.biblioref role="doc-biblioref"}); translating (correctly/incorrectly) or circling the target item, verbally referencing (or marking in writing) the target item ([Leow, 2001](#ch26.xhtml_ref26_22){.biblioref role="doc-biblioref"}; [Bowles, 2003](#ch26.xhtml_ref26_5){#ch26.xhtml_R_ref26_5 .biblioref role="doc-biblioref"}; [Leow et al., 2003](#ch26.xhtml_ref26_25){#ch26.xhtml_R_ref26_25 .biblioref role="doc-biblioref"}). Levels of awareness may differ between studies; for example, [Leow (1997)](#ch26.xhtml_ref26_20){.biblioref role="doc-biblioref"} identified three levels (from low to high): cognitive change (noticing), meta-awareness (reporting of being aware), rule formation (understanding), and [Rosa and Leow (2004)](#ch26.xhtml_ref26_33){#ch26.xhtml_R_ref26_33 .biblioref role="doc-biblioref"} identified two levels: noticing and understanding. The quantity of awareness instances produced under specific experimental conditions is usually examined.

Another construct that has been measured via TAs is *depth of processing* (DOP) ([Craik & Lockhart, 1972](#ch26.xhtml_ref26_8){#ch26.xhtml_R_ref26_8 .biblioref role="doc-biblioref"}). Different definitions of DOP can be found in the literature: "the differential amount of cognitive or mental effort, attention, or time cognitively spent while processing incoming information" ([Hsieh et al., 2015](#ch26.xhtml_ref26_16){#ch26.xhtml_R_ref26_16 .biblioref role="doc-biblioref"}, p. 141); the extent to which form--meaning connections are made for target items during exposure (e.g., [Thinglum et al., 2019](#ch26.xhtml_ref26_36){#ch26.xhtml_R_ref26_36 .biblioref role="doc-biblioref"}); or "the relative amount of cognitive effort, level of analysis, elaboration of intake together with the usage of prior knowledge, hypothesis testing and rule formation employed in decoding and encoding some grammatical or lexical item in the input" ([Leow 2015](#ch26.xhtml_ref26_23){#ch26.xhtml_R_ref26_23 .biblioref role="doc-biblioref"}, p. 204).

TAs have also been coded in DOP levels, usually on a 1--3 scale (e.g., [Leow et al., 2008](#ch26.xhtml_ref26_27){#ch26.xhtml_R_ref26_27 .biblioref role="doc-biblioref"}); the number of instances of DOP elicited tend to be examined as well. Whereas low levels of DOP are associated with shallow processing (e.g., simply circling the target form), medium levels of DOP correspond to minimal processing of the target forms (e.g., rising intonation of the target form); high levels of DOP have been associated with several descriptors: metalinguistic comments about the target form, correctly translating the target form, spending much time processing the target item, providing a partially, fully, or (in)accurate target underlying grammatical rule (see [Adrada-Rafael, 2016](#ch26.xhtml_ref26_3){#ch26.xhtml_R_ref26_3 .biblioref role="doc-biblioref"}; [Hsieh et al., 2015](#ch26.xhtml_ref26_16){.biblioref role="doc-biblioref"}; [Leow, 2015](#ch26.xhtml_ref26_23){.biblioref role="doc-biblioref"}; [Morgan-Short et al., 2012](#ch26.xhtml_ref26_31){#ch26.xhtml_R_ref26_31 .biblioref role="doc-biblioref"}).

While some studies share similar coding criteria for TAs, there is variation between studies. For example, in [Morgan-Short et al. (2012)](#ch26.xhtml_ref26_31){.biblioref role="doc-biblioref"}, noticeably pausing before or after the target item was coded as intermediate DOP, whereas in [Adrada-Rafael (2016)](#ch26.xhtml_ref26_3){.biblioref role="doc-biblioref"} it was coded as low DOP. Additionally, levels of DOP and levels of awareness seem to be interrelated, and the distinction between them can sometimes be blurred; for example, both a high level of awareness (understanding) and a high level of DOP have been operationalized as meta-levels involving rule formulation. Some scholars have attempted to address how awareness and DOP may interrelate or affect L2 processing differently (e.g., [Thinglum et al., 2019](#ch26.xhtml_ref26_36){.biblioref role="doc-biblioref"}), but this enquiry needs further exploration.
:::

::: {.section aria-labelledby="sec26_3_2_2"}
#### Issues with TTCs {#ch26.xhtml_sec26_3_2_2}

When employing TTCs, researchers decide on target items, modality (written/ aural), type/number of distractors, filler sentences, and trials needed to reach criterion (e.g., correctly answering the target items along with a distractor three times in a row). These decisions, for example, numbers of filler sentences/distractors to be included can influence results. Supplementary measures like rate, duration, and latency can be added ([Cooper et al., 2019](#ch26.xhtml_ref26_7){.biblioref role="doc-biblioref"}). Proficiency levels and individual differences (IDs) (moderating or intervening factors) may also be considered. The inclusion of supplementary measures like response times (RTs) can make inferences about input processing more robust. [Fernández (2008)](#ch26.xhtml_ref26_11){.biblioref role="doc-biblioref"}, who introduced TTC in PI research, illustrates issues in employing this procedure.

[Fernández (2008)](#ch26.xhtml_ref26_11){.biblioref role="doc-biblioref"} proposed TTC as an alternative to the traditional pre-/post-test research design. According to this author, TTC reveals "changes in behavior as learners try to process []{#ch26.xhtml_p383 .pagebreak aria-label=" page 383. " role="doc-pagebreak"}linguistic forms and structures" (p. 285). In this study, beginning-intermediate learners of L2 Spanish were exposed to an "online treatment" consisting of two training conditions (EI + SI and SI only); TTC measured the effects of EI on learner processing of the target items.

In the first experiment, L2 leaners heard OVS sentences linked to the FNP (e.g., *lo*~cl~ *llaman*~v~ *sus padres*~sub~ "His parents call him") while two pictures appeared on the screen (e.g., picture A depicted two parents calling the boy, and picture B depicted a boy calling his parents). Participants had to choose the correct picture (A or B) corresponding to what they heard. Response time was tracked; and unlimited time to respond was given. Feedback (correct/incorrect) was provided right after each response. Although [Fernández (2008)](#ch26.xhtml_ref26_11){.biblioref role="doc-biblioref"} collected RTs, the author did not follow the standard procedure of asking participants to respond as quickly as possible to maximize the chance for these time-sensitive data to reflect unfolding cognitive processes ([Jiang, 2013](#ch26.xhtml_ref26_18){.biblioref role="doc-biblioref"}). Moreover, since learners could not briefly preview the pictures (at least 2 seconds) before hearing aural stimuli, they might have needed to listen to the entire sentence before evaluating and selecting the correct picture, especially considering participants' relatively low proficiency level.

The second experiment in [Fernández (2008)](#ch26.xhtml_ref26_11){.biblioref role="doc-biblioref"} focused on the lexical preference principle (LPP) (L2 learners' overreliance on lexical information over grammatical markers). (See also White & Wong, this volume, for more information about the LPP.) Participants heard a subordinated clause (e.g., *juegue* ~pres\ subj~ *al tenis todos los días*... "I/she/he play(s) tennis every day") while they were presented on the screen with two main clauses conveying certainty or doubt (e.g., *No pienso que...*/*Es evidente que*... I don't think that.../It is obvious that...). Participants needed to choose the main clause that correctly fit. Since participants were required to pay simultaneous attention to both written and aural stimuli, this setup potentially led participants to make a decision after listening to the entire subordinate clause, especially participants in the SI group (Fernández herself suggested that the SI group might have needed more time to figure out "what they should pay attention to in the input", p. 297). In other words, in [Fernández (2008)](#ch26.xhtml_ref26_11){.biblioref role="doc-biblioref"}, decision making may have happened after encoding and not necessarily during encoding based on the nature of the task.

Even though [Fernández (2008)](#ch26.xhtml_ref26_11){.biblioref role="doc-biblioref"} suggests that TTC "tracks learners' behavior while they are engaged in activities designed to promote acquisition" (p. 278), whether TTC fully captures learners' moment-by-moment processing is debatable. Arguably, TTC can show at what point *in the course of the treatment* learners begin to consistently establish the right form--meaning connections in each learning conditions, and *at what speed*, if RTs are collected and analyzed.

Scholars replicating [Fernández (2008)](#ch26.xhtml_ref26_11){.biblioref role="doc-biblioref"} noted TTC essentially measured "participants' accuracy on the practice items," that is, a type of formative assessment ([Villegas & Morgan-Short, 2019](#ch26.xhtml_ref26_48){#ch26.xhtml_R_ref26_48 .biblioref role="doc-biblioref"}, p. 191). Another replication used the last ten items of the treatment as the posttest and those same ten items in reverse order as the pre-test since they argued that TTC is "not a standard measure in instructed SLA" ([VanPatten & Price, 2012](#ch26.xhtml_ref26_45){.biblioref role="doc-biblioref"}, p. 115).
:::
:::::
:::::::::

::::: {.section aria-labelledby="sec26_4"}
## Summary {#ch26.xhtml_sec26_4}

TAs have been used to explore the effects of instructional explicitness on L2 processing and capturing cognitive processes like noticing and awareness, especially in early learning stages when input becomes intake. However, defining noticing, awareness and depth of processing presents challenges. Due to variance in how different constructs have been operationalized and how TA data have been coded and interpreted across studies, providing clear definitions and coding criteria of TAs (e.g., detailed descriptors for each level of the construct studied) is needed for both research synthesis and replications. Finally, issues of reactivity need to be addressed when employing TA protocols.

[]{#ch26.xhtml_p384 .pagebreak aria-label=" page 384. " role="doc-pagebreak"}Regarding TTC, it is important to consider the factors researchers decide upon when employing TTC, such as the number of trials needed. It is also crucial to evaluate what kind of processing information TTC data do or do not offer as direct evidence of the primary task (reading/listening of the L2 stimuli) before making inferences about online processing, especially if what constitutes a trial is learners' accuracy on practice items during treatment. Response time data, if collected and analyzed alongside the TTC data, can serve as a large-grained index of learners' perceived difficulty in interpreting the target items.

::: {.section aria-labelledby="sec26_4_1"}
### Offline Measures in IP/PI Research {#ch26.xhtml_sec26_4_1}

Not all offline data elicitation measures are suitable for conducting IP/PI research since within an IP framework, acquisition does not proceed from rule internalization; rather, it depends on whether learners connect a particular form with a particular meaning/function during comprehension at a given moment in time ([VanPatten & Rothman, 2014](#ch26.xhtml_ref26_46){#ch26.xhtml_R_ref26_46 .biblioref role="doc-biblioref"}). According to [VanPatten (2015)](#ch26.xhtml_ref26_39){#ch26.xhtml_R_ref26_39 .biblioref role="doc-biblioref"}, offline measures such as grammaticality judgment tasks (GJTs) delve into rule internationalization, thus failing to address whether learners have in fact processed the input. VanPatten's perspective possibly explains the scarcity of GJTs in IP/PI research.

In contrast, offline measures that do gauge whether learners accurately understood the meaning of sentences -- such as interpretation tasks (like sentence--picture matching) and sentence completion tasks (e.g., [VanPatten & Cadierno, 1993](#ch26.xhtml_ref26_43){.biblioref role="doc-biblioref"}) -- are commonly employed in IP/PI research. Additionally, accuracy measures can be tracked throughout an experiment to determine whether learners improve differently across varying experimental conditions; this was the case in the work of [Marijuan et al. (2016)](#ch26.xhtml_ref26_29){#ch26.xhtml_R_ref26_29 .biblioref role="doc-biblioref"} who employed a SPR task and *who-type* questions ("who did what to whom?") to analyze the processing of OVS sentences in L2 Spanish, employing a within-subjects design.
:::

::: {.section aria-labelledby="sec26_4_2"}
### Triangulation Between Offline and Online Methods {#ch26.xhtml_sec26_4_2}

IP/PI scholars who only use offline measures to address L2 processing can only infer what a learner must have done during the act of comprehension. Online measures can be used in combination with offline measures to provide robust empirical evidence related to the set of processing strategies universally used by L2 learners when processing language input, as the IP model proposes, and allow researchers to better understand the effects that PI interventions have in mitigating IP difficulties. As [VanPatten and Jegerski (2010)](#ch26.xhtml_ref26_44){.biblioref role="doc-biblioref"} noted, different types of learners may arrive at the same interpretation after listening to/reading a sentence but may have done so in different ways. Additionally, learners may perform similarly after treatment, but participants in different groups may present different processing patterns in real-time during exposure.

[Ito and Wong's (2022)](#ch26.xhtml_ref26_17){#ch26.xhtml_R_ref26_17 .biblioref role="doc-biblioref"} study on the processing of a French causative structure among intermediate learners illustrates this approach. They used a picture-selection eye-tracking (ET) task to assess phonetically variable input (PVI) and training item size effects on PI training. Offline analysis showed no accuracy differences post-training across the three training sizes examined. However, ET data revealed that in the short and medium training conditions, participants reduced the number of times they looked at the incorrect picture. Moreover, voice variability reduced learners' real-time responses only in the medium training. The authors concluded that PVI does not hinder L2 syntactic learning and suggested that extensive auditory trainings (beyond 96 items) might not provide benefits due to the cognitive load of auditory processing compared to the written modality. This example underscores how online data enable researchers to capture processing differences that offline data alone cannot offer.
:::
:::::

::::: {.section aria-labelledby="sec26_5"}
## []{#ch26.xhtml_p385 .pagebreak aria-label=" page 385. " role="doc-pagebreak"}Current Contributions and Research {#ch26.xhtml_sec26_5}

This section focuses on current L2 research that has employed TAs and TTC procedures in combination with offline measures to help advance our understanding of IP.

::: {.section aria-labelledby="sec26_5_1"}
### Current L2 Research Employing TAs {#ch26.xhtml_sec26_5_1}

Different interconnected research strands can be identified in the literature combining TAs with offline data. One strand has explored the impact of input enhancement (IE) or textual enhancement (TE) (i.e., visually emphasizing target forms through bolding, highlighting or other manipulations) on L2 processing and L2 development. [Alanen (1995)](#ch26.xhtml_ref26_4){.biblioref role="doc-biblioref"} studied the effects of explicit rule-based instruction vs. implicit meaning-based instruction (using visual enhancement) for the acquisition of locative suffixes and consonant alteration in L2 Finnish. These manipulations aimed to draw learners' attention to form during the study phase. In the learning phase, participants read a passage with the target forms for meaning. Although IE did not affect sentence-completion task results, TA data revealed that learners who had paid attention to the target items during the study phase seemed to have acquired some aspects of them, regardless of treatment (Enhance, Rule, or Rule + Enhance). A limitation in [Alanen's (1995)](#ch26.xhtml_ref26_4){.biblioref role="doc-biblioref"} study was that TA data were only collected during the study phase (before exposure).

In a pre-, post-, delayed posttest design, [Leow (2001)](#ch26.xhtml_ref26_22){.biblioref role="doc-biblioref"} and [Leow et al. (2003)](#ch26.xhtml_ref26_25){.biblioref role="doc-biblioref"} employed TAs during exposure in conjunction with offline measures. [Leow (2001)](#ch26.xhtml_ref26_22){.biblioref role="doc-biblioref"} studied the formal imperative in Spanish among first-year college level students, and [Leow et al. (2003)](#ch26.xhtml_ref26_25){.biblioref role="doc-biblioref"} studied the Spanish present subjunctive and Spanish present perfect. In [Leow (2001)](#ch26.xhtml_ref26_22){.biblioref role="doc-biblioref"}, TAs revealed that amount of reported noticing was statically similar for both groups, and even though more participants in the ET group made specific comments on some targeted forms, written IE had a non-significant role in L2 readers' comprehension and processing of the target forms. [Leow et al. (2008)](#ch26.xhtml_ref26_27){.biblioref role="doc-biblioref"} supported [Leow's (2001)](#ch26.xhtml_ref26_22){.biblioref role="doc-biblioref"} results.

[Bowles (2003)](#ch26.xhtml_ref26_5){.biblioref role="doc-biblioref"} replicated [Leow (2001)](#ch26.xhtml_ref26_22){.biblioref role="doc-biblioref"} with fourth-semester level students. TAs showed that more participants in the TE group reported instances of noticing in comparison to the TE group; however, this difference was not significant arguably due to a small sample size. Results of the multiple-choice immediate recognition posttest showed a significant correlation between reported noticing and comprehension scores for both groups. Moreover, TA protocols revealed variance in how learners processed the L2 input even in the same treatment. Some made little or no comments while others used the target forms as the starting point for deeper processing.

To examine if DOP impacts the processing of enhanced target items, [Leow et al. (2019)](#ch26.xhtml_ref26_24){#ch26.xhtml_R_ref26_24 .biblioref role="doc-biblioref"}, studied the same target items as [Leow et al. (2003)](#ch26.xhtml_ref26_25){.biblioref role="doc-biblioref"} with first-year college students. Learners were exposed to two types of enhanced texts: one drawing attention to enhanced items, and another prompting them to provide a rule for the enhanced items. TA data showed that the + rule group processed the more salient item (the present perfect) more deeply than the -rule group. Production scores indicated that the + rule group outperformed the -rule group in the immediate posttest for both target items (present perfect and present subjunctive). Although over half of the + rule group participants failed to formulate the underlying rule, those who did showed high levels of both DOP and awareness. The authors argued that, in addition to DOP, achieving awareness at the level of understanding may be necessary to firmly internalize new linguistic data.

A series of L2 studies, which integrated TAs and offline measures, have examined the relationship between instructional conditions with varying degrees of explicitness (e.g., +/- explicit feedback, +/- grammatical information), levels of awareness/DOP, and accuracy. In line with [Leow (1997)](#ch26.xhtml_ref26_20){.biblioref role="doc-biblioref"}, who argued that increased awareness enhances recognition and accurate production of []{#ch26.xhtml_p386 .pagebreak aria-label=" page 386. " role="doc-pagebreak"}noticed forms, [Rosa and O'Neill (1999)](#ch26.xhtml_ref26_34){#ch26.xhtml_R_ref26_34 .biblioref role="doc-biblioref"} found that fourth-semester learners of Spanish who received explicit explanation about Spanish conditional sentences during a problem-solving task (a multiple-choice jigsaw puzzle) produced more instances of awareness at the level of understanding, as revealed by TA data. Moreover, their performance significantly improved in the immediate multiple-choice recognition task compared to the less explicit group. Similarly, [Rosa and Leow (2004)](#ch26.xhtml_ref26_33){.biblioref role="doc-biblioref"}, who studied the same structure, found that fifth-semester learners in a more explicit condition reported higher levels of awareness, leading to increased intake and higher accuracy rates for the noticed forms.

[Hsieh et al. (2015)](#ch26.xhtml_ref26_16){.biblioref role="doc-biblioref"} found that Spanish beginners exposed to computerized face-to-face instruction providing grammatical explanation (and no feedback) on the verb *gustar* (to please) exhibited increased awareness at the understanding level. In contrast, the group engaged in learner-initiated practice with implicit feedback through computer-assisted instruction exhibited increased cognitive effort. The authors argued that, without metalinguistic information, learners may need additional cognitive effort to infer the target form, potentially leading to improved retention of the target forms as seen in delayed posttests. [Adrada-Rafael (2016)](#ch26.xhtml_ref26_3){.biblioref role="doc-biblioref"}, who studied the Spanish imperfect subjunctive, showed that intermediate learners in a more explicit condition exhibited greater DOP, as revealed by TA data. Deeper DOP correlated with improved comprehension and a more accurate production of the target forms.

Since prior knowledge may be linked to higher levels of awareness ([Leow, 1997](#ch26.xhtml_ref26_20){.biblioref role="doc-biblioref"}), [Thinglum et al. (2019)](#ch26.xhtml_ref26_36){.biblioref role="doc-biblioref"} examined the role that prior knowledge (PK) played in how deeply learners processed Spanish vocabulary. The treatment -- a crossword puzzle and a matching task -- exposed the +PK group to the target words. Meanwhile, the -PK group (control) completed the same tasks but with control words. After that, learners read a Spanish text for meaning. Participants in the +PK group outperformed their peers in the -PK group in their ability to accurately recognize and produce the lexical target items. TA data showed that even though both groups exhibited a similar amount of processing overall, only the +PK group exhibited a higher level of correctly processing the form and meaning of the target lexical items on average.

Recently, researchers have investigated the role of DOP on heritage language development. For example, [Zamora (2017)](#ch26.xhtml_ref26_49){#ch26.xhtml_R_ref26_49 .biblioref role="doc-biblioref"} studied how L2 learners and Spanish heritage speakers (HSs) used Spanish pluperfect subjunctive in conditional sentences under two experimental conditions \[+/-explicit feedback\]. Results showed that Spanish HSs employed prior knowledge to process the input whereas L2 learners relied more on metalinguistic strategies (e.g., explicit grammar). Spanish HSs outperformed L2 learners in the immediate posttest (fill-in-the-blank). Explicit feedback facilitated instances of higher levels of processing, and DOP was positively correlated with better performance on the delayed posttest.

Another strand of research combining TAs with offline measures re-examines [VanPatten's (1996)](#ch26.xhtml_ref26_37){#ch26.xhtml_R_ref26_37 .biblioref role="doc-biblioref"} primacy of meaning principle (PMP) -- investigating whether L2 learners attending to/processing the input for meaning can simultaneously attend to/process the target forms. In [Leow et al. (2008)](#ch26.xhtml_ref26_27){.biblioref role="doc-biblioref"}, second-semester Spanish learners read a passage containing four lexical and grammatical items; participants needed to circle one of the target items depending on the experimental condition. Multiple-choice comprehension scores showed no significant differences between experimental and control conditions. TA data revealed varying levels of target item processing among learners, from merely circling the target form to interpreting/translating it. Although the authors hypothesized that increased DOP might hinder comprehension due to cognitive overload, findings did not support this. Overall, TA data revealed relatively low DOP, with minimal simultaneous processing of form and meaning, rendering results inconclusive. [Morgan-Short et al. (2012)](#ch26.xhtml_ref26_31){.biblioref role="doc-biblioref"} replicated [Leow et al. (2008)](#ch26.xhtml_ref26_27){.biblioref role="doc-biblioref"} with third-semester learners of Spanish. [Morgan-Short et al. (2012)](#ch26.xhtml_ref26_31){.biblioref role="doc-biblioref"} also found that paying attention simultaneously to form and meaning did not []{#ch26.xhtml_p387 .pagebreak aria-label=" page 387. " role="doc-pagebreak"}affect comprehension. By contrast, participants who processed the grammatical or lexical forms more deeply while reading for meaning, as shown by the TAs, also exhibited higher comprehension scores.

To summarize, whereas in some studies TA data align with offline data in that significant changes can be observed in both, in other cases, TA data show that learners in specific conditions (e.g., +TE) (or at least a subgroup of them) process L2 input differently even though no significant changes can be observable in accuracy performance. Superior accuracy performance (as measured by offline tasks, such as multi-choice recognition/comprehension tasks) seems to be more strongly linked to awareness at the level of understanding ([Schmidt, 1990](#ch26.xhtml_ref26_35){.biblioref role="doc-biblioref"}), which entails arriving at the correct form--meaning connection for target items or discovering the underlying rule; meanwhile, high DOP seems to also contribute to, but doesn't seem to automatically lead to, better performance ([Leow, 2015](#ch26.xhtml_ref26_23){.biblioref role="doc-biblioref"}; [Thinglum et al., 2019](#ch26.xhtml_ref26_36){.biblioref role="doc-biblioref"}).

The variance in results has been attributed to different factors such as the level of proficiency of the learners, sample size, the nature of the task, the research design employed, the extent to which learners process target items deeply, and the type of learners involved.
:::

::: {.section aria-labelledby="sec26_5_2"}
### Current L2 Research Employing TTC {#ch26.xhtml_sec26_5_2}

As discussed above, [Fernández (2008)](#ch26.xhtml_ref26_11){.biblioref role="doc-biblioref"} investigated the effects of EI prior to SI practice on two target structures in L2 Spanish -OVS sentences associated with the FNP (30 items: 20 OVS, 10 distractors), and the subjunctive mood associated with the LPP (30 items: 21 subjunctives, 9 indicatives). To reach criterion, beginning--intermediate learners needed to correctly process three target items plus a distractor in a row. No effect of EI was found for word order. However, EI had beneficial effects for processing the Spanish subjunctive sooner and faster (based on response times).

A series of replications employing TTC (but not response times) followed [Fernández (2008)](#ch26.xhtml_ref26_11){.biblioref role="doc-biblioref"}. [VanPatten and Borst (2012b)](#ch26.xhtml_ref26_42){#ch26.xhtml_R_ref26_42 .biblioref role="doc-biblioref"} and [Villegas and Morgan-Short (2019)](#ch26.xhtml_ref26_48){.biblioref role="doc-biblioref"} supported Fernández's findings. [VanPatten and Borst (2012b)](#ch26.xhtml_ref26_42){.biblioref role="doc-biblioref"} examined OVS sentences (50 items: 38 OVS, 12 SVO), also with third-semester Spanish learners, including a grammatical sensitivity measure. A pre-, post-, and delayed posttest (an interpretation task) measured L2 knowledge gain. Results showed no difference between the (+/- EI) conditions: both groups began processing OVS sentences correctly at about the same point during treatment; no significant correlation was found between grammatical sensitivity and TTC. Meanwhile, [Villegas and Morgan-Short (2019)](#ch26.xhtml_ref26_48){.biblioref role="doc-biblioref"} examined Spanish subjunctive development in second-semester Spanish learners. The authors included a pre-, post-, and delayed posttest, and controlled that the training conditions (explicit/implicit) were balanced for the amount of time and number of examples shown before practice. EI helped learners in the explicit group perform better than the implicit group during treatment. The assessment tasks showed an advantage in the posttest for the explicit group that dissipated over time. Moreover, a moderate positive relationship between working memory (WM) capacity (the ID variable they examined) and L2 development was found for the explicit group, but not for the implicit group.

[Culman et al. (2009)](#ch26.xhtml_ref26_9){#ch26.xhtml_R_ref26_9 .biblioref role="doc-biblioref"} studied the role of EI on the processing of OVS sentences in German (a language with accusative case marking on articles). In this study, the target items (30 items: 10 OVS, 10 SVO, 10 distractors) seemed to have been more "spaced out" than in Fernández's study. A large number of first- and third-semester learners did not reach criterion since they would have needed to correctly answer "eight or nine items in a row to meet criterion" (p. 25). After redefining their scoring procedure, results showed that the +EI group outperformed the SI group. However, the superior performance of the +EI group might be attributed to frequency []{#ch26.xhtml_p388 .pagebreak aria-label=" page 388. " role="doc-pagebreak"}effects since there were not enough items for the SI group to "catch up" during treatment (p. 29). Follow-up studies investigating the role of EI on OVS-type sentence processing in L2 German ([Henry et al., 2009](#ch26.xhtml_ref26_14){#ch26.xhtml_R_ref26_14 .biblioref role="doc-biblioref"}; [Henry et al., 2017](#ch26.xhtml_ref26_15){#ch26.xhtml_R_ref26_15 .biblioref role="doc-biblioref"}; [VanPatten & Borst, 2012a](#ch26.xhtml_ref26_41){#ch26.xhtml_R_ref26_41 .biblioref role="doc-biblioref"}) also found a strong advantage for EI during training, as measured by TTC. [Henry et al. (2009)](#ch26.xhtml_ref26_14){.biblioref role="doc-biblioref"} employed 30 items (20 OVs, 10 SVO) and [Henry et al. (2017)](#ch26.xhtml_ref26_15){.biblioref role="doc-biblioref"} and [VanPatten & Borst (2012a)](#ch26.xhtml_ref26_41){.biblioref role="doc-biblioref"} employed 50 items (38 OVS, 12 SVO). In these studies, +EI groups processed sentences more quickly suggesting that EI facilitates form--meaning mappings. [Henry et al. (2017)](#ch26.xhtml_ref26_15){.biblioref role="doc-biblioref"}, who also studied focused prosody as a variable, found that learners who received EI or prosodic cues during training improved on both comprehension and production tasks in the posttest, but gains were only sustained for comprehension in the delayed posttest.

[VanPatten et al. (2013)](#ch26.xhtml_ref26_47){#ch26.xhtml_R_ref26_47 .biblioref role="doc-biblioref"} reported the effects of EI on the processing of OVS-type sentences across languages (Spanish, German and Russian), including the results of [VanPatten and Price (2012)](#ch26.xhtml_ref26_45){.biblioref role="doc-biblioref"} on a causative structure in French (50 items). TTC data revealed that only German and French L2 learners who received EI prior to SI treatment began processing sentences sooner. Overall, grammatical sensitivity that was also measured in these studies was not a significant factor.

More recently, [Glimois (2019)](#ch26.xhtml_ref26_12){#ch26.xhtml_R_ref26_12 .biblioref role="doc-biblioref"}, who investigated the effect of EI, SI and input flood (IF) to support the processing of numeral classifiers in L2 Chinese, found that TTC data and posttest data indicated that providing EI prior to SI correlates with faster learning rates and greater immediate outcomes in English speakers unfamiliar with Chinese.

To summarize, in addition to analyzing TTC data, the replications that followed [Fernández (2008)](#ch26.xhtml_ref26_11){.biblioref role="doc-biblioref"} included a pre- and posttest (and in some cases, a delayed posttest) to examine learning gains after practice. Whereas most replications conducted among L2 learners at the beginning/intermediate level found that EI, as a component of PI, seems to play a role in mitigating processing problems such as the FNP and LPP, that mitigation seems to depend on different factors such as the target structures chosen, the L2 language chosen, and the number of trials included in the research design. OVS-type sentences in L2 Spanish associated with the FNP seem to be pervasive. Moreover, participants in SI (implicit) groups seem to need more trials to reach criterion. Results are not always clear-cut since, when accuracy scores are re-analyzed in a different way; in [VanPatten and Price (2012)](#ch26.xhtml_ref26_45){.biblioref role="doc-biblioref"}, the benefits of EI were found to dissipate when the authors compared the scores of the ten final items in the treatment against the scores of those same ten items, but in inverse order. Finally, the retention of the learning gains for the EI (explicit) condition was not always sustained over time.
:::
:::::

::: {.section aria-labelledby="sec26_6"}
## Recommendations for Practice {#ch26.xhtml_sec26_6}

When triangulating online data and accuracy data, it is critical to be aware of the type of data different online techniques provide to researchers and how those data can be interpreted to explain results. When online data and offline data neatly align in the same direction, drawing conclusions will be less challenging. If online data does not align with offline data, researchers need to ask themselves what factors are at play that can be influencing results. In some cases, online data may somewhat align in the same direction as offline measures, but the pattern is not as evident. Replicating the study using a different (or additional) online technique, including additional offline assessment measures, increasing the sample size (if the sample size is small), selecting a different target item associated with the L2 strategy, and testing the hypothesis with learners with different levels of proficiency are viable options to consider.

In relation to TAs, caution is needed when addressing implicit/explicit learning using this technique since "a good deal of language acquisition is implicit"; learners may notice something []{#ch26.xhtml_p389 .pagebreak aria-label=" page 389. " role="doc-pagebreak"}in the input but not process it ([VanPatten, 2014](#ch26.xhtml_ref26_38){#ch26.xhtml_R_ref26_38 .biblioref role="doc-biblioref"}, p. 194--195). On the other hand, as [Rebuschat et al. (2015)](#ch26.xhtml_ref26_32){#ch26.xhtml_R_ref26_32 .biblioref role="doc-biblioref"} have argued, just because participants are unable to verbalize relevant features of the linguistic forms while performing TAs doesn't necessarily mean that learning proceeds without awareness. TAs may not be sensitive enough to fully capture all relevant conscious knowledge. Moreover, learners may acquire explicitly one specific feature of a complex L2 phenomenon but acquired other aspects of the same phenomenon without awareness.

Regarding TTC, it is important to prevent participants from making correct responses by chance and evaluate how many distractors to include and how to distribute them. Also, when comparing the efficacy of different pedagogical interventions, participants need to be offered enough trials to reach criterion. Piloting the TTC procedure seems advisable. Furthermore, since different studies investigating the same linguistic phenomenon may employ a different number of trials and/or different trial distributions, comparability between studies need to be addressed.
:::

::: {.section aria-labelledby="sec26_7"}
## Future Directions {#ch26.xhtml_sec26_7}

It has been argued that the most robust findings are those that reappear in studies using a variety of techniques ([Mitchell, 2004](#ch26.xhtml_ref26_30){#ch26.xhtml_R_ref26_30 .biblioref role="doc-biblioref"}). Combining different online and offline measures (and even comparing the results of more than one online technique in interrelated studies) can help us better understand different linguistic phenomena in IP research.

Investigating the role that individual differences (IDs) might have on L2 processing vis- à-vis L2 performance seems promising. New research in this area should include IDs not fully examined yet (e.g., motivation). It is encouraging to observe that different student populations -- for example, heritage speakers (HSs) -- are progressively being included in L2 processing studies, and scholars are also making use of online/offline data to problematize the concept of "incomplete acquisition" to refer to HS competence. Future studies employing online/offline measures with HSs with different linguistic profiles would enhance their representation in cognitive-oriented research. Also, studying target structures difficult to acquire in advanced learners would shed light on leaners' performance and processing patterns at later stages in learning. Finally, employing online/offline measures in immersive contexts, such as study abroad, where L2 input is rich, can also offer new insights about L2 development in naturalistic settings.
:::

::: {.section .reference aria-labelledby="r26_1"}

# []{#ch27.xhtml_p392 .pagebreak aria-label=" page 392. " role="doc-pagebreak"}Epilogue [Interview with Bill VanPatten]{.chsubtitle .subtitle role="doc-subtitle"} {#ch27.xhtml_h1}

Joe Barcroft

Washington University in St. Louis

Wynne Wong

The Ohio State University

DOI: [10.4324/9781003041412-34](https://dx.doi.org/10.4324/9781003041412-34){aria-label="D.O.I. link to this document."}
:::

The following interview with Bill VanPatten about his model of input processing (IP) and the future of IP research was conducted on September 17, 2021. We hope the questions and answers are useful to readers of this volume. We would like to thank Bill VanPatten for graciously accepting to share his thoughts with us.

1.  **[Question 1:]{.custom-label} Where would you like to see research on input processing go in the coming years and decades?**

    **BVP**: I would like to see input processing (IP) do a couple of things, one of which is to tie the external to the internal. What I mean by that is that we have a lot of research where we look at what learners are processing and what they're not processing, but the actual link to acquisition and how a linguistic system develops over time is missing. What is the connection between processing X in the input with something happening in your developing system, whatever your mental representation is? Those connections between processing and acquisition aren't being made, and they might be difficult to do, but I think that's one area where we could move toward. Michael Sharwood Smith and John Truscott have done some of that with their MOGUL[^45] approach, but I think we could do more along those lines.

    Another avenue to pursue involves this question: why is it that at different stages learners seem to be selecting different things? I use *selecting* here not in some conscious sense but as something about the way the learner's mind/brain interacts with the data out there and seems to select some data and ignore others. So why is it that some data get selected? Why aren't all data selected? Why these data and not those data? That's what I tried to do early on in my model. That's what my model is trying to understand: why certain things happen and not others. And then I don't think we fully understand why it is that the beginning learner is processing these things but not these other things. And why does a more advanced learner process these things but not these other things? So why don't we have a model for that? We don't really have that mapped that out very well if at all.

2.  []{#ch27.xhtml_p393 .pagebreak aria-label=" page 393. " role="doc-pagebreak"}**[Question 2:]{.custom-label} What do you think about efforts to broaden the focus of IP research beyond a primary focus on morphosyntax?**

3.  **BVP**: I think that's possible. As you know, my original model was on morphosyntactic properties because that's what I'm trained in. It wouldn't be the same model; it wouldn't be my model because there could be different models and processing for different kinds of things. Right? So there could be one model for syntax, one model for pragmatics, one model for lexis ... and so on ... and these things could eventually somehow connect; we connect them in a bigger framework.

4.  **[Question 3:]{.custom-label} To what extent do you think it is useful to review research not conducted within an IP perspective from an IP perspective?**

    **BVP**: I think it depends on what you're looking at. You can look at other kinds of things that have been done in the research and ask yourself, "Is there something in this research that will be reflective of what the learners were doing in processing input?" And that is actually what I did early on in my career, for example, when I looked at staged development of *ser* and *estar*. After I documented what learners were doing over time with the copular verbs in Spanish, I asked myself, "Is this reflective in any way of what they might be processing from the environment? So what is it that they're getting from the environment and how is that feeding into the system?" So you can look at things like learner production and staged development and then try to do a brain experiment or a thought experiment and think backwards about what might be happening during processing that's contributing to the stages. Again, I'm just talking about morphosyntax---but once you develop models for other things like lexical or pragmatic processing, you could probably apply the same thought process. But you can do that thought experiment knowing that it's not just a simple process, and that there are other things going on, too, but IP must be some piece of that puzzle.

    Another thing that got me started in thinking about IP, were natural orders that were documented back in the 1970s and the early 1980s, and I asked myself, "Why are certain things coming in before other things and what might those things reflect about learners' processing of input? Again, it's not 100% about processing input because there are other things going on ... But you can do that thought experiment, you think backwards based on the data you get. I'm not sure what other data we would be looking at, but I think this is perfectly reasonable to look at something, and if it lends itself to do that thought experiment, (e.g., What might this reflect about IP?) I think it's perfectly legitimate to do.

5.  **[Question 4:]{.custom-label} Do you have any suggestions when it comes to the future of research methodology in studies on IP or processing instruction?**

    **BVP**: Yes, I mean we have a lot more techniques that get at what we might call more implicit or less conscious processing, things like self-paced reading and self-paced listening and eye-tracking and so on. Some of those tools weren't available back when I was getting started, and they're available now, and they might be useful in finding out what learners are actually doing in moment-by-moment processing. The problem is in dealing with *ab initio* learning. How do you track their processing when they know nothing? A major problem with processing research is that []{#ch27.xhtml_p394 .pagebreak aria-label=" page 394. " role="doc-pagebreak"}researchers have to wait until learners have sufficient knowledge of something so they can read sentences or hear sentences well enough to participate in an experiment. That's great, and we can make some inferences, but we have to figure out some way to use those tools to get at people who are just starting. What are they actually doing?

    I know Kara Morgan-Short has done some work like this with ERPs. And she's done work with true beginning learners. However, the issue with that line of research is that that it tells us basically some broad-scale explicit--implicit stuff but not the selection of particular data over other data. So I think that other tools where we can actually see what learners are focusing on---and I hate to use *focus* because that sounds conscious---what they're focusing on at a given point in time. There's got to be some way for us to start doing that, and we just haven't done it.

6.  **[Question 5:]{.custom-label} What do you view to be the most critical issues for future research in processing instruction and structured input and its instructional implications?**

    **BVP**: That's a good question, Joe \[Barcroft\] and Wynne \[Wong\]. For future work on processing instruction (PI), I think we've researched so many things from different perspectives and so on, that I'm not sure, actually, what the critical issues are. I think some people think they know what the critical issues are, but I think they're mistaken because they don't quite understand what PI actually is ... what the claims are about it. I think that because some of the stuff we've done and, more recently, looking at trials-to-criterion and Wynne's work, for example, which is on how processing strategies change in real-time ... I think those are revealing, very useful, and important because they actually do tell us something. The claim is that somehow PI is changing processing behaviors for particular things. So how do we show that? I think that this line of research is important. I don't know if I would call it critical, but it's very important. Again, I think when this research with what Wynne's done with eye-tracking and Nick Henry's research with self-paced reading show some real things about when processing behavior starts to change over time. But again, we're not actually looking at the behavior itself because we're watching what participants are doing when they push a button.Some of the work on secondary effects of PI, I think, is interesting, but I don't know if we're at a point where we can actually say that there are ripple effects on the rest of processing behaviors. I think it might be that processing is built, one by one, so you can change your processing behavior on something in one domain, with a particular structure, but you haven't changed \[your behavior\] with another structure that might be similar in appearance. My guess is that this is something we can look at. The idea that a learner lays the foundation first with one thing means that then the learner can start to work on another thing, and this might accelerate processing for learners because they already have a procedure that they can start to tap into in a different domain. So, it's not like learners are always reinventing the wheel when they go through a new kind of PI when the processing problem is the same or similar.

7.  **On Additional Thoughts: Thank you for answering those five questions. At this point if there is anything you would like to add, in terms of ideas reflections or suggestions, please feel free to add them**.

    **[]{#ch27.xhtml_p395 .pagebreak aria-label=" page 395. " role="doc-pagebreak"}BVP**: One thing we don't look at or talk about is, in the absence of something like PI, what triggers changes in IP? For example, let's take the first-noun strategy. Left to their own devices, without any kind of intervention like PI, what causes learners to abandon the first-noun principle or the first-noun strategy independently of instruction? Is it what Lydia White says, \[that\] there is some kind of parsing or processing failure, and that triggers the internal mechanisms? Maybe a piece of data somehow gets into the learner's system, and that triggers a process. We don't really understand why processing behaviors change. We know they have to change. We know they do, so how *do* they change? That's one thing we don't quite understand. That would mean looking at some of the different processing principles and strategies. Some may be related more to early stage or might be more later stage. To what extent, for example, do different principles kick in at different times or become active? What role do they play at different stages of acquisition, for example? We don't know those kinds of things, and so I think those are important things for us to be looking at over time.

    Another thing---and this is a real esoteric and sort of broad issue and I don't know to what extent it is feasible to talk about or look at---is, for lack of a better term, interface. Megan Smith and I published an Element on the explicit and implicit learning of language in the Cambridge Series. One of the things we've talked about is this problem of the word *interface*. Many researchers talk about the interface between explicit and implicit learning. If you look at the literature carefully, and we did, you cannot find a definition of *interface* anywhere. Nobody knows what an interface is or how it works; it is just a basket term we use. Because we believe there must be some connection, we call it *interface*, but what is the interface? How does something interface with something else? When I use the term *interface* here, I'm acknowledging the fact that I have no idea what it means. How does IP interface with the internal learning mechanisms, such as the general learning mechanisms that underlie acquisition as well as specialized mechanisms like maybe UG or something? Just the fact that you process something in input, does that mean it is usable by the internal system, whether they are general learning mechanisms or UG or some combination, or can they get ignored? We know little about that. You can process something, but maybe it's not usable yet by the system, so why does that happen and under what circumstance does that happen, and so on?

    Some of these things, I think, Michael Sharwood Smith and John Truscott get into in their MOGUL model. Those are issues that I think are some broader issues that people can be thinking about in terms of the relationship between IP and acquisition more generally. In short, acquisition doesn't stop at IP. Instead, IP is part of a much more complex phenomenon we call *language acquisition*

::: {.endnotes role="doc-endnotes"}

[^1]: . (2021) point out that VanPatten's model of input processing also has properties of a theory. In reference to VanPatten's model/theory, this volume uses model and theory interchangeably.

[^2]: [Leow (2012)](#ch3.xhtml_ref3_17){#ch3.xhtml_R_ref3_17 .biblioref role="doc-biblioref"} for several definitions of what constitutes intake in (I)SLA.

[^3]: .e., [Zalbidea, 2021](#ch3.xhtml_ref3_39){#ch3.xhtml_R_ref3_39 .biblioref role="doc-biblioref"}; [Ziegler, 2017](#ch3.xhtml_ref3_40){#ch3.xhtml_R_ref3_40 .biblioref role="doc-biblioref"}). We do not include these studies because they are situated at the output processing stage and not the input-to-intake stage of the L2 learning process.

[^4]: , self-paced reading or word monitoring tasks, which could be used with the same stimuli (in different modes). Our concern is that such methods do not reflect natural reading or listening processing and, consequently, may not be easily extrapolated to account for what takes place in the instructed setting.

[^5]: .

[^6]: .

[^7]: : we capitalize concepts in order to distinguish a semantic primitive like GO from the English word *go* which will be italicized. We also use *italic* font for emphasis, double quotes "..." when citing, and single quotes '...' for glosses of the meaning of expressions or as 'scare quotes'. In phonemic transcriptions dots "." separate syllables and underscores "\_" separate word forms.

[^8]: . How the languages were simplified in our various studies depended on the goals of each individual study. In our studies, we exposed adult *ab initio* learners (all native speakers of English) to small sets of words, limited syntactic constructions, and only a subset of the repertoire of sounds permitted in the source language. Lab-languages contrast with artificial languages that are created from scratch by researchers (e.g., [Braine, 1963](#ch7.xhtml_ref7_9){.biblioref role="doc-biblioref"}; [Morgan & Newport, 1981](#ch7.xhtml_ref7_49){#ch7.xhtml_R_ref7_49 .biblioref role="doc-biblioref"}; [Saffran et al., 1996](#ch7.xhtml_ref7_55){.biblioref role="doc-biblioref"}; [Valian & Coulson, 1988](#ch7.xhtml_ref7_62){.biblioref role="doc-biblioref"}). Whether using Lab-languages or artificial languages, these studies involve "controlled input". However, what is rarely discussed is the fact that many artificial languages exhibit highly non-natural properties. Nonetheless, the extent to which simplified Lab-languages and artificial languages reflect the properties of target natural languages is a matter of empirical investigation.

[^9]: , we mean that none of the three systems can be reduced to any of the others; representations are built out of system-unique primitives by unification operations that are subject to system-unique constraints. By "modular", we mean that speech and language processors compute each type of []{#ch7.xhtml_p99 .pagebreak aria-label=" page 99. " role="doc-pagebreak"}representation with only limited access to information from the other representations of a word or sentence being computed.

[^10]: to refer to 'stuff' outside the learner's mind in the learning environment. For us, this means objective properties of the speech signal, which can be described in acoustic-phonetic terms, but usually are not. However, many researchers end up reifying abstract linguistic constructs, assuming that learners do indeed mentally represent them (e.g., syllables, words, meanings). This confounds the objective foundation with the cognitive a priori. Accordingly, we distinguish *exposure* from *input*. We do not use the term *intake* as sometimes this term is used as a cover term to denote abstract mental representations, i.e., what learners have attended to and processed in some way as they listen to speech. [VanPatten (2004, p. 7)](#ch7.xhtml_ref7_63){#ch7.xhtml_R_ref7_63 .biblioref role="doc-biblioref"} clarifies this interpretation, claiming that *intake* is whatever is in working memory. In our approach, input is not always what is available in working memory. We believe that much of "classification" and "abstraction" involves operations over representations in long term memory and that "language learning" is not restricted to what can be assessed by real-time processing.

[^11]: .

[^12]: .

[^13]: , Person, Number) regardless of whether they express ACTIONs or STATEs, or individual- or stage-level PROPERTIES. If one decides on a set of word classes by relying on formal marking, then not all languages motivate a formal contrast between "Verbs" and "Adjectives". The conservative position, the one we adopt, is to say that the status of adjectives remains unresolved in the theories of word classes and phrase structure.

[^14]: to facilitate exposition. This notational choice should not distract from the fact that the notation expresses a universally available concept. Thus, French verbs of motion {*aller*, *conduire*, *descendre*, *monter*, *...*}, German verbs of motion {*gehen*, *hinein/gehen*, *heraus/gehen ...*} and so on will all be expressed by the same universal predicate GO.

[^15]: ," [Leeser et al. (2021)](#ch8.xhtml_ref8_25){.biblioref role="doc-biblioref"} have pointed out that the model also has properties of a theory. In reference to VanPatten's work, this chapter uses model and theory interchangeably.

[^16]: [VanPatten, 1996](#ch8.xhtml_ref8_39){.biblioref role="doc-biblioref"}, pages 130--140 for a more complete discussion on intake and UG.

[^17]: , see [VanPatten (1996](#ch8.xhtml_ref8_39){.biblioref role="doc-biblioref"}, pp. 51--53) and VanPatten (2004, pp. 23--24).
