[^19]: . By convention conceptual structures are written in capital letters.

[^20]: .

[^21]: , the TOPRA model is more consistent with the idea of transfer appropriate processing (TAP) proposed by [Morris et al. (1977)](#ch11.xhtml_ref11_39){#ch11.xhtml_R_ref11_39 .biblioref role="doc-biblioref"} and the study they reported, which found that semantic processing was not always more effective than structural processing. Differences between TOPRA and TAP include the following: (a) TOPRA was developed based on findings of research motivated primarily by LOP, not TAP; (b) TOPRA was created/developed independently from TAP, and consistencies between the two were later noted; (c) the movable lines in the TOPRA model reflect quantities of different types of processing, something not included in TAP; (d) the TOPRA model, at least to date, has been used primarily to focus on the form, meaning, and mapping components of vocabulary learning whereas TAP has not.

[^22]:

[^23]: , how "new L2 vocabulary learning" is defined and assessed. The original study by [Hulstijn and Laufer (2001)](#ch11.xhtml_ref11_24){#ch11.xhtml_R_ref11_24 .biblioref role="doc-biblioref"} that tested the prediction of the hypothesis used cued recall, where target L2 word forms were shown, and the participants were asked to provide either L1 equivalents or L2 explanations. Some later studies subsequently tested the involvement load hypothesis (e.g., [Folse, 2006](#ch11.xhtml_ref11_15){#ch11.xhtml_R_ref11_15 .biblioref role="doc-biblioref"}; [Keating, 2008](#ch11.xhtml_ref11_29){#ch11.xhtml_R_ref11_29 .biblioref role="doc-biblioref"}; [Kim, 2006](#ch11.xhtml_ref11_35){#ch11.xhtml_R_ref11_35 .biblioref role="doc-biblioref"}; [Zou, 2017](#ch11.xhtml_ref11_46){#ch11.xhtml_R_ref11_46 .biblioref role="doc-biblioref"}), but these were inconsistent in their selection of the type of vocabulary tests (see [Yanagisawa & Webb, 2021](#ch11.xhtml_ref11_44){#ch11.xhtml_R_ref11_44 .biblioref role="doc-biblioref"}, for the meta-analysis). This indicates that researchers do not have a clear consensus regarding what is actually learned within the involvement load framework making it difficult to directly compare the involvement load hypothesis and TOPRA. The other point is that the involvement load hypothesis was proposed in the context of incidental vocabulary learning. It is an open question how the three involvement load factors work in intentional L2 vocabulary learning.

[^24]: , which again shows a preference for subject-before-object processing that is not based on the position of the verb. It would be highly relevant to examine how L2 German learners interpret these ambiguous NNV structures in order further assess the influence of the FNP in different languages.

[^25]: , the direct object marker *a* would also have to be added to make it grammatical in Spanish: pro *escuchan bien **a** los niños*. One could argue that the lack of an *a* in the experimental sentence would dissuade learners from positing a null subject. However, research (e.g., [Bowles & Montrul, 2009](#ch14.xhtml_ref14_11){#ch14.xhtml_R_ref14_11 .biblioref role="doc-biblioref"}; [VanPatten, 1984](#ch14.xhtml_ref14_42){.biblioref role="doc-biblioref"}) has shown that learners often disregard direct object marking, so this factor is not likely to shape sentence interpretation in a significant way.

[^26]: . As [Strange (1995)](#ch16.xhtml_ref16_84){#ch16.xhtml_R_ref16_84 .biblioref role="doc-biblioref"} explains, "contrastive analyses of the phoneme inventories of different languages do not contain enough detail about the articulatory or acoustic structure of phonetic segments to allow researchers to make informed predictions about perceptual patterns or possible learning difficulties" (p. 8).

[^27]: .g., lexical tones in Mandarin), but for simplicity, I focus for the most part on segmental (e.g., consonant, vowel) units.

[^28]: (e.g., [McClelland & Elman, 1986](#ch16.xhtml_ref16_72){#ch16.xhtml_R_ref16_72 .biblioref role="doc-biblioref"}; [Norris, 1994](#ch16.xhtml_ref16_75){#ch16.xhtml_R_ref16_75 .biblioref role="doc-biblioref"}), with phonetic categories mediating this relationship and with lexical representations containing these abstract units rather than fine-grained acoustic details (cf. [Johnson, 1997](#ch16.xhtml_ref16_55){#ch16.xhtml_R_ref16_55 .biblioref role="doc-biblioref"}; [Klatt, 1989](#ch16.xhtml_ref16_58){#ch16.xhtml_R_ref16_58 .biblioref role="doc-biblioref"}; [Marslen-Wilson & Warren, 1994](#ch16.xhtml_ref16_71){#ch16.xhtml_R_ref16_71 .biblioref role="doc-biblioref"}).

[^29]: , but this error-and-efficiency-driven learning process remains qualitatively the same over time (for discussion, see [Carroll, 1999](#ch16.xhtml_ref16_16){#ch16.xhtml_R_ref16_16 .biblioref role="doc-biblioref"}).

[^30]: . From a phonological perspective, successful IP in the L2 requires more than just the correct mapping of acoustic information onto L2 phonetic categories. For example, probabilities of co-occurrence of phonetic categories (i.e., phonotactic information), which differ from language to language, also play an important role in speech perception ([Dupoux et al., 1999](#ch16.xhtml_ref16_27){#ch16.xhtml_R_ref16_27 .biblioref role="doc-biblioref"}; [Kabak & Idsardi, 2007](#ch16.xhtml_ref16_56){#ch16.xhtml_R_ref16_56 .biblioref role="doc-biblioref"}) and in helping listeners locate word boundaries in []{#ch16.xhtml_p235 .pagebreak aria-label=" page 235. " role="doc-pagebreak"}the speech signal (e.g., [Weber & Cutler, 2006](#ch16.xhtml_ref16_97){#ch16.xhtml_R_ref16_97 .biblioref role="doc-biblioref"}). Suprasegmental information such as lexical stress and intonation also mediate L2 speech processing (e.g., [Connell et al., 2018](#ch16.xhtml_ref16_25){.biblioref role="doc-biblioref"}; [Dupoux et al., 2008](#ch16.xhtml_ref16_28){.biblioref role="doc-biblioref"}; [Tremblay et al., 2018](#ch16.xhtml_ref16_88){.biblioref role="doc-biblioref"}). For the sake of conciseness, I do not discuss these issues here.

[^31]: , they are assumed to attend to that cue for perceiving this contrast.

[^32]: . They differ from synthesized stimuli in that they sound more natural (if the acoustic manipulation is well done) and show natural variability in those acoustic parameters that are not manipulated. The great majority of recent studies on L2 speech perception and processing use naturally produced nonmanipulated or resynthesized speech stimuli.

[^33]: , many could fall within this framework.

[^34]: , affective and referential. Referential activities are task-essential, while affective activities are not. Throughout the chapter, references to PI and SI refer to referential activities.

[^35]: . (2013) did not include a production task.

[^36]: , the effectiveness of EI may be influenced by the delivery and availability of the EI and the learners' ability to understand it. See the "Future Directions" section.

[^37]: , note that OVS structures vary across languages. EI may interact with language-specific factors such as the case syncretism or the salience of case markers, such that it is helpful for one OVS form but not another.

[^38]: , neither implicit nor explicit knowledge is in principle superior to the other, and all learners draw on both types of knowledge to some degree.
